1.1 
GENERAL 
1.1.1 
General Considerations 
1.1.1.1 
The Vehicle Operations Structures Standards (GVOSS) define the Ford motor 
Company Vehicle Operations Manufacturing Engineering (VOME) program and 
equipment requirements that shall be followed by all suppliers of equipment to 
Ford Motor Company VOME. 
1.1.1.2 
Tier 1 Integrators shall integrate these standards into their business practices 
and equipment as it relates to conducting business with Ford Motor Company 
VOME. 
1.1.1.3 
Using these standards as set forth in the GVOSS in no way absolves the Tier 1 
Integrators of their responsibilities to exhibit competency in the design, 
engineering, manufacturing, commissioning and any other facet of business 
practice as it relates to delivering equipment that satisfies all VOME equipment 
safety, environmental, quality and functional requirements. 
1.1.1.4 
The use of the word “shall” is used to indicate a strict requirement for compliance 
to the subject material. All noncompliant conditions must be approved using the 
deviation process. The word “should” is used to indicate the material is a 
suggestion or guideline. 
1.1.1.5 
Where references to other standards are included, they are considered 
requirements by incorporation into these standards. Where conflicts between 
standards exist, the requirements of this standard shall govern. 
1.1.1.6 
Requirements mentioned in this specification, which are also mentioned in either 
the Ford Motor Standards of Industrial Equipment or Structures Standards 
Specifications for Automated Tooling, Welding Machines and Fixtures, are done 
to emphasize the specific item and does not relieve the Tier 1 Integrator of the 
other requirements pertinent to that item. 
1.1.1.7 
When existing equipment must be modified, the Standards used shall be in 
accordance to the "actual" Standards in-place on site, except for any items 
mentioned in the Statement of Work for that project. After that, the requirements 
of this Specification becomes effective. New equipment and lines shall comply 
with this specification. 
1.1.1.8 
Any reference in this document to ‘Tier 1 Integrator’ is deemed for the ‘Line 
builder that received the main contract from Ford Motor Company’. 
1.1.1.9 
Any reference in this document to ‘Tier 2 Equipment supplier’ is deemed for 
equipment supplier to the Tier 1 Integrator, including any Ford to Specify and 
Ford to Furnish items. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 5 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS

1.1.1.10 
When there is any conflict of requirements between this document and any 
project site local laws/code, Specifications, Norms etc., then the project site 
local law/code requirements shall take precedence, unless a written agreement 
between Ford Motor Company and the Tier1 Integrator is available. 
1.1.1.11 
All Tier 1 Integrators, Tier 2 and FTF suppliers shall be responsible for informing 
their suppliers and subcontractors of these standards where applicable. 
1.1.1.12 
Where the Ford Global Manufacturing Standards differ with the requirements 
contained within this standard, the requirements within this standard shall 
prevail. 
1.1.1.13 
For the protection of personnel and property the design, build, installation and 
operation of the equipment shall comply with all existing OSHA, NFPA, Federal, 
State, Provincial, and Local codes including Ontario Hydro, Ontario Pre-Start 
Health and Safety Review, CSA, UL and Chicago City codes where applicable. 
The cost of any standards compliance verification requirements shall be the sole 
responsibility of the Tier 1 Integrator. 
1.1.1.13.1 
P.E Certifications shall align with the authority having jurisdiction where the 
tooling and equipment is to be installed. 
1.1.1.13.2 
TEVC tooling shall be designed to resist the seismic loading per the “ASCE7-16 
Chapter 13” and the IBC 2018.  The design shall be based on the equipment 
vertical, horizontal loading and the moment at the base utilizing the E-stop loads. 
1.1.1.14 
NRTL field inspections 
1.1.1.14.1 
Tier 2 and FTF equipment suppliers are required to provide NRTL inspections 
and approvals of their equipment as a system. (ex. joining supplier equipment, 
robot supplier, etc.). A NRTL re-inspection is required on kitting existing control 
panels and/or system.  Tier-1 integrator shall produce a copy of the original and 
revised drawings and pictures for NRTL inspectors’ review and approval. 
1.1.1.14.2 
For the Dearborn complex, Chicago Assembly and Oakville Assembly plants: 
• A NRTL field inspection is required on new or kitted tools. 
• Tier 1 integrator to provide overall system NRTL inspections and 
approvals which includes FTF/FTS control panels such as PDPs, Tooling 
HMI enclosures, etc. 
• Completion of build at integrators’ floor and prior to shipment to a Ford 
facility 
• Completion of installation at the receiving plant prior to production. For 
Oakville Assembly plant a Pre-Start Health and Safety Review is required 
after plant installation and prior to start of production. 
• An NRTL field re-inspection is required on kitting existing control panels 
and/or system.  Tier-1 integrator shall produce a copy of the original and

SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 6 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
revised drawings and pictures for NRTL inspectors’ review and approval. 
1.1.1.15 
Special attention shall be paid to requirements for bilingual labels and signage 
where they may exist. 
1.1.1.16 
Safety to operation and maintenance personnel shall be a prime consideration in 
the equipment design. The design and build of equipment shall meet or exceed 
Ford Corporate Safety and VOME Safety standards. 
1.1.1.17 
The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible for providing equipment which meets Ford 
Motor Company’s FPS (Ford Production System) and GPDS (Global Product 
Development System) requirements. 
1.1.1.18 
Ford Motor Company requires all components used on equipment and tooling to 
be purchased New. Used, Refurbished or Repurposed components shall be 
prohibited unless prior approval is provided, in writing, by VOME Management 
and VO Purchasing. 
1.1.1.19 
All tooling systems shall include robot battery replacements before shipping to 
the plants. 
1.1.1.20 
When a Tier 2 Equipment Supplier wants to include a component or piece of 
equipment within their system which has not been previously approved and used 
within Ford, a design review must be held.  Examples include: new servo motors 
on spot weld guns, new feeding equipment for drawn arc bracket welding, etc.  
This design review is to be scheduled by the Tier 2 Equipment Supplier to 
include VOME and Controls personnel as required.  
1.1.1.21 
Welders shall be qualified by an AWS Certified Weld Inspector (CWI) as 
prescribed in the American Welding Society AWS D1.1/D1.1M Section 4. 
• Canadian Standard CSA W47.1-1983 for equipment being installed in Canada. 
• Equivalent country and local Welder certification qualifications shall apply. 
1.1.1.22 
Use of Non-approved wireless devices shall follow the procedural guidelines 
contained within the Ford Regional Wireless Device Approval Guidelines. 
Devices - Wireless Device Approval (sharepoint.com).  
1.1.1.23 
The main purpose of evaluating a proposed wireless device is to determine 
whether the device wireless characteristics will conflict with other devices’ 
wireless characteristics on the plant floor.

1.1.1.24 
The use of the word “Cell” in this document, refers to a safety zone within a given 
system as defined during the Line layout review. Any given system (Line or Sub-
Assembly) may have multiple cells (safety zones).  A Cell - a safety zone which 
shares gates, entry/exit light screens and e-stops – is an area that can be 
entered to perform minor permitted tasks without affecting the safeties of any 
adjacent cells. 
1.1.2 
Deviations 
1.1.2.1 
Deviations to any portion of this specification shall only be allowed after a 
GVOSS standard Action Request Form is submitted and approved prior to 
Design Approval. All Action Request Forms for a Deviation shall be input via the 
E2KS system. Review the GVOSS Deviation Creation SPL. 
1.1.2.2 
The deviation process applies to all Tier 1 Integrators, VOME employees, new 
model program launch team members, stamping and/or assembly plant 
personnel. 
1.1.2.3 
The deviation process shall be followed for any requested change to the controls 
and/or mechanical components that have been specified and approved for a 
given new model program or those components that have been deployed and set 
up in a given legacy plant's General Stores inventory (Reference: Industrial 
Material Component List for that given plant). 
1.1.2.4 
Deviations to Strategies 
1.1.2.4.1 
Definition: Body Construction Engineering (BCE) Processes that have been 
either internally or globally agreed upon. 
1.1.2.4.2 
Any modifications to these strategies require a formal Action Request Form for a 
Deviation signed-off BEFORE proceeding. All BCE Action Request Forms for a 
Deviation shall be input via the E2KS system. 
1.1.2.4.3 
Strategy modifications are typically driven by unique constraints within a specific 
plant. They may also be driven by installation of tooling within a footprint planned 
for future due to an investment decision. 
1.1.2.4.4 
Strategy changes are typically driven by repetition of the same modification or 
internal and/or global agreements. 
1.1.2.4.5 
Base Quote - as per the SOW any deviation that is part of the formal Base Quote 
must be signed-off and included as record as part of the Base Quote agreement. 
1.1.2.4.6 
Post Base Quote - a deviation must be written for items identified during the 
design/build/install process that change or modify the strategy. These must be 
immediately written and given to the respective VIS, or commodity supervisor if a 
VIS not part of the organization, for internal agreement and signature BEFORE 
proceeding with any further work. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 8 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS

1.1.2.5 
Process Deviations 
1.1.2.5.1 
Deviation Procedure: Deviations to the flex-compliant standard cells or templates 
shall be submitted by a requesting activity to the Car / Truck Processing Group, 
utilizing the Flex Compliant Standard Deviation Request Form. These deviation 
requests will be reviewed by the Car / Truck Supervisor /Managers / Chief 
Engineer and if approved, will be signed off for program use only. Deviation 
request forms should be sent to: Process & Strategy Development Engineer. 
1.1.2.6 
Deviations to GVOSS Standards 
1.1.2.6.1 
Definition: Body Construction Engineering (BCE) Tool & Equipment Standards as 
identified in the GVOSS. 
1.1.2.6.2 
Any change to these Standards requires a formal Action Request Form for a 
Deviation signed off BEFORE proceeding. For Action Request Forms for a 
Deviation shall be input via the E2KS system. 
1.1.2.6.3 
GVOSS modifications are typically driven because of unique conditions within a 
tool that affects a specific plant. 
1.1.2.6.4 
GVOSS changes are typically driven by repetition of the same modification or 
unit cost improvements. 
1.1.2.6.5 
Base Quote - as per the SOW, any deviation that is part of the formal Base 
Quote shall be signed-off by VOME and the Ford Motor Company Buyer in 
writing and included for record as part of the Base Quote agreement per the Ford 
Motor Company Purchasing Global Terms and Conditions of the Purchase 
Agreement. 
1.1.2.6.6 
Post Base Quote - a deviation shall be written for items identified during the 
design/build/install process that change or modify adherence to the GVOSS 
standards. These shall be immediately written and given to the respective VIS for 
Internal agreement and signature BEFORE proceeding with any further work. 
1.1.3 
Quoting Projects 
1.1.3.1 
Quotes shall be to the project Statement of Work (SOW) 
1.1.3.2 
Alternates to the project SOW shall only be allowed when specifically stated in 
the SOW. 
1.1.3.3 
Supplier terms and conditions or other elements included by the Tier 1 Integrator, 
not specifically identified in the project SOW are non-binding to Ford Motor 
Company unless agreed upon, in writing by the Ford Motor Company Buyer who 
originated the Request for Quote (RFQ) per the Ford Motor Company Global 
Terms and Conditions of the Purchase Order (PO) when issued.

1.1.3.4 
Tier 1 Integrators shall provide the list of the FTF items with the associated 
budgetary as part of the bid. 
1.1.4 
 
Environmental Stewardship and Safety 
1.1.4.1 
Environmental Concerns 
Tier 1 Integrators shall comply with Ford environmental requirements. These 
requirements are stated in the following standards: 
• FAP03-132: Production, Non-Production, and Post-Production Materials. 
• Restricted Substance Management Standard (RSMS). The RSMS is the 
requirement for the Tier 1 Integrator to comply with prohibited materials. 
EQT04-001 WW: Contractor Environmental Awareness Training. 
• EOP06-005: Environmental Operations. Section 7.3 is the management of 
on- site contractors. 
• EOP06-006: Environmental Requirements Associated with Excavation, 
Removal, Investigation, Disposal Activities. 
Contact Corporate Safety for the latest EOP06-005 or EOP06-006 Environmental 
Operations documents. 
1.1.4.2 
Tier 1 Integrators shall comply with the RESTRICTED SUBSTANCE 
MANAGEMENT STANDARD  WSS-M99P9999-A1.  
NOTE: Special attention shall be made for the following materials: 
• Cadmium or its compounds (P) All applications except those listed below 
0.01%. 
• Cadmium or its compounds (D) Only for Zinc containing paints with more than 
10% Zinc (Cadmium prohibition threshold for these zinc paints is 0.1%) 
0.01%. 
• Lead or its compounds (P) All products 0.1%. 
• Chromium (VI)-salts (Cr+6; Hexavalent) (P) All Products 0.1% (m). 
1.1.5 
Ford Furnished Items 
1.1.5.1 
Ford Motor Company will supply commercial items as listed on the Ford to 
Furnish (FTF) Items Sheet included with the quotation package project SOW. 
1.1.5.1.1 
Tier 1 Integrator shall provide the list of the FTF items with the associated 
budgetary as part of the bid. 
1.1.5.2 
Ford to Furnish Sheets 
1.1.5.2.1 
FNA Only: BCE (Body Construction Engineering) Ford to Furnish sheets are to 
be accessed through the SharePoint site at:

1.1.5.3 
Other Requirements 
1.1.5.3.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator will provide earliest and latest required dates in a Ford 
Furnished Item request letter. This letter will provide required dates for all Ford 
Furnished Materials including sheet metal. A “corrected” copy of the request 
letter should be written in a timely manner to meet schedules and lead times. 
Ford to Furnish is a “Form” to be filled out by vendor. 
1.1.5.3.2 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall ship excess Ford Furnished Items, at their expense, to 
a Ford Motor Company designated location upon completion of the program. 
1.1.5.3.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator is to inspect Ford Furnished Items as they are received and 
report any discrepancies or shortages to the Ford Manufacturing Engineer. 
1.1.5.4 
Identification of FTF Equipment  
 
NOTE: See also FTF Lessons Learned for additional requirements. 
1.1.5.4.1 
All Station numbers on the FTF sheets need to be in a 3-digit format for Station 
identification. Example: instead of Station 10; use Station 010. 
1.1.5.4.2 
All Robot numbers on the FTF sheets need to be in a 2-digit format for Robot 
identification. Example: instead of Robot 1; use Robot 01. 
1.1.5.4.3 
Use exact robot ID in weld gun approvals for tracking purposes. 
1.1.5.4.4 
A Robot / Joining Equipment Matrix shall be maintained by integrators and be 
submitted after the initial weld gun and robot orders are complete, revised and 
submitted after process changes. 
1.1.5.4.5 
All Nex-Gen Control Panels shall be identified by the Station of their physical 
location. See Nex-Gen FTF Panel Station Identification. 
1.1.5.4.6 
For Nex-Gen Line Level control panels use station “000”. See Nex-Gen FTF Line 
Level Panel Identification. 
 
1.1.6 
Vendor Tool Tryout Material Requirements 
1.1.6.1 
Vendor Tool Tryout Material (VTTO) 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 11 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.1.6.1.1 
Generic VTTO quantities are documented in the VTTO Material Requirements 
document. These quantities should be used as the starting point when ordering 
VTTO material and should be adjusted per the specific requirements of the 
program. 
1.1.6.1.2 
VO Manufacturing Engineer (ME) and Tier 1 Integrator (if available) shall start 
reviewing the Preliminary Version of the VTTO Requirements Form (VRF) with 
the BCE VTTO Sheet Metal Coordinator 40 Weeks prior DCV Trim/Chassis MRD 
(TCMRD). (Reference LQOS 214 – Vendor Tool Tryout and Manufacturing 
Partial Builds and VOPGON207). The Preliminary VRF shall be submitted to the 
VTTO Sheet Metal Coordinator 30 weeks prior to the DCV TCMRD. Form must 
be signed by the LL6 of the program.  If the quantities exceed “VTTO Material 
Requirements” Totals, document must include signature from the Program LL5/4. 
1.1.6.1.3 
Final Version of the VRF shall be submitted to VTTO Sheet Metal Coordinator 22 
weeks prior to DCV TCMRD. Verify VRF Form is populated and delivery dates 
match program Gantt Chart. Form must be signed by the LL6 of the program.  If 
the quantities exceed “VTTO Material Requirements” Totals, document must 
include signature from the Program LL5/4. 
1.1.6.1.4 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall also verify that quantities of VTTO Material support the 
“build your own sub-assembly” process. 
1.1.6.1.5 
Material Receive date for programming/tryout/debug shall be 3 weeks prior to 
power on date. 
1.1.6.1.6 
Buyoff Material Receive date shall be 3 weeks prior to buyoff date. This is for the 
remainder of the sheet metal that is not required for programming/tryout/debug. 
1.1.6.1.7 
If required receive date is before DCV TCMRD then an alternate plan shall be 
developed. VTTO metal will not be available prior to DCV TCMRD. 
1.1.6.1.8 
Tier 1 Integrator shall review VRF and New Model Programs Build Schedule at 
10 Weeks prior to DCV TCMRD with BCE Sheet Metal Coordinator and VO ME 
to ensure scheduled delivery dates meet program timing. 
1.1.6.1.9 
After a 10 week prior to TC VP MRD review, the Tier 1 integrator shall develop a 
VTTO usage plan based on Final VRF quantities and tool buyoff timing. 
1.1.6.1.10 
The Tier 1 Integrator will be responsible to track the vendor tool tryout metal at 
their own expense that is received into their facilities to prove out the tooling. 
1.1.6.1.11 
The tracking of the material shall start with the first delivery of material and will be 
on a daily basis. 
1.1.6.1.12 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible to verify that the part numbers on the 
tag, the shipper, the VRF, the actual part and the process flow chart match. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 12 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.1.6.1.13 
The filled out VTTO Requirements Form (VRF) tracking matrix, including any 
discrepancies, will be reported to the Manufacturing Engineer, Vendor Interface 
Supervisor, and VTTO Sheet Metal Coordinator on a weekly basis prior to the 
weekly VTTO Sheet Metal Delivery Review Meeting. 
1.1.6.1.14 
Present updated VRF (with tracking) at Weekly VTTO Sheet Metal Delivery 
Review Meeting (Track % received or QTY received vs. total QTY). 
1.1.6.1.15 
Items not resolved in Weekly Meeting are to be elevated in VTTO Supervisor. 
1.1.6.2 
Prototype stampings and end items required for VTTO (Vendor Tool Tryout) that 
are supplied by Ford Motor Company and soft tool suppliers and shipped to the 
Tier 1 Integrator’s build location will be accompanied by Level 3-Dimensional 
Data on ONLY the first six sets of the total quantity ordered. All parts with data 
requirements must be received with attached data to their respective receiving 
location. Materials designated for delivery to the New Model Program 
Development Center DOES NOT require dimensional data. 
1.1.6.3 
Fasteners and Joining 
1.1.6.3.1 
For the purpose of this section the term fasteners shall include but is not limited 
to: 
• 
Bolts 
• 
Clinch Nuts 
• 
Pierce Nuts 
• 
Pierce Studs  
• 
Rivets 
• 
Special Nuts 
• 
Weld Nuts 
• 
Weld Studs 
1.1.6.3.2 
The procurement of all fasteners (weld nuts, weld studs, SPAC nuts, clinch nuts 
etc.) will be the responsibility of the Tier 1 Integrator and to be included in the 
Tier 1 Integrators’ quote. 
1.1.6.3.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible for furnishing fastener information and 
fasteners to the Tier 2 suppliers. 
1.1.6.3.4 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible for fastener purchase timing to support 
Tier 2 equipment buy-off and VTTO. 
1.1.6.3.5 
Wire for welding equipment (laser, mig, etc.) will be the responsibility of the Tier 1 
Integrator to procure and to be included in their quote.

• Adhesive

The procurement of all Dispensed Materials (Mastic, Sealer, Adhesive, etc.) will

1.1.6.4.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible for furnishing Dispensed Material

(SOW).

1.1.7 
FMEA Requirements 
1.1.7.1 
Tier 1 Integrators shall use the Ford Motor Company FMEA Handbook Version 
4.2 (or later version), reference manual for all FMEA's. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
1.1.7.2 
Tier 1 Integrators shall use the AIAG manual "FMEA for Tooling & Equipment 
(Machinery FMEA)" Version/Edition: 1 (or later version), Product Code: MFMEA-
1 as the reference manual and source of forms for all MFMEA's. This manual 
supports the machinery (the term machinery as used in this manual applies to 
tooling and equipment also) design process from design development through 
design approval. 
1.1.7.3 
See also the MFMEA Form for reference. 
1.1.7.4 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall support the development of the Process FMEA 
(PFMEA) as documented in the statement of work. See also VOPGOG-212. 
1.1.7.4.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall coordinate and document the Machinery FMEA 
(MFMEA) for any new equipment application. 
1.1.7.5 
Tier 1 Integrators shall update all applicable FMEA’s as required to maintain 
accuracy with Process and Machinery changes. FMEA’s are “living” documents 
and shall be updated to reflect “Current State”. 
1.1.7.6 
Tier 1 Integrators shall update all PFMEA’s to reflect process changes through 
the course of a program including Engineering Changes. 
1.1.7.7 
Tier 1 Integrators shall update all MFMEA’s as long as their equipment is in 
service at Ford Motor Company.

1.1.8 
Reliability and Maintainability 
1.1.8.1 
The design of all equipment must conform to Ford Motor Company Reliability and 
Maintainability Specification for Manufacturing Equipment (R&M Specification), 
Ford Motor Company Global Manufacturing Standards, Ford Motor Company 
Engineering CAD & Drafting Standards (FECDS), applicable local, state and 
federal codes and the standards included herein. 
1.1.8.2 
The Tier 1 Integrator will compile and maintain a Program Evidence Book for 
each system. This book will contain all documentation generated to support the 
program. It will include all elements on the R&M 5-point process as defined in the 
R&M Specification (See also the Manufacturing Reliability Reference Guide for 
additional information): 
1. Data Driven Understanding of Historical Equipment Performance 
– Mean Time Between Failure 
– Mean Time to Repair 
– Equipment Availability 
– Hierarchy Fault Tree Analysis 
2. Focus on Reliability Growth 
– Analysis of the data to determine the major failure modes 
– Discovery of the root cause of failures 
– Engineer solutions to the major failures by addressing the root 
cause 
– Machinery FMEA analysis 
– Verification of the effectiveness of the design change 
3. Tier 2 Supplier / Component R&M (see Tab 15 of Buy-Off Book, 
referenced          
           in 1.2.5 and 1.2.6.) 
– Component historical performance 
– Component reliability growth 
– Component application letters as verification of proper usage. 
4. Preventative Maintenance and Logistics (Spare Parts) 
– MFM Preventative Maintenance Proforma (see GVOSS Section 
1.2.2) 
– Recommended Industrial Materials List (see GVOSS Section 1.2.8) 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 16 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.1.9 
Manufacturing Process 
1.1.9.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible for providing equipment that will support the 
manufacturing 
process 
control, 
process 
capability 
and 
process 
target 
requirements, thru-put requirements and all Ford Motor Company standards. 
1.1.9.2 
Once a process is agreed to, the Tier 1 Integrator shall optimize the system 
throughput via joining processing, robot programming and positioning as part of 
buyoff criteria. Specified line rate is only a minimum criterion. 
1.1.9.3 
The design requirement shall follow VORGOG D05: Bodyshop Capacity 
Guidelines. 
1.1.9.4 
Part Manufacturing Process Identification 
1.1.9.4.1 
When a manufacturing process utilizes more than one tool, face plate or line 
segment that can manufacture exactly the same part, a means of identifying 
which tool, face plate, or line segment shall be implemented. A mechanical 
stamp shall be the means used for identification. 
1.1.9.4.2 
When a manufacturing process requires the date of manufacture or other 
tracking identifier is necessary, a mechanical stamp shall be the means used for 
identification. 
• Bodyshop in-process subassembly do not get date stamp unless it is 
service part / subassembly. 
• All closure manufacturing assembly gets date stamp. 
• Date stamp must be visible on service part / subassembly. 
• Stamping Plant in-process subassembly & service part / subassembly 
shall get mechanical date stamp. 
• Date stamp may not be closed out on BIW (shipped to Bodyshop). 
• Date stamp must be visible on service part / subassembly. 
1.1.10 
Equipment Simulations 
Simulation models of all workstations or line designs shall be created in 
Process Simulate. (Reference; Process Simulate Specification, Version 
2.0.0). 
 
1. Process Simulate simulations must show a complete line or station 
simulation with no errors and follow all manufacturing methods.   
2. Process Simulate files should follow the TC-0054 – Study Data 
Management for Body method for file creation and delivery to Teamcenter 
3. Process Simulate files should be loaded to the MANUFACTURING 
STUDIES folder in Teamcenter.

Dash 9B Process Simulate Study)

1.1.11 
Mechanical Design CAD 
1.1.11.1 
Ford Motor Company Engineering CAD & Drafting Standards are available in this 
document and other supporting documentation, including specifications and 
methods.  Mechanical Design CAD must be delivered to Ford through 
Teamcenter and must be classified in Teamcenter. 
1.1.11.2 
All equipment must be designed using the applicable Ford Motor Company, 
Virtual Body Shop Design Software Tools and Standards. For C3PNG programs 
(see sec 1.1.11.2.1), FIDES will be the requirement for Fixture Design.  For 
Digital Thread (DT) programs (see sec 1.1.11.2.2),  NX will be the requirement 
for Fixture Design.  Exceptions can be made to use NX on C3PNG programs, but 
no exceptions will be made on DT programs.  Consult your VIS or SOW for 
program and PLM information.  
Reference the FIDES Specification. 
Reference the NX Specification.

FIDES

tools with automation.

populated based on manufacturing method

2. Joining Equipment:

HENROB-10199- A107-PR1).

format. No guns or humans or extra geometry will be in this group, 
see FIDES spec and methods.

1.1.11.3 
Import of product data into FIDES fixture design software can be either original 
CAD (IDEAS, CATIA) or equivalent in JT file format from Team Center. Internal 
studies have shown the import of data in JT format to be faster and will maintain 
data integrity. 
1.1.11.4 
Data exchange with Ford Motor Company must be implemented using Program 
PDM (Product Data Manager) i.e., Metaphase / TCe (Team Center Engineering), 
or Certified CDSP (CAD Data Service Provider). 
1.1.11.5 
All tool designs shall be loaded into TCe prior to invoicing for mechanical design.

Maintenance access. 
1.1.12.4

1.1.13 
Finite Element Analysis (FEA) 
1.1.13.1 
A Finite Element Analysis (FEA) shall be completed for all new and reworked 
non-steel tooling frames and end-effectors. Steel applications require an FEA 
unless there is successful surrogate data for the design. This requires the 
approval of the VIS or Mechanical Design Follow-up representative. 
1.1.13.2 
FEA model must account for static and dynamic loading. 
1.1.13.3 
Calculations and FEAs are to be completed and certified by a qualified licensed 
structural design engineer. 
1.1.13.4 
FEA results must predict zero structural failures over a 9-year life of production 
cycling. 
1.1.13.5 
Production tooling requires a minimum 5.0 Ultimate Strength Factor of Safety 
(USF) or Design Factor of Safety. 
1.1.13.6 
The tooling design team may adjust application specific USF requirements based 
on risk factors and exposure to the specific tooling. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 24 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.1.14 
Part Locators 
1.1.14.1 
The electronic locator LD-File must be adhered to for all part and assembly 
locating (Clamp & Pin Points, i.e.: Master Control holes, notches, and surfaces). 
An LD is issued for both Component Parts and Assemblies. An Assembly LD will 
be provided for every geometry station identified on the Dimensional 
Management Engineering LD (AFD) Assembly Flow Diagram. Any revision to the 
locating, clamp & pin points requires a WLRR to be written per Operating 
Procedure, 
Locator 
Revision 
Request 
VOPGOG-334 
(MEETS 
Link: 
https://www.wlrr.ford.com/dcdWEB/wlrrHome.do). Users must have a Covisint 
identification password to access this site from outside Ford. Any design 
revisions incorporated prior to an approved WLRR are considered unauthorized 
design changes and will be addressed as such. 
1.1.15 
Virtual Body Shop (VBS) and Digital Pre-Assembly (DPA) 
1.1.15.1 
For adherence to the Virtual Body Shop (VBS) Specification, the following sub 
items are required on all equipment designs. Acceptable methods of execution of 
these functions are defined in the Body Construction Tier 1 Integrator 
Requirements and Specifications for VBS dated 02/06/07. 
1.1.15.2 
Tier 1 Integrators participating in DPA for a program shall document their 
estimated and actual hours using the form "DPA Time Tracking". 
1.1.16 
New Tools for Current Production Using Carry Over (C/O) Product 
1.1.16.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator and Ford Motor Company representative shall assess the 
current production dimensional characteristics versus the design intent (CAD 
data). 
1.1.16.2 
The Ford Motor Co representative could be any of the following: 
• 
The program VLS 
• 
Commodity supervisor 
• 
Plant dimensional control engineer 
• 
Manufacturing engineer 
1.1.16.3 
The desired outcome is to return production product to dimensional design intent 
wherever possible. If this is not possible the product design shall be updated to 
reflect the current production requirements. Carry over Level 3 & Level 4 points 
must be used in this validation in final home line assembly plants.  
1.1.16.4 
Carry over CMM holding fixtures and programs must be utilized during home line 
buyoff and ongoing control plans. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 25 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.1.16.5 
It shall be determined if unique tool design or build requirements are necessary 
due to discrepancies between product design and current production dimensional 
output. 
1.1.16.6 
When there is no program launch timeline with milestones, one shall be 
developed. 
1.1.16.7 
General considerations for quoting and execution: 
1.1.16.7.1 
Dimensional review shall be performed as early as possible. 
1.1.16.7.2 
For quoting purposes, line item estimates should be made for; 
• 
Parts 
• 
Measurement 
• 
Tooling requirements 
• 
Additional build requirements that may be necessary 
1.1.16.7.3 
It may be necessary to provide a process and tooling capable of building product 
to both design intent and current dimensional output. 
a. 
Increased grind spacer dimensions 
b. 
Added clamps and or locators 
1.1.16.7.4 
Conduct a current tooling measurement (SMX, scanning, etc.) to identify locator 
positions and or added locators and compare to the released locator drawings. 
1.1.17 
Shipping Equipment 
1.1.17.1 
The shipping requirements for the fixture must be considered during the design 
stage to avoid or minimize dismantling. The overall width of the fixture must not 
exceed 12 feet (3.7m) skidded. Fixtures exceeding 12 feet x 12 feet x 18 feet 
long shall have Ford Manufacturing Engineer approval prior to release for build. 
When it becomes necessary to dismantle the equipment, physical means must 
be provided to reconstruct the fixture with minimum effort and equal accuracy. 
Line blocks of error-proof design are required for all structures which must be 
disassembled for shipping. Utilize dowels or keys for ease of re-assembly. 
1.1.17.2 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide necessary preparation for shipment and 
loading on Ford designated carriers of all fixtures, transfers, welding machines, 
balconies, etc. This preparation is to include final clean-up, painting and dress-
up. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 26 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.1.17.3 
Any tool, fixture or machine which exceeds legal size and/or weight limitations for 
transportation to the receiving plant shall be dismantled for shipment. In general, 
these limits are 12-foot (3.7m) width, 12-foot (3.7m) height and 60,000lbs 
(27,216kg) with variations permitted depending on the specific receiving state, 
routing and season. It is the Tier 1 Integrator’s responsibility to ensure that 
LEGAL load limits are complied with and attained at their expense. 
1.1.17.4 
Preparation for Shipping 
1.1.17.4.1 
All items which are dismantled for shipment are to be match-marked for ease of 
re-assembly. 
1.1.17.4.2 
All components of the system which are disassembled and crated for shipment 
must have car line and tool numbers on four sides of the crate. Stencil in two-
inch-high letters, white paint. 
1.1.17.4.3 
All electrical plugs and receptacles and power and control wiring which is 
disassembled shall be tagged. 
1.1.17.4.4 
All electrical plugs and receptacles shall be capped to prevent contamination of 
the conductors. 
1.1.17.4.5 
All pneumatic and hydraulic fitting and lines are to be drained, sealed and match-
marked for shipment. 
1.1.17.4.6 
All hydraulic lines and ports shall be capped and plugged to prevent 
environmental contamination from residual fluid leakage. 
1.1.17.4.7 
Hydraulic reservoirs shall be cleaned prior to shipment to the plant. 
1.1.17.4.8 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall apply rust preventative treatment to all exposed steel 
surfaces prior to shipment. 
1.1.17.5 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide all necessary skids, support columns, spacers, 
legs, tie straps, etc. to permit loading and damage-free shipment to the receiving 
plant. 
1.1.17.6 
The tryout hydraulic fluid shall be shipped separately in sealed containers with 
each set of equipment. Flushing fluid or contaminated fluid shall not be shipped. 
Hydraulic fluid shall be filtered, at drain down, prior to shipment. 
1.1.17.7 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall ship two complete sets of all Control Drawings 
(including 1A) for each machine in the master control cabinet. Mechanical 
Designs are available in Team Center. One set may be relocated by plant 
personnel once shipped. 
1.1.17.8 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall ship the PLC logic program as per VOME Control 
Engineering Specifications. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 27 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.1.17.9 
An Acceptance/Inspection Report (Form Tool Acceptance Report-9271) must be 
submitted by Tier 1 Integrator for each tool prior to Ford inspection. The plant 
copy of the inspection report shall be inserted into the packing slip and forwarded 
with each tool to the assembly plants. 
1.1.17.10 
For Nex-Gen: The Tier 1 Integrator shall ship drawings and engineering 
information for commercial items in the Next-Gen control power panel (CPD).  
For GVOSS: The Tier 1 Integrator shall ship drawings and engineering 
information for commercial items in the GVOSS drawing box normally mounted 
on the cell fence near main control power panel (PDP). 
1.1.17.11 
All shipping shall be in accordance with the Ford Full-Service Shipping 
Requirements. 
1.1.17.12 
Equipment shipped to Ford Motor Company Facilities shall conform to Ford 
Motor Company Manufacturing Standards GMA02-729E-KA1; “Weight Marking 
of Manufacturing Equipment” and GMA02-729E-KA2; “Manufacturing Equipment 
Handling and Packaging”. See also: GMA02-729 Parent Document and VO w-
KA1 and w-KA2 Compliance Guidelines. 
1.1.17.13 
ISPM No. 15 Compliance 
1.1.17.13.1 All equipment shipped over international borders shall comply with the 
requirements of the latest release of ISPM No. 15. 
1.1.17.13.2 It is the Tier 1 Integrators' responsibility to obtain the latest release of ISPM No. 
15; ISPM No. 15 – 2009 (Reference Only). 
1.1.18 
Equipment Shipping, Receiving and Marshalling 
1.1.18.1 
Shipping Equipment from Tier 1 Integrator 
1.1.18.1.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator and installation contractor shall jointly determine the timing 
and shipping order of equipment shipped to the plant. 
1.1.18.1.2 
The installation contractor shall have a representative at the Tier 1 Integrator's 
location to observe the equipment teardown and loading. The Tier 1 Integrator 
shall have a representative available to answer any questions. 
1.1.18.1.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall accurately identify shipment contents on the Bill of 
Lading for each shipment. 
1.1.18.2 
Material Received by the Installation Contractor 
1.1.18.2.1 
The installation contractor is responsible for confirming that the contents of each 
Bill of Lading are received with each shipment. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 28 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.1.18.2.2 
The installation contractor is responsible for lost materials once the shipper for a 
shipment is signed as received by the installation contractor. 
1.1.18.2.3 
The installation contractor is responsible for assuring materials received are 
stored in the proper marshalling area. 
1.1.18.3 
Material Received by the Receiving Plant 
1.1.18.3.1 
The receiving plant is responsible for confirming that the contents of each Bill of 
Lading are received with each shipment. 
1.1.18.3.2 
The receiving plant is responsible for lost materials once the shipper for a 
shipment is signed as received by the receiving plant. 
1.1.18.3.3 
The receiving plant is responsible for assuring materials received are stored in 
the proper marshalling area. 
1.1.18.4 
Plant Marshalling Area 
1.1.18.4.1 
The receiving plant is responsible for identifying the equipment marshalling areas 
at the receiving plant. 
1.1.19 
Plant Labor Clearance 
FNA only; Tier 1 Integrators shall follow the requirements of the Ford Motor 
Company SPL (Single Point Lesson) titled "Outside Contracting", including 
Attachment 1 and its checklist when requesting plant labor clearance. 
1.1.20 
Construction Safety 
1.1.20.1 
Tier 1 Integrator must comply with Safety Guidelines for Contractors as stated in 
the following Ford Land Facilities Construction Specifications, 
• 
01100 - Construction Safety Guideline 
• 
01105 - Site Safety Contractor Responsibilities 
• 
01110 - Dedicated vs. Dual Role Safety Engineer 
1.1.20.2 
Slings 
1.1.20.2.1 
Tier 1 Integrators shall provide the Single Point Lesson – Protection for Synthetic 
Slings - July 17, 2015 to their installation contractors. This shall be distributed by 
the installation contractor to their affected employees working on the installation 
at the Ford Motor Company facility. The installation contractor shall confirm back 
to the Tier 1 Integrator that they have delivered this message to their employees. 
1.1.21 
Launch Support 
1.1.21.1 
This section addresses the process to be followed to track manpower (field 
service) while on Launch. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 29 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.1.21.2 
The below process must be followed when tracking manpower on Launch in 
order to be paid for Field service.  
The form Weekly Field Service Sheet shall be used.  
The Tier 1 supplier will use weekly sign-off of install in place of daily sign-off 
requirement following GVOSS 1.1.21.3 
Step 1: Each day the personnel from the Tier 1 Integrator base working at the 
Ford Manufacturing Plant will be required to have their daily Weekly field 
service sheet filled for the days they have worked that week.  
Step 2: This sheet must have an accurate description documenting what was 
accomplished for the day. Each individual will review the sheet with the 
respective manufacturing engineer and determine "what" if any is warranty. 
The sheet will then be marked up accordingly and signed by the 
manufacturing engineer at the end of the week. If the manufacturing engineer 
is not available, another Ford launch person can be used to review and sign 
the sheet. The sheet must be signed Weekly. No exceptions. If the sheet is 
not signed by the Manufacturing Engineer or Launch Leader, then Ford will 
not be charged any hours for the week. If any problems arise, the vendor 
must call the Ford Launch Manager who will resolve the concern.  
1.1.21.3 
The Vendor program manager will use the Weekly Field Service Sheet 
information as input to the Master Field Service Time Summary Sheet. These 
reports will be reviewed with the Launch Leader along with the Field Service 
Tracking Summary on a weekly basis to monitor performance to labor hours 
quoted for the program. 
1.1.22 
VIM Matrix 
1.1.22.1 
This section addresses the process to be followed to track launch issues while on 
launch. 
1.1.22.2 
The form Global VIM Matrix shall be used to track all VOME Facility and Tooling 
launch issues that impact safety, throughput, and quality. 
1.1.22.3 
The VIM Matrix shall be reviewed as part of the Launch Team Exit Criteria. 
1.1.22.4 
The plant shall continue to use the VIM Matrix after launch to track any ongoing 
issues requiring VOME support.

1.1.23.1 
Global PROTAG Procedure w-GPP001: Signage to be used as an official status 
report and visual aid indicating the installation status of facilities, machinery, and 
tools. PROTAG signs are posted on individual machine assemblies such as tool 
cells, conveyor sections, control panels, etc. An equivalent process may be used 
with concurrence from Corporate Safety

1.1.23.1.1 
Equipment not typically included in the PROTAG Process (i.e. Single source of 
energy equipment, Chain or cable hoists, Torque tube applications, vin stamping 
or scribing equipment, etc.) shall follow the Safety Buyoff Procedure. 
1.1.23.1.2 
Upon completion of the Safety Buyoff Procedure, the team will complete the 
Safety Buyoff Signoff Evidence Document.   
1.1.23.2 
The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible for providing the 1A Drawing Package for 
equipment installation. (Ref. Supplier Responsibilities 1A Drawings). The drawing 
package shall include: 
Assembly Plant System Layout (Plan View) 
• Robot Layout 
• Pit and Floor Plate Layout 
• Drop Layout 
− 
Electrical Drop Layout 
− 
Air Drop Layout 
− 
Water Drop Layout 
• Fence Layout 
• Cable Tray Layout 
• Assembly Plant Elevations and Sectional Views 
• Facilities Equipment Interface Drawings 
1.1.23.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible for the equipment placement, alignment, 
leveling and anchoring at the assembly plant installation in a manner appropriate 
for the equipment application. 
1.1.23.4 
The Tier 1 shall provide all special robot risers, RTU mounting structures, 
wireways, cable trays, or conduit for electrical, pneumatic and water services, 
plus mounting provisions to facilitate a complete robot installation. Power cables, 
wires, hoses, etc. shall be laid in cable trays with covers. The cable trays shall be 
mounted on top of uni-strut or equivalent to keep the tray above the floor. 
1.1.23.5 
All Installation, Commissioning and Proving of Robots shall be the responsibility 
of the Installation Contractor. The Installation Contractor’s responsibility will 
include the unpacking of the robots, preparation, civil works through the Plant, 
installing to the floor, etc. Robots shall be installed using GVOSS approved 
methods and equipment. Base plates and robot-stands shall be designed to 
comply with GVOSS standards. 
1.1.23.6 
During the course of the tooling build in the plant the Tier 1 Integrator and VOME 
signoff initials are required to complete the Controls Logix Build Checklist.

1.1.24 
Floor Anchors 
1.1.24.1 
Installation of floor anchors shall conform with OSHA 1926.1153 and FAS08-223. 
1.1.24.2 
Floor anchor selection shall be predicated on the core samples of the plant floor 
and equipment dynamic behavior. 
1.1.24.2.1 
TEVC tooling anchoring into the concrete slab/foundation shall be designed to 
resist the seismic loading per the “ASCE7-16 Chapter 13” and the IBC 2018.  
The foundation and anchors shall be designed based on the equipment vertical, 
horizontal loading and the moment at the base utilizing the E-stop loads. 
1.1.24.3 
Facilities Implementation is responsible for providing the core samples and 
results, unless specified otherwise in the Statement of Work. Core samples are 
required to determine the thickness of slab and compressive strength. 
1.1.24.4 
When core samples are not taken the floor concrete design information for depth 
and compressive strength shall be used. This shall satisfy the Core Sample 
requirement. 
1.1.24.5 
A copy of the floor core sample results shall be provided to the plant. 
1.1.24.6 
The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible to obtain the core sample results or design 
information from Facilities Implementation. Floor anchor selection shall be 
predicated on the specified floor/foundation criteria defined in Global Facilities 
Forum (GFF). 
1.1.24.7 
All floor anchors shall be manufactured by Hilti and installed per Hilti installation 
specifications by Hilti Installation Trained personnel. 
1.1.24.7.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall arrange an onsite anchor installation training to be 
provided at no cost by a Hilti Field Service Engineer or Hilti Qualified 
Representative. For all of the specified products, proof of the training shall be 
maintained and available upon request. 
1.1.24.8 
All Post-Installed Floor Anchors shall consist of the following anchor types by 
Hilti. 
Adhesive Anchors for cracked and uncracked concrete shall be: 
Hilti HIT-HY 200 (A or R) Safe Set with the HIT-Z or HIT-ZR rod per ICC ESR-
3187. 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 32 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
For deeper embedded depths or longer projection lengths: 
Hilti HIT-HY 200 (A or R) Safe Set System with Hilti hollow drill bit and VC20/40 
vacuum system with HAS (E or B) threaded rod per ICC ESR-3187. 
*HY200 is a fast cure adhesive. 
 
 
            
 
                         
TOX #’s 
HIT HY 200-A 
HIT HY 200-R 
Component A: 190618 
Component A: 190597 
Component B: 190619 
Component B: 190598 
 
 
SPECIAL NOTE on Oversized Holes (holes with a larger annular gap):  
1. Hilti HIT-RE500V3 Safe Set System with Hilti hollow drill bit and vc 20/40 
system with HAS-E-55 or HAS-B-105 threaded rod per ICC ESR-3814. 
 
 
 
2. Hilti HIT-RE500V3 Safe Set System with Hilti hollow roughening tool with 
HAS-E-55 or HAS-B-105 threaded rod per ICC ESR-3814 for diamond 
cored holes. 
 
 
 
*RE500V3 is a slow cure adhesive. 
 
TOX #’s 199021 
HIT RE-500V3 
Component A: 190597 
Component B: 190598

1.1.24.9 
Tier 1 Integrators shall define anchor requirements and types utilizing plant floor 
core results and submit for review and approval of applicability by a Hilti Field 
Engineer. The minimum required information that shall be provided to the Hilti 
Field Engineer for review shall include the plant core sample, anchor diameter, 
embedment depth, the type of anchor recommended, chemical type (HY200 or 
RE500V3), total embedment depth, drill type, dynamic and static forces, etc. 
 
  
 
 
  
   
                                                         
 
Note: Contact Hilti Automotive Desk at 800-950-6165 for Field Engineer Contact 
Information. 
1.1.24.10 
The Hilti Field Engineer shall provide an engineering Approval of Applicability 
sign-off document on the anchor requirements defined per application by the Tier 
1. 
1.1.24.11 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall clearly identify anchor requirements on the appropriate 
equipment installation drawings. All installation drawings shall include the anchor 
catalog number and description (size, embedment depth, type). 
1.1.24.12 
All robot base plates, angle brackets for base plates, RTUs (Robotic Transfer 
Units), risers, welding electrode tip dresser stands, and tip changer stands that 
are floor mounted shall be anchored to the floor using chemical anchors. 
Additional chemical anchor applications shall be determined as per GVOSS 
section 1.1.24.9 above.

below for chemical anchors. The size of the note should be approximately 2 
inches x 3 inches and be located above the sheet title block.

1.1.26 
 
Post Installation Activities 
1.1.26.1 
House Keeping (Post Installation) 
1.1.26.1.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall visually check and clean all areas worked. Any loose 
debris (weld rods, wire ties, bolts, trash, etc.) shall be removed. 
1.1.26.1.2 
All painted areas of the equipment shall be touched-up to cover field welding, 
marring and scratches incurred during installation, rigging and placement. 
1.1.26.1.3 
All machined surfaces shall be clean of debris and footprints. 
1.1.26.1.4 
All floor-mounted cable tray covers shall be cleaned of marks and footprints. 
1.1.26.1.5 
All equipment shall be cleaned of dust that may be present as a result of 
construction activities in the plant. 
1.1.26.2 
Unused Equipment and Component Removal 
1.1.26.2.1 
Vehicle Operations General Office requires that the following guidelines be 
conformed to after balance-out of the previous model. 
• Tier 1 to include in program quote as a separate line item cost. 
• Tier 1 shall update all documentation. 
 
1.1.26.2.2 
Unless otherwise specified in the SOW, all tooling units that are not used for the 
current production model and all associated controls components shall be 
removed from the equipment. Tier 1 Integrator to provide cost in the program 
quote as a separate line item. 
1.1.26.2.3 
All multiple model units used for integrated build shall be locked in a static 
position to support the current production model only and all associated controls 
components shall be removed from the equipment. 
1.1.26.2.4 
All control panel items used for integrated build that do not support current model 
production including PLC logic shall be removed. 
1.1.26.2.5 
Unit replacement shall only be pursued as a last resort when reliability, 
maintainability or equipment performance is an issue. 
1.1.26.2.6 
Tier 1 Integrator shall update all documentation. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 36 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS

1.1.27 
Warranty 
1.1.27.1 
All tooling, conveyors, equipment and components, including those associated 
with new model kits, shall be warranted for a period of three years beginning at 
MP1. Issues opened within the 3-year post MP1 warranty period that are not 
closed with an effective & implemented PCA, will remain under warranty until the 
PCA is implemented. 
1.1.27.2 
From MP1 onward: 
1.1.27.2.1 
The plant is fully responsible for the equipment. 
1.1.27.2.2 
Warranty Claims, for designed parts and purchased parts, will be handled by the 
plant. 
1.1.27.3 
Components 
a. Consumables can be a warranty issue if: 
• They are mis-applied by the integrator contrary to the Manufacturers 
Specification. (OEM’s and Tier 1 Integrators have a responsibility to know 
the environment where their equipment will be used. If the wrong 
component is applied in the wrong environment although it is a 
consumable and can be an issue. 
• The consumables are failing because of other specifications set by the 
installing or integrating OEM (the warranty claim should be directed to the 
installer or integrator). 
• Process is incorrect (how the components are used in the manufacturing 
systems can cause abuse). 
b. Premature failure is an indicator of a problem (weakness in the component, 
misapplied, wrong environment, or poor process vs. component capability) 
c. The assumption that all specifications are correct when a consumable or 
component fails is incorrect. 
d. Warranty claims are not always the responsibility of the component 
manufacturer. Integrators, installers or the end user may have the warranty or 
cost responsibility for a failure of a manufacturers or Tier 1 Integrators 
component. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 37 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.1.27.4 
Performing Warranty Work at the Plants 
1.1.27.4.1 
Labor and materials required to implement a given warranty campaign must be 
covered by the responsible integrator/sub-tier supplier. 
1.1.27.4.2 
FNA ONLY: Regarding labor clearance, the given integrator and plant need to 
meet to negotiate what the work and validation content (hours) is per direct and 
indirect classification for these actions prior to doing the actual work. If an 
agreement cannot be reached, then the appropriate VOME and plant Labor 
Relations personnel shall step in as an intermediary to assist in reaching a 
compromise. 
1.1.27.4.3 
FNA ONLY: Once an agreement is reached as to the work content, an 
agreement must be made as to who is going to perform the actual work while the 
responsible integrator/sub-tier supplier supervises to ensure the work is done 
properly as to not void what is left on the given warranty. 
1.1.27.4.4 
FNA ONLY: If, under the existing local labor agreement the following 3 
conditions are met: 
a. The warranty work is considered a "core" responsibility, and 
b. The given plant has skilled tradesmen capable of doing the given work, and 
c. The plant has skilled tradesmen that are available ("not fully" utilized) to do 
the work according to the integrator/sub-tier supplier's specifications and 
under their supervision, then, as long as the affected program's project has 
not been closed, the integrator shall take the lead in providing the appropriate 
VOME launch personnel with an equivalent program credit so that VOME can 
submit a Plant Authorization Request (PAR) to the given plant to cover the 
given work order. If the affected program's project has been closed, the 
responsible integrator shall reimburse the plant directly by writing a check to 
Ford Motor Company and delivering it to the affected plant(s) Financial 
Controller so that they can ensure that the funds are deposited in the 
appropriate account and control the related spending. 
1.1.27.4.5 
FNA ONLY: If the given plant does not want to do the work or they are not 
capable of doing certain aspects of the given work: 
a. The integrator/sub-tier supplier must negotiate to do the work with their 
personnel or utilize a mutually agreed upon unionized source. 
NOTE: In 1999 an agreement to assign an affected skilled tradesperson with the 
integrator/sub-tier supplier was made, however it is not on a one-to-one ratio. 
The company recognizes the advantage of assigning a tradesperson to 
accompany a supplier for training purposes, and will continue to do so where 
practical, within the current agreement and the given plant's training budget. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 38 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.1.28 
EQUIPMENT OBSOLESCENCE 
1.1.28.1 
Ford requires that the Tier 2 Equipment Suppliers’ components remain in 
production for at least 14 years. For any components in the supplier’s 
supply chain that become obsolete or no longer available, it is the 
responsibility of the supplier to: 
1. 
Formally notify Ford Motor Company within 5 years, 3 years and 1 
year prior to component obsolescence 
2. 
Provide an alternative component to replace the obsolete 
component having the same functionality and cost for Ford approval. 
3. 
If an alternative component cannot be provided prior to the time of 
component obsolescence, then the supplier shall provide Ford a solution 
that ensures enough spare components through the 14-year milestone. 
1.2 
PROGRAM / PROJECT REQUIREMENTS 
1.2.1 
Vehicle Operations Engineering 
1.2.1.1 
Project Management  
1.2.1.2 
Project Planning 
The following points pertain to all project planning:  
• All program planning is to use project planning software to monitor and report 
project performance to schedule. 
• All kit jobs and installation plans are to use project planning software.  
• Tasks must be scheduled in units of days. 
• All tasks MUST be auto scheduled. 
• Task descriptions and predecessor relationships are examples and will need 
to be adjusted for each project during discussions with the Ford 
representatives. 
• For Tooling and Conveyor installation - schedule all days as working days. 
• No blank lines/tasks. 
• No page breaks. 
• Do not include a contact list in the schedule. 
• Do not include GPDS program milestones. 
• The project status date must be updated every time the schedule is submitted 
to a Ford representative. 
• Native files in .MPP format must be sent (no .PDF files). 
• No pictures or charts embedded in the MS PROJECT FILE. 
• Language for all tasks must be in English.

Furnish - Home (sharepoint.com).

constructed to identify actions that will be taken to restore the project timing. The 
Timing Recovery Plan shall be reviewed at the Project Review. 
1.2.2.7.2 
If the Timing Recovery Plan requires intervention or action to be taken by Ford 
Motor Company prior to the Project Review, the Launch Leader shall be informed 
immediately of the situation and actions required.

1.2.3 
Maximo for Maintenance (MFM) Requirements for New Tooling and 
Conveyors 
1.2.3.1 
General Information 
MFM is a preventative maintenance information system, which is an enabler of 
the Global Maintenance Operating System (GMOS).  
1.2.3.1.1 
The MFM preventive maintenance information will be transmitted between the 
Tier 2 machine component suppliers through the Tier 1 Tooling or Conveyor 
Suppliers, Vehicle Operations Manufacturing Engineering to the Plant 
Maintenance Planner. 
1.2.3.1.2 
The plant will develop and install EQUIPMENT ID NUMBERS labels for new 
tooling. 
1.2.3.2 
MFM Data Format 
1.2.3.2.1 
The MFM data format shall be in the form of an approved Proforma. The tooling 
and conveyor supplier are responsible for obtaining the Proforma from an 
approved Proforma supplier. 
1.2.3.3 
MFM Data Upload 
1.2.3.3.1 
The plant will review the Proforma after receiving it and add plant specific 
information to the Proforma. 
1.2.3.3.2 
The plant Maintenance Planner is responsible for uploading the Proforma into the 
plant's data base. 
1.2.3.4 
PM Maintenance Elements and PM Library 
1.2.3.4.1 
The Vehicle Operations MFM Specialist is responsible for maintaining the list of 
standard maintenance elements.  
1.2.3.4.2 
The standard maintenance elements are listed in the Preventative Maintenance 
Master List and their associated tasks are within the Preventative Maintenance 
Library. These documents can be found on the Facility Exchange SharePoint 
site: 
https://azureford.sharepoint.com/sites/FacilityExchangeExternal/IndependentSyst
emsServices/Forms/AllItems.aspx 
1.2.3.4.3 
The standard maintenance elements are to be utilized on all new programs.  
1.2.3.4.4 
The PM Library shall be maintained for accuracy, time to complete PM task, lock 
out/non-lockout, frequency, visual, predictive and if a monitored fault can replace 
a PM. Contact the VO MFM Specialist for the latest list. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 43 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.2.3.5 
Defining PM Maintenance Elements 
1.2.3.5.1 
The first step in defining PM's is the MFMEA. Every PM should be associated to 
a control or a prevention of an identified failure mode. The PM should be entered 
in the Current Machinery Controls Prevention or Recommended Action column of 
the MFMEA.  
1.2.3.5.1.1 
Use the VOME M-TASK Process Flow Chart to develop M-TASKS and Sub 
Tasks using the MFMEA. 
1.2.3.5.1.2 
The M-TASK Process Roles and Responsibilities shall be used in the M-TASK 
development. Not applicable for Final Assembly. 
1.2.3.5.2 
R&M data should be used to identify PM elements. 
1.2.3.5.3 
PM Elements should be restricted to common-cause failure modes. 
1.2.3.5.4 
PM Elements to be avoided: 
a. Inspection of equipment for loose fasteners is waste of resources. Suppliers 
are expected to know how to properly design a joint, specify, install and 
tighten a fastener so that it does not loosen. 
b. Oil changes are usually specified as annually. This is the speed reducer 
manufacturers' recommendation because they do not know how their 
component is going to be used. It is purely over protection. Oil changes 
should be condition based. The best method is to establish an oil analysis 
program and monitor oil condition for trending changes and degradation. 
c. Inspections that require disassembly of a component are high risk. Not only 
are these types of inspections time consuming but there is the risk that 
something could go wrong during reassembly resulting in damage that did 
not pre-exist. 
d. Requirements to check panels for tight wire connections are not realistic. 
Between the main enclosures, junction boxes, terminal boxes and remote 
panels there are probably over 1,000,000 wire connections. Checking each 
connection individually is not practical. 
e. Any type of "General" inspection is also non-value added. This is basically 
telling you to look at the machine and see if something is wrong. This is not 
only vague but is an alert that there is a lack of understanding of the machine 
failure modes. This basically alludes that there is some unknown force acting 
randomly on the equipment to cause some sort of problem somewhere on 
the machine. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 44 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.2.3.6 
Classifying PM Elements 
When identifying PM’s, it is necessary to understand equipment failure modes 
and their root causes. Many can be categorized as follows: 
• 
Adjustment. For example: 
– 
Chains are subject to wear elongation. Chain take-ups need to be 
periodically inspected and adjusted.  
• 
Lubrication. Examples include: 
– 
Bearings require lubrication at specified frequencies. 
– 
Chain requires lubrication to reduce wear. 
– 
Chain sprockets require lubrication to reduce wear. Sprocket wear 
results in reducing sprocket pitch diameter which can cause chain 
binding or failure. 
– 
Robot Cyclo units require lubrication on a regular basis. 
• 
Wear.  Examples include: 
– 
Un-lubricated synthetic bearings and bushings will exhibit wear. 
– 
Permanently lubricated bearings will wear-out in a shorter time than 
lubricated bearings. 
– 
Belts should be inspected for signs of cracking and wear on a regular 
basis. 
– 
Hoses and wire insulation on moving equipment are subject to rubbing 
and fraying resulting in failure. These should be inspected at regular 
intervals. 
• 
Misalignment.  Examples include: 
– 
Driving-to-driven shaft misalignment will result in coupling damage. 
– 
Misalignment causes increased overhung load in speed reducers 
resulting in output shaft seal and bearing failure. 
– 
Misaligned chain sprockets will result in sprocket wear. 
– 
Pulley misalignment will result in belt chafing. 
– 
Rolling element bearings will fail due to overloading caused by 
misalignment. 
• 
Contamination.  Examples include: 
– 
In air counterbalance systems the cylinder rod end is "open-to-
atmosphere" with a breather to prevent airborne debris from entering the 
cylinder. Over time the breather will become blocked with debris. The 
breather needs to be periodically replaced. 
– 
In welding areas with high levels of expulsion, material will build up and 
create a mechanical interference. Periodic inspections and cleaning 
should be performed to keep equipment motion envelopes clear. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 45 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
– 
Robot control panels require air circulation for cooling. The filter 
elements require inspection and changing at regular intervals. 
– 
Speed reducer oil should be sampled and analyzed for condition and 
contamination on a regular basis. 
• 
Overloading. Examples include: 
– 
Speed reducer oil should be sampled and analyzed for gear and bearing 
wear particles caused by overloading on a regular basis. 
– 
Motors will operate at higher temperatures when overloaded. 
1.2.3.7 
Performing PM Elements 
After PM's that prevent failure have been identified the next step is to determine 
how to perform them. PM should fall into the following categories: 
• 
Action (See 1.2.3.7.1) 
• 
Visual Inspection (See 1.2.3.7.2) 
• 
Instrumentation Inspection (See 1.2.3.7.3) 
1.2.3.7.1 
Action: This type of PM just directs some task to be performed on a regular 
basis. For example: 
• 
Change a filter 
• 
Drain a reclassifying filter 
• 
Grease a bearing 
1.2.3.7.2 
Visual Inspection: A visual inspection would be something that can be observed 
with the naked eye to determine if further action or a CWO should be taken. For 
example: 
• 
Frayed belt 
• 
Frayed hoses 
• 
Broken leafs on shunts 
1.2.3.7.3 
Instrumentation Inspection: This is the category that the majority of inspections 
should fall into. This type of inspection is commonly referred to as Predictive. 
Many failure modes can be detected as they develop and before they become 
catastrophic. Using predictive technologies, it can be determined if a problem is 
developing before it becomes visible to the naked eye or is audible to the human 
ear.  
Examples of candidates for this type of inspection are: 
• 
Rolling elements in bearings 
• 
Motor windings 
• 
Gears 
• 
Couplings 
• 
Pump Impellers 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 46 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
• 
Busses 
• 
Electrical Connections 
• 
Cooling Systems (Weld gun) 
1.2.3.8 
Evaluating PM Maintenance Elements  
When evaluating PM's, there are three major steps: 
1. First, establish task specifics such as: 
– 
Specific action verses a general activity. 
– 
Attribute being inspected for is well defined. Is it well defined and 
measurable (quantitative) or subjective (qualitative)? 
– 
Quantify signal characteristics for the onset of the failure. 
2. Second, relate the failure mode to the PM. For instance: 
– 
What is the failure mode, and will the PM task prevent it or identify it in 
sufficient time to correct before it results in unscheduled production 
down-time? 
3. Third, identify the method to perform the PM: 
– 
When is the maintenance action to be performed? 
– 
When is a developing failure of a component observed visually? 
– 
When is instrumentation the only method to detect component 
degradation prior to incurring unscheduled downtime? 
1.2.3.9 
Maintenance Element Review and Optimization 
1.2.3.9.1 
The review and optimization process is to validate PM elements for correctness 
and value added.  
1.2.3.9.2 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall have all applicable MFMEA's available upon request 
for the review. 
1.2.3.9.3 
The Vehicle Operations MFM Specialist will determine who is required to 
participate in the reviews. This may include but is not limited to: Tier 1 
Integrators, Tier 2 suppliers, component manufacturers, plant personnel, launch 
team members and other division employees. 
1.2.3.9.4 
Thirteen months prior to Job 1 (MP1), the tooling and conveyor suppliers shall 
submit to the Vehicle Operations MFM Specialist their equipment manuals and 
list of standard maintenance elements used in their systems. 
1.2.3.9.5 
Standard maintenance elements shall be optimized nine months prior to Job 1 
(MP1). The Vehicle Operations MFM Specialist will determine which standard 
maintenance elements will be optimized for each new program.  This 
determination will take into account timing since the last optimization took place, 
along with the frequency of equipment usage.  Not every standard maintenance 
element will be optimized for every program, depending on the benefit gained.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 47 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.2.3.9.6 
Thirteen months prior to Job 1 (MP1), the tooling and conveyor suppliers shall 
submit to the Vehicle Operations MFM Specialist their list of new standard 
maintenance elements used in their systems.  
1.2.3.9.7 
New maintenance elements shall be optimized nine months prior to Job 1 (MP1). 
1.2.3.9.8 
Tooling and conveyor suppliers shall submit to Vehicle Operations MFM 
Specialist any additional tasks required for unique equipment being integrated 
with their system. 
1.2.3.9.9 
The Tier 1 tooling and conveyor suppliers are required to put new preventative 
maintenance tasks in the PM Library format. The Tier 1 tooling and conveyor 
suppliers are responsible for obtaining signoff from all Tier 2 component 
suppliers on the maintenance tasks documented within the standard 
maintenance elements and/or any unique equipment being supplied for the 
program. Furthermore, the Tier 1 tooling and conveyor suppliers are responsible 
for submitting the new PM Library maintenance tasks for review and approval to 
the Vehicle Operations MFM Specialist for adding to the PM Library. 
1.2.3.9.10 
For Final Assembly the following shall apply. 
1.2.3.9.10.1 The review and optimization process is to validate PM elements for correctness 
and value added.  
1.2.3.9.10.2 The Tier 1 Integrator shall have all applicable MFMEA's available upon request 
for the review. 
1.2.3.9.10.3 The tooling and conveyor suppliers shall submit and review their list of standard 
maintenance elements used in their systems to the manufacturing engineer and 
the MFM Specialist. 
• 
At Design Buyoff the supplier shall submit an MFMEA and associated M-
Task to the VOME MFM Specialist.  
• 
The MFMEA and M-task should continue to evolve throughout the 
development of the tool.  Any additional tasks must be discussed with the 
manufacturing engineer and MFM Specialist prior to final buyoff. 
• 
A finalized MFMEA and M-Task must be included in the Buyoff Book at 
buyoff. 
1.2.3.9.10.4 The tooling and conveyor suppliers shall submit and review their list of standard 
maintenance elements used in their systems to the manufacturing engineer and 
the MFM Specialist. At Design Buyoff the supplier shall submit an MFMEA and 
associated M-Task to the VOME MFM Specialist. The MFMEA and M-task should 
continue to evolve throughout the development of the tool.  Any additional tasks 
must be discussed with the manufacturing engineer and MFM Specialist prior to 
final buyoff.

1.2.3.12 
Tool Profile Sheets 
1.2.3.12.1 
At ten months before Job 1 (MP1), the tooling and conveyor suppliers shall 
submit to the Vehicle Operations MFM Specialist the Tool Profile Sheets. 
• 
Use the form Tool Profile Template – Assembly for Assembly Plants. 
• 
Use the form Tool Profile Template – SBU for Stamping Plants. 
• 
These documents can be found on the Facility Exchange SharePoint site. 
1.2.3.12.2 
Tool profile sheets list the equipment by tool and station number. The Tool Profile 
Sheets will be filled out as directed in the Instructions tab of the Tool Profile 
Sheet document. All new assets are required to be documented in the Tool 
Profile Sheet. 
https://azureford.sharepoint.com/sites/FacilityExchangeExternal/IndependentSyst
emsServices/Forms/AllItems.aspx 
1.2.3.12.3 
In the event that the tooling document is a kit, the Tier 1 Integrator is required to 
fill out the Changes tab of the Tool Profile Sheet and document which changes 
are required in the Remarks and/or Notes column for the existing tools. The 
purpose of filling out this form is for the Plant Maintenance Planner to make the 
necessary changes in PCON on existing equipment. An example would be an 
existing robot that is moved from one area to another. Another example would be 
a deletion of a station. 
1.2.3.12.4 
The Vehicle Operations MFM Specialist will review the Tooling Profile Sheet and 
return it to the tooling and conveyor suppliers for corrections and approval.   
1.2.3.12.5 
The tooling and conveyor suppliers will forward the approved Tooling Profile 
Sheet to an approved Proforma supplier eight months prior to Job 1 (MP1).

1.2.3.14.1

1.2.3.14.2

the Plant Maintenance Planner and the Vehicle Operations MFM Specialist 5 1/2 
months prior to Job 1 (MP1).

The Vehicle Operations MFM Specialist shall review the Proforma with the Plant 
Maintenance Planner or their designated representative and together make any 
necessary corrections and assist in loading into the PCON and MFM system.
