1.2.4 
Process Throughput Simulations 
1.2.4.1 
A throughput simulation shall be completed for each tooling and equipment 
assembly system unless otherwise specified by the Ford Motor Company Launch 
Leader for the given product program. 
1.2.4.2 
SIMUL8® and WITNESS are the preferred simulation software applications. 
1.2.4.3 
The requirements of the simulation shall be consistent with the requirements of 
GVOSS Sections 1.2.6, General Buy-Off Procedures; and contained in Tab 22 of 
the Buy-Off Book. 
1.2.4.4 
The simulation shall be completed by the tooling and equipment assembly 
system integrator(s) and reviewed with the VOME Program Manager, Launch 
Leader and Plant Launch Coordinator by the 50% Design Complete milestone. 
1.2.4.5 
Process and/or layout changes that may affect the throughput of a given tooling 
and equipment assembly system need to be incorporated into the simulation 
model and rerun to validate that the desired throughput requirements will still be 
met with the given changes.   
1.2.4.6 
The final version of the simulation model must represent the 100% Design Level 
and "As Built" condition of the tooling and equipment assembly system and be 
validated to ensure that it reflects the actual productivity being experienced by 
the given plant. 
1.2.4.7 
The final version of the vendor simulation model and input files shall be delivered 
to the Ford Motor Company Discrete Event Simulation (DES) group. 
1.2.5 
Tool Certification 
1.2.5.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall compile certification information for tools supplied to 
Ford Motor Company into a "System Certification Book". One copy of the System 
Certification Book, with one copy of the book on electronic media, with data in a 
format readable by the receiving assembly plant enclosed within the book, shall 
be delivered to the receiving assembly plant at time of equipment buy-off. An 
additional copy of the book shall be supplied to the Ford Motor Company 
Manufacturing Engineer upon request. 
1.2.5.1.1 
The measurement system and equipment used for the certification shall be 
identified and included in the System Certification Book. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 51 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.2.5.1.2 
Equipment used in the tool certification process shall meet the requirements of 
QS-9000 for Control of Inspection, Measuring and Test Equipment. 
1.2.5.1.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator, upon request, shall provide an up-to-date ISO Calibration 
Certificate, for the equipment used for the tool certification process. 
1.2.5.2 
Base Certification. the Tier 1 Integrator shall make provisions for a reproducible 
coordinate system using the equipment base to provide a fixed reference system. 
This shall be certified and used in the initial certification process and if 
necessary, for re-verification after equipment installation at the assembly plant. 
Instructions and documentation related to reproducing the certification coordinate 
system shall be part of the System Certification Book documentation. 
1.2.5.2.1 
Base Certification: The certification of the base shall be performed to determine 
the flatness of the surface and location of the datum target holes. 
1.2.5.2.2 
Design and Construction of Bases:   
a. Bases shall be designed and built with uncovered areas on the base surface 
large enough to allow the placement of a scale standard adjacent to each 
leveling screw.  This will allow a repeatable leveling scheme for certification and 
installation at the assembly plant. 
b. To provide a fixed reference system, each base shall have a 0.250-inch 
diameter datum target hole in each corner of the base approximately 50 mm from 
each edge. The points will be numbered R1 through R4 starting with the point 
closest to the base datum edges and continuing in a clockwise manner as shown 
in Figure 1.2.4.a. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 52 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
 
Figure 1.2.4.a. Datum Target Hole Location 
1.2.5.2.3 
Base Certification Tolerances:  Base flatness, level and target datum hole 
location shall meet the following requirements: 
• 
Base flat to within 0.15 mm over every leveling screw. 
• 
Base level to gravity within ±0.15 mm. 
• 
Use base target datum holes to establish coordinate system alignment. 
Target datum holes shall fit to within ±0.07 mm to nominal dimensions 
provided. 
1.2.5.3 
Pallet Certification: Pallets shall be certified off of a single declared master 
station or holding fixture. The best fit plane used for certification shall be within 
0.07mm for all points measured. 
1.2.5.4 
Tooling Plates: All machined surfaces shall be flat, parallel and perpendicular 
within 0.03 mm per foot Total Indicator Reading (T.I.R.). Additional requirements 
are: 
• 
Surfaces must be flat within 0.13 mm T.I.R. over the entire surface. 
• 
Supplier to provide verification documentation. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 53 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.2.5.4.1 
Certification of tooling plates or fixtures mounted to a single piece of rotating or 
indexing equipment shall be from a single reference frame. The remaining tooling 
frames must be certified to the original reference frame and all benchmarks must 
be revalued and fixture must be within dimensional spec with respect to the 
original tooling plate. 
1.2.5.5 
Trunnions and Frames: All machined surfaces must be flat, parallel, and 
perpendicular within 0.13 mm Total Indicator Reading (T.I.R.). Suppliers shall 
provide verification documentation. 
1.2.5.5.1 
Dual Face Tooling Set Up and Certification must have at least 3 samples per 
face after initial certification on all rotating equipment. 
1.2.5.5.2 
All dual face tooling must be used, tuned in and verified during all build events; 
VTTO (buyoff), TTO, TT, PP and MP1.  This is mandatory. Exceptions will be 
addressed on an individual basis. 
1.2.5.6 
Certification Target Datum Holes: Certification Target datum holes in bases, tool 
plates, pallets and frames shall have the body coordinate X, Y and Z dimensions 
stamped next to the holes. 
1.2.5.7 
If certification targets are to be used in the certification process, they shall be 
constructed as shown in Figure 1.2.4.b. Their use shall be documented and 
included as part of the System Certification Book. 
1.2.5.8 
Pass Point Blocks and Target Level Blocks: Pass point and target level blocks 
shall be used in the certification process. They shall be constructed as shown in 
Figure 1.2.4.c. Their use and location on the tool shall be documented and 
included as part of the System Certification Book. Additional considerations 
include the following: 
a. The pass point block shall be a cold roll steel block with a 0.2500-inch 
diameter reamed hole through the center to accept the standard inspection 
target. 
b. When positioning the pass point blocks, the following conditions shall be met: 
– 
The pass point blocks shall be evenly distributed throughout the entire 
envelope of the object being measured, with consideration given to, 
access from as many certification positions as possible. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 54 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
 
Figure 1.2.4.b Certification Target 
– 
Pass point blocks shall only be attached to units or stanchions that are 
permanently doweled or attached to the object being measured. 
– 
The pass point blocks shall be protected from damage due to paint, weld 
flash or other sources, by using the pass point block cover or a plug, 
which will not damage the pass point bore.  All pass point covers, or 
plugs shall be colored red for visibility (see Figure 1.2.4.d). 
– 
All pass point blocks shall be permanently marked PP1, PP2, etc. 
– 
A minimum of six pass point blocks for small tools, (6-feet x 6-feet or 
less) and a minimum of ten pass points for tools over 6-feet x 6-feet. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

    Figure 1.2.4.c Pass Point and Level Target Block

Revision Date: June 08, 2025

     Figure 1.2.4.d Pass Point Plug 
 
c. Once the pass point blocks have been welded in position, the operator shall 
target their locations by inserting a standard inspection target or tooling ball 
into the reamed hole in the pass point block.  Their relative location to the 
coordinate system established for certification shall be documented and shall 
become part of the final inspection report for the tool. 
d. The pass points, once established and valued, shall be the sole method of 
orientation to the object being measured.  They shall allow the operator to 
enter the system and work in and around the object within acceptable 
accuracy parameters. 
e. When the coordinate system is generated using Best Fit to known pass point 
locations, the resulting values of the transformation shall not exceed ±0.07 
mm to the nominal dimensions provided.  If the allowable tolerance is 
exceeded, the operator shall determine what has changed since the original 
coordinate system was generated and make the necessary corrections. 
1.2.5.9 
When a tool has moving part locators, (dump units, slide units, etc.) all locators 
shall be in the work position and under power prior to certification. 
1.2.5.10 
If there are locators that are indexed for multiple models, they shall be certified in 
each work position. 
1.2.5.11 
All substantial weight shall be present at the time of certification to avoid a 
change in the tools geometry after weight is added. 
1.2.5.12 
It shall be the operator's responsibility to ensure that all environmental conditions 
are considered and compensated for as necessary to ensure accurate, 
repeatable measurements.

Revision Date: June 08, 2025

1.2.5.13 
Locating Block Surface Certification 
1.2.5.13.1 
Locator files (LDs) shall be used to identify which surfaces are used in a 
tool/process. A minimum of five points shall be checked on the locator block 
surface. The points shall be certified to the latest release of the product 
contacting the surface at time of certification.  
1.2.5.14 
Locator Pin Certification 
1.2.5.14.1 
Locator files (LDs) shall be used to identify which holes are used in a 
tool/process. Pin centerline shall be certified to the latest release of the product 
at the sheet metal pierce-point. 
1.2.5.15 
 
Locator Certification Tolerances: 
• 
Locator block 
– 
±0.13 mm in the controlled axis 
– 
±0.50 mm in the uncontrolled axis 
• 
Locator pin  
– 
±0.13 mm in the controlled axis 
– 
±0.50 mm in the uncontrolled axis 
• 
Locator Bushings for Plastic NAB Pins 
– 
±0.13 mm in the controlled axis 
1.2.5.16 
All tooling and Programmable Locator Units (PLU's) shall maintain the same 
positional tolerances. This positional tolerance must be maintained by the PLU 
under all torsional, axial, and direct load paths applied by tool weight, part weight, 
and weld impact loading applied at the furthest spot weld distance from the pin or 
NC Block. 
1.2.5.17 
System Certification Book 
1.2.5.17.1 
The System Certification Book shall contain the following: 
1. Each system shall have a section in the System Certification Book, use the 
form as shown in Figure 1.2.4.d to provide system information. 
2. Each fixture for each system shall have a section in the System Certification 
Book, use the form shown in Figure 1.2.4.e to provide tool information. 
3. Each Fixture section of the System Certification Book shall contain the 
following: 
– 
Base certification documentation, consisting of the following: 
o 
A base certification sketch including equipment location relative to 
object, leveling screw location, "as measured" values for each body 
millimeter line, R1-R4 holes labeled and axis orientation. 
o 
A computer printout of the transformation results for the 
measurements used to develop the "body coordinate system." 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 58 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
o 
A computer printout of the data points captured to develop 
coordinate system and all millimeter line measurements (after 
transformation, in "body coordinates"). 
– 
Pass point documentation consisting of the following:   
o 
Construct a plan view diagram that identifies the approximate 
location of all pass point blocks. 
o 
A printout of all pass point and base target datum hole values, 
relative to the "body coordinate system," generated for inspection. 
4. Locator Block/Pin Documentation: A Locator Certification Report shall be 
completed for each fixture certified. The Locator Certification Report shall 
contain the following information: 
– 
Equipment Used. 
– 
Equipment Calibration Date. 
– 
Equipment Operator(s). 
– 
Product Part Number and Revision Level. 
– 
Locator Drawing (LD) File Number and Revision Number. 
– 
Locator Identification Number. 
– 
Unit Number. 
– 
Nominal Values for X, Y and Z. 
– 
Corrected Values for X, Y and Z. 
– 
Difference of Nominal to Corrected X, Y and Z. 
– 
Corrected Vector Deviation from Block Surface to Product Surface. 
– 
Notation in Comment Section of actions taken to correct the Locator. 
– 
Grind Spacer thickness and axis affected. 
– 
The Locator Block and Locator Pin Tolerances (Section 1.2.5.15) shall 
be shown in the Locator Certification Report header. 
1.2.5.18 
Re-verification at the Stamping or Assembly Plant:  The following procedure shall 
be followed to re-verify the dimensional integrity of each tool being installed.  A 
system-specific installation plan shall be used to coordinate the installation and 
should be considered when planning the certification process. 
1. Mandatory requirements: 
– 
The tool/fixture is under power. 
– 
If any components were removed to facilitate shipment, they must be 
replaced prior to verification. 
2. Verify Base Flatness/Level: Measure base flatness/level over every leveling 
screw as measured in the initial set up at the tool’s build source.  The base 
must meet or exceed the tolerances for flatness/level as described in Section 
1.2.4.2.3.  If the base does not meet these tolerances, it must be re-leveled 
and re-worked until acceptable. 
3. Best Fit to Pass Points: 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 59 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
– 
The Tier 1 Integrator will verify that the tool/fixture geometry has not 
changed or has been damaged during transit. 
– 
Best fit to the pass points. This information should be displayed in “body 
coordinate” values. If the measurements are within ±0.07 mm to the 
nominal dimensions provided, the re-verification is complete. 
– 
If an acceptable transformation cannot be achieved, the operator is 
forced to start the certification process over and perform a complete 
certification including establishing “new” pass point values using the 
base machined surface and millimeter lines to generate the coordinate 
system. 
4. Measurements:  Re-verification of surfaces and pins is required only if “Best 
Fit to Pass Points” (above) is unacceptable. 
5. Re-verification Report Format:  Five copies of the following items are to be 
submitted at the completion of the certification process.  The first copy of 
each will be retained by the Tier 1 Integrator, the second will be sent to 
Vehicle Operations General Office (VOGO) Launch Leader, the third will be 
sent to the Assembly Plant Body Area Manager, and the fourth and fifth will 
be sent to affected personnel. 
– 
Title page, (See Figure 1.2.4.e). 
– 
A computer printout of the base level and flatness measurements. 
– 
A computer printout of acceptable transformation results. 
– 
A copy of the original pass point values and pass point sketch. 
– 
If a complete certification was required, all documentation shall be 
reproduced.

1.2.5.19 
Tool Repeatability Validation 
1.2.5.19.1 
The purpose of this section is to provide the steps required for Tool Repeatability 
Validation. Includes Web-Based Locator Revision Request (WLRR), Gage RR, 
Drill Hole Study, Closure Hang Static Repeatability Study and PCF Validation. 
1.2.5.19.2 
Web-Based Locator Revision Request (WLRR) Process 
1.2.5.19.2.1 Any deviations from the Assembly LD file or the LD Assembly Flow Diagram shall 
be managed by submitting a Web-Based Locator Revision Request (WLRR). 
1.2.5.19.2.2 DCD – WEB-Based Locator Revision Request - (WLRR):   
          https://www.wlrr.ford.com/dcdWEB/wlrrHome.do 
1.2.5.19.3 
Gage Repeatability 
1.2.5.19.3.1 Tier 1 Integrators shall be responsible for writing their own CMM programs for the 
manufactured subassemblies in order to provide the dimensional data necessary 
to prove their tooling capability. 
1.2.5.19.3.2 The Tier 1 Integrator along with the responsible Ford Manufacturing Engineer will 
determine if production gages are available for use or if the tooling vendor shall 
provide a Ford-approved alternative method for holding the subassembly. 
1.2.5.19.4 
Drill Hole / Laser Tracker Study 
1.2.5.19.4.1 Scribe line technique Drill Hole / Laser Tracker (where applicable) process shall 
be completed on all geometric setting tools. Respot tooling shall be checked as 
required. Display the scribe unit. Save for Ford Motor Company Buy-Off event. 
1.2.5.19.4.2 Multiple geometric setting tools shall use the same set of metal. 
1.2.5.19.5 
Closure Static Repeatability Study – Closure Hang Only 
1.2.5.19.5.1 Closures Static Repeatability Study results for closures hang only for all clamp 
and pin points and any diagnostic points (e.g., margin, flush, seal gap) identified 
by the team. See also the Closures Static Repeatability Study Worksheet for 
procedure. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 63 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.2.5.19.6 
PCF Validation 
1.2.5.19.6.1 A validation study to identify and correct dimensional issues will be performed on 
new tooling systems. This is to ensure parts assembled in tooling system 
dimensionally replicate parts built in a Part Coordination Fixture (PCF). Activity is 
to be performed prior to tooling buyoff at the vendor, if PCF gages and parts are 
available. Validation study for kitted tooling systems should be completed during 
the TTO build but must be completed prior to the TT (1PP Builds). Validation 
study to be performed by Tier 1 Integrator through tooling system using the 
following process: 
1.2.5.19.6.2 The Manufacturing Engineer and the Tier 1 Integrator will utilize one set of VTTO 
material or Hard Die Material and have parts built up in PCF tools with screws. 
This activity and Vendor Tool Buyoff dates must be coordinated with PCF the 
group for timing and fixture availability. 
1.2.5.19.6.3 The subassembly will be taken apart and then analyzed through the tooling 
system in a manner similar to the Drill-Hole study. Each stage of geometry will be 
analyzed prior to welding and should be reviewed in the PCF Fixtures after 
welding to ensure that all Master Locating Pins are still able to be engaged and 
that all original screw holes line up. 
1.2.5.19.6.4 All issues identified in this study are to be documented in an Issues Matrix by the 
Tier 1 Integrator and resolved. In cases where the tooling and the PCF fixtures do 
not produce similar results that Tier 1 Integrator will validate that the tooling is 
dimensionally in specification using a laser tracker. The Tier 1 Integrator shall 
document the results of the validation. 
1.2.6 
Buy-Off Procedures 
NOTE:  For kit buy-off, procedures may vary. Refer to Section 2.10. 
In order for the tool buy-off to be successful, the event shall begin at tool design. 
The book shall evolve as the tooling and processes are developed. 
The objective is to deliver tooling / process, which shall meet safety, quality and 
timing requirements. 
The buy-off event is a culmination of all the work and planning involved in 
delivering the tooling and process to the plants. 
All requirements defined in the Vehicle Operations – Structures Standards 
(GVOSS) need to be achieved “Prior to Buy-Off”. Deviations require Chief 
Engineer Approval. 
The Tier 1 Integrator Project Manager shall be responsible for the development, 
coordination and completeness of the Buy-Off book documentation. 
The form Buy Off Book Readiness Status by Tool may be used to determine Buy 
Off book readiness for the Tool Buy Off.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 64 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
The form Tooling Buy-Off Status shall be used to track status of the Buy-Off 
Process. 
1.2.6.1 
Agenda. The following agenda will be used for all buyoffs. Agenda (insert times 
based on the buy-off start time). 
• 
Ten Minutes 
Introductions 
• 
Ten Minutes 
Process overview – plant/Tier 1 Integrator line layout 
• 
Ten Minutes 
Program Lessons Learned matrix review. 
•

                                   Station Cycle Time Report, Weld Teardown Report 
                                   Sealer/Adhesive/Mastic Teardown Report   
•

Building safety guidelines and buy-off procedures 
•

Tooling review per VO spec. 
• 
If appropriate, Lunch 
(insert time) lunch 
•

•

•

             Review Buy-off Book and sign Special Inspection  
 
 
                        Report 
 
1.2.6.2 
Prior To Buy-Off Date 
These elements require completion prior to the date of the Ford Motor Company 
Buy-Off event. Additional items may be required for unique tooling systems such 
as Hemmers, Laser Systems, etc. 
• 
The Tier 1 Integrator Project Manager shall notify the Ford Motor Company 
VIS 14 days in advance of the Buy-Off date so there is adequate time to 
notify other Ford personnel that need to be in attendance. 
• 
No tooling buyoffs shall be scheduled until all buyoff requirements are met 
and validated by the Vendor Interface Supervisor (VIS). If all the tooling 
requirements cannot be met or the tooling must be shipped to make program 
timing, the Ford Motor Company VIS will instruct the Tooling Vendor Project 
Manager to fill out an Action Request Form.  
• 
It is recommended that the Tier 1 Integrator conduct an internal buy-off trial 
run prior to the Ford Motor Company VO / plant buy-off date. A fault list shall 
be made available and reviewed with Ford VOGO Representative. 
• 
Line re-start shall be achievable in a reasonable time (two minutes or less) 
without the use of the PLC / CRT. 
• 
Buy-off Book review shall be scheduled by Tier 1 Integrator Project Manager 
one week prior to actual buyoff with the VIS and Tooling/Standards Manager.

1.2.6.3.8 
Tab 8: 2500 Cycle Run Data  
NOTES: 
• 
When the machine cycle time of the tooling system is above 70 
seconds, a 20-hour run may be utilized in place of a 2500 cycle run.   
• 
All the requirements for a 2500 cycle run apply to the 20-hour run. 
• 
Required Buy-Off Book documentation 
– 
2500 Cycle Run Fault Log  
– 
Combine Detailed Summary Reports for Machine Faults, Cycle Times 
and Manual Interventions from the Ford approved data capture system 
(FIS). 
– 
If a Ford approved data capture system is not available with the system, 
a copy of the PLC fault log shall be included in the Buy-Off Book. The log 
must cover the entire duration of the 2500 cycle run. 
• 
Reports for the 2500 cycle run must begin at the initial start of the 2500-cycle 
run. 
• 
The 2500 cycle run shall be restarted from zero if a component fails, 
overheats, exhibits premature wear, demonstrates excessive vibration, 
loosens, or expected performance degrades. Ford representatives must 
concur on the corrective actions listed on the 8D before the 2500 cycle run 
restarts. 
• 
The 2500 cycle run shall continue from the stop point if there is a loss of 
incoming power, air, or water. Any other failures must restart from zero.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 67 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
• 
The Ford approved data collection systems shall be used to monitor the 
entire duration of the 2500 cycle run. A Ford representative is required to 
review the machine monitoring system data collected during the entire 2500 
cycle run. The 2500 cycle run may be conducted in ten-hour segments (to 
match a typical overtime shift). Report printout for 2500 cycle run to be 
included in Tab 9 of the buy-off book. 
• 
10 hour runs, via a controlled stop, are to be approved by Ford VIS. 
• 
Any work performed on the tools between 10-hour runs shall be approved by 
a Ford representative. 
• 
All work performed on the tools between 10-hour runs shall be documented 
and the list put into the Buy-Off Book. 
• 
Tip dress every 60 welds with cutter blades and holders removed.  This will 
require turning on the "TD MOTOR BYPASS" switch on the robot interface 
panel to avoid motor current faults. 
• 
Tape feed SPR systems shall have the tape removed during the 2500 cycle 
run and auto repeat. Reference the auto repeat SPL and the equipment 
manufactures manual for instructions on how to remove the tape.  
• 
Magazine and blow fed self-pierce rivet systems will be validated during the 
2500 cycle run. Following alterations have taken place for this to occur: 
1. Unique robot program (program 01) will be used to clear rivet at nose 
during 2500 cycle run.  Reference the latest Rivet Robot Programming 
Guide.   
2. PLC call robot program 01 every 10 units.  Docking for re-fill will occur 
after cartridge low limit has been reached.  Reference the latest Ford 
GLX Logic Template. 
3. Prior to starting 2500 cycle run, a container shall be placed 
underneath strip out block to catch discarded rivets. 
4. Cube feeders shall be filled with enough rivets to complete 2500 cycle 
run without stopping for re-fill. 
• 
Run at line rate. 
• 
The 2500 cycle run shall be conducted in “Automatic Repeat” mode. 
• 
Any deviation to performing the 2500 cycle run shall be signed by the 
Structures Chief Engineer or delegate. 
• 
Robots: 2500 cycles in “Auto-Repeat” per GVOSS requirements, however, to 
reduce time spent in verification of tooling premature failure in low volume 
lines, robots and tooling can be programmed to run independent of and in 
parallel with each other. Every attempt must be made to replicate as much of 
the robot’s work path as possible to test the robot dress routing. Temporary 
logic can be installed to bypass the robot zone “clears” and an alternate 
program select will be required to run the 2500 cycle program which is offset 
in space from the normal robot program. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 68 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
 
• 
Batch Build Lines: 2500 cycles in “Auto repeat” per GVOSS buy-off 
requirements. After 2500 cycle run only required unique components used to 
build next batch need to be cycled per the GVOSS requirement. Examples 
include: unique end effector, hemmer, weld gun, etc. 
• 
Pallet System 2500 Cycle Run-Off Requirements: 2500 cycle run is 
performed without pallets transferring between stations (the pallet is treated 
as a part and the 2500 cycle runs are performed without parts). 
EXCEPTION: A closed-loop pallet system shall run empty pallets.  
• 
Pallet system with an air or servo actuated pallet clamp or unclamp station: 
This station shall run 2500 cycles with a pallet, cycling the coupling and de-
coupling devices. 
• 
BOSCH weld timers are to be set to 5000 amps for the duration of the 2500 
cycle run 
• 
UIR monitoring in the Bosch weld timers post cap dress is to be disabled for 
the duration of the 2500 cycle run 
1.2.6.3.9 
Tab 9: FIS Machine Monitoring – Where Applicable 
1.2.6.3.9.1 
Design Criteria Sheets (DCS) that are aligned with the FIS assets are submitted 
and current.  All of the data included on the DCS are required to be specific to the 
FIS assets as defined, including, but not limited to, MTBF, MTTR, and Cycle 
Times (Source: Commodity Availability Sheet specifically for FIS Assets).     
1.2.6.3.9.2 
MTBF/MTTR benchmarks must be made with the same tool used to develop the 
MTBF/MTTR for the simulation model. However, the equipment included to derive 
the MTBF/MTTR may differ between FIS and Simulation based on the FIS asset 
definitions.   
1.2.6.3.9.3 
Cycle time benchmarks should align with the FIS assets.  They should follow the 
Sequence of Operation chart unless actual cycle time is not represented in a 
single Sequence of Operations chart. Deviations from the process must be noted 
on the DCS.

1.2.6.3.11 
Tab 11: Weld Teardown Summary 
• 
Required Buy-Off Book documentation 
– 
Weld Teardown Report  
– 
Weld Gun Water Flow Check-Off Sheet 
– 
Weld Information Spreadsheet (consult BCE welding for specific format) 
– 
Joining System Validation Check Sheet 
– 
List of proposed robots with bypass functionality (please see 6.12.2.2) 
signed by the ME and Joining Engineer. 
• 
Weld teardown units shall have 100% weld effectiveness. If 100% is not 
achieved on the first unit, corrective actions shall be taken to address the 
discrepant welds. Discrepant welds are shown in the Weld Teardown Report 
"Code Types". Once the actions are completed, another teardown shall be 
done on the discrepant welds and all welds produced by the affected weld 
gun for verification. This process shall continue until 100% weld effectiveness 
is achieved or until the Integrator runs out of metal.  If there is a metal 
shortage that prevents the completion of the weld validation process during 
VTTO at the Integrator's site, then it shall be resumed during TTO at the 
appropriate plant(s). 100% effectiveness shall be achieved with 70% or less 
heat setting. 
• 
Display weld teardown unit. Save for Ford Motor Company buy-off event. 
• 
Any assemblies or units built off integrator tools at their shop that will be 
saleable or used for vehicle testing shall meet the welding requirements of 
the applicable Engineering Specifications. At a minimum, the first and last 
units/assemblies built shall be torn down and evaluated. Any significant 
equipment changes, or time breaks between builds require additional 
teardowns at the re-start of the tool. All discrepant welds shall be corrected in 
accordance to the specification prior to shipping the assemblies or units. 
Upon completion of the teardowns and any repairs, the Plant Welding 
Engineer shall review and approve the data.

1.2.6.3.19 
TAB 19:  Sealer/Adhesive/Mastic Teardown Report  
• 
Required Buy-Off Book Documentation (Contact BCE Joining for the latest 
template for the dispensed material teardown report.) 
– 
Adhesive/Sealer/Mastic – Tier 2 Buyoff Form 
– 
Sealer/Adhesive/Mastic Teardown Report and Summary 
– 
Illustration sheet showing sealer/mastic/adhesive path. 
– 
Visual Build Sheet (VBS) 
– 
Equipment Parameter Sheet  
– 
Robot bead path node map 
• 
Sealer/adhesive/mastic teardown unit shall have 100% sealer/adhesive/ 
mastic effectiveness. If 100% is not achieved on the first unit, corrective 
action shall be taken to address the discrepant sealer/adhesive/mastics. 
Once the actions are completed, another teardown shall be done on the 
discrepant sealer/adhesive/mastics and all sealer/adhesive/mastics produced 
by the affected sealer/adhesive/mastic gun for verification. This process shall 
continue until 100% sealer/adhesive/mastic effectiveness is achieved or until 
the integrator runs out of metal. If there is a metal shortage that prevents the 
completion of the sealer/adhesive/mastic validation process during VTTO at 
the integrators site, then it shall be resumed during TTO at the appropriate 
plant(s).

• 
Display sealer/adhesive/mastic teardown units and save for Ford Motor 
Company buy-off event.

1.2.6.5 
Tool Buy-Off Procedures – Body Construction Engineering 
1.2.6.5.1 
Vehicle Operations Buy-Off Attendees: Body Construction Engineering Chief 
Engineer (or designate), Body Construction Engineering Manager (or designate), 
Vendor Interface Supervisor, Dimensional Management Engineer, Manufacturing 
Engineering Representative, Lead VOME Controls Engineer, VOME Safety and 
Plant Buy-Off Team, including Plant Safety Representatives.  
1.2.6.5.2 
VO/Plant Buy-Off Attendees: Vendor Interface Supervisor, Manufacturing 
Engineering Representative, Plant Buy-Off Team. 
1.2.6.5.3 
Phases of a buy-off are listed below: 
• 
Phase I: System shall be running in “Auto Repeat” (Dry Cycle).  The 
Customer Test will be performed (Refer to 1.2.5.6). 
• 
Phase II: System shall be in full “Automatic” ready to run parts.  One part will 
be loaded, and the customer test will be performed. 
• 
Phase III: System shall be in full “Automatic” ready to run parts. Two parts 
will be loaded back to back and a third will be loaded after a one-unit skip.  
All shall run through the complete line in full “Automatic” without stoppage.  
The customer will induce no faults during this run.  Back to back run shall 
include each model. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 76 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
• 
Phase IV: This phase is a review of the data in the Buy-Off Book. The people 
most familiar with the data must be available to present the section and 
answer questions as they arise. 
• 
Phase V: (Optional) this phase is a review of the process through each 
station. This can be completed prior to the Management Buy-Off Date. 
NOTE: All Buy-Off personnel (Tier 1 Integrator and Ford) must use safety locks. 
1.2.6.6 
Customer Test 
1.2.6.6.1 
The tooling Marquee or PCB emulator must be temporarily set-up to verify 
functionality during the customer test. 
1.2.6.6.2 
The customer will induce a random sampling of faults into the system to cause 
system stoppage. Induced faults will consist of random checking of safety 
circuits, proximity and limit switches, logic and fault messages. The Tier 1 
Integrator shall have sufficient people on the line to demonstrate the ability of 
trained personnel to diagnose the cause of the stoppage and restart capability. 
Cause of system stoppage or inability to restart shall be indicated on system 
diagnostic display. 
NOTE: The sampling of faults during the V.O./Plant Buy-Off will be thorough. 
Adequate time must be included in the Critical Path Timing for validation of fault 
recovery. 
1.2.6.6.3 
The Body Construction Engineering Manager or delegate shall initial the Special 
Tool Inspection Report, prior to tool shipment. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 77 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.2.7 
Global 8D, 8D and 5D 
1.2.7.1 
All 8D's shall be entered into the Ford Motor Company Quality Global 8D system 
unless otherwise specified by the Ford Motor Company VOME. Tier 1 Integrators 
can access the Global 8D from the FSP (Ford Supplier Portal) hosted by 
Covisint. The Global 8D application is accessed from the GQA (Global Quality 
Analysis) application on the "Applications" tab from the FSP Home page. 
1.2.7.2 
When directed by VOME to complete a Global 8D, the Ford Motor Company ME 
(Manufacturing Engineer) shall open the Global 8D. A Global 8D can only be 
opened by a Ford Motor Company employee. 
1.2.7.3 
Ford Motor Company VOME shall determine who will hold the lead responsibility 
for an 8D and 5D. This may be either a Tier 1 Integrator, Ford-to-Furnish supplier 
or another supplier. 
1.2.7.4 
The lead supplier shall supply a list of employees and their Covisint ID's that will 
be part of the team. At least one supplier team member is required to have a 
Covisint ID to have edit privileges. The Tier 1 Integrator Covisint ID is entered 
into the "Covisint ID Converter" to generate a cross referenced FSN ID 
necessary to use the Global 8D application. Tier 1 Integrator employees can 
obtain Covisint ID's from their Corporate Security Administrator. 
1.2.7.5 
When using a Global 8D, once the ME opens a Global 8D the lead Tier 1 
Integrator team member will be responsible for updating the 8D. 
1.2.7.6 
The Tier 1 Integrators are responsible for their own Global 8D training if it is 
necessary. 
1.2.7.7 
Tier 1 Integrators can view Global 8Ds on which any employee of their company 
is a team member. They can edit Global 8Ds on which they themselves are team 
members. (They must be added to the Global 8D team using their Covisint ID.) 
1.2.7.8 
For all Tier 1 Integrators to be able to view a Global 8D each Tier 1 Integrator 
must be represented on the team. 
1.2.7.9 
When directed, the Tier 1 Integrator shall furnish an 8D using the 8D Blank Form. 
1.2.7.10 
When directed, the Tier 1 Integrator shall furnish a 5D in lieu of an 8D. Use the 
form 5D Blank Form. 
1.2.7.11 
After program launch and during the Warranty Period, equipment and component 
Tier 1 Integrators shall complete 8D’s and 5D’s as directed by VOME and the 
Plant. The use of the Global 8D, 8D or 5D is at the plants’ discretion. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 78 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.2.8 
Non-Production Industrial Materials for Equipment 
1.2.8.1 
Tier 1 Integrator Furnished Industrial Materials 
1.2.8.1.1 
There shall be no industrial materials furnished by Tier 1 Integrators using 
program funding as part of any program. 
1.2.8.2 
General 
1.2.8.2.1 
Industrial materials are components and materials kept in plant inventory for the 
specific purpose of replacement upon failure. Prior to this standard, industrial 
material were referred to as recommended "spare parts". The term Industrial 
Materials has been adopted to resolve nomenclature issues which exist between 
VOGO Division Engineering, IM, and the Finance community. 
1.2.8.2.2 
Industrial materials are any component subject to failure either through infant 
mortality or during the component's useful life that should be in the inventory 
system at the plant to support unscheduled equipment repair of in-production 
failures.  
1.2.8.2.3 
Tier 2 Equipment Suppliers must ensure critical industrial materials will be 
available for immediate purchase or warranty replacement to support TT and PP 
Builds when these builds occur at the Tier 1 Integrator. The critical industrial 
materials must be identified prior to VTTO. 
1.2.8.2.3.1 Tier 2 Equipment suppliers shall provide all manufacturer component part 
numbers contained within their supplied products on the industrial material lists 
submitted to the Tier 1. 
1.2.8.2.4 
Tier 1 Integrators should review the FAP03-207 & the Industrial Materials 
Training Module to familiarize themselves with the process and deliverables to 
Ford Motor Company required by this section. 
1.2.8.2.5 
All Additive Manufactured details shall have the associated designs, CAD files in 
the proper printing format, material, and type of machine it was produced on 
provided with the IM list. Alternative materials and their respective machines 
should be included if applicable. 
1.2.8.3 
Roles and Responsibilities 
1.2.8.3.1 
See the Industrial Materials Process Roles and Responsibility Matrix. Not 
applicable for Final Assembly. 
1.2.8.3.2 
The Ford Motor Company receiving plant shall identify a Spare Parts Coordinator 
(SPC) for the program who will be the single point of contact to receive 
documentation from the Tier 1 Integrators. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 79 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.2.8.3.3 
VOME oversees the process for correct management and timing by the Tier 1 
Integrator. 
1.2.8.3.4 
Tier 1 Integrator is the single point of control for Tier 1, Tier 2 and Ford-to-
Furnish (FTF) suppliers to coordinate and follow the process and deliver the 
industrial materials lists to the plant. See also the Industrial Materials List 
Development Process Flow Chart. 
1.2.8.3.5 
The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible for the correctness and completeness of Tier 
2 lists they deliver to the receiving plant. 
1.2.8.3.6 
Tier 2 and FTF Suppliers shall deliver the required industrial material lists for 
their equipment to the Tier 1 Integrator concurrent with the receipt of their 
equipment at the Tier 1 Facility. 
1.2.8.3.7 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide a list of report types for the program to the 
plant SPC for tracking purposes. 
1.2.8.4 
 
Industrial Materials Process Timing 
NOTE: Job 1 means: MP1 for Program. 
1.2.8.4.1 
Refer to the Industrial Materials Process Timing Matrix for list timing. The file 
Industrial Materials Process Timing Tool calculates timing with a user-entered 
Job 1 date. 
1.2.8.4.2 
For Final Assembly the following shall apply. 
1.2.8.4.2.1 
The supplier shall submit a preliminary Industrial Materials Component List for all 
Tier 1, Tier 2, FTF, and commercial components at 100% Design Buyoff. 
1.2.8.4.2.2 
The supplier shall submit a finalized, updated Industrial Materials Component List 
for all Tier 1, Tier 2, FTF, and commercial components at final buyoff. 
1.2.8.4.2.3 
If tool in-service date is less than 5 months from Buyoff, the Tier 1 Supplier and 
VOME must inform the Plant to use the Industrial Material list at the Design 
Buyoff to initiate 1295's, particularly for long lead and critical components. 
1.2.8.5 
Kits and Pull-Ahead Equipment 
1.2.8.5.1 
The date equipment begins producing current model parts shall be referred to as 
the “Commissioning Date”. 
1.2.8.5.2 
A separate Industrial Materials Acquisition Process Timing Matrix shall be 
developed for kits and equipment installed for an NMP that will also run current 
production. 
1.2.8.5.3 
The Commissioning Date in the Industrial Materials Acquisition Process Timing 
Matrix shall be the date the kits or equipment will begin to run current production.

1.2.8.6 
Field Definitions for Reports 
1.2.8.6.1 
"Supplier" shall be the company responsible for supplying the system to Ford 
Motor Company. 
1.2.8.6.2 
"System" shall be the system supplied to Ford Motor Company as identified in 
the manufacturing process. 
1.2.8.6.3 
"Item Number" shall be a sequential series of natural numbers beginning with 1 
to (n) to identify the line number for the entries on the forms. 
1.2.8.6.4 
Component "Manufacturers Part Number" shall be in the same format as it 
appears in the manufacturer's catalog.  
1.2.8.6.4.1 
When a component manufacturer uses a component I.D. number in addition to 
the component part number, component part number shall not be transposed with 
the component I.D. number. 
1.2.8.6.4.2 
A tooling or sub-system suppliers’ part number shall not be used for a component 
that is commercially available directly from the manufacturer or distributor. The 
tooling or sub-system suppliers' part number can be entered into the "Comment" 
column for reference purposes. 
1.2.8.6.5 
“Tool Number”; Unless otherwise stated in this section the "Tool Number" shall 
be the tool number stored in Team Center as defined in Section 2.2.3, for 
example 016ZF-12345678-30-2E. 
1.2.8.6.6 
“Tool Name”; The name of the tool on the tool drawing. 
1.2.8.6.7 
"Station" shall be the station Process Number designation as defined in Section 
2.2.3, for example 6W-010. Note, use 3 digits for the station. 
1.2.8.6.8 
"Unit" shall be the unit designation as defined in the tool or station, unit BOM. 
1.2.8.6.9 
“Equipment ID Number” shall be the number used in the Tool Profile Sheet. 
See GVOSS Section 1.2.3.13 for additional information. 
1.2.8.6.10 
"Quantity" shall be the quantity of the component used on the unit. 
1.2.8.6.11 
"Total Quantity" shall be the total quantity of the component used in the system. 
When completing this field in the "Industrial Materials – Component Distribution 
Report" form, enter the value of this field once, at the end of the component 
distribution. This is the value entered into the "Industrial Materials – Component 
List" form "Total Quantity" field. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 81 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
NOTE: The following additional fields are used in the “Industrial Materials - 
Component List" form. 
1.2.8.6.12 
"Ford Manufacturer’s Part Number" is the part number as it appears in PART 
SPEC. This will be the same as the Manufacturer’s part number with the 
exception that 
– 
All letters are capital 
– 
All blanks are removed 
– 
No special characters appear (+, -, /, etc.) 
– 
"O" is changed to 0 
– 
"I" is changed to 1 
Example: 
Banner sensor 
Manufacturers Part Number:  
Bi20-CA40130-ADZ30X2-B1131/S1009 
PART SPEC Part Number:  
B120CA40130ADZ30X2B1131S1009 
1.2.8.6.13 
"Component Description" shall contain the component name and significant 
characteristics of the component from either the component technical information 
as it appears in the manufacturer’s catalog and technical literature for purchased 
components or developed by the manufacturer for manufactured components 
and proprietary components manufactured under license.  
Example: 
Part Number: 100S-C60DJ14BC 
Part Manufacturer: ALLEN BRADLEY 
• 
INCORRECT Part Description: “Contactor” 
Reason: This is a Part Name and does not provide a description of the 
part. 
• 
CORRECT Part Description: “IEC Safety Contactor, 3-Pole AC (Three Main 
Contacts), N.O. 1, N.C. 4 Auxiliary Contacts, 24V Coil Voltage, 97A, 
460VAC, 3-Phase, 75 Hp” 
Reason: This describes the part more completely. 
When the component is part of a manufacturer’s subassembly the 
subassembly part number shall also be included in the Component 
Description. If used on multiple subassemblies include all 
subassembly part numbers.   
1.2.8.6.14 
"Component Manufacturer" shall be the component manufacturer's name. DO 
NOT ENTER A DISTRIBUTOR’S NAME. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 82 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.2.8.6.15 
“Ford MCC Number” is the Ford Motor Company inventory control leave the 
entry blank. 
1.2.8.6.16 
"Reference Only" is a "yes/no" field that indicates the component is included for 
information purposes only. When this field is not initialized for components in the 
MCC Library the Tier 1 Integrator shall complete this field where applicable. 
1.2.8.6.17 
"Industrial Materials" is a "yes/no" field for material used in Preventative 
Maintenance (PM) activities such as lubricants and filters. They are used in 
conjunction with an MFM PM activity. These items should be ordered as expense 
items on an as-needed basis when the PM activity is scheduled. When this field 
is not initialized for components in the MCC Library the Tier 1 Integrator shall 
complete this field where applicable. 
1.2.8.6.18 
"Consumable" is a "yes/no" field for materials that are consumed in the 
manufacturing process such as welding electrodes. These items need to be 
maintained in inventory. 
1.2.8.6.19 
"Recommended Quantity" is the component quantity the equipment 
manufacturer recommends keeping in inventory. When this field is not initialized 
for components in the MCC Library the Tier 1 Integrator shall complete this field 
where applicable. 
This quantity shall be determined through the Tier 1 Integrators' compliance to 
the Ford Motor Company R&M 5 Point process. The data used in the 
determination of Recommended Quantities shall be made available to 
substantiate the quantities entered. 
1.2.8.6.20 
"Comment" is used for additional information that will assist in identifying 
additional information that is useful to the plant regarding the component. This 
field will be utilized for components in the MCC Library to identify the following 
types of materials: 
1. OOR (Order on Request) 
2. Expense 
NOTE 1: When the comment field is initialized any additional comments shall be 
appended to the existing comment. 
NOTE 2: All other pricing information should be provided by the CMS 
(Commodity Material Supplier). 
1.2.8.6.21 
"Total Quantity" shall be the total quantity of the component used in the system. 
When completing this field in the "Industrial Materials – Component Distribution 
Report" form, enter the value of this field once, at the end of the component 
distribution. This is the value entered into the "Industrial Materials – Component 
List" form "Total Quantity" field. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 83 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.2.8.6.22 
"Applicable ZF/ZM Number" shall be the information necessary to order a part 
that cannot be purchased from a component manufacturer. These are 
components that are manufactured by the equipment OEM and are only shown 
on blueprints. 
1.2.8.7 
Selecting Industrial Materials for Reports 
1.2.8.7.1 
The Tier 1, Tier 2 and FTF suppliers and plant will agree on materials to be 
included in the industrial materials reports and the types of reports for the 
program. 
1.2.8.7.2 
Components Recommended as Industrial Materials 
When creating industrial materials lists special care should be taken to assure all 
necessary components are included in the industrial materials lists. The 
components in industrial materials list is more than a list of purchased materials 
from the equipment BOMs (Bill of Materials). It is a list of materials that have a 
probability of useful life failure. Examples of these are: 
• 
Proximity, Laser and Limit switches 
• 
Clamps 
• 
Air Cylinders 
• 
Electrically actuated ball valves 
• 
Gear motors, Speed Reducers, Gear boxes, electric motors and couplings 
• 
Electric Valves 
• 
Switching electrical components such as relays 
• 
PLC and Associated components 
• 
Rolling element bearings 
1.2.8.7.3 
Components that are NOT Recommended Industrial Materials 
When creating industrial materials lists special care should be taken to assure 
extraneous components are not included in the industrial materials lists. 
Extraneous materials are those without a known failure mechanism.  
Examples of some commonly listed extraneous materials are: 
• Control, Terminal and Junction box enclosures 
• Weldments, Angle Brackets, Miscellaneous Brackets and Risers 
• Angle Brackets 
• L-Blocks 
• Main Control Panel Cables 
• Din Rail 
• Terminal Blocks 
• Fuse Blocks 
• Receptacle Covers 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 84 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
• Tags 
• Cable Tray and Wire Duct 
• Surge Tanks 
• Crane, Enclosed Track and Patented Rail 
• Tool Installation Hardware 
• Unistrut 
• ECPL Placards 
• Tier 2 subsystems listed by quote number (These should have their own 
sheet) 
1.2.8.7.4 
Factors to Consider when Selecting Industrial Materials 
1.2.8.7.4.1 
Useful Life 
• What the expected useful operating period is before failure. 
1.2.8.7.4.2 
Environmental factors 
• How the production environment reduces the useful life of the component. 
1.2.8.7.4.3 
Time to Restock 
• How the availability of components is affected by the order and delivery 
process. 
1.2.8.7.4.4 
Production losses 
• 
The number of production units lost if a spare is not on hand. 
1.2.8.8 
Industrial Materials Scrubbing Process – Not Applicable for Final Assembly 
1.2.8.8.1 
The VOME Launch Leader, Tier 1 Supplier Project Manager, and Plant Launch 
Leader shall select representatives to participate in the Industrial Materials 
Scrubbing Phase I Process. The Scrubbing Phase I Process is a review of the 
Industrial Materials Component lists for missing and extraneous materials, 
proper classification of materials and stock levels prior to submitting the list to the 
plant for final review and the 1295 process. 
 
NOTE: If during Scrubbing Process Phase I the following cannot be determined: 
– High Cost and Long Lead, or 
– Shared 
 
The component should be included on the Industrial Materials – Component List 
with a recommended quantity of (1). These questions will be addressed in 
Scrubbing Process Phase II when more accurate information relative to the 
component acquisition will be available. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 85 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.2.8.8.2 
The Industrial Materials Scrubbing Process Phase I for Tier 2 and FTF shall be 
conducted at the Tier 2 and FTF manufacturer location, prior to delivery of the 
completed Industrial Materials lists to the Tier 1 supplier, unless otherwise 
agreed upon by all involved parties. 
1.2.8.8.3 
The plant SPC shall lead the Industrial Materials Scrubbing Process Phase II. 
1.2.8.8.4 
The "1295 NMP Process Flow Chart" can be used for the plant approval process 
for NMP 1295's. 
1.2.8.9 
Industrial Materials Distribution Reports – Not Applicable for Final 
Assembly 
NOTE: Remove all formulas and links from reports delivered to the Ford Motor 
Company plants. 
1.2.8.9.1 
Equipment ID Numbers – Special Note: 
All Forms in this section have a requirement to identify the Equipment ID 
Numbers. The Equipment ID Numbers are identical to those generated in the 
Tool Profile Sheets in GVOSS Section 1.2.3.12 There are fields in the 
Distribution Reports to generate an Equipment ID Number which in turn MUST 
be correlated to the Tool Profile Sheets.  
If the Distribution Report Equipment ID Number field is populated from the 
Tool Profile Sheet Equipment ID Number, it is not necessary to complete 
the information used to construct an Equipment ID Number in the 
Distribution Report.

Distribution Report" form. This report will list each location where each 
component is utilized within their supplied equipment. When completed, the data 
on this form is to be sorted by: 
• 
Manufacturer 
• 
Manufacturer Part Number 
• 
Tool 
• 
Station 
• 
Unit  
1.2.8.9.3

Material – Component List" described below. 
1.2.8.9.4

"Industrial Materials FTF and Tier 2 Distribution Report" to identify the location of 
the equipment within their system.

different equipment. (Primarily, but not exclusively, small parts conveyors).

locations are easily identified. More than one picture may be used if 
necessary. 
9. Arrows inserted into the picture identifying the motor location. 
10. Arrows inserted into the picture identifying the speed reducer location. 
11. Arrows inserted into the picture identifying the encoder location (when used). 
12. Arrows inserted into the picture identifying the Posidyne Clutch-Brake 
location (when used). 
13. A text box inserted at the tail-end of each motor identification arrow 
containing text identifying the function, manufacturer, and ordering 
information for the motor. 
14. A text box inserted at the tail-end of each speed reducer identification arrow 
containing text identifying the function, manufacturer, and ordering 
information for the speed reducer. 
15. A text box inserted at the tail-end of each encoder identification arrow 
containing text identifying the function, manufacturer, and ordering 
information for the encoder. 
16. A text box inserted at the tail-end of each Posidyne Clutch-Brake 
identification arrow containing text identifying the function, manufacturer, and 
ordering information for the clutch/brake.

1.2.8.16 
Change Control 
1.2.8.16.1 
Equipment changes after the initial industrial materials reports are generated 
must be captured, reports updated and distributed accordingly. 
1.2.8.16.2 
When the change obsoletes a component, the Excel cells in the report shall be 
highlighted in red and the date of the change will be entered in the comment 
column. See the “Change Legend” in 1.2.8.16.4 below. 
1.2.8.16.3 
When the change adds a component, the Excel cell shall be highlighted in green 
and the date of the change will be entered in the comment column. See the 
“Change Legend” in 1.2.8.16.4 below.

SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 94 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.2.8.16.4 
When the change increases the quantity of a component or information is 
changed, the Excel cell shall be highlighted in yellow and the date of the change 
shall be entered in the comment column. See the “Change Legend” below. 
 
1.2.8.16.5 
When the Equipment ID Number changes the text "Equipment ID Number 
change" with the date of the change shall be entered in the comment column.

1.2.8.18 
Industrial Materials Covered by Warranty 
• 
Although a one to three-year warranty is in place on components, all 
components determined to be required to support the equipment should be 
held in plant inventory.  
Note: These will be components that are J.I.C., Critical, Consumable or any 
other classification where the plant determines stocking the component is 
necessary. 
• 
In the event of component failure, a replacement must be readily available 
from plant stores. 
• 
A component which fails under warranty should be returned-to-inventory with 
a warranty replacement received from the Tier 1 Integrator. 
• 
Although a one to three-year warranty is in place on components, all 
components determined to be stocked should be held in plant inventory. 
• 
In the event of component failure, a replacement must be readily available. 
• 
A component which fails under warranty should be restocked with a warranty 
replacement.

1.2.8.19.1

a.  Tool Profile Sheets, submitted by the tooling and conveyor suppliers,

2. Spare Parts to be linked to assets must exist in Maximo Inventory Module

The approved Proforma Supplier is Independent Systems Services (ISS) who will

weeks before Job1/MP 1 and be submitted to the Plant by 1 Month before Job 1/ 
MP 1. 
1.2.8.19.5 
ISS will provide only one (1) Spare Part MFM Proforma to the plant.  Any items 
not included in the initial Proforma will be the responsibility of the plant to 
configure. 
1.2.8.19.6 
ISS will not be responsible for reconciling any changes to the PCON Reference 
Numbers that are provided on the Tool Profile Sheet.  The plant will be 
responsible for configuring any unlinked spare parts resulting from changes to 
the PCON Reference Number.

The purpose of this section is to define the process to procure all documentation 
for the Safety Evidence Book. 
1.2.9.1 
Safety Evidence Book shall include the following documents and tabs: 
1.2.9.2 
Original Safety Evidence Book Sign-Off Sheet 
1.2.9.3 
Tab 1: A hard copy of the ECPL(Energy Control and Power Lockout)/ECSPMT 
(Energy Control for Setup and Permitted Minor Tasks) Placards are included with 
the equipment and ALL electronic files (in native format, not scanned in or .pdf) 
are included with the Safety Evidence Book 
1.2.9.4 
Tab 2: Noise abatement data as per the Occupational Noise Standard FAS08-
212 and Global Manufacturing Specification 
w-SX1 Noise Abatement 
Specification. 
1.2.9.4.1 
w-SX1 Attachment A – Equipment Sound Level Data Form (must be first 
document in tab). 
1.2.9.4.2 
Attachment B - Layout documenting measurement locations. 
1.2.9.4.3 
w-SX1 Attachment C – Request for Deviation Form (if required). 
1.2.9.5 
Tab 3: Risk Assessments 
1.2.9.5.1 
Risk Assessments shall be performed per w-SRA-01 procedure which satisfies 
the requirements of FAS08-036.  
1.2.9.5.2 
Sign-Off by third party organization when third party safety validation is required.

and Installation Assessment found in w-EL10 Appendix 1, see list below: 
• 
w-EL10 
• 
Appendix 1 – PSS Design and Installation Assessment 
• 
Appendix 2 – PSS Permit to Work

1.3.20.4.1. Worksheet is available in TeamCenter under Standards and 
References. 
1.2.9.7.1 
Sign-Off by third party organization when third party validation is required by 
regional requirements.

1.3 
SAFETY 
1.3.1 
Definitions: 
ACTUATING CONTROLS: A foot or hand control used with the machine tool 
control system to initiate the cycle. 
ANTIREPEAT: The part of the control system designed to limit the machine tool 
to a single stroke or cycle if the tripping or actuating means is held operated. 
Antirepeat requires the release of all tripping mechanisms or controls before 
another stroke or cycle can be initiated. 
AWARENESS BARRIER: An attachment that by physical contact warns the 
operator of an approaching or present hazard. 
AWARENESS SIGNAL OR DEVICE: A device that by means of audible sound or 
visible light, warns the operator of an approaching or present hazard. 
BARRIER: A device or object that provides a physical boundary to a hazard. 
CONCURRENT: Acting in conjunction and is used to describe a situation in 
which two or more controls exist in an operated condition at the same time. 
CONTROL SYSTEM: Sensors, manual input and mode selection elements, 
interlocking and decision-making circuitry and output elements to the machine 
tool operating devices of mechanisms. 
CYCLE: A complete movement of the machine tool from an initial start position to 
that same start position. 
DEVICE: Safeguarding, control or attachment that: 
• 
restrains the operator from reaching into the point of operation, or: 
• 
prevents normal or hazardous operation if any part of an individual’s body is 
inadvertently within the point of operation. 
• 
maintains or restrains the operator or the operator’s hands during the 
hazardous portion of the machine cycle at a safe distance from the point of 
operation. 
FIXED BARRIER GUARD: A guard that is attached to the machine tool or its 
framework in such a manner so as to enclose, either by itself or in conjunction 
with other guards or devices, the point of operation. 
FOOT CONTROL: A foot operated device used with a machine tool control 
system. All foot operated devices have guards to prevent unintended operation. 
FRAME: The stationary supporting structure of the machine tool. 
GUARD: A barrier that prevents entry of an individual’s hands or other body part 
into the point of operation. 
HAND CONTROL: A hand operated mechanism or device designed to be used 
with the machine tool control system. 
HAZARD: Any condition that constitutes a potential physical danger to personnel. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 100 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
HOLDING DISTANCE: The amount of travel of the machine tool during which the 
operator is compelled to hold the operating means actuated. 
INTERLOCK: A method of electrical control that is used with a guard or device to 
prevent motion or initiate immediate stopping commands if the guard or device is 
removed or opened. 
INTERLOCKED BARRIER GUARD: A barrier attached to, or around, or both, the 
frame of the machine tool and electrically interlocked in such a manner that the 
machine tool will not cycle or will not continue to cycle unless the guard itself or 
its hinged or movable sections enclose the point of operation. 
MAINTENANCE PERSONNEL: Individuals who care for, inspect and maintain 
machine tools. 
MANUFACTURER: Any person who designs or manufactures guards and 
devices. 
OPERATOR: An individual who performs production operations associated with 
the machine tool and who controls the movement of the machine during normal 
operations. 
OPERATOR CONTROLS: An operator control is a pushbutton, switch, lever, 
hand wheel or other device used to initiate, cycle, reset control or stop the motion 
of a machine tool. 
PERIMETER GUARD: A combination of fixed and interlocked barrier guard 
panels enclosing the periphery of the machine tool in such a manner as to guard 
the point of operation from a safe distance and not be attached to the machine 
tool or its framework. 
PLATFORM: A working space for one (1) or more persons, elevated above the 
surrounding floor or ground level, such as a balcony or platform for the operation 
of machinery and equipment or storage. 
POINT OF OPERATION: The area of the machine tool where the material is 
actually positioned and work is performed during the machine cycle. 
PRESENCE SENSING DEVICE: A device designed, constructed and arranged 
to create an electro optical sensing field that will detect the presence of the 
operator’s or others hand or other body parts and send a signal to stop or 
prevent hazardous motion of the machine tool, regardless of the plane of 
intrusion. Photo eyes are not approved or acceptable for this application. 
READY BUTTON: A one- or two-hand momentary contact control utilized to 
satisfy a condition of an automatic operation. Anti-repeat, anti-tie down circuitry 
required. 
SAFETY BLOCK: A physical blocking device that when inserted between 
opposing machine or tooling members, prevents hazardous motion of one or 
both members. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 101 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
SAFETY DISTANCE: Safety distance is the distance established for use with a 
particular device and is measured from the nearest point of operation hazard to 
the device or the device’s effective sensing point that will ensure that the 
operator’s hand or other body parts cannot reach the point of operation during 
the hazardous portion of the machine cycle. 
SAFEGUARD: A guard, device or method used by the employer to protect the 
operator or others from point of operation hazards. 
SAFE HOLDING: A method of point of operation safeguarding that protects the 
operator by requiring the operator to hold the work piece at a distance from the 
point of operation such that the operator cannot reach the point of operation 
during the hazardous portion of the machine cycle.    
SAFE POSITION OF CONTROLS: A method of safeguarding the point of 
operation by requiring that the operator be positioned at the machine control 
station at a distance from the point of operation such that the operator cannot 
reach the point of operation during the hazardous portion of the machine cycle. 
STOP CONTROL – EMERGENCY OR MASTER: A stop control, when actuated, 
initiates immediate stopping action of all hazardous motion of the machine tool. 
TOEBOARD: A barrier at floor level erected along exposed edges of a floor 
opening or platform to prevent falls of materials. 
TWO HAND CONTROL DEVICE: A device that requires the concurrent use of 
both of the operator’s hands in order to initiate or continue the machine cycle 
during the hazardous portion of the machine cycle. Anti-repeat, anti-tie down 
circuitry. 
TWO HAND TRIP: A device that requires momentary, concurrent operation of 
the trip controls or levers by the operator’s hands in order to initiate the machine 
cycle. 
UNINTENDED OPERATION: The actuation of the machine tool not intentionally 
initiated by the operator that could create a hazardous condition. 
1.3.2 
ECPL AND ECSPMT 
1.3.2.1 
Energy Control and Power Lockout (ECPL) as well as ECSPMT as defined in 
FAS08-100 procedures are required on all tools. Prior to tooling buy-off, the 
following must occur: 
• 
Health and Safety Checklist for Equipment that satisfies the requirements of 
FAS08-036 to be completed by the Tier 1 Integrator. 
• 
For Ford of Europe: VOME "Safety of Machinery" checklist (ASE 2002e) to 
be in line with Machinery Directive 2006/42/EC as well as local legal 
requirements. 
• 
Review of tooling for ECSPMT procedures as documented in FAS08-100. 
• 
Review of the Robot Safe Zone Access Placards. FAS08-100 Sec. 2.29. 
• 
Plant and VO verify lockout functionality. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 102 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
• 
Compare 1As to ECPL drafts. Markup drafts as required. 
• 
Initial and date all drafts. 
• 
ECPL placard supplier will update all initialed drafts to paper color versions 
and post with original drafts prior to VO / plant tooling buy-off. 
1.3.2.2 
The following shall be strictly adhered to for lockout: 
a. Each power source shall have a main disconnect accessible from working 
level. The disconnect shall be lockable in the “off” position. 
b. Electrical disconnects shall conform to and be installed in accordance with 
Ford Global Manufacturing Standard w-ES20. 
c. Pneumatic disconnect shall conform to and be installed in accordance with 
ISO 4414:2010 or most recent version. 
1.3.2.3 
All stored energy shall be dissipated or blocked with non-defeatable devices. 
1.3.2.4 
Lockout provisions shall be provided at a location convenient to maintenance 
personnel servicing equipment. 
1.3.2.5 
All lockouts shall be identified as to what area of tooling they disable and shall 
highlight any immediate adjacent units, etc. which they do not. 
All placards shall follow FAS08-102; Energy Control and Power Lockout 
Placards.  
1.3.2.6 
“A” size robot controller panels shall use a 3” x 3” ECPL Placard vs the standard 
4” x 4” Placard. 
1.3.2.7 
Arc Flash 
1.3.2.7.1 
Arc Flash shall be applied in accordance with Ford Motor Company Occupational 
Health and Safety standard FAS08-160: Practices and Design for the Prevention 
of Injuries from Electrical Systems. 
1.3.2.7.2 
Electrical hazard warning labels for arc flash shall be provided on the following 
equipment operating at 50 volts or more, including equipment with hazard 
classification Level 0: 
• 
All electrical power distribution system equipment, including switchgear, 
substation equipment, buss duct, motor control centers, power distribution 
panels and similar equipment.  
• 
All industrial control panels, including main panels, secondary panels and 
field disconnects. 
1.3.2.7.3 
All panels fed from labeled main panels should use generic arc flash hazard 
labels containing only the nominal system voltage, arc flash boundary, and arc 
flash PPE category values. Only under special conditions determined by the 
ESAP or the Technical Review Committee should these values be different from 
main panel. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 103 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.2.7.4 
Arc flash warning labels are not required for equipment where there is no 
expectation for examination, adjustment, servicing, or maintenance while 
energized, unless the facility’s ESAP or the Technical Review Committee 
determines that labels are necessary to ensure the safety of personnel working 
on such equipment. Some examples of equipment that normally does not require 
labels are listed below (this is not an all-inclusive list):  
• 
Junction, pull and terminal boxes  
• 
Convenience receptacles, light switches, light fixtures  
• 
Any panels, boxes, or control enclosures with voltage of 50 volts or less  
• 
Any other panel or box that is not expected to be opened without first being 
de-energized by locking out the disconnect switch on an upstream panel. 
1.3.2.7.5 
Calculations for single-phase AC systems and direct current (DC) systems are 
not a part of IEEE 1584. The incident energy of single-phase systems can be 
estimated from the line-to-line voltage, but the result is expected to be 
conservative. The incident energy of DC systems should be estimated using the 
“Doan” method. 
1.3.2.7.6 
The Tier 1 Tooling Integrator shall provide and install generic arc flash hazard 
labels prior to tool buyoff as described in 1.3.2.6.3 and FAS08-160 for all 
equipment not directly connected to the facilities electrical buss, such as robot 
controllers, sealer controllers, rivet controllers, motor controllers, and drives. If 
the Installation Contractor determines that different hazard values are needed, 
the Tier 1 Integrator shall replace labels during system installation with the 
corrected values (Protag White). 
1.3.2.7.7 
The Installation Contractor shall provide and install the permanent arc flash 
hazard labels for all equipment that is fed from the facilities electrical buss. This 
label shall include the name and location of the equipment, power source, 
nominal system voltage, restricted approach boundary, arc flash boundary, and 
arc flash PPE category values as shown in FAS08-160. 
1.3.2.7.8 
The Installation Contractor shall provide short circuit, coordination, and arc flash 
studies used to generate the hazard labels to the VO Controls department and 
the plant facilities representative (printable PDF and engineering software source 
files). The printable file shall be provided to the Tier 1 integrators.
