1.3.3 
General Information 
1.3.3.1 
Scope 
This standard establishes the safety requirements that apply to automated 
welding and assembly tools and equipment. These requirements are generally 
applicable to all types of welding and assembly tools but is not intended to be 
inclusive and to not relieve sellers of any responsibility toward compliance with 
applicable Federal, State, Local and Company safety requirements.  
Reference also the Global Manufacturing Standard for Safety of Machinery, 
Specification w-KA0 and w-KA0 Machine Safeguarding Letter of Interpretation. 
1.3.3.1.1 
Risk Assessments 
The need and implementation of the safety requirements shall be predicated on 
Risk Assessments using the Global Risk Assessment Procedure FAS08-036. 
1.3.3.2 
Purpose  
The purpose of this standard is to establish the design, construction and 
performance requirements of safeguarding devices, methods and materials 
utilized to protect operators and others from hazards associated with automated 
welding and assembly tools and equipment. 
1.3.3.3 
Application 
The requirements of this standard shall be applied to all new and reworked 
machine tools and equipment procured or reworked for Vehicle Operations, after 
the date of this standard. The Division Safety Engineer is available for 
consultation concerning requirements. 
1.3.3.4 
Responsibility 
1.3.3.4.1 
It shall be the buyer’s responsibility to assure that guards, devices and methods 
are designed in accordance with the requirements of this standard. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 108 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.3.4.2 
It shall be the seller’s responsibility to ensure that the guards and devices are 
constructed and installed in the seller’s build location in accordance with this 
standard. 
1.3.3.5 
Safety to operating personnel shall be a prime consideration in the equipment 
build. All necessary covers, guards, skirts (to prevent operator’s toes from getting 
under the welding machine platen, etc.) shall be included in the build. For the 
protection of personnel and property, installation and operation of all facilities and 
equipment shall comply with existing codes, regulations and Company 
standards. Operating procedures, specifications, contracts and Purchase 
Notifications must make specific statements which will assure compliance with 
the rules and regulations promulgated under the Ford Motor Company safety 
standards and applicable National, regional and local safety standards. 
Equipment shall be subject to review by Ford Safety Department representative. 
1.3.3.6 
The Tier 1 Integrator will also quote and provide the following guards at no 
additional expense to Ford Motor Company: 
• 
Coupling guards 
• 
Finger pinch point guards at the load station 
1.3.3.7 
A review of all safety items will be conducted when the mechanical design is at 
the 50% completion level, including the Unit 1A installation drawings. This review 
will be to establish tentative approval or determination of items to be added or 
revised. A review will also be conducted at the build Tier 1 Integrator during tool 
tryout to determine final approval. All Ford Motor Company and National, regional 
and local safety requirements must be included in Tier 1 Integrator’s original 
quote. 
1.3.3.8 
Pre-Start Health and Safety 
1.3.3.8.1 
The Province of Ontario requires a Pre-Start Health and Safety (PSR) Report 
“Regulation 851, Section 7” to be completed and signed by a Professional 
Engineer for all tooling shipping into any Ontario Plant. The Pre-Start Health and 
Safety is a professional review of safety for the construction, addition or 
installation of a new apparatus, structure, protective element or process or 
modification to the same. A written report with supporting documentation bearing 
the seal and signature of a professional engineer licensed in Ontario is required. 
Reference Ontario Health and Safety: Regulation 851 Section 7 and Guidelines 
for Pre-Start Health and Safety Reviews: How to Apply Section 7 of the 
Regulation for Industrial Establishments. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 109 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.4 
Machine Guarding – General Requirements 
1.3.4.1 
The need and application for guarding shall be determined from a Risk 
Assessment. One or more methods of machine guarding shall be provided to 
protect operators and other employees from hazards in the machine area, such 
as those created by the point of operations, power transmission apparatus and 
other moving machine components that present exposures with injury producing 
potential. Examples of machine guarding are barriers, two-hand controls, and 
presence sensing devices. 
1.3.4.2 
Machine guarding shall be such that it does not present a hazard in itself. 
1.3.4.3 
Machine guard construction shall comply with OSHA Regulations / Code of 
Federal Regulations (CFR) Standard 1910.219 subsections (m) and (o). 
Openings shall comply with OSHA Standard 1910.217 Table 0-10 for equipment 
in the United States and Mexico. 
1.3.4.4 
Machine guards shall comply with CCOHS / Canadian enviroOSH Legislation 
Standard Z-432-04. Openings shall comply with CCOHS Table C-4 for 
equipment in Canada. 
1.3.4.5 
Machine guards shall comply with Machinery Directive 2006/42/EC for equipment 
in Europe. 
1.3.4.6 
Any area of a welding fixture, machine or automation equipment that can be 
hazardous to the operator or repairman must be guarded. 
1.3.4.7 
Each area to be guarded will be identified on the designs with an “ND” (No 
Drawing) detail number and type of guard required. 
1.3.4.8 
Machine guards shall be adequate to provide the intended purpose. The size 
shall be such that they may be easily handled by one person if frequent removal 
is necessary. Large guards shall be sectionalized and provided with access 
doors or panels. Doors shall be hinged to swing horizontally outward and shall be 
provided with latches. Slide gates must remain in open state. 
1.3.4.9 
All tooling automation shall have peripheral guarding with an adequate number of 
swinging or sliding gates for convenient maintenance accessibility. All gates shall 
be electrically interlocked. 
1.3.4.10 
Hinged guards for maintenance access shall be interlocked to all controlled 
hazards within the safeguarded space. The safety-related stop function shall 
satisfy the required performance level according to ISO 13849-1 and the risk 
assessment, minimum Category 3 PLd. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 110 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.5 
Machine Guarding Safety Doors 
1.3.5.1 
Machine Guarding Safety Doors shall have: 
• 
Vertical opening direction – top to bottom, bottom to top. 
• 
A door line photocell 
• 
Integrated door safety closed limit switch 
• 
Foam/Rubber and protection profile on the closing bar. 
• 
Automatic reversing motion when photocells are violated. 
• 
Leveling capability. 
• 
Detection to stop motions prior to collision on obstruction. 
1.3.5.2 
Curtain Requirements 
1.3.5.2.1 
Dependent upon application, curtain material shall be made of woven polyester 
monofilament, transparent/colored PVC, galvanized steel or anodized aluminum.  
Operator workstation curtains shall be orange in color. They shall be equipped 
with transparent or anti-UV window depending upon application. Mig welding 
stations shall utilize Orange with a small viewing window. Non-operator 
automated stations shall be yellow in color.  
1.3.5.3 
Safety Requirements 
1.3.5.3.1 
A Risk Assessment to be completed to ensure proper performance levels are 
being achieved and adherence to governing standards are being met. 
1.3.5.4 
Controls Requirements 
1.3.5.4.1 
Electrical hardware components shall meet all GVOSS requirements. Any 
exceptions must be reviewed and approved by VOME controls lead. 
1.3.5.4.2 
Powered drive unit shall be servo-controlled (VFD) with circuit protection for 
overload and overheat. 
1.3.5.4.3 
Door position switches shall be safety-rated. 
1.3.5.4.4 
Motion enable relays/logic and motion commands shall be interlocked with line 
Safety PLC by Tier-1 Integrator.   
1.3.5.4.5 
Tier-1 Integrator is responsible for installation and commission of system. 
1.3.6 
Point of Operation Guarding 
1.3.6.1 
Design and Construction of Barrier Guarding 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 111 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.6.1.1 
All barrier guards shall be designed and constructed for ease of operation and 
handling by one person. Large, cumbersome, heavy guards shall not be used. 
1.3.6.1.2 
The design, construction and attachment of guards shall ensure no entry to the 
point of operation by reaching over, under, around or through the barrier guards 
during the hazardous portion of the machine cycle. 
1.3.6.1.3 
Material used in the construction of barrier guards shall be of such design and 
strength to prevent the operator and others from reaching into the point of 
operation with their hands or other body parts during the hazardous portion of the 
machine cycle. 
1.3.6.2 
Application and Use 
1.3.6.2.1 
Barriers attached to the machine shall use NAAMS Global Standard Components 
- Assembly Perimeter Fence System Components. Chapter C; Safety Screen 
System Components or ISO 14120 compliant fencing.  
Additional requirements that supersede NAMMS include: 
• 
Only fixed and/or electrically interlocked panels may be used. 
• 
Overall height of guard measured from floor shall not be less than 2200mm. 
• 
Opening from floor to bottom of guard shall be 180mm unless local law 
dictates otherwise. 
• 
Interlocked access door may slide horizontally or be hinged to swing 
horizontally. 
• 
Opening provided by a sliding or swinging interlocked gate must be a  
 minimum of 700mm in width. 
• 
Interlocked plug and receptacles are to be mounted between 900mm and 
1200mm above floor level. 
• 
Guard Panels shall be painted Safety Black. 
• 
Gates shall be painted Safety Orange. 
• 
Fence Posts shall be painted Safety Yellow. 
• 
Gate switch lockout devices shall be mounted to the posts as shown in the 
drawings: 
- 
Lockable Gate Plug Mounting 59in Post 
- 
Lockable Gate Plug Mounting 131in Post with Cable Tray Mount 
- 
GVOSS When adding/changing existing equipment the fencing 
systems already in place should be followed. 
1.3.6.2.2 
Barriers around periphery of machine shall use NAAMS Global Standard 
Components - Assembly Perimeter Fence Components Chapter C; Safety Screen 
System Components or ISO 14120 compliant fencing. Additional requirements 
include:

1.3.6.2.2.1 
 When adding/changing existing equipment the fencing systems already in place 
should be followed 
1.3.6.2.3 
Robot safety requirements are detailed in Ford Corporate Safety Standard 
FAS08-131: Robots and Robotic Systems.  
1.3.6.3 
Two-Hand Controls 
1.3.6.3.1 
Two-hand controls are a method of point of operation guarding for operators. A 
two-hand control only reduces risk to operator. Other engineering controls 
(guards or devices) determined by the risk assessment may be required to 
protect the operator and other personnel. 
1.3.6.3.2 
Where two-hand controls are used to reduce the risk to the operator, the two-
hand control device shall require the concurrent actuation of the operator's 
controls during the hazardous portion of the machine cycle such that the operator 
cannot reach the hazard(s) before a safe condition is achieved. 
1.3.6.3.3 
Two-hand tripping devices requiring momentary actuation of both controls may 
only be used when at least one more point of operation method is present, i.e., 
barrier guarding, presence sensing. 
1.3.6.3.4 
Where more than one operator works on the same machine which is 
safeguarded by two-hand controls, each operator shall be provided with two-
hand controls. The concurrent operation of all separate two-hand-start station 
shall be necessary to obtain start command. Arrangements to enable lockout of 
control station if the number of operations is reduced shall be carried out in a fail-
safe manner. 
1.3.6.3.5 
Two-hand control devices used to reduce the risk to the operator shall satisfy the 
functional characteristics of ISO 13851 Type III-B and ISO 13849-1 Category 3 
PLd. 
1.3.6.4 
Presence Sensing Devices 
1.3.6.4.1 
Red and green indicators shall be provided at each operator station to aid the 
operator in proper entry of the protected zone. The indicators should be easily 
viewed by the operator when entering the protected zone. 
1.3.6.4.2 
Presence sensing devices shall protect the operator or others from point of 
operation hazards by disabling the run command to the machine if the devices’ 
sensing field is interrupted during the hazardous portion of the machine cycle. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 115 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.6.4.3 
Presence sensing devices utilized for operator protection must be positioned to 
require the operator to interrupt the device to perform the assigned task, usually 
loading or unloading. 
1.3.6.4.4 
When an intrusion is removed from a presence sensing field, an actuating control 
must be utilized to start or restart the machine cycle. 
1.3.6.4.5 
Presence sensing devices shall be interlocked to all controlled hazards within 
reach of the operator. The safety-related stop function shall satisfy the required 
performance level according to ISO 13849-1 and the risk assessment, minimum 
Category 3 PLd. 
1.3.6.4.6 
Presence Sensing Device Initiation (PSDI) of machine cycle shall not be used. 
1.3.6.4.7 
Minimum field of coverage is located in the Ford Global Manufacturing Standard, 
w-EL4 specification. 
1.3.6.4.8 
Field of coverage and beam center requirements for presence sensing devices 
utilized for periphery protection on automatic operations (no operator) will be 
specified by the Division Safety Engineer for each individual application. 
1.3.6.4.9 
Presence sensing devices shall be selected from the Ford Approved Source List. 
1.3.6.4.10 
Operator Light Screen Brackets shall be adjustable in three planes. Light screen 
bracket construction shall be NAAMS Global Standard Components - Assembly 
standard Chapter R; Peripheral Components - with an added bolt as shown in the 
picture below. 
 
 
1.3.6.4.11 
Light screen brackets shall be Safety Yellow. 
1.3.6.4.12 
Light Screens shall be mounted so that the diagnostic display is easily viewed 
from outside the light screen sensing area. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 116 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled

Revision Date: June 08, 2025

 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.6.4.13 
Unistrut with a protective cover shall be added to protect the cables. Additional 
protection (covers) shall be added as necessary to prevent entanglement of the 
operator with the light screen cables. 
NOTE:  90º cable ends may be used. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
1.3.7 
Power Transmission Guarding  
1.3.7.1 
V-belts, flat belts, chain drives, couplings, gears and other components of power 
transmission systems shall be guarded with a cast or sheet metal guard so that 
access to the bite of the drive or any of the moving parts can be gained only by 
removal of the guard. 
1.3.7.2 
The guard shall be of such durability that it will contain any part that breaks or 
otherwise fails in service. 
1.3.7.3 
Couplings, keyways, revolving collars and exposed high-speed shafts shall be 
suitably guarded. 
1.3.7.4 
Guards, access doors, and panels to compartments for; drives, gears, belts, 
couplings or related hazards shall be bolted into position. Compartment access 
doors exceeding 150 (968 square cm) square inches shall be hinged to swing 
horizontally. 
1.3.7.5 
See also GVOSS Section 1.4 for Visual Factory requirements of guarding. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 118 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.8 
Guardrails (Pipe Barriers)  
1.3.8.1 
Guardrails are only intended to keep personnel from unknowingly entering a 
particular area and also as protection on overhead welding/working surfaces. 
1.3.8.2 
Guardrails are not considered as alternates for point of operating guarding. They 
are awareness barriers and may only be used for machine guarding with the 
approval of the Plant and Division Safety Engineer. 
1.3.8.3 
Guardrail design shall meet the requirements of the governing code.  
1.3.8.4 
Standard guardrails shall consist of 1-1/2 inch (nominal) internal diameter 
standard pipe and fittings as required. 
1.3.8.5 
The minimum height for guardrail shall be 42” or 1100mm or the height specified 
by the governing code, whichever is greater, with a second guardrail mounted 
midway between the top guardrail and the floor level. 
1.3.8.6 
Vertical support posts shall be installed at a maximum of every 5’-0” (1500mm) 
on center. 
1.3.9 
Control Circuits 
1.3.9.1 
Energy Source Main Disconnect and Lockout 
1.3.9.1.1 
Each machine shall be equipped with a main electrical disconnect capable of 
being padlocked only in the open (off) position. 
1.3.9.1.2 
Each machine with air supply shall have a main lockout and exhaust valve 
capable of being padlocked in the closed (off) position. 
1.3.9.1.3 
The operating handle of primary energy sources should be located between 920 
mm (36 inches) and 1680 mm (66 inches) above working level. Secondary (local) 
disconnects such as conveyors should be no less than 250 mm (10 inches) 
above working level. Positions outside of this range require approval. 
1.3.9.2 
Electrically Interlocked Safety Devices 
1.3.9.2.1 
Electrically interlocked safety devices shall be selected from the Ford Approved 
Source List. 
1.3.10 
Barrier Guards 
1.3.10.1 
Disconnecting an interlock plug on a barrier guard must stop and prevent from 
cycling all hazardous machine motion accessible through the barrier. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 119 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.10.2 
An interlock plug shall be interlocked to all controlled hazards within the 
safeguarded space. The safety-related stop function shall satisfy the required 
performance level according to ISO 13849-1 and the risk assessment, minimum 
Category 3 PLd. 
1.3.10.3 
When all gate and safety plugs have been replaced, it will be necessary to select 
Gate Reset and initiate Automatic Mode to resume automatic operation. 
Connecting or reconnecting a safety gate interlock plug shall never start or 
restart the cycle. 
1.3.11 
Actuating Controls for Automation 
1.3.11.1 
General Requirements 
1.3.11.1.1 
Each machine operator shall be provided with a type of actuating control. 
1.3.11.1.2 
All actuating controls are required to be anti-tie down and anti-repeat. 
1.3.11.1.3 
All actuating controls used as safeguarding devices shall be located at a safe 
distance from the hazard. Ring guards or shrouds may be used to reduce the 
reaching distance or protect against unexpected startup. (See 1.3.1 Definitions: 
ACTUATING CONTROLS: A foot or hand control used with the machine tool 
control system to initiate the cycle.) (ANSI B11.19 10.8, 10.9: These subclauses 
only apply when two-hand or single actuating controls are being used for risk 
reduction.) 
 
 
 
1.3.11.2 
Two-hand Controls  
1.3.11.2.1 
Refer to ISO 13851 (latest revision), Safety of machinery - Two-hand control 
devices - Functional aspects and design principles for additional requirements. 
1.3.11.2.2 
All two-hand controls shall have the individual hand controls arranged by design, 
construction or separation to require the use of both hands for actuation per ISO 
13851 Annex A. 
1.3.11.2.3 
Point of Danger 
1.3.11.2.3.1 The distance of the controls from the nearest dangerous movements (point of 
danger) must be such that movement stop before the operator can reach them. 
1.3.11.2.3.2 The minimum safe distance from the point of danger shall be provided based on 
the calculation s = vt, where: 
v = 1.6 m/s (meter/second 
t = braking time (seconds) 
1.3.11.2.3.3 This safety distance shall also apply to movable two-hand controls. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 120 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.11.2.3.4 Any timing safety relay shall be of a fixed delay of 0.5 seconds, or, if adjustable, 
shall have a maximum setting of 0.5 seconds. 
1.3.11.2.4 
Two-hand cycle start buttons shall require manual activation of both controls until 
a point is reached in the cycle where an operator cannot remove his hands and 
place them or any body part within a pinch point. 
1.3.11.2.5 
Two-hand momentary trip may only be used when at least one more fail-safe 
point of operation guarding method is present and operates in conjunction with 
the two-hand tripping device. Examples are fixed barriers, presence sensing 
devices, and interlocked barriers or moveable interlocked gates. 
1.3.11.3 
One-Hand Control “Ready” Button 
1.3.11.3.1 
A one-hand “ready” button may only be used when: 
• 
The operator ready button satisfies a set-up condition for an automatic 
operation and, 
• 
The operator station is protected with an approved presence sensing 
device(s). 
1.3.11.4 
Foot Control 
1.3.11.4.1 
A foot actuating control shall only be used on operations requiring work holding 
and only if safe holding can be accomplished. 
1.3.11.5 
Other Operator Controls 
1.3.11.5.1 
Clamp. Operations with clamp control shall meet the applicable requirements for 
two-hand controls. 
1.3.11.5.2 
Unclamp. Unclamp control need only be a one-hand control unless an operator is 
exposed to a pinch point when a clamp is opened. 
1.3.11.5.3 
Emergency Stop. Each operator must have an emergency stop device in 
accordance with the requirements of this section. 
1.3.12 
Emergency Stop Controls 
1.3.12.1 
Requirement 
1.3.12.1.1 
The need for emergency stop installation shall be determined in the risk 
assessment, or as mandated specifically in Section 1.3.11 or elsewhere in the 
GVOSS. 
• 
Emergency Stop Devices shall conform to the requirements of ANSI B11.19 
and B11.20 or EN ISO13850. 
1.3.12.1.2 
An Emergency Stop command shall:  
• 
Override all other function and operations in all modes for hazardous motion. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 121 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
• 
Remove power to the machine actuators, which causes a hazardous 
situation(s), as quickly as possible without creating other hazards. 
• 
Have a reset function if the device does not have a latching function. 
• 
Not initiate a restart of hazardous situations when reset. 
• 
Be sustained until it is reset.  This reset shall be possible only at that location 
where the command has been initiated.  The reset of the command shall not 
automatically restart the machinery but only permit restarting. 
1.3.12.1.3 
The emergency stop command shall be either a Category 0 or Category 1 stop. 
The choice of stop shall be determined by the risk assessment of the machine. 
• 
Category 0 is an uncontrolled stop by immediately removing power to the 
machine actuators. 
• 
Category 1 is a controlled stop with power to the machine actuators available 
to achieve the stop then remove the power when the stop is achieved. 
1.3.12.1.4 
Components of the fixture shall not drift or continue in motion under the following 
conditions: 
• 
after depression of the emergency stop pushbuttons. 
• 
exhaust of the pneumatic surge tank to atmosphere. 
• 
loss of electrical power or normal electrical power disconnection for 
maintenance. 
• 
between production shifts. 
NOTE:  A controlled e-stop is not considered drifting. Once the machine has 
completed the e-stop process, it should not drift. 
1.3.12.2 
Approved Devices Description 
1.3.12.2.1 
The Emergency Stop Pushbutton shall be of the mushroom type and apply the 
principle of direct opening action with mechanical latching. The actuator shall be 
colored RED. The background shall be colored YELLOW. Neither the actuator 
nor the background should be labelled with text or symbols. 
1.3.12.2.2 
Emergency Stop Pushbuttons shall never be guarded. 
1.3.12.2.3 
The Emergency Stop cable switch shall apply the principle of direct opening 
action with mechanical latching. An emergency stop command shall be 
generated when the cable is slack or is broken or disengaged.  
• Emergency Stop cable shall be constructed of 1/8-inch (3.0 mm) diameter, 
bright red plastic coated, 7 x 19 -strand airplane type cable not exceeding 
75 feet (23.0 m) in length 
• The cable shall be supported along its entire length at suitable intervals to 
keep the rope in plane parallel to the machine. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 122 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
• Conveyor applications shall have separate cables provided for each side of 
the conveyor. 
• Emergency Stop cable(s) must be labeled for function. Minimum lettering 
height is one inch and all lettering i to be block style upper case. Letters 
are to be black on a yellow background. 
1.3.12.3 
Location and Application 
1.3.12.3.1 
All valves shall be located such that during a manual override (by pressing the 
valve manual actuator), when the device controlled by the valve moves, it shall 
not create a pinch point or hazard to the person manually actuating the valve. 
1.3.12.3.2 
In automated assembly systems an emergency stop device shall be located at 
each operator control station.  Each operator that loads or unloads from an 
automated tool shall have an emergency stop button.  These requirements also 
apply where a foot pedal or two-handed "Ready" control is used. Each operator 
panel that allows control of the machine shall have an emergency stop button, 
this includes robot control panels.  Any additional areas such as platforms, 
balconies and back of lines will be addressed through the risk assessment 
process to determine the need for additional emergency stop buttons. 
1.3.12.3.3 
Emergency stop pushbuttons shall be positioned so that they are directly 
accessible and capable of nonhazardous actuation by the operator and others 
who could need to actuate them. The Emergency stop pushbuttons should be 
mounted between 600 mm (24”) and 1700 mm (67”) above floor level. Where 
practical, the pushbutton should be located as follows: 
• Run stand: Center position for 2-hand control, last device on right 
side for single run button application 
• HMI or pushbutton station: lower right corner 
• Freestanding enclosure: lower right corner, 1300 mm (51”) above 
floor level 
1.3.12.3.4 
For operator load and unload to process equipment where the operation is not 
loading or unloading directly to an automation, e.g., slat conveyors, IP delivery 
conveyors, and similar, the requirement for emergency stops shall be determined 
through the risk assessment process. 
1.3.12.3.5 
Emergency stop cables shall be positioned so that they are directly accessible 
and capable of nonhazardous actuation by the operator and others who could 
need to actuate them. The Emergency stop cable should be mounted between 
600 mm (24”) and 1700 mm (67”) above the floor level but shall not exceed 2150 
mm (84”) above the working level. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 123 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.13 
Safety Blocks and Pins 
1.3.13.1 
Safety blocks and pins are physical blocking devices that are placed in tooling 
members to prevent motion that otherwise could occur from potential or residual 
energy. When a machine can be operated in “Manual Mode” with the Safety Pins 
or Safety Blocks in place, the Safety Pins and Safety Blocks shall be engineered 
for forces due to gravity and forces from the actuator in a static condition. 
1.3.13.2 
Risk Assessments 
1.3.13.2.1 
A Risk Assessment shall be conducted on all applications that require Safety 
Blocks and Safety Pin; “The risk assessment shall address operator, 
maintenance, installation and decommissioning tasks”. 
1.3.13.2.2 
The Risk Assessment shall be used to determine if a manual, automatic or both 
devices are required to control the risks. 
1.3.13.3 
Safety blocks and safety pins are intended for locking equipment in a desired 
position for maintenance while the machine power is locked out.  
• 
Storage positions shall be adjacent to the point-of-use. 
• 
Safety blocks and safety pins shall be interlocked into the machine automatic 
circuit when in their storage position. 
• 
Removal of a safety block and safety pin from its storage location shall 
prevent the machine from cycling in Automatic while the safety pin is in the 
machine receiver.  
• 
Safety PLC applications shall use a proximity switch on the pin holder that 
detects the safety block and safety pin in its storage location. 
1.3.13.4 
Interlock switches and receptacles from the Ford Approved Source List shall be 
used for safety blocks and pins and shall be arranged such that it is not possible 
to cycle a machine in Automatic with a safety block or pin in place in the machine 
or machine member. 
1.3.13.5 
Safety pins shall be painted Safety Yellow and located adjacent to the area they 
serve, or clearly identified and mounted on a painted Safety Yellow plate 
attached to the station or transfer control panel. 
1.3.13.6 
Safety pin receivers and their associated component receiver-pass thru receiver 
being controlled shall be painted with a Safety Yellow background with Safety 
Black diagonal stripes around the receiver hole. (See example below.) 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 124 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
 
1.3.13.7 
Safety Blocks and Receivers shall follow SBU standard DR8 for Pressroom Die 
Blocks. 
1.3.13.8 
Manual Safety Pins and Receivers 
   
 
Example Safety Pins 
1.3.13.8.1 
Safety pins shall comply with ANSI B11.19 and be adequately sized to restrain 
the static weight of the machine element being pinned and forces from the 
actuator in a static condition. 
1.3.13.8.2 
The Safety Factor shall be 4:1 for single Safety Pin designs; for multiple Safety 
Pin designs, the safety factor shall be 2:1 for each pin. 
1.3.13.8.3 
Proper engagement of the Safety Pin from the receiver into the machine element 
being controlled must be visually verifiable that the safety pin is fully engaged 
into the receiver hole and properly engaged into the machine element safety pin 
engagement hole. (See visual factory example below.) 
 
 
 
 
         Fully Engaged        
 Not Fully Engaged 
1.3.13.8.4 
All Safety Pin applications shall be included in the design and review process 
that is typically subject to review by a professional engineer (P.E.); field changes 
to safety pin locations shall be PE Certified. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 125 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.13.8.5 
25mm (1.0”) diameter Safety Pins and their associated components shall be 
constructed to NAAMS Section AA: Safety Pins. 
1.3.13.8.6 
Safety Pin designs and material shall be approved by the PE as part of the PE 
certification process. See section 1.3.13.8.4. 
1.3.13.8.7 
Safety Pins shall have a chamfer on the working end. 
1.3.13.8.8 
Safety pins shall be accessible from floor and maintenance platform level when 
feasible and without physical entry into the danger zone of the mechanism being 
pinned. 
1.3.13.8.9 
The Pin Receiver Hole shall be a maximum 2.0mm (0.080”) larger than the pin 
diameter. 
1.3.13.8.10 When the Pin Receiver Hole is into tubing the hole shall be embossed. The 
embossment thickness shall be a minimum 5mm (3/16). 
1.3.13.8.11 The unsupported space between the Safety Pin receiver and the machine 
element being pinned shall not exceed 12mm (1/2”). 
1.3.13.8.12 When the Safety Pin receiver is extended beyond the back of the receiver 
mounting surface to satisfy 1.3.12.12.10 above, the receiver extension shall be 
properly gusseted to manage the moment of torque generated on the Safety Pin 
receiver by the machine element being pinned. 
1.3.13.8.13 Gussets shall be in tension to the load of the machine element being pinned in 
order to function correctly. 
1.3.13.8.14 When the Pin engagement hole into the machine element being pinned is into 
tubing the hole shall be embossed. The embossment thickness shall be a 
minimum 5mm (3/16”). 
1.3.13.8.15 Safety Pin engagement into the machine element being pinned shall extend at 
least 12mm (1/2”) beyond the side opposite from the surface of entry but must 
extend beyond the member (in the case of tubing) being pinned. This distance 
shall not include the chamfer at the end of the pin. 
1.3.13.8.16 Receiver embossments, extensions and gussets shall be continuous seam 
welded around their entire periphery. Stich welding (skip welding) is prohibited. 
1.3.13.8.17 All welding shall be performed by welders qualified by an AWS Certified Weld 
Inspector (CWI) as prescribed in the American Welding Society AWS 
D1.1/D1.1M (2010 or latest edition) Section 4, or by UNI EN ISO 9606-1 (2013 or 
latest edition), or by Canadian Standard (CSA W47.1-1983) and WPS Qualified 
in accordance to AWS D1.1/D1.1M (2010 or latest edition) or UNI EN ISO 15614-
1 (2012 or latest edition). 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 126 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.13.8.18 Copies of the welder's qualification test record shall be made available upon 
request. 
1.3.13.8.19 All Safety Pin receiver and related welds made in the field shall be inspected and 
certified by a 3rd party AWS Certified Weld Inspector (CWI). The certification 
shall be retained by the Tier 1 Integrator in their permanent records. 
1.3.13.9 
Mandatory Applications 
1.3.13.9.1 
All Power Roll Tables with Lifters shall be equipped with (4) manual safety pins, 
(1) in each corner of the lift frame to control the gravity hazard. 
1.3.13.10 
Automatic Safety Pins 
1.3.13.10.1 Automatic safety pins shall be sized to restrain the full dynamic load of the 
actuating mechanism. 
1.3.13.10.2 Automatic safety pins shall have separate switches that sense the pin in both the 
retracted position and extended position. 
1.3.13.10.3 Automatic safety pins shall be “Power Off-Spring Set” (spring extended and 
pneumatically retracted). 
1.3.13.11 
Toggle mechanisms used in lieu of Automatic Safety Pins is prohibited. 
1.3.13.12 
The use of Power Clamps used in lieu of Automatic Safety Pins is prohibited. 
1.3.13.13 
Special latch mechanisms used in lieu of Automatic Safety Pins is prohibited. 
1.3.14 
Open-Sided Floors, Platforms and Balconies 
1.3.14.1 
Guardrails and Toe-Boards  
Guardrail design shall meet the requirements of the governing code as a 
minimum. The minimum height for guardrail shall be 42” or the height specified 
by the governing code, whichever is greater. 
1.3.14.1.1 
Regardless of height, an open-sided platform or welding surface above or 
adjacent to dangerous equipment shall be guarded with a guardrail and a toe-
board. 
1.3.14.1.2 
An open-sided floor or platform four feet or more above adjacent floor or ground 
level shall be guarded with a guardrail and toe-board. Maximum opening at 
bottom of the toe-board is 1/4-inch. 
1.3.14.1.3 
A stationary elevated platform secured to the building or structure that is used for 
storage may be equipped with standard guardrails that are removable in lieu of 
fixed standard guardrails. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 127 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS

1.3.14.1.4 
All elevated platforms shall have a 4-inch Safety Yellow Enamel stripe painted 
around the perimeter. 
1.3.14.2 
Floor Material  
All walking and working surfaces shall be a slip resistant material. 
1.3.14.3 
Working Clearance. Refer to OSHA Regulations / Code of Federal Regulations 
(CFR) Standard Number: 1910.303. 
1.3.14.3.1 
The dimension of the working space in the direction to live parts operating at 600 
volts or less that require examination, adjustment, servicing or maintenance while 
live shall conform to the most current NEC Article 110.26. 
1.3.14.3.2 
Door opening space must be provided to allow the front of an electrical enclosure 
to be opened to 90 degrees away from the enclosure. 
1.3.14.3.3 
Minimum headroom of working space around service equipment shall be 
2100mm, according to ISO 14122. 
1.3.14.4 
Fixed Stairs  
1.3.14.4.1 
Refer to FAS08-040. Additional requirements for Ford North America: refer to 
OSHA Regulations / Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Standard Number: 
1910.25. 
1.3.14.4.2 
Fixed stairs shall be provided for all platforms 12” in height and greater. 
Individual steps shall be prohibited. See Examples below.

1.3.14.6 
Fixed Ladders  
1.3.14.6.1 
Refer to OSHA Regulations / Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Standard 
Number: 1910.23. See Figure below for a sample graphic of a fixed ladder. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
1.3.14.6.2 
Side rails for a climbing aid shall be provided on both sides of a fixed ladder. 
1.3.14.6.3 
A Fixed ladder installation shall be limited to 24 feet (7.3 m) or less, in length. 
Elevations over 24 feet (7.3 m) requiring access will be provided with stairs 
(where feasible), or a series of offset fixed ladders (with cages and platforms) 
that do not exceed 24 feet (7.3 m) in individual length. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 130 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.14.6.4 
Cages shall be installed on all ladders over 16 ft. (5 meters) in length. 
1.3.14.6.4.1 Cages must also be installed on ladders less than 16 feet (5 m) in length when 
the ladder is positioned such that the user is subjected to a fall of over 24   feet. 
(Example – a 15-foot ladder installed as the higher of two offset ladders to 
access an elevation of 39 feet).                             
1.3.14.6.4.2 The bottom of the cage must be within 7 feet (2.1 m) of the ground or platform.   
1.3.14.6.4.3 Cages must be continuous throughout the length of the fixed ladder(s) except for 
access, egress, and other transfer points. 
1.3.14.6.4.4 Cages must be designed, constructed, and maintained to contain employees in 
the event of a fall, and to direct them to a lower landing. 
1.3.14.6.4.5 Cages shall be designed in accordance with drawing number 56ZF-22284. 
 
1.3.14.6.5 
Offset ladder platforms must provide a minimum horizontal surface of at least 24 
inches (61 cm) by 30 inches (76 cm).

1.3.14.7 
Clearances 
1.3.14.7.1 
The perpendicular distance from the center line of the rungs or steps on the 
climbing side of a fixed ladder shall not be less than 36 inches (914 mm) for a 
pitch of 76 degrees and not less than 30 inches (762 mm) for a pitch of 90 
degrees to the nearest permanent object, except with respect to a cage. The 
minimum clearance for intermediate pitches between these two limits shall be in 
proportion to the slope. 
1.3.14.7.2 
The perpendicular distance from the center line of the rung or step on the back 
side of a fixed ladder shall not be less than seven inches. 
1.3.14.7.3 
Step-across distance shall be measured from center line of step or rung to 
nearest edge of structure served by ladder. Minimum step-across distance is 
2-1/2 inches (63.5 mm) and maximum is 12 inches (305 mm). 
1.3.15 
Signs, Labels, Markings, Nameplates 
1.3.15.1 
Disconnecting Means 
1.3.15.1.1 
Each disconnecting means shall be identified with a nameplate to indicate its 
purpose, unless located and arranged so the purpose is evident. 
1.3.15.2 
Operator Controls 
1.3.15.2.1 
All operator controls must be labeled for function.  
1.3.15.3 
Barrier Guards 
1.3.15.3.1 
Barrier guards attached to or around the machine periphery must be match 
marked. The match marking scheme is to be stamped in the upper corners of the 
guard frame with a minimum 1/4-inch characters. 
1.3.16 
Color Coding 
1.3.16.1 
Guards

All barrier guard panels shall be painted Safety Black Enamel.

1.3.16.1.2 
Power transmission guards shall be Safety Yellow Enamel, Expanded metal /

Standard guardrails and toe boards shall be painted Safety Yellow Enamel.

Moving Machine Components: Hazardous moving machine components shall be

1.3.17 
Secondary Safety Restraints

1.3.18 
“Slip Resistant” Steel Floor Material Usage  
1.3.18.1 
Type 1 
1.3.18.1.1 
For service balconies, catwalks and maintenance platforms (with minimum traffic) 
usage shall be confined to one of the four approved steel “slip resistant” floor 
materials (ALGRIP™, Mebac®, SlipNOT® Grade II or steel grating). To maintain the 
load capability of 1/4-inch steel Plate, ALGRIP™ shall be ordered in 5/16-inch 
thickness and Mebac® and SlipNOT® Grade II in 1/4-inch. 
Steel grating shall have the loading capability and CoF of the materials listed 
above and maintain an opening of approximately 4 inches (101mm) square. 
 
Note: For Stamping Areas the use of diamond plate with a slip-resistant coating 
that meets the minimum coefficient of friction requirement of 0.8. 
1.3.18.2 
Type 2 
1.3.18.2.1 
For operator work platforms (level surface) usage shall be confined to Mebac® 
(3-0) or SlipNOT® Grade I which, are the only approved steel “slip resistant” floor 
materials at this time. 
1.3.19 
Automatic Sprinkler System Installation  
Tier 1 Integrators must coordinate with facilities contractors to leave space and 
coordinate integration into the plant fire protection system. Refer to “Fire 
Protection Engineering – Bulletin #8” 
1.3.20 
Light Screens and Scanner 
1.3.20.1 
Light screens and laser scanners shall be covered with a metal guard. NAAMS 
light screen stands are approved and acceptable. 
1.3.20.1.1 
Blanking of light screens is prohibited. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 134 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.20.2 
Light screens between Facilities equipment and Tooling equipment shall belong 
to the Tooling equipment supplier unless clear stated otherwise in the equipment 
SOW. 
1.3.20.3 
Light Screen Distance Validation 
1.3.20.3.1 
Light screen placement shall follow the Ford Motor Company Operator/Robot 
Shared Space Light Screen Placement standard. 
1.3.20.3.2 
Each operator light screen application shall be documented with the light screen 
distance calculation worksheet which shows the distance calculation according to 
the equation in the w-EL4 directive. Worksheet is available in TeamCenter under 
Standards and References. 
1.3.20.4 
All Tier 1 Integrators shall follow the requirements of ISO 13855 when calculating 
the minimum safe distance. This equation requires the use of the worst-case 
stopping time of the machine/equipment, which is given by the original equipment 
supplier. When the worst-case stopping time of robot/machine is not used, an 
approved risk assessment is required, and integrators shall plan light screen 
distances using probable machine stopping times utilizing the light screen 
worksheet for line layout planning. Reference the latest version of the light 
screen worksheet for the current E-stop time values. Light screen worksheet is 
available in Teamcenter. 
1.3.20.4.1 
Once the tool is built, the light screen distance validation shall be accomplished 
by videoing (30 frames per second) the machine stopping action while breaking 
the light screen. The video shall include the indicator on the light screen and all 
of the machine motions. At least ten measurements should be taken under the 
worst-case scenario. The highest measured value or the mean plus three 
standard deviations, whichever is greater, should be used. The completed video 
shall be imported into a PowerPoint document. This PowerPoint document shall 
include the receiving plant, machine number, time and date of video, distance 
equation, and names of persons attending. This PowerPoint document shall be 
titled with tool number and included in the Buy-Off documentation. For light 
screen distance calibration reference the Light Screen Video Example. Once the 
minimum safe distance has been determined by video validation, the light screen 
placement is to be adjusted to minimum safe distance. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 135 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.3.21 
Access Hatches and Ladders 
1.3.21.1 
Access hatches to maintenance pits shall be lightweight and shall be sized to be 
easily opened and handled by one person. 
1.3.21.2 
Access hatches shall lock in the upright position. 
1.3.21.3 
Access hatches shall have ladders with extendable handrail. The extendable 
handrail shall lock in the upright position. 
1.3.21.4 
When the access hatch is in the open position, physical barriers shall alert and 
prevent others from accidentally falling into the opening. 
1.3.21.5 
Access hatches shall be located such that sufficient clearance from obstructions, 
aisles, parts, etc. is maintained when the hatch is open, allowing safe access to 
the hatch. 
1.3.22 
Guard Posts 
1.3.22.1 
When guard posts are specified in the SOW this section shall apply. 
1.3.22.2 
Guard posts shall be installed along aisles to protect personnel and equipment 
when they are in close proximity to vehicle traffic or as required to protect 
personnel and equipment from harm or damage. 
1.3.22.3 
Guard post and handrail construction shall conform to the requirements specified 
by National and local regulations and codes. 
1.3.22.4 
Guard post and handrail design, construction and installation must be approved 
by Ford Motor Company prior to fabrication. 
1.3.23 
Operator Walking and Working Surfaces 
All operator walking and working surfaces shall comply with the requirements of 
FAS08-040.

1.3.23.1 
Changes in floor elevation greater than to 1.5 inches shall require 14-gauge 
sheet metal edging painted Safety Yellow such that the trip hazard risk from the 
change in elevation is minimized.  See Typical Platform Drawing below for sheet 
metal edging details. See also Sheet Metal Edging examples below.

1.3.23.2 
Ramps, when specified, shall have angles of 20 degrees to the horizontal or less. 
The surface shall be non-skid material. Predictable contaminants shall be taken 
into consideration when selecting the surface.  Any exceptions shall be reviewed 
by VOME Safety to determine if a hazard could exist with the steeper angle. See 
example below: 
 
Ramp Example 
1.3.23.3 
Ramp surfaces shall conform to GVOSS section 1.3.18.1; "Slip Resistant" Steel 
Floor Material usage. 
1.3.23.4 
Stairways 
1.3.23.4.1 
Stairway step surface material shall conform to GVOSS section 1.3.18.1; “Slip 
Resistant” Steel Floor Material usage.  
1.3.23.4.2 
Stairway nosing shall be painted Safety Yellow for contrast to improve step 
identification. 
1.3.23.5 
VOME Strategy for Wood Flooring (Section 1.3.23.6) and Ergonomic Flooring 
(Section 1.3.23.7). 
• 
VOME engineering will include anti-fatigue flooring (wood or Wearwell) costs 
in facility program funding for NEW workstations. A new workstation is 
defined as a brand-new workstation where one did not exist before or 
significant facility change that includes concrete and/or platform changes.  
• 
Wood or Wearwell flooring will be installed on all mainline workstations, IP 
lines and subassembly workstations to support the operator walk path as 
defined by MODAPTS.  
• 
Steel grating, and non-slip flooring material such as Wearwell can be used 
instead of wood if needed due to environmental conditions (high fluid, air flow 
requirements in booths).  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 138 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
• 
Tire room, MSC/Kitting area, and large pick areas greater than 20 feet walk 
(e.g.: roof pick, engine pick, transmission pick) do not require anti-fatigue 
flooring. 
1.3.23.6 
Wood Flooring 
This section pertains to wood flooring when specified. 
See also Walking Working Surface SPL. 
1.3.23.6.1 
Surface material shall be 5-layer plywood or equivalent. 
1.3.23.6.2 
The walking surface, borders and stripes shall be painted with BASF 
MASTERSEAL® Slip-resistant acrylic coating or equivalent. Color to be BASF 
MASTERSEAL® French Gray #458. 
1.3.23.6.3 
All elevation changes shall be painted with a four-inch yellow stripe. 
1.3.23.6.4 
If beveled edges are used, they shall be 30 degrees to the horizontal. 
1.3.23.6.5 
All leading edges at stairways shall be secured with a metal threshold. 
1.3.23.6.6 
When wood flooring is specified, it shall be constructed as shown in the image 
below. 
 
1.3.23.6.7 
Anti-fatigue matting shall not be placed on top of wood flooring. 
1.3.23.6.8 
Tier 1 Integrators should account for the wood flooring thickness in the 
ergonomic studies and tools design. Wood flooring shall be defined on the 1A 
based upon operator envelope for each station. 
1.3.23.6.9 
It is not required to install wood flooring for buyoffs at Tier 1 Integrator shops. 
This work shall be done in the plant by the plant forces or contractor and 
complete by MP1. 
1.3.23.6.10 Steel platforms should be designed, built and installed prior to tooling buyoff, see 
GVOSS section 1.3.17 for additional details.

1.3.23.7 
Ergonomic Flooring  
This section pertains to Ergonomic Flooring when specified. 
1.3.23.7.1 
Ergonomic flooring shall not be placed on top of wood flooring. 
1.3.23.7.2 
Ergonomic flooring, unless otherwise specified, shall be Wearwell ErgoDeck® 
General Purpose (GP).

Style shall be “Solid”. 
1.3.23.7.4 
Flooring color shall be “Charcoal”.

Perimeter edges of flooring shall be “Yellow”. 
1.3.23.7.6 
Perimeter edging shall be “Ramp” style. See example image below.

Edging shall be affixed to concrete flooring using Tapcon® flat head concrete 
screws. 
1.3.23.7.8 
Edging shall be affixed to elevated steel platforms using flat head sheet metal 
screws appropriate for the application. 
1.3.23.7.9 
All leading edges at stairways shall be secured with a metal threshold.

1.3.23.8 
Operator Workstation Dunnage Risers 
 
 
 
 
 
 
1.3.23.8.1 
The term riser shall be used for any operator elevated working surface that is 1 
standard OSHA stair maximum tread height or less. 
1.3.23.8.2 
The riser shall be wood construction with joists at 16” centers with 3/4" plywood 
walking surface. 
1.3.23.8.3 
The walking surface, borders and stripes shall be painted with BASF 
MASTERSEAL® 658 Slip-resistant acrylic coating or equivalent. 
1.3.23.8.4 
The edges around the perimeter of the riser subject to operator egress into 
PMHV traffic shall be painted with Safety Yellow border and diagonal stripes. 
See image below. 
 
All other edges shall be identified with a solid Safety Yellow border stripe.

1.3.23.9 
Floor Openings 
1.3.23.9.1 
When there is a floor opening in which a person could fall 4 feet (1220 mm) or 
more an approved method for protecting employees from the fall hazard must be 
used. Examples of approved methods are below and should be documented in 
the risk assessment. Other methods may be available and must be documented 
in the risk assessment. 
• Guardrail Systems that meet either regional or OSHA 1910.29 
requirements. 
• Safety Net Systems that meet either regional or OSHA 1926 subpart M 
requirements.

1.3.25 
Moving Equipment in Operator Accessible Floor Areas 
1.3.25.1 
Equipment in operator accessible floor areas, that extends and retracts, shall 
have the pinch point hazard zone around the perimeter identified with alternating 
diagonal (45°) safety yellow and safety black stripes, uniformly spaced with a 
border painted safety yellow (a.k.a. “tiger striping”). Striping must extend at least 
12” from edge of hazard. See example below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
1.3.25.2 
Equipment in operator accessible floor areas, that has semi-automatic motion, 
shall have its path of motion visually identified on the floor by use of black and 
yellow paint stripe at the perimeter of the motion path.

1.4 
VISUAL FACTORY REQUIREMENTS FOR NEW EQUIPMENT 
1.4.1 
Overview 
1.4.1.1 
The purpose of this portion of the standard is to provide a reference of existing 
Visual Factory requirements for new tooling and identify additional requirements 
not already covered in other sections of the GVOSS. Where the requirements of 
this standard conflict with other sections of the GVOSS this standard shall 
prevail. Omission from this section does not relieve responsibility for 
requirements of other GVOSS sections or standards. 
1.4.1.2 
Where requirements exist elsewhere in the GVOSS, the section number is 
provided for reference. The referenced sections should be read in their entirety to 
understand their full scope and application. 
1.4.1.3 
Tier 1 Integrators may use additional labeling beyond that specified in this 
standard. 
1.4.1.4 
Material for labels specified in this standard shall be of material suitable for the 
environment. They shall be resistant to water, chemicals, oil, temperature and 
weld expulsion.  
1.4.1.5 
The location of labels will be concurred with by the Ford ME, VIS and the 
receiving plant. 
1.4.1.6 
Hazard labels shall conform to ISO 3864-2 and ANSI Z535.4. The label shall 
contain a Hazard severity panel, a Symbol panel containing one or more ISO 
7010 hazard (warning) symbols, and a Message panel. 
1.4.1.7 
Brady® offers a complete line of printers, materials and software to print the 
labels used in this section. These systems may be used at the Tier 1 Integrators' 
discretion. 
1.4.1.8 
Tier 2 suppliers shall provide their equipment with all visual factory requirements 
set forth with in this section. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 148 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.4.2 
Signal Words/Hazard Labels. 
1.4.2.1 
Signal Words and Label Layout 
1.4.2.2 
All Signal Words are to be in the native language and the common units of 
measure for the installation site shall be used unless otherwise designated in 
SOW. Examples shown are Reference ONLY 
1.4.2.2.1 
ANSI Z535 Signal Word definitions 
 
Signal Word 
Symbol 
"DANGER" indicates an imminently 
hazardous situation which, if not 
avoided, will result in death or 
serious injury. This signal word is to 
be limited to the most extreme 
situations. 
 
"WARNING" indicates a potentially 
hazardous situation which, if not 
avoided, could result in death or 
serious injury.  
 
"CAUTION" indicates a potentially 
hazardous situation which, if not 
avoided, may result in minor or 
moderate injury. It may also be used 
to alert against unsafe practices. 
 
 
1.4.2.2.2 
The hazard severity panel shall contain the ISO 7010‑W001 general warning 
sign, except where local requirements conflict.

1.4.3.2.8 
Label placement on pipes shall be as shown below. 
 
• 
For uninterrupted straight runs from 40 ft to 80 ft (12m to 24.5m) place a 
label in the center of the run. 
• 
For uninterrupted straight runs over 80 ft (24.5m) labels are to be evenly 
spaced in the run not to exceed 40 ft (12m) between labels. 
1.4.3.2.9 
Placement of labels shall be to assist in the identification of materials and flow 
directions in pipes of pneumatic circuits. This standard shall not be over-
interpreted so that the result is labeling which is cluttered and confusing. 
1.4.3.2.10 
Pneumatic Flow Labels 
1.4.3.2.10.1 Supply line pipes shall have a label. The label shall have the words 
"COMPRESSED AIR" in white capital letters on a blue background. 
 
1.4.3.2.10.2 In addition to the labeling requirements of Section 1.4.1.6 the following parts of 
the air delivery system shall be labeled: 
• 
At the facilities drop ball valve prior to the main air P1 Lockout-Exhaust valve 
a label shall also be placed at the ball valve.  
If there is no ball valve the label shall be placed at the coupling connecting 
the facilities drop to the equipment air service. 
• 
At the P1 Lockout-Exhaust valve.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 160 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
• 
Compressed Air Receivers (Surge Tank) air entering and exiting 
• 
Main Air Header 
• 
Main Air Header Drop to a Pn (n > 1) Lockout-Exhaust valve 
• 
Main Air Header Drops to a p1 Lockout-Exhaust valve 
• 
Air entering a pn (n > 1) to a pn Lockout-Exhaust valve 
• 
Air entering a ball valve 
1.4.3.2.10.3 The following components shall be labeled with an arrow only for circuit flow 
clarification at air “In” and “Out” of the component: 
• 
Filter Separators 
• 
FRL units and Regulators further than 10’-0” from the air Lockout valve. 
• 
Tee’s 
• 
Additional arrows shall be added to complex circuits to add clarification. 
1.4.3.2.11 
Water Flow Labels 
1.4.3.2.11.1 Supply line pipes shall have a label. The label shall have the words "WATER 
SUPPLY" in white capital letters on a green background. 
 
1.4.3.2.11.2 Return line pipes shall have a label. The label shall have the words "WATER 
RETURN" in white capital letters on a green background. 
 
1.4.3.2.11.3 In addition to the labeling requirements of Section 1.4.1.6 the following parts of 
the water system shall be labeled: 
NOTE: The water system is a closed loop system; these requirements apply to

facilities drop to the equipment water supply and return service.

1.4.3.3 
Machine Process Flow identification shall be clearly identified and consist of a 
black arrow with the word "FLOW" in capital letters. The identification shall be 
placed on each side of each station. 
 
1.4.4 
Gage Labels (Marking) 
1.4.4.1 
Gages shall be marked in the following manner: 
• 
Green – Safe, within specified operating range. (For fixed set points use a green 
line to identify the proper gage setting.) 
• 
Yellow – Safe but outside operating range 
• 
Red – Unsafe: continued operation will result in injury or death. 
1.4.4.2 
The method of gage marking shall be the application of label material to the 
inside of the gage face cover for gages that are not fluid filled. Fluid filled gages shall 
have the gage markings on the outside of the gage face cover. Ranges shall be "pie" 
shaped as illustrated in the example images below.

1.4.5 
Lubrication Labels 
1.4.5.1 
Grease Fittings shall be identified with a 1-inch diameter circle colored white. 
 
1.4.5.2 
Grease manifolds shall be labeled with a white tag with the words "GREASE 
MANIFOLD". 
1.4.5.3 
Gearbox oil fill point shall be identified with a 1-inch diameter circle colored 
green. 
An alternate method is to paint the oil fill plug green. 
 
1.4.5.4 
Gearbox Oil Drain Point shall be identified with a 1-inch diameter circle colored 
yellow. 
An alternate method is to paint the oil drain plug yellow.

1.4.7 
Toxicology Labels 
Any material required to be used on equipment for the purpose of maintenance 
which has a MSDS sheet requires a Ford Motor Company TOX number. The 
material manufacturers name, part number, material type and Ford Motor 
Company MATS TOX number shall be displayed on a label and attached to the 
equipment at the point of use on the equipment. Material type should be simple 
words such as; oil, grease, etc. 
 
 
 
The rectangle lamicoid label should be placed at or as near the lubrication fill 
point as possible. 
See Section 1.4.11.4 for robot requirements where lubrication sheets exist for the 
robot.

Engineer will provide). 
•

Effectors, Tool Change Stands, etc., (Ford Manufacturing Engineer will 
provide).

build manual changeover, (Ford Manufacturing Engineer will provide). 
NOTE: For colors provided by the Ford Manufacturing Engineer; these color 
choices are at the discretion of the program team. The choices should be 
coordinated for consistent application throughout the manufacturing systems. 
• 
Pallet Locator Striping

1.4.8.14 
Flex Complaint Tooling 
• 
Paint Colors of Flex Compliant Tooling and Equipment. 
• 
Stenciling/Tagging of Flex Compliant Tooling and Equipment. 
Model specific tool trays, end effectors and tool storage stands shall have a 
model identification paint marking (model ID paint tag) on the tool for a visual aid 
to easily identify what model a specific tool builds. 
• 
Model ID block shall be 75 mm x 150 mm in the die carline color as defined 
in section (WDX20-04M), outlined in a 20 mm black or white band for 
contrast.   
• 
Minimum (1) paint ID block per tool. 
• 
The preferred location for the primary model ID paint block is the lower right-
hand corner near the Standard Ford tool tag.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 169 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
• 
A model ID block shall be visible from working position, storage position 
and/or changeover position; multiple paint ID block markings may be 
required.  
– 
Secondary paint ID blocks may be smaller if required, not to be less than 
50 mm x 50 mm. 
When multiple models are built within the same tool the Model ID paint tag shall 
use the lead model color. 
• 
Lead model is the first model released or the one with the most tooling 
content within the tool.   
– 
This shall be applied consistently within any line or segment.  
• 
The secondary model running within the tool shall be identified by a 25 x 300 
mm paint tag directly below and adjacent to the primary model paint ID tag 
• 
Secondary model units or clamps shall be identified by a minimum of (1) 50 x 
50 model ID paint tag in a visible location on the model specific unit or blade. 
Model ID Paint tag may utilize stenciling for clarification between models or 
variants within a model, i.e. “Step”. vs. “Base” tailgate   or “Moon roof”. vs.  “No 
Moon roof”.

1.4.8.19 
Machine Control Colors. 
Operator Controls Button Colors: 
• 
For new tooling with NextGen controls architecture, follow NextGen   
                    controls specifications. 
 
• 
For new tooling with GVOSS controls architecture, the color codes   
                    shall be: 
Operator Pushbutton Colors: 
GREEN: 
Power On 
RED: Emergency Stop 
WHITE: 
Cycle Start 
BLACK: 
Stop / Off 
BLUE: Reset 
YELLOW: 
Interrupt Cycle 
 
Indicator Colors: 
RED: Emergency 
YELLOW: 
Abnormal 
BLUE: Mandatory action 
GREEN: 
Normal / Safe 
WHITE: 
No specific meaning 
 
(IEC 60204-1:2016 / NFPA 79-2018) 
1.4.8.19.1 
Presence sensing device indication: Presence sensing devices such as operator 
and perimeter light screens shall have an operator control box located near the 
device but outside the hazard zone. It shall have a red indicator mounted on top 
to indicate tripping. The stop function shall be reset by a pushbutton outside the 
hazard zone where the entire zone can be observed by the operator. Muting 
lamps shall be yellow. 
1.4.8.19.2 
Operator/Robot Shared Workspace. 
• For NextGen controls architecture: Any control panel which controls an 
operator / robot shared workspace, shall have a yellow reset button to 
reset and Secondary Circuit fault once fault condition is cleared.  
 
• For GVOSS controls architecture: Operator load, and unload stations

should be equipped with a run stand operator station with the following 
controls: Emergency stop Red with yellow background, Cycle start Zero 
force ergonomic button, Reset Blue, Interupt cycle Yellow, Enable/bypass, 
Selector switch.

1.4.8.19.3.1 When floor mounted cable trays per the risk assessment are determined to be

working surface requirements defined in GVOSS section 1.3.23, FAS08-040, 
OSHA 1910.22, ISO 14122-2 and local regional authority. 
1.4.8.19.3.2 Floor mounted aluminum ladder tray over 16” wide shall incorporate structural 
supports to reduce tray cover deflection when using 12ga steel covers. (See Fig. 
1.). Reference 70ZF-20013207 for support requirements at cover transitions so 
sheet metal covers do not flex. No additional support is required if using ¼ 
aluminum plate.

1.4.11.3 
Operator Load Sheet: An Operator Load sheet shall be made for each operator 
that loads parts in the system. This includes conveyors and subassembly tools. 
The exact format will be determined by the ME, VIS and the Ford Receiving 
Plant. 
The Operator Load sheet should contain the following: 
• 
Illustration of each part to be loaded. 
• 
Part number for each part. 
• 
Name for each part. 
• 
Part load sequence. 
• 
Illustration of parts properly loaded in tool nest or fixture. 
See samples below for examples of different formats. 
The operator load sheet shall be on 11 x 17-inch paper with a plastic laminate 
overlay for protection. 
The sheet shall be attached to the fence line (or appropriate location) in the 
operator workstation where it is visible and readily accessible for reference.

1.4.11.5 
1A9, Lubrication Chart and Lube Placard: A lubrication chart and placard shall be

6. TOX number 
7. "M" Task Reference Number

1.5 
EQUIPMENT DOCUMENTATION 
1.5.1 
Scope 
1.5.1.1 
The scope of this section is to identify the Tier 1 Integrator deliverables to provide 
information to the plant relative to the operation and maintenance of equipment. 
1.5.1.2 
The requirements of this section shall apply to all new model programs without 
regard to scalability, capital projects, and running changes. This applies to all 
new equipment, kits and equipment modifications.  
1.5.2 
Electronic Information System (E.I.S) – General 
1.5.2.1 
An E.I.S. shall be supplied for all programs. The E.I.S. is provided in lieu of 
operator manuals. The function of the E.I.S. is to provide access to information 
necessary to support the operation and maintenance of equipment. 
1.5.2.2 
The Electronic Information System (E.I.S.) application developed by Independent 
Systems Services (ISS) for Ford Motor Company resides on Ford Motor 
Company corporate servers. This system contains VOME Program Electronic 
Information for Body Construction Engineering, Final Assembly, Paint 
Engineering and Low Bay Stamping Assembly.  
1.5.2.3 
The E.I.S. application is to be used by both FNA and IMG regions. Other regions 
must identify in their SOW which E.I.S. content is required and how it is to be 
provided to the plants.

1.5.3 
Tier 1 Integrator Responsibility 
1.5.3.1 
Tier 1 Integrators are responsible for funding their portion of the E.I.S. and shall 
include the development cost in their quote. 
1.5.3.2 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide a single point of contact to manage the 
exchange of information and files to the E.I.S. supplier, VOME Launch Leader 
and their Vender Interface Supervisor. 
1.5.3.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator, E.I.S. supplier and Ford Motor Company VOME Launch 
Leader shall agree on a delivery schedule of documentation to support the 
development and completion of the E.I.S. This shall consist of preliminary 
information to support the development of the E.I.S. architecture and final data to 
populate the system. Delivery of documentation to the E.I.S. supplier shall not be 
considered complete until all finalized "As Built" documentation is provided. 
1.5.3.4 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall supply electronic copies of all deliverable 
documentation to the E.I.S. supplier necessary to support the E.I.S. This shall 
include but is not limited to mechanical drawings, control drawings, Risk 
Assessments, process documentation, Maintenance Extraction Plans, line 
layouts, ECPL Placarding, visual build sheets and all other documentation and 
drawings generated to satisfy the Tier 1 Integrators' contractual obligations to 
Ford Motor Company to satisfy the terms and conditions of their purchase order. 
1.5.3.5 
FIDES data for mechanical drawings shall use the AFXi module of the FDX data 
exchange between suppliers or other Ford Motor Company approved method. 
1.5.3.6 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible for coordinating the delivery of required 
data from Tier 2 and component suppliers. 
1.5.3.7 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide to the E.I.S. supplier all information necessary 
to support the contents required by this standard. 
1.5.3.8 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide to the E.I.S. supplier any Illustrations, 
photographs, exploded views, etc. that can be used to provide additional 
clarification and understanding. 
1.5.3.9 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide the E.I.S. supplier access to Ford Motor 
Company equipment in their construction facility for the purposes of 
documentation, photographing and filming of equipment. 
1.5.3.10 
For Kitted Programs the Tier 1 Integrator shall supply the E.I.S. supplier with lists 
of information relative to incrementally updating the E.I.S to add, remove or 
modify information that will become obsolete. This shall include but is not limited 
to: 
• 
Units to be removed 
• 
Units being added 
• 
Units being modified

The Tier 1 Integrator shall validate the E.I.S. for completeness and accuracy.

1.5.4.1 
The E.I.S. supplier shall be the single point of contact, responsible for acquisition

system. 
1.5.4.3 
The E.I.S. supplier shall review the E.I.S. contents with the VOME and the 
receiving Plant's Launch Teams for completeness and correctness.

1.5.5 
BCE E.I.S. Requirements/Contents 
1.5.5.1 
General Requirements 
1.5.5.1.1 
The E.I.S. SOW shall define the scope of the program work and shall comply 
with the GVOSS requirements where it may differ. 
1.5.5.1.2 
The document E.I.S. Breakdown of Responsibilities identifies the overall tasks 
and responsibilities for the E.I.S. 
1.5.5.1.3 
The document E.I.S. Standard Delivery Timing Matrix shall be used to track 
performance to schedule for E.I.S. deliverables. 
1.5.5.1.4 
The following items will pertain to the Operational/Maintenance Manual. 
• 
All material shall be suitable for reproduction in electronic and printed 
formats. 
• 
All text must be written in both English and the local language spoken at the 
receiving plant or an language agreed to by the VOME Launch Leader, using 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 186 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
common terminology that is understandable by the workers responsible for 
operating and maintaining the machine/equipment.  A glossary is required for 
technical terms, acronyms, and any unusual terminology. 
• 
Illustrations, photographs, exploded views, etc. are to be used to the greatest 
extent possible to provide additional clarification. 
• 
A Table of Contents shall be located at the beginning. 
• 
A Table of Contents is also required for each section and be located at the 
front of each section. 
• 
All text must be clear and legible.  Text font shall be Arial.  
• 
All materials will be formatted in a consistent manner throughout.  Format 
commands should always be used instead of tabs. 
1.5.5.2 
Content and Format Requirements for Procedures:   
• 
Rule #1:  Write procedures using the Action – Location – Acknowledgement 
format.  All procedures shall be written using the Action – Location – 
Acknowledgement format.  Each step in a procedure should begin by telling the 
worker what action to perform in the step (Action).  Then the procedure should 
explain where the action is to take place (Location).  The step should conclude 
by explaining what the machine/equipment will do after the required action is 
performed (Acknowledgement).  
• 
Rule #2:  Write ECPL steps into procedures.  Safety is a primary concern at 
Ford, therefore, all applicable safety precautions, warnings, cautions, and notes 
shall be integrated directly into the steps of the procedures.   
1.5.5.3 
The E.I.S shall be organized as follows:  
• 
Section #1 
Introduction and Overview 
• 
Section #2 
Safety 
• 
Section #3 
Machine Operation 
• 
Section #4 
Troubleshooting Alarms and Fault Codes 
• 
Section #5 
Electrical Maintenance  
• 
Section #6 
Mechanical Maintenance 
• 
Section #7 
Hydraulics & Pneumatics 
• 
Section #8 
Preventive Maintenance 
• 
Section #9 
Subsystem and Component Equipment Information 
• 
Section #10 
Equipment Instruction (Training) Manuals 
1.5.5.3.1 
The system overview shall include system descriptions and layouts necessary to 
acquaint the E.I.S. user of the various systems and system function for those 
systems contained in the E.I.S. Digital photographs and Digital video of the 
system and system operation shall be provided and linked to this section.

1.5.5.3.2 
The system overview shall link to station and tool specific information. This shall 
be the description and layout necessary to acquaint the E.I.S. user of the station, 
station function, tool and tool function within the systems contained in the E.I.S. 
Digital photographs and Digital video of the station, station function, tool and tool 
function shall be provided and linked to these sections. 
1.5.5.4 
Station and tool information shall link to the appropriate mechanical and controls 
drawings. This shall include Kit Sheets for Kitted Programs. 
1.5.5.5 
The mechanical and controls BOM shall link to component information. 
Component information shall consist of manufacturers' literature, specification 
sheets and maintenance information. Digital photographs and Digital video shall 
be provided and linked to the components. 
1.5.5.6 
When interactive drawings are supplied, appropriate details and symbols in the 
mechanical and controls drawings shall link to the component information. 
1.5.5.7 
MFM and PM information shall comply with current Ford Motor Company 
procedures. Upon request, Vender Interface Supervisor or his designated 
representative shall provide current specifications for the MFM and PM 
information.

Upon request the E.I.S. supplier shall submit the E.I.S. information structure, 
illustrating data links and navigation for review and approval. This may be 
accomplished through a presentation upon agreement with Ford Motor 
Company. 
1.5.5.9 
Section #1: Introduction & Overview. This section shall describe what the 
machine/equipment does and how it does it.  If it has multiple workstations, 
describe what happens at each workstation. Additional requirements include:   
• 
Describe the complete sequence of operation. Use illustrations, photographs 
or drawings to help Ford workers understand how the machine/equipment 
works. 
• 
Identify major machine/equipment components and describe their function. 
• 
Describe any automatic gauging that is built into the machine/equipment. 
1.5.5.10 
Section #2:  Safety. This section shall describe all safety devices used in the 
machine/equipment in terms of location, function, and procedure for resetting.  If 
applicable, provide layout of safety installations and safety zones/areas 
associated with the machine/equipment. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 188 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS

1.5.5.11 
Section #3:  Machine Operation. This section shall contain all procedures that 
operators need to know to perform their jobs. It shall cover how to power-up from 
every mode, power-down from every mode, operate in manual mode, automatic 
mode, standby mode, maintenance mode, etc. Procedures shall be included on 
how to engage and recover from an Emergency Stop. Incorporate safety 
precautions directly into the steps of the operating procedures. Cover all the 
following: 
• 
Power-Up Procedures.  Include separate procedures for powering up from 
each power-down mode to each of its power-up mode such as:  
– 
From completely powered down, to power up in the manual mode. 
– 
From completely powered down, to power up in the automatic mode. 
– 
From emergency stop, to powered up in the automatic mode. 
– 
From standby/warm-up mode, to powered up in the automatic mode. 
• 
Power-down Procedures. Separate procedures are needed for powering 
down from each powered-up mode. Typically, document power-down 
procedures for the following:  
– 
From automatic mode to completely powered down. 
– 
From manual mode to completely powered down 
– 
From standby and warm-up modes to completely powered down. 
• 
Safety Procedures.   
– 
Place general safety warnings at the beginning of the procedure.  Be 
sure that they are extremely clear, explicit, and tell the worker exactly 
what they should do to stay safe. 
– 
Incorporate safety precautions, warnings, cautions, and notes directly 
into the steps of the procedures.  Begin steps that contain a safety 
warning with an eye-catching “Safety Alert” in colored text. 
1.5.5.12 
Section #4:  Fault Codes and Alarms 
1.5.5.12.1 
Troubleshooting Fault Codes and Alarms are a critically important area and it 
must cover fault codes and alarms that could come up on the controller (with the 
exception of programming faults). 
1.5.5.12.2 
Identifying faults on the HMI and navigation of screens to locate more specific 
information on the faults shall be covered. 
1.5.5.12.3 
Resetting the Fault or Alarm from the HMI shall be covered.  
1.5.5.12.4 
Navigation back through the HMI screens to restart the machine in Automatic 
Mode shall be covered. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005

• 
Overview of basic electrical controls 
•

Software 
– 
Type Used,

Pin outs for communication cables, 
– 
Samples of ladder diagrams.

Other Components (if applicable), such as:
