Special diagnostics/monitoring systems 
– 
Robots 
– 
Vision systems 
– 
Special Sensors 
• 
Machine wiring design 
• 
How to adjust electrical components 
• 
How to use troubleshooting screens on the PanelView 
• 
Troubleshooting guide 
NOTE:  All applicable safety precautions, warnings, cautions, and notes shall be 
integrated directly into the steps of the Electrical Maintenance procedures.

perform.  This section would typically cover procedures on:

Replacement of:

Belts 
– 
Bearings

1.5.5.16 
Section #8:  Preventive Maintenance.  
1.5.5.16.1 
This section shall contain all preventive maintenance procedures needed to keep 
the machine/equipment in proper operating condition in accordance to the MFM 
Tasks covered in the previous sections. 
1.5.5.16.2 
The procedures shall clearly explain how to perform the preventative 
maintenance tasks.   
1.5.5.16.3 
Preventive maintenance procedures typically cover a variety of cleaning tasks, 
checking lubrication levels, pneumatic pressures, hydraulic pressures, inspecting 
wear parts, replacing filters, checking tension on belts and chains, re-calibrating 
the machine/equipment, replacing batteries, etc.  
NOTE:  All applicable safety precautions, warnings, cautions, and notes shall be 
integrated directly into the steps of the Preventive Maintenance Procedures.

1.6 
ASSEMBLY ERGONOMICS  
1.6.1 
Purpose 
1.6.1.1 
Ergonomics is the science of fitting jobs to people. The goal of ergonomics is to 
reduce sprain and strain type injuries, also commonly referred to as 
Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSD). MSDs can develop in workers when they are 
asked to repeatedly use awkward postures and/or high forces during their daily 
work activities. These activities can involve repetitive reaching, bending over, 
lifting, pushing/pulling, carrying objects, gripping, using continuous force with 
hand/power tools and working with vibrating equipment to name just a few 
activities. The purpose of this standard is to reduce the risk of operators 
sustaining MSDs through the proper ergonomic design of new processes, tooling 
and equipment.  
1.6.2 
Process and Reporting 
1.6.2.1 
As referenced in the Tooling Bid Package, the Tier 1 Integrator is responsible for 
ensuring that every Material Handling System (MHS) tool (hoist, articulating arm, 
assist, pick tool, hand tool, etc.) and workstation designed for Ford Motor 
Company is compliant with Vehicle Operations Assembly Ergonomic Guidelines. 
Compliance to these guidelines is evaluated based on the nature of the work 
being performed using ergonomic design/sign-off checklists.  These checklists 
must be completed by the Tier 1 Integrator at specific buy-off phases of the 
tool/workstation and signed off by the responsible parties noted on the sign-off 
sheets.   
The following are the Assembly Ergonomic Design/Sign-off Checklists which 
must be completed, including signatures, at the specified milestones by the Tier 
1 Integrator: 
• 
Assembly Ergonomic Workstation Design/Sign-off Checklist on ALL 
STATIONS WITH A PRODUCTION OPERATOR. This is a summary of all 
the manual tasks in the workstation to determine the potential risk of injury.  
NOTE: The VOME Ergonomist will make the determination if an operation 
requires any further analysis. Contact VOME for Ergo Program contact 
information. 
• 
Assembly Ergonomic Tooling (MHS) Checklist on all ergonomic aids (tool/ 
hoist/ articulating arm/ assist) being implemented to be used by a Production 
Operator. 
1.6.2.2 
The two Assembly Ergonomic Design/Sign-off Checklists listed above are 
required to be completed and signed off by the responsible parties noted on the 
sign-off sheets at the following buy-off phases: 100% Design, 100% Build Vendor 
Floor (when appropriate, contact VOME lead) and VO & Safety Buy-off Plant 
Floor <J1>. Required signatures differ by buy-off phase and are noted at the 
bottom of each checklist. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 196 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.6.2.3 
At the 100% Design Buy-off Review the Tier 1 Integrator will gather all key 
participants in the tooling/workstation design process to discuss the assembly 
ergonomic deliverables for each assembly process. This can be accomplished in 
either a specific ergonomic kick-off meeting or as a part of an overall kick-off 
meeting. The function of the meeting is to review the assembly process and 
define information, studies, roles and responsibilities and reviews required to 
meet the goal of zero red ergonomic issues on new model programs. The 
meeting should consist of the following personnel: 
• 
Program Ergonomist or suitable alternate.  
• 
Ford Motor Company Manufacturing Engineer. 
Optional participants could include the Ford Motor Company Plant LEC (Local 
Ergonomic Committee) Representative (Proactive Champion). However, travel 
restrictions due to budgeting often limit their participation. 
1.6.2.4 
Accurate tooling and workstation buy-offs result when the workstation is 
replicated to match what will be installed in the assembly plant. The following are 
elements of the workstation design which are deemed to be essential for the Tier 
1 Integrator to replicate for the tooling/workstation buy-off process: 
• 
At design and initial layout of new machinery and equipment, consideration 
shall be given to the surrounding walking and working surfaces:  
– 
Ingress and egress from the workstation free of obstructions which could 
present slip and trip hazards.  
– 
Access to tools, assist devices, parts and other components necessary 
to perform the job.  
– 
Access for staging stock containers.  
• 
Racks/dunnage – accurate racks/dunnage are essential to enable the 
engineer/ergonomist to review the inputs used in the assessment of the 
manual handling activities. If a hoist/assist has been determined to be 
required, accurate dunnage ensures the compatibility between the parts/hoist 
and dunnage/rack can be determined. If a lift or tilt table is required for the 
workstation, it should be available. The availability or lack of availability of the 
dunnage/rack and associated ergonomic equipment must be indicated at 
each buy-off phase on the Assembly Ergonomic Tooling (MHS) Checklist in 
the Rack / Dunnage / Pallet design section or comments box. 
• 
Frame/body/fixture – the unload point and surrounding environment at which 
the part is either manually loaded or installed with a hoist/assist must be 
accurately depicted, including carrier. If a part is loaded to a frame, body, 
fixture, or conveyor, then the Tier 1 Integrator is expected to contact the 
VOME representative and make plans for the necessary items to be provided 
for this tooling review.  
• 
Carriers – When production personnel must access product while the product 
is held in a carrier, an Ergonomic review of the carrier shall be conducted 
during the workstation buy-off. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 197 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
• 
Correct workstation dimensions – the Tier 1 Integrator must ensure that the 
correct dimensions of the actual workstation in the assembly plant are 
replicated for the tooling/workstation review. All working heights and reaches 
that the operator in the plant will be required to perform (from racks, fixtures 
to sub-assemblies, frame, body, etc.) must be accurately duplicated on the 
vendor floor (this includes any required lift and tilt tables).  
• 
Correct support tooling – accurate tooling buy-offs, in particular the 
evaluation of push/pull forces, can only be achieved with actual bridge/rail 
systems and balancers in place.  
• 
Correct parts – accurate parts (weight, dimensions, etc.) are essential to 
determine the effort that is required to manipulate the hoist/assist. 
• 
Time Study Data – accurate operator-machine charts must be available 
which list all pertinent work and corresponding cycle time requirements for 
the operation being assessed. 
1.6.2.5 
To enable all parties to track compliance to the Assembly Ergonomic 
specifications, the Tier 1 Integrator must maintain copies of all Assembly 
Ergonomic Design/Sign-off Checklists for each tool and/or workstation. For Body 
Shop tooling, this is accomplished by the Tier 1 Integrator creating and 
maintaining a stand-alone Assembly Ergonomic "Evidence Book". The contents 
of the Ergonomics Evidence Book/section of the Buy-off Book are as follows:  
• 
Completed, with required signatures, an Assembly Ergonomic Workstation 
Design/Sign-off Checklist on every workstation that has a production 
operator. 
• 
The Ergonomic analysis results/summary from ErgoWeb™ Software. 
• 
When tools from ErgoWeb™ are utilized, analysis results shall be compared 
against the Assembly Ergonomic Guidelines provided in Figure 1.6.6. in this 
document for acceptability. 
• 
Completed, with required signatures, an Assembly Ergonomic Tooling (MHS) 
Checklist on each ergonomic aid (tool/ hoist/ articulating arm/ assist) being 
implemented to be used by a production operator.  
The Ergonomic Evidence Book or Assembly Ergonomics section of the Tooling 
Buy-off book will be part of the equipment buy-off documentation. 
1.6.2.6 
Any aspect of the MHS tooling (tool/hoist/articulating arm/assist/hand tool) and/or 
workstation which is found to be non-compliant with the Ergonomic Checklists 
will be tracked by the Tier 1 Integrator in the Issues Matrix. The matrix will record 
the following:  
• 
The ergonomic requirement(s) which failed the design criteria specified in the 
Assembly Ergonomic Design/Sign-off Checklists,  
• 
The action plans with target completion dates to resolve the issue(s) 
including ergonomic equipment such as lift/tilt tables as well as material 
handling aids such as hoists.

Revision Date: June 08, 2025

1.6.2.7 
Following the Vehicle Operation's DPA5 processes, outlined by Virtual Process 
Engineering, the VOME will coordinate the following with the Tier 1 Integrator: 
• 
Completion of an ergonomic assessment (based on DPA5 checks) for each 
new or modified process. 
• 
Resolution of any ergonomic concerns (failed DPA5 checks) raised during 
the DPA5 process with concurrence of the VOME Program Ergonomist. 
• 
The ergonomics section of the buy-off book will include ergonomics studies 
completed as part of the DPA5 assessments. 
1.6.2.8 
Ergonomics Risk Assessment Tool Standard Design Minimum Specifications can 
be found in the Global Reference of Ergonomic Assessment Tools (GREAT) 
book, located in the Policies, Procedures, Bulletins, and Guidelines section on 
Ford Motor Company Corporate Ergonomics Website:  
https://azureford.sharepoint.com/sites/hr-GlobalOHS/sitepages/home.aspx (Ford 
Intranet access required). 
Contact 
VOME 
Program 
Ergonomist 
for 
Regional 
Material 
Handling 
Requirements. 
Failure to meet Ergonomic Design Specifications outlined in the recommended 
analysis tools is not acceptable under any circumstance and must be recorded 
on the Issues Matrix for issue tracking.  
NOTE: All parts weighing more than 1kg (3lbs) must be included in all ergonomic 
analyses (including NIOSH CLI). Reach zones must also be evaluated for 
compliance.

Ford recognized ergonomic analysis tools: 
•

for Ergo 101 training is done through the My Learning & Development.  
https://www.mylearning.ford.com/ 
• 
(Ford CDSID required to access). Class ID # 18038.

For all EU programs, training is available. 
• 
ErgoWeb Enterprise is Ford Motor Company’s approved software to be used 
in the analysis of select work activities within the design of a workstation. 
• 
ErgoWeb Enterprise requires membership and information can be found at 
https://ergoweb.com/. Access to the software can be found at  
https://enterprise.ergoweb.com/alogin.html. 
 
• 
ErgoWeb - Job Evaluator Toolbox requires membership and can be found at 
https://ergoweb.com. It is accessible by signing on and clicking on the Ergo 
Tools link found on the left-hand side of the web page.

1.6.4 
Timing and Summary of Ergonomic Deliverables  
Table 1.6.4 provides a summary of deliverables for the Tier 1 Integrator, VOME, 
VOME Ergonomist, Plant Launch Team and Local Ergonomic Committee (LEC) 
in regards to proactive ergonomic evaluation of workstations and new tooling. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
           
                         Table 1.6.4 Ergonomics in New Tooling 
1.6.5 
Assembly Ergonomic Design/Sign-off Checklists 
1.6.5.1 
As described in section 1.6.2.1, there are two ergonomic design/sign-off 
checklists sheets which will be completed by the Tier 1 Integrator: Assembly 
Ergonomic Tooling (MHS) Checklist and the Assembly Ergonomic Workstation 
Design/Sign-off Checklist. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 200 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.6.5.2 
Assembly Ergonomic Tooling (MHS) Checklist. This checklist is used to 
document the proactive nature of evaluating ergonomic factors in the equipment 
development cycle. The checklist is to be used to evaluate all ergonomic aids 
(tool/hoist/articulating arm/assist) used by a Production Operator. The objective 
is to have all tooling ergonomically acceptable by Job 1. 
1.6.5.3 
Figure 1.6.5.3 shows a blank Tooling (MHS) Checklist. Letters A through F 
identify key areas. See below for a description of what information is required in 
these areas.  
A 
Buy-off Phase: These are the sign-off points: The Tier 1 Integrator is to have 
resolved all ergonomic issues with the information and resources available at 
the sign-off point. 
B 
Handle Design: Ergonomic line items are related to the handle design, 
working height of the operator, adjustment capability of the handles. These 
potential ergonomic risk factors are to be addressed during the equipment 
development cycle. 
C 
Controls Design: Ergonomic line items are related to the trigger force, and 
width and labeling of the control button. These potential ergonomic risk 
factors are to be addressed during the equipment development cycle. 
D 
Manipulation of Tool: Identifies potential risk related to the force required for 
a one and two arm push / pull, two arm push / pull and lateral movements 
using the tool. These potential ergonomic risk factors are to be addressed 
during the equipment development cycle. 
E 
Workstation Overview: Ergonomic line items are related to operator posture 
while obtaining, transferring and decking part, removal of part from dunnage. 
These potential ergonomic risk factors are to be addressed during the 
equipment development cycle. The section also references the completion 
and delivery of Operator - Machine Charts which include all pertinent work 
on the operation. The tool must be evaluated to ensure it meets the proper 
cycle time requirements for the particular line. 
F 
Rack / Dunnage / Pallet Design: Ergonomic line items are related to rack 
design and compatibility with the tool / lift assist. These potential ergonomic 
risk factors are to be addressed during the equipment development cycle. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 201 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
 
Figure 1.6.5.3 Assembly Ergonomics Tooling (MHS) Checklist 
1.6.5.4 
Assembly Ergonomic Workstation Design/Sign-off Checklist. This checklist is 
used to document the proactive nature of evaluating ergonomic factors in the 
equipment development cycle. The worksheet is to be used to evaluate work 
workstations with production operator and maintenance personnel interaction. 
The objective is to have all workstations ergonomically acceptable by Job 1. The 
assessment of the workstation using the design checklist looks at important 
factors related to product, process and tooling. Examples are listed below: 
• 
Product Related: 
– 
Rack and container design with part orientation. 
– 
Interface or rack or container with hoist/assist. 
– 
Size and weight of parts and assemblies. 
• 
Process Related: 
– 
Manual handling activities.  
– 
Operator walk distance. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 202 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
– 
Ease or difficulty of operator to achieve proper part orientation. 
– 
Operator posture (twisting, bending, reaching, working above shoulder). 
– 
Frequent / repetitive motion. 
• 
Tooling Related: 
– 
Operator lift height. 
– 
Operator reaches. 
– 
Ease or difficulty of operator to achieve proper part orientation. 
– 
Obstructions to the operator. 
– 
Handle design / adjustability. 
1.6.5.5 
Figure 1.6.5.5 shows a blank Workstation Design/Sign-off Checklist.  Letters A 
through E identify key areas. See below for a description of what information is 
required in these areas.  
A. Buy-off Phase: These are the sign-off points. The Tier 1 Integrator is to have 
resolved all ergonomic issues with the information and resources available at 
the sign-off point. 
B. Work Activities: Manual Handling, Assembly and Tool Usage are related to 
the physical demands of work performed by the plant personnel. These 
activities are to be assessed and optimized during the equipment 
development cycle.  
C. Job Element: Contains the job element task that is to be analyzed. 
D. Analysis and Results: This identifies the appropriate ergonomic analysis tool 
to use and the results of that analysis. 
E. Required Signatures: Concurrence that the responsible parties agree with 
the state of the work stations at the time of the review.

ErgoWeb™ – Job Evaluator Toolbox does not provide the user the ability to

tubes on power tools. The Single Point Lesson shown in Figure 1.6.6 provides

Ergonomic Workstation Design/Sign-off Checklist. All power tools must comply

1.6.7 
Summary of the Ergonomics Guidelines for the Decanted Marketplace 
The decanted marketplace document describes the design, assessment methods 
and physical requirements associated with material handling systems.  These 
guidelines are designed to aid engineers and Local Ergonomics Committees 
(LECs) to optimize route design for efficiency while maintaining safety.  These 
guidelines shall be considered the minimum standard and may be satisfied by 
fulfilling more stringent contractual, legal, or local legislated requirements.  It is 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 208 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
the responsibility of the regional engineers and (LECs) to conduct regional 
requirement / law searches that may impact the ergonomics of the DCMP and 
associated systems for moving material from dockside to marketplace to lineside. 
It is recognized that unique situations may arise, and it is therefore 
recommended that the Ergonomist/LEC assess these unique conditions as they 
arise. Summary of the Ergonomics Guidelines for the Decanted Marketplace. 
1.6.8 
Single Point Lesson - Cart Design Reference   
Carts should be evaluated as soon in the development process as possible.  
Certain characteristics can be evaluated during the design phase, others must be 
evaluated during a physical review.   This measurement method and 
specification applies to all carts, trollies or dollies pushed / pulled by human 
operators that are related to ME Programs that go through PA and carts that are 
tagged for repair. Such carts include (but are not exclusive to); Regression, Tear 
Down, Gauging, Rubber Maid and In-Process Carts.  Single Point Lesson – Cart 
Design Reference.

1.8 
INDUSTRY 4.0 TECHNOLOGIES 
1.8.1 
General 
Industry 4.0 This term refers to the intelligent and permanent linking and 
networking of machines and machine-operated processes in industry.  The field 
of digitization, communication and information technology can be used to 
network people, machines and the resulting products with each other and thus 
generate significantly improved productivity.  
1.8.1.1 
The Tier 1 is responsible to develop with the approved component manufacturer 
the component types, locations and collection requirements needed to meet this 
specification. 
1.8.1.2 
Approved GVOSS Controls Architecture . 
1.8.1.2.1 
Approved Next-Gen Controls Architecture. 
1.8.1.3 
The Tier 1 supplier is responsible for the integration and delivery of technologies 
as covered in this section.   
1.8.1.4 
The Ford Condition Based Monitoring (CBM) interface will be used to collect, 
monitoring, analyze, and report on a range of condition metrics designed to 
determine the immediate and predictive health of the equipment it monitors. 
1.8.1.5 
The data collected and sent via the Ford Application Enabled Platform (AEP) 
network to the CBM Dashboard shall follow GMS02-700AM IIOT Common Data 
Model Standard for Plant Floor Devices. 
1.8.2 
Vibration Sensors 
1.8.2.1 
Vibration sensors shall communicate to the manufacturers’ base station/gateway 
device. For use cases in this section, approved vibration sensor solutions require 
the ability to wholly support Ford owned/controlled applications.  They shall 
Create, Communicate, Process data within Ford’s control.   With written 
exception only, solutions that require service outside of Ford will be considered. 
1.8.2.2 
Vibration sensors shall have the capability to measure temperature.  
1.8.2.3 
The installation assets which are deemed Critical Path include Underbody, 
Bodyside Main, Framing, Closure Hang, and Box Line. Also included are the 
required path transfer equipment between the listed tooling systems and the 
required path transfer equipment from Metal Finish to Delivery to Paint  
1.8.2.3.1 
Vibration sensors shall be installed on the motors of the following equipment: 
PRBs, VDL, Facility Turntables, Shuttles, Cross Transfers, Two Strands, Roller 
Flights and Hem Press Motors. Any additional equipment to be monitored shall 
be identified in the program SOW. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 1 - GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 211 of 212 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
1.8.2.4 
Tier 1 responsibilities 
1.8.2.4.1 
The Tier 1 is responsible for procuring the sensor hardware and base / gateway 
device from the Approved GVOSS Components List. 
1.8.2.4.2 
The Tier 1 installation shall be in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions 
for the make/model selected.  
1.8.2.4.3 
The Tier 1 is responsible to implement data communication and collection from 
the sensors to the sensor manufacturer’s base station/gateway device.  This will 
be performed in accordance with the manufacturer’s guidelines for configuration.   
1.8.2.4.4 
If wireless data communication is used, wireless communication shall comply 
with corporate wireless networking team. The designated Approved wireless 
specifications provided in the Ford Wireless Communication Protocol document 
shall be adhered to. 
1.8.2.4.5 
The vibration monitoring system from sensor to data collector shall be functional 
and validated at Tooling Buyoff at the Tier 1. 
1.8.3 
Rockwell Embedded Logic Solution 
1.8.3.1 
The Rockwell Embedded Logic Solution is a software solution capable of using 
Allen Bradley PLCs to transmit IIOT data to an upper-level MQTT broker.  IIOT 
data is compiled by the plc into a JSON payload, aligned to the Ford Common 
Data Model (CDM) GMS02-700AM and then publishes to an MQTT broker. 
1.8.3.2 
The Rockwell Embedded Logic Solution can be used for collection of IIOT data 
message types MachineData, PartData, MachineConfig, or MachineState. 
1.8.3.3 
To use the Rockwell Embedded Logic Solution, use of Allen Bradley 
ControlLogix L83/L84 PLC is required.  Use of CompactLogix 5380/5480 shall be 
approved by Ford VOME Controls.

2.1 
APPROVED PARTS 
2.1.1 
This section contains lists of approved components, equipment and 
manufacturers suitable for use in Ford Motor Company equipment. 
2.1.2 
Use of components, equipment and manufacturers not approved without 
submission and approval of an Action Request Form requesting a Deviation prior 
to sourcing, will result in mandatory replacement of the components and 
equipment by the supplier at the supplier’s expense. 
2.1.3 
The sources are listed alphabetically and do NOT indicate preference of product 
or manufacturer. 
2.1.4 
Controls component manufacturers used on equipment shall be consistent with 
those specified by the Ford Motor Company VOME Program book. When there is 
no program book the Ford Motor Company VOME P&SD Senior Controls 
Engineer or VIS for the program shall identify approved component 
manufacturers. 
2.1.5 
The Ford Motor Company VOME Senior Controls Engineer for the program shall 
identify any applicable Standard Ford controls drawings to be used. 
 
2.1.6 
Approved Sources of Electrical Equipment 
2.1.6.1 
All equipment and components shall be approved by a National Recognized 
Testing Laboratory. 
2.1.6.2 
All components shall be UL or CSA listed or equivalent based on region (i.e. CE 
for Europe). 
2.1.6.3 
Any substitution to current designs which is not physically/functionally 
interchangeable shall be approved in writing by appropriate Structures Standards 
Controls Design Follow-Up Personnel. 
2.1.6.3.1 
Approved Next-Gen Controls Architecture Electrical Components.  
2.1.6.3.2 
Approved GVOSS Controls Architecture Components. 
2.1.6.4 
Removed 
2.1.6.5

All Components shall comply with w-EL10 and w-E1-QSSL (Electrical Safety 
Qualified Safety Source List).

2.1.11 
Purchase-Alter Components 
A component is a Purchase-Alter if it is finished and usable as purchased from 
the component manufacturer, but altered by the Tier 1 Integrator or Tier 2 
equipment manufacturer in such a manner as to: 
1. Render it unavailable from the original component manufacturer. 
2. Replacements for the component can only be purchased from the Tier 1 
Integrator or Tier 2 equipment manufacturer. 
2.1.11.1 
Purchase-Alter components are prohibited. 
2.1.11.2 
Purchase-Alter components are allowed only after the submission and approval 
of an Action Request Form – Deviation. 
2.1.11.3 
Any Action Request Form – Deviation submitted for Purchase-Alter components 
must be accompanied with a detailed engineering analysis and explanation of 
why the component must be altered and what the benefit of the alteration is. 
2.1.11.4 
Action Request Forms – Deviation submitted for Purchase-Alter components that 
are the result of lack of engineering foresight shall be rejected. 
2.1.11.5 
All Purchase-Alter components shall be maintained in inventory by the Tier 1 
Integrator or Tier 2 equipment manufacturer who altered the component.  
2.1.11.6 
Each Purchase-Alter component shall have a detailed "Plan-for-Every-Part" for 
ordering and delivery to the using Ford Motor Company plant when required for 
Warranty and non-Warranty replacement. This shall include: 
2.1.11.6.1 
24/7 ordering contact information 
2.1.11.6.2 
24/7 shipping information and plan to support plant delivery requirements 
2.1.11.6.3 
Pre-negotiated pricing for non-warranty replacement 
2.1.12 
Purchase-Make Details 
A component is Purchase-Make if it is purchased from a component 
manufacturer and additional manufacturing processes are required by the Tier 1 
Integrator or Tier 2 equipment manufacturer in order to use it. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 11 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.1.12.1.1 
Additional machining operations, such as adding mounting holes, shaft bores and 
keys. 
 
Examples: rack gears, pinion gears, sprockets, shafting. 
2.1.12.2 
The Tier 1 Integrator and Tier 2 equipment manufacturers shall create an 
electronic file of all Purchase-Make detail drawings in a readable pdf format. 
2.1.12.3 
The individual file names shall be the part numbers as listed in the Industrial 
Materials Component List.  
2.1.12.4 
This electronic file shall be used for the Industrial Material ordering process, so 
the details do not require to be ordered from the Tier suppliers. 
2.1.12.5 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible for obtaining the electronic files from 
the Tier 2 suppliers. 
2.1.12.6 
The electronic file shall be delivered to the appropriate plant Area Manager. 
2.2 
DEVELOPMENT OF DRAWINGS 
2.2.1 
Measurement Systems  
2.2.1.1 
General 
2.2.1.1.1 
All details that contact the product, i.e., rest locators, locating pins, clamp fingers, 
etc., shall be dimensioned in metrics. 
2.2.1.1.2 
All fabricated and/or machined details, i.e., bases, stanchions, "L" blocks, fitting 
spacers, rough locators, etc., shall be dimensioned in metric. 
2.2.1.1.3 
All fasteners, i.e., screws, dowels, etc. will be metric. Fastener torque values to 
be in Newton Meters. 
2.2.1.1.4 
All Ford standard components and commercially available items shall remain to 
existing standards. 
2.2.1.1.5 
All other commercial components shall be metric when feasible.  
2.2.1.1.6 
Transfers within a tooling system will have their construction dimensioned in 
metrics.  Travel distance, both horizontal and vertical, dimensioned in metrics. 
2.2.1.1.7 
Ratings for calculating rotary devices in inch or foot pounds shall remain to 
existing U.S. customary unit standards. 
2.2.1.1.8 
Identification of utility drops and locating production equipment on installation 
drawings shall use U.S. customary units (if applicable) and equivalent metric 
dimensions for interface with plant facilities. 
2.2.1.1.9 
All welding equipment, machine control components, i.e., electrical, hydraulic, 
pneumatic, cooling, lubrication and their related components shall remain to 
existing standards. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 12 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.2.1.1.10 
All pneumatic and electric motor driven hand tools, i.e., drill motors, impact 
wrenches, nut runners, etc., will be retained as purchased. 
2.2.1.1.11 
All new measuring dials, gauges, or other similar devices will be ordered with 
metric capability. 
2.2.1.1.12 
Structural shape stock sizes, i.e., tubing, angles and channels, if in inches shall 
remain in inches. 
2.2.1.2 
Policy – U.S. Customary Unit Standards Application: Automation tooling for 
products designed to U.S. customary unit standards, new or revised, will be 
designed in U.S. customary units. 
2.2.1.3 
Policy – Metric Standards Application: Welding and automation tooling for 
products designed in metric (millimeters) will be designed using metric standards 
as per Ford Motor Company Policy and Metric Guidelines listed below. 
2.2.1.4 
Metric Identifications/Definitions 
• 
Hard Metric Definition: Dimension in millimeters established in metric and not 
as a result of conversion from inches to equivalent metric. 
• 
The millimeter unit will be used for identifying metric dimensions.   
– 
Example: 
20.0 = Twenty Millimeters 
• 
All other units of measurement will remain in U.S. customary unit standard. 
• 
To identify a number less than 1.0, use a zero in front of the decimal point. 
– 
Example: 
0.5 
• 
Accuracy: It shall be noted that equivalent metric dimensions require one 
less decimal place than the comparable inch dimension. 
• 
Practical Tolerances. The selection of tolerances to specific application is 
controlled by part print specifications and/or function of components as 
applied to the equipment. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 13 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.2.1.5 
Assembly Tooling Dimensional Tolerances 
Use the NAAMS Global Standard Components - Assembly, Chapter A; Design 
Drawing Standards and Tolerances Section A-6, tolerances stickers. These are 
repeated below for reference: 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 14 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.2.1.6 
Metric Grid Lines: 
• 
Grid line spacing on part prints will be in increments of 100 mm.  The vertical 
"O" line is a plane in front of vehicle 2000 mm in front of dash panel face. 
• 
The horizontal "O" line is a plane below tire level 500 mm below top of frame. 
• 
The center line of vehicle will remain as the "O" line for inboard - outboard 
dimensions. 
2.2.1.7 
Identification of Grid Lines: 
• 
Metric grid lines will be shown on the tooling layouts in all primary views and 
sections to maintain part orientation.  The metric unit will be encircled. 
• 
Grid lines that are whole numbers do not require unit identification and do not 
require the use of a decimal point and zeros. 
• 
Grid lines that are not whole numbers will require one decimal place.  
2.2.2 
Numbering and Identification Conventions 
2.2.2.1 
Tool Numbers shall follow GMS02-727 for Manufacturing Data Numbers. 
2.2.2.2 
Reference the GVOSS BCE Tool Number Flow Chart and Component Lettering 
System which also provides the CPSCII Cross Reference Table and an overview 
of the Tool Number assignment process used in Section 2.2.3. 
2.2.2.3 
Station Numbers shall be identified using (3) digits in all filenames, 
documentation and stenciling. 
Example: Station “5” would be identified as Station “005”. 
2.2.2.4 
Robot Numbers shall be identified using (2) digits in all filenames, documentation 
and stenciling.  
Example: Robot “1” would be identified as Robot “01”. 
2.2.2.5 
Operation Numbers shall be a combination of the CPSC II, I.E. Number and the 
station number, separated by a hyphen (“- “). 
Example: Underbody Respot, Station 10 would be identified as operation 
7Y-010. 
Note: For the purpose of tagging and stenciling the following terms are 
sometimes used interchangeably. 
− Operation Number and Station Number 
− CPSC II, I.E. Number and GSPAS Code 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 15 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.2.3 
Tool Numbering Process for Vehicle Operations Engineering Assembly 
Tools 
2.2.3.1 
Requirements for Tooling Numbering System 
2.2.3.1.1 
All Tool Numbers shall be generated from GSPAS, as it is the official Process 
Documentation Tool for Assembly Tooling. GSPAS keeps track of used numbers 
and delivers the next available number globally maintaining uniqueness. It will 
generate an 8-digit number based on Tool Classification (e.g., 154ZF-12345678, 
156ZF-…, 016ZF-…, 049ZF-…, 056ZF-…, 070ZF…, 122ZF …, etc.). 
2.2.3.1.2 
Unique identification numbers are required for all the robots in the body shop. 
2.2.3.1.3 
Each of these numbers shall be unique so the designs can be stored and tracked 
in Team Center. 
2.2.3.1.4 
Ford Motor Company DOES NOT want to issue a unique “016ZF” Number for 
each component. Suppliers shall use the letter designation listed in GVOSS 
2.2.2.2. A more detailed explanation of this method is explained in the following 
sections. 
2.2.3.1.5 
When kitting an existing tool, changing or adding some tooling details use the 
original tool number. 
2.2.3.1.6 
When installing new tools for a new model to an existing line e.g. tool trays, end 
effectors etc. A new tool number 016ZF-12345678 that is to cover all added tool 
in particular segment e.g. 6X, 6Y etc. is required. 
2.2.3.2 
Numbering Process for Lines 
2.2.3.2.1 
General Information 
2.2.3.2.1.1 
For each Line there will be a 154ZF number for what will be considered 
Assembly Equipment in Line. 
2.2.3.2.1.2 
There will be a 016ZF number pulled for the line that will account for the product 
specific tooling that will be used in the line. (e.g., 016ZF-55555555) (See Figure 
2.2.3.2.f). 
2.2.3.2.1.3 
Examples of product specific tooling that will be part of the line include: 
• Carrier Hooks  
• Fixtures  
• Load Assists  
• End Effectors  
• Mechanical Loader  
• Nest Table  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 16 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• Pedestal Welder Stand  
• Robot End Effectors  
• Sealer Stand  
2.2.3.2.1.4 
Each Sub Assembly feeding the line will have its own 156ZF or 154ZF and 
016ZF Number (e.g., 156ZF-76598012 & 016ZF-12345678). 
2.2.3.2.2 
Pallet Lines 
2.2.3.2.2.1 
If the line is made up of 12 Roller Beds (Station’s #10 (010) thru #120), with a 4-
Unit storage surge between the 6th (Sta # 60) and 7th (Sta # 70) roller beds, then 
the 154ZF will cover all the roller beds in the line including the drives and all the 
necessary controls. Each roller bed in the line will have a 056ZF number, as a 
standard component, based upon if it is a drop and locate, or a roll-thru station. 
2.2.3.2.2.2 
The surge will also have a 056ZF number as a standard component. (e.g., 
154ZF-22222222 – See Figure 2.2.3.2.a). 
 
154ZF-22222222 
 
Figure 2.2.3.2.a 
 
 
 
2.2.3.2.3 
Non-Pallet Lines 
2.2.3.2.3.1 
In this scenario the line is made up of a combination of stations. It is a group of 
multiple cells (Type #1 thru #13) that can be considered as a line or sub-
assembly.  (See Figure 2.2.3.2.b, e.g., Dash or Door Line). 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 17 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.2.3.2.3.2 
The transfer between cells is either manual with magazines or with robots etc. 
that are considered part of the cells. In this case there will be a 154ZF line 
number made up of cells. The 154ZF will cover the assembly of the cells 
including fencing, trunnion, turntables, tip-ups, slides, etc. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure 2.2.3.2.b 
 
 
2.2.3.3 
Numbering for Product Specific Tool Designs 
2.2.3.3.1 
For new and/or kitted tooling lead by VOME the Ford Motor Company 
Manufacturing Engineer generates a tool number for a sub-assembly or line from 
GSPAS (016ZF-12345678). 
• For Plant Projects the Ford Motor Company PVT Engineer generates a 
tool number for a sub-assembly or line from GSPAS (016ZF-12345678). 
Ford Motor Company Manufacturing Engineer generates a tool number for 
a sub-assembly or line from GSPAS (016ZF-12345678). 
2.2.3.3.2 
The complete tool number shall be shown on drawings and built tools for tracking 
purposes.  
2.2.3.3.3 
Selection of station numbers shall be concurred with by the Ford Motor Company 
VOME Design Follow-Up Engineer at or before 50% design review to deter 
duplicate station numbering. (The review shall occur PRIOR to Controls Design 
so duplication does not flow through to all activities.) 
2.2.3.3.4 
For identical assembly lines located in different plants a separate tool number 
shall be assigned for each line. 
2.2.3.3.5 
Refer to Stenciling/Tagging of Flex Compliant Tooling and Equipment for 
numbering specifications and examples 
2.2.3.3.6 
Stations are numbered 010, 020, 030 and the tooling associated with each 
station will be assigned as below. 
1. 016ZF-12345678-010 for Station 10 and would have an Operation Number of 
6W-010 in FIDES for Framing Preclamp (CPSC II, I.E. Number 6W) 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 18 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2. 016ZF-12345678-020 for Station 20 and would have an Operation Number of 
6W-020 in FIDES for Framing Preclamp (CPSC II, I.E. Number 6W) 
3. 016ZF-12345678-030 for Station 30 and would have an Operation Number of 
6W-030 in FIDES for Framing Preclamp (CPSC II, I.E. Number 6W) 
2.2.3.3.7 
Stand-alone single station sub-assemblies will have their own 016ZF tool 
numbers with their own station number. See also Station Numbering Examples. 
2.2.3.3.8 
For lines that are R.H. or L.H. the letter “R” or “L” will be added after the station 
number. 
2.2.3.3.9 
Robot Numbering (Example): 
1. 6X-020-01, 6X-020-02, 6X-020-03, 6X-020-04 
2. 6X; CPSC Process Code for Framing Dimensional Setting 
3. 020; Station Number 
4. 01, 02, 03 and 04; Robot Numbers in the Station. 
5. 6X-020R-01 and 6X-020L-01 for right and left lines 
6. In the case of parallel identical R and L Lines (RH Line A and RH Line B) the 
robot numbers will be 6X-020AR-01, 6X-020BR-01, 6X-020AL-01 and 6X-
020BL-01. 
NOTE:  When there are more than 2 parallel lines, each shall be identified with a 
subsequent sequential alphabetical character, (i.e. C, D, E, etc.). 
NOTE:  Refer to Vehicle Operations Standard Robot Numbering System Drawing 
Number 56ZF-28495 for Robot Numbering Set-Up.  
2.2.3.3.10 
A robot which has a material handling end effector, the mechanical design for the 
end effector will be stored per example format: 
1. 016ZF-12345678-030-1E1 and would have an Operation Number 6W-030 in 
FIDES for Framing Preclamp (CPSC II, I.E. Number 6W) 
2. 016ZF-12345678-030-2E1 and would have an Operation Number 6W-030 in 
FIDES for Framing Preclamp (CPSC II, I.E. Number 6W) 
NOTE: The above are examples of two robots (#1 and #2) in Station 30 that 
have end effectors. In case Robot 1 has two end effectors (w/tool changer) 
they will be 1E1 and 1E2. 
NOTE:  Using the above robot example (016ZF-12345678-030-1E1), If an 
existing End Effector is replaced due to a new program kit, a letter will be 
added to the end of the Station Number:  016ZF-12345678-030-1E1A.  Each 
new kit will generate a concurrent letter – A, B, C, D...     
2.2.3.3.11 
A pedestal weld gun used with the MH robot will have a CAD mechanical design 
stored per example format: 
1. 016ZF-11221122-010-2W1 and would have an Operation Number 8D-010 in 
FIDES for Underbody Auxiliary Floor (CPSC II, I.E. Number 8D).   
2. Figure 2.2.3.6.a shows an example of a MH Robot #2 in Station #10 used for 
welding the subassembly.

If a MH robot has two pedestal welders, the pedestal welders will be numbered

016ZF-12345678-010-1W1 and would have an Operation Number 8C-010 in

2.2.3.3.13 
A sealer stand that needs to be designed for a MH robot will have a CAD 
mechanical design stored per example format:  
1. 016ZF-12345678-030-3S1 and would have an Operation Number 8A-030 in

Station Number:  016ZF-12345678-030-3S1A.  Each new kit will generate a 
concurrent letter – A, B, C, D...     
2.2.3.3.14 
A nest (holding) table that needs a design will be numbered per example format: 
1. 016ZF-12345678-160-3N1 and would have an Operation Number 8X-160 in 
FIDES for Underbody Dimensional Setting (CPSC II, I.E. Number 8X). 
2. 016ZF-12345678-160-4N1 and would have an Operation Number 8X-160 in 
FIDES for Underbody Dimensional Setting (CPSC II, I.E. Number 8X). 
NOTE:  Contrary to the above rule, if Station # 160 is the last station in the 
line, and Robot #1 in station is picking up a part from a nest station running it 
through an operation (pedestal weld sealer etc.), and then putting the part in 
another nest station, and there is no unloading it from the second table, then 
the following numbers apply: the first table will be 016ZF-12345678-160-1N1 
as described above and the drop off table will be 016ZF-12345678-160-1N2. 
NOTE:  Using the above example (016ZF-12345678-160-1N1), If a tool is 
replaced due to a new program kit, a letter will be added to the end of the 
Station Number:  016ZF-12345678-160-1N1A.  Each new kit will generate a 
concurrent letter – A, B, C, D...     
2.2.3.3.15 
Robots that pick up a part or assembly from one fixture (STA) or nest table and 
load it to another fixture (STA) or nest table is assigned to the station it is loading 
into. See examples in Figures 2.2.3.6.b.

2.2.3.3.16 
A magazine or conveyor that is supplied by the tooling vendor will be numbered 
per example format: 
1. 016ZF-12345678-010-M1 
2. 016ZF-12345678-010-M2 
2.2.3.3.17 
Figure 2.2.3.6.b is an example of three magazines in Station 060. All magazines, 
conveyors, etc. provided by facilities will have the standard 016ZF numbers. In 
this case if part holding details or block designs need to be provided to facilities 
then the above numbers will be used to store the designs.   
2.2.3.3.18 
All Magazines, conveyors, etc. provided by facilities will have the standard 016ZF 
numbers. If part holding details or block designs need to be provided to facilities, 
then the above numbers will be used to store the designs. 
2.2.3.3.19 
Body Skids in System will have a 049ZF tool number assigned.   
2.2.3.3.20 
Body Skid Checking Fixture will have a 016ZF tool number assigned.  For 
example: 016ZF-12345678 
2.2.3.3.21 
Body vision systems in a line will have the same 016ZF tool number as the line 
with the letter “VN” assigned after the station number. For example: 016ZF-
12345678-250-VN1 
NOTE:  Figure 2.2.3.6.a, Example 3 has an example of a vision system 
(Perceptron) in Station 250 of the line. 
2.2.3.3.22 
A pallet vision system which will be off-line will have its own tool number. For 
example: 016ZF-32145876-010-VN1 
2.2.3.3.23 
Carrier hooks will have a designation per example format: 016ZF-12345678-M. 
2.2.3.3.24 
Carrier hook checking fixture will have a designation per example format: 016ZF-
12345678. 
2.2.3.3.25 
For a turntable or trunnion, the tooling plate designs will be numbered and stored 
per example format: 
 
       (6) SIDED Turntable:  
 
        (4) SIDED Turntable: 
1.   016ZF-12345678-020-1T1A   
      016ZF-12345678-020-1T1A

3.   016ZF-12345678-020-1T3C  
      016ZF-12345678-020-1T3C 
4.   016ZF-12345678-020-1T4D  
      016ZF-12345678-020-1T4D 
5.   016ZF-12345678-020-1T5E

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
NOTE:  Using the above example (016ZF-12345678-020-1TA), If a tool is 
replaced due to a new program kit, a letter will be added to the end of the Station 
Number:  016ZF-12345678-020-1T1AA, 016ZF-12345678-020-1T4DA.  Each 
new kit will generate a concurrent letter – A, B, C, D...     
2.2.3.3.26 
For common tool plates used on separate faces of trunnion or turntable, follow 
the above naming convention. 
2.2.3.3.27 
The above are examples of tool plates on a turntable or trunnion at Station 20 in 
the line with two unique tool plates, 1T1A and 1T1B. Figure 2.2.3.6.c shows 
examples of tool plates on a turntable or trunnion as a stand-alone sub assembly 
with two unique tool plates, 1T1A and 1T1B. 
2.2.3.3.28 
Turntable and Trunnion controls and robot numbering reasons: 
1. Zones around the turntable or trunnion will be numbered 1, 2 etc.  
2. Robots in the stop stations will then be numbered based on these stop station 
numbers (e.g., 5B-010-31, 5B-010-32). 
3. The zone in which the robot performs its work shall determine the number 
assigned to that robot, and not the physical location of the robot.  
4. 2.2.3.6.c; Example 1 has an example of a 4-sided turn table. 
5. 2.2.3.6.c; Example 2 has an example of a 6-sided turn table. 
2.2.3.3.29 
Back-up robot weld gun identification: 
1. Weld guns will be numbered 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, etc. regardless of what side the 
vehicle guns are on. 
2. The robot number is the item that differentiates the weld guns from one side 
of the line to the other (e.g., left hand robot is #2, right hand robot is #1). 
2.2.3.4 
Control Drawing Numbering, ACAD File Naming Convention, Standard Title 
Block and Standard Tool Numbering 
2.2.3.4.1 
Control Drawing Packages Shall adhere to the following drawing conventions.  
For Next-Gen lines: ACAD Control Drawing Packages (1A5, 1A6, 1A7, 1A8, 
1A9) shall adhere to the following drawing number conventions, Next-Gen ACAD 
File Naming Conventions, and attached Ford Standard Title Block and Standard 
Tool Numbering Updates. 
For GVOSS lines: Eplan Control Drawing Packages shall adhere to the attached 
GVOSS Guidelines, GVOSS Naming conventions, and GVOSS Standard and 
Ford Title Block. 
2.2.3.5 
General Rules for Numbering 
2.2.3.5.1 
In the case of kitting existing tools, use the original numbering strategy that was 
used when the tools were developed, and add a concurrent letter – A, B, C, D….. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 22 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.2.3.5.2 
If there are two identical parallel lines (for example framing buck or respot lines) 
then they would have only one common tool number. All tooling in the lines will 
have only one set of design files. The only number changes will be for the robot 
numbers and maybe for controls. For example: 
1. The robots in Line A will be 6X-030A-01 and 6X-030A-02. 
2. The robots in Line B will be 6X-030B-01 and 6X-030B-02. 
2.2.3.5.3 
If a line is split into 2 Segments A and B (B following A) use the same tool 
number for both segments and number, the stations in Segment B starting with 
the sequential station number after the last station in Segment A. For example:  If 
Segment A has Stations 010 thru 050, then the first station of Segment B will be 
Station #060. 
2.2.3.5.4 
In Framing, the Body Buck, Roof Buck and Respot Lines would be considered as 
separate lines and will have separate tool numbers. The respot line could be in 
two segments in which case they would have the same tool number and 
consecutively numbered stations. 
2.2.3.5.5 
Use of process codes for stations: All stations on the Framing Body Buck Line, 
Roof Buck Line, UB Main (Marriage) Line or Bodyside Main Line would have the 
respective process codes. (e.g., All stations in the Buck line would be 6X-010, 
6X-020….6X-050…etc.).  
NOTE:  Even though Station 50 on the line is a Respot Station it will be a 6X and 
not a 6Y. This is because there will be a Station 50 on the Framing Respot Line 
which will then create two Stations 6Y-050, resulting in duplicate robot numbers.  
2.2.3.5.6 
For tools that are symmetrically opposite, two consecutive numbers will be 
assigned for assembly equipment and tool number example as shown below: 
1. Right Hand  
Equipment Number: 156ZF-77443270 
Tool Number: 016ZF-66776678  
2. Left Hand 
Equipment Number: 156ZF-77443271 
Tool Number: 016ZF-66776679 
2.2.3.5.7 
In some cases, there is a need to add a couple of stations in the middle of an 
existing line/process: 
1. Example 1:  There is an existing line with stations 7F_010,7F_020, 7F_030, 
7F_040 and 7F_050 and there is a need to add two stations / operations 
between Station 7F_030 and 7F_040. In this case the two new added stations 
can be numbered anything between 030 and 040. Preferably 7F_033 and 
7F_036. This gives Ford the capability in the future to add other stations on 
either side of 33 and 36. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 23 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2. Example 2:  There is an existing line with stations 7F_002, 7F_004, 7F_006, 
7F_010, 7F_020, 7F_030 etc., and there is a need to add two stations / 
operations between Station 7F_004 and 7F_006. In this case there is only 
one digit between 4 and 6 and so the stations are numbered 7F_005.0 and 
7F_005.5. This gives Ford the capability in the future to add other stations on 
either side of 005.0 and 005.5 if necessary.

2.2.4 
Drawings and Standards 
All Standard Drawings are located in Team Center. The drawing update process 
shall be posted in Team Center. 
2.2.4.1 
The supplier shall fill out and complete a unit sequence specification sheet for 
every unit. Information to include: the at rest position of every cylinder (including 
weld guns), a sketch of each unit, complete with written sequence of operation by 
cylinder number, limit switches, cylinder functions and cubic inch displacement of 
each cylinder. Welding information required is: transformer size (with gun 
number), metal stack up, gun mechanical ratio, tip force, weld time, secondary 
amps required, and primary amps. (For each weld gun). (Reference the unit 
sequence specification sheet). After initial sheets are sent, revisions shall be 
highlighted and recorded in a change column. 
2.2.4.2 
An approval by a Ford Motor Company representative on a drawing copy related 
to machinery, tooling, equipment or controls constitutes only a concurrence in the 
general design, layout, or function. Drawing approval does not constitute 
approval of design details or adequacy of the unit to perform to the specifications. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 28 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.2.4.3 
The responsibility for the design, strength of materials and the correct functioning 
of the complete unit in accordance with the requirements remain solely with the 
seller supplier. Preliminary designs require approval prior to proceeding with the 
final designs and details. Revised prints shall be submitted for change 
concurrence. All changes or modifications found necessary to bring the unit 
within standards remains the sole responsibility of the supplier. 
2.3 
MACHINE DESIGN 
2.3.1 
General Mechanical Design 
2.3.1.1 
Drawings 
2.3.1.1.1 
The mechanical and controls drawing shall have a complete Bill of Materials 
listing for component ordering information. The BOM on the drawing MUST align 
with the Industrial Materials Component List and reference the original 
manufactures component part number. 
2.3.1.1.2 
The term Layout Drawing may refer to a 154Z, 156ZF or 003ZF Layout Drawing. 
2.3.1.1.3 
Master Line Layout 
2.3.1.1.3.1 
For 1A and Master Line Layout Drawings, refer to the Ford Land Facilities 
Construction Specifications, Bidding Requirements - Section 00210 and its 
attachments. 
2.3.1.1.3.2 
The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible for the accuracy of facilities drops (Electrical, 
Pneumatic and Water) call-outs they enter onto the Master Line Layout for their 
systems. The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible for costs incurred due to 
incorrect call-outs. 
2.3.1.1.3.3 
The Power Drop Legend shall be used to identify plant facilities service drops 
and type of drop on the Layout Drawings. 
2.3.1.1.3.4 
The Tier 1 integrator is responsible to submit the Facility Drop Matrix Template 
for used by the facility group for sizing of the Power Drops and other facilities 
services. 
2.3.1.1.3.5 
Layout Drawings shall have plan views and elevation views. 
2.3.1.1.3.6 
Hydraulic valve stands, pneumatic valve stands, control panels, J-box mounting, 
and cable tray shall be shown on the Layout Drawings. 
2.3.1.1.3.7 
The location of all floor mounted hydraulic shut-offs shall be shown on the Layout 
Drawings. 
2.3.1.1.3.8 
Electrical drops from the plant electrical bus to any control, weld or power 
distribution panel shall be free of overhead obstructions (i.e. cable trays, header 
pipe, balcony/mezzanine, supports, etc.). 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 29 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.1.1.3.9 
Panels where the electrical drops are not free from overhead obstructions, a 
1.2m clearance to the overhead obstruction shall be required. 
2.3.1.1.4 
All external controls (mechanical, electrical, pneumatic and hydraulic) are to be 
located to protect against possible damage and provide accessibility for 
maintenance.  
2.3.1.1.5 
Sizing sheets shall be filled out for all cylinders and motors. 
2.3.1.1.6 
Access doors to the equipment shall be strategically placed along the tooling for 
ease of programming and maintenance. 
2.3.1.2 
Machine Sequence 
2.3.1.2.1 
Machine Controls Sequence Requirements will be provided by the Mechanical 
Design Source for establishing fixture sequence of and cycle time operation and 
machine control coordination with the machine controls Supplier. 
NOTE: See GVOSS Section 3.14.1.8 on special requirements for Turntable and 
Trunnion index times. 
2.3.1.2.2 
The Machine Sequence will be such that all machine units are interlocked to 
prevent damage to either the machine units or product while the machine is 
operating in either "Automatic" or "Manual" modes. 
2.3.1.2.3 
For "Manual" operation, if a motion is "Clear to Advance" it shall also be "Clear to 
Return". 
2.3.1.2.4 
The template Man-Motion Sequence Chart Template shall be used for the 
Machine Sequence.  
• 
File Naming Convention for Tools: “Tool Number Station Number Tool 
Sequence Chart”.  
• 
File Naming Convention for Robots: “Tool Number Station Number Robot 
Sequence Chart”.  
NOTE: 16 Template Menu Icons are provided for convenience. Where Icon 
durations differ from actual durations the actual durations shall be entered 
manually. This is especially important when durations are generated as part of 
the supplier's MODAPTS study. The Start, Total and Bar will still be generated on 
the Sequence Chart Template. IN LATER VERSIONS OF EXCEL, THE MENU 
WILL APPEAR IN THE “ADD-INS” TAB. 
2.3.1.2.5 
Sequence Chart Templates shall be stored in Team Center with the 
"Manufacturing Controls Data" for the program in the Station folder. 
2.3.1.3 
Manual Machine Sequences 
2.3.1.3.1 
The use of air logic and air logic components shall only be used when PLC is not 
economically or functionally practical to use. Tooling must be designed to limit 
the number of motions/logic components used and 2 hand anti tie down controls 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 30 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
must be used as needed (per safety requirements). All air logic tooling must be 
approved by VOME engineering. 
• A motion is defined as the forward and return of a device. For example, a 
tool, up/down, clamp/unclamp, rotate left/right, tilt - forward/return, etc. 
2.3.1.3.2 
The following conditions shall be met: 
• Only manually actuated, limit, hand saver valves are used to pilot the 
directional valves, which provide machine motion.  
• Pilot-operated valves are to be used for machine motions and not 
sequencing. 
• Valves and control device selection shall comply with the list of approved 
components.  
2.3.1.4 
Clearance Requirements 
2.3.1.4.1 
The following minimum clearances for collision considerations must be observed 
between fixed and moving parts in the machine design. 
2.3.1.4.2 
Transfer motions shall have a minimum clearance of 20 mm (e.g. from shuttle 
location to tool interference contour). 
2.3.1.4.3 
For guided motions (e.g. swivel unit) within a tool a minimum of 10 mm clearance 
shall be maintained. Take into consideration welding and flame-cutting 
tolerances and interferences. Machine edges for clearance if necessary. 
2.3.1.4.4 
Robotic gun clearance shall be a minimum of 10 mm (in FIDES). Take into 
consideration clearance changes related to electrode burn off. 
2.3.1.4.5 
Hand guns 30 mm to the tool during insertion shall have a clearance of 10 mm 
between the electrode and the tool interference contour at the weld spot. 
2.3.1.4.6 
Moving parts (e.g. nut runner, swivel units) shall have a clearance of 10mm 
nominal considering kinematic, tooling contour and production parts. 
2.3.1.4.7 
Loading parts to fixture shall have a clearance of 10mm nominal considering 
kinematic, tooling contour and production parts. 
2.3.1.4.8 
Fixture to parts shall have a clearance of 10mm nominal considering kinematic, 
tooling contour and production parts. 
2.3.2 
Cables, Cords and Receptacles  
2.3.2.1 
All cables shall be verified for proper sizing. 
2.3.2.2 
All individual devices shall be plug-in 3-pin, 4-pin, 5-pin or 8-pin plug and 
receptacle. This includes pressure switches, flow switches, light screens and 
photocells in addition to proximity switches and solenoid valves as currently 
defined by 70ZF-20013210 for Control Logix. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 31 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
2.3.2.2.1 
The maximum length of cable shall be 20 feet or 6 meters for all fixtures and 
tooling plates. Other applications such as cables for gate plugs, accumulating 
conveyors, I/O blocks, may exceed 6 meters but the total excess length shall be 
minimized, hung on hook or looped back in cable tray and shall not exceed the 
lesser of 5 feet or 10% of the total run. 
2.3.2.3 
Tooling shall be designed to keep single and multi-conductor cables off the base 
of the fixture. 
2.3.2.4 
Metal “Cage” Style cord grips shall not be used on any cord that flexes during 
normal cycle. 
2.3.2.4.1 
Cable and cord labels shall have a font type of Arial Alt. Mono For Brady 
(Verdana is permitted only for label machines without this brady font available). 
Labels shall be printed in CAPITAL letters, bold, with a minimum font height of 
3mm. 10-point font is preferred, which yields a 3.5mm height font. The label type 
shall be self-laminating vinyl wrap around. Any deviation from this requirement 
requires Ford VO controls approval. 
2.3.2.5 
M12 Cord Splitters 
2.3.2.5.1 
Cord Splitters shall be accessible for replacement. 
2.3.2.5.1.1 
Cord Splitters shall not be mounted flush. (1/2”/12mm Spacers are required). 
2.3.2.5.2 
Cord Splitters shall be mounted to a fixed surface and not suspended by the 
connectors. 
2.3.2.5.3 
Cord Splitters shall not be plugged directly into an IP67 block I/O module. 
2.3.2.5.4 
When stacking Cord Splitters: 
2.3.2.5.4.1 
No more than 3 shall be stacked on tooling fixtures or stands. 
2.3.2.5.4.2 
No more than 2 shall be stacked on robot end effectors. 
2.3.2.5.4.3 
Spacers are required between all stacked tool cord splitters. 
2.3.2.6 
I/O modules that have inline connections the following shall apply: 
2.3.2.6.1.1 
Shall not be mounted flush. 
2.3.2.6.1.2 
Shall be mounted using a standoff at least 1 ½ times the diameter of the 
receptacle. 
2.3.2.6.1.3 
Shall have receptacle/cable clearance of at least 1 ½ times the length of the 
receptacle to nearest obstruction. (as shown below) 
2.3.2.6.1.4 
Bend radius of cable shall not be exceeded when installing (or removing) cable 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025

from the I/O module. 
 
              
 
 
        
   
 
2.3.3 
Switches and Sensors 
NOTE:  Contact the Structures Standards Machine Controls Group for current 
technical information regarding proximity switch applications and callouts. 
2.3.3.1 
Each motion in any fixture or transfer shall be sensed for position by using a 
proximity or limit switch. This sensing is required to sequence machine and 
assure control of parts (i.e. Clamp is on master locating surface). Additional 
sensor considerations are listed below: 
• 
No switches in series for DC applications.  
• 
One switch is required per motion to indicate "returned" or "start" position. 
• 
A minimum of one switch is required per valve group of motion(s) to indicate 
"advanced" or "work" position. Additional switches may be required due to 
sequence or dimensional needs. 
• 
On semi-automatic sub-assemblies, switches used to indicate returned are 
required only where necessary for sequencing. 
2.3.3.2 
Limit, Proximity, and Laser Switch Usage Guide 
2.3.3.2.1 
Laser switches used for part sensing shall be agreed to by the component 
supplier and follow the Tier II sign off process. 
2.3.3.2.2 
Round, barrel-type proximity switches may be used for close tolerance sensing of 
part present and tooling/equipment location. 
2.3.3.2.3 
Additional information on the types and uses of sensors can be found in the 
individual Program Sensor Design Guide. 
2.3.3.2.4 
See the Approved Manufactures Product Selection Guides, the SOW, and the 
Approved GVOSS Electrical Components list (Reference only) for examples of 
Program Sensor Types. 
2.3.3.2.4.1 
Fail-safe switch signals are to be utilized to prevent a process from carrying on if 
the switch is failed/unplugged. If the process is looking for a switch signal to not 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 33 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
change state between a good sensing condition and a no sensing condition, then 
that switch must have a normally closed signal and the software must look for a 
positive ‘on’ signal such that a failed/unplugged switch doesn’t allow the process 
to carry on. These applications must be reviewed and approved by VO Controls. 
2.3.3.2.5 
Limit Switches – External Sensing – Typical Applications 
• Part present – machine upper fixture 
• Hydraulic unit dirty filter 
2.3.3.2.6 
Proximity Switches – External Sensing – Typical Applications 
• Body Framing Pillar Units  
• Chain Type Transfer 
• Clamping Units 
• Dump 
• Ejector or Unloader 
• Hold Down Clamps 
• Hydraulic – Accumulator (no series connection allowed) 
• Locating Pins 
• Lock Pins 
• Model Part Sensing 
• Model Positioner 
• Multi-position Weld Index Cylinders (mid-stroke weld position) 
• Part Present – tabletops, lifters and machine lower fixtures # 
• Pivots 
• Power Roll Stops 
• Shot Pins – slides, dumps, indexes and pivot cylinders. 
• Slides 
• Transfer Rail Out of Guides 
• Two (2) position weld index cylinders (back-to-back), etc. 
2.3.3.3 
Fixture shall include sensing devices as required to assure that all parts are in 
proper location for transferring, welding and/or initiation of next machine function. 
For example, includes “all clamps open” switch for automatic transfer and “clamp 
closed” switch when clamping on a master locating surface. 
2.3.3.4 
Part verification sensors are required for detection of correct model parts at loose 
part load stations and for “all parts present” detection. Where model detection 
and verification are required, a positive fail-safe signal shall be utilized for each 
model being detected. A loss of signal does not constitute a model. 
Note: Dual channel laser switches (ex. Q4x & Q5x) used for model detection with 
1 (NO) contact and 1 (NC) contact are only to be used when two unique model 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 34 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
features are not available.  VOME approval is required for the use of these dual 
channel laser switches in those applications. 
2.3.3.5 
Proximity Switches, Laser Switches and Vision Cameras 
2.3.3.5.1 
All proximity switches shall be short circuit protected and used per application 
guide. The proximity switch shall be mounted in such a manner that the sensing 
face is vertical.  
2.3.3.5.2 
The actuator shall not exceed 1/2-inch (13mm) diameter and shall be parallel to 
the sensing face. 
2.3.3.5.3 
For Tooling Fixtures, each switch or actuator shall be adjustable in at least two 
planes. 
2.3.3.5.4 
Switch status LEDs shall be visible. 
2.3.3.5.5 
For Final Assembly Engineering, external sensing proximity switches can be 
mounted with sensing faces in other than vertical position with the approval of the 
Ford follow-up person. 
2.3.3.5.6 
Proximity switch actuator devices shall be mechanically sufficient to withstand 
any deflection resulting from damage during maintenance functions.  
2.3.3.5.7 
Each proximity switch shall have a unique actuator.  
2.3.3.5.8 
Actuators shall not trip more than one switch. 
2.3.3.5.9 
For Body Construction Engineering, all standard proximity switch applications for 
part sensing shall utilize a coating or cover to minimize damage due to weld flash 
and slag build-up. 
2.3.3.5.10 
Cylinder position proximity (examples such as home and work positions) 
switches shall be numbered by unit number on which it is mounted, cylinder 
number, and switch number, (cylinder and switch number shall be sequential).  
 
Present sensors (examples such as station/robot part presents, muting sensors, 
pallet sensing switches) and Field sensors (examples such as pressure, flow, 
float, temperature) and named from sensing level purpose (examples such as 
cell, station, robot, fixture), plus sensor type abbreviation number/count. For 
GVOSS controls see GVOSS Naming and abbreviations. For Next-Gen controls 
see Next-Gen Naming and abbreviations.  
 
NOTE: For controls to match mechanical; names must start with a letter not 
a number. 
2.3.3.5.11 
PLC I/O description (for logic and messages) shall include the unit, cylinder, 
switch number and description of the area that the unit contacts and function. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 35 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
For Next-Gen controls see 70ZF-20013205 Lamacoid Tags examples. 
Example: U114-C1-2PXS upper "D" pillar clamp closed. 
 
For GVOSS controls see GVOSS Naming and abbreviations. Example: 
S023TT1FA U110C08PX1 UPPER D PILLAR CLAMP CLOSED. 
2.3.3.5.12 
All switches shall have a tag that includes the Name, description and I/O 
address. The switch tag shall be engraved with text no smaller than 0.125” (3 
mm) and be mounted adjacent to the switch. 
Both lamicoid and soft lamicoid (thick sticker) are allowed provided they are 
flame resistant and the ink does not fade. In some cases, such as on panels for 
pushbutton boxes; pushbuttons and selector switches for example: the soft 
lamicoid are preferred over lamicoid (as they do not cut your hand). 
 
2.3.3.5.13 
All laser proximity switches and vision cameras shall be match-marked with their 
final setting at tooling buy-off. This shall be a scribed line filled in with white paint. 
2.3.3.5.14 
Cube Proximity Switches used for machine position, part presence in a fixture, 
operator loaded End Effector or load assist shall be 20mm sensing range. 
Protective brackets required. 
Cube Proximity Switches used for part presence in a M/H Robot End Effector 
that is not operator loaded shall be 50mm sensing range. Protective brackets not 
required. 
2.3.3.6 
Limit Switches 
2.3.3.6.1 
The part of the limit switch actuator that contacts the limit switch shall be 
adjustable with an adequate locking means and have a width at least three times 
that of the limit arm contact. A cam with a minimum of two ¼ inch -20 (6mm-20) 
retaining screws is preferred. 
2.3.3.6.2 
Where any limit switch actuating means is designed to ride over the limit switch 
arm, the limit switch shall be of machine tool type design to accommodate the 
over-travel.  
2.3.3.6.3 
Limit switches shall be numbered by unit number on which it is mounted, cylinder 
number, and switch number, (cylinder and switch number shall be sequential).  
2.3.3.6.4 
PLC I/O description (for logic and messages) shall include the unit, cylinder, 
switch number and description of the area that the unit contacts and function.  
2.3.3.6.5 
All switches shall have a lamacoid tag that includes the PLC description and 
input address. The tag shall be engraved with text no smaller than 1/8 inch and 
be mounted adjacent to the switch (See drawing package for an example.) 
2.3.4 
Panels 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 36 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.4.1 
Short Circuit Current Rating (SCCR) 
2.3.4.1.1 
This applies to all new panels and panels relocated to a different buss plug. 
2.3.4.1.2 
Control panels having modifications to the power circuit must also meet this 
requirement. 
2.3.4.1.3 
All control panels containing power circuits shall be evaluated and marked with 
the SCCR. 
2.3.4.1.4 
For Next-Gen controls enclosure SCCR ratings see Next-Gen Section 8. 
For GVOSS controls enclosure SCCR ratings see GVOSS Section 14. 
2.3.4.2 
Include provisions for redundant HMI's to not be over-ridden from the primary 
HMI when used.  
2.3.4.3 
General 
2.3.4.3.1 
All control panels, I/O racks (except those in remote pushbutton panels) and 
processors shall be on one side of the line and coordinated with adjacent 
systems as to minimize line jumping.  
 
2.3.4.3.2 
All control panels shall be designed with hinges on the same side or with 
mechanical stops on the door that will prevent damage to lights and accidental 
tripping of E-Stops when doors are opened. 
2.3.4.4 
In order to sustain fixture modularity, flexibility and to minimize costs, flexible I/O 
junction boxes shall be mounted on the “product specific” equipment (i.e. tooling 
base or plate) when used. 
2.3.4.5 
Cable Trays 
2.3.4.5.1 
Cable trays shall be used to provide support for cables connecting fixtures, main 
panel, remote panels and facility power drops where the use of conduit is not 
specified. Electrical pin plugs shall be used at teardown disassembly points to 
accommodate shipping and for any panel not attached to the fixture. 
2.3.4.5.1.1 
When floor mounted cable trays per the risk assessment are determined to be 
utilized as a walking and working surface it shall conform to all walking and 
working surface requirements defined in GVOSS section 1.3.23, FAS08-040, 
OSHA 1910.22, ISO 14122-2 and local regional authority. Ref. 1.4.8.19.3 
2.3.4.5.2 
to the Ford Land Facilities Construction Specification Section 26 05 00. See also 
the Cooper B-Line Cable Tray Manual and the manufacturers recommendations 
for reference. 
2.3.4.5.3 
Cable tray runs shall be continuous unless stated otherwise in this section. Splice 
plates can be used to maintain continuity but may require bonding jumpers 
depending on application.

Horizontal to vertical transitions shall use vertical elbows (see example below). 
 
2.3.4.5.5

2.3.4.5.6 
Horizontal to vertical transitions from cable tray to equipment shall use waterfalls 
(see examples below). This is a requirement for all cables with the exception of 
Ethernet cables.
