2.3.4.6 
Cable and Hose Management 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 39 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.4.6.1 
Cable and Hose Carrier Systems (CAT-Track) 
 
 
2.3.4.6.2 
The equipment design shall accommodate the carrier system motion envelope. 
2.3.4.6.3 
Cable and hose shall have a minimum bend radius (MBR) less than the curve 
radius of the carrier system. The cable and hose manufacturer’s specifications 
and guidelines shall apply. 
2.3.4.6.4 
Cable shall be rated “continuous flex”. 
2.3.4.6.5 
Cable and hoses shall be suitable for use in the equipment operating 
environment. 
2.3.4.6.6 
Carrier system size shall be in accordance to the manufacturers Load Diagram 
for cable/hose weight per foot and travel distance. See example below. 
 
2.3.4.6.7 
When using the Load Diagram always select the size from the curve above and 
to the right of the load – travel plot point as shown below. 
 
2.3.4.6.8 
When determining cable system height and width arrange cables and hoses in 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 40 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
the carrier system so as to allow adequate movement. The largest cable or hose 
in the carrier system should have 20% clearance of the carrier system height and 
the total of all cables/hoses should have 20% clearance of the carrier system 
width. See example below. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 41 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.4.6.9 
Cables and hoses shall be arranged in the carrier system so that load is evenly 
distributed across width with largest and heaviest cables and hoses are located 
to the outer sides of the carrier system. 
 
2.3.4.6.10 
Separators shall be used between different sizes of cables and hoses to maintain 
proper spacing and to reduce cable and hose jacket wear. 
 
 
2.3.4.6.11 
Large cable or hoses shall not be placed in same compartment with small ones. 
 
2.3.4.6.12 
All cables and hoses shall be firmly clamped per the manufacturer’s 
specifications for strain relief at both ends of the carrier system to avoid damage 
to the inner structure of the cables and hoses; clamps shall be over a wide area 
of the outer jacket. Always leave slack between clamps and termination points to 
avoid stress on cables and hoses. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 42 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.4.6.13 
Guide trays shall be used when self-supported travel lengths are exceeded. This 
occurs when the total travel required exceeds the ability of the cable carrier 
system to support its own weight plus the weight of contained hoses and cables, 
resulting in negative camber. 
 
2.3.4.6.14 
In applications where the upper run of the cable carrier system is not self-
supporting a continuous support structure shall be provided. 
 
The cable carrier system support structure shall be designed to the 
manufacturer’s specifications for the application. 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 43 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.4.6.15 
Gliding applications shall require the addition of manufacturer approved “glide 
shoes” or similar attachments to reduce friction between the upper run and lower 
run of the cable carrier. 
 
 
 
 
 
Guide trays shall be designed to the manufacturer’s specifications for the 
application. 
2.3.4.6.16 
Cables and hoses shall not have twists, bends or kinks at the time of installation. 
Cable and hose shall be unspooled (do not uncoil) from their shipping reel 
several hours in advance of installation, see image below. It is preferred to hang 
cables and hoses for 24 hours, at the minimum they shall be laid out in straight 
line to allow kinks and curves to straighten out. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
2.3.4.6.17 
All cable track systems components shall be free from sharp edges. 
 
2.3.4.6.18 
Robot Transfer Units (RTU) 
2.3.4.6.18.1 Suppliers shall provide a MFMEA with the appropriate maintenance tasks 
associated with their product identified. 
2.3.4.6.18.2 Guide trays shall be 11ga (~3mm) steel minimum. 
2.3.4.6.18.3 Guide tray mounting brackets shall be 11ga (~3mm) steel minimum.  
2.3.4.6.18.4 Guide tray mounting brackets shall extend to the bottom edge of the tapered 
lead-in of the tray.  
2.3.4.6.18.5 The use of shims is prohibited on guide tray mounting brackets.   
2.3.4.6.18.6 All guide tray adjoining joints shall have a smooth transition and be secured by 
using 11ga (~3mm) minimum steel plate.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 44 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.4.6.18.7 The transition plate shall be a minimum of 3 inches (~76mm) wide and span the 
height of the tray side. Plate height shall start from the bottom of the tray and end 
at the bottom edge of the lead-in radius. 
2.3.4.6.18.8 Guide trays shall have an angled lead-in to the guide tray. 
2.3.4.6.18.9 Tray mounting brackets shall be secured to the tray.  
2.3.4.6.18.10 Tray mounting brackets shall be at a 90-degree angle and mounted with the 
flange positioned outward. 
2.3.4.6.18.11 The preferred mounting method shall be “center mount”. 
2.3.4.6.18.12 Guide trays for “center fed systems” shall have an angled lead at the beginning 
of the RTU tray system to protect the track when in the fully extended and 
retracted positions. 
2.3.4.6.18.13 RTU tracks can be low-mounted “side system” only on an approved exception 
basis. 
2.3.4.6.18.14 Robot tow arms shall ensure the track will ride upon itself at the earliest distance 
of travel. 
2.3.4.6.18.15 Tow arm brackets shall be installed square and perpendicular to the RTU guide 
rails. 
2.3.4.6.18.16 RTU tow arms will have dowel holes installed to mate the tow bracket. 
2.3.4.6.18.17 RTU slide plates will have receiving dowel holes for tow arm bracket mounting. 
2.3.4.6.18.18 RTU tow arm brackets shall have dowel holes installed to mate the RTU Tow 
arm to the RTU slide plate. 
2.3.4.6.18.18.1 
Guide trays and troughs shall be installed square and perpendicular to the   
RTU guide rails. 
2.3.4.6.18.19 Each installation shall be inspected at the final customer site for proper 
installation (PROTAG WHITE or equivalent build phase) before MP1, i.e., 
alignment, guide tray condition, etc. 
2.3.4.7 
HMI's are required for tooling and conveyors. 
2.3.4.8 
Lift-off hinges for freestanding control panels shall be acceptable. 
2.3.4.9 
Lockable door handles shall be prohibited on any control panel. 
2.3.4.10 
Junction boxes for light screens shall be located outside perimeter fences.

2.3.4.11 
Control enclosures housing electronic devices shall have temperature 
calculations performed using Hoffman or equivalent software. Ambient 
temperature of 110° F, 43° C shall be used. If control enclosure(s) cannot 
dissipate required thermal energy, a heat exchanger or air conditioner shall be 
utilized. 
2.3.5 
Compressed Gas 
2.3.5.1 
Compressed Gas refers to bottled commercial industrial gases used as part of 
the manufacturing process. This includes, but is not limited to: 
• 
Argon 
• 
Nitrogen 
• 
Oxygen 
• 
Carbon Dioxide 
2.3.5.2 
National or Local Standards for Compressed Gas shall prevail. 
2.3.5.2.1 
Minimum requirements are referenced in the CGA Standards. 
2.3.5.2.2 
For Ford of Europe, please reference the DVGW-Worksheet G600 / TRGI 2008. 
2.3.5.3 
CGA Standards. The Compressed Gas Association (CGA) sets the standards for 
compressed gas containers and delivery systems. All compressed gas 
connections shall follow the CGA Standard V-1. All other CGA standards shall 
apply to compressed gas systems. Table 2.4.6.a provides a CGA compressed 
gas fitting chart for reference only. Figure 2.4.6.b provides graphics of various 
fittings. 
 
 
 
 
 
                 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
                                              Table 2.4.6.a Compressed Gas Fitting Chart

SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 46 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled

Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 47 of 143

Figure 2.4.6.b Compressed Gas Fitting Connection Images (Sheet 2 of 2) 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 48 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.5.4 
All breaks in the delivery system between the compressed gas source and the 
point of use shall use the same CGA fittings. 
2.3.5.5 
All hoses and tubing shall be rated for the compressed gas Maximum Allowed 
Working Pressure (MAOP). Upon request the supplier shall have the delivery 
system MAOP and Maximum Operating Pressure (MOP) available for review. 
2.3.5.6 
Where the MAOP is less than the compressed gas cylinder pressure, a pressure 
relief valve shall be supplied to prevent over-charging the system and hose or 
pipe bursting. 
2.3.5.7 
Compressed gas supply lines shall be secured in a manner so that in the event 
of a failure, there shall be no risk to employees. 
2.3.5.8 
When a regulator diaphragm failure and subsequent venting of the gas presents 
a health risk to employees, the regulator valve bonnet shall be vented to a safe 
location. 
2.3.5.9 
The compressed gas delivery system shall be leak tested prior to buy-off and 
after installation at the plant. After installation a certification document shall be 
delivered to the BCE Launch ME for the system and the plant M.E. Manager. 
2.3.5.10 
All compressed gas cylinders shall be clearly identified with the gas type 
contained within the cylinder. Under no circumstances is an unmarked cylinder to 
be used in the system. 
2.3.6 
Hydraulic Design Guidelines 
2.3.6.1 
Hydraulic Pressure Lines and Hoses 
2.3.6.1.1 
Hydraulic pressure lines shall be constructed of steel pipe and tubing ONLY, no 
synthetic tubing or push-on fittings shall be used. 
2.3.6.1.2 
Hydraulic hoses shall be used to connect the hydraulic lines to the hydraulic 
device. 
2.3.6.1.3 
The length of hydraulic hose shall be kept as short as possible for the 
application. 
2.3.6.1.4 
Hydraulic pressure lines, tubing, hoses shall have Safety Orange tape wrapped 
at both ends before the fittings to identify them as hydraulic lines. 
2.3.6.2 
Hydraulic Cylinders 
2.3.6.2.1 
All cylinders shall be located so that flow control and cushion adjustments are 
readily accessible. 
• 
Clearance holes in weldments or castings shall be provided if necessary, to 
meet this requirement. 
• 
Every effort shall be made to mount the flow controls and cushions in an 
accessible area to allow adjustment during manual machine operation.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 49 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
Flow controls located away from cylinder shall have a tag to indicate unit 
number and the description of its function. 
2.3.7 
Pneumatic Design Guidelines 
2.3.7.1 
General 
2.3.7.1.1 
Tier 1 Integrators shall be responsible for proper sizing of all pneumatic elements 
in the system including but not limited to; piping, tubing, hosing and components. 
2.3.7.1.2 
Tier 1 Integrators are responsible for properly identifying the operational 
pneumatic pressure, pressure drop and volume requirements for all systems, 
sub-systems, and components. 
2.3.7.1.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall notify the VOME Launch Leader, VOME ME and Plant 
Launch Leader in writing of the plant compressed air pressure and the system 
effective air demand in SCFM for each system they supply as early in the 
program as possible. This will be used to evaluate the plant pneumatic 
generation and delivery systems’ ability to support the system pneumatic 
requirements. 
Note: It is important for the plant to understand the minimum air pressure 
required for system function as early in the program as possible in the event the 
pressure exceeds what is currently being generated. 
2.3.7.1.4 
The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible for the design and engineering of pneumatic 
component motion. (Orifices or adjustable flow controls may be used to control 
motion, however, when flow controls are utilized the supplier will be responsible 
for equipment damaged as a result of mis-adjustment.) 
2.3.7.1.5 
All motions shall cease on an E-Stop. 
2.3.7.1.6 
Each zone shall have a minimum of two quick disconnects (for pneumatic hand 
tools) piped prior to the air Lock-Out and Exhaust valve. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 50 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.7.1.7 
The preferred method of "E" stopping and holding pneumatic transfers is with a 
mechanical or shaft brake. 
2.3.7.1.8 
All pneumatic schematics shall be labeled Unit 1A7. 
2.3.7.1.9 
All pneumatic devices, (i.e., clamps, cylinders, valves), on new equipment shall 
use G ports. Kitted tools shall use the fitting type currently in use on the tools. 
2.3.7.1.10 
Teflon tape shall not be used on any threaded connection. An anaerobic sealer, 
made for threaded connections, shall be used on all taper threaded connections. 
2.3.7.1.11 
All manual valves that block air from entering a portion of a pneumatic circuit 
shall be an approved Lock-Out Exhaust type. 
2.3.7.1.12 
Any part of the circuit that cannot be exhausted thru the Lock-Out and Exhaust 
valve shall be tagged "Caution Trapped Air" and a 1/2-inch (12mm) wide, Safety 
Yellow band will be placed every 6 inches (152mm) on the tube, hose, cylinder, 
and/or tank containing the trapped air. 
2.3.7.1.13 
Tool integrators shall be responsible for air connection from tooling air supply to 
process tilt tables where required. 
2.3.7.1.14 
Prior to shipment of tool, all air pressure regulator settings shall be locked 
(tighten lock nut) and recorded for update on tags and pneumatic drawings. 
2.3.7.2 
Metric Fittings and Tubing 
2.3.7.2.1 
Tubing shall not be coated with hexavalent chromium (chrome 6) with no 
exceptions.  
2.3.7.2.2 
Tube shall be protected against corrosion. 
2.3.7.2.3 
Tubing shall not be painted. 
2.3.7.2.4 
Installed pipe/tubing work should be labeled with the flow direction. 
2.3.7.2.5 
Tube Fittings  
2.3.7.2.5.1 
Fittings shall not be of the types that are positioned by the thread. 
2.3.7.2.5.2 
Taper threads are not permitted. 
2.3.7.2.5.3 
World Thread Fittings are not permitted. 
2.3.7.2.5.4 
Universal fittings are not permitted. 
2.3.7.2.6 
Pipe work/tubing fittings shall be staggered to allow access. 
2.3.7.2.7 
Minimum tubing size shall be 8.0mm O.D. for all systems. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 51 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled

2.3.7.2.9

2.3.7.2.10 
Hose Fittings 
2.3.7.2.10.1 JIC 37-degree flare fitting is required for applications using Imperial Size Hose 
(i.e. ½” hose) and NPT Pipe. 
2.3.7.2.10.2 24-degree Metric L fitting is required for connection to metric tubing or 
components. 
2.3.7.2.11 
All tubing will be steel seamless type. All tubing runs of less than 5 meters point 
to point will be of one piece with no connectors of any kind except termination 
points. All piping/tubing to be well supported – a brace or fixation point shall be 
installed per manufacturers support recommendations, based on tubing size. 
2.3.7.2.12 
Plastic lines are not permitted without VOME Controls Engineer approval. 
2.3.7.2.13 
Tape shall not be used on any threaded connections. 
2.3.7.2.14 
Threaded pipe connections shall be NPT thread. 
2.3.7.2.15 
Supplier shall be responsible to purchase and deliver all items including tubing, 
clips, hoses, manifolds and fittings.  Supplied tubing straight runs should be 
continuous 6-meter runs.

2.3.7.2.16 
Component port and tube fittings are noted below. Machine base porting for 
pneumatics shall follow the specification listed below. The OEM shall submit a 
formal deviation request to use a different porting scheme. 
2.3.7.2.16.1 Port Connection: ISO 1179-1 (BSPP) port to accept ISO 228-1 (“G”) threads with 
seal face. 
2.3.7.2.16.2 Tube Connection: metric bite type (ISO 8434-1), 24 degree with soft seal 
2.3.7.2.17 
The transition from NPT to “G” shall be after the air or water drop from the 
header. The hose connected to the end of the drop (after lockout) shall have a 
JIC 37-degree NPT Flare fitting (not the hose from header to lockout). The other 
end of the hose shall meet the requirements of section 2.3.7.2.16. See below for 
examples of application and transition requirements.

2.3.7.3 
Pneumatic Piping and Fittings 
2.3.7.3.1 
All piping and fitting in this section shall meet the requirements of the referenced 
ASTM Standard. 
2.3.7.3.2 
Pipe sizes up through 2 inches, threaded and mechanical joints. 
2.3.7.3.2.1 
Pipe shall be ASTM A53 Schedule 40 black steel. 
2.3.7.3.2.2 
Threaded fittings shall be Standard Class 150 malleable iron ASTM A197, 
banded ends. 
2.3.7.3.2.3 
Threaded unions shall be Class 300 malleable iron ASTM A197, ground joint 
brass-iron. 
2.3.7.3.2.4 
Threaded couplings shall be standard black steel ASTM A53. 
2.3.7.3.2.5 
Pneumatic piping shall not be painted. 
2.3.7.3.3 
Pipe sizes 2-1/2 to 4 inches, threaded and mechanical joints 
2.3.7.3.3.1 
Pipes 2-1/2 to 4 inches shall be Schedule 40 black steel ASTM A53, ERW 
(Electric-Resistance Welded) Grade B. 
2.3.7.3.3.2 
Threaded fittings shall be standard Class 300 malleable iron ASTM A197, 
banded ends. 
2.3.7.3.3.3 
Threaded unions shall be Class 300 malleable iron ASTM A197, ground joint 
brass-iron. 
2.3.7.3.3.4 
Threaded couplings shall be standard black steel ASTM A53. 
2.3.7.3.4 
Pipe sizes 2-1/2 inches and larger, welded joints 
2.3.7.3.4.1 
Pipes 2-1/2 to 10 inches shall be Schedule 40 black steel ASTM A53, ERW 
(Electric-Resistance Welded) Grade B. 
2.3.7.3.4.2 
Weld flanges shall be standard Class 150 forged steel ASTM A105 with ANSI 
B16.5 weld neck. 
2.3.7.3.4.3 
Flanged fittings shall be standard Class 125 cast iron ASTM A126. 
2.3.7.3.4.4 
Weld fittings shall be standard black steel ASTM A234, butt welding. 
2.3.7.3.4.5 
Gaskets shall be composition 1/16" thick. 
2.3.7.3.4.6 
Nuts and bolts shall be Steel ASTM A307, Grade B, hex head, machine bolts. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 55 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.7.4 
Air Distribution System (Air Header) 
2.3.7.4.1 
Pneumatic Systems and pipe sizes are to be designed accordingly to handle the 
peak demand of the line or sub-assembly. 
2.3.7.4.2 
An adequately sized air distribution system (Header) shall be used to provide air 
to all fixtures with one plant air drop for each distribution system (Header).  
2.3.7.4.3 
The plant air drop location shall be shown on the Unit 1A Installation Drawings. 
See “Power Drop Legend” in GVOSS Section 2.3.1.1.2.3 for legend symbols and 
proper annotations. 
2.3.7.4.4 
Air Distribution System (Header) Design 
2.3.7.4.4.1 
All air distribution systems (Header) shall be fed by a compressed air receiver 
(surge tank). See GVOSS 2.3.7.5. 
2.3.7.4.4.2 
The preferred air distribution system (Header) design is a loop design. 
2.3.7.4.4.3 
All fittings, valves, filters and other components of the air distribution system 
(Header) shall be accounted for when calculating the total effective length of line 
for the pipe. 
2.3.7.4.4.4 
Special attention shall be made to avoid designs, pipe reducers and components 
that will restrict air flow. 
2.3.7.4.4.5 
All air demand calculations shall be in units of SCFM (with conversion to CMH if 
required.). 
2.3.7.4.4.6 
When pipe sizing calculations are between pipe diameters the next larger 
diameter shall be used. 
2.3.7.4.5 
Air Distribution (Header) Branch Systems 
2.3.7.4.5.1 
Air distribution branch systems shall be used to deliver compressed air from the 
air distribution system (Header) to the point of use. 
2.3.7.4.5.2 
All branch connections to an air distribution system (Header) shall be on the top 
at a 90-degree angle above parallel relative to floor. 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 56 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.7.4.5.3 
For FAE and Paint Engineering each air distribution system (Header) branch 
connection port is to be equipped with a ball valve. See also section 2.3.7.11.1 
for additional requirements for ball valves. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
2.3.7.4.5.4 
Branch system air to the point-of-use shall not pass through more than one (1) 
air regulator. 
2.3.7.4.6 
All air distribution system (Header) shall have one (1) or more drip legs. 
2.3.7.4.7 
The air distribution system (Header) shall have a pitch so that condensation runs 
down to the drip leg. Use multiple drip legs where necessary. 
2.3.7.4.8 
Each drip leg shall have a ball valve drain at the lowest point. 
2.3.7.4.9 
At each operator station the air distribution system (Header) shall be equipped 
with plugged tees as shown in 56ZF-20040272 as provision for air-operated 
material handling equipment such as lift tables, turntables and lift assists. Plugs 
shall be square head. Countersunk plugs are prohibited. 
 
                       Square Head Plug      Countersunk Plug 
2.3.7.5 
Compressed Air Receivers (Surge Tank) 
2.3.7.5.1 
Compressed air receivers (Surge Tank) shall follow the requirements of OSHA 
1910.169 or ISO 13445, based on region. 
2.3.7.5.2 
Compressed air receivers (Surge Tank) shall be constructed to ASME Boiler and 
Pressure Vessel Code Section VIII.

SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
Certification Tags shall not be painted over when painting the compressed air 
receiver. Certification documentation shall be provided when delivery to Mexico 
in adherence to NOM-020-STPS 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
2.3.7.5.3 
Plant Facility compressed air drops to the equipment main air drop, for 
equipment having an air distribution system (Header) shall be to a compressed 
air receiver (Surge Tank). See example below.

2.3.7.5.4 
Compressed air receivers (Surge Tank) shall be sized to supply 10X maximum 
air demand during operating cycle at line rate. 
2.3.7.5.5 
Compressed air receivers (Surge Tank) shall be stenciled "Pressure Vessel".

2.3.7.5.6 
Compressed air receivers (Surge Tank) shall have a Certified Spring-Loaded 
Safety Valve constructed and installed to ASME Section VIII. The total relieving 
capacity of such safety valves shall be such as to prevent pressure in the 
receiver from exceeding the maximum allowable working pressure of the receiver 
by more than 10 percent. Certification tags shall be not be removed from the 
device. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
2.3.7.5.7 
Every air receiver (Surge Tank) shall be equipped with an indicating pressure 
gage (so located as to be readily visible). The pressure gage shall be equipped 
with a spread-out scale 3 PSI (0.2 bar) in order to indicate small pressure 
variances.

2.3.7.5.8 
A drainpipe and valve shall be installed at the lowest point of every air receiver 
(Surge Tank) to provide for the removal of accumulated oil and water. Adequate 
automatic traps may be installed in addition to drain valves. The drain valve on 
the air receiver (Surge Tank) shall be opened and the receiver completely 
drained frequently and at such intervals as to prevent the accumulation of 
excessive amounts of liquid in the receiver. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
2.3.7.5.9 
Compressed air receivers (Surge Tank) shall have the ability to bleed off air from 
the compressed air receiver (Surge Tank) from floor level. A compressed air 
exhaust silencer shall be added to reduce noise level. Note: w-SX1 does not 
apply.

2.3.7.6 
Pneumatic Lock-Out and Exhaust Valves 
• 
Lock-Out and Exhaust Valves are energy isolation valves used in equipment 
compressed air supply lines.  
• 
Air is shut off by pushing the red handle inward; downstream air is 
simultaneously exhausted through the exhaust port.  
• 
For the Festo Shut off Air is Shutoff by Twisting the handle clockwise; 
downstream air is simultaneously exhausted through the exhaust port. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 62 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
Ford Motor Company lock-out rules require that the valve be padlocked in 
this position to prevent the handle from being pulled out inadvertently when 
used during maintenance. 
 
 
2.3.7.6.1 
Manual Lock-Out and Exhaust valves shall be required at the Main Facilities Air 
Drop. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
2.3.7.6.2 
Manual Lock-Out and Exhaust valves shall be required for each automation 
tooling station. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 63 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
 
 
 
 
2.3.7.6.3 
All individual pieces of equipment requiring air to be shut off and padlocked for 
maintenance independent of an existing Lock-Out and Exhaust valve shall have 
a Manual Lock-Out and Exhaust valve. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 64 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.7.6.4 
Each Lock-Out and Exhaust valve shall be tagged to identify what is being 
locking out. 
 
2.3.7.6.5 
Lock-Out and Exhaust valves shall be located 48 to 60 inches (1220mm to 
1525mm) from either floor or platform level.  
2.3.7.6.6 
All air Lock-Out and Exhaust valves shall be located downstream from 
Compressed Air Receivers (Surge Tanks) (i.e., Lock-Out and Exhaust valves 
shall not dump air from Compressed Air Receivers). 
2.3.7.6.7 
A pressure gage shall be provided downstream in close proximity to the Lock-Out 
and Exhaust valve. For Lock-Out and Exhaust valves adjacent to filter/dryer the 
gage shall be located after the filter/dryer.

2.3.7.6.8 
A pressure switch shall be provided downstream of each pneumatic Lock-Out 
and Exhaust valve. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
2.3.7.7 
Conditioning of Incoming Equipment Air 
2.3.7.7.1 
Tier 1 Integrators shall be responsible for conditioning incoming plant air 
(elimination of oil, moisture, debris, dew point, etc.) to support all pneumatically 
operated components. This shall be accomplished by using a filter/dryer after the 
Compressed Air Receiver (Surge Tank) and utilizing an automatic drain valve to 
vent the dryer. 
 
 
2.3.7.7.2 
Filter/dryers shall always be sized at or larger sizes, when between sizes, for the 
SCFM (Nm3/h) flow rate required by the system. 
2.3.7.7.3 
Filter/Dryer ports shall be the same diameter as the pneumatic delivery lines they 
are connected to. 
2.3.7.7.4 
Reducer fittings are prohibited to fit a larger diameter supply line to a smaller port 
Filter/Dryer. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 66 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.7.8 
Regulators 
2.3.7.8.1 
Pneumatic circuits shall have regulators to adjust pneumatic pressure. 
2.3.7.8.2 
Each regulator shall have a separate gage. 
 
 
 
2.3.7.8.3 
Each regulator shall be tagged to identify what device air is being regulated and 
the pressure setting for the regulator. 
 
2.3.7.8.4 
All regulators downstream of Lock-Out and Exhaust valves shall be reverse flow 
capable. 
2.3.7.9 
Re-classifiers 
2.3.7.9.1 
All re-classifiers shall comply with the OSHA (or National/Regional) limits for air 
exhausted into the atmosphere.  
2.3.7.9.2 
For Paint Shop installations all compressed air exhaust ports shall either be 
vented outside the building or be equipped with re-classifiers capable of filtering 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 67 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
out oil particles 100%. 
2.3.7.9.3 
For Paint Shop installations when air is to be exhausted inside the Paint Shop, 
the Tier 1 Integrator must submit re-classifier specifications to the VOME 
Engineer for approval prior to ordering the re-classifiers.  
2.3.7.10 
Pneumatic Manifolds and Bulkheads 
2.3.7.10.1 
All pneumatic valve manifolds or manifold banks shall be fed from both ends. 
They shall be shown and called out as having pressure and exhaust ports piped 
from both ends. Manifolds shall have service for maximum flow. 
2.3.7.10.2 
The flow capacity total of all valves in a manifold bank shall be equal to or less 
than the supply and exhaust ports through the manifold bank. 
2.3.7.10.3 
Any manifold flowing lubricated air shall have a filter/reclassifier, one pipe size 
larger than the largest exhaust port, piped to each end of the manifold for a total 
of two. 
2.3.7.10.4 
Welded pneumatic distribution “pipe” style manifolds shall be hydrostatically 
tested and certified. The certification tag shall be attached to the manifold and 
shall not be removed. 
2.3.7.10.5 
All pneumatic bulkheads shall be stamped with a letter/ number. 
2.3.7.10.5.1 All corresponding hoses shall be labeled accordingly.  
2.3.7.11 
Valves 
2.3.7.11.1 
Ball Valves 
2.3.7.11.1.1 All ball valves in pneumatic circuits shall be latch lock and padlock able with 
safety vent. 
 
NOTE: Ball valves at the end of the facilities air drop connecting to a system 
compressed air receiver (surge tank) or to vent a compressed air receiver shall 
NOT be a safety vent style to prevent exhausting air from the receiver. 
2.3.7.11.1.2 Ball valves shall not be used to shut off air to a manifold. 
2.3.7.11.1.3 Unless otherwise specified ball valves shall be permitted after a station Lock-Out 
and Exhaust valve for single air lines supplying air to no more than one (1) 
device.

At the bottom of the facilities drop where air enters the system service stand. 
DO NOT use vented ball valve. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
• 
Exhausting air to atmosphere such as; 
– 
Compressed air receivers (Surge tank). DO NOT use vented ball valve. 
 
 
– 
Air system moisture drain. DO NOT use vented ball valve.

Components prior to a Lock-Out and Exhaust valve such as:

2.3.7.11.1.5 Ball valve handles shall be Red for “normally closed” and Green for “normally 
open” to identify their state during equipment operation. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
          
2.3.7.11.2 
Pneumatic Valves 
2.3.7.11.2.1 Pneumatic valves shall comply with the approved components list for the 
applicable program.  
2.3.7.11.2.2 Valves shall be grouped by size of valve bases. 
2.3.7.11.2.3 All 24 VDC pneumatic valves shall require no more than 180 mA to actuate. 
2.3.7.11.2.4 All 24 VDC pneumatic valves shall be equipped with a feedback diode across the 
coil. 
2.3.7.11.2.5 Air valves and manifold layouts shall be designed with all Solenoid 14 of the 
valves on the same side of the manifold.  
• 
“Work” action is Solenoid 14. 
• 
“Home” is Solenoid 12 on dual solenoid valves. 
• 
For dual solenoid valves, the return solenoid shall be the next consecutive 
output address. 
• 
For single solenoid valves, the first action is Solenoid 14.  
The following is an example to help with further clarification. 
• 
Locating pins that are extended for the load position are considered to be in 
the home position. These pins shall be piped such that the detent side of the 
valve holds the pins extended while the operator is in the light screen loading 
the part.  
• 
Clamps are open for the load position are considered to be in the home 
position. These clamps shall be piped such that the detent side of the valve 
holds the clamps open while the operator is in the light screen loading the 
part.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 71 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
Model select units are moved into position prior to the clear to enter light. The 
movement to their load position is considered the home position. The model 
select units shall be piped such that the detent side of the valve holds the 
unit in position while the operator is in the light screen loading the part. 
2.3.7.11.2.6 Valves for operator load stations shall be three-position open center, spring 
centered, detented on the 12-side home position and 14-side work position for 
any machine action that could result in a crushing or pinching injury to the 
operator should any cylinder stick. This includes weld gun valves. Motions that 
are beyond the operators’ normal reach while loading/unloading are exempt.  
Any motion on the 14 solenoid (work side) of the valve shall be reviewed as to 
ensure if the light screen is interrupted prior to completing the movement 
additional hazards are not created. Units such as dumps, and slides shall be 
reviewed and any hazard due to gravity shall be addressed during the design 
phase. For example, this can be accomplished through the use of a mechanical 
detent or pilot operated check valves. 
2.3.7.11.2.7 Each vacuum cup shall have its own Ford GVOSS approved vacuum generator. 
A 2-position detented valve shall be used with solenoid 14 (“A”) for “vacuum on” 
and solenoid 12 (“B”) for “vacuum off”. 
2.3.7.12 
Cylinders 
2.3.7.12.1 
All cylinders shall be permanently lubricated. 
2.3.7.12.2 
Permanently lubricated cylinders shall not be used with lubricated air. 
2.3.7.12.3 
All pneumatic cylinders shall be sized to provide adequate force with 65 PSI (4.5 
bar) supply pressure.  
2.3.7.12.4 
Robot weld guns shall be sized to provide adequate force with 80 PSI (5.5 bar) 
supply pressure. 
2.3.7.12.5 
An Action Request Form for a Deviation shall be submitted for devices requiring 
pneumatic supply pressure greater than 80 PSI (5.5 bar). 
2.3.7.12.6 
Piping between an air pilot-operated check valve and the cylinder port it controls 
shall be hard piped and as short as possible. 
2.3.7.12.7 
Cylinders shall use either a fixed orifice or flow control to regulate cylinder speed. 
• 
Fixed orifices are preferred. 
• 
Consult the component manufacturer to determine their recommendation to 
regulate flow control. Failure to comply with this may result in lost production 
charges. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 72 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.7.12.8 
When fixed orifices are used on pneumatic cylinders to control maximum speed, 
they shall be color coded by orifice size according to the chart below: 
• 
1.0 Millimeter = “Black”  
• 
1.5 Millimeter = “Green”  
• 
2.0 Millimeter = “Red”  
• 
2.5 Millimeter = “White”  
• 
3.0 Millimeter = “Yellow”  
• 
Any size not listed = “Orange” 
2.3.7.12.9 
All cylinders shall be located so that flow control and cushion adjustments are 
readily accessible. 
• 
Clearance holes in weldments or castings shall be provided if necessary, to 
meet this requirement.  
• 
Every effort shall be made to mount the flow controls and cushions in an 
accessible area to allow adjustment during manual machine operation.  
2.3.7.12.10 Any pneumatic motion within a tool that is over 6” in the horizontal direction or is 
in any direction affected by gravity shall incorporate an air pilot-operated check 
valve located as close to the device as possible. 
2.3.7.13 
Pneumatic Counterbalance Systems 
2.3.7.13.1 
Design parameters for transfer counterbalance calculations are found in 70ZF- 
5858. (Current version is in Team Center.) Key parameters are listed below: 
• 
Maximum counterbalance cylinder pressure (transfer up) = 60 PSI (4.1 bar).  
• 
Maximum counterbalance pressure differential (up & down) = 2 PSI (0.15 
bar) (L/C XFRS).  
2.3.7.13.2 
Pneumatic machine functioning operations shall not be connected to the 
counterbalance air system.  
2.3.7.14 
Tags 
2.3.7.14.1 
Tags shall not include the Program Name. 
2.3.7.14.2 
Tags are to be shown and flagged out by the build supplier on the drawings for 
each valve, pressure regulator and gage pressure settings. 
2.3.7.14.3 
Tags shall include the station number. 
2.3.7.14.4 
Tags shall include the robot number when the device being tagged is associated 
with a robot. 
2.3.7.14.5 
Tags shall include the name of the device the tag is associated with. 
2.3.7.14.6 
Valve tags shall include the valves machine function and valve number. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 73 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.7.14.7 
Tags are to be affixed to the machine at the device being tagged. 
2.3.7.14.8 
All tags shall be attached with Loctite 454. 
2.3.7.14.9 
Tags that cannot be attached to a surface (i.e. Black Oxide) using Loctite 454 
shall be attached using drive screws. 
2.3.7.15 
Gauges 
2.3.7.15.1 
All pneumatic gauges shall be marked as described in Section 1.4.4.2. 
 
 
2.3.7.16 
Pressure Switches 
2.3.7.16.1 
All pressure switches shall be set to ± 10% of required operating pressure of 
device being monitored. 
2.3.7.16.2 
Only pressure switches with self-contained lights shall be used. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 74 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.7.16.3 
Switches shall be tagged and numbered with valve numbers, I/O address, unit 
number, and pressure setting when associated with an air solenoid valve. 
 
 
2.3.7.16.4 
For robots with pneumatic weld guns, pneumatic equalization or other air 
operated device an air pressure switch is required for every robot. 
2.3.7.17 
Synthetic Tubing (Push to Connect) 
 
2.3.7.17.1 
Synthetic tubing standards include the following: 
2.3.7.17.2 
Synthetic Tubing shall only be used for process equipment (i.e. fixture tooling, 
robot end effectors, and adhesive, mastic or sealer systems).  
2.3.7.17.3 
All components shall meet the UL-94 Flame Retardant Specification or EN 
60695-11-10/-20.  
2.3.7.17.4 
Fittings and tubing shall be from the same manufacture that is selected on a per 
program/plant basis.  
2.3.7.17.5 
All synthetic tubing shall be double walled.  
2.3.7.17.6 
Flexing applications shall anchor tubing to eliminate any strain on the fitting.  
2.3.7.17.7 
All tubing and fittings shall meet a 120 PSI (8.3 BAR) minimum pressure rating  
2.3.7.17.8 
All fittings shall meet ISO 1179-1 and ISO 228-1 with a minimum G1/4” with Face 
Seal for Pneumatic/water. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 75 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.7.17.9 
Tier 1 Integrators shall be responsible to assure dress out of all synthetic tubing 
is protected against kinking of the tube. 
2.3.7.17.10 All synthetic tube Push to Connect fittings shall have a weld flash protective boot 
(protective boot not required in non-welding stations and /or systems).  
2.3.7.18 
Hose and Tube Color 
2.3.7.18.1 
Pneumatic Applications: 
Synthetic Tube: 
1. Pressure Feed – BLUE  
2. Extend to Work Position – RED  
3. Retract to Home Position – WHITE  
4. Vacuum – WHITE 
Magnetic Clamp “On” (Work Position) 
5. Vacuum Blow Off – BLUE – Magnetic Clamp “Off” (Home Position) 
Hose: 
1. BLUE – all applications Pressure Feed – BLUE 
2.  If Blue Hose is used in place of synthetic tube for operation, then the 
appropriate color tape shall be applied to each end. 
2.3.7.18.2 
Water Applications: 
For kitting of tooling, match the existing hose colors. 
 
 
 
 
2.3.7.18.3 
For New Tooling lines or sub-assemblies the colors for synthetic tubing or hose 
shall be: 
2.3.7.18.3.1 Water In – Green 
2.3.7.18.3.2 Water Out – Black 
2.3.7.18.4 
For hose applications, fittings shall be Male for Water In and Female for Water 
Out at weld guns, fixtures and EOAT.

2.3.8.1 
All machine and fixture commercial items supplied by the supplier shall conform 
to the approved water components list.

Page 76 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.8.2 
Water systems design shall comply with Ford Facility Construction Specification 
23 21 10 Hydronic and Process Water Systems Design Guide. 
2.3.8.3 
All water manifolds shall be made of copper, brass or stainless steel. Ferrous 
metal or aluminum manifolds will not be accepted. All manifolds and piping shall 
be secured using metal clamps. Other water components are to be brass, except 
for the centrifugal filter which shall be stainless steel. 
2.3.8.4 
All piping shall be copper (Reference – 56ZF-30384, see Team Center for latest 
file) or PVC (Reference section 2.3.9). 
2.3.8.4.1 
Pipe Material other than PVC or Copper may be used with an approved deviation 
from the Standards Group. The deviation shall be applied across all new facility 
lines. 
2.3.8.4.1.1 
Pipes Used in 56ZF-30384 need to be reviewed by the Vehicle Operations Body 
Construction Controls Engineer assigned to the program. 
        
 
 
2.3.8.4.1.2 
Reference – 56ZF-30384, 3D Model Example. 
2.3.8.4.2 
For Ford of Europe: All pipe installation along a transfer line to be of ABS plastic 
material (PVC-C not allowed) and shall comply with the requirements outlined in 
EN ISO 15494. 
2.3.8.5 
All water flow switches shall be piped with unions on each side of the switch 
unless the water flow switch can be replaced without disturbing existing piping. 
2.3.8.6 
Adequately sized headers shall be used to provide water to all fixtures within an 
individual transfer area with one plant drop for each service. This shall also be 
shown in the 1A Line Layout and the Installation Drawings. 
2.3.8.7 
Provide separate flow switches for fixture and weld control.  
2.3.8.8 
For pipe sizes for fixture drops and for calculating water demand and typical 
piping reference – 56ZF-30384. 
2.3.8.9 
For each automatic weld station not using a "Water Saver" valve, provide a 
pneumatically actuated single solenoid controlled full port ball valve in the "Water 
In" line.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 77 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.8.10 
Supplier shall test water-flow capability for each weld gun and weld station 
according to the requirements of the Weld Gun Water Flow Check-Off Sheet 
found in 1.2.6.3.11. Differential is measured at the station water drops. Flow 
readings shall be recorded in the Buy-Off Book in the form of completed Check-
Off Sheets. 
2.3.8.11 
Return header shall be same size as inlet header. 
2.3.8.12 
All connections to a system header shall be at the top at a 45 to 90-degree angle 
above parallel relative to floor. 
2.3.8.13 
All water schematics shall be labeled Unit 1A8. 
2.3.8.14 
One transformer and associated tips shall constitute a standard water circuit. 
2.3.8.15 
Shut-off valves shall be located 48 to 60 inches (1.2 to 1.5 meters) from either 
floor or platform level. 
2.3.8.16 
“Unistrut’ pipe/tubing clamps shall not be used.  
2.3.8.17 
For water service design, see the latest water drawing 56ZF-30384 in Team 
Center. The water header pipe shall be sized to the tool requirements and 
include spare capacity for (4) future weld guns to be added to the line without a 
header size change. There shall be no "percent spare" added.  If the calculation 
for the water header pipe shows a non-standard size, the next size up shall be 
used. 
2.3.8.18 
For water lines returning from weld gun components (e.g. shunt block, 
transformer, or servo motor) and component-to-component connections see Ford 
Cooling Water Hose Connections on ARO Weld Guns and the Ford Cooling 
Water Hose Connections on OBARA Weld Guns drawings for clarification. 
2.3.8.19 
Water gages shall not be marked until the tooling is in the home manufacturing 
plant. All water gages shall be marked as described in Section 1.4.5.2. 
2.3.8.20 
Teflon tape shall not be used on any threaded connection. An anaerobic sealer, 
made for threaded connections, shall be used on all tapered connections. 
2.3.8.21 
Weld water quality requirements include the following: 
• 
Water pressure: 65 PSI +/-20 PSI 90 PSI maximum (4.5 bar +/-1.5 bar 6.2 
bar maximum). 
• 
Pressure differential: 20 PSI (1.5 bar) minimum at station drop, 30 PSI (2 
bar) preferred. 
• 
For Welding Applications, the minimum flow rate is 8 liters per minute (l/min) 
• 
Water temperature: 88 degrees Fahrenheit (31 degrees Celsius). 
• 
Water solids maximum size: 841 microns filter to a beta of 2/150. 
• 
P.H.: Alkaline 7.0 to 9.0. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 78 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
Conductivity: 5000 ohms per centimeter. 
2.3.9 
PVC Water Header 
2.3.9.1 
Scope 
This standard is for new systems only. 
The purpose of this section is to outline the implementation of Polyvinyl Chloride 
(PVC) water header pipe in lieu of a copper header.  The Tier 1 Integrator shall 
cost all Ford approved options in order to provide the best cost solution.  
2.3.9.2 
The Tier 1 Integrator is to ensure the design, installation, disassembly and re-
installation is common for all systems from each Tier 1 Integrator. The following 
document will serve as direction for the requirements of PVC headers on all new 
tooling lines. 
2.3.9.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator will be responsible to provide a fully functional system that 
meets the design and installation requirements of the product manufacturer. Any 
non-compliance will be the responsibility of the Tier 1 Integrator to resolve at no 
additional cost to Ford Motor Company. 
2.3.9.4 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall design and assemble the system using the 
recommended methods of the PVC manufacturer’s guidelines, the Plastics Pipe 
Institute and any other applicable industry standards. 
2.3.9.5 
All PVC pipe and fittings shall conform to the following standards: 
• 
ASTM D 1784 – Rigid Vinyl Compounds 
• 
ASTM D 1785 – Standard Specification for Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) Plastic 
Pipe Schedule 40, 80 and 120 
• 
ASTM D 2464 – Standard Specification for Threaded Polyvinyl Chloride 
(PVC) Plastic Pipe Fittings, Schedule 80 
• 
ASTM D 2467 – Standard Specification for Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) Plastic 
Pipe Fittings, Schedule 80 
• 
ASTM D 2564 – Standard Specification for Solvent Cements for Polyvinyl 
Chloride (PVC) Plastic Piping Systems  
2.3.9.6 
Manufacturers of PVC pipe will not be specified. The Tier 1 Integrator shall be 
responsible for the quality of the product selected for use. 
2.3.9.7 
Design 
2.3.9.7.1 
Water service stands shall follow current Ford Standards.  
2.3.9.7.2 
The robot and weld timer stand shall follow current Ford Standards.  
2.3.9.7.3 
Water header pipe and fittings shall use schedule #80 rated PVC pipe, where the 
intended operating temperature will not exceed 140 degrees F (60 degrees C). 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 79 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.3.9.7.4 
The maximum diameter for PVC pipe shall be 3 inches.  
2.3.9.7.5 
Headers that require the PVC pipe to be greater than 3 inches will need to be 
reviewed by both the Vehicle Operations Body Construction Controls Engineer 
and the VO Facilities engineer assigned to the program. An approved deviation is 
required. 
2.3.9.7.6 
For new installations, the system design will be based on a maximum water 
velocity of 5 feet per second (1.5 meters per second). 
2.3.9.7.7 
Systems shall be designed such that the minimum water velocity does not drop 
below 2 feet per second (0.6 meters per second). 
2.3.9.7.8 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible to size and install the fittings properly.  
2.3.9.7.9 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible to size and install flex hose properly. 
2.3.9.7.10 
The flex hose shall be mounted such that no additional stress will be placed upon 
the PVC header. 
2.3.9.7.11 
The PVC header shall use threaded molded tees for all connections to flex hose. 
Saddle connections are not permitted. 
2.3.9.7.12 
The transition from the PVC header to equipment and service stand ball valves 
shall be flex hose. 
2.3.9.7.13 
On PVC installations that are replacing copper headers, threaded brass fittings 
will be required to connect flex hose to the header. 
2.3.9.7.14 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall include expansion joints, when required based on 
engineering calculations.  Piston style expansion joints are not permitted. 
2.3.9.7.15 
All ball valves shall be bronze or brass. 
2.3.9.7.16 
Alternative Materials to PVC shall be reviewed and approved by the Ford 
Standards Group. 
2.3.9.8 
Kits 
2.3.9.8.1 
For kit installations, header flow rates greater than 5 feet per second (1.5 meters 
per second) will need to be reviewed by the Vehicle Operations Body 
Construction Controls Engineer assigned to the program. 
2.3.9.9 
Build 
2.3.9.9.1 
Metallic materials shall not be mixed, due to corrosion issues (i.e. when 
connecting brass (such as a swivel hose fitting) use brass). Steel fittings shall not 
be used. 
2.3.9.9.2 
The system shall be allowed to move freely or “float” to avoid potential stress 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 80 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
cracking. 
2.3.9.9.3 
Proper system support may require additional supports between tall posts. 
“Floater” T’s may be mounted to the air header and/or cable tray to provide 
additional support as required. 
2.3.9.9.4 
Sections of pipe shall be connected together using socketed PVC couplings and 
welded, per product manufacturer's approved solvent welding techniques. 
Flanged fittings may be used in lieu of socketed PVC couplings at predetermined 
shipping break points. Threaded plastic unions are not permitted. 
2.3.9.9.5 
In order to minimize the effects of the "water hammer", all ball valves shall be 
opened slowly.  
2.3.9.9.6 
All automated ball valves shall be provided with flow controllable solenoids, when 
connected directly to PVC. 
2.3.9.9.7 
The pipes shall not require painting but shall have flow direction arrows per the 
GVOSS Visual Factory standards located in GVOSS Section 1.4.  
2.3.9.9.8 
PVC shall not be run on the floor, use copper pipe. 
2.3.9.10 
Shipping and Installation 
2.3.9.10.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible to properly instruct the installation contractors 
on how to install and disassemble, for shipping purposes, the PVC piping 
system. Clear installation instructions shall be created by the Tier 1 Integrator 
and followed by their installation contractors, to ensure accuracy. 
2.3.9.10.2 
The PVC pipe header shall be thoroughly flushed with water to ensure all debris 
is removed so the water savers do not become contaminated with PVC pipe 
shavings. This shall be done prior to the installation of the flex hose connecting 
the header to the ball valves and the facilities header pipe. 
2.3.9.10.3 
When preparing the system for shipment, the PVC header pipe shall be cut at 
predetermined strategic break points identified by Tier 1 Integrator and 
reconnected using socketed PVC couplings and manufacturer approved solvent 
welding techniques.  Slip couplings may be used for minor repairs. 
2.3.9.10.4 
Static electricity will be generated when cutting PVC pipe. The shavings created 
will tend to cling to the inside of the pipe. A thorough flushing of the pipes with 
water shall occur to ensure all debris is removed. 
2.3.9.10.5 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide the necessary solvent cement, primer and 
couplings when reinstalling the system at the end user’s facility. All solvent 
cement and primer shall have a Ford Tox number. Tier 1 Integrator shall provide 
documentation stating that the glue and primer used is compatible with the 
chemicals used to treat the water at the plant receiving the equipment.  A water 
quality report can be obtained from Vehicle Operations Body Construction 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 81 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
Facilities. 
2.3.9.10.6 
The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible to inspect the entire water system on their 
equipment and repair all leaks at the end user’s facility before buy-off. 
2.3.10 
Controls Files and Documentation 
2.3.10.1 
Controls print packages are to consist of the application design drawings 
(Electrical, Pneumatic, Water and Lubrication). Next-Gen lines in AutoCAD 
following Next-Gen standards and sections. GVOSS lines following GVOSS 
standards and sections. 
Shall also include the following: 
• 
One set of each Ford standard design / enclosure used in the application. 
• 
One set of each Ford to specify design / enclosure used in the application. 
• 
One set of each robot interface design. 
• 
One set of any PLC directly controlled interface used in the application. This 
to include any auxiliary equipment which is not a Ford standard design / 
enclosure such as a load assist, hemmer, panel, box, etc. 
 Additional documents shall include the following: 
• 
Sequence Charts per GVOSS 2.4.1.6 
• 
Cylinder and Switch Layouts per GVOSS 2.4.1.6 
• 
Lubrication Sheets (1A9 Drawings) 
2.3.10.2 
At integrator build facility one print package for each line/sub-assembly, tool or 
conveyor is to be available on the floor during the build and buy off process. 
2.3.10.3 
Upon shipment of the tooling or conveyor to the plant, one set of software files 
stored on electronic media is to be provided to Ford VO Controls program lead. 
The files are to include all up to date PLC files, PLC Ford compare files, Drive 
files such as dno files, Ford Ethernet Calculator files, Weld WPD Calculator files, 
Control PDP Calculator files, any other Controls Calculations files and check 
sheets, HMI files (such as .mer files), EDS files for new components, Nut Runner 
program files, Vision program files and robot programs (full backup). At the 
receiving plant one print package for each line/sub-assembly, tool or conveyor is 
to be available on the floor during the install and start up process. 
2.3.10.4 
At the receiving plant one print package for each cell, tool or conveyor is to be 
available on the floor during the install and start up process. 
2.3.10.5 
At the receiving plant the software files are to be backed up as changes occur on 
the CRW computer to maintain a consistent duplication of operating files. These 
files are to include PLC files (PLC files are to include up to date vfd files), HMI 
mer and apa files, joining (welding, riveting, sealing, etc.) weld application files, 
robot programs, robot images and any other robot external application files. Old 
files are to be deleted as changes are proven functional. These files are to be 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 82 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
stored and organized on the CRW computer per the structure identified within the 
programming guide. 
2.3.10.5.1 
All system controllers that contain software files must have a connection path to 
the CRW and must have the ability to backup software application files. 
Whenever possible, auto backup features must be utilized (ex. Robot programs, 
Henrob Programs, etc.) **Note – in NextGen Controls Architecture systems this 
is to be achieved utilizing an upper-level data port connection. In GVOSS 
Controls Architecture systems this is to be achieved utilizing the single port 
connection** 
If auto backup feature is not supported by the application, every system controller 
at a minimum must have a means to manually backup software files from the 
local programming workstation. This can be achieved utilizing an additional I/O 
level programming port connection. (ex. Nutrunner systems, Vision Systems, 
Laser Scanners, etc.) ** Note – CRW’s have multiple Network Interface 
Controllers beyond the standard PLC level network and the data network 
specifically for the purpose of connecting these types of applications when 
needed.  ** 
2.3.10.5.2 
CRWs with MPN access shall support MDT’s PDS autosave.  PDS is a software 
package that shall backup the CRW folders to a plant server.  The integrator 
shall complete and submit a “CRW Folder Backups Worksheet For PDS.xlsx” 
form to a VOME Controls representative for each CRW installed.  After PDS is 
configured by Ford IT, the integrator shall confirm the functionality of this system 
by reviewing the weekly PDS reports until validation.  Upon validation, the 
integrator shall submit a copy of PDS report to a VOME Controls representative. 
2.3.10.6 
Upon plant buyoff of the tooling up to date print packages are to be provided on 
the plant floor and a copy of the print packages in pdf format loaded in the 
line/sub-assembly CRWs. One set of software files are to be loaded in 
Teamcenter per the structure identified in the programming guide. The files are to 
include all up to date: PLC files, Drive files such as dno files, Ford Ethernet 
Calculator files, Weld WPD Calculator files, Control PDP Calculator files, any 
other Controls Calculations files, HMI files (such as apa files), Routers, and robot 
programs (full backup), Robot Images weld control, sealer, vision and all other 
external applications. Teamcenter file storage shall be updated by the supplier 
until supplier responsibility ends. Completion of full service requirements three 
years from Job1 on new tools or at Job 1 for kits.

2.4.1.1.1 
For Control Logix applications the processor power shall be 24VDC. 
2.4.1.1.2 
All processors shall be programmable at floor level with all panel doors closed. 
Adequate duplex outlets with program ports shall be provided for programming 
support equipment. 
2.4.1.1.3 
Provide a cycle count circuit for all tooling and a circuit to operate the fixture in 
"Auto-Repeat" cycle. Conditions required are listed below: 
• 
Fixture must be in "Automatic". 
• 
All safety devices, pressure switches and light curtains must be operable. 
• 
"Auto Repeat Cycle" to start only after Automatic Cycle is initiated, without 
part present, “Auto Repeat’ is selected on HMI and "Initiate Auto Repeat" 
HMI pushbutton is depressed.” Auto Repeat Cycle" to continue until “Auto 
Repeat” selector switch is deselected, and fixture comes to rest, or a physical 
part is detected. The handstands must be depressed to initiate the first cycle 
in the “Auto Repeat Cycle”. 
• 
A time delay is to be provided between cycles. 
• 
On multiple station lines, the controls design shall include a line "Auto Run" 
and "Empty" mode. Please see the HMI implementation guidelines for 
location of the "Auto Run" and "Empty" selector. 
2.4.1.2 
Operator Workstation 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 84 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.4.1.2.1 
Any fixture with T-stands will give the operator, (prior to the machine going into 
its “Automatic Weld” Cycle for Body Construction Engineering) the capability to 
maintain a yellow mushroom head button and return the fixture to its “At-Rest” 
position without going into the "Manual Mode".  
2.4.1.2.2 
Any fixture with a single button handstand will be able to return the fixture to its 
“At-Rest” position with the yellow "Reset" button. The unclamp buttons may be 
used for this function.  
2.4.1.2.3 
If the fixture has a model selection set-up, the selection shall be through the 
Operator HMI. Model select on T-stand.  with approved deviation.  
2.4.1.2.4 
All present operator safety standards shall be adhered to. 
2.4.1.3 
The automatic operating time of each station or machine shall be monitored, 
starting with the cycle start operating and ending with the completion of the 
machine cycle. When a machine stoppage occurs, an audible alarm shall sound 
on the Process Control Board (PCB) and the PCB shall indicate the station is 
faulted. 
2.4.1.4 
All multi-conductor plugs, and receptacles used at fixture and/or machine 
terminal boxes and enclosures shall be completely wired. Spare wires are to be 
labeled at each end with plug and pin number. No multi-conductor plugs or 
receptacle over (64) pins can be used without prior approval of Structures 
Standards Controls Design Section. For receptacles of 24 pins or more, ten 
percent (10%) spare conductors is the minimum requirement, twenty percent 
(20%) of those spares shall be terminated, the rest are to be insulated from each 
other and laid neatly in the bottom of the panel. 
2.4.1.5 
All lamacoid tags, which are not attached with a hold-down nut of some device, 
shall be attached with Loctite 454. 
2.4.1.6 
All commercial items (transfer devices, magazines, etc.) shall comply with 
appropriate specifications, including standard components. 
2.4.1.7 
Tags for illuminated pushbuttons shall be engraved with the output number for 
the light and the input number for the pushbutton. 
2.4.1.8 
Entry / exit light screens to a transfer line shall have a reset box located at light 
screen box shall have a stacked LED “Red” light mounted on top indicating a 
fault condition and a “Yellow” light indicating a muted light screen. Remote reset 
of light screen is unacceptable. 
2.4.1.9 
Each Operator Load Station: 
• Shall require a small Ford Standard Operator HMI Display for part present 
indicator, current and next parts to load.

2.4.1.9.1 
Next Part and Part Loaded information shall originate from the IBSS broadcast 
and shall include export models.

Any Operator Load Station (online or offline) shall have an HMI dedicated which 
displays individual part present indicators to indicate which parts are loaded and

flash when parts are not loaded. The HMI shall be visible to the Operator.

2.4.1.11 
Any fault that drops automatic mode shall be latched and display appropriate 
message.  
2.4.1.12 
For Body Construction Engineering, all buffers in the manufacturing line shall be 
monitored In FIS and status displayed on a process control board. 
2.4.1.13 
All proximity sensing and camera systems shall comply with manufacturers’ 
application guide. 
2.4.1.14 
For Body Construction Engineering, new model light shall be added to all model 
load stack lights if additional model is to be built using same tools. 
2.4.1.14.1 
For Control Logix applications the new model indicator will be on the operator 
HMI. 
2.4.1.15 
For Body Construction Engineering, manufacturing lines that use stud welders, 
nut welders, riveting, adhesives, mastics, and sealers shall include a two position 
“Run-Out” selector switch. The two positions shall be “Run Continuous” and 
“Run-Out”. When “Run-Out” is selected the equipment will not load another part 
into the stud welder, nut welder, riveting, adhesive, mastic, or sealer station and 
clear the line of parts from that station to the unload operation. The purpose is to 
minimize the number of non-conforming parts produced once noticed by the 
unload operator. After corrections have been completed the selector switch is 
placed back into “Run Continuous”. The selector switch shall be part of the HMI 
screens. 
2.4.1.16 
Fuse coordination is required. Either adherence to the fuse suppliers’ 
coordination ratio chart or commercially available software to ensure a dead 
short circuit at the load will clear ONLY the fuses at the load shall be used. 
Controls engineer shall sign off on software fuse coordination. 
2.4.2 
Power Distribution 
All multi-stage lines shall have a single 480/120-volt distribution system or 
equivalent based on regional requirements for all panel and fixture lighting. 
2.4.2.1 
Circuit breakers shall be used instead of fuses to isolate and protect all output 
cards and circuits. Circuit breakers shall be used as protection on all 110-volt 
branch circuits. 
2.4.2.2 
Distribution Systems greater than 400 Volts shall be included in the design 
showing: 
• 
All robot distribution centers – robot cells with more than two robots require a 
common lockout and restart 
• 
All sources of power being dropped into all weld centers (For Body 
Construction Engineering) 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 87 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
All floor level lockable disconnect switches. Reference FAS08-100. 
• 
Detail callout of power lockout tags for each disconnect in the system. 
Including the words "Not Main Disconnect Switch" 
• 
Review current ECPL (Energy Control Power Lockout) Guidelines for 
complete tag requirements. 
• 
Detail tags for processor of what they feed 
• 
Detail tags on Panel to tell where being fed from (I/O & Power) 
2.4.2.3 
Wire sizes shall be shown on all wiring diagrams. Adequate fuse protection shall 
be included on all 400 volt or greater wiring runs. Distribution blocks shall be 
used. No daisy-chaining shall be allowed. 
2.4.2.4 
All magnetic contactors/relays shall have surge suppressers on the coils. 
2.4.3 
Welding Power 
2.4.3.1 
Welding indicators shall include: 
• 
Pressure switch PLC input HMI indicator for each pressure regulator in the 
welding circuit. 
• 
Maintenance required light and PLC input for each weld timer. 
• 
One current sensor from each weld timer: being the weld control's weld 
complete (EOH) signal. 
• 
Weld fault HMI indicator for each weld timer. Reset weld fault pushbutton 
shall initiate re-fire of only timers with a weld fault; putting the weld control in 
external no weld shall allow a weld complete to clear the weld fault. 
• 
An HMI diagnostic message shall be included for each weld timer. 
• 
Timer fault HMI indicator and PLC input for each weld timer to indicate major 
weld control malfunction. 
2.4.3.2 
Conductors for welding power shall not occupy the same wireway, junction box, 
distribution box or conduit as conductors supplying power to motors or controls. 
2.4.3.3 
Each automatic welding tool shall include a selector switch and pushbutton to 
accomplish a manual weld.  
2.4.3.4 
All fixture weld applications shall utilize a separate HMI screen to display and 
reset individual steppers. 
2.4.3.5 
All hard fixture weld applications shall utilize individual steppers to optimize tip 
life. 
2.4.3.6 
Any time a gun change occurs (tool changers only), a diagnostic message will 
indicate gun change has taken place. (Intent was to keep a record as to when a 
gun change took place). 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 88 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.4.3.7 
Control system (HMI, etc.) shall include an “Auto Tip Dress Bypass” selector 
switch. When enabled robot will continue to run, when stepper steps out robot 
will go to maintenance position and remain until tips are dressed / changed and 
stepper is reset. 
2.4.4 
Cabling 
2.4.4.1 
Double ended sacrificial cords shall be either one meter or 1/2 meter in length.  
2.4.4.2 
Panel, Junction and Terminal Box Wiring 
2.4.4.2.1 
Wiring bundles in junction and terminal boxes which move with tooling shall be 
secured in such a manner so they will not move when the tooling cycles. 
Movement of wire bundles could result in wire fatigue and failure at terminal 
connections. 
2.4.5 
Indicator Lights/Displays 
2.4.5.1 
Indicating lights shall be tagged per current controls standards. Next-Gen 
controls see section 8. GVOSS controls see section 14. When using lamacoid 
engraved tags for indicating lights: An individual “White” lamacoid engraved tag 
with description plus device address in the lower right corner. Indicating lights 
shall be tagged per current controls standards. Next-Gen controls see section 8. 
GVOSS controls see section 14. When using lamacoid engraved tags for 
indicating lights: An individual “White” lamacoid engraved tag with description 
plus device address in the lower right corner. For inputs, the address of the 
output required for the input to be made shall be added to the lower left corner of 
the tag. When using soft lamacoid (thick sticker) tag for indicating lights: An 
individual “White” soft lamacoid tag with description and a separate “White” soft 
lamacoid tag with device address on the right side. For inputs a separate “White” 
soft lamacoid tag with address of the output required for the input to be made 
shall be added to the left side.  
Any tags for Emergency Stop pushbuttons for input/output/description shall be 
yellow with black lettering and shall not be on the standard required yellow ring or 
red pushbutton. See Section 1 Emergency Stop pushbuttons. 
2.4.5.2 
A clear protective cover shall be supplied to prevent implosion of the fluorescent 
tube(s). 
2.4.5.3 
The fixture HMI shall include provisions for manual operation of the complete 
fixture. The scroll list shall be installed in the same sequence of as the fixture 
functions. Scroll list shall be used for complicated sequences.  
2.4.5.4 
Buttons or lights shall be no lower than 40 inches (1 meter) from floor or lights 
higher than 76 inches (2 meters) from the floor. (Buttons are always below 
lights). 
2.4.5.5 
HMI shall be provided at the central console and at each station control panel. 
Diagnostic Fault Codes (message numbers) shall be determined in the PLC via 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 89 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
action/reaction detection logic. Each input device, except manual fixture control 
inputs, shall be assigned a unique fault code. The display shall be programmed 
to supply complete description of the input, the input address and the address of 
the output that was activated to initiate the motion. The display is to mirror the 
fault data output to the factory information system.  
2.4.5.6 
For Body Construction Engineering, Process Control Boards shall be supplied, 
one per Transfer Area of Automation.  
2.4.5.7 
See the Process Control Board (PCB) specification for PCB requirements. 
2.4.5.8 
HMI indicators are required for each safety gate and safety pin.  
2.4.5.9 
HMI indicators shall be included for all incoming/outgoing interlocks including 
model information. 
2.4.5.10 
Stack Light Operation 
Loader Stack Lights (Reference Drawing: 70ZF-20013143) 
• 
Red Light – on Solid means “Do Not Enter” 
• 
Amber Light – on Solid means “All Correct Parts Loaded” 
• 
Green Light – on Solid means “Clear to Enter” 
• 
Red Light and Green Light – Flashing Simultaneously means  
 
“Not in Automatic” 
• 
Red Light and Green Light – Flashing Alternately means “Light  
 
Screen Fault” or “The equipment is waiting for the  
 
“Ready Button” to be depressed” 
• 
Amber Light – Flashing means “All Correct Parts and/or Details NOT 
 
Loaded” 
• 
Amber Light – Rapid Flashing after “Ready to Run” Button has been 
depressed means “Load Hoist Not Clear”. 
• 
White Light – Flashing means station at 70% of allocated load time. 
• 
White Light – on Solid means allocated load time exceeded. 
On Line Tools 
• 
Solid Red – Machine Fault 
• 
Flashing Red – Robot or Station in Bypass(1 second on and 1 second off)” 
 
Rapid Flashing means “Station Starved” 
 
For FEA this applies to Error Proofing Bypassed. 
 
Off Line Tools 
• 
Solid Red – Machine Fault 
• 
Flashing Red – Robot or Station in Bypass (if applicable) 
 
(1 second on and 1 second off) 
• 
Solid Amber – Not Auto and Power On 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 90 of 143

• 
Flashing Amber – Robot or Station Tip Change Warning (if applicable) 
 
(1 second on and 1 second off) 
• 
Rapid Flashing Amber – Light Screen Fault 
 
(0.3 seconds on and 0.3 seconds off) 
• 
Solid Green – Machine in Auto (Everything OK) 
NOTE:  For Body Construction Engineering, for stations with nut runners or nut 
feeders, add blue light to top of stack and drive solid with nut runner or nut feeder

2.4.6 
24 VDC Control Systems 
2.4.6.1 
All DC devices are to operate at 24 volts. 
2.4.6.2 
Proximity switches shall be 3-wire sourcing type with 4-pin micro connector.  
2.4.6.3 
See the Electrical approved source list for proximity switch cord manufacturer 
and wire size.  
2.4.6.4 
The DC I/O will be sinking inputs and sourcing outputs. Approved devices are 
listed on approved components list and approved on a program basis. 
2.4.6.5 
All DC indicator lights, except ground lights, will be LED 24-volt DC full voltage 
type. 
2.4.6.6 
Pushbuttons used with DC inputs shall use low voltage contact blocks. 
2.4.6.7 
Heat dissipation shall be determined for sizing all distributed I/O enclosures.  
2.4.6.7.1 
Reference 8.3.3.17.2 for wiring color codes. 
2.4.6.8 
The DC power supplies shall be 24 volts. LED ground detector lights shall be 
used on all field device ungrounded supplies to detect grounds. 
2.4.6.9 
There will be no more than one switch in series to an input. 
2.4.6.10 
Individual input indicators will not be required if HMI is provided. Use HMI 
standard. 
2.4.6.11 
Where machine control devices are used to control low current 24-volt circuits, 
gold flash contacts or reed cartridge type contacts shall be used.

2.5.3 
Operational Equipment 
2.5.3.1 
Automatic Units  
• 
When a failed secure is detected: 
• 
The fastener shall be reversed at least three threads automatically and retry 
the failed secure up to 3 times. 
• 
If after the initial process and retries (+3) have been executed there are still 
failed secures: 
• 
Write all failed secures through AIS to the in-plant QLS tracking system and 
report on HMI, FIS and PCB, then release vehicle to the next station.  
• 
For off-line sub assembly batch build systems there will be no AIS tracking. 
All failed secures to be marked as a suspect part and tracked for 
inspection/repair. 
• 
When the minimal successful secures (defined by process) are completed on 
all mating surfaces: 
• 
The vehicle may be released automatically from station to be repaired 
downstream with warnings on failed secures reported on HMI, FIS and PCB. 
• 
Machine Control Logic shall be included to bypass only ONE Nut runner per 
group performing the joining operation per flange or joint area. If a nut runner 
is bypassed write all failed secures through AIS to the in-plant QLS tracking 
system and report on HMI, FIS and PCB, then release vehicle to the next 
station. 
• 
This condition is to be reviewed, tested and approved during the mechanical 
and controls design buyoff meetings. Typical applications would be 
Automated Hood install, Automated Door Hang, Automated Liftgate Hang. 
The intent is to only transfer a vehicle if all parts are safely attached to the 
body and proper faults enunciated. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 99 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled
