2.6 
ERROR-PROOFING 
2.6.1 
General 
2.6.1.1 
Unless specified otherwise in this section, Error-Proofing requirements shall 
conform to the standards and specifications set forth in the Ford Motor Company 
GVOSS and VOPFAG-250. 
2.6.1.2 
Error-Proofing of part loading and component installation is done to prevent 
process errors, assure product quality and reduce equipment downtime. Error-
Proofing has two facets: 
1. Prevention – Uses a device that will not allow for a concern to be created or 
produced. It will either stop the system for correction or automatically adjust 
the error to prevent it from being a defect. Examples: Part locators in fixtures 
and jigs, DC rundown for torque, part present sensors, templates, material 
pick gates, machine logic, etc. 
2. Validation – Uses a device to determine if an action was successfully 
completed to satisfy process requirements before it leaves the station or area 
and give immediate feedback so that the root cause can be identified, and 
corrective actions can be implemented to prevent the defect from being 
produced again. Examples: Automation 100% inspection for dimensions. 
 
2.6.1.3 
AIS Boxes 
2.6.1.3.1 
All AIS Boxes shall adhere to the AIS Box Install Specification. 
2.6.1.3.2 
Error Proofing Configuration & Validation Spreadsheet shall be completed for all 
AIS box process configurations. 
2.6.1.3.3 
Complete Error-Proofing Configuration Matrix to document all configuration 
requirement and changes. Follow the R & R listed in the Help tab of the 
document. 
2.6.1.3.4 
Reference the Error-Proofing Configuration Matrix – Notes and Instruction 
document to gather and maintain the information in the Matrix. 
2.6.1.3.5 
All nut runner applications shall also adhere to the AIS Box Install Specifications. 
2.6.2 
Error-Proofing Methodology 
Errors should be addressed at the source. Start with “detect” devices to initiate 
corrective action after the error is made. The goal is to implement devices that 
“prevent” the defect from being made. The typical procedure is listed below. 
1. Describe the error to be prevented or detected. 
2. Quantify the concern and isolate the action that creates it. Use historical data 
from the assembly and stamping plants and Lessons Learned from past 
programs. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 102 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3. Identify and review any current standards and procedures that control the 
error. 
4. List all possible factors that could cause the error. 
5. Identify possible solutions. 
6. Evaluate each solution with regards to set criteria (cost, complexity, timing, 
maintainability, etc.). 
7. Choose the best solution. 
8. Develop a plan for implementation. 
9. Inform the assembly plant that the error-proofing device will be implemented, 
how it works, how to maintain it, how to check that it is working, etc. 
10. Design and build the error-proofing device. 
11. Validate it prior to buy-off.  
12. Standardize the error-proof device if applicable. 
2.6.3 
Common Categories of Errors 
The following are categories under which possible Errors can occur. 
• 
Adjustments 
– 
Moving parts, tooling, or fixtures into their correct position. 
– 
Anytime an adjustment is made, an error could occur. 
– 
Using subjective opinion or intuition to make adjustments can result in 
errors and defects. 
• 
Tooling and Tooling Changes 
– 
Tooling is any working part of a power-driven machine. 
– 
Tooling wear and breakage result in defects. 
• 
Dimensions/specifications/critical conditions 
– 
Dimensions: a measurement used to determine the precise location for a 
part/operation (height, width, length, depth) 
– 
Specifications and critical conditions include temperature, pressure, 
speed, tension, coordinates, number and volume. 
– 
Any deviation from the exact dimensions or specification for the product 
or operation would lead to an error. 
• 
Many/Mixed Parts 
– 
When using a wide range of parts in varying quantities, the selection of 
the right part and the right quantity is essential. 
– 
The opportunity for error increases as the number of highly similar parts 
increases. Example: Weldnuts. 
• 
Multiple Steps 
– 
The process may have a number of small operations that must be done 
in a specific order. 
– 
Errors may arise if a step was skipped or sequenced in the wrong order. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 103 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
– 
Operators not following the operator worksheets. 
• 
Infrequent Production 
– 
If an operation is not regularly completed, the task may not be completed 
in the proper sequence. 
– 
Operators from different departments or different shifts to fill in. 
• 
Symmetry/Asymmetry 
– 
Symmetry: opposite sides of an object are identical in size and shape. 
– 
If opposite sides look identical, the sides could be confused during the 
operation resulting in an error. 
– 
Asymmetry: opposite sides of a part may look identical, but they are 
actually different sizes or shapes. 
– 
Slight differences in size and or shape may lead to confusion on which 
side of the part to use. That confusion may lead to an error. 
• 
Environmental Conditions 
– 
The physical circumstances in and around the workplace that influence 
quality and performance. 
– 
Conditions such as poor lighting, dirt, and oil may result in defects. 
2.6.4 
Process Error-Proofing 
2.6.4.1 
Parts load operations into the line shall be error proofed. This can be 
accomplished using pins, wipers or other applicable methods. 
2.6.4.2 
The proper loading of parts shall be verified using controls sensing. 
2.6.4.3 
In the event of an improper load of a part the machine controls shall not progress 
in the machine sequence and display a properly enunciated fault. No motion shall 
be enabled until the error is corrected to prevent the error from affecting the 
process. 
2.6.4.4 
In process where controls sensing is not feasible (small parts conveyors and 
magazines for example) an acceptable methodology is to use a hinged "profile" 
guard. A profile guard is used prior to entering the station fence line where 
operator action can be taken to correct the error without requiring lock-out of the 
automation. The profile guard has a cut-out in the shape of a properly loaded 
part. When an improperly loaded part attempts to pass-through the guard 
opening the guard pivots on the hinge, which is controls sensed and triggers a 
fault condition. 
2.6.4.5 
In instances where parts loaded "nest" within each other it is often difficult to use 
sensing techniques.  
Identify a distinct feature on the nested part that can be sensed independently 
from the nesting part. If none exists a product change should be pursued to add 
a feature if possible. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 104 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
Often this feature can be sensed using a sensor attached to a pivoting device 
which will sense the part prior to the machine proceeding in the process 
sequence. 
It should be noted that this is not a preferred method as the pivoting mechanism 
adds additional failure modes to the machine. 
2.6.4.6 
A mechanical amplifier can be used in instances where there exists a small 
dimensional difference that requires sensing. A mechanical amplifier is basically 
a lever which uses the ratio of the fulcrum point to gain a larger degree of motion 
from one end to the other. In the example below a 5mm movement on one end 
results in 15mm at the other. The purpose is for the part to set on the short end 
of the lever which will result in a larger movement at the other end. A switch flag 
can be attached to the other end and sensed with a switch. This allows switch 
sensing for small movements. 
 
 
5 mm  
30 mm  
9 0 mm  
1 5 mm  
Mechanical Amplifier Example 
 
 
2.6.4.7 
Part sensing using the edge of the sensing field is prohibited. This has proven to 
be unreliable and problematic in the long term of equipment operation. Part 
sensing should be at the middle of the sensing field. 
2.6.4.8 
Part Load Sequencing 
2.6.4.8.1 
When part loading is sensitive to the sequence in which the parts are loaded, the 
sequence shall be sensed, and a fault triggered when a sequence error occurs. 
2.6.4.9 
Dimensional Quality 
2.6.4.9.1 
Dimensional quality of finished assemblies can be checked using optical 
measurement methods.  
2.6.4.9.2 
Physical measurements are used as a validation method. 
2.6.4.10 
Incoming Inspection 
2.6.4.10.1 
Incoming inspection is when a part is checked in the load operation for 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 105 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
completeness from another operation. This is prohibited. Examples of this type of 
inspection would be the presence of; weld nuts, weld studs, pierce studs, small 
brackets and pierced holes on either a PIA part or a part manufactured 
elsewhere in the plant on a subassembly tool. 
2.6.4.10.2 
The exceptions to this are: 
1. It is predefined as part of the VOME Process. 
2. To locate or relocate an existing process part-present sensor to sense the 
desired feature with the approval of the Ford ME and the plant. 
2.6.4.10.3 
Incoming inspection requires a signed process deviation form. 
2.6.4.10.4 
Incoming inspection by operators is prohibited. Due to human factors in the 
production environment this is inherently unreliable. 
2.6.4.11 
Manual Process Visual Aids and Validation 
2.6.4.11.1 
Manually installed or applied Body Shop Materials, Parts and Assemblies which 
are in-accessible after next operation shall comply with the requirements of 
this section. 
2.6.4.11.2 
Operations requiring Visual Aid Sheets and Electronic Detection to validate 
installation: 
1. Manually installed Baffles. 
2. Manually applied Adhesive that carries a Control Bond designation. 
3. Manually Snapped-in Baffles with expanding Sealer for NVH. 
4. Manually loaded Weld-in Baffles with expanding Sealer for NVH. 
5. Manually applied Sealer tapes with strong locating features such as push 
pins or with the required positional tolerance of the application greater than 
+/-5mm, followed immediately by Automation. 
2.6.4.11.3 
Operations requiring only Visual Aid Sheets:  
1. Beta Patches for Dent/Ding.  
2. Beta Braces for Structure. 
3. Manually applied Adhesive and/or Sealer Non-Control Bond. 
2.6.4.11.4 
Visual Aid Sheets shall follow the requirements of GVOSS Section 1.4.19. 
2.6.4.11.5 
When required, VOME shall determine the type of electronic validation to be 
used. Examples of electronic validation are laser or camera detection. 
2.6.4.11.6 
Operations requiring Visual Aid Sheets and Vision Sensing for Error-Proofing: 
1. Only manual application of Sealer Tapes with weak locating features, for 
which the required positional Tolerance is less than +/- 5mm. 
2. Multiple manual applications of Sealer / Structural Tapes, Adhesive / Sealer 
Beads in a station, followed immediately by Automation. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 106 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.6.4.12 
Process Templates 
2.6.4.12.1 
Any manual process should be evaluated to determine if the operation can be 
error-proofed using process templates. 
 
2.6.4.12.2 
In manual process operations an operator aid should be supplied to prevent 
errors and maintain consistency in the process. 
2.6.4.12.3 
For sealing of small parts, a simple table with a path guide constructed of metal 
rod bent in the sealant path affixed to the table will suffice. For larger parts a 
removable template that locates to part features may be required.  
2.6.4.12.4 
In manual welding applications guides are provided that are used to locate the 
weld gun adapters for welding. 
2.6.4.12.5 
For placement of Beta Braces and similar applications, Class 2 lasers shall be 
used to visually identify the location where the Beta Brace is to be attached to the 
body pane, see example below. 
 
 
See also the SBU Error-Proofing Standard Practice EP-157-1 and EP-157-2 for 
an example of the application. 
Class 2 Lasers shall not be positioned within the operators' line of sight in the 
operators’ working envelope. The lasers shall be positioned either higher or lower 
than the operators' line of sight to ensure the operators’ eyes are not exposed to 
it directly.  
Use of this technique requires a risk assessment with Plant Safety buy-off to 
ensure the installation meets the requirements for Class 2 laser exposure. 
2.6.4.13 
When a racked or stacked part is obtained by a robot for automatic loading and 
the potential for obtaining more than one part exists, multiple part detection shall 
be provided. 
2.6.4.13.1 
The detection method shall be agreed upon by the Ford VIS, ME, plant and the 
Tier 1 Integrator. 
2.6.4.13.2 
The method selected shall be a Lessons Learned and entered into the Ford 
Tracking System by the ME for the purpose of future categorization of 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN

methodologies. 
 
2.6.4.14 
Visual Factory  
2.6.4.14.1 
Operator Load Sheets 
2.6.4.14.1.1 Every load operation shall have an Operator Load Sheet. 
Refer to Visual Factory requirements (Section 1.4) for Operator Load Sheets that 
will aid in error proofing equipment. 
2.6.4.14.2 
SPL (Single Point Lessons) 
2.6.4.14.2.1 SPL techniques can be applied to error-proof operator activities. These will be 
applications where device use cannot error-proof an activity or operator load 
sheets cannot be applied. 
2.6.4.15 
PFMEA 
2.6.4.15.1 
All instances of process related errors that require error-proofing as a control 
shall be identified as failure modes in the PFMEA.  
2.6.4.15.2 
The error-proofing technique shall be the process control for the error. 
2.6.5 
Maintenance Error-Proofing 
2.6.5.1 
When reviewing equipment designs, consideration should be made to error-
proofing maintenance activities, component replacement and fault recovery. 
2.6.5.2 
Component Positioning 
2.6.5.2.1 
Components that require positional accuracy when replaced or removed for 
service (weld gun cradles, speed reducers, pillow blocks, etc.) should have 
jackscrews placed in two axes to: 
1. Preserve location when the component is re-installed or replaced. 
2. Enhance ability to make small adjustments for alignment when replacing or 
re-installing components. 
2.6.5.2.2 
Where position is important and adjustment is not necessary, heel blocks are 
sufficient. 
2.6.5.3 
Visual Factory 
2.6.5.3.1 
Visual Factory techniques can be applied to error-proof maintenance activities.  
2.6.5.3.2 
These will be applications where device use cannot error-proof an activity. 
2.6.5.4 
SPL 
2.6.5.4.1 
SPL techniques can be applied to error-proof maintenance activities. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 108 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.6.5.4.2 
These will be applications where device use cannot error-proof an activity or 
Visual Factory techniques cannot be applied. 
2.6.5.5 
MFMEA 
2.6.5.5.1 
All instances of maintenance related errors that require error-proofing as a 
control shall be identified as failure modes in the MFMEA.  
2.6.5.5.2 
The error-proofing technique shall be the design or machinery control for the 
error.

2.7 
STANDARD LAYOUTS 
NOTE:  Deviation Procedure:  Any deviation to the flex-compliant standard cells 
or templates shall be submitted by a requesting activity to the Car / Truck 
Processing Group, utilizing the Flex Compliant Standard Deviation Request 
Form. These deviation requests will be reviewed by the Car / Truck Supervisor / 
Managers / Chief Engineer and if approved, will be signed off for program use 
only. Deviation request forms should be sent to:  Process & Strategy 
Development Engineer. 
2.7.1 
Pallets for Standard Cell Roll Tables 
2.7.1.1 
General 
2.7.1.1.1 
This guideline provides Ford unique requirements and specifications for pallets 
used for geometry setting in the standard layout tooling. 
2.7.1.1.2 
When a Flow Drill Screw (FDS) process is being performed on the Product while 
on the pallet, a Finite Element Analysis (FEA) shall be completed on the pallet to 
ensure that the forces of the FDS Process do not deflect the pallet and pallet 
stanchions.  The applied FDS forces are determined by the FDS fastener size, 
the gage stack-up, and the types of materials being joined, and needs to be 
derived by the FDS OEM for the FEA model. Pallet FEA analysis is to comply with 
all requirements as specified by VOSS Sec. 1.1.13 Finite Element Analysis (FEA). 
2.7.1.2 
Body-in-White (BIW) Pallets 
2.7.1.2.1 
The pallet (see Figure 2.7.2.1) consists of three distinct sections, the fore-most 
area for the front structure locators, the mid area for the center pan locators, and 
the rear area for the rear pan locators. In some cases, there may be shared 
locators between sections.  
Note: It is preferable to use a single pallet through all body construction and 
framing operations.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 110 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
           
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure 2.7.2.1 Pallet 
LEGEND 
A = Pin Clamps 
B =  Z Pad 
C =  Fixed Pin 
D = Z Net 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 111 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.7.1.2.2 
Pallet-to-Body Requirements 
1. Minimum of four pin clamps; air-engaged; two near front, two near rear (see 
A in Figure 2.8.2.1). Certain designs might require additional pin clamps at 
the front structure. Pin clamps are preferred over separate pin and adjacent 
clamp configuration. Pin clamps do not require as much space as the 
separate units, thus allowing better access for welding. 
2. All net surfaces, pins, and pin clamps fingers to correspond to product 
locators. The body locators are to be adjustable in all (3) coordinates (X, Y, 
Z) and shall utilize (1) 5.0mm grind spacer which is to be used for pallet 
certification. 
• Pallet locator details are to be designed to the “Pallet” locator variant in 
the Assembly LD file (LDXXXX-9X___-X_XX-XX; LDXXXX-8X___-X_XX-
XX; LDXXXX-6X___-X_XX-XX). 
3. All Body locators are to be referenced from pallet-to-tool 4-Way, 2-Way and 
(Z) nets. 
4. Zero line of the pallet is to correspond with the center of the vehicle. 
5. Slides for the Rear Pan or Rear Sill locators may be used for multi-
vehicle/model pallets. Consider weight factors when designing the slides.  
2.7.1.2.3 
Pallet-to-Tool Requirements 
1. Pallets shall include a 4-way hole at front center for mating to a pin at the 
stop station.  
2. Pallets shall include a 2-way blade with slot for (Y) control at the rear center 
for mating to guide rollers or a 2-way locating pin at the stop station. 
3. Pallets shall include six (Z) nets for positioning at the stop station. Nets shall 
include grind spacers in construction to allow for pallet certification (see D in 
Figure 2.8.2.1). 
4. Pallets shall include (4) Certification Target Datum Holes or Laser Tracker 
Artifacts.  
5. Construction of Certification Target Datum Holes shall follow GVOSS Section 
1.2.5 Tool Certification requirements. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 112 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled

the pallet CAD file. At tool buy-off, pallets are certified with the use of CMM/Laser 
Tracker fixtures. Once pallets are in the assembly plants, they are inspected on 
an ongoing basis via automatic vision stations (section 2.8.2.4).

The CMM/Laser Fixture shall use the same pallet-to-tool pins and nets as are

2.7.1.4 
Pallet Inspection Stations 
Vision stations verify the pallets on an ongoing basis in the assembly plant. 
Typical aspects of an inspection station include the following:  
a. Use same pallet-to-tool pins and nets as are used in production. 
b. Include brushes forward of each pin and Z net to remove debris from the 
station pads. 
c. Measure center point on each pad (see Figure 2.7.1.4 below). 
 
TYPICAL 
MEASUREMENT 
PATTERN FOR PADS. 
MEASURE 3 mm UP FROM NET PAD.  
 
THIS SHOULD BE APPROXIMATELY ONE 
HALF OF THE PIN ENGAGEMENT AREA. 
 
PERCEPTRON SCANS THE SURFACE AND 
CALCULATES THE CENTER. 
S3 mm

Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025

 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
d. Measure fixed pins at approximately 3 mm above the net pad.  
e. Program to re-measure the pallet if the first reading fails. If second reading 
fails, the pallet goes to repair. This eliminates lost time due to the possibility 
of an initial false reading. 
f. 
All locator pins / nets inspection tolerance should be set to 0.75 mm.  Any 
measurement above 0.75 mm would be considered a failure. 
Note:  Refer to Section 10 for further details regarding vision systems.

2.7.1.5 
Pallet Repair Loop Layout 
Pallets that do not pass inspection are routed to a repair station. The picture 
below shows the preferred layout of a typical Pallet Repair Loop.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
2.7.2 
Other Requirements 
2.7.2.1 
Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) of closeout welds - line layout design is required 
to accommodate provisions for the safe and effective ultrasonic NDT of spot 
welds. To enable NDT of closeout welds, a pull-out station is required to be 
established at a point prior to the operation where the welds become closed-out. 
A pull-out station is required to enable presentation of a part/subassembly to the 
inspector for an offline inspection task. Such a layout design shall also include 
provisions for the re-introduction of the part/subassembly back into the line flow 
with the proper production scheduling. 
2.7.2.2 
Maintenance Access 
2.7.2.2.1 
The purpose of this section, the terms component, sub assembly and assembly 
shall be used interchangeably. 
2.7.2.2.2 
The maximum weight of a manual lift shall be 23 kg (50 lbs.) for a single 
individual. 
2.7.2.2.3 
The maximum height for a lift shall be 42” (1066mm). 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 119 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.7.2.2.4 
Any deviation to the above limits shall require an ergonomic study. 
2.7.2.2.5 
A maintenance accessibility study shall be completed in the Design Phase for all 
machine components that require maintenance or component removal from a 
line/sub-assembly for maintenance/replacement. This shall include but is not 
limited to: 
• 
Robots 
• 
Weld Guns 
• 
Joining Tools 
• 
Hemming Tools 
• 
Gear Motors 
2.7.2.2.5.1 
Robot risers that are 1800mm and higher shall utilized one of the approved 
maintenance accessibility methods below. 
 
Approved Methods: 
Portable Maintenance platform: 
• Platforms shall be interlocked into a safe storage position. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Robot Riser Maintenance platforms: 
• P.E. Certified structure incorporated into the robot riser design.

2.7.2.2.6 
The maintenance accessibility study shall include the supplier, Ford Motor 
Company Assembly plant launch team and VOME. 
2.7.2.2.7 
The maintenance accessibility study shall consist of a line Layout drawing and 
shall identify the LARGE component on the machine, the weight of the 
component, the height of the component from floor level, The Center of Gravity of 
the Component and the pathway maintenance personnel shall take to remove 
the component from the cell. See the Extraction Plan Example below: 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
2.7.2.2.8 
The pathway shall be clear of trip points. 
2.7.2.2.9 
The maintenance accessibility study shall include how the trades will access the 
component (i.e. floor level, maintenance platform, ladder or some other means), 
for the removal of components and equipment, for maintenance and 
replacement. For example: 
2.7.2.2.9.1 
If fall protection is required for overhead mounted components (i.e. lanyards) 
2.7.2.2.9.2 
If temporary or permanent lanyard anchors are required 
2.7.2.2.9.3 
Identification of the type of lifting equipment required. 
2.7.2.2.9.4 
Identification of the type of rigging equipment required for the lift. 
2.7.2.2.10 
When a lift with rigging is required, a rigging study shall be performed to identify 
the equipment necessary for lifting and rigging the lift. The rigging study shall 
consist of 2 parts. 
1. Removal from equipment (for example a large gearing unit) 
2. Removal from the line/sub-assembly 
2.7.2.2.11 
Hoist attachment points shall be included under overhead structures where a 
come along or portable hoist is required to remove a component. 
2.7.2.2.12 
Hoist attachment points shall require a P.E. signature. 
2.7.2.2.13 
Hoist attachment points shall have a 5X design factor for the rated load. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 121 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.7.2.2.14 
Hoist attachment points shall be load rated with the rated load marked at the 
attachment point. 
2.7.2.2.15 
Jibs attached to existing overhead structures to extend rigging reach are 
acceptable. 
2.7.2.2.16 
Jib rail shall be Patented Rail. See also GVOSS section 3.8.16 for Patented Rail 
requirements. 
2.7.2.2.17 
Patented Rail Jibs and the overhead structure they are mounted to shall require 
a P.E. signature. 
2.7.2.2.18 
Welding on Patented Rail is prohibited. 
2.7.2.2.19 
Patented Rail Jibs shall be load rated with the rated load marked on the rail. 
2.7.2.2.20 
Where manual carts are identified to be used to remove components from a 
line/sub-assembly a clear pathway shall be provided. 
2.7.2.2.21 
Where portable hoists are identified to be used to rig and remove components 
from a line/sub-assembly a clear pathway shall be provided. 
2.7.2.2.22 
When a Carry Deck Crane is to be used the minimum tonnage of the crane for 
the rigging task shall be identified. This shall include the load weight and boom 
reach. 
2.7.2.2.23 
Where Carry Deck Cranes are to be used to rig and remove components from a 
line/sub-assembly a clear pathway to the Carry Deck Crane work position shall 
be provided. 
2.7.2.2.24 
The Carry Deck Crane work position shall have sufficient clear working space to 
use the outriggers where required. 
2.7.2.3 
Equipment with cooling fans are required to be outside of the welding booth on 
any laser aluminum welding booth. 
2.8 
MECHANICAL DESIGN 
2.8.1 
Small Part Conveyor Part Nest Tooling, Friction Conveyor and Power and 
Free Part Hooks 
2.8.1.1 
All designs shall be in FIDES. 
2.8.1.2 
In all applications the part being conveyed shall be presented to the robot end 
effector unloading the part or assembly from the part nest or part hook in a 
repeatable manner.  
2.8.1.3 
Part nest and part hook tooling designs shall be simple, efficient and low cost 
that functions within the inherent positional variation of the conveyor prior to 
considering Vision solutions.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 122 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.8.1.4 
Part nests / part hooks for multiple product models, shall be designed for 
commonality where feasible. For each non-common part nest/hooks, separate 
part nest/hooks shall be provided.  
2.8.1.5 
Small Part Conveyor Part Nest Tooling 
See GVOSS Section 2.1.10.1 for Small Parts Conveyors Sourcing. 
2.8.1.5.1 
Part nests shall be of bolted design. 
2.8.1.5.2 
Part nest details shall be laser cut where possible. 
2.8.1.5.3 
Weldments shall be avoided where possible. 
2.8.1.5.4 
Pins shall be undersized, (see 3.2.1.3.1) to suit the positional variation of the 
conveyor so the part is not over constrained in the part nest at the load and 
unload point. 
2.8.1.5.5 
Part nest tooling risers shall be attached and positioned to the pallet using bolts 
and dowel pins.  
2.8.1.5.6 
Part nest tooling details touching the part shall be attached and positioned with 
NAAMS specified bolts and clearance holes for socket head cap and hex head 
screws. Clearance holes provide minor adjustment of the nest tooling details. 
2.8.1.5.7 
All other part nest tooling details that are not touching the part i.e. guide wipers 
shall have designed in features providing adjustability in needed direction, slots 
etc... 
2.8.1.5.8 
Locating pins, retainers and grind spacers for part positioning shall be designed 
per standard NAAMS tool guidelines. 
2.8.1.5.9 
Alteration of clearance holes are permitted after catastrophic events to requalify 
tooling. 
2.8.1.5.10 
Vertical conveyor part locating pins shall have a minimum of 10mm engagement 
through the tooling hole. 
2.8.1.5.11 
Parts and assemblies that are positionally unstable while on the part nest shall 
have their position stabilized at the unload position to enable the robot end 
effector to unload the part from the part hook.  
2.8.1.5.12 
For additional requirements related to small part conveyor part nests see GVOSS 
Sections 
• 
3.4 Magazine Part Feeders  
• 
3.5 Accumulating Pallet Conveyors  
• 
3.6 Enclosed Track Conveyors  
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 123 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.8.1.6 
Power & Free and Friction Conveyor Part Hooks 
2.8.1.6.1 
Power & Free/Friction Conveyor part hooks attached to the load bar (Load Bar 
defined as the means of attachment point for Product Specific Hooks to the 
conveyor trolleys) must have an in-station positioner to ensure fore/aft 
consistency. 
2.8.1.6.2 
Part hooks attaching to the load bar or pallet, must be located at the unload/pick 
position to enable consistent positioning of the part or assembly in the “X”, “Y” 
and “Z” axis.  
2.8.1.6.3 
The part hook shall have a datum for the part attachment characteristics for 
where the part rests on the hook. 
2.8.1.6.4 
The datum shall be used as part of the hook positioning methodology. 
2.8.1.6.5 
The load bar shall not be used to position the part hook. 
2.8.1.6.6 
The part hook load bar attachment points, load and unload positioning method, 
part hook positioning characteristic and the relationship to the part hook locating 
characteristics shall be evaluated to validate the part positioning method will work 
within the inherent variation of the system. 
2.8.1.6.7 
Precision part loading to the part hook shall be avoided. Examples are load to 
pins and using small holes in the part for rests. 
2.8.1.6.8 
The datum shall also be used for the part hook qualification fixture. 
2.8.1.6.9 
All part hooks shall have a qualification fixture. 
2.8.1.6.10 
Parts and assemblies that are positionally unstable while on the part hook shall 
have their position stabilized at the unload position to enable the Robot End 
Effector to unload the part from the part hook.  
2.8.1.6.11 
Robot end effectors shall have a locator strategy to ensure the part or assembly 
can be loaded and unloaded to and from the part nest or part hook within the 
inherent positional variation of the conveyor system.  
2.8.1.7 
Small Part Conveyor Part Nest Tooling Qualification Fixtures 
2.8.1.7.1 
Tooling nest qualification fixtures are required for ALL Small Part Conveyor Part 
Nest Tooling unless otherwise specified by Ford VOME. 
2.8.1.7.2 
The Tier1 Integrator shall furnish a qualification fixture to the magazine supplier 
for use by the magazine supplier to qualify the “part next” tooling: 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 124 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.8.1.7.3 
The conveyor supplier shall provide mounts (datum’s) on the conveyor frame for 
the purpose of mounting the nest tooling qualification fixture. The conveyor 
supplier and Tier1 Integrator shall concur on the design of the mounts. The 
qualification fixture shall utilize pins, which will set into a 2-way and 4-way 
bushing on each side of the conveyor. Other locating methods are permitted with 
the Ford Motor Company VOGO BCE Manufacturing Engineer and VIS/MDFU 
approval. 
2.8.1.7.4 
Qualification fixtures designed to be used on bolted construction part nests shall 
be light weight frame construction i.e. aluminum.  
 
 
 
2.8.1.7.5 
The qualification position shall be at a stop with pallet anti-backup. 
2.8.1.7.6 
The preferred location of the qualification fixture when not in use is outside of the 
automation. 
When the qualification fixture is not located at the conveyor stop switch it must 
have a separate switch for the qualifying position.  
• The switch shall be the same manufacturer and model as used at the 
conveyor stop position. 
• The “qualification mode” switch shall be enabled by either a HMI selection 
or a selector switch. The “qualification mode” shall change nest tooling 
stopping control from the automation end of the conveyor to the 
qualification position switch. The VOME controls engineer shall determine 
what method is best suited for the application. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 125 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.8.1.7.7 
The Tier1 Integrator shall ship the "part nest" tooling to the conveyor supplier. 
The ship date must support the conveyor delivery date. After receiving the 
conveyor, the Tier1 Integrator is responsible for re-qualifying the "part nest" 
tooling, if necessary. When "part nest" tooling re-qualification is necessary, the 
Tier1 Integrator shall contact the conveyor supplier for the qualifying procedure. 
The qualification fixture shall be returned to the Tier1 Integrator when the 
magazine is shipped to their facility. 
2.8.1.7.8 
The conveyor supplier shall furnish the equipment supplier a "Notice of 
Qualification" to the equipment supplier stating the "part nest" tooling has been 
qualified. 
2.8.1.7.9 
A single-point lesson shall be posted at the qualification position or attached to 
the qualification fixture that provides instructions on enabling the “qualification 
mode” and the use of the qualification fixture. This Instruction Manual and/or a 
single-point lesson shall be posted in TCE with other conveyor data. 
2.8.1.8 
Power & Free and Friction Conveyor Part Hooks Qualification Fixtures 
2.8.1.8.1 
Tooling nest qualification fixtures are required for ALL Power & Free and Friction 
Conveyor Part Hooks unless otherwise specified by Ford VOME. 
2.8.1.8.2 
The Tier1 Integrator shall provide a qualification fixture for the Power & Free and 
Friction Conveyor Part Hooks to qualify the part hooks. 
2.8.1.8.3 
The positioning method, part hook positioning details and the relationship to the 
part hook locating details shall be evaluated to validate the part positioning 
method will work within the inherent variation of the system. In general, 
positioning method should represent complete system as it is provided in 
load/pick stations. 
2.8.1.8.4 
Part hook and load bar positioning tool in repair spur, where checking fixture 
shall be used, shall be provided by Facilities and Tooling supplier in the same 
manner as for load and unload stations. 
2.8.1.8.5 
Power & Free and Friction Conveyor Part Hooks Qualification Fixtures should 
follow, but not limited to, example shown at below picture.

In cases where surrounding allows it, Power & Free and Friction Conveyor Part

mode” and the use of the qualification fixture. This Instruction Manual and/or a 
single-point lesson shall be posted in TCE with other conveyor data.

2.10.1

Figure 2.3.1 provides an overview of a typical kit process.

Figure 2.3.1.  Kit Flow Diagram 
2.10.1.2 
Kit/Tool design shall follow the Locator Drawing (LD) Assembly Flow Diagram 
(AFD). If there is no LD AFD, contact Vehicle Operation Dimensional 
Management Engineering and request that an LD AFD be created. If an LD AFD 
requires revisions, submit a Web-Based Locator Revision Request (WLRR), 
which will notify Vehicle Operations Dimensional Management Engineering of the 
needed revisions. 
2.10.1.3 
Kit/tool designs are to follow the shop assembly LD file. If there is no LD file to 
support the kit, contact Vehicle Operations Dimensional Control and request that 
an LD shop assembly drawing is created. If there is a shop assembly LD file, but 
it requires revisions, complete the Locator Revision Request and send it to 
Vehicle Operations Dimensional Control. 
2.10.1.4 
The supplier is responsible for validating total system integration into assembly 
assembly/facility tooling. The supplier shall follow all applicable buy-off and 
equipment documentation instructions procedures. The supplier shall update all 
drawings for effected equipment. Upon completion of kit design, supplier shall 
download revised mechanical & controls drawings into Team center.  Attachment 
K shall be submitted, signed off, and archived by OEM    
2.10.1.5 
When required, a 1A-10 Kit Sheet shall be generated for each unit to outline a 
step-by-step description of rework requirements. A Bill of Material shall also be 
added to the kit sheet for ordering of kit items only. The VOME ME shall 
determine the need for Kit Sheets. 
2.10.1.6 
A Bill of Materials for each unit shall be generated for kit items only. 
2.10.1.7 
When kit changes affect a total system or subsystem, a process planning layout 
shall be provided as part of the kit change requirements.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 129 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.10.1.8 
A reduced layout (1A) shall be made to show a plan view outline of the system 
and location of the rework kits. The kit numbers shall be added to aid assembly 
plant personnel in locating area of fixture where the kit is to be installed. The 
layout shall also include the type and location of any new facility drops required 
for the program. A Description Sheet shall be attached to this and shall identify 
the kit number, system location, station number (where applicable) and 
description of the kit. 
2.10.1.9 
For new complete geometry fixtures, the supplier shall provide a tool certification 
report verifying all locators are within specification. 
2.10.1.10 
 To determine the scope of work for each kit, the supplier shall utilize the 
Kit_Development_Worksheet.xlsm to establish try-out requirements, buy-off book 
deliverables and supporting documentation for each kit.  This worksheet will be 
used to track status of all kits, and act as an agenda for the kit buy-off. 
2.10.1.11 
Existing electrical panels that are being moved (moving from one location to 
another), modified (performing any work inside the panel to make changes), or 
re-purposed for the use in a new or kitted application shall be updated to conform 
to the requirements of  FAS08-160 Electrical Safety.  
Note: A field check is required to develop a tracking matrix of panels in non-
compliance and is to be reviewed with VOME Controls 
2.10.2 
Field Review 
2.10.2.1 
A field review of the affected equipment for the program shall be conducted prior 
to <PA> to support program costing. The field review should include but is not 
limited to the following items that will affect the kit designs: 
• 
Identify worn-out components. 
• 
Undocumented changes that have an effect on equipment cycle time. 
• 
Plant projects. 
• 
Undocumented mechanical changes. 
• 
Mechanical interference zones. 
• 
Electro-mechanical drive loading 
• 
Changes made to control the build of the current production vehicle. 
• 
Improper panel tags. 
• 
Inadequate panel tags. 
• 
Missing panel tags. 
• 
Temporary tags. 
• 
Improper panel layout. 
• 
Missing valve tags. 
• 
Missing wire tags. 
• 
Improper wire tags. 
• 
Changes made to existing logic. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 130 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
PLC and junction box, I/O. 
• 
Physical jumpers. 
• 
Logical jumpers. 
• 
Panel layout. 
• 
Document Hardware and Software Interfaces for kit compatibility. 
• 
Improperly documented or undocumented logic changes, electrical drawing 
changes and electrical drawing changes. 
• 
Changes to current model logic. 
• 
Incomplete diagnostics. 
• 
Any missing/incomplete software logic tasks comparing to the latest released 
Ford Logic Template. This shall apply to all the processors in the kitted 
segment. 
• 
Safety Signatures Present and Locked/Annunciated. 
2.10.2.2 
A list of all the components to be reviewed for replacement should be available 
for the field review. 
2.10.2.3 
All kit components shall be consistent with the components used in the existing 
equipment installation. The use of new components, not held in plant inventory, 
shall be agreed upon by the plant and VOME. 
2.10.2.4 
Components requiring replacement, refurbishment, or redesign shall be identified 
and confirmed during the field review.  Each component identified during field 
review will require a work plan to be developed and approved by the tooling 
supplier, VOME, and receiving plant. 
2.10.2.5 
Electro-mechanical drives shall be evaluated "in-place" by recognized experts in 
this field. A list of drives to be evaluated, the company performing the evaluation, 
evaluation method and cost shall be developed prior to <PA> to support program 
costing. All reports generated from this examination are to be shared with the 
tooling supplier, VOME, and receiving plant. 
2.10.2.6 
Copies of the current plant PLC, HMI, Robot programs and electrical, pneumatic, 
water and other relevant drawings are to be used as a starting point for all 
modifications. An unedited "file" copy of each program shall be kept as a backup. 
2.10.2.7 
The supplier shall take digital images of the “Before” state of equipment to be 
kitted. The images shall be of sufficient content so the impact of the kit to existing 
equipment can be recognized. The images shall be part of the 1A-10 package 
and the Buy-Off documentation upon request of Ford VOME. 
2.10.2.8 
Supplier 
shall 
review 
that 
all 
safety 
signatures 
are 
present 
and 
locked/annunciated. Any Safety Signature that is not present, locked or 
annunciated shall be documented and reviewed with the Ford VOME Launch 
Team.

2.10.3 
Kit Requirements 
2.10.3.1 
All kits for plant installation shall be assembled, as complete as possible, to 
reduce the installation time at the assembly plant. 
2.10.3.2 
All required loose components, i.e., cables, hoses, fittings, commercial items, 
screws, dowels, wiring, etc., are to be shipped with each kit and properly 
identified. A Bill of Material shall also be generated and added to the 1A-10 sheet 
for ordering of all items required for installation of kits from tooling to service 
points. 
2.10.3.3 
All kits shall be of a tapping plate construction where design permits, with set-up 
dimensions and machined edges to aid in kit installation. 
2.10.3.4 
All equipment that is reworked or reconditioned in the supplier's shop shall be 
cleaned, repainted and re-stenciled if required to match current tooling. They 
shall also be inspected mechanically, electrically and reworked, where 
necessary, with Ford approval. An itemized list of the necessary rework or 
replacement of parts shall be submitted to the Ford Motor Company for approval.  
2.10.3.5 
The supplier shall include all industrial material requirements for each kit. The 
delivery of the industrial materials reports shall support the kit installation 
schedule at 50% kit complete.  
2.10.3.6 
If the original program industrial materials reports followed GVOSS Section 1.2.8, 
the original reports shall be modified, with changes highlighted in yellow, to 
reflect the new requirements. New components that must be entered into 
inventory shall have the notation "New for <Program Name>" entered into the 
comment column on the "Industrial Materials – Components List". 
2.10.3.7 
All patterns, castings and details shall be legibly stamped with detail or pattern 
numbers. 
2.10.3.8 
Suppliers shall ship a complete set of prints, including Unit 1A-10 Instructions, 
with each kit. 
2.10.3.9 
Suppliers shall package each kit separately and identify before shipping. 
2.10.3.10 
Kits shall be identified with receiving plant, car line, model, tool number, unit 
number, kit number, weight and shipper-number stenciled on each box. 
2.10.3.11 
An Acceptance/Inspection Report, Special Tool Inspection Report, shall be 
submitted by supplier for each kit prior to Ford inspection. The plant copy of the 
inspection report shall be inserted into the packing slip and forwarded with each 
tool to the assembly plants. 
2.10.3.12 
Kits shall be reviewed and signed by all the representatives listed in the Kit Buy-
Off Checklist. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 132 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.10.3.13 
Short Circuit Current Rating (SCCR): Control panels operating at line voltage 
shall meet the SCCR requirements in GVOSS Section 8. Non-current-limiting 
fuses shall be replaced with new current-limiting fuses. 
2.10.3.14 
Evaluate and provide any missing arc flash labeling for panels fed from buss 
ducts with a voltage feed greater than 400 volts. 
2.10.3.15 
All new electrical panels and components must confirm to Ford Electrical Safety 
Standard FAS08-160. 
2.10.3.16 
Replace any missing panel, J-box or field device lamacoid tags. 
2.10.3.17 
Replace any missing panel wire tags or field device cord tags. 
2.10.3.18 
Correct any existing system ground faults. 
2.10.3.19 
Remove any components no longer required for operation. All pushbuttons or 
pilot lights removed shall be covered with plugs. 
2.10.3.20 
HMI’s shall be revised to follow the GVOSS standard for HMI’s. 
2.10.3.21 
For any new devices, e.g. proximity switches, relays, lights, etc., match existing 
system architecture and cross components to manufacturers on the Nex-
Gen/GVOSS VOME Approved Component List. 
2.10.3.22 
Update FIS logic and faults for the changes to the system. Prepare/update fault 
lists for plant FIS admin. However, this contractor must be present for validation. 
2.10.3.23 
When ECPL placarding is affected by the kit work performed, new ECPL 
placarding and icons shall be provided. When kit work effects lubrication 
requirements, new lubrication placards and charts shall be provided. 
2.10.3.24 
Supplier shall provide a list of obsolete components requiring replacement. 
2.10.3.25 
Protag process shall be utilized for all equipment where the initiation/change of 
an ECPL placard is required. (Reference FAS08-036). 
2.10.3.26 
Surge protection of safety control circuits: Surge protection devices shall be 
installed at the line side of the DC power supplies feeding Programmable Safety 
Controllers. 
2.10.3.27 
For robotic rack load/unload installations where the existing rack fleet has not 
been supplied as a geometry rack (Ref. 3.15.1.5), the supplier shall utilize the 
existing rack location strategy. 
2 Locators shall be on opposing sides of the rack stand/turntable as shown in 
Fig. 1.

2.10.4 
Prior to Buy-Off 
2.10.4.1 
Review the content of the kit with the supplier, Launch Team, VO 
Representatives, and the receiving plant. 
2.10.4.2 
VO checklist will be completed and signed off by appropriate personnel. 
2.10.4.3 
Commercial Components Sign-Off Sheets completed & EIS Sign- Off. 
2.10.4.4 
Kit content and scope of work shall be reviewed with the receiving plant prior to 
Weekend Work Request meeting. 
2.10.4.5 
When kits affect the following as new or modified applications:  
1. Robots: 
New installation or new EOA Tooling 
• 
Robot reach studies shall be completed and the requirements for Ford 
Motor Company Occupational Health and Safety Standard FAS08-131. 
are satisfied.  
• 
A FEA shall be run for updated design. 
• 
Robot load requirements shall be validated.  
• 
Physical weights shall be taken and validated to design. 
• 
Design sign-off on robot application (from robot manufacturer). 
• 
After Installation Robot generated torque compliance for each axis. A 
robot manufacturer's representative must sign off all robot torque 
documents. Documentation shall include robot and station number on 
print out. 
• 
Mocked-up components shall be placed to best represent plant 
conditions for all kits involving reprocessed robots. Exceptions shall be 
signed off by VOME to deviate from this standard.

Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025

 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
RTU Checklist if applicable 
2. Payload analysis for lifters and drives validated.  
3. Risk assessments completed.  
4. Back-up welding strategy addressed.  
5. Ergonomic studies completed. 
2.10.4.6 
All kits shall be tried out prior to shipping to the plant. The exception shall be 
small parts that are shipped in cartons, such as locator blocks. The Vendor 
Interface Supervisor shall provide further direction to the supplier if a question of 
applicability of this requirement arises. 
2.10.4.7 
New complete fixtures, robots (with new EOT tooling), turntables, trunnions, 
power roll beds and standalone part conveyors shall require a 20-hour run/ 2500 
cycle run before buyoff.  
2.10.4.8 
No tooling buyoffs shall be scheduled until all buyoff requirements are met and 
validated by the Vendor Interface Supervisor (VIS). 
2.10.4.9 
Customer Buy-Off Book 
Prepare Buy-Off Book with the following Table of Content items: 
•

matrix.

• 
Open Issues matrix with resolution plans and timing for each item 
•

• 
Industrial Materials Reports  
• 
Commercial Component Sign-off.  
• 
Safety Risk Assessment using the process in FAS08-036, Attachment 3. 
• 
Startup risk assessment. Kit Installation Risk Assessment. This shall be 
approved by Plant Body Area Manager and VOGO section manager(s) 
responsible for the effected tooling. 
• 
Exceptions List for systems validation to be completed at the receiving plant. 
• 
Validated field checklist.  
• 
MFM updates. (if applicable) 
• 
Updated process (if applicable) 
• 
ECPL placards and icons. 
• 
Lubrication charts and placards. 
• 
Meeting Sign-in Sheet from 100% Mechanical and 100% Controls Design 
Reviews (specify tools reviewed). 
•

• 
Documentation of Finite Element Analysis for High-Stress Applications

2.10.5 
Prior to Shipment to Plant 
2.10.5.1 
Identify a marshalling area at the plant for receiving kits. 
2.10.5.2 
Ensure labor clearances are secured for installation. 
2.10.5.3 
Kits are palletized by station to be able to set in work area without requiring re-
palletizing of components. 
2.10.6 
Manual Change-Over of Model-Specific Tooling 
2.10.6.1 
General  
2.10.6.1.1 
A manual model changeover is when tooling needs to be manually changed-out 
or replaced in part by skilled trade personnel in order to run a different vehicle 
model. 
2.10.6.1.2 
The strategy to use manual model change-over shall be made by the Ford Motor 
Company Process Engineer and Launch Leader for the given program prior to 
<PA> so that the Tier 1 Integrator can properly develop associated costs. 
2.10.6.1.3 
Requirements for the manual model change-over shall be included in the 
statement of work for the given project. 
2.10.6.1.4 
If the manual model change-over process must be performed during scheduled 
production, the time to accomplish such change-over shall be incorporated into 
the simulation model for the given subsystem. 
2.10.6.2 
Tool Construction 
2.10.6.2.1 
Tools shall be constructed in such a manner that the manual change-out will not 
result in component wear. 
2.10.6.2.2 
Tooling shall be identified with its weight. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 136 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.10.6.2.3 
Rigging points shall be clearly identified. 
2.10.6.2.4 
Tooling and details shall be identified with the model line color code. This may be 
accomplished with an appropriately placed color stripe. 
2.10.6.2.5 
Tooling plates shall be identified with the vehicle line model designation stenciled 
over a background of the vehicle line color. 
2.10.6.2.6 
A key sheet shall be provided identifying the location of details to be changed. 
2.10.6.3 
Training and Visual Aids 
2.10.6.3.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide training to the plant on the manual model 
change-over procedure. 
2.10.6.3.2 
The procedure shall be in a PowerPoint presentation. Presentations for manual 
change-over of the D-219 Hood Line and Fender Line are provided as examples 
of a more complex change-over procedure. 
2.10.6.3.3 
The procedure shall also be delivered to the plant in a printed format in a binder 
with laminated pages. If sufficient, the PowerPoint presentation can be used for 
this purpose. 
 
2.10.7 
Updating Drawings 
The procedure below expands on the task of making machine control drawing 
changes on existing plant equipment if the plant is to install the kit under supplier 
supervision: 
2.10.7.1 
Obtaining original drawings: It is the responsibility of the supplier to locate and 
obtain the Hardware drawings and Software files. 
2.10.7.2 
Changes to original hardware drawings shall be done on a separate layer, plotted 
in a different color and have change/revision notes.  
2.10.7.3 
Revise design on hash-marked drawings. Mark up reproduced original with 
changes hashed and marked to show the following: 
• 
Additions to circuit – circled 
• 
Deletions to circuit – cross-hatch deletions (do not erase anything) 
2.10.7.4 
Conversion sheets for control drawings to include the following: 
• 
Brief description of mechanical, sequence and welding changes being made, 
including the numbers of the mechanical units being changed. 
• 
Sheet numbers of the control drawings affected by the change. 
• 
Stock listing the items to be added for the control change. 
• 
Legend information for engraving of legend plates with a copy of hashed 
drawings of push-button and light layout for build Supplier use 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 137 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
Include note to build Supplier to pre-assemble and pre-wire as much as 
possible all components prior to shipping of kit. 
• 
Include list of wire numbers required for installation. 
2.10.7.5 
Hashed drawings and conversion sheets are to be submitted to the machine 
controls section for review. 
2.10.7.6 
After review of the change by the machine controls section, the original drawings 
are to be revised to the reviewed changes. 
• 
Drawings to be revised to the changes made on the hashed prints. 
• 
Spare components are not to be removed from the drawing but shown as 
spares. 
• 
All changes are to be recorded in the change column. 
2.10.7.7 
A 1A-10 sheet shall be generated for all kits. 
2.10.7.8 
The supplier shall furnish all drawings and control diagrams per the Structures 
Standards specifications. All printouts for tryout are the responsibility of the 
supplier. 
2.10.8 
Kit Installation 
2.10.8.1 
Preparing for Kit Installations 
2.10.8.1.1 
Installation Scope of Work 
2.10.8.1.1.1 To aid in visualizing the scope of kit work for a program, the Tier 1 Integrator 
shall construct a system layout identifying the location of each kit for the 
program. Locations of each kit shall be annotated with the supplier’s name, kit 
number and a short description of the kit contents.  
See also Kit Example1 and Kit Example2 
2.10.8.1.1.2 The system layout shall also include the type and location of any new facility 
drops required for the program. 
2.10.8.1.1.3 New facility drops shall also be shown on the 1A layout drawings. 
2.10.8.1.2 
Installation Planning 
2.10.8.1.2.1 A plan in MS Project shall be developed to identify the planned kit installation 
dates for each kit predicated on plant scheduled downtime and equipment 
availability. 
2.10.8.1.2.2 The planned install date for each kit shall be added to the Scope of Work layout. 
2.10.8.1.2.3 When installation date changes due to an installation cancellation or inability to 
install the kit due to unforeseen time constraints, the prior installation date shall 
be over-struck (not deleted) and the new planned installation date added. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 138 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.10.8.1.2.4 After the kit is installed and the Production Functional Validation is complete, the 
date completed shall be added to the Scope of Work layout. 
2.10.8.1.3 
Timing Charts 
2.10.8.1.3.1 All kits shall have a timing chart in MS Project that identifies all activities and their 
durations for the kit installations. See also the Kit Timing Chart Example. 
NOTE: Suppliers can add items as needed but are not allowed to delete items, 
(Show NA). 
2.10.8.1.3.2 The timing chart shall include a prioritization and ordering of the individual kit 
installations. 
2.10.8.1.3.3 Timing shall take into consideration non-working hours for breaks and lunch 
periods. 
2.10.8.1.4 
Installation Risk Assessment 
2.10.8.1.4.1 The Tier 1 Integrator shall complete an installation risk assessment for the kits in 
each installation period to determine if there is sufficient readiness to proceed 
with the kit installation. Use the form Kit Installation Risk Assessment: 
• 
Kit Installation Risk Assessment   
This shall include, but is not limited to: 
• 
Mechanical interference checking complete. 
• 
Completion of Process Simulate simulation studies. 
• 
Programming requirements. 
• 
FIS requirements. 
• 
Additional supplier support that is required such as Robot, Weld Timer, Laser 
and other subsystem manufacturer or integrator. 
• 
Material requirements. 
• 
Labor requirements, quantities and required skill sets. 
• 
Equipment availability requirements. 
• 
Strip out plans. 
• 
Latest start time to complete work. 
• 
Earliest finish time to complete and validate work. 
• 
Product sheet metal requirements to support work. 
• 
Product sheet metal requirements for any pre-production start-up try-out and 
validation. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 139 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.10.8.1.5 
Labor Planning (FNA Only) 
2.10.8.1.5.1 At the beginning of the program the Tier 1 Integrator shall work with VOME and 
the plant to identify plant labor involvement with the kit installations. 
2.10.8.1.5.2 Tier 1 Integrators shall construct a Labor Planning Chart; see Labor Planning 
Chart Example and Labor Planning Chart Blank that can be used by suppliers for 
labor planning. The chart should include, but is not limited to: 
• 
Installation Date 
• 
Planned Hours for the installation 
• 
Number of Shifts and Shift length (in hours) planned 
• 
Equipment Identification 
• 
Kit Numbers that will be installed 
• 
Priority [L(ow), M(edium), H(igh)] 
• 
Warranty [Y/N] 
• 
Task Description 
• 
Tier 1 Integrator Management Staff 
• 
Tier 1 Integrator Technical Support Personnel by category 
• 
FTF Supplier Support 
• 
Tier 2 Supplier Support 
• 
Contract Labor by trade 
• 
Plant Labor by trade 
2.10.8.1.5.3 Labor Planning shall be identified for the following 3 categories: 
• 
Installation 
• 
Pre-Production Functional Validation 
• 
Production Functional Validation 
2.10.8.1.6 
Skills Assessment (FNA Only) 
2.10.8.1.6.1 The Tier 1 Integrator shall develop a skill assessment for each labor classification 
to identify the skill-sets required for the kit installation. 
2.10.8.1.6.2 When contractor labor is utilized the Tier 1 Integrator shall validate the trades 
persons have the skills and experience necessary for the installation. 
2.10.8.1.6.3 When Ford Motor Company Plant Trades are utilized the Tier 1 Integrator shall 
work with the Plant M.E. Manager to identify the trades persons best suited to 
work on the installation. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 140 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.10.8.1.7 
Pre-Installation Review with VOME and the Plant (FNA Only) 
2.10.8.1.7.1 The Tier 1 Integrator shall have a kit pre-installation review with the plant one 
week for weekend work and one month for extended shut-down work prior to 
submitting the plant labor clearance for the installation. Additional meetings shall 
be held "as-needed". 
2.10.8.1.7.2 The reviews shall include the plant trade leaders. 
2.10.8.1.8 
Plant Labor Clearance (FNA Only) 
2.10.8.1.8.1 GVOSS Section 1.1.19 for the Plant Labor Clearance process and Checklist shall 
be followed. 
2.10.8.1.9 
Shipping and Received Material 
2.10.8.1.9.1 Do not ship kits without formal buy-off and kit checklist being complete unless 
there is an approved "Action Request – Deviation" authorizing it. 
Note: When required to support the installation timing and logistics or VOME 
Department practices, the VOME Department Program Launch Manager can 
waive this requirement. The waiver must be in writing. 
2.10.8.1.9.2 All equipment and materials shall be staged in a marshalling area. The receiving 
plant is responsible for identifying the equipment marshalling area at the 
receiving plant. See GVOSS Section 1.1.18.4. 
2.10.8.1.9.3 For responsibilities of Material Received by the Installation Contractor, see 
GVOSS Section 1.1.18.2. 
2.10.8.1.9.4 For responsibilities of Material Received by the Receiving Plant, see GVOSS 
Section 1.1.18.3. 
2.10.8.1.9.5 Tier 1 Integrators shall provide a material coordinator to validate the receipt of all 
required materials prior to the kit installation. 
2.10.8.1.9.6 A kit shall be considered "ready for installation" only when all materials identified 
on the 1A-10 and supporting BOM's are on site and staged in the marshalling 
area. 
2.10.8.1.9.7 The Tier 1 Integrator materials coordinator and the Plant shall be responsible for 
staging the materials for the planned kit installation in the marshalling area. 
2.10.8.2 
Installing Kits 
2.10.8.2.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall conduct a final installation line-up meeting with the 
Contractor and Plant (1) hour prior to the start of the installation period to assure 
the kit installation objectives are understood and answer any questions. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 141 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.10.8.2.2 
Prior to any programming changes the Tier 1 Integrator shall make back-up 
copies of the controller, robot and any other programs that are being changed as 
part of the kit installation. 
2.10.8.2.3 
Tier 1 Integrators shall use the Kit Installation Checklist to confirm work is 
complete and all pertinent aspects of the installation have been addressed: 
• 
Kit Installation Checklist   
A blank formatted sheet is provided at the end for items that are not addressed in 
the individual worksheets. 
Individual line items on the worksheets should be added or removed in order to 
satisfy the requirements of section 2.10.8.2.4 below. 
 
2.10.8.2.4 
Each kit shall have an installation checklist that is: 
• 
Aligned with the activities on the timing chart. 
• 
Aligned with the work content of the 1A-10. 
• 
Contain the craftsmanship requirements for the kit. 
• 
Includes housekeeping. 
• 
Includes activities necessary for pre-production try-out and validation. 
2.10.8.2.5 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall identify a person that is responsible for filling out the 
checklist and a separate person who will validate the checklists. 
2.10.8.2.6 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall retain the completed checklists in a binder on site and 
include them in Kit Buy off Book and be made available for VOME review. 
2.10.8.3 
Kit Installation Support and Validation 
2.10.8.3.1 
Installation Support 
2.10.8.3.1.1 Tier 1 Integrators shall provide coordination, technical support and supervision of 
supplier personnel for the installation. 
2.10.8.3.1.2 Prior to the installation, VOME, the plant and Tier 1 Integrator shall agree upon 
the supplier, plant and contract labor support (FNA Only) for the installation. 
2.10.8.3.1.3 The categories of installation support from the Tier 1 Integrator shall consist of, 
but is not limited to; 
• 
Installation Coordinator 
• 
Material Control 
• 
Mechanical 
• 
Electrical 
• 
Controls 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 142 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
Robot 
• 
Sealing/Adhesive Systems 
• 
Other Technologies 
• 
FTF Supplier (coordinate with Ford Motor Company VOME) 
• 
Tier 2 Suppliers 
• 
Change Control 
• 
Labor Tracking and Time Keeping 
• 
Project Management 
Note: The actual personnel supporting these categories will be predicated on the 
agreed upon results of GVOSS section 2.3.9.3.1.2 above. 
2.10.8.3.2 
Pre-Production Functional Validation Support 
2.10.8.3.2.1 The purpose of the Pre-Production Validation is to prepare the equipment for 
production by validating equipment operation and function after the kit installation 
prior to the start of scheduled production. This shall include, but is not limited to; 
• 
Safety 
• 
Product Quality 
• 
Product Recertification 
• 
Station Cycle Time 
2.10.8.3.2.2 Tier 1 Integrators shall provide coordination, technical support and supervision of 
supplier personnel for the installation Pre-Production Functional Validation. 
2.10.8.3.2.3 Prior to the installation, VOME, the plant and Tier 1 Integrator shall develop the 
plan with objectives and scope for the validation. 
2.10.8.3.2.4 Prior to the installation, VOME, the plant and Tier 1 Integrator shall agree upon 
the supplier, plant and contract labor support for the validation. 
2.10.8.3.2.5 The equipment shall be operated in "Automatic" mode and process production 
units through the manufacturing system for Pre-Production Validation. 
2.10.8.3.2.6 Issues found in the Pre-Production Functional Validation shall be corrected and 
documented. The documented issues shall be used to identify GAPS in the Kit 
Installation Checklist so they can be updated accordingly to prevent future 
occurrences. 
2.10.8.3.3 
Production Functional Validation Support 
2.10.8.3.3.1 The purpose of Production Function Validation is to perform a more thorough 
validation the kits are functioning to design intent and not causing unscheduled 
down time of the equipment. 
2.10.8.3.3.2 Tier 1 Integrators shall provide Production Functional Validation support for two 
scheduled production shifts where full line speed has been achieved off of the 
department "pay-point" (i.e. "Hang-to-Paint" for Body) with no issues. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 2 - DESIGN 
Revision Date: Jan 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 143 of 143 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2.10.8.3.3.3 Tier 1 Integrators shall provide coordination, technical support and supervision of 
supplier personnel for the installation Production Functional Validation. 
2.10.8.3.3.4 Prior to the installation, VOME, the plant and Tier 1 Integrator shall develop the 
plan with objectives and scope for the validation. 
2.10.8.3.3.5 Prior to the installation, VOME, the plant and Tier 1 Integrator shall agree upon 
the supplier, plant and contract labor support for the validation. 
2.10.8.3.3.6 Issues found in the Production Functional Validation shall be corrected and 
documented. The documented issues shall be used to identify GAPS in the Kit 
Installation Checklist so they can be updated accordingly to prevent future 
occurrences.

3.1 
SCREWS, DOWELS AND WASHER SPECIFICATION FOR INSTALLATION 
OF THREADED FASTENERS  
3.1.1 
Nylon patch screws are preferred. Nylon patch screws shall conform to the 
requirements of the NAAMS Global Standard Components–Assembly, Chapter F; 
Fastener Components. 
The use of anaerobic adhesive is not required when using nylon patch screws. 
Nylon patch screws can be reused up to 3 times if the fastener has never been 
torqued past its yield point. 
3.1.2 
All other threaded fasteners not covered in 3.1.1 above, shall have an application 
of Loctite® 242™ anaerobic adhesive, according to the manufacturer's 
recommendations.  Since previously used fasteners are not allowed for use in 
tooling, the references to cleaning the fasteners with solvent and the application 
of Loctite Primer N can be ignored. 
3.1.3 
Threads 
3.1.3.1 
Bolt threads shall have a minimum 1.5 times bolt diameter thread engagement in 
threaded holes. 
3.1.3.2 
All threaded holes in aluminum manufactured (“Make”) details shall use Heli-Coil 
inserts to prevent thread pull-out. 
3.1.3.3 
When a joint is held with a screw and nut, a minimum of 2 threads of the screw 
shall be visible past a nut. 
3.1.4 
All fasteners shall comply with the NAAMS Global Standard Components–
Assembly, for fasteners found in Chapter F; Fastener Components. 
This shall include all Components, Standards and Procedures in Chapter F. 
3.1.5 
Structural fasteners shall comply with the requirements of GVOSS Section 
3.11.2: Structural Fasteners. 
3.1.6 
Jam Nuts.  
3.1.6.1 
The thin nut shall be placed on the bolt first as shown in the image below. The 
thin nut is to be tightened to between 25%-50% of the specified tightening torque. 
The second (thick) nut is then placed on the bolt and the thin nut held to prevent 
rotation by a spanner while the thick nut is tightened to the full torque value. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 8 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
3.1.7 
The supplier bears the sole responsibility for the proper engineering of bolted 
joints, specifying fastener systems and torque requirements of all fasteners to the 
fastener manufacturers’ specifications. See also GVOSS section 3.1.4. 
3.1.8 
The supplier bears the sole responsibility for the proper engineering of dowels 
into each application. 
3.1.9 
Safety wiring of fasteners is prohibited. When a supplier deems there is an 
engineering justification for the use of safety wire, it will be approved only after an 
Action Request Form is submitted and approved. Be aware of the following when 
using safety wire: 
• 
Apply safety wire as shown on the attached stock list sheet illustration (refer 
to Figure 3.1.9).  
• 
Suppliers are not allowed to drill fasteners for safety wire when it voids the 
fastener manufacturers' warranty. 
 
 
Figure 3.1.9.  Safety Wire 
3.1.10 
Metric fasteners will be used on all tooling. Use NAAMS for “K” Factor for metric 
screws. Reference NAAMS Global Standard Components–Assembly, Chapter F; 
Fastener Components  
3.1.11 
Counterfeit Fasteners 
3.1.11.1 
S/CI (Suspect/Counterfeit Items). Counterfeit Fasteners fall under S/CI. The 
occurrence of S/CI in industry is an ongoing problem. S/CI fasteners may meet 
ANSI/ASME/ISO dimensional specifications but are substandard in their 
performance due to material or process flaws. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 9 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.1.11.2 
Preventing S/CI. All fastener manufacturers for all fasteners used in equipment 
supplied to Ford Motor Company shall meet the following requirements: 
• 
Meet or exceed the standards for fasteners identified in the NAAMS Global 
Standard Components – Assembly, Chapter F; Fastener Components. 
• 
Fasteners which fall outside the GVOSS requirements of this section and are 
manufactured to other specifications, should meet the standard they were 
manufactured to. 
• 
Provide manufacturing lot traceability. 
3.1.11.3 
Testing. The following are guidelines for fastener testing: 
• 
The testing should be in accordance to the appropriate standards applicable 
to the fastener as identified in the NAAMS Global Standard Components – 
Assembly, Chapter F; Fastener Components. 
• 
Fasteners which fall outside the GVOSS requirements of this section and are 
manufactured to other specifications, should be tested to the standard they 
are manufactured to. 
3.1.11.3.1 
Testing Laboratory Certification: The testing laboratory shall have A2LA 
(American Association for Laboratory Accreditation) Certification for Mechanical 
Testing to the applicable standards for Fasteners. 
3.1.11.3.2 
Unfailed Fastener Testing (Typical): 
• 
Chemical Analysis 
• 
Tensile Strength 
• 
Proof Load 
• 
Surface Hardness 
• 
Core Hardness 
• 
Magnetic Particle Inspection 
• 
Dimensional Inspection 
3.1.11.3.3 
Failed Fastener Testing (Typical): 
• 
Chemical Analysis 
• 
Tensile Strength 
• 
Surface Hardness 
• 
Core Hardness 
• 
Magnetic Particle Inspection 
• 
Micro Hardness of Fracture 
• 
Macro Examination 
• 
Micro Examination 
• 
Grain Size Evaluation 
• 
SEM (Scanning Electron Microscope) Evaluation  
• 
Report of evaluation. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 10 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.1.11.4 
General Suspect/Counterfeit Indications include the following: 
• 
No manufacturer’s or grade mark (unless certified to a specification not 
requiring marking). 
• 
Evidence of machining marks. 
• 
Poor thread form, evidence of wear, or dressing. 
• 
Head marks shown on the DOE Suspect Fastener Head Mark List: Suspect 
Bolt Head Marks  
• 
Foreign manufacturer not meeting Public Law 101-592 (Fastener Quality 
Act). 
• 
No markings for nuts or washers packaged with labels indicating that they 
were manufactured to a code or MIL-SPEC that requires marking. 
• 
Head markings are marred, missing, or appear to have been altered. 
• 
Head markings are inconsistent with a heat/lot. 
• 
Double stamping. 
• 
Head marks with raised marks and depressed marks on same bolt (not 
normal manufacturing process). 
3.1.11.5 
Approved Manufacturers. The following manufacturers are approved without 
testing: 
• 
Safety Socket 
• 
Unbrako® - SPS Technologies 
• 
Holo-Krome® 
• 
Kerr Lakeside Inc. 
3.1.11.6 
Approval of other Manufacturers. Manufacturers not listed in 3.1.11.5 above may 
be approved upon submission of an Action Request Form provided they meet: 
• 
GVOSS 3.1.11.2 
• 
GVOSS 3.1.11.3 
• 
GVOSS 3.1.11.3.1 and a copy of the Testing Laboratory Certification 
accompany the request. 
• 
The testing in GVOSS 3.1.11.3.2 is met if not already covered in the 
applicable standards of GVOSS 3.1.11.2. 
3.1.11.7 
Prohibited Fasteners. Fasteners bearing the following headmarks should be 
treated as defective without further testing. 
• 
TUZ 
 
Maudle Industrial, Co., LTD.

No. 1295-1, Chong Cheng Rd., Chi Feng Vill., 
Wu Feng Hsiang Taichung

3.1.11.8 
Replacing Counterfeit Fasteners. When counterfeit fasteners are identified in use 
on tools and equipment supplied to Ford Motor Company, the Tier1 Integrator is 
responsible for the replacement of all fasteners identified as counterfeit. This 
includes Tier 2 equipment and components purchased by the Tier1 Integrator.
