3.2 
TYPICAL MACHINE COMPONENTS 
3.2.1 
Locators, Rest Surfaces, Pins and Bushings 
NOTE: Locator dimensions have changed from 25.0 mm to 20.0 mm wide. This 
change is for new model programs. Legacy (existing) product lines that currently 
use 25.0 mm wide locator surfaces shall remain 25.0 mm unless otherwise 
directed by Ford Motor Company Dimensional Management Engineering (DME). 
3.2.1.1 
General. Production welding equipment and body closure tooling part locating 
blocks, pin locators and positioning fingers shall support TVDCD (Total Vehicle 
Dimensional Control Discipline) objectives as follows: 
3.2.1.1.1 
Locating Blocks and Positioning Fingers: Details shall be NC machined to 
computer-aided design data within ±0.08 mm tolerances from DATUM surfaces 
to part contact surfaces. 
3.2.1.1.2 
Pin Locator Blocks: Details that contact the part shall be N.C. machined to 
computer-aided design data.  The mounting hole for the pin shall be located 
within ±0.03 mm from DATUM. 
3.2.1.1.3 
Dowel holes to be located within ±0.03 mm and screw attach holes within ±0.13 
mm from DATUMS. 
3.2.1.1.4 
NC locator shall be certified with electronic coordinate measuring machine 
(CMM) to above tolerances.  At the time of tool buy-off, the Ford Manufacturing 
Engineer shall be provided with a computer-generated printout (in metric 
dimensions) containing the following information: 
NC locator number (Definition of Locator Symbols) 
Construction source and build date 
Theoretical (nominal) points 
Actual points and deviation  
Dimensional Control “Locator Definition Module”   
 
3.2.1.1.5 
Fitting Spacers 
3.2.1.1.5.1 
Fitting Spacers used to position the locators (not fingers) to the certification 
tolerances have a "nominal" five (5) millimeter thickness. When setting part 
geometry, use two (2) 5 mm Fitting Spacers; grind one (1) Fitting Spacer only. 
After dimensional verification of the locators and fingers, the ground Fitting 
Spacer shall be tack welded to the tooling attaching surface (not the NC locator 
or finger) by the supplier. On respot and other non-geometry setting applications, 
use one (1) 5 mm Fitting Spacer and do not tack-weld it to the attaching surface. 
See example below. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 17 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.1.1.5.2 
On Hand Apply Tools, Fitting Spacers used to position the locators (not fingers) 
to the certification tolerances have a "nominal" five (5) millimeter thickness. 
When setting part geometry use only one (1) Fitting Spacer. After tooling 
certification do not tack-weld the ground Fitting Spacer to the attaching surface. 
Stamp the nominal size of the ground Fitting Spacer onto the net or pin retainer. 
3.2.1.1.6 
The mounting and heeling surfaces of the locators will be located at a whole (not 
fractional) millimeter number (5.0 mm preferred) and verified by CMM or Laser 
Tracker.  "L" blocks shall not be shown on the details of the part location blocks, 
pin locators and fingers.  The "L" block mounting and heeling surfaces can vary 
up to plus or minus two millimeters with the fitting spacers compensating for the 
stack up of tolerances in the tooling, including the "L" block. 
3.2.1.1.7 
Positioning Finger: Locate by adjusting it to the certified locating block.  This is 
accomplished by activating the clamp that toggle locks the arm against the 
internal clamp stop.  Using the fitting spacers adjust the finger to achieve a gap 
that does not exceed the maximum material thickness of the located product. 
3.2.1.1.8 
Locating blocks, positioning fingers and fitting spacers shall have a black oxide 
finish. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 18 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.1.1.9 
New applications requiring locators to contact Class A surfaces must be avoided. 
If during the locator development stage, no other alternative exists, then the 
application must be formally documented as a deviation. Applications previously 
identified on the Standard Master Control Point files as Class A surface locators 
do not require a deviation. 
All part locating blocks that contact Class A part surfaces shall be 50.0 mm wide 
urethane and should not break through major geometry character lines unless 
otherwise specified. All edges must be 3 mm radius with ball corners NC cut. 
Mating pressure fingers when required shall be constructed in a manner that 
does not damage the Class A surface. (Class A refers to all exterior and interior 
parts of the vehicle that are exposed after paint or trim.) Urethane covered details 
shall be 55-65 durometer on the Shore "A" scale and colored red. The master 
control point drawing will reflect the 50.0 mm wide control surface. All part 
locating blocks and their mating pressure fingers that contact Class B and Class 
C part surfaces shall be 20.0 mm wide steel details. (Class B refers to parts of 
the vehicle not covered by trim materials but exposed to view when the vehicle 
door or enclosure lids are in an open position. Class C refers to parts that are 
wholly or partially concealed during normal use of the vehicle.) 
3.2.1.1.10 
All part locating blocks and their mating pressure fingers that contact Class B and 
Class C part surfaces shall be 20.0 mm wide steel details. (Class B refers to 
parts of the vehicle not covered by trim materials but exposed to view when the 
vehicle door or enclosure lids are in an open position. Class C refers to parts that 
are wholly or partially concealed during normal use of the vehicle.) 
3.2.1.1.11 
All blocks and fingers shall be designed to clear the part bend radii with a 
standard 3.0 x 3.0 mm chamfer unless the radii exceed the normal metal 
thickness as identified on the part print.  The periphery (edges) of the block and 
finger shall have square corners (no radii or chamfer) unless otherwise specified 
on an individual basis by the Ford Manufacturing Engineer. 
3.2.1.1.12 
Non-locator rest blocks used in part transfer applications that contact Class A 
part surfaces shall be urethane and should not break through major geometry 
character lines unless otherwise specified. All edges must be 3 mm radius with 
ball corners NC cut. Urethane covered details shall be 40-45 durometer or less if 
required by the application, on the Shore "A" scale and colored red. The block 
shall be sized appropriately to prevent damage to the Class A surface. 
The supplier bears the sole responsibility for understanding the dynamic forces 
applied to the part during transfer. Designing the rest block accordingly and 
preventing damage to the Class A surface during part transfer. 
3.2.1.1.13 
Part locating backup blocks shall not be attached to series 50 or larger 
commercial clamps, including Part Compensating type clamps, for the purpose of 
locating parts in the tooling. Part locating backup blocks may be attached to 
Series 40 Clamps.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 19 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.1.2 
Master Control Pins Tolerances, Subassembly Operations. A subassembly 
operation is defined as an operation where the two master control pins engage a 
single stamping within each loose component of the subassembly. Specifications 
and tolerances for master control locating pins used in subassembly operations 
are addressed in two conditions listed below:   
Condition #1:  A single part located in one plane by one or more ROUND H 
(main) Master Control Holes (MCH's) and one or more SLOTTED h (secondary) 
MCH's: 
A round locating pin shall be used in each round H MCH for four-way master 
control.  The nominal pin diameter shall be 0.15 mm less than the nominal 
diameter of the round H MCH in the part. 
A flat-sided pin shall be used in each slotted h MCH for two-way master control.  
The width of the pin in the control direction shall be 0.15 mm less than the 
nominal width of the slotted h MCH in the control direction. 
Condition #2:  A Single Part located in one plane by one or more ROUND H 
MCH's and one or more ROUND h MCH's: 
A round pin shall be used in each round H MCH for four-way master control.  The 
nominal pin diameter shall be 0.15 mm less than the nominal diameter of the 
round H MCH in the part. 
For h MCH two-way, there are two options:  
NOTE: Selection of Options #1 or #2 for two-way master control of a single part 
requires approval from the Ford Manufacturing Engineer. 
Option # 1 - A Diamond Pin shall be used in each round h MCH for two-way 
master control.  The nominal pin width in the control direction shall be 0.15 
mm less than the nominal diameter of the round h MCH in the part. 
Option # 2 - A Round Pin shall be used in each round h MCH for two-way 
master control.  The nominal pin diameter shall be 0.53 mm less than the 
nominal diameter of round h MCH in the part. 
3.2.1.3 
Requirements for standard non-developed locator pins 
3.2.1.3.1 
All locating pins shall be NAAMS Global Standard Components – Assembly, 
Chapter I; Locating Pins and Retainers Components. The pin material shall be 
Uncoated H13 48-52 HRC. 
 
 Respot Pin (tool) size (0.28mm less than Dia.) shall be used in all areas except 
Geo Stations and where otherwise noted. See below. 
 MH Robot – Pin (tool) size (1.00 mm less than part hole Dia.) shall be used. 
 Operator load table - Pin (tool) size (1.00 mm less than part hole Dia.) shall be 
used. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 20 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 Robot pass tables - Pin (tool) size (1.00 mm less than part hole Dia.) shall be 
used. 
 Accumulating conveyor carrier – Pin (tool) size (1.00 mm less than part hole Dia.) 
shall be used.  
 P&F conveyor carrier - Pin (tool) size (1.00 mm less than part hole Dia.) shall be 
used. 
 Stud Weld Applications – GEO pin (tool) size (0.15 mm less than part hole Dia.) 
shall be used. 
 Weld Nuts/Bolts – GEO pin (tool) size (0.15 mm less than part hole Dia.) shall be 
used. 
 Sealer/Adhesive Applications – Respot Pin (tool) size (0.28 mm less than part 
hole Dia.) shall be used. 
 Geometry setting Robotic Vision Hang and Docking End Effectors – GEO pin 
(tool) size (0.15 mm less than part hole Dia.) shall be used. 
 Rack / Dunnage Stands - Pin (tool) size (2.00 mm less than rack / dunnage 
locating hole Dia.) shall be used. 
3.2.1.3.2 
Locating pins used with high strength steels (i.e. Boron, Dual Phase (DP), 
Martensitic (MS) Ferritic Bainite (FB), etc.) shall be uncoated H13 48-52 HRC. 
3.2.1.3.3 
Locating pins used with aluminum product shall follow NAAMS Standard H13 for 
material and hardness except for the following conditions: 
• When pinning Hydro-form Product 
• When pinning locator features that are not punched square to grid, or when 
pins are not square to punch angle. 
For the above conditions, DLC (Diamond Like Carbon) coating or equivalent shall 
be used.  
DLC coating requires the pin material to be made from 4140 steel. 
• Hardened specification: 52-56 Rc 
• Surface finish: 0.16 micrometer prior to coating 
• Respot pin size (0.28mm less than Dia.) shall be used in all areas except Geo 
Stations 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 21 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• If locating pin is not square to grid in the Geo Station the pin shall be respot 
size 
• Coating process shall not negatively impact pin hardness 
3.2.1.3.4 
All pin retainers shall be NAAMS Global Standard Components – Assembly, 
Chapter I; Locating Pins and Retainers Components. 
3.2.1.3.5 
No integral pin and block set-up shall be permitted. 
3.2.1.3.6 
Locating pins shall not be retained using set screws. 
3.2.1.4 
Requirements for non-standard locator pins developed for special 
applications.  
3.2.1.4.1 
All locating pins will be machined within + 0.00 mm/-0.02 mm from nominal pin 
diameter, as described in 3.2.1.2 and 3.2.1.3.  
3.2.1.4.2 
All locating pins shall comply with the NAAMS Global Standard Components – 
Assembly, Chapter I; Locating Pins and Retainers Components material and hardness 
standards. 
3.2.1.4.3 
Locating pins used with aluminum product shall follow NAAMS Standard for 
material and hardness except for the following conditions: 
• When pinning Hydro-form Product 
• When pinning locator features that are not punched square to grid, or when 
pins are not square to punch angle 
• When Assembly Processes exerts high levels of force/friction on pins (e.g. 
BIW Framing Buck) 
For the above conditions, DLC (Diamond Like Carbon) coating or equivalent shall 
be used.  
DLC coating requires the pin material to be made from 4140 steel 
• Hardened specification: 52-56 Rc 
• Surface finish: 0.16 micrometer prior to coating 
• Respot pin size (0.28mm less than Dia.) shall be used in all areas except Geo 
Stations 
• If locating pin is not square to grid in the Geo Station the pin shall be respot 
size 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 22 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.1.4.4 
All pin retainers shall be NAAMS Global Standard Components – Assembly, 
Chapter I; Locating Pins and Retainers Components. 
3.2.1.4.5 
No integral pin and block set-up. 
3.2.1.4.6 
Locating pins (non-standard) shall not be retained using set screws. 
3.2.1.4.7 
Special attention shall be given to provide proper pin lead for the application. 
3.2.1.4.8 
Special attention shall be given to provide proper pin sheet metal engagement 
length for the application. 
 
3.2.1.5 
Locator pins shall engage the product a minimum of 3 mm, not including the 
taper lead. Maximum engagement shall not exceed 6 mm without review and 
concurrence of the VOME ME. The locator pin tip shall not be closer than 4 mm 
to an adjacent part. See image below. 
NOTE: Maximum engagement requirements do not apply to Pin Clamps. 
 
3.2.1.6 
Industrial Materials.  All pins shall be loaded into Team Center. When identifying 
locating pins on the Industrial Materials - Component List the following shall 
apply: 
• 
For NAAMS Pins 
a. NAAMS pins shall use the NAAMS part number for the manufacturer's 
part number. 
b. The manufacturer's name shall be "NAAMS Standard" 
• 
For Developed (manufactured to a non-NAAMS design) Pins 
a. The manufacturer's part number shall be the detail number 
b. The manufacturer shall be the Tier1 Integrator. 
c. The information for "ZF/ZM Number", "Sheet Number", "Detail Number" 
and "Blueprint Date" shall be entered on the Industrial Materials – 
Component List. 
• 
Descriptions:  All pin descriptions shall contain the following information: 
a. Pin locator type

- 
2-way 
- 
4-way 
- 
Diamond 
b. Pin diameter 
- 
2-way dimension 
- 
4-way dimension 
- 
Diamond 2-way and 4-way dimension 
- 
Special Manufacture – Hole square to surface not square to grid 
c. Pin overall length – “L” Dimension on NAAMS drawing. 
3.2.1.7 
NAB Pins and Bushings 
3.2.1.7.1 
Locating bushings for plastic NAB pins shall be +0.15mm over the nominal 
diameter of the NAB pin. 
3.2.1.7.2 
Locating bushings for plastic NAB pins shall be manufactured to +0.00 mm/-0.02 
mm of the diameter specified in 3.2.1.8.1 above. 
3.2.2 
Slides  
3.2.2.1 
Slide Units shall use linear bearings. 
3.2.2.2 
The bearing manufacturer application engineer or bearing manufacturer’s 
engineering representative shall approve the application of the product. 
3.2.2.3 
Linear bearing blocks shall have the bearing lubrication points connected to a 
lubrication junction block.  
3.2.2.4 
Slides shall utilize positive stopping method in the work position to assure 
repeatability. 
3.2.2.5 
The actuating mechanism and slide shall not bind in the extend position, retract 
position or during transition from one position to the other. 
3.2.2.6 
Shock absorbers shall be used when their absence will result in damage to the 
unit or surrounding tooling.  
3.2.2.7 
The moving member of the slide shall include mechanical locking of the slide in 
both work position and returned position. 
3.2.2.8 
Any adjustment point on the slide shall be accessible. 
3.2.2.9 
The prime mover and its attachment point to the moving member shall be 
accessible for maintenance. 
3.2.2.10 
In the event of air loss, if the moving member is subject to motion by force of 
gravity, provisions shall be made for using a manual safety pin to secure the 
moving member in each position from falling during maintenance. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 24 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.2.11 
In the event of air loss, if the moving member is subject to motion by force of 
gravity and is in the work envelope of an operator, an "air-off/spring set" safety 
pin shall be used and be engaged each machine cycle when the operator has the 
ability to enter the unit’s envelope of motion. This requirement must be 
coordinated with the latest safety standards. 
3.2.2.12 
The use of standard slide packages, such as those manufactured by Welker, 
requires the concurrence of the VOME Design Follow-up Engineer and VIS. They 
do not require an Action Request form for a Deviation. 
3.2.2.13 
Purchased standard slide packages shall meet the requirements of this section 
however they are allowed to deviate from other sections of the GVOSS. 
3.2.3 
Fitting Spacers, Shims and "L" Blocks 
3.2.3.1 
The use of shims is prohibited except in Hem Tools. 
3.2.3.2 
Fitting spacer shall be NAAMS Global Standard Components – Assembly, 
Chapter J; Shims and Spacers Components. 
3.2.3.3 
"L" blocks shall be NAAMS Global Standard Components – Assembly, Chapter H; 
L-Blocks Components 
3.2.3.4 
“L” blocks shall attach with one screw and two dowels (3-Hole pattern) at a 
minimum. 
3.2.4 
Pivot Units 
3.2.4.1 
Pivots shall utilize positive stops in the work direction to assure repeatability. 
3.2.4.2 
The actuating mechanism and pivot shall not bind in the extend position, retract 
position, or during transition from one position to the other. 
3.2.4.3 
Shock absorbers shall be used when their absence will result in damage to the 
unit or surrounding tooling. 
3.2.4.4 
The moving member of the pivot shall not move off of location while in working 
position. 
3.2.4.5 
Any adjustment point on the pivot shall be accessible. 
3.2.4.6 
The prime mover and its attachment point to the moving member shall be 
accessible for maintenance. 
3.2.4.7 
All pivot mechanisms, stops, structure, etc., shall be designed to provide a 
minimum 4:1 safety factor relative to the forces applied. 
3.2.4.8 
A locking (over center) linkage mechanism is preferred over a latching 
mechanism in designs where cylinder action is employed on pivot unit motion. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 25 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.4.9 
In the event of air loss, if the moving member is subject to motion by force of 
gravity, provisions shall be made for using a manual safety pin to secure the 
moving member in each position during maintenance. 
3.2.4.10 
In the event of air loss, if the moving member is subject to motion by force of 
gravity and is in the work envelope of an operator, an "air-off/spring set" safety 
pin shall be used and be engaged each machine cycle when the operator has the 
ability to enter the unit’s envelope of motion. This requirement must be 
coordinated with the latest safety standards. 
3.2.4.11 
On pivot units that are mounted to moving equipment that disconnects from the 
air supply for transport, the pivot unit shall be secured in the transport position by 
an "air-off/spring set" safety pin.  
NOTE: Clamps used to secure position are prohibited. 
3.2.4.12 
All pivot mechanisms shall be designed with bearings. 
3.2.4.13 
Pivot bearings shall be either spherical plain bearings or spherical roller bearings. 
3.2.4.14 
All pivot shafts shall be retained by a keeper plate. All keeper plates must be 
accessible for service. 
3.2.4.15 
Use of NAAMS Global Standard Components – Assembly, Chapter N; Pivots Index (or 
equivalent) pivot shafts where practical is preferred. 
3.2.4.16 
"Stub" Shafts shall be used where shaft removal is difficult or impossible due to 
length of the shaft or weight or location of the unit. 
3.2.4.17 
All pivot shafts shall be accessible for removal. 
3.2.4.18 
Roll pins for retaining shafts are prohibited. 
3.2.5 
Clamps 
3.2.5.1 
Power Clamps 
3.2.5.1.1 
All pneumatically operated Power Clamps shall be NAAMS Global Standard 
Components – Assembly, Chapter G design. 
3.2.5.1.2 
Pneumatic clamps shall be designed to operate at 65 psi (4.5 bar, 450 kPa). 
3.2.5.1.3 
Clamp Mounting 
3.2.5.1.3.1 
All tooling risers and clamp risers shall be of a one-piece burnout construction. 
End Effector risers shall continue to use the NAAMS angle bracket/blade 
strategy. 
For Kitted Programs – NAAMS angle bracket and blade strategy shall be used 
unless stated in Statement of Work. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 26 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.5.1.3.2 
Clamps shall use dowel hole construction for locating. 
3.2.5.1.3.3 
Clamps shall not be heeled or shouldered to the riser. 
3.2.5.1.3.4 
Front Mount clamps shall have a gap between the upper and lower notch 
surfaces. See example below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.5.1.4 
Clamp arms shall be as short as possible. Refer to clamp manufacturer's 
specifications. 
3.2.5.1.5 
NAAMS Global Standard Components – Assembly, Chapter G; Power Clamps 
standard machined clamp arms shall be used. If this task is not feasible then 
consider modification of a standard non-machined arm. Special arms should be 
avoided. 
3.2.5.1.6 
All power clamps shall use fixed orifices if not already fitted or provided in the 
clamp ports. See also 2.4.7.11.8. 
3.2.5.1.7 
Clamp guidelines shall include the following: 
• All clamps are mechanically locked in the closed position but not locked in the 
open position.  
• There is a button on the clamp that can be used to override the mechanical 
lock of the clamp in the closed position. 
3.2.5.1.8 
All power clamps shall have a proximity switch as their signaling device. 
3.2.5.1.9 
The opening angle of the power clamp shall be identified and indicated (i.e. 
match mark) on the power clamp. 
3.2.5.2 
Clamp “L” Blocks and Spacers 
3.2.5.2.1 
Four-way adjustment with an "L" block and two 5.0 mm fitting spacers shall be 
used on clamp arm locator “Finger” in the following application: 
• 
Finger contacts two surfaces 
• 
Finger contacts surface in a corner 
3.2.5.2.2 
One 5.0 mm fitting spacer shall be used in all other applications 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 27 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
NAAMS Global Standard Components – Assembly, Chapter H; L-Blocks 
Components for "L" blocks. (One screw and two dowels at a minimum per 
mounting surface.) shall be used. 
3.2.5.2.3 
NAAMS Global Standard Components – Assembly, Chapter J; Shims and 
Spacers Components for fitting spacers (5.0 mm nominal thickness) shall be 
used. 
3.2.5.3 
Clamps used as Locator Back-Up Dumps 
3.2.5.3.1 
Totally enclosed side arm power clamps are preferred for control NC surface 
locator backup dumps. 
3.2.5.3.2 
External stops are required for all applications. 
3.2.5.3.3 
The stationary stop shall be flat and the stop on the arm shall be crowned. The 
crown stop shall have a fitting spacer behind it. 
3.2.5.3.4 
The backup arm shall be generally shorter than the finger arm. 
3.2.5.3.5 
"L" block(s) must be used with fitting spacers for tool certification.  
3.2.5.3.6 
Wedge lock clamps shall not be used in applications where a part could fall or be 
damaged due to loss of pressure.  
3.2.5.3.7 
Total clamp arm and locator package weight must not exceed published clamp 
manufacturers weight load specification. 
3.2.5.3.8 
Fixed orifices are preferred however, flow controls are allowed if the clamp speed 
cannot be adequately controlled with fixed orifices. 
3.2.5.4 
Clamps used as Pin Dumps 
3.2.5.4.1 
Totally enclosed side arm power clamps are preferred for pin dumps. 
3.2.5.4.2 
Pin dumps are approved for respot operations only, i.e., respot operations where 
model mix requires dumping pins for clearance.  
3.2.5.4.3 
External stops are required for all applications. 
3.2.5.4.4 
The stationary stop shall be flat and the stop on the arm shall be crowned. The 
crown stop shall have a fitting spacer behind it. 
3.2.5.4.5 
Parts are only to be loaded when pins are in clamp extended position; never 
pivot pins into the part. 
3.2.5.4.6 
Wedge lock clamps shall not be used in applications where a part could fall or be 
damaged due to loss of pressure.  
3.2.5.4.7 
Total clamp arm and pin package weight must not exceed published clamp 
manufacturers weight load specification. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 28 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.5.4.8 
Fixed orifices are preferred however, flow controls are allowed if the clamp speed 
cannot be adequately controlled with fixed orifices. 
3.2.5.5 
Non-Standard Clamp Arm Detail Drawings   
3.2.5.5.1 
The fabrication datum’s (O-Lines) on all clamp arms are the center of the 
octagon in two directions.  The side of the arm that is nearest the center of the 
clamp is the datum in the third direction. 
3.2.5.5.2 
The machining datum’s (O-Lines) on all clamp arms are the center of the dowel 
in the clamp closest to the arm in two directions. The clamp mounting surface is 
the datum in the third direction.  Show the head of the clamp in phantom lines for 
reference. 
3.2.5.5.3 
Design the arms by modifying a standard NAAMS non-machined arm. 
3.2.5.6 
Magnetic Clamps 
3.2.5.6.1 
Magnetic Clamps shall be Magswitch®. 
    
 
3.2.5.6.2 
All magnetic clamps shall use proximity sensors to detect each state change, i.e. 
magnetic field on-magnetic field off. 
3.2.5.6.3 
Proximity sensors shall not require adjustment. 
3.2.5.6.4 
Unless otherwise specified the magnetic clamp, proximity sensor shall be Balluff, 
model as specified by Magswitch®. 
3.2.5.6.5 
The proximity sensors shall be identified separately from the Magswitch® on the 
Industrial Materials lists. 
3.2.5.6.6 
Magswitch engineering shall be consulted during project design reviews to 
ensure the latest CAD models and assemblies are utilized, and that best practice 
application standards for Magswitch products are implemented in the design. 
 
3.2.5.7 
Applications:

Pneumatic Actuation (3 way/5 position Valve required) 
• 
Magnetic Holding on Flat surface and less than 2.0mm = Magswitch D   
Series 
• 
Magnetic Holding on Areas requiring custom pole shoes to match metal 
contour = T or AR series   
Electric Actuation (24Vdc Powered; Ethernet/IP Communication)  
• Magnetic Holding requiring proper part present, double part detection, bin 
picking or destacking = E Series   
 
3.2.6 
Couplings, Companion Flanges, and Shaft-Hub Connectors 
3.2.6.1 
General Coupling Considerations:  Couplings are used to connect driving shafts 
to driven shafts. The appropriate coupling shall be selected according to 
application. Requirements include the following: 
3.2.6.2 
The use of rigid couplings shall be prohibited. 
 
3.2.6.3 
The use of Flex-Rigid Gear or single disc couplings shall be acceptable in 
floating shaft applications only. 
     
 
3.2.6.4 
When applicable, disc couplings are preferred due to the following: 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 30 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
a. No requirement for lubrication or maintenance. 
b. No requirement for disassembly for inspection. 
c. Resistance to environment. 
d. Discs can be checked while equipment is running. 
e. No backlash. 
f. No components are subject to wear. 
3.2.6.5 
Coupling hubs shall be attached to shafts using a straight bore with key seats 
and redundant setscrews. All couplings shall minimally be bored for an ANSI 
B4.1 FN1 (ISO standard 286) light drive fit on the shafts they are assembled to.  
3.2.6.6 
Couplings shall be installed in accordance to the coupling manufacturer’s 
installation instructions. 
3.2.6.7 
Couplings shall be accessible for maintenance and replacement. 
3.2.6.8 
When the coupling is guarded, provisions shall be made for ease of inspection. 
The preferred method is a hinged guard. 
3.2.6.9 
Couplings shall be accessible for the purposes of mounting equipment to check 
shaft alignment. When the coupling is guarded, the guard shall be constructed in 
a manner to facilitate ease of removal. 
3.2.6.10 
Coupling keyways shall be cut according to American Gear Manufacturer 
Association AGMA 9002-B04 (inch) or AGMA 9112-A04 (metric) or ISO Standard 
21.120.30: Keys and keyways, splines. 
3.2.6.11 
Coupling Alignment 
Driving and driven shafts shall be aligned within the coupling manufacturer’s 
recommended specifications for offset and angular alignment. Alignment to 
manufacturer’s maximum specifications shall not be allowed. Additional 
requirements include the following: 
a. The coupling manufacturer’s alignment specifications, both recommended 
and maximum shall appear on the equipment assembly drawings. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 31 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
b. Either Reverse Indicator Method or Laser Alignment System, using 
equipment specifically designed for measurement of shaft alignment, shall be 
used to measure shaft alignment. The preferred method is laser alignment. 
 
 
 
 
  
c. Laser trackers, theodolites and transits are not acceptable measurement 
tools for shaft alignment. 
d. Shaft alignment shall be measured and documented prior to coupling 
installation. The documentation shall include: 
Coupling manufacturer information 
Coupling manufacturer’s maximum shaft alignment specifications 
Coupling manufacturer’s recommended shaft alignment specifications 
Coupling model number and type 
Coupling service factor 
Distance BSE (Between Shaft Ends) 
Measured shaft offset alignment 
Measured shaft angular alignment 
e. Ford Motor Company shall approve the method and equipment used to 
measure shaft alignment. 
f. 
The shaft alignment documentation shall be included in the Equipment Buy-
Off book. 
g. The shaft alignment shall be measured and documented after equipment 
installation in the receiving Ford Motor Company Facility. One copy of this 
documentation shall be provided to the Plant ME Manager and one copy 
shall be provided to the VOGO Body Launch Leader. 
3.2.6.12 
Shaft-Hub Connectors 
The listing below applies to shaft-hub connectors not covered in 3.2.6 above. 
3.2.6.12.1 
The use of Taper-Lock® shaft-hub connectors shall be prohibited. (Taper-Lock is 
a registered trademark of Dodge/Rockwell automation.) 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 32 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.6.12.2 
Shaft-hub locks and keyless bushings shall be installed in accordance to the 
manufacturer’s specifications. Special attention shall be made to the bolt 
tightening pattern and torque sequence. 
3.2.6.12.3 
"QD Bushings" (Quick Disconnect Bushings) shall be used in applications 
requiring keys. See example below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.6.12.4 
When using a keyless connector, a permanent match-mark shall be made in 
order to indicate slippage of the connector on the shaft. 
3.2.6.12.5 
Match-marks shall be easily visible to the naked eye for the purpose of visual 
inspection for connector slippage. 
3.2.6.12.6 
Keys shall be used in applications where more than one hub or lever is installed 
on a shaft. 
3.2.6.12.7 
All shaft-hub connectors shall be installed in accordance with manufacturer’s 
specifications. Special attention shall be given to requirements for the cleanliness 
of the shaft, shaft connector, shaft machining tolerances and shaft finish 
requirements.

3.2.6.13.1 
Companion flanges shall be used to connect Cardan shafts to shafting.

SF 
SLF

3.2.7 
Bearings - General  
The appropriate bearing shall be selected according to application and approved 
by Bearing Manufacturer. 
3.2.7.1 
Bearing Size and Life 
3.2.7.1.1 
The bearing L10 life shall be calculated for each bearing application. The bearing 
L10 life shall not be confused with the service life of the bearing. Using the 
bearing service life in lieu of the bearing L10 life is prohibited. 
3.2.7.1.2 
The L10 equation and all parameters used in the L10 calculation shall be 
documented for each bearing application. 
3.2.7.1.3 
The minimum acceptable L10 life shall be 40,000 hours. 
3.2.7.1.4 
When bearings are packed with a permanent type lubricant, the lubricant life 
shall be documented. 
NOTE:  L10 Life of bearings packed with a permanent type lubricant is only valid 
when the lubricant life is greater than the calculated L10 Life. The Bearing Life will 
be limited to the lubricant life. When the lubricant life is less than the Bearing L10 
Life, the L10 life shall be considered invalid.  
3.2.7.1.5 
The use of Rulon® is prohibited for all bearing applications except pneumatic 
cylinder clevis pins where the unit has a hard stop and pneumatic cylinder 
trunnions unless the following documentation is provided: 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 34 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
- Maximum allowable bearing wear 
- Wear factor 
- Load in psi 
- Sliding velocity in ft./min 
- Calculated time to reach the maximum allowable bearing wear in hours. 
3.2.7.1.6 
All bearings shall be sealed-type or sealed to prevent environmental 
contamination and loss of lubricant. 
3.2.7.2 
Spherical Roller Bearings 
3.2.7.2.1 
All shafting shall use double row spherical roller bearings except as specified 
elsewhere in the GVOSS. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.7.2.2 
Spherical roller bearings shall be of the mounted style. Approved mountings are: 
Pillow (Plummer) Block, Split Pillow Block, Flange and Take-Up. (See examples 
below.) 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.7.2.3 
Split Pillow Blocks shall be used in applications where bearing location and 
alignment must be maintained after bearing replacement.  
NOTE: Bearing replacement of a Split Pillow Block mount does not require 
removal of the unit base. 
3.2.7.2.4 
Spherical Roller bearings and mounting units shall be applied, sized and installed 
according to the manufacturers' specifications. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 35 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.7.3 
Spherical Plain Bearings 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.7.3.1 
Spherical Plain Bearings shall be used in linkage and other oscillating 
applications where a connecting pin or shaft rotates less than 360°.  
3.2.7.3.2 
Spherical Plain bearings shall be "Maintenance Free" models using a PTFE 
(polytetrafluoroethylene) liner with seals. 
3.2.7.3.3 
Spherical Plain bearings shall be applied, sized and installed according to 
manufacturers' specifications. 
3.2.7.4 
Ball Bearings 
3.2.7.4.1 
Ball Bearings shall be dual row self-aligning type. 
 
3.2.7.4.2 
Ball bearings shall be of the mounted style. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 36 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.7.4.3 
Ball bearings and mounting units shall be applied, sized and installed according 
to the manufacturers' specifications.  
3.2.7.5 
Linear Ball Bushings 
3.2.7.5.1 
Linear Ball Bushings shall be used for Linear Guide Shafts. 
 
3.2.7.5.2 
Linear Ball Bushings shall be "closed type". 
3.2.7.5.3 
Linear Ball Bushings shall have seals. 
3.2.7.5.4 
Flange Mounted Linear Ball Bushings may be used 
 
3.2.7.6 
Adapter Style Bearings 
3.2.7.6.1 
When using adapter style bearings, the adapter lock nut shall be tightened to the 
bearing manufacturer's specifications. 
 
3.2.7.6.2 
The equipment supplier shall furnish the plant a single point lesson on the proper 
procedure for installing the sleeve and tightening the adapter lock nut. 
3.2.7.6.3 
The equipment supplier shall include the adapter lock nut spanner type and 
ordering information in the Single Point Lesson and include it on the Industrial 
Materials Component List. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005

3.2.7.6.4 
The equipment supplier shall include the adapter lock nut spanner type and 
ordering information on the Industrial Materials Component List. 
3.2.7.7 
Linear Bearings and Rail 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.7.7.1 
For linear rail with special modifications that reference a blueprint (CAD file), the 
blueprint shall be stored in Team Center. 
3.2.7.7.2 
Linear bearings shall be caged ball style or roller spacer style. 
3.2.7.7.3 
Only "Standard" bearing blocks shall be used. 
3.2.7.7.4 
The use of "Long" and "Narrow" bearing blocks is prohibited. 
3.2.7.7.5 
Dowels in the bearing blocks are prohibited.  When carriage dowels are required 
to locate the master carriages the dowels shall be installed external to the 
carriages to "heel" the carriage in position as illustrated in below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.7.7.6 
Options that require the bearing blocks be installed on the rails by the 
manufacturer shall be prohibited. 
3.2.7.7.7 
When rail dowels are required on the master rail, the dowels shall be installed 
external to the rails, on one side to "heel" the rail in position as illustrated below. 
This method will also allow the rail to be straightened, if necessary, per 
Manufacturers Recommendation’s.

3.2.7.7.8 
"Mounting Method 2", as shown below, can be used where it does not interfere 
with bellows function per Manufacturers Recommendation’s. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.7.7.9 
The "Presser Plate" acts as a "Jacking Bolt" to secure the rail position to the 
dowel. "Presser Plates" shall be located with the jacking screw opposite the 
dowel so as not to deflect the rail when tightened against the rail. 
 
3.2.7.7.10 
The manufacturer's specifications for bolt distance from rail end shall be adhered 
to. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.7.7.11 
Rails shall be installed in accordance with the manufacturer's installation 
specifications. 
3.2.7.7.12 
Welding of rails is prohibited.

3.2.7.7.13 
All screw holes in the rail shall have caps or plugs to prevent debris from 
accumulating in the mounting screws. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.7.7.14 
All rail caps or plugs shall be flush to the rail surface, to the rail manufacturer's 
specifications, so as not to cause damage or interfere with the bearing blocks. 
3.2.7.7.15 
All rails used in high contaminate areas or processes (such as welding or VIN 
etching) shall be evaluated and approved by VOME for protective guarding to 
protect the rail if required. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.7.7.16 
When using bellows, the rail length shall be sized to accommodate the bellows 
compressed length. 
3.2.7.7.17 
When appropriate for the application, telescoping sheet metal guarding is 
acceptable.

Full complement needle roller bearings are preferred.

Shafting surface finish and surface hardness shall meet the bearing

3.2.8 
Shafting 
3.2.8.1 
Scope 
This guideline provides Ford requirements and specifications for shafting used on 
but not limited to: 
1. Jack Shafts 
2. Head Shafts 
3. Tail Shafts 
4. Idle Shafts 
5. Pivot Shafts 
6. Stub Shafts 
3.2.8.2 
All shafting shall be minimum SAE 1045 TGP unless otherwise specified. 
3.2.8.3 
It shall be the equipment manufacturers' responsibility to identify and use special 
shafting when required.  
Example: Use of SAE 4140 to increase material endurance limit in the presence 
of fretting to improve shaft life. 
3.2.8.4 
Special shafting shall not be selected to reduce shaft diameter. 
3.2.8.5 
All shafting shall be sized as "commercial" shafting. 
3.2.8.6 
The equipment manufacturer shall make available, upon request, the calculations 
for shafting bending and torsional moments used in the shafting sizing. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 43 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.8.7 
When determining shafting size, the following Service Factors shall be used: 
1. Bending Moment – 2.5 
2. Torsional Moment – 2.5 
NOTE: The 2.5 Service Factors are due to E-Stop conditions which, in most 
cases, introduces a "Suddenly Applied, Heavy Shock Load" to the shafting. 
3.2.8.8 
Shafting shall be sized according to ANSI/ASME B106.1M – 1985 (or later) or 
ISO standard ISO/TC 14 - Shafts for machinery and accessories. 
3.2.8.9 
When it is necessary to turn-down shaft ends (stepped shaft), use as large a fillet 
as possible to keep the stress concentration to a minimum. The fillet shall be 
finished and polished smooth to remove scratches which will result in cracks and 
fatigue failure of the shaft. See example below. 
 
 
 
 
3.2.8.10 
Shafting tolerances shall meet bearing manufacturers' requirements. 
3.2.8.11 
Keyseats shall be machined in accordance to ANSI B17.1 – 1967 (R1998) (or 
later) or ISO Standard 21.120.30: Keys and keyways, splines. 
 
See also Section 3.2.28. Keyways and Keys. 
3.2.8.12 
Welding on shafting shall be prohibited. 
3.2.9 
Sprockets 
3.2.9.1 
Scope 
This guideline provides Ford requirements and specifications for sprockets.  
3.2.9.2 
All sprockets shall be steel. 
3.2.9.3 
Sprocket teeth shall be either flame or induction hardened. 
Small Diameter, Small Pitch (Driving Pinion) – Rockwell C35-50 
Large Diameter, Large Pitch – Rockwell C25-40 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 44 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.9.4 
Sprockets shall be either Type "B" or Type "C" Hub.  
Type "A" Hubs are prohibited except when used with a torque limiter. 
 
 
 
 
3.2.9.5 
QD Weld-On hubs are permitted with prior approval from Ford Motor Company. 
3.2.9.6 
QD Mounted hubs are preferred for driving pinion sprockets. 
 
 
 
 
 
(Section Views show bolts in standard and reverse mounting) 
3.2.9.7 
Taper lock hubs and split sprocket hubs are prohibited. 
3.2.9.8 
Sprocket bores, with keys where applicable, shall not exceed the sprocket 
manufacturers' recommendations for minimum hub wall thickness. 
3.2.9.9 
Driving sprocket bores shall be light drive fit (interference fit) to ANSI B4.1 FN1 or 
ISO standard 606:2015 Short-pitch transmission precision roller and bush chains, 
attachments and associated chain sprockets. (Note: These sprockets are not 
interchangeable with ANSI defined sprockets.)  
3.2.9.10 
Non-driving sprockets shall be location clearance fit to ANSI B4.1 LC6 or ISO 
standard 606:2015 Short-pitch transmission precision roller and bush chains, 
attachments and associated chain sprockets. (Note: These sprockets are not 
interchangeable with ANSI defined sprockets.)  
3.2.9.11 
Sprocket keys shall be cut to the manufacturers' specifications. 
3.2.9.12 
On all power transmission, head shaft and on any application requiring 
synchronization of sprockets, the sprockets shall be keyed. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 45 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.9.13 
All keyed sprockets shall use two setscrews, one over the key and one at 90 
degrees, unless otherwise specified. 
 
3.2.9.14 
Cone point setscrews shall be used on sprockets where the shaft is contacted by 
the set screw.  The shaft shall be drilled for the cone point set screw after final 
alignment of sprockets. 
3.2.9.15 
Cup point setscrews shall be used on conveyor sprockets over the key to secure 
the key to the shaft. 
3.2.9.16 
All setscrews, whether securing the key or contacting the shaft shall be backed-
up by an additional oval point setscrew. 
3.2.9.17 
All idler sprockets with bearings shall be ball bearing type. 
 
 
 
 
3.2.9.18 
Bushing type idler sprockets are prohibited. 
3.2.9.19 
Sprocket diameters shall be engineered to minimize chain chordal action. 
3.2.9.20 
Sprockets used with double pitched chain shall have an odd number of teeth to 
increase sprocket life.

Conveyor sprockets for CC-5 chain shall be fabricated from steel plate, SAE

3.2.9.22 
All lifts, lowerators, VLC’s and transfer cars must have a shaft collar or a set 
collar on each side of every sprocket.

Shaft Collars 
3.2.10.1 
This guideline provides Ford requirements and specifications for shaft collars 
(clamping collars). 
3.2.10.2 
Only two-piece shaft collars shall be used. 
 
 
 
3.2.10.3 
All shaft collars shall be steel.

3.2.11.1 
This guideline provides Ford requirements and specifications for setscrews. 
3.2.11.2 
Commonly referenced setscrew types are shown below:

3.2.11.4 
Setscrew dimensions shall conform to ASME/ANSI B18.3 or ISO standard 
21.060.10: Bolts, screws, studs

3.2.12 
Springs 
3.2.12.1 
This guideline provides requirements and specifications for springs. 
3.2.12.2 
All compression type spring applications shall utilize NAAMS Global Standard 
Components – Stamping, Chapter S; Springs and Accessories Die Springs. 
3.2.12.3 
All springs shall conform to the ISO 10243 International Standard classification, 
including color coding. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.12.4 
Springs shall be adjustable to their free length to allow replacement without the 
spring being under compression. 
 
 
 
3.2.12.5 
Springs shall not exceed 25% deflection. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 48 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.12.6 
Spring ends shall be closed and ground square to assure square seating and 
provide maximum bearing surface. 
 
3.2.12.7 
All springs shall use rods to control spring deflection. 
 
3.2.12.8 
A take-up adjusting bolt shall be considered a rod for the purposes of this 
standard. 
3.2.12.9 
Rod diameter shall not exceed the spring manufacturers' specifications. 
3.2.12.10 
See GVOSS Section 1.4.6 for Visual Factory requirements for compressed 
springs. 
3.2.12.11 
The preferred style of extension (tension) spring is in-line with full loop ends. 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 49 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.12.12 
Extension springs shall be guarded to prevent injury in the event of spring failure. 
3.2.12.13 
Disc springs are permitted only with prior approval from Ford Motor Company. 
 
3.2.12.14 
Disc springs arranged in stacks shall follow the manufacturers' recommendations 
for stack construction. 
 
 
 
        
3.2.12.15 
Disc springs arranged in stacks require a guide to prevent lateral movement 
under load. 
3.2.12.16 
Guides may be located on the inside diameter or in the outside diameter of the 
springs, but the internal guide is to be preferred. 
3.2.12.17 
The disc spring manufacturer's recommendations shall be followed for the 
clearance between the guide surface and the inside or outside diameter of the 
spring. 
3.2.12.18 
When possible, the guide rod and end thrust faces should be case hardened to a 
minimum of RC 55. 
3.2.12.19 
The guide surface should be turned to either a fine finish or preferably, ground.  
3.2.12.20 
The guide surface should be lubricated with molybdenum based high pressure 
grease. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 50 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.13 
Jackscrews (Jacking Bolts) 
3.2.13.1 
Jackscrews shall be minimum 3/8 inch (10mm) square head setscrews and shall 
use a hex jam nut to lock it in place.  
 
3.2.13.2 
Jackscrews shall be ASTM 574 – alloy steel, Rc 45 minimum through heat 
treated with class 2A threads. 
3.2.13.3 
Jackscrew tips may be either cup point (shown above) or half dog point (shown 
below). 
 
3.2.13.4 
Jackscrews shall be minimum 50mm in length. 
3.2.13.5 
When installed, the jackscrew shall be placed in such a manner so that there is 
approximately 25 mm, adjustment in both directions. 
 
3.2.13.6 
All jackscrews shall be located in an easily accessible location for adjustment. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 51 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.14 
Wear Bars 
3.2.14.1 
All steel wear bars for conveyor track shall be SA500 as manufactured by SAS 
Global Corporation or equivalent, see typical properties below 
 
 
 
3.2.14.2 
Floor conveyor wear bars shall be cut at the track splice on a 45-degree angle to 
the centerline of the wear bar in the horizontal plane. 
3.2.15 
Take-Up 
3.2.15.1 
All conveyors shall be equipped with tail shaft take-up units to maintain and 
adjust chain and belt tension. 
3.2.15.2 
The preferred method is the use of take-up frames (see example below). 
 
3.2.15.3 
Take-ups shall use spherical roller bearing housed units (see GVOSS 3.2.7). 
3.2.15.4 
Unless otherwise specified, all take-ups shall be spring type (see example 
below). 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 52 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.15.5 
The take-up spring shall be positioned on the take-up as shown above for 
maintenance access for adjustment. 
3.2.15.6 
The fixed end of the spring shall be seated on a hardened washer (see GVOSS 
3.2.12). 
3.2.15.7 
The adjustment end of the spring shall have one hardened washer, one 
adjustment nut, and one jam nut. 
3.2.15.8 
The take-up rod shall be long enough to back-off the adjustment nut far enough 
to relieve all spring pressure. 
3.2.15.9 
The adjustment and jam nut shall be accessible for adjustment tool use. 
3.2.15.10 
The take-up rod shall be positioned or guarded in such a manner so as to not 
present a hazard to personnel. 
3.2.15.11 
Proper take-up adjustment visual factory requirements shall follow GVOSS 
section 1.4.6.2. 
3.2.16 
Power Transmission Chain 
Power transmission refers to the connection of the driving system to the driven 
system. 
3.2.16.1 
Power transmission chain shall be roller chain. 
3.2.16.2 
Chain shall conform to ANSI B29.1 or ISO standards catalogue ISO/TC 100 - 
Chains and chain sprockets for power transmission and conveyors. 
3.2.16.3 
The use of off-set links in chain is prohibited. 
3.2.16.4 
The minimum chain size for power transmission is RC-60. 
3.2.16.5 
Unless otherwise specified, chain shall be duplex riveted style. 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.16.6 
Master links shall be painted safety white. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 53 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.16.7 
All roller chain shall be preloaded prior to installation for alignment of the various 
chain components such as pins, bushings and link plates to reduce initial chain 
elongation. 
3.2.17 
Chain Drives 
This section applies to power transmission from the driving to driven system. 
The figure below illustrates a typical chain drive. 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.17.1 
Sprockets shall have a minimum of 21 teeth. 
3.2.17.2 
The drive ratio i defined as i = S2/S1, shall be greater than 1. 
3.2.17.3 
The maximum drive ratio is 8:1. 
3.2.17.4 
Drive chain shall be an even number of pitches. 
3.2.17.5 
The driving sprocket shall be an odd number of teeth. 
3.2.17.6 
Sprocket center distance shall not be less than 30 times the chain pitch nor more 
than 50 times chain pitch. 
3.2.17.7 
The driving sprocket shall have a minimum of 6 teeth engaged with the chain and 
has at least 120º of chain-wrap (wrap angle) around the sprocket. 
 
 
 
3.2.17.8 
Applications were chain travels in the horizontal plane are Prohibited. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 54 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.17.9 
The driving sprocket shall be adjustable for the purpose of adjusting chain 
tension. 
3.2.17.10 
The amount of adjustment travel, beyond the initial set-up, shall be minimum 1% 
of the sprocket center distance. 
3.2.17.11 
Drive Orientation 
3.2.17.11.1 In horizontal shaft orientation a line extended between the driving and driven 
shaft centers as shown below, shall be inclined less than 45 degrees, with the 
slack strand on the bottom. 
 
3.2.17.11.2 In vertical shaft orientation when one shaft is located vertically above the other 
the drive shall be positioned slightly off the vertical as shown below. The tension 
side of the chain should be as close to a vertical orientation as possible. 
 
3.2.17.12 
Chain drives shall be guarded in accordance to GVOSS sections 1.3.6 and 
1.3.15. 
3.2.17.13 
Chain drives shall comply with the GVOSS Visual Factory standards. (Ref. 
GVOSS Section 1.4). 
3.2.18 
Conveyor Chain 
3.2.18.1 
Conveyor chain shall be roller chain. 
3.2.18.2 
Chain shall conform to ANSI B29.1 or ISO standards catalogue ISO/TC 100 - 
Chains and chain sprockets for power transmission and conveyors. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 55 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.18.3 
The use of off-set links in chain is prohibited. 
3.2.18.4 
The minimum ANSI roller chain size for conveyors is RC-60. 
3.2.18.5 
Unless otherwise specified, chain shall be riveted style. 
NOTE: Some conveyor chain may be specified as cotter style. 
 
3.2.18.6 
Master Links shall be painted safety white. 
3.2.18.7 
CC-5 Chain shall conform to the following: 
• 
Ford Global Manufacturing Standard E-CC005 for 6 inch (150mm), Pitch 
Deep Link Roller Chain 
• 
Branch Drawing 3XF-9577, Sheets 22 thru 23 for Production Chain 
• 
Branch Drawing 3XF-9577, Sheet 24 for Accumulating Chain 
3.2.18.8 
Master links shall be painted safety white. 
3.2.18.9 
All roller chain shall be preloaded prior to installation for alignment of the various 
chain components such as pins, bushings and link plates to reduce initial chain 
elongation. 
3.2.19 
Leaf Chain 
Leaf chains have a greater strength/weight ratio than Roller chains. 
3.2.19.1 
Chain used for counterbalances with cylinder lift shall be leaf chain. 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 56 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.19.2 
Leaf chain shall be made from interlaced plates connected with hardened pins as 
defined in ISO 4347 latest edition. 
3.2.19.3 
Leaf chains shall be used with sprockets since there is no means of engagement.  
3.2.19.4 
Only lengths of factory assembled chain shall be used. It is prohibited to build 
lengths from individual components. 
3.2.19.5 
Damaged chains shall not be repaired. The entire chain may be compromised 
and shall be discarded and replaced with new chain. 
3.2.19.6 
Welding shall be prohibited on any chain or component.  
3.2.19.7 
Welding spatter should never be allowed to come in contact with chain or 
components. 
3.2.19.8 
If a chain must be heated with a cutting torch for removal, the chain shall not be 
reused. 
3.2.19.9 
Joining chains together shall only be done by the chain manufacturer. 
3.2.19.10 
The minimum ultimate strength of a chain is the minimum load at which it will 
break when subjected to a destructive tensile test. It shall not be used as the 
working load. 
3.2.19.11 
Chains from different manufacturers shall not be used in the same application. 
3.2.19.12 
Chain shall be an odd number of pitches with inner links at both ends. 
3.2.19.13 
Chain shall use a clevis connected to the inner links at both ends as shown 
below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 57 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.20 
Synchronous Belts 
3.2.20.1 
Belts used for power transmission shall be synchronous style and shall be 
capable of handling the tension applied by the drive motor with a 2.0 service 
factor applied to the nameplate rating. 
 
 
 
 
3.2.20.2 
Belt Registration 
This section applies to applications for positioning systems. 
3.2.20.2.1 
Static registration systems which move from an initial static position to a 
secondary static position shall be designed for minimum system backlash. 
3.2.20.2.2 
Dynamic registration system which performs a registering function while in 
motion with torque loads varying as the system operates shall be designed so 
that registration inaccuracies from belt elongation, backlash, and tooth deflection 
are minimized. 
3.2.20.2.3 
Additional guidelines that may be used in designing registration critical drive 
systems are as follows: 
• 
Design with large sprockets with more teeth in mesh. 
• 
Keep belts tight, and control tension closely. 
• 
Design frame/shafting to be rigid under load. 
• 
Use machined sprockets to minimize radial run out and lateral wobble. 
3.2.20.3 
The belt tensile modulus shall be chosen to provide resistance to harsh shock 
and impulse loads. 
3.2.20.4 
Belts shall be dimensionally stable to minimize belt elongation. 
3.2.20.4.1 
Backlash from clearance between the belt teeth and the sprocket grooves shall 
meet the application requirements. Some considerations are: 
• 
Torque carrying capability 
• 
Resistance to ratcheting 
• 
Belt wear 
• 
Drive noise 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 58 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
Drive life 
3.2.20.5 
Sprocket size, installation tension and belt type shall be selected to minimize the 
amount of belt tooth deformation from torque loading in the design. 
3.2.21 
Synchronous Belt Sprockets 
3.2.21.1 
Sprockets shall have guide flanges to prevent the belt from riding off the edge of 
the sprocket. 
 
 
 
 
3.2.21.2 
Horizontal shaft power transmission drives shall have flanges on each side of 
both sprockets. 
3.2.21.3 
Vertical shaft power transmission drives shall have one sprocket flanged on both 
sides and the other sprocket flanged on the bottom side only. 
3.2.21.4 
The following guidelines should be used when selecting flanged sprockets on all 
other applications: 
1. Two-sprocket systems, the minimum flanging requirements are two flanges 
on one sprocket or one flange on each sprocket on opposite sides. 
2. On systems where the center distance is more than eight times the diameter 
of the small sprocket, both sprockets should be flanged on both sides.  
3. On drives with more than two sprockets, the minimum flanging requirements 
are two flanges on every other sprocket or one flange on every sprocket—on 
alternating sides around the system. 
3.2.22 
Synchronous Belt Drives 
This section applies to power transmission from the electromechanical drive to 
the driven system that is connected by a belt. 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 59 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.22.1 
Fixed (nonadjustable) center distance drive designs are prohibited. 
3.2.22.2 
Belt drives shall use an overhung load connection factor of 1.3. 
3.2.22.3 
The tensioned belt shall not exceed 70% of the speed reducers overhung load 
capacity. 
3.2.22.4 
The tensioned belt shall not exceed 70% of the driven shaft overhung load 
capacity or shaft bearings load capacity. Extreme belt tensioning shall be 
avoided as it reduces belt life and results in bearing damage. 
 
 
 
 
3.2.22.5 
Belt tension shall be adequate to prevent ratcheting (tooth jumping) under full 
load and e-stop conditions. 
3.2.22.6 
The driving sprocket shall have a minimum of 6 teeth engaged with the belt and 
has at least 60º of belt-wrap (wrap angle) around the sprocket. 
3.2.22.7 
The driving sprocket shall be adjustable for the purpose of adjusting belt tension. 
3.2.22.8 
Belt pulleys shall use keyed bushings. 
3.2.22.9 
The amount of adjustment travel, beyond the initial set-up, shall be minimum 1% 
of the sprocket center distance. 
3.2.22.10 
The driving and driven sprockets shall be aligned to the belt manufacturers' 
specifications. Parallel and angular misalignment is to be minimal to reduce belt 
wear. 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 60 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.22.11 
Belt drives shall be guarded in accordance to GVOSS sections 1.3.6 and 1.3.15.  
3.2.22.12 
Belt drives shall comply with the GVOSS Visual Factory standards. (Ref GVOSS 
Section 1.4). 
3.2.22.13 
An additional label shall be affixed to the guard identifying the belt deflection 
when using a spring tester (see example below) and frequency when using 
ultrasonic tensioning for the belt when properly adjusted. 
 
3.2.22.14 
Belts shall be re-tensioned after the 2,500-cycle run for buy-off. 
3.2.23 
Fixed (Nonadjustable) Center Distance Synchronous Belt Designs 
Fixed center distance designs are typically used in power roll beds to transmit 
power from roller assembly to roller assembly (see image below). 
 
3.2.23.1 
Fixed center designs shall only be used lightly loaded or motion transfer 
applications. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 61 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.23.2 
Care should be taken when using a fixed center design for drives requiring high 
motion quality or registration precision. 
3.2.23.3 
When using a fixed center design, the supplier shall take into consideration the 
effects of system tolerance accumulation in the system design. 
3.2.23.4 
Belt tension shall not result in overloading of bearings, shafting, or belts from a 
high-tension condition due to system tolerance accumulation.  
3.2.23.5 
Belt tension shall not result in loose operating conditions that result in 
accelerated wear and belt ratcheting due to system tolerance accumulation. 
3.2.23.6 
Belts shall not ratchet upon system e-stop. 
3.2.23.7 
Fixed center designs should have the center distance held as accurately as 
possible. Typical tolerances should be within 0.002”—0.003” (0.05mm—0.08mm) 
center-to-center. 
3.2.23.8 
Sprockets for fixed center systems should be produced with a machining process 
for accuracy. Molding and sintering processes are generally not capable of 
holding the finished O.D. sufficiently accurate for these systems. 
3.2.23.9 
Belt pulleys shall be welded to the shaft. 
3.2.23.10 
The performance capabilities of the drive system should be verified by testing 
belts produced over their full-length tolerance range on drive systems 
representing the full potential center-distance variation. 
3.2.23.11 
The belt manufacturer shall approve the application design. 
3.2.23.12 
Unless otherwise specified, guarding shall comply with GVOSS Section 1.3.6. 
3.2.24 
Powered and Non-Powered Rollers 
This section applies to roller assemblies of roll beds used for skid and pallet 
transport. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 62 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.24.1 
All powered and non-powered rolls shall be a common design for, but not limited 
to, all power roll beds and two-strand speed-up rolls provided for a given project. 
3.2.24.2 
When multiple suppliers are providing powered and non-powered rolls in their 
equipment, they shall concur on a common design. 
Ford Motor Company shall provide assistance when requested. 
3.2.24.3 
The powered and non-powered roller assembly shall be comprised of two 
flanged rolls welded to a tubular steel shaft.  The tube steel shaft/roller weldment, 
which includes ball bearings pressed into machined seats on both ends to 
provide rotational capability, shall be assembled to a steel axle that is fixed to the 
conveyor frame rails.  For additional information regarding the powered/non-
powered roller design, refer to the following Branch Standard drawings: 
• For skid conveyor applications, refer to 3ZF-5410-36424 sheet 3 for drive 
rollers and 3ZF-5410-36424 sheet 4 for driven rollers.  
• 
For pallet conveyor applications, refer to 3ZF-5410-36424 sheet 1 for the 
driven roller and 3ZF-5410-36424 sheet 2 for the driven roller.  
• 
For combined skid/pallet conveyor applications, refer to 3ZF-5410-36424 
sheet 5 for drive rollers and 3ZF-5410-36424 sheet 6 for driven rollers. 
3.2.24.4 
HTD tooth profile synchronous belts shall be used to drive the rollers.  
3.2.24.5 
Belt sprockets shall be welded to the tubular steel shaft. 
3.2.24.6 
Rollers may be either steel or polyurethane coated steel depending on the 
application.  It is the suppliers' responsibility to make the proper selection for the 
application.   
3.2.24.7 
Polyurethane coated rollers must have a durometer rating sufficient to provide 
the required duty cycle without replacement for a minimum of 10 years. 
3.2.24.8 
All rollers shall be sized to accommodate at least 33% of the required duty cycle 
which includes but is not limited to total design load (skid/pallet with part) at the 
required speeds, torques and e-stop conditions. 
3.2.24.9 
The equipment manufacturer shall provide the roller manufacturer with the 
design belt tension. 
3.2.24.10 
The equipment belt tension shall not exceed the roller manufacturers' 
recommendation. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 63 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.25 
Floor Conveyor Belts 
This section does not apply to GVOSS section 3.7: Belt Conveyors. 
3.2.25.1 
All belts used for conveyance shall be endless steel reinforced rubber with 
vulcanized splice and shall be new and free of imperfections. The belt supplier 
shall be consulted regarding the application duty-cycle and shall provide written 
approval of the selected belt size and type, including the splice design 
requirements. 
3.2.25.2 
Belts used for in-line skidded product conveyance shall minimally be wide 
enough to cover one- and one-half times the width of the bottom of the skid. 
3.2.25.3 
Belt sprockets and pulleys may be keyless bushing.  When using keyless 
bushings, the bushing shall be sized to handle the torque applied by the drive 
with a 1.3 service factor. 
3.2.25.4 
The supplier shall include one belt splicing kit of each size/type belt used for all 
applications in the Industrial Materials list. 
3.2.26 
Torque Limiters 
3.2.26.1 
Only detented style Torque Limiters shall be used. 
 
3.2.26.2 
Friction style torque limiters are prohibited. 
3.2.26.3 
Torque limiters shall use coil springs. 
3.2.26.4 
Leaf spring torque limiters are prohibited. 
3.2.26.5 
The detent spring compression shall be adjustable. 
3.2.26.6 
Torque limiters shall have a switch to sense when the unit is out of detent. 
3.2.26.7 
When the torque limiter is out of detent, drive motion shall stop. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 64 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.26.8 
Torque limiters shall have a tag mounted next to them that identifies the direction 
of rotation to restore the unit to detent. The tag shall have both text and a 
directional arrow and identify if the direction is forward or reverse. 
3.2.26.9 
Torque limiters shall be initially set-up to function for the proper overload capacity 
at approximately 50% of spring compression. 
3.2.27 
Cardan Shafts 
3.2.27.1 
All Cardan shafts shall be spline type (see figure below). 
 
 
 
3.2.27.2 
Only "Z" and "W" configurations that operate in a single plane shall be allowed. 
 
 
 
 
"Z" Configuration 
 
 
 
"W" Configuration 
3.2.27.2.1 
Each design using "W" configurations shall require the approval of the VOME 
Standards Engineer. 
3.2.27.2.2 
"W" configurations are prohibited for use in applications where the companion 
flanges change relationship to one another. For example, the transfer drives on a 
Lift-And-Carry transfer system where the companion flange moves in a vertical 
position relative to the electromechanical drive position. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 65 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.27.3 
All applications shall follow the manufacturers' specifications for permitted 
operating deflection angles (ß1 and ß2 shown in the figures above). 
3.2.27.4 
In all application, the shafts shall be aligned so that the difference between 
operating deflection angles ß1 and ß2 is within the manufacturers' specifications 
for difference between deflection angles. 
3.2.27.5 
Support structures for the driving and driven system shall be constructed to 
maintain shaft alignment. 
3.2.27.6 
Support structures for the driving and driven system shall be constructed and 
anchored so that there is no system vibration. 
3.2.28 
Keyways and Keys 
3.2.28.1 
The key shall fit tightly to the shaft keyway. Recommended tolerance on keyway 
width is +0 / -0.002 inch (-0.05 mm). 
3.2.28.2 
Recommended tolerance on key width is +0.002 / -0 inch (+0.05 mm)  
3.2.28.3 
A tight side-to-side fit is required between the keys and the shaft and hub 
keyways. Hand fitting at assembly is usually required. 
3.2.28.4 
The key shall have clearance with the top of the keyway. A minimum headroom 
clearance of 0.005 inch (0.125 mm) is recommended. 
 
 
 
 
3.2.28.5 
The key corners shall be chamfered for the keyway radii fit in accordance to 
ANSI B17.1 or ISO Standard 21.120.30: Keys and keyways, splines. 
3.2.28.6 
Keys shall be flush to the end of the shaft. 
 
 
 
 
3.2.28.7 
Double setscrews shall be used to secure keys. Cone-point setscrews shall be 
used over the key to secure the key to the shaft and shall be backed up by an 
additional oval-point setscrew.  
3.2.28.8 
Use Lock-Tite 242 (or equivalent) removable thread lock on the key setscrews.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 66 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.2.28.9 
Setscrews over keys shall be paint marked after tightening. 
3.2.28.10 
Step keys shall be prohibited on new equipment. 
 
 
 
 
 
3.2.29 
Shaft Keepers 
3.2.29.1 
All shafts and pins used in clevis, pivot and linkages shall be prevented from 
rotation using keepers. 
3.2.29.2 
Cotter pins are prohibited.

3.3 
MOTORS, GEAR MOTORS AND SPEED REDUCERS 
3.3.1 
General Notes 
3.3.1.1 
For the purposes of this standard, a gear motor shall be defined as follows: 
• 
The motor is 'integrated' and directly attached to the speed reducer. This 
connection is usually a gear press fit onto the shaft of the motor. 
• 
The motor is mounted to the speed reducer using a "C"-faced flange. This 
connection uses a keyed coupling. 
3.3.1.2 
For the purposes of this standard, the term speed reducer and gearbox are 
interchangeable. 
3.3.1.3 
The Tier1 Integrator shall provide an accurate list of speed reducers, motors and 
gear motors planned to be used on any project. This list shall include 
manufacturer, part numbers, quantities, horsepower, rpm, etc. This list will be 
included in all quotes and may require the Tier1 Integrator to field check existing 
conditions. This list should be in Excel format and submitted to the Ford Motor 
Company VOME ME. 
3.3.1.4 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for mounting and or assembling the 
speed reducer in accordance with the manufacturer's installation procedures. In 
no manner shall any installation be provided such that it will violate the speed 
reducer manufacturer's warranty. 
3.3.1.5 
Every effort is to be made to communize speed reducer manufacturer, model and 
ratio throughout the project. 
3.3.1.6 
All speed reducers are to be mounted in such a manner that they can be 
removed without extensive effort.  
3.3.1.7 
The Tier 1 integrator shall review speed reducer mounting styles with Ford Motor 
Company for suitability to maintenance requirements.  
3.3.1.8 
All fluid fill and drain ports shall be accessible for plug removal and fluid drain 
and fill without removal of the speed reducer or any other adjacent equipment. 
3.3.1.9 
Gear motors shall be either shaft or foot mount. Speed reducers are to be 
mounted in the manufacturer's recommended 'normal' mounting position. Speed 
reducers may be inline, parallel offset or right angle. Right angle mounting is 
preferred as it will reduce the footprint and space requirements. 
3.3.1.10 
Right angle speed reducers shall use helical bevel gearing. 
3.3.1.11 
Double extended output shaft speed reducers where only one output shaft is 
used are prohibited. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 68 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.3.1.12 
Worm gear speed reducers 
3.3.1.12.1 
Cylindrical Worm gear speed reducers shall be prohibited. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.3.1.12.2 
Double Enveloping Worm gear speed reducers are permitted. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.3.1.13 
Step Shafts are prohibited. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 69 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.3.2 
Calculations 
The speed reducer manufacturer shall be contacted to validate application and 
sizing. Sizing by a distributor is considered invalid unless they are authorized, in 
writing, by the manufacturer. 
The intent is that the final drive sizing meets or exceeds the manufacturers sizing 
where service factors are concerned.  
This requirement for manufacturer calculations is so that: 
• 
The manufacturer is aware of the use of their product. 
• 
The manufacturer agrees that the sizing is sufficient. 
• 
The manufacturer will warrant their product for 3 years. 
The equipment supplier will be responsible for verifying all motor and gearbox 
calculations prior to build. The calculations shall include, but are not limited to the 
following: 
3.3.2.1 
OHL (Overhung Load) calculations for both the motor and gearbox shall include: 
• 
OHL Calculation 
• 
Transmission Element Factor 
• 
OHL Location Relative to Shaft Mid-Point 
• 
Manufacturers Permissible OHL 
3.3.2.2 
Driven System Calculations used in Motor Sizing shall include: 
• 
Acceleration Time for Driven System 
• 
Deceleration Time for Driven System 
• 
Cycle Time for Driven System 
All motor and speed reducer combinations on tool and facility conveyance cycling 
applications shall use a minimum of 1.5 seconds for the acceleration and 
deceleration curves. For conveyors requiring a creep speed prior to stopping the 
load, a second deceleration curve will be applied at this 1.5 second minimum. 
The typical creep speed shall be in the range of 15 feet (4.5 meters) per minute 
or 3 inches per second (75 mm/sec). All other applications shall not be below the 
drive manufacturers’ specified acceleration and deceleration durations for the 
application.  
• 
Efficiency Factors used 
• 
Friction Coefficients used 
• 
Identify Driven System Elements used in Calculations 
• 
Mass of Driven System Elements 
• 
Moments of Inertia for Driven System Elements 
• 
Torque  
• 
Maximum Speed 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 70 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.3.2.3 
Driven Systems Transferring Elements, Calculations used in Motor Sizing shall 
include: 
• 
Transferred Element Mass 
• 
Transferred Element Inertia 
3.3.2.4 
Motor Sizing Calculations shall include: 
• 
Rotor Inertia 
• 
Motor Efficiency 
• 
Motor r.p.m. 
• 
Motor Service Factor 
3.3.2.5 
Gearbox Sizing Calculations shall include: 
• 
Gearbox Efficiency 
• 
Ratio 
• 
Application Maximum Output r.p.m. 
• 
Application Maximum Input r.p.m. 
• 
Service Factor 
• 
Gearbox Manufacturers Maximum Torque Rating 
• 
Application Maximum Torque 
3.3.2.6 
Motion Profile. A Motion Profile shall be constructed as part of the calculation 
process (see Figures 3.3.2a and 3.3.2b).
