3.3.3 
Service Factor and Sizing 
Considerations for service factor and sizing include the following: 
3.3.3.1 
All horsepower and Kilowatt calculations shall use the speed reducer and motor 
manufacturer's published efficiency rating. 
3.3.3.2 
All speed reducers and horsepower, Kilowatt calculations, including formulas, are 
to be available for review upon request by Ford Motor Company VOME ME or 
Mechanical Standards Department prior to build.  
3.3.3.3 
All speed reducers shall use a minimum service factor of 2.0 which classifies 
them as Class 3 Heavy Shock. The intent is that the operating load capacity is 
50% of the speed reducer's ultimate load capacity. 
3.3.3.4 
All single-speed motors supplied with a gearmotor shall have a minimum service 
factor of NEMA 1.25 or IEC Duty Cycle S1. The Service Factor or Duty Cycle is 
to be determined in relation to the driven load.   
3.3.4 
Hollow Output Shaft Speed Reducers 
3.3.4.1 
For all equipment that does not require a main and standby drive, hollow shaft 
mount reducers are allowed. Hollow shaft mounting will eliminate the need for 
drive bases, adjusting plates, couplings, sprockets and belts. 
3.3.4.2 
Units greater than 60 lbs. (approx. 27 kg) in weight shall have overhead 
clearance in the machine so that a lift assist can be used for the removal of the 
unit. 
3.3.4.3 
Hollow output shafts using stub shafting may be used if pre-approved by the Ford 
Motor Company VOME Mechanical Standards Group. 
3.3.4.4 
Individual shaft ends may have steps machined in them to suit an application. 
Machined steps shall have a radius in the step to prevent stress concentration 
points. 
3.3.4.5 
Hollow output shaft gear motors mounted directly to the structure and using a 
stub shaft to connect to the driven system shall use a Flex-Flex coupling to 
connect the stub shaft to the driven system. See also GVOSS 3.2.6 - Couplings 
and Shaft Hub Connectors. 
3.3.4.6 
All shafts that are fabricated to fit hollow bore reducers are to be made from 
minimum 1045 ASTM steel. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 73 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.3.4.7 
Rigid (foot or flange) mounted hollow output shaft speed reducers used with 
bearing mounted shafting shall be prohibited. See example below.  
 
 
 
    
3.3.5 
Hollow Output Shaft Torque Arms 
Hollow output shaft speed reducers mounted directly to the driven system shall 
use a torque reaction arm with clevis attachment and rubber bushing (see Figure 
below). 
 
 
 
 
 
     
             
 
 
Correct 
Incorrect 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 74 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.3.5.1 
The arm is to be mounted on the driven system side of the speed reducer or in 
the manufacturer's recommended position.  
3.3.5.2 
Torque arms shall be easily removable with tools.  
3.3.5.3 
A universal torque arm mount shall be able to fit all sizes of approved speed 
reducer vendor combinations. 
3.3.5.4 
When the speed reducer is mounted directly to the driven system the speed 
reducer shall not be rigidly attached directly to the structure at any point. 
3.3.5.5 
Custom torque arms shall not be allowed without prior review and written 
permission from Ford Motor Company VOME. 
3.3.5.6 
The machine frame - torque arm attachment shall be a clevis. 
3.3.5.7 
The torque arm – clevis pin shall not rotate after installation. 
3.3.5.8 
The torque arm – clevis pin shall be coated with a Mo grease to prevent fretting. 
3.3.5.9 
The torque arm – clevis pin shall be easily accessible for removal. 
3.3.5.10 
The torque arm shall have a rubber bushing in the clevis attachment end to allow 
for minor misalignment. 
3.3.5.11 
The torque arm clevis shall be rigidly mounted to the support structure so as to 
not deflect under load. 
3.3.6 
Hollow Output Shaft – Shaft Locks 
3.3.6.1 
To control shaft thrust and for ease of disassembly, all foot-mounted hollow 
output shaft speed reducers shall employ a shrink disk or twin tapered bushing 
type shaft lock located on the opposite side of the torque arm (external side of 
the driven system). See also GVOSS 3.2.6 Couplings and Shaft Hub 
Connectors. 
3.3.6.2 
The output shaft shall be coated with a Mo grease to prevent fretting unless 
otherwise specified by the speed reducer manufacturer. See also GVOSS 3.15 – 
Preventing Shaft Fretting. 
3.3.6.3 
The shaft length that protrudes thru a shaft lock shall be sized so that it will fit all 
of the approved speed reducer vendor's sizes. 
3.3.6.4 
Shaft Locks are to be installed and tightened per the speed reducer and shaft 
lock manufacturer's instructions and specifications.  
3.3.6.5 
Snap rings shall not be used as the sole method to secure a foot-mounted speed 
reducer to the shaft.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 75 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.3.6.6 
Shafting finish will not be turned, ground and polished when using a shaft lock. 
The contractor is to supply all shafts with the micro surface finish in accordance 
with the shaft lock or reducer manufacturer's specification. 
3.3.6.7 
Speed reducers with hollow output shafts shall have the shaft lock guarded with 
a fixed cover. The cover shall be Safety Yellow. 
 
3.3.6.8 
Fixed covers shall be Safety Yellow. 
3.3.7 
Foot-mounted Speed Reducers 
3.3.7.1 
Foot-mounted speed reducers shall employ thru-hole mounting. 
3.3.7.2 
Mounting to the speed reducer casting holes that are drilled and tapped is to be 
avoided. 
3.3.7.3 
Foot-mounted speed reducers shall be checked for "soft foot" when performing 
shaft alignment. 
3.3.7.4 
Stainless steel shims only shall be used to make adjustments for "soft foot" 
corrections. 
3.3.7.5 
Foot-mounted speed reducers are to be supported by a 1 inch (25 mm) thick 
plate minimum. 
3.3.7.6 
Foot mounted reducers for chain or belt drive shall use a tensioning plate (take 
up). 
3.3.7.7 
The tensioning plate shall have sufficient adjustment to permit the easy removal 
of any sprocket, chain, pulley or belt mounted to the output shaft. 
3.3.7.8 
The tensioning plate shall have bolts to lock the plate in place after adjustment. 
3.3.7.9 
Foot-mounted speed reducers shall use two sets of jackscrews; one set in each 
direction to adjust the speed reducer location for alignment. 
3.3.7.10 
All electromechanical drive adjusting plates shall use a minimum of two 5/8-inch 
(16 mm) jackscrews to adjust the tensioning. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 76 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.3.8 
Gearing Requirements 
3.3.8.1 
Gear teeth are to be carburized case or induction hardened to 58-62 Rockwell C. 
3.3.8.2 
Helical gearing shall have a minimum of 98% efficiency per stage of reduction. 
3.3.8.3 
Gearing sets shall provide a backlash rating less than or equal to 15 arc minutes 
maximum. 
3.3.9 
Lubrication Requirements 
3.3.9.1 
All speed reducers and gear boxes shall be fitted with sight gages. See GVOSS 
Section 1.4.10.3 for sight gage requirements. 
3.3.9.2 
Sight gages shall be easily visible for lubricant level inspection purposes. 
3.3.9.3 
All speed reducers shall come pre-filled from the factory with a Ford Motor 
Company approved synthetic lubricant. 
3.3.9.4 
The equipment supplier shall be required to validate that the speed reducer or 
gearbox is pre-filled with sufficient lubricant and lamicoid tagged with the proper 
Ford Tox number. Failure to supply the speed reducer or gearbox with the proper 
lubricant at the required level will not be a cause to void the 3-year warranty.  
3.3.10 
Motors 
3.3.10.1 
Motor enclosures and bearings shall have a minimum degree of protection of IP 
54. 
3.3.10.2 
All motors shall comply with the Energy Independence and Security Act of 2007 
(EISA) as amended and IEC – International Electrotechnical Commission.  
3.3.10.3 
All motors shall be TEFC (Totally Enclosed Fan Cooled).  
3.3.10.4 
All motors shall be supplied with Class "F" Insulation of 4000 VAC RMS. 
3.3.10.5 
2-speed motors shall be prohibited. 
3.3.10.6 
Motors driven by a VFD shall be "Inverter-Duty" or "Vector-Duty" rated by the 
manufacturer for the drive type application. 
3.3.10.7 
Inverter-Duty and Vector-Duty motors shall meet the requirements of; 
• NEMA MG1-31 
• IEC – International Electrotechnical Commission – ISO referred 
3.3.10.8 
When using dual voltage motors, the applicable Control systems Voltages shall 
be used. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 77 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.3.10.9 
Dual voltage motors shall have a label affixed to the motor, near the terminal box, 
identifying the motor as dual voltage and the voltage being used. 
3.3.11 
Brakes – General 
3.3.11.1 
Brakes shall be spring-set (Power-Off). 
3.3.11.2 
Brakes shall be disk type. 
3.3.11.3 
Brakes should only be used on applications where they are required. For 
example: 
• 
Uncontrolled e-stop (Emergency Stop) conditions where the risk assessment 
identifies the e-stop time requires a brake. 
• 
Where positional holding is required and not controlled with a VFD.  
3.3.11.4 
All brakes on VFD applications shall be considered as emergency stop or holding 
brakes only. The VFD shall be used to decelerate the load down to creep speed 
and stop prior to engaging the brake. In no such manner shall the application rely 
solely on the brake to stop the load except in an emergency stop situation. 
3.3.12 
External Brakes 
3.3.12.1 
External brakes shall be used for transfer line lift drives. The brake shall be 
connected to the lift crank arm shaft. 
3.3.13 
Integral Mounted Brakes (Brake motor) 
3.3.13.1 
All Integral Mounted Brakes shall be sized for 3 million cycles. Brakes not 
providing this capacity shall not be used. 
3.3.13.2 
All brakes shall be sized for twice the motor nameplate/rated torque that the 
motor is capable of developing.  
3.3.13.3 
Brake rectifiers shall be mounted in the motor terminal box. 
3.3.13.4 
All brakes shall have a manual release. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 78 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.3.14 
SEW Eurodrive Gearmotors 
 
3.3.14.1 
General 
This section contains requirements when using SEW Eurodrive gearmotors.  
3.3.14.2 
The supplier shall inform SEW that the electromechanical drive is for use at Ford 
Motor Company on the issued P.O to SEW and shall be GVOSS compliant, 
along with the Program it is to be used on, at the time of first contact for the 
program. 
3.3.14.3 
All SEW Eurodrive gearmotors, motors, gear boxes and speed reducers shall 
have a SEW "Smart Number" assigned by SEW Eurodrive (FNA only). 
NOTE: The SEW Smart Number is NOT the SEW Eurodrive Quote Number. 
3.3.14.4 
The Smart Number shall be used as the "Manufacturers Part Number" in all 
Industrial Materials reports and the "Motor and Speed Reducer Use and Ordering 
Information" documentation for ordering information. The nameplate information 
shall be used as the "Component Description". 
3.3.14.5 
For all motors: 
NOTE: The Motor Series identified below are subject to change relative to the 
Federal government EISA (Energy Independence and Security Act) motor 
efficiency rules and implementation dates. 
• 
All motors used in duty-type S1, continuous duty applications shall be DRN 
Series (Per 10CRF 431 Subpart B).  
(Efficiency Class IE3) TEFC (Totally Enclosed Fan Cooled). 
• 
All motors used in duty-type S3 cyclic-duty applications shall be DR2L Series 
intended for intermittent duty for better efficiency. (Efficiency Class IE3) 
TEFC (Totally Enclosed Fan Cooled). 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 79 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
- All equipment suppliers responsible to ensure mechanical designs 
accommodate motor size 
3.3.14.6 
Motor windings shall be Low Voltage Δ (Delta) and High Voltage Y (Wye or Star) 
connections. 
3.3.14.7 
Motor voltage shall be specified as, the applicable Control systems Voltages 
shall be used. 
3.3.14.8 
Gearmotors shall have a catalog service factor of 2.0 or greater. 
3.3.14.9 
All sizing shall include the SEW Workbench data. The Workbench data shall be 
part of the manufacturer’s application sign-off and be made available upon 
request. 
3.3.14.10 
All SEW drives shall comply with GVOSS visual factory standard including tox # 
lamicoid tags, drain and fill identification, etc. (ref. section 1.4) 
3.3.14.11 
Brakes 
3.3.14.11.1 Motor brake coil voltage shall be specified for a power supply of the motor high 
voltage. See 3.3.14.7. 
3.3.14.11.2 Brake rectifier voltage shall be specified as 500 VAC. 
3.3.14.11.3 Brake rectifier shall be BGE (red). 
3.3.14.11.4 SEW Eurodrive UR brake relay (rapid response) shall be used in VFD, 2-speed 
motor and Allen Bradley SMC-3 Soft Starter applications. 
3.3.14.11.5 SEW Eurodrive SR brake relay shall be used in "across-the-line" starting 
applications. 
3.3.14.11.6 Brake contactor shall be AC contactors with contacts in utilization category AC-3 
to EN 60947-4-1; Low-voltage switchgear and control gear: Part 4: Contactors 
and motor-starters -- Section 1: Electromechanical contactors and motor-starters. 
3.3.14.11.7 The brake contactor circuit shall use an Electrocube RG1676-16 RC filter for 
contact arcing and noise suppression. 
3.3.14.12 
Use with Inverters 
3.3.14.12.1 The inverter shall be sized to motor current not Power Rating. 
3.3.14.12.2 The inverter shall be wired as per requirement as per the applicable Control 
systems specified Voltages. 
3.3.14.12.3 Motors shall operate in constant torque mode. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 80 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.3.14.12.4 Inverter applications operating at frequencies greater than 60 Hz require the 
following: 
• 
Application Design Review. 
• 
Approved Action Request – Deviation Form. 
• 
Motor Manufacturer Approval. 
• 
The motor must be designed to operate at the desired frequency. 
• 
Motor must be connected to low voltage delta winding. 
• 
Application shall not exceed 104 Hz. 
• 
Inverter (VFD) shall be appropriately sized to the application and NOT the 
motor nameplate FLA (Full Load Amperage). 
3.3.14.12.5 Inverter duty motor applications connected 266V delta (low voltage connection) 
shall be sized to 104 Hz. 
• 
This configuration produces constant torque throughout the speed range.   
• 
This is typically for applications where extended speed range is needed. 
• 
Reducer ratio must match the required maximum application speed and 
horsepower to the 104 Hz motor rpm.   
• 
The motor nameplate hp will be rated for region power frequency; (FNA 
example: 60 Hz). 
3.3.14.12.6 Motors wired for Low Voltage; 266 VAC, 3-phase, 50 or 60 Hz shall have a Low 
Voltage tag (see below) affixed to the motor VFD panel and on the equipment 
next to the motor. 
 
3.3.14.12.7 Inverters shall not exceed 90 Hz (2,700 rpm Motor speed) for motors without the 
approval of Ford Motor Company and SEW. Based on gearing and mounting 
position the maximum allowable motor speed is a function of the reducer gearing 
and mounting position. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

3.3.14.13.2 Driven shaft diameter and finish shall be to the manufacturers' specifications. 
3.3.14.13.3 Speed reducers with hollow output shafts shall have the shaft lock guarded with 
a fixed cover. The cover shall be Safety Yellow.

Movi (VFD) Technologies 
3.3.14.17.1 Wherever possible MoviFIT controllers shall be mounted to the tool or conveyor.

3.3.14.17.3 In all cases when the top of the MoviFIT controller is less than 36 inches (915

3.4 
MAGAZINE PART FEEDERS (INDEXING MAGAZINES) 
3.4.1 
General Communications 
3.4.1.1 
This section pertains to the Ford Motor Company requirements for magazine part 
feeders. 
3.4.1.2 
The intent of this section is to provide standards to deliver magazine part feeders 
as modular sub-systems to the Tier 1 Integrator. This includes both mechanical 
and controls. 
3.4.1.3 
Unless specified otherwise in this section, magazine part feeder requirements 
shall conform to the standards and specifications set forth in the Ford Motor 
Company GVOSS. 
NOTE: Special attention shall be paid to providing information, documentation 
and processes necessary to support Ford Motor Company Equipment R&M 
efforts and policies. 
3.4.1.4 
Indexing magazines shall be used only when the automation controls the 
indexing unless approved by MDFU and VIS.  
3.4.1.5 
Deviations to this section shall be allowed only after a GVOSS "Action Request" 
form is submitted and approved. 
3.4.1.6 
The Ford Motor Company P&SD (Process and Strategy Development) 
Department shall verify the magazine supplier has access to all applicable Ford 
Motor Company Standards. 
3.4.1.7 
The Ford Motor Company P&SD Department for each Program shall provide the 
information necessary to quote the magazines to meet the <PA> cost target. This 
includes, but is not limited to: 
• 
Part drawing (part number and description) 
• 
Part orientation on magazine 
• 
Part weight and approximate center of mass 
• 
Process (Where used in the manufacturing process) 
• 
Magazine length 
• 
Magazine width 
• 
Magazine capacity 
• 
Load and unload method (robot, automatic or manual) 
• 
Process cycle time 
• 
Maintenance platform size (where applicable) 
3.4.1.8 
The tooling and magazine suppliers shall have regular communications for the 
purpose of simultaneous engineering to exchange information relevant to the 
magazine and tooling "nest" design. The appropriate Ford Motor Company ME 
(Manufacturing Engineer) shall also be made aware of the meeting schedule.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 86 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.4.1.9 
The appropriate Ford Motor Company P&SD Department VIS (Vendor Interface 
Supervisor) for the given tooling OEM shall have the final say in resolving any 
points of contention between the magazine supplier and the Tier1 Integrator. 
3.4.2 
Tier1 Integrator Responsibilities 
3.4.2.1 
The Tier1 Integrator shall provide the magazine supplier with design parameters 
necessary to design the magazine. This shall include but is not limited to: 
• 
Tooling nest layout with significant dimensions. 
• 
Stop locations. 
• 
Locator scheme for tooling nest. 
• 
Acceptable positional repeatability of the part nest when mounted to the 
magazine. See 3.4.3.4 
• 
Required index time 
3.4.2.2 
The Tier1 Integrator shall submit all requests for changes either mechanical, 
electrical or controls to the magazine supplier for approval prior to requesting 
change approval from the Ford Motor Company VOME Manufacturing Engineer. 
3.4.2.3 
The Tier1 Integrator shall maintain a log of all magazine changes requested and 
approved. 
3.4.2.4 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for the integration of the magazine 
wiring and logic into their system. The magazine controls and logic shall function 
to the magazine manufacturers design intent. 
3.4.2.5 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for the magazine HMI screens and 
logic. 
3.4.2.6 
Conveyor Part Load and Unload 
3.4.2.6.1 
The Tier1 Integrator shall provide the part present sensing and any mechanical 
units required to stabilize parts at the load and unload escapements. Part-
present sensing shall also provide error proofing at the load escapement to 
identify an improperly loaded part and prevent it from reaching the unload 
escapement. 
3.4.2.6.2 
The magazine supplier is responsible for the positional repeatability of the part 
nest when mounted to the magazine. The Ford Motor Company VOME ME, VIS, 
Tier1 Integrator and magazine supplier shall concur on the acceptable positional 
variation. The target positional variation is 1.0 mm total variation. Variation shall 
be measured as the stop flag position at the stop position. 
3.4.2.6.3 
When a magazine has an automatic or robotic interface at the load or unload 
escapement, the Tier1 Integrator shall verify their end effector design interfaces 
properly with the pallet tooling "nest" design. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 87 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.4.2.7 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for the proper shipment of the 
magazines to the final receiving facility. 
3.4.2.8 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for the proper installation of the 
magazines in the final receiving facility. 
3.4.2.9 
The Tier1 Integrator shall report any damage to the magazine to the magazine 
supplier. 
3.4.2.10 
After receiving the magazine from the magazine supplier, the Tier1 Integrator is 
responsible for any cost incurred for repairing damage to the magazine.  
3.4.2.11 
Prior to any repairs being performed, the Tier1 Integrator shall agree to the scope 
of work and who will complete the work. 
3.4.2.12 
After completing repairs to a damaged magazine, the Tier1 Integrator shall buy-
off the acceptance of repairs.  
3.4.2.13 
The Tier1 Integrator is to take the lead on any 8D's relating to the magazine 
throughout the magazine life cycle. 
3.4.3 
Magazine Supplier Responsibilities 
3.4.3.1 
The magazine supplier shall construct a critical path for each magazine to 
monitor conformance to delivery timing. 
3.4.3.2 
Any deviations shall be submitted through the supplier and approved by Ford 
Motor Company VOME through the Action Request process 
3.4.3.3 
Part Nest Tooling 
3.4.3.3.1 
The magazine supplier shall provide the Tier1 Integrator with a tooling envelope 
and design parameters to use for magazine tooling "part nest" design. Design 
parameters include but are not limited to: 
• 
Chain attachment design 
• 
Maximum part nest height 
• 
Maximum part nest width 
• 
Maximum part nest length 
• 
Part center of mass 
• 
Part orientation 
3.4.3.3.2 
The magazine supplier shall verify the center of mass of the tooling "nest" and 
part will function properly on the magazine. 
3.4.3.3.3 
The magazine supplier shall install the magazine "part nest" tooling provided by 
the equipment supplier. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 88 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.4.3.3.4 
When "slat-type" magazines are used, the magazine supplier shall provide the 
Tier 1 Integrator with sub-plate design parameters. Design parameters include 
but are not limited to: 
• 
Sub-plate size. 
• 
Sub-plate to slat hole-pattern 
3.4.3.3.5 
When "slat-type" magazines are used, the magazine supplier shall be 
responsible installing the "part nest" sub-plate, which mounts to the slats. 
3.4.3.3.6 
When "slat-type" magazines are used, the "part nest" sub-plate mounting holes in 
the slat shall be tapped. The use of tapping plates welded to the underside of the 
slat or welding nuts (as long as the mechanical properties of the nut is not 
compromised by the welding process) to the underside of the slats is acceptable. 
When welded nuts are used, the threads shall be "chased" after welding to 
restore the threads from any deformation caused by the welding process. Other 
methods may be employed with the approval of the Ford Motor Company VOME 
VIS. 
NOTE: The primary objective is that the tool nest sub-plate be removed without 
requiring access to the backside of the slat. 
3.4.3.3.7 
The magazine supplier shall qualify the "part nest" tooling after installation using 
the qualification fixture furnished by the equipment supplier. The qualification 
fixture shall be returned to the Tier1 Integrator when the magazine is shipped to 
their facility. 
3.4.3.3.8 
The magazine supplier shall furnish the equipment supplier a "Notice of 
Qualification" to the equipment supplier stating the "part nest" tooling has been 
qualified. 
3.4.3.4 
The magazine supplier is responsible for the positional repeatability of the part 
nest when mounted to the magazine. The Ford Motor Company VOME ME, VIS, 
Tier1 Integrator and magazine supplier shall concur on the acceptable positional 
variation. The target positional variation is 1.0 mm total variation. Variation shall 
be measured as the stop flag position at the stop position. 
3.4.3.5 
The magazine supplier, upon receiving a change request from the Tier1 
Integrator shall provide a definitive response within 5 days. 
3.4.3.6 
All change and deviation requests shall be submitted to Ford Motor Company 
VOME through the Tier1 Integrator. 
3.4.3.7 
The magazine supplier shall furnish the Tier1 Integrator with proper handling, 
shipping, and installation instructions for the magazine. 
• 
All lift points shall be clearly identified and labeled on the conveyor. 
• 
All break points shall be clearly identified, and match marked on the 
conveyor. 
• 
Rigging and lifting the conveyor by the head or tail shafts shall be prohibited. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 89 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.4.3.8 
The magazine supplier shall buy-off the Tier1 Integrators' integration of the 
magazine into their system prior to Ford Motor Company buy-off of the 
equipment. This shall be documented and included in Tab 15 of the equipment 
suppliers' buy-off book. 
3.4.3.9 
The magazine supplier shall provide support for the magazine installation in the 
Ford Motor Company receiving plant. 
3.4.3.10 
The magazine supplier shall buy off the installation of their magazine at the Ford 
Motor Company receiving plant prior to cycling the magazine. 
3.4.3.11 
Upon receiving a report of damage to the magazine from the Tier1 Integrator or 
Ford Motor Company receiving plant, the magazine supplier shall assess the 
impact of the damage on the magazine function and useful life. 
3.4.3.12 
Prior to any repairs being performed, the magazine supplier shall submit a scope 
of work and who will complete the work to the Tier1 Integrator. 
3.4.3.13 
After completing repairs to a damaged magazine, the magazine supplier shall 
buy-off the acceptance of repairs. 
3.4.4 
Mechanical 
3.4.4.1 
Guards 
3.4.4.1.1 
The need and application for guarding shall be determined from a Risk 
Assessment. 
3.4.4.1.2 
All guarding shall be constructed from sheet metal, using expanded metal for 
visual inspection ports. The expanded metal opening size shall conform to 
OSHA's Code of Federal Regulations, Subpart O, 1910.217, Table O-10 for 
maximum permissible openings for guards. Any guarded point that needs to be 
accessed for problem diagnostic purposes, Preventative Maintenance activities 
and viewing the operation of the magazine shall be viewable without requiring 
guard removal. (See also GVOSS Section 1.4.10.) 
3.4.4.1.3 
At operator load areas, an outward swinging hinged silhouette guard shall be 
constructed of lightweight lexan/plexiglas with cutouts of the tooling profile to 
prevent access to the returning pallets prior to the load position. See Fig. 1 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 90 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
                                      
 
                                                                  Fig. 1 
3.4.4.2 
The magazine supplier shall supply a temporary structure, when necessary for 
try-out and buy-off at their facility and installation at the Tier1 Integrator's facility. 
The temporary structure shall allow the magazine to function in a manner 
identical to the final installation in the assembly plant. The part load points and 
unload points shall be the same location and elevation as dictated by the 
process. The structure shall also provide for mounting of the maintenance 
platforms and the maintenance platforms shall be mounted for the try-out and 
buy-off. 
3.4.4.3 
All switch brackets shall be adjustable. 
3.4.4.4 
Head shaft and tail shaft diameter shall be the same. 
 
3.4.5 
Electromechanical Drives 
3.4.5.1 
Drive motors and speed reducers shall conform to the requirements of GVOSS 
Section 3.3. 
3.4.5.2 
Approved gear motor and speed reducer manufacturers are: 
• 
SEW 
3.4.5.3 
Cycling rates of 89 JPH and under shall use brake motors for positional stopping. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 91 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.4.5.4 
Cycling rates of 90 JPH and over shall use a C-Face mounted Posidyne Clutch 
Brake for positional stopping. 
 
 
 
 
 
3.4.5.5 
Motor torque overload protection shall be provided. The torque overload 
protection shall protect the driving and driven system from damage in the event 
of a conveyor jam. 
3.4.5.6 
When the drive is directly coupled to the head shaft with a chain drive, detented 
torque limiters shall be used to provide motor torque overload protection. The 
torque overload protection shall protect the driving and driven system from 
damage in the event of a conveyor jam.  
3.4.5.7 
Friction type torque limiters shall be prohibited. 
3.4.5.8 
Detented torque limiters shall be used to provide motor torque overload 
protection. The torque overload protection shall protect the driving and driven 
system from damage in the event of a magazine "jam". 
3.4.5.9 
Detented torque limiters shall be equipped with a detection switch that shall shut-
off the drive motor and display a fault in the HMI when an overload condition 
occurs 
3.4.6 
Part Nest Tooling 
3.4.6.1 
Nut and bolt construction of brackets and tooling "part nest" shall be prohibited. 
3.4.6.2 
All part holders on magazine part feeders shall be designed for error-proofing so 
parts cannot be loaded between holders or improperly loaded. 
3.4.6.3 
All magazine part nests attached to the chain shall have a line block mounted to 
the magazine frame at the unload station for alignment of the part holder to the 
unload mechanism. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 92 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.4.7 
Maintenance Platforms 
3.4.7.1 
Maintenance platforms and guarding shall conform to GVOSS Section 1.3. 
3.4.7.2 
Maintenance platforms shall be provided on overhead magazines, where 
maintenance access requires personnel to stand at a height over six (6) feet from 
the floor (Reference ““Free Fall Distance” in FAS08-108). The maintenance 
platform should provide access to the drive, head shaft, tail shaft and tooling. 
When the magazine width is greater than 42 inches maintenance platforms shall 
be provided on both sides of the magazine along with a crossover walkway. See 
example below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Maintenance Platform with Crossover Walkway Example

3.4.7.3 
For maintenance activities at 6’ to 12’ use one of maintenance platforms as 
shown below. These maintenance platforms are to be positioned as close as 
possible to the conveyor structure. Minimum 33” needs to be provided between 
maintenance platform floor and conveyor drive centerline. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.4.7.4 
When maintenance personnel are required to stand at a height over twelve (12) 
feet from the floor, access to the conveyor shall be provided by upper platforms 
that run full length of conveyor as shown. See fig. 1. Special landing zones at 
each end of the conveyor are required to facilitate drives maintenance, pallet 
removal, etc. which otherwise are difficult to access from mezzanine. Minimum 
33” needs to be provided between maintenance platform floor and conveyor drive 
centerline.

3.4.8 
Magazine Chain Lubrication 
3.4.8.1 
Magazines shall have an automatic lubrication system for the magazine chain. 
The lubrication system reservoir shall be accessible for maintenance and clearly 
marked for the full and low lubricant levels to support Ford Motor Company visual 
factory objectives. 
3.4.9 
Operation 
3.4.9.1 
The operator part load height shall be determined by an ergonomic study.  
3.4.9.2 
The magazine part capacity shall be defined as the number of part holders that 
can be safely loaded. 
3.4.9.3 
Magazines will automatically index parts until the unload station has a (sensed) 
part in place.  The magazine will then function on a demand signal for parts from 
the fixture. 
3.4.9.4 
All magazines must include detection to turn on a yellow globe light that indicates 
75% empty and a limit to turn on a flashing red globe light that indicates empty. 
3.4.10 
Alignment 
3.4.10.1 
All points used to establish magazine "level" and "straight" shall be clearly 
identified. 
3.4.10.2 
Magazines shall be aligned for proper function over their useful life. The 
magazine supplier's installation procedures shall be followed. Their installation 
procedures shall include alignment procedures and requirements. Ford Motor 
Company VOME guidelines for alignment are as follows: 
• 
Head shaft to tail shaft sprocket-to-sprocket axial alignment should be 
±1.0mm (±0.039") or less.  
• 
Head shaft and tail shaft parallel alignment shall be 1° or less.  
For horizontal magazines the head shaft and tail shaft shall be level to gravity. 
For vertical magazines the head shaft and tail shaft shall be perpendicular to 
gravity.  
3.4.11 
Mounting 
3.4.11.1 
Each floor-mounting pad on the frame shall have leveling hardware.  
3.4.11.2 
Leveling hardware shall conform to GVOSS Section 1.1. 
3.4.11.3 
The magazine supplier shall furnish all leveling and mounting hardware with the 
exception of anchors and anchor bolts. Anchors and anchor bolts shall be the 
responsibility of the Tier1 Integrator when installing the magazine at their facility 
and the installation contractor when installing the magazine at the Ford Motor 
Company receiving facility. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 99 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.4.12 
Controls 
3.4.12.1 
Controls component manufacturers used on magazines shall be consistent with 
those specified by the “Approved Controls Components” document supplied by 
VO Controls. If there is no approved component for the application, VO Controls 
Engineer or VIS for the program shall identify approved component 
manufacturers. 
3.4.12.2 
The Ford Motor Company VO Controls Engineer for the program shall identify 
any applicable Standard Ford controls reference drawings to be used. 
Standard Reference Drawings for Legacy systems (non-Next Gen Controls 
architecture): 
70ZF-20010764: Indexing Magazine using Brake Motor 
70ZF-20010765: Indexing Magazine using Posidyne Clutch-Brake 
70ZF-20016032: Indexing Conveyor Drive Panel (Movifit)  
Controls Reference Drawings for Next Gen and GVOSS Controls architecture 
are stored in Team Center. 
3.4.12.3 
Unless specified otherwise by the Ford Motor Company VO Controls Engineer 
for the program, the following Controls Architecture shall be used: 
• 
Single panel with magnetics and I/O (see example below). 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The magazine shall have a separate motor starter panel and enclosure. 
3.4.12.4 
Control cabling required to be removed from a terminal box for shipment shall be 
receptacle and plug type. 
PANEL CLOSED 
PANEL OPEN

3.4.12.5 
Permanent cables and mounting brackets for magazine control enclosures 
mounted on the tooling fence line shall be the responsibility of the Tier 1 
Integrator. 
3.4.12.6 
Cabling from magazine control enclosures to tool station panels is the 
responsibility of the Tier 1 Integrator. 
3.4.12.7 
Temporary cables and stands for control enclosures to be mounted on the tooling 
fence line necessary for Try-Out and Buy-Off at the magazine supplier shall be 
the responsibility of the magazine supplier. 
3.4.12.8 
Magazine control boxes shall be accessible from floor level or maintenance 
platform while the magazine is running. 
3.4.12.9 
Magazine valves shall be accessible from floor level or maintenance platform 
while the magazine is running. 
3.4.12.10 
Magazine controls drawings shall be provided. 
3.4.12.11 
Magazine logic integration guidelines shall be provided. 
3.4.12.12 
Magazine fault messages shall be provided. 
3.4.12.13 
A magazine stop shall be provided. The magazine stop shall utilize a safety cable 
to stop the magazine.  This shall stop the motor only. The magazine stop shall be 
“Control Reliable”. The safety cable must be able to be reached in the entire load 
area. The safety cable shall be aircraft type cable with orange plastic covering. At 
10'-0" intervals, a yellow 1.25” x 6” lamacoid tag with ¾” black letters bearing the 
words "Conveyor Stop", shall be attached to the conveyor stop cable. (Ref 70ZF-
20013205) (See Fig. 1). A separate cable shall be provided for each side of the 
magazine. The cable will be straight and cannot make any turn.  “Magazine Stop” 
pushbuttons may be used in lieu of safety cables with the approval of the Ford 
Motor Company VOGO Manufacturing Engineer, VIS and Controls Engineer.

3.4.12.14 
The magazine stop shall not interfere with any manual load operation. 
3.4.12.15 
All magazines shall be equipped with a run-bar. The Ford VOME Controls 
Engineer shall provide direction on Ford reference drawings, function and design 
requirement. 
 
3.4.13 
Qualification Fixtures 
3.4.13.1 
Qualification fixtures shall be provided for all conveyors. 
3.4.13.2 
Qualification fixture framework shall be constructed from lightweight aluminum. 
3.4.13.3 
Qualification fixtures shall mount securely to the conveyor framework at a stop 
location to qualify the pallet tooling. 
3.4.13.3.1 
The conveyor supplier shall provide mounts (datum’s) on the conveyor frame for 
the purpose of mounting the nest tooling qualification fixture. The conveyor 
supplier and Tier1 Integrator shall concur on the design of the mounts. The 
qualification fixture shall utilize pins, which will set into a 2-way and 4-way 
bushing on each side of the conveyor. Other locating methods are permitted with 
the Ford Motor Company VOGO Manufacturing Engineer and VIS/MDFU 
approval. 
3.4.13.4 
Qualification fixtures shall be designed to utilize standard NAAMS components. 
3.4.13.5 
Qualification fixtures shall provide 5mm gap checks to the pallet tooling. (See 
Fig.1)

3.4.14 
Buy-Off 
3.4.14.1 
Requirements for holding a buy-off at the magazine supplier shall be determined 
by the VO ME. The buy-off event should be with the ME, VIS, Tier1 Integrator, 
magazine supplier and plant (optional). This section provides guidelines for the 
buy-off event when it is required. 
3.4.14.2 
There shall be a buyoff event at the magazine manufacturer facility prior to 
shipment to the system integrator’s facility. 
3.4.14.3 
The magazine shall be connected to all air and electrical services required for 
functioning of the equipment. 
3.4.14.4 
Air pressure shall be set at a maximum 65 p.s.i. 
3.4.14.5 
Magazine controls shall be connected to a PLC loaded with the magazine logic 
and fault messages. 
3.4.14.6 
The magazine logic shall be modified to operate the magazine in an automatic 
repeat mode. 
3.4.14.7 
The automatic repeat mode shall simulate magazine function in the 
manufacturing environment of the final installation.  
3.4.14.8 
Function of the torque overload protection required by Section 3.4.5.9 above 
shall be demonstrated at the time of the buy-off.  
3.4.14.9 
The buy-off shall be the same as GVOSS Section 1.2.6 with the following 
exceptions: 
• 
Only Buy-Off Book Tabs, 3, 5-8, 15, 16, 18 and 21 apply.

• 
The buy-off event shall consist of a Buy-Off Book review and GVOSS 
Section 1.2.6.5 Phase I.

3.5 
ACCUMULATING PALLET CONVEYORS 
3.5.1 
General Communications 
3.5.1.1 
This section pertains to the Ford Motor Company requirements for accumulating 
pallet conveyors. 
3.5.1.2 
The intent of this section is to provide standards to deliver accumulating pallet 
conveyors as modular sub-systems to the Tier 1 Integrators. This includes both 
mechanical and controls. 
3.5.1.3 
Unless specified otherwise in this section, accumulating pallet conveyor 
requirements shall conform to the standards and specifications set forth in the 
Ford Motor Company GVOSS. 
NOTE: Special attention shall be paid to providing information, documentation 
and processes necessary to support Ford Motor Company Equipment R&M 
efforts and policies. 
3.5.1.4 
Deviations to this section shall be allowed only after a GVOSS "Action Request" 
form is submitted and approved. 
3.5.1.5 
The Ford Motor Company P&SD (Process and Strategy Development) 
Department shall verify the conveyor supplier has access to all applicable Ford 
Motor Company Standards. 
3.5.1.6 
The Ford Motor Company P&SD Department for each program shall provide the 
information necessary to quote the conveyors to meet the <PA> cost target. This 
includes, but is not limited to: 
• 
Part drawing (part number and description) 
• 
Part orientation on pallet 
• 
Part weight  
• 
Approximate center of mass 
• 
Process (where used in the manufacturing process) 
• 
Conveyor length 
• 
Conveyor width 
• 
Number of parts to be accumulated 
• 
Process cycle time 
• 
Conveyor style (floor, overhead or vertical) 
• 
Load and unload method (robot, automatic or manual) 
• 
Maintenance platform size (where applicable) 
3.5.1.7 
The tooling and conveyor suppliers shall have regular communications for the 
purpose of simultaneous engineering to exchange information relevant to the 
conveyor and pallet tooling "nest" design. The appropriate Ford Motor Company 
ME (Manufacturing Engineer) shall also be made aware of the meeting schedule. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 105 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.5.1.8 
The appropriate Ford Motor Company P&SD Department VIS (Vendor Interface 
Supervisor) for the given tooling OEM shall have the final say in resolving any 
points of contention between the conveyor supplier and the Tier1 Integrator. 
3.5.1.9 
Each installation shall be inspected at the final customer site for proper 
installation (PROTAG WHITE or equivalent build phase) before MP1. 
3.5.1.10 
When an accumulating conveyor is shared between two systems. (Robot load 
and Unload, Operator load Robot Unload) the receiving system Tier 1 purchases 
the conveyor. 
3.5.1.11 
When an accumulating conveyor is shared between two systems. (Robot load to 
operator unload) the load system tier 1 purchases the conveyor. 
3.5.2 
Tier1 Integrator Responsibilities 
3.5.2.1 
The Tier1 Integrator shall provide the conveyor supplier with design parameters 
necessary to design the conveyor. This shall include but is not limited to: 
• 
Tooling nest layout with significant dimensions. 
• 
Stop locations. 
• 
Locator scheme for tooling nest. 
3.5.2.2 
The Tier1 Integrator shall submit all requests for changes either mechanical, 
electrical or controls to the conveyor supplier for approval prior to requesting 
change approval from the Ford Motor Company Manufacturing Engineer. 
3.5.2.3 
The Tier1 Integrator shall maintain a log of all conveyor changes requested and 
approved. 
3.5.2.4 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for the integration of the conveyor 
wiring and logic into their system. The conveyor controls and logic shall function 
to the conveyor manufacturers design intent. 
3.5.2.5 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for the conveyor HMI screens and logic. 
3.5.2.6 
When a conveyor has a manual operator interface at the load or unload 
escapement, the Tier1 Integrator shall assist in the design of the tooling nest in 
accordance with all GVOSS ergonomic requirements. 
3.5.2.6.1 
Unload System Deliverables 
• Safety I/O module for Conveyor E-Stops, Gate switches and cable stops 
• Power supplies for conveyor I/O and lube system 
• Light curtains if required unload side. 
• Provide motor cable 
• Safety Input for Light curtain (If required)

3.5.2.7 
Conveyor Part Load and Unload 
3.5.2.7.1 
The Tier1 Integrator shall provide the part present sensing. 
3.5.2.7.2 
Part present sensing shall also provide error proofing at the load escapement to 
identify an improperly loaded part and prevent it from reaching the unload 
escapement. 
3.5.2.7.3 
The Tier1 Integrator shall provide mechanical units necessary to stabilize parts at 
the load and unload escapements. 
3.5.2.7.4 
Clamping directly to any feature of the part nest is prohibited. 
3.5.2.7.5 
Pallets hold down and hold down clamping, when required is allowed. See the 
example below. 
 
3.5.2.7.6 
When used, pallet hold down and hold down clamping design, construction and 
installation shall be the responsibility of the conveyor supplier. 
3.5.2.8 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for the proper shipment of the 
conveyors to the final receiving facility. 
3.5.2.9 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for the proper installation of the 
conveyors in the final receiving facility. 
3.5.2.10 
The Tier1 Integrator shall report any damage to the conveyor to the conveyor 
supplier. 
3.5.2.11 
After receiving the conveyor from the conveyor supplier, the Tier1 Integrator is 
responsible for any cost incurred for repairing damage to the conveyor.  
3.5.2.12 
Prior to any repairs being performed, the Tier1 Integrator shall agree with the 
conveyor supplier on the scope of work and who will complete the work. 
3.5.2.13 
After completing repairs to a damaged conveyor, the Tier1 Integrator shall buy-
off the acceptance of repairs.

3.5.2.14 
The Tier1 Integrator is to take the lead on any 8D's relating to the conveyor 
throughout the conveyor life cycle. 
3.5.3 
Conveyor Supplier Responsibilities 
3.5.3.1 
The conveyor supplier shall construct a critical path for each conveyor to monitor 
conformance to delivery timing. 
3.5.3.2 
Any deviations shall be submitted through the supplier and approved by Ford 
Motor Company through the Action Request process. 
3.5.3.3 
All change and deviation requests shall be submitted to Ford Motor Company 
through the Tier1 Integrator. 
3.5.3.4 
The conveyor supplier, upon receiving a change request from the Tier1 Integrator 
shall provide a definitive response within 5 days. 
3.5.3.5 
The conveyor supplier shall provide the Tier1 Integrator with the conveyor control 
design intent, drawings and logic function of the conveyor operation. This shall 
include the sequence of operation and all warning and fault sensors and 
conditions. 
3.5.3.6 
The conveyor supplier shall be responsible for "Try-Out" logic for Buy-Off at their 
facility. 
3.5.3.7

1) 
Motor 
2)

All the switches required for pallet stop operation (Pallet Present, 
downstream, full etc.)

valves.

The conveyor should include valve manifolds / Islands with I/O 
modules for the stops Load, Unload, Mid, Additional Mid Stops. 
a. 
Load pre-stop. 
b. 
Load Stop 
c. 
Mid stops if required. 
d. 
Unload Pre-stop.

3.5.3.8 
Part Nest Tooling 
3.5.3.8.1 
The Conveyor Supplier shall be responsible for the design, build and assemble 
the pallet "part nest" tooling. 
3.5.3.8.1.1 
Part nest tooling shall be made adjustable. 
3.5.3.8.2 
The pallet design shall clearly indicate areas where "part nest" tooling fasteners 
and dowels are prohibited.  
3.5.3.8.3 
Design parameters include but are not limited to: 
• 
Pallet design 
• 
Maximum part nest height, width and length 
• 
Part center of mass 
• 
Part orientation 
• 
Pallet weight 
• 
Pallet load capacity 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 110 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.5.3.8.4 
The conveyor supplier shall verify the center of mass of the pallet with tooling 
"nest" and part will function properly on the conveyor. 
3.5.3.9 
The conveyor supplier shall furnish the Tier1 Integrator with proper handling, 
shipping, installation, and operating instructions for the conveyor. 
• 
All lift points shall be clearly identified and labeled on the conveyor. 
• 
All break points shall be clearly identified, and match marked on the 
conveyor. 
• 
Rigging and lifting the conveyor by the head or tail shafts shall be prohibited. 
3.5.3.10 
The conveyor supplier shall buy-off the Tier1 Integrators' integration of the 
conveyor into their system prior to the Ford Motor Company buy-off of the 
equipment. This shall be documented and included in Tab 15 of the Tier1 
Integrator’s Buy-Off Book. 
3.5.3.11 
The conveyor supplier shall provide support for the conveyor unloading, rigging, 
and installation at the Ford Motor Company receiving plant. 
3.5.3.12 
The conveyor supplier shall buy-off the installation of their conveyor at the Ford 
Motor Company receiving plant prior to cycling the conveyor. 
3.5.3.13 
Upon receiving a report of damage to the conveyor from the Tier1 Integrator or 
Ford Motor Company receiving plant, the conveyor supplier shall assess the 
impact of the damage on the conveyor function and useful life. 
3.5.3.14 
Prior to any repairs being performed, the conveyor supplier shall submit a scope 
of work and who will complete the work to the Tier1 Integrator. 
3.5.3.15 
After completing repairs to a damaged conveyor, the conveyor supplier shall buy-
off the acceptance of repairs. 
3.5.3.16 
When a conveyor has a manual operator interface at the load or unload 
escapement, the Tier1 Integrator shall assist in the design of the tooling nest in 
accordance with all GVOSS ergonomic requirements. 
3.5.3.17 
When a conveyor has an automatic or robotic interface at the load or unload 
escapement, the Tier1 Integrator shall verify their end effector design interfaces 
properly with the pallet tooling "nest" design. 
3.5.4 
Mechanical 
3.5.4.1 
Guarding 
3.5.4.1.1 
The need and application for guarding shall be determined from a Risk 
Assessment. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 111 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.5.4.1.2 
All guarding shall be constructed from sheet metal, using expanded metal for 
visual inspection ports. The expanded metal opening size shall conform to 
OSHA's Code of Federal Regulations, Subpart O, 1910.217, Table O-10 for 
maximum permissible openings for guards. Any guarded point that needs to be 
accessed for problem diagnostic purposes, preventative maintenance activities 
and viewing the operation of the conveyor shall be viewable without requiring 
guard removal. (See Also GVOSS Section 1.4.10.) 
3.5.4.2 
The conveyor supplier shall supply a temporary structure, when necessary for 
try-out and buy-off at their facility and installation at the Tier1 Integrators' facility. 
The temporary structure shall allow the conveyor to function in a manner identical 
to the final installation in the assembly plant. The part load point and unload point 
shall be the same location and elevation as dictated by the process. The 
structure shall also provide for mounting of the maintenance platforms and the 
maintenance platforms shall be mounted for the try-out and buy-off.  
3.5.4.3 
All switch brackets shall be adjustable. 
3.5.4.4 
Head shaft and tail shaft diameter shall be the same. 
3.5.5 
Electromechanical Drives 
3.5.5.1 
Motor torque overload protection shall be provided. The torque overload 
protection shall protect the driving and driven system from damage in the event 
of a conveyor jam. 
 
3.5.6 
Pallets 
3.5.6.1 
Conveyors using pallet clutches shall use clutches that do not require adjustment 
and are non-adjustable. 
3.5.6.2 
Nut and bolt construction of brackets and "part nest" tooling shall be prohibited. 
3.5.6.3 
Pallets will be clearly marked with an identification number. 
3.5.6.4 
Each pallet shall be equipped with a “bad-pallet” detection system, including 
details on each pallet that can be manually set such that a proximity switch at the 
load station will detect the “bad pallet” and release it empty for later off-shift 
repair. 
3.5.6.5 
Failure of a conveyor transport chain shall not allow the pallet to fall from the 
conveyor. 
3.5.6.6 
When multiple pallets are used for a "part nest", the pallets must be mechanically 
linked. 
3.5.7 
Qualification Fixtures 
3.5.7.1 
Qualification fixtures shall be provided for all conveyors.

 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.5.7.2 
Qualification fixture framework shall be constructed from lightweight aluminum. 
3.5.7.3 
Qualification fixtures shall mount to the conveyor framework at a stop location to 
the qualify pallet tooling. 
3.5.7.3.1 
The conveyor supplier shall provide mounts (datum’s) on the conveyor frame for 
the purpose of mounting the nest tooling qualification fixture. The conveyor 
supplier and Tier1 Integrator shall concur on the design of the mounts. The 
qualification fixture shall utilize pins, which will set into a 2-way and 4-way 
bushing on each side of the conveyor. Other locating methods are permitted with 
the Ford Motor Company VOGO Manufacturing Engineer and VIS/MDFU 
approval.

Qualification fixtures shall be designed to utilize standard NAAMS components.

3.5.8 
Pallet Stops 
3.5.8.1 
Stops shall not be mounted in a manner that causes a pallet to stop at a point 
where the chain is engaged with the sprocket without the expressed written 
concurrence of the conveyor manufacturer and the Ford Motor Company VOGO 
VIS. 
3.5.8.2 
When loss of stop valve power results in the stop remaining in the "Open" 
position, allowing pallets to pass unchecked, metering stops shall be provided. 
The use of metering stops pertains to the use of detented valves to actuate the 
stops. When there is a loss of power to a detented valve it retains its last state. If 
that state is "Open" it will allow pallets to pass the stop unchecked. 
3.5.8.3 
The use of single solenoid valves is prohibited when the unexpected closing of 
the stop may cause damage to the pallet. 
NOTE: The use of metering stops may be omitted at the direction of the Ford 
Motor Company VO Controls Engineer when a control architecture defined by the 
VO Controls Engineer is better suited to the application. 
3.5.8.4 
A metering or hold-back stop shall be provided prior to the pre-load stop on the 
pallet return side of the conveyor to prevent multiple-feeding of pallets. The 
metering stop shall be located and adjusted so that it can close without damaging 
the pallet or conveyor when pallets are accumulated against the stops. See 
Figure 3.5.5.a. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
       Figure 3.5.5.a Pallet Stop Positions at Load Point

A
B
C
Delivery FLOW 
A – Metering Stop 
B – Preload Stop 
C – Load Stop

C

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.5.8.5 
A metering or hold-back stop shall be provided prior to the pre-unload stop on the 
pallet unload side of the conveyor to prevent influencing the pallet being 
unloaded due to the accumulation of pallets. The metering stop shall be located 
and adjusted so that it can close without damaging the pallet or conveyor when 
pallets are accumulated against the pre-unload stop. See Figure 3.5.5.b. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  Figure 3.5.5.b Pallet Stop Positions at Unload Point

Maintenance Platforms 
3.5.9.1 
Maintenance platforms and guarding shall conform to GVOSS Section 1.3. 
3.5.9.2 
For detailed info regarding maintenance platforms see GVOSS Section 3.4.7. 
3.5.10 
Falling Parts Guards 
This section applies to accumulating pallet conveyors utilizing floor mounted 
structures. See GVOSS Section 3.11 for Floor Mounted Structure requirements. 
Falling Parts Guards shall be provided for conveyor installations where 
necessary to prevent injury to employees and provide maintenance access in the 
event parts fall from carriers.

 Accumulating Pallet Conveyor with Falling Parts Guard 
3.5.10.1 
Falling Parts Guards shall have 42-inch-high side rails to protection against the 
fall hazard.  
3.5.10.2 
Side rails shall be painted Safety Yellow. 
3.5.10.3 
Falling Parts Guard openings where a person can fall 6 feet, or more are 
considered as fall hazards and shall be identified so as to alert employees to the 
need for appropriate protective measures. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 116 of 304

 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.5.10.4 
The hazard shall be identified in the basket guard by a 3-0' wide section of 
Mebac (3-0) or Slip-not Grade I floor material.  
3.5.10.5 
This flooring is to be installed 6 feet back from the edge of the Falling Parts 
Guard.  
3.5.10.6 
The length of the flooring will run from right side of the falling parts guard to the 
left side of the falling parts guard. 
3.5.10.7 
This flooring is to be painted with alternating diagonal (45°) stripes painted Safety 
Yellow and Safety Black as shown below. 
 
3.5.10.8 
The remaining 6 feet to the open end of the falling parts guarding shall be 
painted Safety Red 7.5R-4.0/14. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.5.10.9 
The hazard shall be identified at eye level with a 12 inch x 12 inch Danger sign, 
shown below, posted 6 feet from edge of falling parts guard. Multiple signs will be 
utilized where multiple approaches to the hazard are identified. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 117 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.5.10.10 
Access ladders to falling parts guarding will be so positioned as to have a 
minimum distance of 6 feet from point of egress to any openings in the guard 
floor.  
3.5.10.11 
When ladders are located next to the walls, there must be a minimum of 1-foot 
clearance between the back of the ladder and the face of the wall.  
3.5.10.12 
Falling Parts Guard Design, Fabrication and Installation  
3.5.10.12.1 When required falling parts guards shall be provided under all conveyors where 
the lowest portion of the conveyor or part carrying device is greater than 7'-0" 
from the plant floor or platform.  
3.5.10.12.2 The falling parts guard shall be 2-inch x 4-inch No. 2 gage welded wire mesh. 
3.5.10.12.3 The falling parts guard shall be painted Safety White N 9.5. 
3.5.10.12.4 All trimming of guard sections shall be done with wire cutters. The joining of 2 
sections of the falling parts guarding shall be done only by the using the guard 
manufacturer supplied spiral coils.   
3.5.10.12.5 The side sections (vertical sections) shall be assembled to the horizontal 
sections with the 8-gage steel spiral coil.  
3.5.10.12.6 The side sections shall be painted Safety White N 9.5. 
3.5.10.12.7 All unwired edges for the falling parts guard sections must land on the structural 
steel members and be welded to the structural steel. Welds shall be 3/16” fillet, 
2” long with 12” spacing. 
3.5.10.12.8 Falling parts guard sides shall be a minimum of 2'-0" high from the bottom of the 
guard, with running L2½ x 2½ x ¼ angles at 3'-6". The angles shall be painted 
Safety Yellow. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 118 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.5.10.12.9 The minimum allowable clear headroom dimension from the bottom of the guard 
supports to the finished floor shall be 6'-8" over work areas and 12’ over aisles 
unless otherwise specified or approved. 
 
3.5.10.12.10 The installation of the falling parts guards and its support steel shall be such that 
the guards shall be capable of supporting the maximum part load, plus the 
maximum carrier load, plus the impact load of the falling part and carrier, plus the 
load of two maintenance personnel.  
3.5.10.12.11 Guard panels shall not exceed 32 square feet without structural steel support 
members.  
3.5.10.12.12 Falling Parts Guards shall have a minimum or 24" clearance on both sides of the 
part, to provide a maintenance walkway and a minimum of 6" clearance under 
the load.

3.5.10.12.13 All control devices (pneumatic valve boards, etc.) in the Falling Parts Guard shall 
be located on the side of the guard with 24" minimum clearance. Control devices 
shall not be located in areas of potential pinch points due to part or carrier travel.  
3.5.10.12.14 Platforms with handrails shall be provided at ladder access points to the Falling 
Parts Guard so that there are no obstructions exiting the ladder to enter the 
guard. 
 
 
3.5.11 
Conveyor Chain Lubrication 
3.5.11.1 
Conveyors that require lubrication on the chain to function correctly shall have a 
Castrol LubeCon® or DigiLube central automatic lubrication system for the 
conveyor chain. Check with the Assembly or Stamping Plant for preference. 
When there is no plant preference then the lubrication system shall be 
LubeCon®. 
3.5.11.2 
The conveyor supplier shall be responsible for the proper unit selection, 
lubrication circuit design, component selection and function of the lubrication 
system. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 120 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.5.11.3 
Each conveyor shall have a 1A9 Lubrication Chart and Placard. See GVOSS 
Section 1.4.11.5. 
3.5.11.4 
The lubrication system reservoir shall be accessible for maintenance and clearly 
marked for the full and low lubricant levels to support Ford Motor Company visual 
factory objectives. 
3.5.12 
Alignment 
3.5.12.1 
All points used to establish conveyor "level" and "straight" shall be clearly 
identified. 
3.5.12.2 
Conveyors shall be aligned for proper function over their useful life. The conveyor 
supplier's installation procedures shall be followed. Their installation procedures 
shall include alignment procedures and requirements. Ford Motor Company 
guidelines for alignment are as follows: 
• 
Head shaft to tail shaft sprocket-to-sprocket axial alignment should be 
±1.0 mm (±0.039 inch) or less.  
• 
Head shaft and tail shaft parallel alignment shall be 1° or less.  
• 
For horizontal conveyors the head shaft and tail shaft shall be level to gravity. 
For vertical conveyors the head shaft and tail shaft shall be perpendicular to 
gravity.  
3.5.12.3 
For horizontal conveyors the head shaft and tail shaft shall be level to gravity. For 
vertical conveyors the head shaft and tail shaft shall be perpendicular to gravity. 
This shall be documented and included in the buy-off book. 
3.5.13 
Mounting 
3.5.13.1 
Truss mounting of conveyors is prohibited. 
3.5.13.2 
Each floor-mounting pad on the frame shall have leveling hardware.  
3.5.13.3 
Leveling hardware shall conform to GVOSS Section 1.1.23. 
3.5.13.4 
The conveyor supplier shall furnish all leveling and mounting hardware with the 
exception of anchors and anchor bolts. Anchors and anchor bolts shall be the 
responsibility of the Tier1 Integrator when installing the conveyor at their facility 
and the installation contractor when installing the conveyor at the Ford Motor 
Company receiving facility. 
3.5.14 
Controls 
3.5.14.1 
Controls component manufacturers used on conveyors shall be consistent with 
those specified by the “Approved Controls Components” document supplied by 
VO Controls. If there is no approved component for the application, VO Controls 
Engineer or VIS for the program shall identify approved component 
manufacturers. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 121 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.5.14.2 
The Ford Motor Company VO Controls Engineer for the program shall identify 
any applicable Standard Ford controls reference drawings to be used. 
Standard Reference Drawings for Legacy systems (non-Next Gen Controls 
architecture):  
70ZF-20010767: Pallet Conveyor – robot load and unload w/o walkway 
70ZF-20010768: Pallet Conveyor – robot load and unload w/ walkway  
70ZF-20010769: Pallet Conveyor – operator load and robot unload 
Controls Reference Drawings for Next Gen and GVOSS Controls architecture 
are stored in Team Center. 
3.5.14.3 
Unless specified otherwise by the Ford Motor Company VO Controls Engineer 
for the program, the following Controls Architecture shall be used: 
• 
Single panel with magnetics and I/O (as shown in Figure 3.5.6.3). 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure 3.5.6.3.  Accumulating Pallet Conveyor Panel 
3.5.14.4 
Control cabling required to be removed from a terminal box for shipment shall be 
receptacle and plug type. 
3.5.14.5 
Permanent cables and mounting brackets for conveyor control enclosures 
mounted on the tooling fence line shall be the responsibility of the Tier 1 
Integrator. 
3.5.14.6 
Cabling from conveyor control enclosures to tool station panels is the 
responsibility of the Tier 1 Integrator. 
3.5.14.7 
Temporary cables and stands for control enclosures to be mounted on the tooling 
fence line necessary for try-out and buy-off at the conveyor supplier shall be the 
responsibility of the conveyor supplier. 
3.5.14.8 
Conveyor control boxes shall be accessible from floor level or maintenance 
platform while the conveyor is running. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 122 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.5.14.9 
Conveyor valves shall be accessible from floor level or maintenance platform 
while the conveyor is running. 
3.5.14.10 
Conveyor controls drawings shall be provided. 
3.5.14.11 
Conveyor logic integration guidelines shall be provided. 
3.5.14.12 
Conveyor fault messages shall be provided. 
3.5.14.13 
A conveyor stop shall be provided. The conveyor stop shall utilize a safety cable 
to stop the conveyor.  This shall stop the motor only. The conveyor stop shall be 
"Control Reliable". The safety cable must be able to be reached in the entire load 
area. The safety cable shall be aircraft type cable with orange plastic covering. At 
10-foot intervals, a yellow 1.25” x 6” lamacoid tag with ¾” black letters bearing 
the words "Conveyor Stop", shall be attached to the conveyor stop cable. (Ref 
70ZF-20013205) (See Fig. 1). A separate cable shall be provided for each side of 
the conveyor. The cable will be straight and cannot make any turn. Conveyor 
pushbuttons may be used in lieu of safety cables with the approval of the Ford 
Motor Company VOGO Manufacturing Engineer, VIS and Senior Controls 
Engineer. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
              Fig. 1 
3.5.14.14 
The conveyor stop shall not interfere with any manual load operation. 
3.5.14.15 
The control system for the unload escapement will be locked out when the 
unload section of the tooling or equipment is locked out. Power shall be available 
to the conveyance motor and load escapement. 
3.5.14.16 
The controls system for the load escapement will be locked out when the load 
section of the tooling or equipment is locked out. Power shall be available to the 
conveyance motor and unload escapement. 
3.5.14.17 
For each pallet stop, there shall be a switch to indicate "pallet in stop". 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 123 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.5.14.18 
For each pallet stop, there shall be a switch after the stop to indicate "pallet clear 
of stop". 
3.5.14.19 
Pallets shall not be released from the entry stop at the load or unload 
escapement, until the pallet released from the load or unload escapement 
actuates the pallet clear of stop switch. 
3.5.14.20 
When the conveyor delivery or return side is full, no pallet shall rest over the 
"pallet clear of stop" switch. 
3.5.14.21 
All accumulating pallet conveyors that interface with an operator shall be 
equipped with a run-bar. The Ford VOME Controls Engineer shall provide 
direction on Ford reference drawings, function and design requirements. 
 
3.5.15 
Buy-Off 
3.5.15.1 
Requirements for holding a buy-off at the conveyor supplier shall be determined 
by the VO ME. The buy-off event should be with the ME, VIS, Tier1 Integrator, 
conveyor supplier and plant (optional). This section provides guidelines for the 
buy-off event, when it is required. 
3.5.15.2 
There shall be a buy-off event at the conveyor suppliers' facility prior to shipment 
to the Tier1 Integrator's system integration facility. 
3.5.15.3 
The conveyor shall be connected to all air and electrical services required for 
operation of the equipment. 
3.5.15.4 
Air pressure shall be set at maximum 65 psi. 
3.5.15.5 
Conveyor controls shall be connected to a PLC loaded with the conveyor logic 
and fault messages. 
3.5.15.6 
The conveyor logic shall be modified to operate the conveyor in an automatic 
repeat mode. 
3.5.15.7 
The automatic repeat mode shall simulate conveyor function in the 
manufacturing environment of the final installation. 
3.5.15.8 
Function of the torque overload protection required by Section 3.5.5.1 above 
shall be demonstrated at the time of the buy-off.  
3.5.15.9 
The buy-off shall be the same as GVOSS Section 1.2.6 with the following 
exceptions: 
• 
Only Buy-Off Book Tabs 3, 5-8, 15, 16, 18 and 21 apply. 
• 
The buy-off event shall consist of a Buy-Off Book review and GVOSS 
Section 1.2.6.5 Phase I. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 124 of 304
