 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.6 
ENCLOSED TRACK CONVEYORS 
3.6.1 
General 
3.6.1.1 
This section pertains to the Ford Motor Company requirements for enclosed track 
conveyors. 
3.6.1.2 
The intent of this section is to provide standards to deliver enclosed track 
conveyors as modular sub-systems to the Tier 1 Integrators. This includes both 
mechanical and controls. 
3.6.1.3 
Unless specified otherwise in this section, enclosed track conveyor requirements 
shall conform to the standards and specifications set forth in the Ford Motor 
Company GVOSS. 
NOTE: Special attention shall be paid to providing information, documentation 
and processes necessary to support Ford Motor Company Equipment R&M 
efforts and policies. 
3.6.1.4 
Deviations to this section shall be allowed only after a GVOSS "Standard 
Deviation Request" form is submitted and approved. 
3.6.1.5 
The Ford Motor Company P&SD (Process and Strategy Development) 
Department shall verify the conveyor supplier has access to all applicable Ford 
Motor Company Standards. 
3.6.1.6 
The Ford Motor Company P&SD Department for each Program shall provide the 
information necessary to quote the conveyors to meet the <PA> cost target. This 
includes, but is not limited to: 
• 
Part drawing (Part Number and Description) 
• 
Part orientation on hook 
• 
Part weight 
• 
Process (Where used in the manufacturing process) 
• 
Conveyor Length 
• 
Conveyor Width 
• 
Number of parts to be accumulated. 
• 
Process cycle time 
• 
Conveyor style (Floor, Overhead or Vertical). 
• 
Load and unload method (Robot, Automatic or Manual). 
• 
Maintenance platform size (where applicable) 
3.6.1.7 
The tooling and conveyor suppliers shall have regular communications for the 
purpose of simultaneous engineering to exchange information relevant to the 
conveyor and hook tooling "nest" design. The appropriate Ford Motor Company 
M.E. (Manufacturing Engineer) shall also be made aware of the meeting 
schedule.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 125 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.6.1.8 
The appropriate Ford Motor Company P&SD Department VIS (Vendor Interface 
Supervisor) for the given tooling OEM shall have the final say in resolving any 
points of contention between the conveyor supplier and the Tier1 Integrator. 
3.6.2 
Tier1 Integrator Responsibilities 
3.6.2.1 
The Tier1 Integrator shall provide the conveyor supplier with design parameters 
necessary to design the conveyor. This shall include, but is not limited to: 
• 
Tooling nest layout with significant dimensions. 
• 
Stop locations. 
• 
Locator scheme for tooling nest. 
3.6.2.2 
The Tier1 Integrator shall design, build and assemble the "part nest" tooling. The 
chain attachment design provided by the conveyor manufacture shall be used to 
identify areas where fastener and dowel holes are not allowed. 
3.6.2.3 
The Tier1 Integrator shall provide the conveyor supplier with a "hole" chart for the 
"part nest" tooling to be used. 
3.6.2.4 
The Tier1 Integrator shall ship the conveyor part nest tooling to the conveyor 
supplier for installation. The ship date must support the conveyor delivery date.  
3.6.2.5 
After receiving the conveyor, the Tier1 Integrator is responsible for re-qualifying 
the part nest tooling if necessary. When part nest tooling re-qualification is 
necessary, the Tier1 Integrator shall contact the conveyor supplier for the 
qualifying procedure. 
3.6.2.6 
The Tier1 Integrator shall provide the part present sensing and any mechanical 
units required to stabilize parts at the load and unload escapements. Part present 
sensing shall also provide error proofing at the load escapement to identify an 
improperly loaded part and prevent it from reaching the unload escapement. 
3.6.2.7 
When a conveyor has a manual operator interface at the load or unload 
escapement, the Tier1 Integrator shall design the tooling nest in accordance with 
all GVOSS ergonomic requirements. 
3.6.2.8 
When a conveyor has an automatic or robotic interface at the load or unload 
escapement, the Tier1 Integrator shall verify their end effector design interfaces 
properly with the pallet tooling nest design. 
3.6.2.9 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for the conveyor logic. 
3.6.2.10 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for the conveyor HMI screens and logic. 
3.6.2.11 
The conveyor supplier shall approve all changes either mechanical, electrical or 
controls requested by the Tier1 Integrator prior to requesting change approval 
from the Ford Motor Company Manufacturing Engineer. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 126 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.6.2.12 
The Tier1 Integrator shall maintain a log of all conveyor changes requested and 
approved. 
3.6.2.13 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for the proper shipment of the 
conveyors to the final receiving facility. 
3.6.2.14 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for the proper installation of the 
conveyors in the final receiving facility.  
3.6.2.15 
The Tier1 Integrator shall report any damage to the conveyor to the conveyor 
supplier. 
3.6.2.16 
After receiving the conveyor from the conveyor supplier, the Tier1 Integrator is 
responsible for any cost incurred for repairing damage to the conveyor.  
3.6.2.17 
Prior to any repairs being performed, the Tier1 Integrator shall agree to the scope 
of work and who will complete the work. 
3.6.2.18 
After completing repairs to a damaged conveyor, the Tier1 Integrator shall buy-
off the acceptance of repairs. 
3.6.2.19 
The Tier1 Integrator is to take the lead on any 8D's relating to the conveyor 
throughout the conveyor life cycle. 
3.6.3 
Conveyor Supplier Responsibilities 
3.6.3.1 
The conveyor manufacturer shall construct a critical path for each conveyor to 
monitor conformance to delivery timing. 
3.6.3.2 
To minimize industrial materials kept in inventory by the Ford Motor Company 
receiving plant, the conveyor supplier shall ensure that all mechanical and 
controls components are common (manufacturer, type and size) on all supplied 
conveyors.  
The conveyor supplier shall work with the receiving plant to communize on any 
components currently held by the plant in inventory. 
Any deviations shall be submitted through the supplier and approved by Ford 
Motor Company through the Action Request process. 
3.6.3.3 
The conveyor supplier shall provide the Tier1 Integrator with a tooling envelope 
and design parameters to use for hook tooling part nest design. Design 
parameters include but are not limited to: 
• 
Chain attachment design 
• 
Maximum part nest height 
• 
Maximum part nest width 
• 
Maximum part nest length 
• 
Part center of mass 
• 
Part orientation 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 127 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.6.3.4 
The conveyor supplier shall verify the center of mass of the hook with tooling 
nest and part will function properly on the conveyor. 
3.6.3.5 
The conveyor supplier shall perform a part nest and part chording study to verify 
there are no interferences along the hook path around the conveyor. The 
chording study shall include any movement of the chain and part nest caused by 
compliance or fastener fit tolerances in the system. 
3.6.3.6 
The conveyor supplier shall install the conveyor part nest tooling provided by the 
Tier1 Integrator. 
3.6.3.7 
The conveyor supplier shall furnish the Tier1 Integrator a "Notice of Qualification" 
to the Tier1 Integrator stating the part nest tooling has been qualified. 
3.6.3.8 
The conveyor supplier, upon receiving a change request from the Tier1 Integrator 
shall provide a definitive response within five days. 
3.6.3.9 
All change and deviation requests shall be submitted to Ford Motor Company 
through the Tier1 Integrator. 
3.6.3.10 
The conveyor supplier shall furnish the Tier1 Integrator with proper handling and 
installation instructions for the conveyor. 
3.6.3.11 
The conveyor supplier shall buy-off the Tier1 Integrators' installation and 
integration of the conveyor into their system prior to Ford Motor Company buy-off 
of the tooling. This shall be documented and included in Tab 15 of the Tier1 
Integrator’s Buy-Off Book. 
3.6.3.12 
The conveyor supplier shall provide support for the conveyor integration in the 
Ford Motor Company receiving plant.  
3.6.3.13 
The conveyor supplier shall buy off the installation of their conveyor at the Ford 
Motor Company receiving plant prior to cycling the conveyor. 
3.6.3.14 
Upon receiving a report of damage to the conveyor from the Tier1 Integrator or 
Ford Motor Company receiving plant, the conveyor supplier shall assess the 
impact of the damage on the conveyor function and useful life. 
3.6.3.15 
Prior to any repairs being performed, the conveyor supplier shall submit a scope 
of work and who will complete the work to the Tier1 Integrator. 
3.6.3.16 
After completing repairs to a damaged conveyor, the conveyor supplier shall buy-
off the acceptance of repairs. 
3.6.4 
Mechanical 
3.6.4.1 
General 
a. Relative conveyor components shall be designed for a maximum 1000# 
chain pull. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 128 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
b. Under normal operation the conveyor shall not exceed 600# chain pull.  
c. All switch brackets shall be adjustable. 
d. Nut and bolt construction of brackets and "part nest" tooling shall be 
prohibited. 
3.6.4.2 
Chain Drive 
a. Conveyor chain drive shall be of the caterpillar type. 
b. Caterpillar chain shall have fixed forged heat-treated caterpillar dogs. 
c. Caterpillar chain shall be reversible so opposite faces of dogs can be utilized. 
3.6.4.3 
Gear Reducer 
a. The drive gear reducer shall conform to the requirements of GVOSS 
Section 3.3.  
3.6.4.4 
Motor 
a. Electric motors shall conform to the requirements of GVOSS Section 3.3.  
b. Motors shall be sized according to duty-cycle to prevent overheating. 
3.6.4.5 
Overload Protection 
a. The drive shall utilize a torque limiter for overload protection. 
b. Detented torque limiters shall be equipped with a detection switch that shall 
shut-off the drive motor and display a fault in the HMI when an overload 
condition occurs. 
c. Friction type torque limiters shall be prohibited. 
d. The overload-reset procedure shall be posted at the drive. The procedure 
shall be printed on an oil resistant, flame-resistant material. 
3.6.4.6 
Enclosed Track 
a. Steps at welded joints shall not be allowed. All welded track joints shall be 
smooth and continuous. Welded joints shall have the same load-bearing 
capacity as any other section of the track. 
b. Pinched sections in tracks shall not be allowed. 
c. Track shall be supported to limit deflection to 1/8 inch. 
d. Curves shall be heat treated to extend track life. 
e. Track shall be sufficiently braced to prevent sway or lateral movement. 
f. 
At least one hinged inspection gate shall be provided. 
g. At least one chain installation gate shall be provided. The installation gate 
shall be at the exit end of the drive. 
h. Joints shall be designed so wheels maintain continuous contact with the 
bottom flange of the track 
i. 
Joints with an open gap shall be prohibited. 
j. 
Minimum horizontal turn radius shall be 24 inches. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 129 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.6.4.7 
Conveyor Chain 
a. Chain shall be clean and free from foreign material. 
b. The chain shall be lubricated in oil prior to installation. 
c. Chain shall have steel vertical and horizontal load bearing wheels. 
d. Load bearing wheels shall be machined and hardened. 
e. Load bearing wheels shall have open ball bearings with hardened balls and 
races. 
f. 
Chain shall be assembled according to manufacturer’s specifications. 
3.6.4.8 
Chain Take-Up 
a. Chain Take-Up shall be spring type. 
b. Take-Up units shall consist of a 180° horizontal turn, 24-inch radius attached 
to a moveable inner frame, a fixed outer frame with wheel tracks and wheel 
retainers and alignment guides to support and align the inner frame. 
c. The minimum take-up travel shall be eight inches. The effective travel of the 
take-up shall be sufficient to remove one complete job space plus a minimum 
of six inches of additional travel for assembly and disassembly of the 
conveyor chain. 
d. The moveable track on the take-up shall be adjusted so the moveable track 
extends the same distance from both outer sleeve ends. 
e. Both ends of the moveable track shall move freely and parallel during normal 
function. 
f. 
Once the conveyor chain is installed and the take-up properly adjusted, a 
lamacoid or metal tag shall be attached to the take-up, near the spring, 
indicating the spring compressed length and the number of work pitches on 
the installed chain. 
g. Access to conveyor chain take-up for tension adjustment shall not require 
guard removal. 
h. Conveyor chain take-up shall be accessible for adjustment tool use. 
3.6.4.9 
All guarding shall be constructed from sheet metal, using expanded metal for 
visual inspection ports. The expanded metal opening size shall conform to 
OSHA's Code of Federal Regulations, Subpart O, 1910.217, Table O-10 for 
maximum permissible openings for guards. Any guarded point that needs to be 
accessed for problem diagnostic purposes, preventative maintenance activities 
and viewing the operation of the conveyor shall be viewable without requiring 
guard removal. (See also GVOSS Section 1.4.10.1.) 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 130 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.6.4.10 
The conveyor supplier shall supply a temporary structure, when necessary for 
try-out and buy-off at their facility and installation at the Tier1 Integrators facility. 
The temporary structure shall allow the conveyor to function in a manner identical 
to the final installation in the assembly plant. The part load and unload points 
shall be the same location and elevation as dictated by the process. The 
structure shall also provide for mounting of the maintenance platforms and the 
maintenance platforms shall be mounted for the try-out and buy-off. 
3.6.4.11 
When required, Falling Parts Guarding shall be provided. GVOSS Section 9.4 
Safety Requirements for Conveyors, Subsection 9.4.7 - Falling Parts Guard shall 
be used to define the requirement for and construction of Falling Parts Guarding. 
3.6.4.12 
Conveyors shall have an automatic lubrication system for the conveyor chain. 
The lubrication system reservoir shall be accessible for maintenance and clearly 
marked for the full and low lubricant levels to support Ford Motor Company visual 
factory objectives. 
3.6.4.13 
Maintenance platforms shall be provided for drive maintenance. The platform 
shall extend to the chain installation gate. 
3.6.4.14 
All points used to establish conveyor "level" and "straight" shall be clearly 
identified. 
3.6.4.15 
Each floor-mounting pad on the conveyor-mounting frame shall have leveling 
hardware. 
3.6.4.16 
The conveyor supplier shall furnish all leveling and mounting hardware with the 
exception of anchors and anchor bolts. Anchors and anchor bolts shall be the 
responsibility of the Tier1 Integrator when installing the conveyor at their facility 
and the installation contractor when installing the conveyor at the Ford Motor 
Company receiving facility. 
3.6.5 
Power and Free 
3.6.5.1 
“Ski Bar” style stop switches shall be prohibited. 
3.6.5.2 
Mechanically actuated switches for "Carrier Clear of Stop" and other similar 
functions shall be prohibited. 
3.6.5.3 
Carrier position sensing shall use proximity switches only. 
3.6.5.4 
There shall be separate switches to identify "Carrier at Stop" and "Carrier Clear 
of Stop". 
3.6.5.5 
Stops shall close on the "Carrier Clear of Stop" signal and not on the loss of the 
"Carrier at Stop" signal. 
3.6.5.6 
The use of logic timers for stop function is prohibited. 
3.6.5.7 
The use of inclines and declines are prohibited. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 131 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.6.5.8 
The use of track switches is prohibited. 
3.6.5.9 
Chain length shall not exceed 200'. 
3.6.6 
Controls 
3.6.6.1 
Control component manufacturers used on conveyors shall be consistent with 
those specified by the “Approved Controls Components” document supplied by 
VO Controls. If there is no approved component for the application, VO Controls 
Engineer or VIS for the program shall identify approved component 
manufacturers. 
3.6.6.2 
The Ford Motor Company VO Controls Engineer for the program shall identify 
any applicable standard Ford controls drawings to be used. 
3.6.6.3 
Unless specified otherwise by the Ford Motor Company VO Controls Engineer 
for the program, the following controls architecture shall be used:  for an 
enclosed track indexing chain conveyor with manual load and automated unload: 
• 
Junction box with remote I/O 
• 
Separate magnetic panel 
• 
Junction boxes and magnetic panel will be wired into the tool station PLC. 
3.6.6.4 
The conveyor shall have a separate motor starter panel and enclosure. 
3.6.6.5 
Control cabling required to be removed from a terminal box for shipment shall be 
receptacle and plug type. 
3.6.6.6 
Permanent cables and mounting brackets for conveyor control enclosures 
mounted on the tooling fence line shall be the responsibility of the Tier 1 
Integrator. 
3.6.6.7 
Cabling from conveyor control enclosures to tool station panels is the 
responsibility of the Tier 1 Integrator. 
3.6.6.8 
Temporary cables and stands for control enclosures to be mounted on the tooling 
fence line necessary for try-out and buy-off at the conveyor supplier shall be the 
responsibility of the conveyor supplier. 
3.6.6.9 
Conveyor control boxes shall be accessible from floor level while the conveyor is 
running. 
3.6.6.10 
Conveyor controls drawings shall be provided. 
3.6.6.11 
Conveyor logic integration guidelines shall be provided. 
3.6.6.12 
Conveyor fault messages shall be provided. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 132 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.6.6.13 
A conveyor stop shall be provided. The conveyor stop shall utilize a safety cable 
to stop the conveyor.  This shall stop the motor only. The conveyor stop shall be 
"Control Reliable". The safety cable must be able to be reached in the entire load 
area. The safety cable shall be aircraft type cable with orange plastic covering. At 
10-foot intervals, a yellow 1.25” x 6” lamacoid tag with ¾” black letters bearing 
the words "conveyor stop", shall be attached to the conveyor stop cable. (Ref 
70ZF-20013205) (see Fig. 1). A separate cable shall be provided for each side of 
the conveyor. The cable will be straight and cannot make any turn. Conveyor 
pushbuttons may be used in lieu of safety cables with the approval of the Ford 
Motor Company VOGO Manufacturing Engineer, VIS and Senior Controls 
Engineer. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
   
 
Fig. 1 
3.6.6.14 
The conveyor stop shall not interfere with any manual load operation. 
3.6.7 
Buy-Off 
3.6.7.1 
Requirements for holding a buy-off at the conveyor supplier shall be determined 
by the VO ME. The buy-off event should be with the ME, VIS, Tier1 Integrator, 
conveyor supplier and plant (optional). This section provides guidelines for the 
buy-off event, when required. 
3.6.7.2 
There shall be a buy-off event at the conveyor supplier’s facility prior to shipment 
to the Tier1 Integrators system integration facility. 
3.6.7.3 
The conveyor shall be connected to all air and electrical services required to 
operate the tooling. 
3.6.7.4 
Air pressure shall be set at maximum 65 p.s.i. 
3.6.7.5 
Conveyor controls shall be connected to a PLC loaded with the conveyor logic 
and fault messages. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 133 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.6.7.6 
The conveyor logic shall be modified to operate the conveyor in an automatic 
repeat mode. 
3.6.7.7 
The automatic repeat mode shall simulate conveyor function in the 
manufacturing environment of the final installation.  
3.6.7.8 
Function of the torque overload protection required by section 3.6.4.5 above shall 
be demonstrated at the time of the buy-off.  
3.6.7.9 
The buy-off shall be the same as GVOSS Section 1.2.6 with the following 
exceptions: 
• 
Only Buy-Off Book Tabs 3, 5-8, 15, 16, 18 and 21 apply. 
• 
The buy-off event shall consist of a Buy-Off Book review and GVOSS 
Section 1.2.6.5 Phase I.

3.7 
BELT CONVEYORS 
This section pertains to belt conveyors used in stamping, stamping assembly and 
assembly applications by Ford Motor Company.  
Belt conveyors not covered in this section shall be addressed on a case-by-case 
basis by Ford Motor Company and the conveyor supplier. 
3.7.1 
General Communications 
3.7.1.1 
The intent of this section is to provide standards to deliver belt conveyors as 
modular sub-systems to the Tier 1 Integrators. This includes both mechanical 
and controls. 
3.7.1.2 
Unless specified otherwise in this section, belt conveyors requirements shall 
conform to the standards and specifications set forth in the Ford Motor Company 
GVOSS. 
NOTE: Special attention shall be paid to providing information, documentation 
and processes necessary to support Ford Motor Company Equipment R&M 
efforts and policies. 
3.7.1.3 
Deviations to this section shall be allowed only after a GVOSS "Action Request" 
form for a deviation is submitted and approved. 
Action Requests shall follow the normal process channels. 
SBU Action Requests shall be submitted to the SBU Process Engineer for the 
project. 
3.7.1.4 
The Ford Motor Company P&SD (Process and Strategy Development) 
Department or SBU Process Engineer shall verify the conveyor supplier has 
access to all applicable Ford Motor Company Standards. 
3.7.1.5 
The Ford Motor Company P&SD Department or SBU Process Engineer for each 
program shall provide the information necessary to quote the belt conveyors to 
meet the <PA> cost target. This includes, but is not limited to: 
• 
Part drawing (part number and description) 
• 
Part orientation on conveyor 
• 
Part weight and approximate center of mass 
• 
Process (Where used in the manufacturing process) 
• 
Conveyor length 
• 
Conveyor width 
• 
Conveyor capacity 
• 
Number of parts 
• 
Load and unload method (robot, automatic or manual) 
• 
Process cycle time 
• 
Maintenance platform size (where applicable) 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 135 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.1.6 
The supplier and belt conveyor suppliers shall have regular communications for 
the purpose of simultaneous engineering to exchange information relevant to the 
belt conveyor design. The appropriate Ford Motor Company ME (Manufacturing 
Engineer) or SBU Process Engineer shall also be made aware of the meeting 
schedule.  
3.7.1.7 
The appropriate Ford Motor Company, VIS (Vendor Interface Supervisor) or SBU 
Process Engineer for the given tooling OEM shall have the final say in resolving 
any points of contention between the belt conveyor supplier and the supplier. 
3.7.1.8 
For narrow belt conveyors, the use of the SEW KA-30 drives are approved. 
3.7.2 
Supplier Responsibilities 
As used in this standard the term "Supplier" is defined as follows: 
The "Supplier" is the Tier 1 Integrator. 
SBU the "Supplier" is the Primary Equipment Supplier. 
3.7.2.1 
The supplier shall provide the belt conveyor supplier with design parameters 
necessary to design the belt conveyor. This shall include but is not limited to: 
• 
Tooling or stamping press layout with significant dimensions. 
• 
Stop locations. 
• 
Locator scheme for tooling nest. 
• 
Acceptable positional repeatability. 
3.7.2.2 
The supplier shall provide the part present sensing and any mechanical units 
required to stabilize parts at the load and unload escapements. Part-present 
sensing shall also provide error-proofing at the load escapement to identify an 
improperly loaded part and prevent it from reaching the unload escapement. 
3.7.2.3 
The belt conveyor supplier is responsible for the positional repeatability of the 
belt conveyor. The Ford Motor Company ME, VIS or SBU Process Engineer, 
supplier and belt conveyor supplier shall concur on the acceptable positional 
variation.  
3.7.2.4 
When a belt conveyor has an automatic or robotic interface at the load or unload 
escapement, the supplier shall verify their end effector design interfaces properly 
with the conveyor. 
3.7.2.5 
The supplier shall submit all requests for changes either mechanical, electrical or 
controls to the belt conveyor supplier for approval prior to requesting change 
approval from the Ford Motor Company ME or SBU Process Engineer. 
3.7.2.6 
The supplier shall maintain a log of all belt conveyor changes requested and 
approved. 
3.7.2.7 
The supplier shall be responsible for the proper shipment of the belt conveyors to 
the final receiving facility. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 136 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.2.8 
The supplier shall be responsible for the proper installation of the belt conveyors 
in the final receiving facility. 
3.7.2.9 
The supplier shall report any damage to the belt conveyor to the belt conveyor 
supplier. 
3.7.2.10 
After receiving the belt conveyor from the belt conveyor supplier, the supplier is 
responsible for any cost incurred for repairing damage to the belt conveyor.  
3.7.2.11 
Prior to any repairs being performed, the supplier shall agree to the scope of 
work and who will complete the work. 
3.7.2.12 
After completing repairs to a damaged belt conveyor, the supplier shall buy-off 
the acceptance of repairs.  
3.7.2.13 
The supplier is to take the lead on any 8D's relating to the belt conveyor 
throughout the belt conveyor life cycle. 
3.7.3 
Belt Conveyor Supplier Responsibilities 
3.7.3.1 
The belt conveyor supplier shall construct a critical path for each belt conveyor to 
monitor conformance to delivery timing. 
3.7.3.2 
To minimize industrial materials kept in inventory by the Ford Motor Company 
receiving plant, the belt conveyor supplier shall ensure that all mechanical and 
controls components are common (manufacturer, type and size) on all supplied 
belt conveyors.  
The belt conveyor supplier shall work with the receiving plant to communize on 
any components currently held by the plant in inventory. 
Any deviations shall be submitted through the supplier and approved by Ford 
Motor Company through the Action Request process. 
3.7.3.3 
The belt conveyor supplier shall provide the supplier with a part envelope and 
design parameters to use for belt conveyor design. Design parameters include 
but are not limited to: 
• 
Maximum part height 
• 
Maximum part width 
• 
Maximum part length 
• 
Part center of mass 
• 
Part orientation 
3.7.3.4 
The belt conveyor supplier, upon receiving a change request from the supplier 
shall provide a definitive response within 5 days. 
3.7.3.5 
All change and deviation requests shall be submitted to Ford Motor Company 
through the supplier. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 137 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.3.6 
The belt conveyor supplier shall furnish the supplier with proper handling and 
installation instructions for the belt conveyor. 
3.7.3.7 
The belt conveyor supplier shall buy-off the suppliers' integration of the belt 
conveyor into their system prior to Ford Motor Company buy-off of the 
equipment. This shall be documented and included in Tab 15 of the equipment 
suppliers' buy-off book. 
3.7.3.8 
The belt conveyor supplier shall provide support for the belt conveyor installation 
in the Ford Motor Company receiving plant. 
3.7.3.9 
The belt conveyor supplier shall buy off the installation of their belt conveyor at 
the Ford Motor Company receiving plant prior to cycling the belt conveyor. 
3.7.3.10 
Upon receiving a report of damage to the belt conveyor from the supplier or Ford 
Motor Company receiving plant, the belt conveyor supplier shall assess the 
impact of the damage on the belt conveyor function and useful life. 
3.7.3.11 
Prior to any repairs being performed, the belt conveyor supplier shall submit a 
scope of work and who will complete the work to the supplier. 
3.7.3.12 
After completing repairs to a damaged belt conveyor, the belt conveyor supplier 
shall buy-off the acceptance of repairs. 
3.7.4 
Power Transmission 
3.7.4.1 
Unless otherwise specified in this standard, all belt conveyors shall be uni-
directional pulling conveyors. 
 
3.7.4.2 
Shaft material for conveyors used in stamping press lines shall be FLEXOR® 
Steel. 
3.7.4.3 
Head shaft and tail shaft diameter shall be the same. 
3.7.4.4 
All shafting shall be engineered to the application. 
3.7.4.5 
While the preference is to use pulleys with straight-through shafts, provision must 
be made for turn-downs at the bearing journals. In the case of turn-downs, the 
maximum allowable reduction in diameter shall be restricted to 85% of the major 
(base) shaft diameter, rounded up to the next nearest bearing diameter. The 
shoulder at the turn-down shall have a fillet radius. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 138 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.4.6 
Shaft bearings shall be sealed double row, self-aligning spherical roller. 
3.7.4.7 
Pillow block bearing housings shall be split type. 
3.7.4.8 
Head shaft and tail shaft bearings shall be the same. 
3.7.4.9 
Two-piece shaft clamp collars shall be used where applicable. 
3.7.4.10 
Electric motors shall conform to the GVOSS Section 3.3.  
3.7.4.11 
Drive gearbox shall conform to the GVOSS Section 3.3. 
3.7.4.12 
Approved gear motor manufacturers are: 
• 
Nord 
• 
SEW 
• 
Sumitomo 
3.7.4.13 
Approved drum motor manufacturers are: 
• 
Van Der Graaf 
3.7.4.14 
Cycling rates of 89 JPH and under shall use brake motors where positional 
stopping is required. 
3.7.4.15 
Cycling rates over 90 JPH shall use a C-Face mounted Posidyne Clutch Brake 
where positional stopping is required. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005

Revision Date: June 08, 2025

3.7.4.16 
Power transmission roller chain shall conform to ANSI B29.1 or DIN 8187. 
3.7.4.17 
Roller chain shall be manufactured by an approved Ford Motor Company source. 
Approved sources are: 
• 
Rex 
• 
Morse 
• 
Link Belt 
• 
Union chain (Division of U.S. Tsubaki) 
• 
Browning 
NOTE:  An approved source is a manufacturer or distributor with a valid, active 
Ford Motor Company Supplier code. This is necessary for the plant to purchase 
replacement chain direct. 
3.7.4.18 
The use of offset links in chain is prohibited. 
3.7.4.19 
"A" hub sprocket shall be prohibited. 
3.7.4.20 
Power transmission synchronous belts and sheaves shall be: 
• 
Gates Power Grip GT2 
• 
Gates Poly Chain GT2 
• 
Goodyear Eagle Pd 
3.7.4.21 
Friction type torque limiters shall be prohibited. 
3.7.5 
Take-Up 
3.7.5.1 
Take-up shall be adjustable type. 
3.7.5.2 
Take up shall be adjusted to a maximum of 25% of total travel for a new belt, 
leaving 75% for belt elongation adjustment. 
3.7.5.3 
A SPL (Single Point Lesson) specifying the correct setting and adjustment 
procedure for the take-up shall be laminated and attached next to the take-up. 
3.7.5.4 
Once the belt take-up is adjusted, it shall be match marked to the pulley shaft 
centerline to identify the original setting. 
3.7.5.5 
Access to take-up for tension or alignment adjustment shall not require guard 
removal. 
3.7.5.6 
Take-up shall be accessible for adjustment tool use. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 140 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.5.7 
Take-Ups shall allow for the roller to be removed without dismounting the take-up 
bearings from the shaft. (See Example below) 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.7.5.8 
Gravity take-ups shall use gravity rollers (weighted take-up). 
 
3.7.5.9 
The gravity roller and guide mechanism shall use linear guides. 
3.7.5.10 
The guide mechanism shall not affect pulley or gravity roller alignment when 
changing positions. 
3.7.6 
Lubrication 
3.7.6.1 
Belt conveyors shall provide access to all lubrication points on bearings. The 
lubrication points shall be clearly marked to support Ford Motor Company visual 
factory objectives.

SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

3.7.7 
Guarding and Platforms 
3.7.7.1 
Maintenance platforms and guarding shall conform to GVOSS Section 1.3 and 
1.4. 
3.7.7.2 
The space between the frame, belt and pulley at the operator take-off ends shall 
be guarded as shown in the examples below. The guard shall extend 12 inches 
beyond the pulley. 
 
 
 
 
3.7.7.3 
Maintenance platforms shall be provided on conveyors where maintenance 
access requires personnel to climb over 6 feet from the floor. The maintenance 
platform should provide access to the drive, head shaft, tail shaft. When the 
conveyor width is greater than 42 inches maintenance platforms shall be 
provided on both sides of the conveyor. 
3.7.7.4 
The belt conveyor supplier shall supply a temporary structure, when necessary, 
for try-out and buy-off at their facility and installation at the supplier's facility. The 
temporary structure shall allow the belt conveyor to function in a manner identical 
to the final installation in the assembly plant. The part load points and unload 
points shall be the same location and elevation as dictated by the process. The 
structure shall also provide for mounting of the maintenance platforms and the 
maintenance platforms shall be mounted for the try-out and buy-off. 
3.7.8 
Pulleys 
3.7.8.1 
Pulleys for conveyors used in stamping press lines shall be constructed from 
Schedule 40 pipe - minimum. 
3.7.8.2 
Pulley outside diameter shall be determined by the minimum bend radius of the 
belt as provided from the belt manufacturer. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 142 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.8.3 
Driving pulleys shall be covered with a suitable lagging material such as 
Butadiene Acrylonitrile, commonly referred to as Buna N, Nitrile and NBR. 
3.7.8.4 
Pulley outside diameter shall be machined to a uniform diameter, concentric to 
the shaft with 0.040-inch T.I.R. over length. 
3.7.8.5 
Crowned pulleys shall be prohibited. 
3.7.8.6 
Pulley end disk edges shall be chamfered prior to welding into the pulley. 
3.7.8.7 
Pulley end disk shall be fully welded around their perimeter. 
3.7.8.8 
Pulley end disks shall be recessed 1-5/8 inch from the edge of the pulley end. 
3.7.8.9 
Pulleys shall be attached to shafting using keyway QD bushings. 
3.7.8.10 
Pulleys shall use weld-on QD hubs, welded to the end disk. 
3.7.8.11 
QD hubs shall be mounted and welded concentric to the pulley outside diameter 
+/- 0.13mm (+/-0.005 inch) and square +/- 5 minutes of arc to the pulley axis. 
(See example below.) 
 
3.7.8.12 
Pulleys 8 inch or greater in diameter, running at 100 rpm or greater shall be 
dynamically balanced. 
3.7.9 
V-Groove Pulleys 
The following additional requirements shall apply when using V-Groove Pulleys. 
(See example below.) 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 143 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.9.1 
The V-Groove shall be in the center of the pulley. 
3.7.9.2 
V-Groove pulleys shall be 3-piece construction, consisting of 2 pulley roller 
assemblies joined together with a V-Groove billet in the center. 
3.7.9.3 
The V-Groove pulley diameter shall be specified by the belt manufacturer. 
3.7.9.4 
The V-Groove size shall be specified by the belt manufacturer. 
3.7.9.5 
There shall be no sharp edges on the V-Groove. 
3.7.9.6 
The bottom of the V-Groove shall have a radius to prevent stress risers in the V-
pulley assembly. 
3.7.9.7 
The V-Groove billet shall have a clearance bore machined into it that does not 
contact the pulley shaft. 
3.7.9.8 
The V-Groove billet shall extend into the pulley I.D. a sufficient distance to add 
structural integrity to the joint. (See example below.) 
 
3.7.9.9 
The V-Groove billet shall be press-fit (interference-fit) into the pulley prior to 
welding. 
3.7.9.10 
The end of the pulley and the V-Groove billet shall be chamfered, and full seam 
welded. Butt welding of this joint is prohibited. 
3.7.9.11 
Welds shall be ground smooth to the pulley surface. 
3.7.10 
Return Rollers 
3.7.10.1 
Return shoe supports are prohibited. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 144 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.10.2 
Return rollers shall be used to form catenary pockets for control of return belt 
tension. 
 
3.7.10.3 
Conveyors under 6 feet shall not require return rollers unless otherwise specified 
by the belt manufacturer. 
 
3.7.10.4 
Conveyors longer than 6 feet require return way belt supports consisting of 
rollers. Each catenary pocket needs to be of random size to prevent oscillating 
motion of the unsupported belt. The appropriate amount of catenary sag is 
determined by observing the catenary pocket between the first and second return 
rollers after the drive shaft with the conveyor at operating speed.  
 
3.7.10.5 
Catenary pockets shall be directly after the first return roller. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 145 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.10.6 
The belt manufacturer shall concur with the spacing of the first return roller 
location and size of the catenary pocket. 
3.7.10.7 
The belt manufacturer shall concur with the number and spacing of the return 
rollers used to control catenary sag. 
3.7.10.8 
Return rollers shall have sleeve lagging where return roller wear is identified as a 
concern. 
3.7.11 
Pulley and Return Roller Alignment 
3.7.11.1 
Pulley and return rollers shall be level to gravity. 
3.7.11.2 
Pulley and return rollers shall be set square to each other prior to belt installation. 
Parallel shafts are essential to trouble-free, straight conveying. Proper shaft 
alignment is confirmed by ensuring equal values for diagonals "b". 
 
3.7.11.3 
All pulley and return rollers shall be cleaned of all debris and residue prior to belt 
installation. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 146 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.12 
Belts - Continuous 
3.7.12.1 
Belts shall be checked for camber prior to lacing. The following procedure shall 
be used to determine camber: 
 
Camber is measured by drawing a taut line along one edge of the belt (inward 
camber edge) and measuring the maximum deviation from the center point of the 
line to the belt. The percentage of camber is calculated using the following 
formula:  
% camber = Maximum Deviation (inches) x 100  
Length of Taut Line (inches)  
If the percent camber exceeds ½% on a polyester warp belt construction, or 1% 
on a nylon warp belt construction, the belt is not acceptable as the camber 
cannot be easily compensated for using the pulley adjustments. 
3.7.12.2 
Belt Material 
Belt material shall be selected according to application. Special attention shall be 
made to select a material which provides maximum durability and life in a sheet 
metal transport environment. 
3.7.12.2.1 
All Belts shall be Ruff Top style unless otherwise specified. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 147 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.12.2.2 
Severe incline conveyors should use “high coefficient of friction” conveyor 
belting.  These would include 2-ply 150# tan (non-marking) wedge-grip or 3-ply 
135# 70 durometer brown carboxilated nitrile ruff-top belting.   
3.7.12.2.3 
Horizontal conveyors should use either yellow UVX220 urethane or red 90 
durometer carboxilated nitrile ruff-top belt.  Both belts are very good choices for 
abrasion-resistance in sheet metal conveyors. 
3.7.12.2.4 
Telescoping conveyors should consider belting that is both flexible and cut-
resistant.  Flexibility is very important for “back bending” with V-guides attached.  
A good choice for this application would be CB320, a 3-ply 150# belt 
3.7.12.3 
Belt ends shall be squared prior to lacing. 
3.7.12.4 
Belt Lacing 
3.7.12.4.1 
Belts shall use Flexco Alligator® Ready Set™ staple fastening system for 
conveyor belt lacing. 
                        
 
3.7.12.4.2 
All belt applications shall be reviewed for proper lacing selection. 
3.7.12.4.3 
All lacing shall be sized according to belt thickness to ensure proper crimping 
through the belt for additional strength. 
3.7.12.4.4 
Where applicable, metal lacings shall be recessed or “hidden” in the fabric of the 
belt to ensure non-scratching of outer skins. 
3.7.12.4.5 
Belts shall use stainless steel lacing in applications requiring stainless steel or 
aluminum carry way support beds. 
3.7.12.5 
Belts should be tensioned so that the return side catenary sag is less than 2% 
unless otherwise specified by the belt manufacturer. 
3.7.12.6 
The belt manufacturer shall provide requirements for re-tensioning the belts after 
the run-in period. 
3.7.12.7 
All tracking adjustments shall be made using the tail pulley shaft. If achieving 
proper tracking requires head pulley shaft adjustment, the Ford Motor Company 
ME or SBU Process Engineer shall be notified for approval. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 148 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.13 
Belts – Modular Plastic 
When modular plastic belting is specified, the following shall apply. 
3.7.13.1 
Square shafting is preferred for modular plastic belts. 
 
3.7.13.2 
The use of round shafting is allowed upon the submission and approval of an 
Action Request Form and treated as a deviation. 
 
3.7.13.3 
When mounting sprockets on a round shaft, one continuous key across the 
length of the shaft shall be used.  
3.7.13.4 
The sprocket manufacturers mounting specifications shall be followed. 
3.7.13.5 
The center sprocket shall be locked in place to control belt tracking. 
3.7.13.6 
Special belt tracking sprockets shall be used when specified by the belt 
manufacturer. 
3.7.13.7 
Center sprocket locking method shall be approved by the sprocket manufacturer. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 149 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.13.8 
All other sprockets shall be allowed to float to accommodate belt variation. 
3.7.13.9 
Only square bore sprockets whose number of teeth when divided by 4 results in 
a whole number (e.g. 36 ÷ 4 = 9) are permitted. These sprockets are 
automatically timed and can be mounted onto the shaft in any position.  
3.7.13.10 
Wide, heavily loaded belts requiring a center bearing support at both the drive 
and idler ends to limit shaft deflection to a maximum of 0.125 inch (3mm) shall 
use split shafts coupled together with a keyed, double disc coupling. 
3.7.13.11 
Split shafts shall be laser aligned to within the coupling manufacturers' 
installation specifications. 
3.7.13.12 
Sprocket 
spacing 
shall 
follow 
the 
belt 
and 
sprocket 
manufacturers' 
recommendations and specifications. 
3.7.13.13 
When specified by the manufacturer, drive shaft sprocket positioning to the 
plastic portion of the belt hinge and exposed hinge rod shall be followed. 
3.7.13.14 
When specified by the manufacturer, the idling tail and any intermediate shaft 
sprockets, the sprockets positioning to the plastic portion of the belt hinge and 
exposed hinge rod shall be followed. 
3.7.13.15 
The manufacturer's recommendation for sprocket location to the edge of the belt 
shall be followed. 
3.7.13.16 
Sprocket diameter shall be as large as possible for the application to reduce 
chordal action. 
3.7.13.17 
All carry way supports are to have a slight downward radius leading edge. 
3.7.13.18 
The preferred carry way positioning shall prevent the conveyor belt from dropping 
below the horizontal plane of the carry way as it enters the drive or exits the tail 
sprockets as shown below. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 150 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.13.19 
Wear strips, when specified, shall be lubricated polymer. 
3.7.13.20 
Wear strips shall be in a chevron pattern and spaced according to belt 
manufacturer recommendations. 
 
3.7.13.21 
The expansion and contraction of wear strips needs to be accommodated in the 
support configuration to prevent the wear strip from buckling or expanding into 
sprockets, frame, etc. 
3.7.13.22 
In the event of expansion, the wear strip shall not be allowed to touch the 
sprocket.  
3.7.13.23 
Wear strips secured with fasteners shall be designed to thermally expand and 
contract. An acceptable method is to secure the leading edge of the wear strip 
with a fastener that is countersunk below the wear strip’s top surface. Each 
successive hole should be countersunk and elongated and loosely fitted so that 
the wear strip is free to expand or contract under the head of the fastener. 
3.7.13.24 
Modular plastic belt material shall be Acetal. 
3.7.14 
Other Requirements 
3.7.14.1 
All switch brackets shall be adjustable. 
3.7.15 
Operator Load and Unload 
3.7.15.1 
When an operator loads or unloads parts directly from a fixed height belt, the 
height shall be determined by an ergonomic study.  
3.7.16 
Structural 
3.7.16.1 
Conveyor frames shall not deflect when belts are tensioned. 
3.7.16.2 
Belt tracking guides on the conveyor bed shall be prohibited. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 151 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.16.3 
Unless otherwise specified all carry way support beds shall be slider style beds. 
3.7.16.4 
All belt conveyors shall have solid carry way support beds. 
3.7.16.5 
Carry ways shall extend the full length of the conveyor without gaps except for 
pulley clearance. 
3.7.16.6 
Carry ways in the belt path shall be smooth and free of obstructions, such as 
protruding bolt heads and weld flash, which would result in belt damage. 
3.7.16.7 
Carry ways shall be the same height as the pulley. 
3.7.16.8 
Carry ways shall have a slight downward slope at the pulley so that no sharp 
edge is exposed to the belt. 
3.7.16.9 
The end of the carry ways shall be close enough to the pulley so that there is no 
sag in the belt in the space between the carry way and the pulley. 
3.7.16.10 
Carry ways shall be of stainless steel or aluminum construction for applications 
utilizing magnets located beneath the carry way support beds. 
3.7.16.11 
Carry ways shall be strong enough to support a 300 lb. person walking on it 
without deflection. 
3.7.16.12 
All points used to establish belt conveyor "level" and "straight" shall be clearly 
identified. 
3.7.16.13 
Break points for shipping shall be match-marked and clearly visible. 
3.7.16.14 
Each floor-mounting pad on the frame shall have leveling hardware.  
3.7.16.15 
Leveling hardware shall conform to GVOSS Section 1.1.23. 
3.7.16.16 
Conveyors shall be aligned for proper function over their useful life. The conveyor 
Supplier's installation procedures shall be followed. Their installation procedures 
shall include alignment procedures and requirements.  
3.7.16.17 
The belt conveyor supplier shall furnish all leveling and mounting hardware with 
the exception of anchors and anchor bolts. Anchors and anchor bolts shall be the 
responsibility of the supplier when installing the belt conveyor at their facility and 
the installation contractor when installing the belt conveyor at the Ford Motor 
Company receiving facility. 
3.7.17 
Paint Colors 
3.7.17.1 
Paint materials and colors shall conform to the requirements of the GVOSS 
Section 1.4. 
3.7.17.2 
SBU belt conveyors for stamping press lines (High Bay Equipment) shall be 
painted Safety White.

3.7.18 
Tuck-Under Drives 
3.7.18.1 
Tuck-under drives are allowed on uni-directional conveyors upon the submission 
and approval of an Action Request Form and treated as a deviation. 
 
3.7.18.2 
In a tuck-under drive system the drive shaft must be located in a manner that 
provides a sufficiently sized catenary pocket immediately following the drive. 
3.7.19 
Bi-Directional Conveyors 
3.7.19.1 
Bi-directional conveyors shall be center pull. 
 
3.7.19.2 
The drive pulley shall be centered to the end pulleys. 
3.7.19.3 
The drive pulley shall have 180 of belt engagement. 
3.7.19.4 
The drive return rollers shall be sized for the belt loading. 
3.7.19.5 
The drive return rollers shall have Lagging in addition to the driving pulley. 
3.7.19.6 
The belt manufacturer shall concur with the number and spacing of the return 
rollers used to control catenary sag. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 153 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.19.7 
Bi-directional end drives shall require submission and approval of an Action 
Request Form and treated as a deviation. 
 
 
 
3.7.19.8 
On Bi-directional end drives, the idle drive shall not be back-driven, creating 
regenerative current by the active drive. 
3.7.20 
Extendable Conveyor 
3.7.20.1 
Extendable conveyors shall use a slide unit where pulleys "A" and "B" are 
mounted to the slide so that they move in unison to each other. (See example 
below.) 
 
 
3.7.20.2 
In the extended position roller "B" shall not extend to or beyond roller "C". 
3.7.20.3 
The extension mechanism shall use linear guides. 
3.7.20.4 
The extension mechanism shall not affect pulley or roller alignment when 
changing extension positions. 
3.7.20.5 
The extension mechanism shall use a mechanical driven system to change 
positions. 
3.7.20.6 
The extension mechanism shall function in a "Manual Jog" mode only. 
3.7.20.7 
The extension mechanism shall be equipped with over-travel protection in both 
directions. 
3.7.20.8 
The extension mechanism shall be equipped with over-travel detection in both 
directions. 
3.7.20.9 
The extension mechanism shall not function while the conveyor belt is moving. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 154 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.20.10 
Double extendable conveyors shall be configured as shown below. 
 
 
3.7.21 
Incline and Decline Conveyor 
3.7.21.1 
The upper pulley shaft shall be either a pulling drive shaft in the case of an 
incline conveyor or a pushing drive shaft in the case of a decline conveyor. 
3.7.21.2 
The driving pulley shaft shall not be lower than the tail pulley. 
3.7.21.3 
Adjustable height decline conveyors shall not be adjustable to the point the 
driving pulley shaft is lower than the tail pulley. 
3.7.21.4 
The height adjustment mechanism shall use linear guides. 
3.7.21.5 
The height adjustment mechanism shall not affect pulley alignment when 
changing extension positions.  
3.7.21.6 
The height adjustment mechanism shall use a linear actuator to change 
positions. 
3.7.21.7 
The height adjustment mechanism shall function in a "Manual Jog" mode only. 
3.7.21.8 
The height adjustment mechanism shall be equipped with over-travel protection 
in both directions. 
3.7.21.9 
The height adjustment mechanism shall be equipped with over-travel detection in 
both directions. 
3.7.22 
Dual Belt Press Exit Conveyor 
3.7.22.1 
Dual belt press exit conveyors shall use a single drive to drive both pulleys 
synchronously. (See example below.) 
 
 
 
3.7.22.2 
The drive shall be accessible for maintenance and replacement. 
3.7.22.3 
The drive shaft shall be split and coupled together using appropriate couplings 
for the application.

Drive sprockets or drive pulleys shall be properly aligned to the belt head shaft

3.7.27 
Controls 
3.7.27.1 
Control component manufacturers used on belt conveyors shall be consistent 
with those specified by the “Approved Controls Components” document supplied 
by VO Controls or SBU Process Engineer. If there is no approved component for 
the application, VO Controls Engineer or VIS or SBU Process Engineer for the 
program shall identify approved component manufacturers. 
3.7.27.2 
The Ford Motor Company VO Controls Engineer or SBU Process Engineer for 
the program shall identify any applicable standard Ford controls reference 
drawings to be used. 
3.7.27.3 
The belt conveyor shall have a separate motor starter panel and enclosure. 
3.7.27.4 
Control cabling required to be removed from a terminal box for shipment shall be 
receptacle and plug type. 
3.7.27.5 
Permanent cables and mounting brackets for belt conveyor control enclosures 
mounted on the tooling fence line shall be the responsibility of the Tier 1 
Integrator. 
3.7.27.6 
Cabling from belt conveyor control enclosures to tool station panels is the 
responsibility of the Tier 1 Integrator. 
3.7.27.7 
Temporary cables and stands for control enclosures to be mounted on the tooling 
fence line necessary for try-out and buy-off at the belt conveyor supplier shall be 
the responsibility of the belt conveyor supplier. 
3.7.27.8 
Belt conveyor control boxes shall be accessible from floor level or maintenance 
platform while the belt conveyor is running. 
3.7.27.9 
Belt conveyor valves shall be accessible from floor level or maintenance platform 
while the belt conveyor is running. 
3.7.27.10 
Unless otherwise specified, the conveyor will automatically index parts until the 
unload station has a (sensed) part in place.  
3.7.27.11 
Belt conveyor controls drawings shall be provided. 
3.7.27.12 
Belt conveyor logic integration guidelines shall be provided. 
3.7.27.13 
Belt conveyor fault messages shall be provided. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 162 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.7.27.14 
For Legacy Systems, a belt conveyor stop shall be provided. The belt conveyor 
stop shall utilize a safety cable to stop the belt conveyor.  This shall stop the 
motor only. The belt conveyor stop shall be “Control Reliable”. The safety cable 
must be able to be reached in the entire load area. The safety cable shall be 
aircraft type cable with orange plastic covering. At 10-foot intervals, a yellow 
1.25” x 6” lamacoid tag with ¾” black letters bearing the words “Conveyor Stop", 
shall be attached to the belt conveyor stop cable. (See Fig. 1) A separate cable 
shall be provided for each side of the belt conveyor. The cable will be straight 
and cannot make any turn.  “Conveyor Stop” pushbuttons may be used in lieu of 
safety cables with the approval of the Ford Motor Company VOGO ME, VIS and 
VO Controls Engineer or SBU Process Engineer. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
             Fig. 1 
Next Gen belt conveyors shall have a control reliable run/stop pushbutton as 
shown in the standard Ford BCE controls drawings for the program. 
3.7.27.15 
The conveyor stop shall not interfere with any manual load or unload operation. 
3.7.27.16 
All belt conveyors shall be equipped with a run-bar. The Ford VOME Controls 
Engineer or SBU Process Engineer shall provide direction on Ford reference 
drawings, function and design requirements.

3.7.28 
Buy-Off 
3.7.28.1 
Requirements for holding a buy-off at the conveyor supplier shall be determined 
by the VO ME or SBU Process Engineer. The buy-off event should be with the 
M.E., VIS or SBU Process Engineer, Supplier, conveyor Supplier and plant 
(optional). This section provides guidelines for the buy-off event, when it is 
required. 
3.7.28.2 
There shall be a buyoff event at the belt conveyor manufacturer facility prior to 
shipment to the system integrator’s facility. 
3.7.28.3 
The belt conveyor shall be connected to all air and electrical services required for 
functioning of the equipment. 
3.7.28.4 
Air pressure shall be set at a maximum 65 p.s.i. 
3.7.28.5 
Belt conveyor controls shall be connected to a PLC loaded with the belt conveyor 
logic and fault messages. 
3.7.28.6 
The belt conveyor logic shall be modified to operate the belt conveyor in an 
automatic repeat mode. 
3.7.28.7 
The automatic repeat mode shall simulate belt conveyor function in the 
manufacturing environment of the final installation.  
3.7.28.8 
The buy-off shall be the same as GVOSS Section 1.2.6 with the following 
exceptions: 
• 
Only Buy-Off Book Tabs, 3, 5-8, 15, 16, 18 and 21 apply.  
• 
The buy-off event shall consist of a Buy-Off Book review and GVOSS 
Section 1.2.6.5 Phase I.

Revision Date: June 08, 2025

3.8 
MANUAL LIFT ASSIST, RAIL SYSTEMS, BALANCERS AND HOISTS 
See also GVOSS Section 3.9 for requirements of Special Manufactured Hooks 
and Hook Assemblies used with Manual Lift Assists. 
3.8.1 
General 
3.8.1.1 
This section provides requirements for the Tier 1 Integrators and equipment 
manufacturers. All Manual Lift assists, Rail Systems, Balancers and 
Hoists/Cranes provided, relocated, modified, or installed shall conform to all 
requirements of the Ford Motor Company, Plant Engineering Group and the 
Vehicle Operations Safety Department.  
• 
For equipment over 1 ton - (FAS08-128). 
• 
For equipment under 1 ton - (FAS08-145). 
3.8.1.1.1 
w-OETS Check sheet shall be completed prior to signing ProTag Pink (hanging 
ProTag Yellow). Completed copy to be placed in Safety Evidence Book. 
3.8.1.2 
Contractor installation of equipment at the plant shall follow the requirements of 
the applicable Ford Land Facilities Construction Specifications. 
https://azureford.sharepoint.com/sites/FLengineeringstandards 
 
3.8.1.3 
All components shall be selected from the GVOSS approved components list for 
the program. 
3.8.1.4 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible to deliver designs for all manual lift 
assist and/or articulating arm tooling to Ford, in Team Center. The FIDES file for 
these designs will be appropriately classified (e.g. Manual Assistor) and checked 
into Team Center under the 016ZF number assigned to it. 
3.8.1.5 
All product nest tooling, which is part of the lift assist or articulating arm tool, will 
be designed in FIDES. 
3.8.1.6 
The arm or carrier for the product nest may be designed using CAD software 
other than FIDES when sourced to a Tier 2 supplier, but it is the responsibility of 
the Tier 1 Integrator to convert (not necessarily re-master) the design into FIDES, 
before checking into Team Center as stated in section 3.8.1.4. 
3.8.1.7 
Rigging Hardware. 
This section covers the requirements for hardware commonly used with hooks, 
hoists and secondary restraints. The requirements of this section shall apply to 
all applications of the hardware identified in this section. 
The equipment supplier bears the sole responsibility for the proper selection, use 
and installation of the rigging hardware supplied with their equipment. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 165 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
The equipment supplier shall furnish all rigging hardware below the hook where 
hook hoists are used and all rigging hardware to connect equipment directly to a 
hoist cable or chain where used. 
 
3.8.1.7.1 
Definitions for Rigging Hardware: 
DESIGN (SAFETY) FACTOR: An industry term denoting a product’s theoretical 
reserve capability; usually computed by dividing the catalog Ultimate Load by the 
Working Load Limit.  
PROOF LOAD: The average force applied in the performance of a proof test; the 
average force to which a product may be subjected before deformation occurs. 
PROOF TEST: A test applied to a product solely to determine non-conforming 
material or manufacturing defects. 
ULTIMATE LOAD: The average load or force at which the product fails, or no 
longer supports the load. 
RATED LOAD: The maximum allowable working load established by the rigging 
hardware manufacturer. The terms “rated capacity” and “working load limit” 
(WLL) are commonly used to describe rated load.  
SHOCK LOAD: A force that results from the rapid application of a force (such as 
impacting and/or jerking) or rapid movement of a static load. A shock load 
significantly adds to the static load. 
WORKING LOAD: The maximum mass or force which the product is authorized 
to support in a particular service.  
NOTE: The Working Load shall never be less than the Gross Load of the 
equipment. 
3.8.1.7.2 
All components shall be manufactured in compliance to the latest release of; 
• ASME B30.26 
• ISO Standard 53.020.30: Accessories for lifting equipment and standard  
• ISO 77.140.65: Steel wire, wire ropes and link chains 
3.8.1.7.3 
Shackles: All shackles shall be bolt type with cotter pin, see example below. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 166 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• The screw pin shall be fully engaged, with the shoulder in contact with the 
shackle body.  
• The shackle is designed for a cotter pin, it shall be used and maintained in 
good working condition.  
• Alterations or modifications are prohibited. 
• Contact with sharp edges that could damage the shackle should be 
avoided. 
• Shock loading should be avoided. 
• The load applied to the shackle should be centered in the bow of the 
shackle to prevent side loading of the shackle. 
• Multiple sling legs should not be applied to the shackle pin. 
3.8.1.7.3.1 
Materials: The shackle shall have sufficient ductility to permanently deform 
before losing the ability to support the load at the temperatures at which the 
manufacturer has specified for use. 
3.8.1.7.3.2 
Design Factor.  
• 
The design factor for shackles up to and including a 150-ton rated load shall 
be a minimum of 5. 
• 
The design factor for shackles over 150 ton rated load shall be a minimum of 
4. 
3.8.1.7.3.3 
Rated Loads: Rated load shall be in accordance with the recommendation of the 
shackle manufacturer. The terms “rated capacity” and “working load limit” are 
commonly used to describe rated load. Shackle Body Identification. Each new 
shackle body shall have forged, cast, or die stamped markings by the 
manufacturer to show: 
• 
name or trademark of manufacturer 
• 
rated load 
• 
size 
3.8.1.7.3.4 
Shackle Pin Identification. Each new shackle pin shall have forged, cast, or die 
stamped markings by the manufacturer to show: 
• 
name or trademark of manufacturer 
• 
grade, material type, or load rating 
3.8.1.7.3.5 
Loading 
• 
Pin spacers should be used to maintain loading at the center of the shackle 
pin. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 167 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
All efforts shall be made to design the application for in-line loading of the 
shackle. 
• 
Shackles with angular load shall reduce the WLL according to the 
manufacturers' specifications, see figure below. 
 
• 
Off-Center loading is prohibited, see figure below. 
                                                
 
3.8.1.7.4 
Adjustable Hardware 
3.8.1.7.4.1 
Materials: The hardware, excluding bushings and bearings, shall have sufficient 
ductility to permanently deform before losing the ability to support the load at the 
temperatures at which the manufacturer has specified for use. 
3.8.1.7.4.2 
Design Factor. The design factor for adjustable hardware shall be a minimum of 
5. 
3.8.1.7.4.3 
Rated Loads: Rated load shall be in accordance with the recommendation of the 
hardware manufacturer. The terms “rated capacity” and “working load limit” are 
commonly used to describe rated load. 
3.8.1.7.4.4 
Turnbuckle, Eyebolt, and Eye Nut Identification. 
 
Each turnbuckle, eyebolt, and eye nut shall be marked to show: 
• 
Name or trademark of manufacturer 
• 
Size or rated load 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005

Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 168 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
Grade for alloy eyebolts 
3.8.1.7.4.5 
Swivel Hoist Ring Identification.  
Each swivel hoist ring shall be marked to show: 
• 
Name or trademark of manufacturer 
• 
Rated load 
• 
torque value 
3.8.1.7.4.6 
Eye Bolts 
Unless otherwise specified in writing by Ford Motor Company only shoulder nut 
eye bolts and machinery eye bolts are approved for use, see figure below: 
 
 
 
 
 
 
• 
Shoulder eye bolts shall only be used in vehicle assembly applications where 
the equipment motion is controlled (stabilized) by the operator. 
• 
The use of shoulder eye bolts in material transfer applications where there 
exists uncontrolled equipment motion is prohibited. 
• 
A Hoist Hook shall never be engaged into the eye of an eye bolt. An 
intermediate link such as a shackle or link should be used between the eye 
bolt and hoist hook. The equipment supplier shall be responsible for the 
selection of the correct hardware for the application. 
• 
Shoulder mounting surfaces shall be either spot-faced or machined flat. 
• 
Shoulder eye bolts should be secured against rotation. 
• 
The shoulder shall be flush, and the bolt securely torqued to the 
manufacturer’s specifications against the load. 
• 
Use of shoulder nut eye bolts in tapped holes is prohibited. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 169 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
When used in an untapped through-hole the threads shall not extend into the 
load and the nut under the load shall be fully engaged as shown in the image 
below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
• 
When used for angular lifting, the plane of the eye shall be aligned with the 
direction of loading. The working load limit (WLL) must be reduced according 
to the manufacturers' specifications. 
• 
Eye bolts shall be in good working condition prior to use. Alterations or 
modifications shall require the manufacturers' approval in writing. 
• 
Shock loading should be avoided. 
3.8.1.7.4.7 
Eye Nuts 
Only through-hole top nut Eye Nuts are approved for use, see figure below: 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 170 of 304

 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
Eye nuts should be secured against rotation during use. 
• 
The threads of the eye nut shall be fully engaged (see figure below). 
• 
Eye nuts shall only be used for in-line loads (see figure below). 
 
• 
The plane of the eye may be positioned with a flat washer(s) or lock nut. 
• 
Components shall be in good working condition prior to use. Alterations or 
modifications shall require the manufacturers' approval in writing. 
• 
Shock loading should be avoided. 
3.8.1.7.4.8 
Swivel Hoist Rings 
 
• 
When used in a threaded hole, the effective thread length shall be 1-1/2 
times the diameter of the bolt for steel. 
• 
When used in a through-hole application, a nut and washer shall be used. 
The washer and nut shall be in accordance with the swivel hoist ring 
manufacturer’s recommendations. The nut shall be fully engaged. 
• 
The bushing flange shall fully contact the load surface. 
• 
Spacers or washers shall not be used between the bushing flange and the 
mounting surface of the load being lifted. 
• 
The swivel hoist ring shall be tightened to the torque specifications of the 
manufacturer. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 171 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
The swivel hoist ring shall be free to rotate and pivot without interference 
during lifting. 
• 
The load applied to the swivel hoist ring shall be centered in the bail to 
prevent side loading. 
• 
Any attached lifting component shall be narrower than the inside width of the 
bail to avoid spreading. 
• 
Components shall be in good working condition prior to use. Alterations or 
modifications are prohibited. 
• 
Ensure that the swivel hoist ring WLL meets or exceeds the anticipated 
angular rigging tension. 
• 
Shock loading should be avoided. 
3.8.1.7.5 
Wire Rope Clips: All Wire Rope Clips shall be U-Bolt type, see example below: 
 
3.8.1.7.5.1 
Materials: Wire rope clip materials shall be of sufficient strength such that failure 
of the wire rope will occur before failure of the wire rope clip at the temperatures. 
3.8.1.7.5.2 
Design Factor. Due to the nature of the design and use, wire rope clips do not 
have a conventional design factor. Wire rope clips shall be designed to have an 
80% minimum connection efficiency based on the wire rope published minimum 
breaking force with which they are used. 
3.8.1.7.5.3 
Rated Loads: The rated load for wire rope assemblies using compression 
hardware is based on the following factors: 
• 
Wire rope minimum breaking force 
• 
80% minimum connection efficiency 
• 
Design factor of the wire rope application 
3.8.1.7.5.4 
Wire Rope Clip Saddle Identification. 
Each new wire rope clip saddle shall have forged or die stamped markings by the 
manufacturer to show: 
• 
Name or trademark of manufacturer 
• 
Size 
3.8.1.7.5.5 
Assembly – Wire Rope Clips: 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 172 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
Before installing a wire rope clip on plastic coated or plastic impregnated wire 
rope, consult the wire rope clip manufacturer or wire rope manufacturer. 
• 
For U-bolt clips used to create end terminations, the saddle shall be placed 
on the live end of the wire rope, with the U-bolt on the dead-end side 
• 
At least the minimum number of clips as recommended by the manufacturer. 
• 
The spacing and turn-back should be as recommended by the manufacturer. 
Spacing and turn-back are a function of clip size and wire rope size.  
• 
The wire rope clip shall be tightened to the torque recommended by the 
manufacturer. 
• 
After assembly, the connection shall be loaded to at least the expected 
working load. After unloading, wire rope clips shall then be re-tightened to the 
torque recommended by the manufacturer.

3.8.1.7.6 
Links, Rings and Swivels 
3.8.1.7.6.1 
Materials: Links, rings, and swivels shall have sufficient ductility to permanently 
deform before losing the ability to support the load at the temperatures that the 
manufacturer has specified for use. 
3.8.1.7.6.2 
Design Factor. The design factor for links, rings, and swivels shall be a minimum 
of 5. 
3.8.1.7.6.3 
Rated Loads: Rated load shall be in accordance with the recommendation of the 
link, ring, or swivel manufacturer. The terms “rated capacity” and “working load 
limit” are commonly used to describe rated load. 
3.8.1.7.6.4 
Proof Test Requirements: Prior to initial use, welded links and rings shall be 
proof tested by the manufacturer. 
3.8.1.7.6.5 
Proof Load Requirements: The proof load shall be a minimum of 2 times the 
rated load. 
3.8.1.7.6.6 
Link, Ring, and Swivel Identification.  
Each new link, ring, and swivel shall be marked by the manufacturer to show: 
• 
Name or trademark of manufacturer. 
• 
Size or rated load.

SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
Grade, if required to identify rated load. 
 
3.8.1.7.6.7 
Link and Ring Applications. 
• 
Alterations or modifications are prohibited. 
• 
Contact with obstructions that could damage the link or ring should be 
avoided. 
• 
Shock loading should be avoided. 
• 
Links and rings should not be dragged on an abrasive surface. 
• 
The link or ring shall be of the proper shape and size to ensure that it seats 
properly in the hook or lifting device. 
• 
Multiple slings or rigging hardware gathered in a link or ring shall not exceed 
a 120-degree included angle (see figure below). 
• 
The horizontal angle of loading should not be less than 30 degrees unless 
approved by a qualified person (see figure below).

3.8.1.7.6.8 
 Swivels are positioning hardware and are not intended to be rotated under load 
as part of the equipment function. 
• 
Swivels shall only be used for in-line loads (see figure below). 
 
• 
Components shall be maintained in good working condition. 
• 
Alterations or modifications are prohibited. 
• 
Shock loading should be avoided. 
• 
Swivels shall be of the proper shape and size to ensure that they seat 
properly in the hook or lifting device. 
• 
Contact with obstructions that could damage the swivel should be avoided.

3.8.2 
Manual Lift Assists – General 
  
3.8.2.1 
All lift assists shall have an MFMEA.  
3.8.2.1.1 
A risk assessment which satisfies the requirements of procedure FAS08-036 and 
its attachments shall be completed for all lift assists. 
3.8.2.1.2 
Two-hand controls with Anti-tiedown logic are required for all motions contained 
within the lift assist tooling unless stated otherwise in the program SOW or an 
Action Request – Deviation form is submitted and approved. (Ref. 1.3.6.3, 
1.3.11.2) 
3.8.2.1.3 
A Risk-Assessment shall be completed during the 25% Design phase to 
determine required safety measures, mechanical and controls actions. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 178 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.2.1.4 
Process description is to be defined and verified at every design review. All 
positions of lift assist, operator, and associated process tooling motions are to be 
evaluated to be shown as feasible and ergonomically acceptable. 
3.8.2.1.5 
All tooling documentation is required at buyoff on OEMs floor. This includes Risk-
Assessment, w-OETS Checklist, MFEMA, Spare parts list, Control prints, 
Pneumatic diagrams, Ergonomic evaluation, Elevation and install prints, testing 
and Professional Engineer certifications. 
3.8.2.1.6 
Part-load lift assists should be made of rigid structure when possible. This rigid-
beam or torque-tube design eliminates unintended elevation changes and better 
controls the tool. 
3.8.2.1.7 
Nets and clamp fingers that contact vehicle product shall be hardened steel HRC 
58-62 if contacting a non-Class-A part of the body (inner-reinforcement/trim 
edge/weld flange). Class-A contact is to be avoided or protected with alternative 
material selection. Urethane covered details shall be 40-45 durometer or less. 
The contacting block shall be sized appropriately to prevent damage to the 
Class-A surface. 
3.8.2.1.8 
3D Additive components are to be investigated and implemented whenever 
feasible. TPU material to be considered where tool interfaces with the product. 
3.8.2.1.9 
Hook and loop fastening of all hoses and wires are required. "Zip-ties" are 
prohibited unless local code prohibits hook and loop fasteners. This shall prevent 
pinched or restricted airlines and damaged wires. 
3.8.2.1.10 
Pneumatic Requirements: 
3.8.2.1.10.1 Tubing to be specified by the approved component list in the program SOW. 
3.8.2.1.10.2 All fittings are to be G-port fittings unless specified otherwise in the OEM's SOW. 
The use of G-Port fittings drives the use of metric diameter tubing for the entire 
tool. 
3.8.2.1.10.3 Push-lock swivel fittings are preferred. 
3.8.2.1.10.4 Synthetic Tube Requirements: 
3.8.2.1.10.4.1 Hose shall not to be special order. If tubing is single-colored multi-circuit coils, 
then the appropriate color tape shall be applied to each end and match the 
controls/pneumatic drawings. 
3.8.2.1.10.4.2 Tubing color shall be as follows: 
1. Pressure Feed – BLUE 
2. Extend to Work Position – RED 
3. Retract to Home Position – WHITE 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 179 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
4. Vacuum – WHITE– Magnetic Clamp “On” (Work Position) 
5. Vacuum Blow Off – BLUE – Magnetic Clamp “Off” (Home Position) 
 
3.8.2.2 
Handles 
3.8.2.2.1 
Lift-Assist Pneumatic Control Handles shall be provided by the lift assist 
manufacturer. 
3.8.2.2.2 
The right handle shall be for the Common, Up and Down switches.  
3.8.2.2.3 
The right handle switches shall be color coded as follows: 
Common – Unpainted or White 
Down and Home – Red 
Up and Advance - Green 
 
 
3.8.2.2.4 
All motions and functions are to be fully color-coded and labeled as to their 
function.

All handle switches shall be guarded to prevent inadvertent actuation of the

Handle switch guards shall be painted Safety Yellow. 
3.8.2.2.7 
Articulating handle assemblies utilizing gas springs shall have limiters to restrict 
the travel and prevent them from fully extending. See example below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.8.2.3 
Secondary Clamping Devices  
3.8.2.3.1 
The need for secondary clamping devices shall be determined from a Risk 
Assessment. 
3.8.2.3.2 
When secondary clamping devices are necessary the following shall apply: 
3.8.2.3.3 
The secondary devices may be  
1. Clamps that lock in the closed position (toggle lock) 
2. Approved, properly sized, pneumatically-positioned permanent magnets 
encased in non-ferrous housing using pneumatic pressure to move the 
magnetic piston to the “Release” position.  
3. A manually actuated lever connected to a linkage that closes nesting details. 
The linkage design shall be such that it locks in the open and closed position. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 181 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
The chosen device does not need to contact the part yet shall completely support 
the weight of the part during a total loss of air pressure. 
NOTE: Duration of the total loss of air pressure may be weeks over a shut-down 
period. Device leak-down period shall be taken into consideration. 
3.8.2.3.4 
When secondary clamping devices are necessary the following warning lamacoid 
tag shall be installed as depicted in Fig. 1.
