3.8.2.5 
Special attention shall be given to the identification and elimination of “pinch-
points” in the tools.  
1. Tools shall be evaluated for pinch points at:  
Beginning of operation  
Through the operational sequence  
End of operation  
Through the return cycle  
2. Attention shall be paid to identifying pinch points at:  
End stops (adjustable or non-adjustable)  
Over-travel stops  
Bearing-to-guide rail clearances 
Shear points where the moving mechanism passes through a fixed plane 
 
 
3.8.2.5.1 
When pinch points exist, they shall be identified as follows: 
1. The immediate area of the pinch point shall be Safety Yellow with Safety 
Black Striping. 
2. The appropriate Hazard Label shall be attached in close proximity to the 
hazard. (see example below) 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

Hazard labels for pinch points shall have the following information, see example 
below: 
1. Signal Word.  
2. Hazard Identification. 
3. Safety Symbol 
4. Prohibition Symbol 
See also GVOSS Sections 1.4.1.6 and 1.4.2 for additional information on Hazard 
Labels. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.8.2.6 
Hazard labels and tags are not considered alone to be sufficient controls for 
operator pinch points. 
3.8.2.7 
Hazard labels and tags shall be used to identify the hazard for maintenance 
personnel when guarding is removed (see GVOSS Section 1.4: Visual Factory 
for hazard label requirements). 
3.8.2.8 
Noise shall comply with the FAS08-212  w-SX1 Noise Abatement Specification.  
3.8.2.9 
All manual lift assist devices hung solely from a balancer shall be of modular 
construction and utilize carbon fiber elements.  
3.8.2.10 
All moving components shall be painted Safety Orange.

3.8.2.11 
Lift Assist Clear of Automation 
3.8.2.11.1 
Lift assist devices that are used to load and unload to and from automation shall 
be equipped with switches to detect that the lift assist device is clear of the 
automation. 
3.8.2.11.2 
The detection method shall be control reliable (i.e. two switches shall be used for 
all methods). 
3.8.2.11.3 
Cube proximity switches to be used.  The switches will be maintained on while 
the hoist is in the “clear of automation” position (The two switches will detect the 
presents of a common “ski bar”). 
3.8.2.11.4 
The use of light screens is permitted with lift assists on Jib style booms to sense 
the Jib position predicated that the lift assist is clear of the fixture tooling when 
the Jib leaves the light screen sensing area. See image below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Light Screens

3.8.3 
Articulating Arms, Mast and Boom Style Assists 
 
3.8.3.1 
Manual lift assist devices with mechanical joints, in particular mast, boom, jib and 
articulated designs, shall be certified by a Professional Engineer.  
3.8.3.2 
Brakes and Hard Stops  
3.8.3.2.1 
When using active brakes that can be set during the motion of an Articulating 
Arm, Mast and Boom, all of the affected joints shall be designed to withstand 2.0 
times the dynamic forces generated when the brake is set while the unit is in 
motion. 
3.8.3.2.2 
When using a passive “Parking Brake” which secures the Articulating Arm, Mast 
and Boom at a pre-defined, static rest position only, the brake control system 
shall be designed so that the brake cannot be actuated during the period the 
device is in motion. 
3.8.3.2.3 
The use of brakes and whether they are active, or parking brakes shall require 
the concurrence of VOME prior to proceeding with the design. 
3.8.3.2.4 
When using hard stops Articulating Arm, Mast and Boom all of the affected joints 
shall be designed to withstand 2.0 times the dynamic forces generated when the 
hard stop is encountered by the moving part of the device. 
3.8.3.3 
Aluminum construction of mast, boom, jib and articulated designs used for the 
mounting of manual load assist devices is prohibited unless an Action Request 
Form - Deviation is first submitted and approved.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 187 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled

Suspended assists that rotate shall use slewing rings, see figure below.

3.8.4 
Systems Moving with Work 
3.8.4.1 
Unless otherwise approved by Ford Motor Company VOME, all rail systems, 
which provide for a means to temporarily attach the equipment to a moving 
production unit (continuously moving line), product subassembly, or product 
carrier and/or all equipment which is limited by travel or work station space; or 
requires that a production operator rapidly disengage, shall be provided with a 
protection feature such as, but not limited to, end-travel limit switches that 
prevent the accumulation of hazards/forces. 
3.8.4.2 
In the event that the tool becomes entangled or attached with the production unit, 
the equipment provided shall be complete with interlocks, audible horns, local 
fault resets and globe lamps adjacent to the workstations.  
3.8.4.3 
End-of-travel safety controls shall be interlocked to the product conveyance 
equipment.  
3.8.4.4 
All limit switches shall be located to provide adequate time to stop the adjacent 
delivery equipment, and to prevent the rail trolley from reaching the primary end 
stop. 
3.8.5 
Rated Load  
3.8.5.1 
Definitions: 
• 
"Monorail" means a movable hoisting mechanism traveling on a single 
overhead fixed runway structure. 
• 
"Overhead crane" means a crane with a movable bridge carrying a movable 
or fixed hoisting mechanism and traveling on an overhead fixed runway 
structure. 
• 
"Rated load" means the maximum load for which an individual monorail or 
overhead crane is designed and built by the manufacturer and shown on the 
equipment nameplate(s). 
3.8.5.2 
Rated Load Marking  
3.8.5.2.1 
The rated load of an overhead crane shall be plainly marked on each side of the 
crane bridge and hoist and this marking shall be clearly legible from the ground 
or floor. 
3.8.5.2.2 
When the overhead crane has more than one hoisting unit, each hoist shall have 
its rated load marked on it and this marking shall be clearly legible from the 
ground or floor. 
3.8.5.2.3 
The rated load of a monorail shall be plainly marked on each side of the rail and 
hoist and this marking shall be clearly legible from the ground or floor. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 189 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.5.2.4 
When the monorail has more than one hoisting unit, each hoist shall have its 
rated load marked on it and this marking shall be clearly legible from the ground 
or floor. 
3.8.5.3 
Modifications  
3.8.5.3.1 
Overhead cranes and monorails may be modified and rerated provided such 
modifications and the supporting structure are checked thoroughly for the new 
rated load by a registered professional engineer.  
3.8.5.3.2 
When an overhead crane or monorail is rerated the previous rated load 
identification shall be permanently removed. 
3.8.5.3.3 
New rated load shall be displayed in accordance with GVOSS section 3.8.6.2. 
3.8.6 
Secondary Restraints 
3.8.6.1 
Where the failure of a single load bearing fastener in shear or tension could 
cause the overhead equipment to fall or rotate, creating the potential to injure the 
personnel below, overhead equipment shall be fitted with approved secondary 
restraints in accordance with FAS08-145 and w-OETS-001.  
3.8.6.2 
Secondary Restraint Design 
3.8.6.2.1 
A secondary restraint shall never be attached to the primary attachment point it is 
protecting for the failure of. 
3.8.6.2.2 
Per FAS08-145 Section 3.1 and w-OETS-001 Section 4.1.3 (6), the secondary 
restraints shall be designed to twice the Working Load of the Gross Load 
Capacity of the equipment. 
3.8.6.2.3 
The secondary restraint, its connection points, routing and hardware shall be 
shown on the assembly drawings. 
3.8.6.2.4 
The secondary restraint and all associated hardware shall be identified and 
called-out on the Bill of Materials (BOM). 
3.8.6.3 
All redundant supports, safety chains, safety cables, safety lugs, drop stops, 
fasteners and attachments shall be designed to provide a minimum safety factor 
of 2 times the rated load capacity of the primary attachment device which it 
supplements. 
NOTE: The component “Design Factor” shall NOT be used to fulfill this 
requirement. 
3.8.6.4 
All redundant support systems for end trucks and hangers shall be supplied by 
the equipment manufacturers. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 190 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.6.5 
All redundant support systems shall be properly sized, configured and installed 
such that in the event of failure at the primary attachment point, the load drop 
distance is maintained and minimized to the extent possible. 
3.8.6.6 
All redundant support systems provided shall be installed to centralize the load to 
the vertical axis of the supporting member in the event of primary support failure. 
3.8.6.7 
All redundant and intermediate stops, safety cable, chains, fasteners and 
attachments provided shall be installed to accommodate articulation of the 
trolley, balancer, fixture or similar piece of equipment without binding. 
3.8.6.8 
All rigging hardware utilized for secondary restraints shall comply with GVOSS 
Section 1.3.16.1.  
NOTE: The use of crimped sleeve cable clamp connections are only permitted 
when approved by the equipment manufacturer. When using crimped sleeve 
cable clamps a minimum of two (2) clamps must be used. 
3.8.6.9 
All cable clamps, shackles and lugs utilized for trolleys, balancers, end stops, or 
similar pieces or equipment shall be of drop forged design complete with pin-able 
locking detail (where available). The use of crimped sleeve connections is 
approved for use by the equipment manufacturer only. 
3.8.6.10 
Secondary restraint safety cable for bridge trolleys solely through any flange of 
enclosed track and tool rail is prohibited. The use of forged eye bolts through the 
rail flange is the preferred method. Other methods must have a risk assessment 
and are to be approved on a case-by-case basis. 
NOTE: Cable must go through both eye bolts.

3.8.6.11 
Secondary restraint safety cable for hanger assemblies solely through any flange 
of enclosed track and tool rail is prohibited (see also Hanger Assembly 
Secondary Restraint). The use of forged shoulder eye bolts through the rail 
flange is the preferred method. Other methods must have a risk assessment and 
are to be approved on a case-by-case basis. 
NOTE 1: Secondary Restraints are only required at the rail system end hangers. 
NOTE 2: Cable must go through both eye bolts.

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.7 
Enclosed Track and Tool Rail System Design 
3.8.7.1 
The enclosed track system shall be modular design, and self-aligning with 
complete bolt together capability at all splice joints, hangers, end stops and 
connections. 
 
3.8.7.2 
Field welding of connections, track joints, hangers, end stops, and other 
components is prohibited. 
3.8.7.3 
The manufacturer's drawing shall indicate loads, hanging method, torque 
requirements, hanger connection, component match marks and track bracing 
design. The manufacturer’s drawing shall be reviewed and certified as 
acceptable by a registered Professional Engineer. 
3.8.7.4 
All sway bracing and thrust bracing required to stabilize the equipment shall be 
designed in accordance with the equipment manufacturers and professional 
structural engineer's requirements.  
3.8.7.5 
All connections shall be torsional, by design. Compression type connections are 
prohibited. 
3.8.7.6 
The allowable stresses applied to the enclosed track design and stress applied to 
hangers, brackets and individual components or attachments shall not exceed 20 
percent of the minimum yield strength of the enclosed track, equipment or 
materials used.  
3.8.7.7 
Track sections shall be provided so that track deflection under rated load shall 
not exceed 1/450 of the span.  
3.8.7.8 
Hanger center spacing shall be designed based on the rated load and allowable 
deflection for the track.  
3.8.7.9 
Each track section shall have at least two support members. 
3.8.7.10 
Lateral deflection of a track section is prohibited. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 193 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.7.11 
The "rated load" shall be the maximum load the rail system is designed and built 
to handle. Dual girder bridge systems shall identify the “system rated load” on 
each bridge rail. 
3.8.7.12 
The rated load of the equipment shall include the load, articulating arm, 
balancers, hooks, handling devices, production parts, and all equipment 
attachments.  
3.8.7.13 
The rated load shall not include the equipment design safety factors. 
3.8.7.14 
All "rated load" calculations must include both static and dynamic loading, 
including operator induced forces that will be imposed on the rail system. 
3.8.7.15 
The allowable stresses, including side-loading (both conventional and non-
conventional) and operator induced forces (pushing or pulling to achieve 
alignment) applied to the rail systems shall be consistent with the allowable 
operating parameters for the rail design. 
3.8.7.16 
All track and rail sections shall be designed and sized to provide for the effects of 
concentrated loads at the tension and compression flanges, in both normal and 
adverse conditions. 
3.8.7.17 
In determining the capacity of the track, the load on the load carrying (tension 
flange) shall be assumed to be at the point central with the wheel tread. 
3.8.7.18 
When a trolley or similar piece of equipment is to transfer across a joint or from 
one track section to another the design of the system shall take into account the 
stress imposed on the lower load carrying flange. 
3.8.7.19 
Where manually operated, balancers, or related equipment are used, impact 
allowance shall be included in the design calculations for the structures, trolleys, 
and track sections.  
3.8.7.20 
The impact allowance shall be 1/2 percent of the rated load for each foot per 
minute or mm per minute of hoisting or lifting speed with a minimum allowance of 
15 percent and a maximum allowance of 50 percent. 
3.8.7.21 
Additional external environmental factors such as building structural vibration, 
building expansion joints and temperature change shall be considered in rail 
system design where applicable. 
3.8.7.22 
Where multiple rail trolleys, balancers or similar equipment are operated or 
stored on the same runways or track, the design of the equipment shall permit 
the concentrated loads for the multiple pieces of equipment to be imposed on the 
track section and structure.  
3.8.7.23 
Clearances shall be provided at the horizontal turns of all rail systems to allow for 
the chording of the load when negotiating the turn.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 194 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.7.24 
Design of the equipment and clearances provided shall be determined and shall 
give consideration to the size of the load, weight, speed of travel in the turn 
radius and process operation or operator requirements. 
3.8.7.25 
Hoist/Balancer has full travel to last part in rack, does not put chain/wire rope in 
violation of GVOSS 3.8.29.11 and does not bind on coiled air line and other 
festooning. 
3.8.7.26 
The maximum overhang of the bridge girder to the center of a runway rail shall 
not exceed the manufacturers' specifications. 
3.8.7.27 
Bridge girders shall be one piece with no joints. 
   
 
3.8.7.28 
All double girder bridge designs shall incorporate a diagonal bracing frame for 
parallel stabilization. 
       
 
3.8.7.29 
Enclosed track systems for festooning shall adhere to the approved components 
list. Ref. GVOSS 2.1.10. 
3.8.7.30 
Use of Strut Channel, i.e. Unistrut or equivalent as an enclosed track is 
prohibited for production tooling on a moving line. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 195 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.8 
Enclosed Track Turns 
3.8.8.1 
All enclosed track turns shall be factory formed by the manufacturer. All tension 
tubes shall be aligned and installed at the manufacturer's facility. Each end of a 
formed turn section shall be match marked to its mating piece and verified for 
trolley chording clearances at the manufacturer's facility. 
 
3.8.8.2 
The radius of all enclosed track turns shall be designed to allow the carriers or 
trolleys to freely negotiate each horizontal turn without binding. 
3.8.8.3 
The standard radius for enclosed track turns to suit a 4" (100mm) series 
application shall be a minimum of 3'-0" (915mm), unless approved by Ford Motor 
Company and the equipment manufacturer. 
3.8.8.4 
The standard radius for enclosed track turns to suit a 6" (150mm) and 8" 
(200mm) series application shall be a minimum of 4'-0" (1220mm), unless 
approved by Ford Motor Company and the equipment manufacturer. 
3.8.8.5 
The maximum overhangs of straight track section to the tangent point of a 
horizontal turn shall not exceed 6" (150mm). 
3.8.8.6 
At a minimum, each curve shall have 3 hangers. The maximum distance 
between hanger centers shall follow the equipment manufacturer specifications. 
Additional hangers shall be provided in accordance with imposed stresses, wheel 
or carrier loads, and required in accordance with calculated deflection 
parameters. 
3.8.8.7 
All hanger brackets shall be bolted at the runway rail.  
3.8.8.8 
All track joints and end connections shall be supported with a minimum of four 
bolts at each hanger connection. 
3.8.8.9 
Special curves shall be supported and braced in accordance with the rail 
manufacturer's requirements. 
3.8.8.10 
Curved track section shall be installed with the uniform bolt tension required by 
the manufacturer to ensure load trolleys do not bind at a track section, while in 
transit or at a splice joint. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 196 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.8.11 
A straight section of track, sized to suit the appropriate trolley, shall be provided 
between tangents of each horizontal turn. 
3.8.8.12 
For a single trolley 4" (100mm) application, the minimum distance between each 
tangent shall be 12" (305mm). 
3.8.8.13 
For a double trolley 4" (100mm) application with articulating load bars, the 
minimum distance between each tangent shall be 28" (710mm) unless approved 
by Ford Motor Company. 
3.8.8.14 
For a single trolley 6" (150mm) and 8" (200mm) application, the minimum 
distance between each tangent shall be 18" (457mm). 
3.8.8.15 
For a double trolley 6" (150mm) and 8" (200mm) application with articulating load 
bars, the minimum distance between tangents shall be 36" (915mm) unless pre-
approved by Ford Motor Company. 
3.8.9 
Trolleys for Enclosed Track Systems 
 
3.8.9.1 
Trolley load wheels shall be symmetrically true with the axis of rotation.  
3.8.9.2 
All trolley wheels shall be the retainer type, designed for a single row of high 
quality, precision ground ball bearings. 
3.8.9.3 
Trolley side guide rollers shall be symmetrically true with the axis of rotation.  
3.8.9.4 
All trolley side guide rollers shall be designed for double row, full complement, 
and precision ground ball bearings. 
3.8.9.5 
All trolleys shall be equipped with safety lugs or a minimum of 2 axles, so that in 
the event of a wheel failure, the trolley cannot pull through the rail. 
3.8.9.6 
All bearings shall be sealed type. 
3.8.9.7 
Manually propelled rail systems shall comply with the Equipment Ergonomic 
study. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 197 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.9.8 
Load attachments provided by the equipment supplier shall be based on the 
trolley manufacturer's recommendation. 
3.8.9.9 
The use of light capacity trolleys on heavy track is prohibited. 
3.8.10 
Festoon Trolleys for Enclosed Track Rail 
3.8.10.1 
Festoon trolleys for enclosed track rail applications shall only be utilized for 
suspending hose and cable. 
 
3.8.10.2 
The rated load capacity for each festoon trolley shall be minimum 100 lbs. 
(45kg). 
3.8.10.3 
Trolleys shall have four wheels. 
3.8.10.4 
Trolley assemblies shall be provided with anti-kick-up wheels or bearings. 
3.8.10.5 
Trolley assemblies shall be provided with sealed ball bearings. 
3.8.10.6 
Trolley assemblies shall be provided with side guide rollers. 
3.8.10.7 
Festoon trolleys shall be tethered together per trolley manufacturers 
specifications to prevent control cable and hose stress. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 198 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.11 
Enclosed Track End Stops and Bumpers 
3.8.11.1 
The equipment supplier shall provide all end stops, safety cables, bumpers, 
bumper plates, fasteners and additional components required to provide a 
complete, failsafe operating system. 
 
3.8.11.2 
Redundant end stops, one primary and one secondary, shall be installed at each 
end of each runway or track section for each rail system. 
3.8.11.3 
Where cyclic impact conditions are present and end trucks, carriers or trolleys 
will contact the primary end stop frequently or at a high rate of speed, the 
equipment supplier shall provide neoprene or polyurethane bumpers, end stops, 
or additional energy absorbing or energy dissipating equipment. 
3.8.11.4 
Where multiple trolleys are specified for use on the same rail and the travel is not 
limited by an alternate means such as mid-rail stops, then the equipment supplier 
shall incorporate energy absorbing or dissipating stops or bumpers. 
3.8.11.5 
Where multiple runways or the same span on parallel rail sections are specified 
so that multiple carriers are designed for transfer to various locations, then each 
end truck, carrier or load bar provided shall be equipped with energy absorbing 
or dissipating bumpers or stops. 
3.8.11.6 
The end stops or bumpers provided shall be of sufficient length and shall be 
located so that all other parts of the carrier, bridge or load attachments shall not 
contact when two bridge cranes, carriers or trolleys impact. 
3.8.11.7 
Replaceable bumpers shall be designed and installed to minimize parts from 
falling in case of failure or loosening of the bolt at a connection. 
3.8.11.8 
Bumpers provided shall be mounted so there is no direct shear on bolts upon 
impact. 
3.8.11.9 
The size and location of integral or removable bridge and trolley bumpers shall 
be specified by the equipment manufacturer.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 199 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.11.10 
Bumpers which engage the wheel treads are prohibited. 
3.8.11.11 
The location of all end stops and bumpers shall accommodate all process and 
production requirements.  
3.8.11.12 
The design, location and type of end stop, or bumper provided shall be certified 
as acceptable for the application and all operating conditions by the equipment 
supplier and the equipment manufacturer. 
3.8.11.13 
When required by special application, mechanical stops and interlocks shall be 
provided for all drop/lift sections, track switches, spur track, transfers or 
interlocking track transitions, or similar equipment.  
3.8.11.14 
The mechanical stops provided shall be easily accessible, positioned to protect 
the operator from hazard, designed to resist all forces of impact, and shall 
provide the same safety requirements as a standard end stop. 
3.8.11.15 
Where additional equipment is required based on the process or application, the 
equipment supplier shall notify Ford Motor Company VOME prior to quotation. 
3.8.11.16 
Bolts provided for end stop application shall be installed true to the horizontal or 
vertical plane. Bolts provided shall be sized in accordance with the application 
and rail size. Bolts shall not be resized without the equipment manufacturer's 
approval. 
3.8.12 
Enclosed Track Rail System Suspension Mounting Design 
3.8.12.1 
The suitable equipment, track, structures and building modifications shall be 
provided so that the loads of the multiple pieces of equipment, when imposed, do 
not exceed the design limitations of the equipment, track, structures or allowable 
loads at truss connections, panel points, or building members. 
For track mounted from a floor mounted structure see GVOSS Section 3.11 for 
additional requirements. 
3.8.12.2 
Lateral deflection of a track section, rail or support member shall be prohibited. 
3.8.12.3 
Transverse tilt of a track section, rail or support member shall be prohibited. 
3.8.12.4 
Rail systems shall be designed to operate at ambient temperatures between -40 
and +150 degrees Fahrenheit (45 to 65 Celsius). 
3.8.12.5 
Where ambient temperatures exceed 150 degrees Fahrenheit (65 degrees 
Celsius), the equipment provided shall be designed to suit the adverse 
application. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 200 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.12.6 
A minimum clearance of 12" (300mm) overhead and 6" (150mm) laterally shall 
be provided and maintained adjacent to all rail systems. Clearance consideration 
shall be given to: 
• 
Supply services 
• 
Festooning 
• 
Lighting 
• 
Primary equipment 
• 
Adjacent equipment 
• 
Structures 
• 
Supports 
• 
Other obstructions or interference points 
3.8.12.7 
Hangers and Support Brackets 
3.8.12.7.1 
Short suspension, non-adjustable rod hangers or track support brackets shall be 
engineered and provided by the rail manufacturer. All leveling and adjustment 
requirements shall be incorporated in the structural support steel design and 
fabrication. See examples below. 
 
 
 
3.8.12.7.2 
Short suspension, adjustable hangers or track support brackets used to support 
enclosed track systems shall be engineered and provided by the rail 
manufacturer. See examples below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.8.12.7.3 
Adjustable hangers that adjust using threaded elements shall have anti-rotate 
protection for the threaded element. 
3.8.12.7.4 
The use of roll pins for anti-rotate protection is prohibited. 
3.8.12.7.5 
When using adjustable hangers, sway bracing to prevent axial movement shall 
be provided at both ends of the rail. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 201 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.12.7.6 
Each adjustable hanger shall have a secondary restraint. See example below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.8.12.7.7 
Manufacturers’ recommendations for sway bracing shall be followed. 
3.8.12.7.8 
The use of rods threaded their full length in suspension hangers is prohibited. 
See example below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.8.12.8 
The maximum distance for a splice joint hanger to the splice joint shall comply 
with the equipment manufacturers' recommendations. 
3.8.12.9 
All hangers shall be installed true and vertical.  
3.8.12.10 
All suspension fittings, brackets and connections shall bear the imposed load of 
all equipment equally. 
3.8.12.11 
Where track is suspended from hangers, the track shall be braced laterally and 
longitudinally to prevent extensive sway. 
3.8.12.12 
Sway bracing shall not be installed or used to force runways into alignment. 
3.8.12.13 
Sway bracing shall not be installed to supplement the load supported by the 
hangers. (Failure to adhere to this requirement will present moment load and 
inertia to the building, trusses, and/or support structures) 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 202 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.12.14 
Where a rail system could inadvertently interfere with process operation, 
materials, or equipment in transit, the equipment supplier shall provide 
intermediate or mid rail stops complete with a redundant means of support to 
prevent a hazard to personnel or interference with the adjacent equipment or 
operation. 
3.8.13 
Enclosed Track Assembly 
3.8.13.1 
All bolts, nuts, fasteners, suspension brackets and attachments required for 
enclosed track assembly and installation of hoist, balancers, or similar equipment 
shall be supplied by the equipment manufacturer. 
3.8.13.2 
All enclosed track sections shall be saw cut square to required length per 
manufacturer requirements and specifications.  
3.8.13.3 
All joints and connections shall be subjected to the manufacturer’s specified 
tensile stress.  
3.8.13.4 
All joints and connections at track sections and end stops shall be flush and 
square when complete. 
3.8.13.5 
All track sections shall be pre-fit and match-marked to the adjacent mating 
section.  
3.8.13.6 
All end stops, trolleys, hanger brackets and attachments shall be installed in 
accordance with the manufacturer's installation drawings. 
3.8.14 
Enclosed Track System Installation 
3.8.14.1 
Selection of fasteners and required assembly procedures (including associated 
torque specifications and self-locking requirements) shall be implemented as 
specified in this document, by the original equipment manufacturer, and / or the 
assigned registered Professional Engineer (P.E.).  
3.8.14.2 
Joint modification or holes required in track sections shall be by drilling or saw 
cutting only.  
3.8.14.3 
The flame cutting of holes or splice joints shall be prohibited. 
3.8.14.4 
Enclosed track which is installed by the equipment supplier and which presents 
evidence of burning shall be rejected by Ford Motor Company and replaced by 
the equipment supplier at their expense. 
3.8.14.5 
Tension tubes at all splice connections, hangers and end stops shall be factory 
aligned and welded by the track manufacturer. 
3.8.14.6 
Under no conditions shall the equipment supplier attempt to weld or install the 
tension tubes in the field unless prior approval is obtained by both Ford Motor 
Company and the manufacturer.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 203 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.14.7 
All trolley running surfaces shall be aligned and flush in accordance with this 
specification and shall not present shock loading of load wheels, bearings or 
guide wheels at a splice connection. 
3.8.14.8 
All rail systems shall be installed straight, parallel, level and at the same 
elevation. Installation tolerance shall not exceed the values specified below: 
3.8.14.9 
Longitudinal leveling, systems with multiple runways and single rail systems shall 
be level to within 0.250" (6mm) in any support span. The maximum rate of 
change shall be no more than 0.125" (3mm) on 20” (508mm) centers. 
3.8.14.10 
Elevation (runway to runway), bridge systems shall be level to within 0.250" 
(6mm) in span of the bridge. The maximum rate of change shall be no more than 
0.125" (3mm) in any support span. 
3.8.14.11 
Centering runway to runway shall be within 0.1875" (5mm) in overall length of the 
system. The maximum rate of change shall be no more than 0.125" (3mm) on 20' 
(6.0 meters) rail centers. 
3.8.14.12 
Centering for single rail systems, which are to parallel a conveyor or workstation, 
shall be centered to the parallel delivery system to within ±0.1875" (5mm) of the 
monorail system overall length. The maximum rate of change shall be no more 
than 0.125" (3mm) in any support span. 
3.8.14.13 
The equipment supplier shall replace all tension connection and self-locking 
fasteners, which are removed for any reason, with new fasteners provided by the 
original equipment manufacturer. 
3.8.15 
Patented Rail System Design 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.8.15.1 
The use of standard flange "I" beam is prohibited. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 204 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.15.2 
The equipment manufacturer shall provide a manufacturer’s drawing, for each 
piece of equipment provided.  
3.8.15.3 
The drawing shall indicate loads, hanging method, radius design, hangers, splice 
plate attachments, piece marks, and fastener torques. 
3.8.15.4 
The manufacturer’s drawing shall be reviewed and certified as acceptable by the 
equipment manufacturer’s registered professional engineer.  
3.8.15.5 
The equipment supplier shall submit to Ford Motor Company certified structural 
steel and building steel reinforcement drawings. These drawings shall indicate 
loads, hanging method, hanger centers, bracing, reinforcing and structural steel 
design. 
3.8.15.6 
The load carrying (tension) flange width shall be a minimum 3.25” (~82.55mm) 
for all applications. 
 
3.8.15.7 
The minimum hardness of the tension flange running surface shall be 225 / 
Brinell. 
3.8.15.8 
The allowable stresses to the load carrying tension flange shall not exceed 20 
percent of the minimum ultimate tensile strength of the materials used.  
This requirement will also apply to all casting, forging, brackets, stamping and 
attachments. 
3.8.15.9 
The allowable stresses in the compression flange of patented rail shall be 
determined in accordance with the rail manufacturer’s recommendation. In no 
case shall the calculated value exceed 0.6 of the yield strength of the material 
used. 
3.8.15.10 
The runway, bridge crane or hoist rail span(s) shall be in accordance with the 
allowable stresses, load and deflection criteria specified.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 205 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.15.11 
Bridges with a span of 10'-0" (3050mm) or greater shall have diagonal bracing 
from the bridge beam to the end trolley. 
 
3.8.15.12 
When the bridge is to be provided with electrified rail, powered trolley(s), 
festoons for services then the current requirements of the electrical specification 
shall apply. 
3.8.15.13 
In no case shall the lateral or overhung weight of the service median be allowed 
to induce deflection, compression load or lateral tension into the load rail. 
3.8.15.14 
The depth of the web cross section for a patented rail application shall be based 
on applied stress, concentrated wheel loads at the load carrying tension flange, 
span of the rail section, and deflection requirements. 
3.8.15.15 
The minimum depth for the web cross section for a patented rail application shall 
be no less than 4” (100mm). 
3.8.15.16 
All wheel loads shall compensate for imposing stresses on the load carrying 
flange when transferring from a load rail to its mating rail section. 
3.8.15.17 
All end trucks provided for patented rail shall maintain a ratio of span to wheel 
base of not less than 10:1 for spans up to 40’ (12.0 meters) and 8:1 for spans 
over 40’ (12.0 meters). 
3.8.15.18 
All patented rail shall be suspended by Ford Motor Company specified minimum 
four bolt hanger connections.  
3.8.16 
Patented Rail Turns 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 206 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.16.1 
The radius of all curved monorail sections shall be designed to allow the trolleys 
to negotiate the horizontal turns without binding. 
3.8.16.2 
All curved rail sections shall be factory formed, pre-assembled and match 
marked to their mating section. All patented rail mating sections and joint(s) shall 
be stenciled by the patented rail manufacturer prior to shipment. 
3.8.16.3 
The standard radius for all turns shall be 6’-0” (1829mm). Radius turns of less 
than 6’-0” (1829mm) shall only be used with the approval of Ford Motor 
Company. 
3.8.16.4 
When short radius turns of less than 6’-0” (1829mm) must be used, the 
manufacturer’s load trolley chording requirements shall be submitted for review. 
In no case shall the radius be less than 1½ times the wheel base or pin centers. 
3.8.16.5 
The overhang of straight rail to tangent point of a horizontal curve shall be no 
more than 14” (350mm). 
3.8.16.6 
A minimum of one hanger shall be used in the radius of each curve in addition to 
a hangar at each splice joint.  
3.8.16.7 
The maximum distance between each hanger center shall not exceed 3’-0” 
(915mm). Additional hangers shall be provided in accordance with the imposed 
wheel and carrier loads, localize stress, and to suit calculated and specified 
deflection parameters. 
3.8.16.8 
Offset or S curves shall maintain a minimum of 1-1/2 times the wheel base 
centers or pin centers between each tangent point unless approved by Ford 
Motor Company. 
3.8.16.9 
All special curve sections shall be supported, and sway braced in accordance 
with the requirements of the registered engineer and the patented rail 
manufacturer.

 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.17 
Trolleys for Patented Rail Systems 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.8.17.1 
All trolleys provided to Ford Motor Company for a patented rail application shall 
comply with; 
• The safety standards of the U.S. Occupational Safety and Health 
Administration (OSHA),  
• Code of Federal Regulation Book lbs. 29, subsections 1900 to 1910 (latest 
version) 
• ISO Standard 16881-1:2005 Cranes -- Design Calculation for Rail Wheels 
and Associated Trolley Track Supporting Structure -- Part 1: General 
Reference 
3.8.17.2 
All trolleys provided to Ford Motor Company for a patented rail application shall 
comply with ASTM international specification D-149, Dielectric Strength Testing. 
3.8.17.3 
The use of a two-wheel single head trolley shall not be allowed for any rail 
application.  
3.8.17.4 
All trolleys provided shall include safety lugs.  
3.8.17.5 
Safety lugs shall be installed on each side of each rail so that if a failure occurs, 
the rail is centrally loaded about the vertical axis. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 208 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.17.6 
Bolted and/or integral safety lugs shall be provided with all end trucks and 
trolleys utilized for all patented rail applications. 
 
 
 
 
 
3.8.17.7 
All end trucks, carriers, trolleys, etc., with flangeless wheels shall be provided 
with rigidly mounted side guide rollers. 
3.8.17.8 
All end trucks, carriers, etc., provided for patented rail shall include articulating 
trolleys attached to a solid one-piece end truck frame with extensions and energy 
absorbing bumpers. 
3.8.17.9 
All trolley wheels shall be machined symmetrically true with the axis of rotation. 
3.8.17.10 
All trolley wheels shall be drop forged and heat treated with hardened steel 
treads. Trolley wheels shall have a minimum tread hardness of 425 Brinell. 
3.8.17.11 
All trolley wheels shall have heat treated alloy steel axles.  
3.8.17.12 
All trolley wheels shall be designed and sized to accommodate the tension flange 
of the rail being used.  
3.8.17.13 
All trolley wheels shall be axle mounted and shall include a single row of high-
quality precision ground ball bearings. 
3.8.17.14 
All wheel bearings and thrust bearings shall have grease and dust seals.  
3.8.17.15 
All bearings shall be pre-lubricated prior to delivery. 
3.8.17.16 
Rim type or flange type wheels are to be provided. In lieu of side guide style 
trolleys, then the wheels shall be heat treated, hardened and machine concentric 
to the axis of rotation. 
3.8.17.17 
Rim or flange type wheels shall be provided with machine alloy axles, precision 
anti-friction and anti-thrust full ball type or roller type sealed bearings with 
symmetrical inner and outer races. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 209 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.17.18 
All trolley axles shall be symmetrically true and shall accommodate anti-friction 
bearings. 
3.8.17.19 
Axles provided for trolley wheel attachment shall include a stud type connection 
which will bolt to the trolley body.  
3.8.17.20 
The axle provided shall include castellated nuts and retainers external to the 
trolley body to act as a retainer and prevent rotation of the axle.  
3.8.17.21 
Grease zerk fittings and channels shall be integral to the axle for lubrication 
purposes. 
3.8.17.22 
All trolley side guide rollers shall be machined symmetrically true with the axis of 
rotation and shall provide double row, full ball complement, precision, ground 
thrust type ball bearings. 
3.8.17.23 
Use of a light capacity trolley on a heavy flange track shall be prohibited. 
3.8.17.24 
Where application requires a single trolley to act as the primary means of support 
for a balancer, process tool, bridge rail or hoist, the equipment manufacturer shall 
provide safety chain straps with forged steel shackle and castellated retainers to 
act as a redundant securing location for the primary connection. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.8.17.25 
The safety chain strap and safety shackle shall be totally independent of the 
primary connection point and shall pass through each trolley body at an isolated 
location. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 210 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.18 
Festoon Trolleys for Patented Rail 
3.8.18.1 
Festoon Trolleys – 4” (100mm) through 6” (150mm) Non-Powered Patented Rail 
Applications shall only be utilized for suspending hose and cable. 
 
3.8.18.2 
The rated load capacity for each festoon trolley shall be minimum 250lbs 
(115kg). 
3.8.18.3 
Trolley assemblies provided shall be sized to suit either 3.25” or 3.33” (or metric 
sizing equivalent) tension flanges. 
3.8.18.4 
The minimum yield strength of the trolley assembly shall be 1500lbs (680kg). 
3.8.18.5 
The minimum ultimate tensile strength of each trolley assembly shall be 1900lbs 
(860kg). 
3.8.18.6 
Trolley wheels and bearings shall be rated for 375lbs. (170kg) at 100 rpm. 
3.8.18.7 
Trolley assemblies shall be provided with anti-kick-up wheels or bearings. 
 
3.8.18.8 
Trolley assemblies shall be provided with flange type, machined wheels with 
sealed ball bearings. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 211 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.18.9 
Trolley assemblies shall be provided with integral bumpers and drop stops. 
3.8.18.10 
Trolley assemblies, which are not a flange type, shall be provided with side guide 
rollers. 
3.8.19 
Patented Rail End Stops 
3.8.19.1 
The equipment manufacturer shall provide two “redundant” fixed end stops for all 
bridges, monorails, hoists, tool rail, service rail or similar overhead systems. 
3.8.19.2 
Redundant end stops, one primary and one secondary, shall be installed at each 
end of each runway or track section.  
3.8.19.3 
End stops provided shall be designed and mounted so there is not direct shear 
transmitted to the supporting bolts upon impact. 
3.8.19.4 
End stops designed to engage end truck or trolley wheels will not be accepted. 
3.8.19.5 
Where standard flange rail is provided, bevel washers shall be used to insure 
parallel bolting surfaces to the stop and I-beam rail. 
3.8.19.6 
All stops which are installed in the web of the rail shall be of sufficient size to 
contact the trolley, castings, end truck or load bar bumpers only.  
3.8.19.7 
Wheel to stop contact shall be prohibited. 
3.8.19.8 
End stops on bridge runways shall be installed with impact flanges carefully 
aligned and parallel to insure equal loading in the event of contact by end truck, 
load bar or carriers. 
3.8.19.9 
All secondary stops provided shall be positioned so that contact only occurs with 
complete failure of the primary stop. 
3.8.19.10 
The minimum standard for a typical primary and secondary stop connected to the 
tension flange of the rail shall be two separate stops, each providing a minimum 
of two 2 - ½”-13 (12.5mm), grade 5 bolts, with slotted nuts, cotter pins and safety 
cable. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 212 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.19.11 
The minimum standard for a typical primary and secondary stop connected to the 
web of the rail shall be two separate stops. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Each stop will provide a minimum of two 5/8” – 11 (16mm), grade 5 bolts, with 
slotted nuts, cotter pins and safety cable. 
3.8.19.12 
Where application requires the use of energy absorbing end stops on standard 
flange I-beam or patented rail, the minimum standard for the primary stop shall 
be one four-bolt stop with replaceable neoprene, polyurethane or equal bumper 
and a minimum of four 1/2” – 13 (12.5mm), grade 5, bolts with slotted nuts, cotter 
pins and safety cable. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 213 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
The secondary stop shall be flange of web mounted with a minimum of two 1/2” - 
13 (12.5mm) or 5/8” – 11 (16mm), grade 5, bolts with slotted nuts and cotter pins. 
The secondary stop need not be energy absorbing. 
3.8.20 
Patented Rail Safety Cable for End Stops 
3.8.20.1 
Safety cables shall be new for all applications. 
3.8.20.2 
All primary stops provided shall be installed with a secondary means of support. 
One 1/8” (3.2mm) stainless steel safety cable with two wire rope clips are 
considered acceptable.  
3.8.20.3 
The safety cable shall prevent the primary end stop from falling in case of failure 
or loosening of the bolt connection. 
3.8.20.4 
The safety cable shall serve to indicate that the primary stop has failed.  
3.8.20.5 
The safety cable shall pass through the primary and secondary stop or track 
web.  
3.8.20.6 
The safety cable shall be positioned to avoid strand damage or impact by normal 
end truck or trolley actuation. 
3.8.20.7 
The equipment manufacturer shall not install safety cables which are frayed, 
jagged or flame cut. 
3.8.21 
Patented Rail End Stop Bolts and Nuts  
3.8.21.1 
Bolts provided shall be of sufficient length to exclude the threads of the bolts from 
the shear plane. 
3.8.21.2 
Bolts provided shall be installed true to the horizontal or vertical plane. 
3.8.21.3 
Bolts provided shall be sized in accordance with the application and rail size. 
3.8.21.4 
All bolts and nuts shall be properly torqued to the rail manufacturers' 
specifications. 
3.8.21.5 
Bolts mounted vertically shall be inserted from the top down and with nuts on the 
bottom. 
3.8.21.6 
Bolt holes which are placed in the tension flange of patented rail shall be to the 
rail manufacturers' specification. 
3.8.21.7 
Bolt holes for all secondary stops shall be centered a minimum 1½” (38mm) from 
the edge of the rail. 
3.8.21.8 
The diameter of holes provided for end stop application shall not exceed 0.032” 
(1.0mm) over the bolt diameter. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 214 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.21.9 
Bolt holes in the flange or web section of the rail shall be factory drilled by the 
manufacturer, or center punched and drilled by the equipment manufacturer. 
Flame cutting in not permitted. 
3.8.22 
Patented Rail System Suspension Mounting Design 
3.8.22.1 
The load rail shall be supported at each joint from the superstructure or truss 
panel point with an approved clamp or a vertical hanger.  
3.8.22.2 
For rail mounted from a floor mounted structure see GVOSS Section 3.11 for 
additional requirements. 
3.8.22.3 
All vertical hangers at track joints shall be sway braced in each direction, parallel 
and at right angles. 
3.8.22.4 
Lateral bracing shall be installed at each support hanger suspension point.  
3.8.22.5 
Horizontal centers for lateral sway bracing shall not exceed 16’-0” (~5.0 meters) 
for any application. 
3.8.22.6 
With both the approval of the equipment manufacturer’s professional engineer 
and the rail manufacturer, the equipment manufacturer may choose to provide 
bridge bracing or sway bracing fabricated with standard structural steel sections. 
Under no conditions shall the size of the bracing section be less than 2” x 2” x 
5/16” (50 x 50 x 8mm) angle. 
3.8.22.7 
Between splices or joints, the runway rails shall be supported from the 
superstructure or truss panel point in accordance with imposed and localized 
stresses at the compression and tension flange(s) and specified deflection 
criteria. All vertical hangers shall be sway braced at right angles. 
3.8.22.8 
The gap at each patented rail joint shall be held to 0.0625” (~1.6mm) in all axis 
directions. There shall be no more than 0.500” (12.5mm) variance to level for the 
entire length of the system.  
3.8.22.9 
The maximum gap between the ends of the load carrying wheel treads or tension 
flange shall not exceed “0.0312” (1.0mm). 
3.8.22.10 
For bridge crane or hoist applications, the maximum overhang of the bridge 
girder to the center of a runway rail shall not exceed 16” (405mm). This value 
shall include area for the redundant end stops. Where telescoping systems are 
specified, each system shall be qualified based on each application. 
3.8.22.11 
Where double bridge rails are provided to suit, i.e., articulating arms, load bar or 
process tool framework, then the bridge system provided shall incorporate 
articulating trolleys, and diagonal bracing. 
3.8.22.12 
All end trucks, frames, process tool fixtures and load bars shall be designed to 
evenly distribute the carrying load between front and rear load carrying trolley(s). 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 215 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.22.13 
Splice plates for all runways or monorail applications shall be provided by the 
patented rail manufacturer and shall be utilized to cover the web at all patented 
rail joints. Each joint connection shall be bolted design only. Spare alignment 
clamps and fasteners shall be provided in accordance with the manufacturer’s 
design and shall be turned over to Ford Motor Company to suit future 
maintenance purposes. Two sets of spare alignment plates and a complete set of 
all fasteners for each plate or clamp shall be provided for each of ten 
connections. 
3.8.22.14 
All bolts, nuts, fixtures and attachments required for patented rail assembly shall 
be provided by the equipment manufacturer. The use of equipment manufacturer 
provided fasteners will not be accepted. 
3.8.23 
Patented Rail Installation  
3.8.23.1 
All patented rails shall be transit leveled for proper elevation, centered and 
aligned prior to installation of all anti-sway bracing. 
3.8.23.2 
When the bridge, hoists, monorail, or related equipment are to be provided with 
electrified rail, a dedicated festooning rail, or similar service equipment, then the 
equipment manufacturer shall provide the original manufacturer’s attachment and 
hangers for the service rail in accordance with the original manufacturer’s 
requirements. 
3.8.23.3 
All patented rails shall be suspended, brought to proper elevation, aligned and 
leveled with an engineer’s instrument. This work shall be accomplished prior to 
installation of anti-sway bracing. Transverse tilt of the rail sections will not be 
accepted by Ford Motor Company. 
3.8.23.4 
All patented rails, bridges, hoists, runways, monorails and track shall be installed 
straight, parallel, level and at the same elevation.  
3.8.23.5 
Longitudinal leveling for multiple runways and single rail systems shall be 0.500” 
(12.5mm) in overall length with a maximum rate of change of no more than 
0.250” (6.35mm) on 20’ (6.0 meter) centers. Leveling will be verified on the 
running tread at the tension flange. 
3.8.23.6 
Unless otherwise specified by Ford Motor Company, elevation from runway to 
runway shall not exceed 0.250” (6.35mm) in width of the span of the bridge, with 
a maximum rate of change no more than 0.125” (3.2mm) on 20’ (6.0 meter) 
span. 
3.8.23.7 
Centering from runway to runway shall be 0.250” (6.35) in overall length, with a 
maximum rate of change of no more than 0.125” on 20’ (6.0 meter) centers. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 216 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.23.8 
Centering for single rail systems, which parallel a conveyor or workstation, shall 
maintain a center distance of ±0.500” (12.5mm) over the length of the complete 
monorail system to the adjacent delivery equipment. The maximum rate of 
change shall not exceed ±0.125” (±3.2mm) on 20’ (6.0 meter) centers parallel to 
the workstation. 
3.8.23.9 
Sway bracing provided shall not be installed to force runways into alignment. 
3.8.23.10 
Flame cutting of track shall be prohibited.  
3.8.23.11 
All fabrication shall be saw cutting or drilling only. 
3.8.23.12 
All rail installed by the equipment manufacturer, which presents evidence of 
flame cutting, shall be replaced by the equipment manufacturer at his expense. 
3.8.23.13 
All running tread shall be flush, square and level upon completion.  
3.8.23.14 
Grinding of the running tread surface or tension flanges shall be prohibited. 
3.8.23.15 
The running tread of the load carrying tension flanges are not to be painted. 
3.8.23.16 
If fasteners (bolts, nuts, washers, safety cables, etc.) are removed for any 
reason, then all fasteners shall be replaced with new for each occurrence. 
3.8.23.17 
All fasteners provided shall be identical to those removed and meet the original 
equipment specifications. 
3.8.24 
Balancers 
3.8.24.1 
All balancers provided shall be identified with, as a minimum, the manufacturer's 
serial number, date of manufacture, balancer model number, and rated capacity. 
3.8.24.2 
All balancers, fasteners and attachments provided, including casting, forging, 
stampings and weldments, shall be rated so that stresses applied to the 
balancer, connections or attachments do not exceed 12 percent of the material's 
minimum ultimate strength. 
3.8.24.3 
All balancers should provide a means to minimize cable wear, e.g. roller type 
guides. 
3.8.24.4 
All bearings and springs provided in the balancers shall be permanently 
lubricated, sealed type. 
3.8.24.5 
The balancers shall be lubricated prior to shipment. 
3.8.24.6 
All balancers and attachments provided shall be supplier tested for rated load 
and stamped to indicate manufacturer's acceptance. All testing shall be 
performed prior to shipment of the equipment. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 217 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.24.7 
All components provided shall insure test strength is equal to or greater than the 
strength of the spring or air balancer load cable. 
3.8.24.8 
All balancer hooks, lifting hooks, shanks, shackles, lugs or bosses provided with 
the balancers shall be drop forged, heavy duty with safety type locking throats. 
3.8.24.9 
To minimize stress loading of the balancer housing (and optimize tool movement) 
the primary attachment point to a bridge, support structure or a trolley for spring 
type balancers shall include swivel and / or rotation capability. 
3.8.24.10 
All balancers provided shall be equipped with an independent, redundant safety 
support, boss, lug or fittings. The redundant safety support shall be integral to the 
balancer, frame, weldment, stamping or forging. 
3.8.24.11 
The redundant safety support system shall be completely independent of the 
balancers primary support system. 
3.8.24.12 
All components, redundant and primary safety supports shall be capable of 
supporting at a minimum, 2 times the combined weight of the balancer, rated 
load and all attachments. 
3.8.24.13 
All redundant safety supports shall be securely attached to the balancer frame 
with the opposite end attached to an independent support member or dedicated 
trolley. 
3.8.24.14 
Under no condition shall torsional stresses or twisting be transmitted to the upper 
support frame and attachments as a result of the lack of rotation at the balancers 
main cable. 
3.8.24.15 
Where balancers are provided with both primary and lower hook assemblies, 
they both shall comply with GVOSS Section 3.9. 
3.8.24.16 
Where balancers are provided with a rigid primary connection to a trolley or 
structure, all primary load hook assemblies, housings, tie bars, bushings, load 
bars and attachments shall be provided by the equipment manufacturer.  
3.8.24.17 
The equipment manufacturer shall also provide all tie bar fasteners, castellated 
nuts, forgings and attachments.  
3.8.24.18 
The use of cast or malleable attachment or connection cable connection devices 
is prohibited. 
3.8.24.19 
Where field installation of attachments and fasteners are required, then both the 
balancer and trolley manufacturer shall determine and provide to the installer, the 
required tension and torque specifications. 
3.8.24.20 
Support trolleys and all components provided by the equipment manufacturer for 
use in a balancer application shall only be those which are qualified and in 
accordance with all requirements of this specification. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 218 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.25 
Air Balancers 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.8.25.1 
Air balancers shall incorporate an integral automatic safety system which shall: 
• Prevent load drop and cable travel in the event of air loss. 
• Control cable travel into the drum in the event of cable failure or loss of load 
(retract control). Total travel shall be limited to ~3”.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 219 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.26 
Spring Balancers 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.8.26.1 
All "manual" tension adjusting devices shall be lockable and located external to 
the balancer for ease of adjustment. Balancers which require external clamping 
to perform tension adjustment shall not be accepted. 
3.8.26.2 
All spring balancers shall incorporate an integral automatic safety system which 
shall prevent load drop and cable travel in the event of spring failure. 
3.8.26.3 
All spring balancers shall incorporate an integral Cable Arrest safety feature to 
eliminate cable flybacks caused by cable failures or accidental tool detachments. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 220 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.27 
Retractors 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.8.27.1 
Retractors shall be used in applications where it is required to retract a tool or 
load when no force is applied to it. Constant downward force has to be applied in 
order to keep the suspended tool or load in position. The torque output of the 
rewind spring increases as the cable is pulled out of the unit, retracting the 
suspended tool or load to the uppermost adjusted position when released. 
3.8.27.2 
Retractors shall be equipped with Automatic Safety Lock to arrest the load in 
place should the unit lose tension or in the event of a power spring failure.  
3.8.27.3 
Cable guides shall be hardened steel. 
3.8.27.4 
Retractors shall have an adjustable cable stop. 
3.8.27.5 
Retractors shall have a rubber shock absorber. 
3.8.27.6 
Retractors shall have a ratchet lock function that can be manually engaged or 
disengaged. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 221 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.28 
Balancer Trolleys  
3.8.28.1 
Rated load capacity shall be appropriate for the rated load of the balancer 
combined with the weight of the balancer. 
3.8.28.2 
Balancer trolleys shall meet the requirements of 3.8.9 and 3.8.17. 
3.8.29 
Balancer Installation 
3.8.29.1 
All pneumatic balancers shall be installed with an inline automatic drain and 40-
micron inline filters. 
3.8.29.2 
All pneumatic balancers shall be installed with an inline regulator.  
3.8.29.3 
The inline regulator shall be sized to limit inlet pressure to the balancer at 
maximum 100 psi. 
3.8.29.4 
All pneumatic balancers shall be installed without lubricators of any kind in 
accordance with the manufacturer's requirements.  
3.8.29.5 
Where additional process tools require lubrication and are attached or are 
installed adjacent to the balancer assembly, an independent (transparent) 2-
quart bowl oilers shall be used to service the process equipment only.  
In lieu of this, a separate filter, regulator and lubricator assembly may be installed 
inline after the balancers' air service tie-in point. 
3.8.29.6 
All balancers shall be installed perpendicular and parallel to the vertical axis.  
3.8.29.7 
All balancers shall be suspended directly over the operator's work area. 
3.8.29.8 
The balancer, all attachments, the complete process tool which the balancer 
supports or services, and all connections shall be installed prior to adjusting the 
balancer spring tension or pneumatic controls. 
3.8.29.9 
The installation shall include adjustment of the balancer assembly pneumatic or 
mechanical tension controls, spot brakes, speed adjustment, regulators and 
cable stops, when installation of process tools and all attachments are complete.  
3.8.29.10 
All tension, speed, brake, cable stop, and regulator adjustments shall be 
performed in accordance with the equipment manufacturer's requirements. 
3.8.29.11 
See Balancer Requirements for additional information on pneumatic wire rope 
balancer requirements and Spring Balancer Requirements for spring balancers 
and retractors. 
3.8.29.12 
The total weight of load shall not exceed the nameplate capacity of the balancer. 
3.8.29.13 
The angle of deflection from the bottom of the balancer to the load does not 
exceed 10 degrees in any direction. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 222 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.29.14 
The end of travel indicator (ball stop) is not to be repositioned from the factory set 
location and should be positioned approximately 25mm from the bottom of the 
cable/chain guide at the full up position. 
3.8.29.15 
A barrel nut, if supplied as a securing means for the cable-to-hook set up, is 
compliant with this standard with regards to capacity rating.  Adding additional 
clamps to the existing set up is NOT recommended and may serve to weaken 
the wire rope. 
3.8.29.16 
The balancer, all attachments, the complete process tool which the balancer 
supports or services, and all connections shall be installed prior to adjusting the 
balancer spring tension or pneumatic controls. 
3.8.30 
Hoists 
3.8.30.1 
General Hoist Requirements 
3.8.30.1.1 
All hoists provided and all hoist installation shall comply with The Ford Motor 
Company. Manufacturing Standards and all rules, regulations and codes required 
to comply with all local, municipal and federal requirements. 
3.8.30.1.2 
All hoists provided shall be designed to comply with applicable sections and the 
latest edition of the following design standards. 
• 
HMI Hoist Manufacturer’s Institute 
• 
ANSI American National Standards Institute, Inc. 
• 
OSHA Occupational Safety and Health Administration 
• 
AGMA American Gear Manufacturing Association 
• 
AWS American Welding Society 
• 
MHSA Mining Health and Safety Administration 
• 
ASTM American Society for Testing and Materials 
• 
CEN EN 14492-1:2006+A1:2009 
3.8.30.1.3 
All load bearing parts shall be designed to provide a minimum of a 5:1 design 
safety factor. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 223 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.30.1.4 
The hoisting equipment provided by the manufacturer and installed by the 
equipment manufacturer shall comply with hoist duty classification HMI Class H-3 
service or better. See below for reference; 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.8.30.1.5 
The hoists and the means of suspension supplied with the hoist shall be 
designed to withstand all stresses imposed under normal operating conditions 
and while handling loads within the rated capacity times the required safety 
factor(s). 
3.8.30.1.6 
The load suspension, and all parts and attachments shall be designed so that the 
static stress calculated for the rated load shall not exceed 20% of the minimum 
ultimate material strength.  
3.8.30.1.7 
Elements specifically provided to give a visible warning of severe overload by 
structural deformation shall be designed so that the static stress calculated for 
the rated load shall not exceed 30 percent of the minimum ultimate material 
strength. 
3.8.30.1.8 
All hoists provided for use in a Class 1 Zone 1 hazardous area shall be equipped 
with the additional safety equipment and devices highlighted in this specification 
and in accordance with all ANSI and NFPA codes and relevant statutory and 
local Regulatory standards.

3.8.30.1.9 
All hoists which are manually controlled shall include in a readable position, at 
the pendant or controls, the following mandatory warning legend. The legend 
shall be permanently affixed and shall be designed to bring to the operator’s 
attention, warning(s) with cautionary language which shall state the following 
practices as unacceptable (This shall be supplied the hoist manufacturer) 
 
3.8.30.1.10 Under no condition shall a hoist be installed on a supporting structure which has 
a rated load capacity that is less than the rated load capacity of the hoist and all 
attachments. 
3.8.30.1.11 The rated load of the hoist shall be permanently marked on the hoist mechanism. 
The rated load identification shall be clearly legible for all operating conditions. 
3.8.30.1.12 The horsepower/Kilowatt rating of motor requirements for lifting shall be equal to 
the horsepower/Kilowatt rating required for lifting the hoist capacity and all 
attachments at maximum speed times a safety factor of 1.5. 
3.8.30.1.13 Unless otherwise specified, link chain hoists shall be used for load capacity up to 
and including 1000 lb. (450kg). 
3.8.30.1.14 Unless otherwise specified, cable hoists shall be used for 1000 lb. (450kg) 
capacity and up. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 225 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.30.1.15 The hoist application will determine whether an air or electric hoist will be used 
for loads in excess of 1000 lb. (450kg) capacity and shall be pre-approved by the 
hoist manufacturer, the equipment manufacturer’s registered professional 
engineer and Ford Motor Company prior to procurement. 
3.8.30.1.16 The hoist speed shall be determined by the maximum production rate cycle time 
for the operation or process performed. 
3.8.30.1.17 The normal operation of the hoist shall be controlled by an operator using the 
push-button pendant. 
3.8.30.1.18 All “manual” controls shall immediately return to the “off” position when released 
and all hoist motion shall immediately stop. 
3.8.30.1.19 Each control pendant, thumb lever, pushbutton, etc., which is provided to control 
any hoist motion, shall clearly identify and indicate to the hoist operator the 
direction of resulting motion. 
3.8.30.1.20 The function of each switch shall be cast, stamped or molded into each switch. 
3.8.30.1.21 The hoists provided shall be permanently marked with the manufacturer’s name 
and unit identification. Additional information provided shall include the serial 
number, model, type, date manufactured, duty service classification, rated 
amperage or required air pressure criteria. 
3.8.30.1.22 All hoists provided shall be new and of current design so that replacement parts 
shall remain completely interchangeable within the original hoisting assembly. 
3.8.30.1.23 All gears and pinions shall be mounted on precision ball or roller bearings. 
Bearings on the motor shaft, drum and the load bottom blocks shall be totally 
shielded and life-time lubricated. 
3.8.30.1.24 Ford Motor Company shall reject, and the contractor shall replace any load chain 
which shows evidence of prior use or exposure to elements and corrosion.

3.8.30.2 
Electric Hoist Requirements 
3.8.30.2.1 
At a minimum, all electric hoists shall be heavy duty, lug mounted and two-part 
double cable type with pendant push-button control. 
3.8.30.2.2 
All hoist frames shall be designed and constructed to hold all moving parts in 
rigid 
alignment 
and 
in 
accordance 
with 
equipment 
manufacture 
recommendations and applicable standards See section 3.8.29.1.2. 
3.8.30.2.3 
Anti-friction bearings shall be provided throughout the hoist mechanism. 
3.8.30.2.4 
All hoist shafts and gears shall be alloy steel heat treated to insure wear 
resistance. 
3.8.30.2.5 
A mechanically operated internal brake to hold the position of the load shall be 
provided for all applications. 
3.8.30.2.6 
An electronically operated brake shall be provided on the motor. 
3.8.30.2.7 
Drums provided shall be complete with machined grooves and in accordance 
with the current governing Industry Body Hoist Manufacturing standards 
specifications and wire rope manufacturer’s specifications. 
3.8.30.2.8 
Safety type load blocks with sheaves and safety load hook shall be mounted on 
anti-friction bearings. 
3.8.30.2.9 
The drum size shall allow for a minimum of two safety wraps of cable beyond the 
rated lift. 
3.8.30.2.10 The pitch diameter of the drum shall be in accordance with all hoist and cable 
manufactured requirements. 
3.8.30.2.11 Sheaves shall be free of all surface defects which could cause cable damage. All 
sheave rims shall run true to the axis of rotation. 
3.8.30.2.12 Sheaves shall be mounted as to be guarded against rope jamming under all 
operating conditions. All running sheaves shall be equipped with a means for 
lubrication. 
3.8.30.2.13 The pitch diameter of all running and non-running sheaves shall not be less than 
16 and 12 times the diameter of the wire rope. 
3.8.30.2.14 The pitch diameter and fleet angle of all sheaves, running and non-running, shall 
be in accordance with requirement of both the cable and hoist manufacturer(s). 
3.8.30.2.15 A split shaft shall be provided between the motor shaft and hoist gearbox. The 
shafts shall be connected with a coupling or a universal joint. A common shaft 
between the motor shaft and the hoist gearbox is not acceptable. 
3.8.30.2.16 An internal safety type and redundant over-travel switch shall be provided to 
prevent hoist over-travel in the event of primary control switch failure. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.30.2.17 A geared limit switch for normal up and down position stops shall be provided. 
3.8.30.2.18 The hoist motor provided shall be rated at 30 minutes minimum. 
3.8.30.2.19 The hoist motor provided shall be rated for jog and reversing capabilities. 
3.8.30.2.20 The hoist and hoist framework shall provide all fleet angle requirements in 
accordance with the hoist and cable manufacturer. 
3.8.30.2.21 The Power rating for electric motors provided shall be equal to the Power rating 
requirements for lifting the hoist capacity at a maximum speed with an efficiency 
factor of not more than 85 percent. 
 Load (Hoist Rating) x Maximum Speed 
 
33,000 x Efficiency 
3.8.30.2.22 All hoists shall be equipped with an electrically operated integral brake at the 
hoist motor. 
3.8.30.2.23 All hoists shall be equipped with a mechanically operated, self-adjusting load 
brake to hold position and control lowering of the hoists. 
3.8.30.2.24 Mounted securely to the top and bottom of hoist load frame shall be two metal 
plates. The metal plates shall be marked to identify the “load down” and “load up” 
direction of rotation. The metal plates shall be positioned to clearly identify the 
proper rotation of the drum for lowering and raising the load. This is mandatory to 
insure proper rotation and load brake operation. 
3.8.30.2.25 In addition to the internal overtravel limit(s), each hoist shall be equipped with 
one safety over travel limit switch operated directly off the load block. 
3.8.30.2.26 The normal operation of the hoist shall be controlled by an operator using the 
push-button pendant suspended from a balancer. 
3.8.30.2.27 Cable lubrication shall be maintained during equipment assembly at the 
manufacturer’s premises and during final installation by the equipment 
manufacturer or supplier at Ford Motor Company’s facility. The lubrication shall 
be in accordance with Ford Motor Company and the cable manufacturer’s 
requirements. 
3.8.30.2.28 All cable type hoisting equipment shall be covered for transportation and shall be 
stored internal to the assembly facility prior to installation.

3.8.30.3 
Air Hoists 
3.8.30.3.1 
All air hoists provided by the supplier shall be heavy duty, cylinder piston type or 
rotary vane type. All air hoists shall have floor-level operated, stepless pendant 
controls.  
3.8.30.3.2 
Hoist shall include a dust and moisture-proof frame constructed to hold all 
moving parts in rigid alignment and in accordance with equipment manufacture 
recommendations and applicable standards. See section 3.8.29.1.2. 
3.8.30.3.3 
Anti-friction thrust compensating bearings shall be used throughout the hoist 
mechanism. 
3.8.30.3.4 
All hoist shafts and gears shall be heat treated to insure wear resistance and 
meet AGMA Class 6 standards or better. 
3.8.30.3.5 
Link type load chain shall be pre-oiled, certified, proof-tested and precision 
pitched. 
3.8.30.3.6 
Chain Basket 
3.8.30.3.6.1 Chain basket shall be sized for maximum vertical lift. 
3.8.30.3.6.2 Secondary restraints shall be installed on chain baskets and shall be in 
compliance with the chain hoist manufacturers’ standards. 
3.8.30.3.7 
Hoists shall have both external and internal upper limit switches. 
3.8.30.3.8 
Hoists shall have an aluminum pendant with oil resistant hose of suitable length 
complete with strain cables. 
3.8.30.3.9 
All hosing and pendants shall be adjusted by the equipment manufacturer to suit 
the process operator’s preference. 
3.8.30.3.10 Hoist shall have an independent lug, integral boss, or detail as part of the hoist 
frame body dedicated as a means to restrain the hoist frame and load. In the 
event of primary attachment failure, the detail shall be totally independent of the 
primary attachment point. 
3.8.30.3.11 Hoist shall have a non-adjustable internal stall capability designed to limit lifting 
capability to the hoist rated load with all attachments. 
3.8.30.3.12 Seals shall be provided to eliminate leakage. 
3.8.30.3.13 The load brake shall be capable of immediately arresting and indefinitely holding 
the hoist rated load and all attachment times a minimum safety factor of 2.5 times 
the hoist rated load. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 229 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.30.3.14 The load brake shall be capable of immediately arresting and indefinitely holding 
the hoist rated load and all attachments time a safety factor of 2.5 in the event of 
gear, shaft and component or air service failure. 
3.8.30.3.15 The load brake shall provide ample thermal capacity to accommodate the 
frequency of operation required by the production process. 
3.8.30.3.16 The load brake shall be self-adjusting, internal and capable of fine positioning. 
3.8.30.3.17 The hoist duty classification shall compare the class H-3 service or better. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.8.30.3.18 All load bearing parts shall be designed to provide, at minimum, a 5 to 1 design 
factor. 
3.8.30.3.19 Each air hoist provided shall be capable of operating at rated capacity and duty 
cycle with plant air pressure as low as 65 psi (4.5 bar). 
3.8.30.3.20 All pendants provided shall operate satisfactory through a proven range from 20 
to 90 psi (1.4 to 6.2 bar) measured at the pendant. 
3.8.30.3.21 All air hoists provided shall be equipped with an upper hook travel limit, which will 
stop the hoist motions prior to bottoming the hook against the hoist framework. 
3.8.30.3.22 Where air hoists are provided with a chain travel limiting device to complement 
the operator or process function, a second overtravel limiting device shall be 
furnished to perform functions of the overtravel limit. 
3.8.30.3.23 Each air hoist shall include a mechanical brake, or braking system, capable of 
retaining the load and all attachments without danger to the operator or without a 
runaway condition. 
3.8.30.3.24 Each air hoist provided shall include a quick disconnect coupling, 150 psi, locking 
type, at the hoist for easy hoist removal.

Revision Date: June 08, 2025
