3.8.30.3.25 Each air hoist provided shall operate at rated capacity and speed with a dbA 
level of no more than 77 decibels when measured by a standard sound level 
meter set at slow response. 
3.8.30.4 
Rotary Vane Air Hoists – Additional Requirements 
Where rotary vane motors are specified, then in addition to the requirements 
previously stated in 3.8.29.3, the hoist provided will incorporate the requirements 
of this section. 
3.8.30.4.1 
Rotary hoist motors shall be totally sealed against dust, moisture and corrosive 
gasses. 
3.8.30.4.2 
Integral remote valves shall be incorporated to provide smooth starts and 
eliminate overrun. 
3.8.30.4.3 
Motors shall provide infinitely variable speeds for accurate load positioning. 
3.8.30.4.4 
The rotary vane motor shall operate at a rated capacity with air pressure as low 
as 65 psi (4.5 bar). 
3.8.30.4.5 
All connections shall be splined, and all parts shall be tested for hardness, 
accuracy, form and fit. 
3.8.30.4.6 
The entire hoist gear train and load brake assembly shall be lubricated in 
continuous oil bath. 
3.8.30.4.7 
All rotary vane hoists shall be equipped with two independent braking systems. A 
mechanical load brake and an air operated motor brake. The brake(s) provided 
shall automatically close in case of loss of air supply. 
3.8.30.5 
Hoist Reuse 
3.8.30.5.1 
When at Ford Motor Company’s request, the equipment manufacturer is to 
relocate, remove or reuse an existing hoist at a new location, then in addition to 
the 
requirements 
highlighted 
in 
paragraph 
3.8.29.1.3, 
the 
equipment 
manufacturer or original manufacturer shall perform a careful link chain 
inspection. 
3.8.30.5.2 
All hoists which are to be modified, re-rated, relocated or rebuilt, shall be 
inspected by the original manufacturer prior to initial use, certified as acceptable 
by the original manufacturer and shall be rebuilt or modified by the original hoist 
manufacturer(s) to suit the application. 
3.8.30.5.3 
The link chain shall be inspected on all relocated and reused chain hoist 
equipment. 
3.8.30.5.4 
The hoist chain shall be cleaned prior to inspection. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 231 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.30.5.5 
The hoist chain shall be visually checked for gouges, nicks, corrosion, weld 
splatter and distorted links. 
3.8.30.5.6 
The hoist chain shall be inspected for wear at each link contact point. 
3.8.30.5.7 
The hoist chain shall be carefully measured for chain stretch: 
• 
The equipment manufacturer shall measure a 24” (610mm) section of chain 
at the slack end of the chain or a comparable new piece of chain while under 
tension. 
• 
The equipment manufacturer shall measure a 24” (610mm) section of the 
used chain while suspended vertically and under tension. 
• 
A caliper type gauge shall be used to measure the same number of links in 
each 24” (610mm) section of chain. 
• 
If the 24” (610mm) section of used chain is 2-1/2 percent longer than the 
identical 24” (610mm) piece of unused chain, the chain and hoist shall be 
tagged as out of service. The hoist shall be turned over to the Plant Safety 
Department and the chain shall be replaced by the original hoist 
manufacturer. 
• 
If after inspection, the aforementioned items are not noted, the equipment 
manufacturer shall lubricate and install the hoist as contracted. 
 
3.8.30.6 
Hoist Load Hooks 
3.8.30.6.1 
All load hooks, top or bottom, regardless of hoist capacity, shall comply with 
GVOSS Section 3.8.1.7.10. 
3.8.30.6.2 
The load hooks provided shall be free to rotate 360 degrees when supporting the 
load unless otherwise specified by Ford Motor Company. 
3.8.30.6.3 
When Ford Motor Company requests that the equipment manufacturer remove, 
relocate or reuse hoisting equipment, then the equipment manufacturer shall 
inspect each load hook and hook attachment. 
3.8.30.6.4 
All hooks shall be inspected for evident crack(s). If cracks are noted, the hooks 
shall be scrapped. 
3.8.30.6.5 
Hooks shall be inspected for deformation. Those hooks possessing deformation 
which is in excess of ten percent of the normal throat opening shall be scrapped. 
3.8.30.6.6 
Hooks with more than an eight-degree twist from the plane of the unbent section 
shall be scrapped. 
3.8.30.6.7 
Hooks which are missing the “Pin-Loc” gate and return spring shall be scrapped. 
3.8.30.6.8 
Hooks possessing the above characteristics shall be permanently deformed, 
scrapped and replaced with new hooks by the equipment manufacturer. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 232 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.8.30.6.9 
If during his inspection, the equipment manufacturer should note hooks which 
possess cracks, twists or excessive throat openings as indicated above, then the 
hoist mechanism shall be rejected for future service, tagged and returned to the 
Plant Safety Department for additional inspection. 
3.8.30.7 
Hoist Cables, Attachments and Inspection 
3.8.30.7.1 
The hoist cables provided shall have a minimum safety factor of 5:1. 
3.8.30.7.2 
The hoist cable shall be improved plow steel, stranding shall be selected to 
provide maximum flexibility and wear resistance to suit the hoist capacity and 
application requirements. 
3.8.30.7.3 
The hoist manufacturer’s certified drawing showing the hoist reeving shall be 
submitted for review by Ford Motor Company. 
3.8.30.7.4 
Where hoist cable or the hoist cable core are exposed to environmental 
conditions under which the cable or core could be damaged, a suitable cable and 
core having resistance to the adverse condition shall be used. 
3.8.30.7.5 
The hoist cable shall be attached and anchored to the hoist and all lifting 
connections in a failsafe manner. The equipment manufacturer and equipment 
manufacturer’s design shall eliminate all possibilities of disengagement. 
3.8.30.7.6 
If hoisting cables run near enough to other components to make fouling or 
chafing likely in normal operation, then guards to eliminate these conditions shall 
be installed by the original equipment manufacturer. 
3.8.30.7.7 
Cable lubrication shall be maintained during equipment assembly at the 
manufacturer’s or equipment manufacturer’s premises and during final 
installation at Ford Motor Company’s facility. The lubrication shall be in 
accordance with Ford Motor Company and the cable manufacturer’s 
requirements. 
3.8.30.7.8 
After initial testing under load is accomplished and the hoist cables are under 
tension, the equipment manufacturer shall retighten, to the manufacturer’s 
recommended torque, all hoist cable fasteners. 
3.8.30.7.9 
Ford Motor Company’s representative will reject, and the contractor shall replace 
without cost to Ford Motor Company, any hoist cable which presents evidence of 
the following conditions: 
• 
Reduction of cable diameter due to loss of core support. 
• 
Internal or external corrosion due to improper storage or lack of proper 
lubrication. 
• 
Corroded or broken wires at the end connections. 
• 
Corroded, cracked, bent, worn or improperly applied end connections. 
• 
Kinked, crushed, improperly cut, twisted, broken or unstranded cable, or any 
other damage which results in distortion of the cable structure. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 233 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• 
Reduction of cable diameter from nominal diameter as specified by the 
equipment manufacturer. 
• 
Cables which have been subjected to flame cutting and present evidence of 
subsequent core deterioration. 
3.8.30.7.10 When Ford Motor Company requests that a cable hoist be removed, relocated or 
reused, then in addition to the requirements specified in paragraph 3.8.29.1.3, 
the hoist cable and all attachments shall be dismantled, removed and scrapped. 
All components shall be replaced completely with new and all work shall be 
performed under the direct supervision of the original equipment manufacturer. 
3.8.30.7.11 After installation of all wire rope cables by the equipment manufacturer, the 
equipment manufacturer shall procure and wrap tape around the dead end of the 
cable to retard strand fraying. The tape shall not be installed over a clamp, 
thimble or securing device. 
3.8.30.7.12 Should the equipment manufacturer or manufacturer remove or replace, for any 
reason, thimbles, clamps, load cable for balancers or other wire cable 
components, then all fasteners, cables, clamps and thimbles shall be replaced 
with new. The new components shall be in accordance with the requirements of 
the original manufacturer as well as all standards and specifications.

3.9 
SPECIAL MANUFACTURED HOOKS AND HOOK ASSEMBLIES  
3.9.1 
Scope 
This guideline provides Ford-unique requirements and specifications for special 
support or carry hooks and hook assemblies that are not commercially available.  
This includes items like engine carry bars and chain hung devices. 
3.9.2 
Definitions  
CERTIFIED HOOK: A hook that meets or exceeds this guideline and has proper 
documentation relating to proof test, hardness, and design factor. 
DESIGN CAPACITY: The value determined by the application of a design factor 
of four to the load. 
LATCH: A mechanical device used to close the throat opening of a hook.  
LOAD: The total weight imposed on the hook during use.  
NICK OR GOUGE: Sharp notch in hook surface which may act as a stress riser 
in the area of the notch.  
NONDESTRUCTIVE TEST: A test that does not destroy the functional use of the 
hook, such as but not limited to dye penetrate test, magnetic particle test, 
radiography test, and ultrasonic test.  
NORMAL SERVICE: Service that involves operating at less than 65% of rated 
load. 
PROOF LOAD: The specific load applied in performance of the proof test.  This 
is twice the rated load. 
PROOF TEST: A nondestructive load test made to verify the manufacturing 
integrity of the hook. 
RATED LOAD: The maximum allowable working load for application of the 
designed device for normal service.  This may also be referred to as the rated 
capacity or the working load limit. 
QUALIFIED PERSON: A person who, by possession of a recognized degree or 
certificate of professional standing, or who, by extensive knowledge, training, and 
experience, has successfully demonstrated the ability to solve problems relating 
to the subject matter and work. 
3.9.3 
Markings  
3.9.3.1 
Manufacturer’s name shall be forged, cast, or die stamped on a low stress and 
non-wearing area of the hook or hook assemblies for identification purposes.  
3.9.3.2 
All hooks and hook assemblies shall be serialized for purpose of traceability back 
to manufacturer, proof test, and type of steel. 
3.9.3.3 
All hooks and hook assemblies shall be marked with the rated load. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 235 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.9.3.4 
All support chain slings shall also be identified by a permanently affixed durable 
identification stating size, grade, rated capacity, and reach per 
• Occupational Safety & Health Administration (OSHA) 1910.184(e)(1). 
• ISO standard, ISO/TC 111/SC 1 Chains and chain slings 
• ISO standard, ISO/TC 111/SC 3 - Components and accessories 
3.9.4 
Construction  
3.9.4.1 
The hook and hook assembly material shall have sufficient ductility to 
permanently deform before losing the ability to support the workload within the 
temperature range at which the hook will be used. 
3.9.4.2 
 Attachments, such as handles, latch supports, etc., shall not be welded to a 
finished hook in the field. If welding of an attachment is required, it must be done 
at initial manufacturing or fabrication prior to any required final heat treat.  
3.9.4.3 
 Hooks and hook assemblies shall be designed to withstand the forces imposed 
by the rated load based on the yield strength of the hook and hook assembly 
material.  These hooks and hook assemblies must also be designed with a 
minimum design factor of four applied to the load.  This minimum design factor is 
also referred to as the design capacity.  Refer to Section 3.9.7.1 for an example.   
3.9.4.4 
Alloy Grade 80 chain shall be used for all new construction due to its special heat 
treatment and specific design for overhead lifting applications. 
3.9.4.5 
For all hooks and hook assemblies, the in-service position of the device shall be 
factored into rated load determination meaning the angles in which forces are 
applied must be considered.  For chains, the same consideration must be made. 
standard industrial Alloy Grade 80 data tables available to manufacturers. 
3.9.4.6 
Hooks and hook assemblies shall have a rated capacity at least equal to that of 
the alloy steel chain with which they are used, or the sling shall not be used in 
excess of the rated capacity of the weakest component. 
3.9.4.7 
Alloy steel chain slings shall not be used with loads in excess of the rated 
capacities prescribed by standard industrial Alloy Grade 80 data tables to 
manufacturers. Slings not included in these tables shall be used only in 
accordance with the manufacturer's recommendations. 
3.9.4.8 
All welding shall be done in accordance with ANSI/AWS D14.1. 
3.9.4.9 
Hooks and hook assemblies shall be designed to meet normal service 
conditions.  Normal service is defined as operations performed at less than 65% 
of the rated load. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 236 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.9.4.10 
The hook manufacturer shall perform and document a hardness test sample for 
each heat treat batch to assure hardness range compliance.  The hardness test 
must comply to an industrial standard such as Brinell, Rockwell, etc.  
3.9.4.11 
The manufacturer shall provide certification that the special manufactured hooks 
and hook assemblies have been proof tested in accordance with Paragraph 5.2 
of ANSI G61.1-1968 and shall have it available for examination.  Further test 
details are described in Section 3.9.4. 
3.9.4.12 
The requirement for a safety latch on a hook or hook assembly is limited to 
activities where side pulling forces occur frequently at the point of force 
application. 
3.9.4.13 
Hooks and hook assembly shall be evaluated using Computer-Aided Engineering 
(CAE) tools, such as Finite Element Analysis (FEA), to ensure that functional 
design intent is met within the expected usage environment thus allowing earlier 
evaluation and revision when necessary.  The appropriate Ford Product 
Development team and related CAE team are to be contacted for this noted 
review. 
3.9.5 
Testing of New Hooks and Hook Assembly (Proof Testing)  
3.9.5.1 
Hook and hook assemblies shall be proof tested in accordance with ASME B30.2 
or ISO 7597 and ISO 8539. Copies of proof test certificates shall be included with 
the tool when it is shipped to the site where it will be used. 
3.9.5.2 
Hook and hook assemblies shall not be subject to welding, weld spatter, cutting, 
grinding of significant nature, unless prior review and approval is received from 
the original equipment manufacturer.

SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

3.10 
BODY AND PAINT SKIDS  
NOTE:  See Section 2.8.3 for body pallet requirements for pallets used for 
geometry setting in the standard layout tooling. 
3.10.1 
General 
3.10.1.1 
This section pertains to the Ford Motor Company, BCE (Body Construction 
Engineering) requirements for skids used only in the Body Shop and skids that 
go through the Paint process. These are defined as: 
• 
Paint Skid –Two independent “I” beam rails held together by tie bars which 
are removable after paint or two independent “I” beam rails held together by 
tie bars which are not removable and transported back to the Body Shop 
after paint. 
• 
Body Skid –Two independent “I” beam rails, which are held together by tie 
bars which are welded in place or Steel Tubular Structure (Optional for some 
Body Shops) and are not to be used in the Paint process.

The Ford Motor Company BCE or VOME Paint department shall verify the skid

3.10.1.5 
The skid supplier shall have regular communications for the purpose of 
simultaneous engineering to exchange information relevant to the skid body rest 
and locator tooling. 
3.10.1.6 
All skid designs shall be in FIDES. 
3.10.2 
Tier1 Integrator Responsibilities 
3.10.2.1 
When Ford Motor Company BCE or VOME Paint selects a skid supplier through 
a competitive bid process, the appropriate department shall furnish the Tier1 
Integrator with the skid supplier information, scope of work, cost breakdown for 
skid to be used and the parameters used for the quotes (see 3.10.1.5 of this 
standard). 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 239 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.10.2.2 
When Ford Motor Company BCE or VOME Paint selects a skid supplier through 
a competitive bid process, the supplier shall include the skid cost provided in 
3.10.2.1 above, with integration costs in their quote. 
3.10.2.3 
When Ford Motor Company BCE or VOME Paint selects a skid supplier through 
a competitive bid process, the supplier shall issue the purchase order to the skid 
supplier for the cost provided in 3.10.2.1 above. 
3.10.2.4 
When Ford Motor Company BCE or VOME Paint selects a skid supplier through 
a competitive bid process, the supplier shall provide project management for skid 
compliance to Ford Motor Company standards, specifications and delivery. 
3.10.2.5 
When Ford Motor Company BCE or VOME Paint selects a skid supplier through 
a competitive bid process, change requests by the skid supplier shall be 
submitted by the supplier and approved and negotiated in accordance to Ford 
Motor Company policy. 
3.10.2.6 
The skid supplier shall design the skid body rest and locator tooling. 
3.10.2.7 
The skid supplier is responsible for obtaining sign-off of skid design from Ford 
Motor Co. Body shop, trim, paint, final manufacturing engineers, along with the 
above plant departments. 
3.10.2.8 
The skid supplier shall furnish a certification fixture for use to certify the body rest 
and locator tooling.  
3.10.2.9 
When a skid has a manual operator interface at the load or unload escapement, 
the skid supplier shall design the body rest and locator tooling in accordance with 
all GVOSS ergonomic requirements. 
3.10.2.10 
When a skid has an automatic or robotic interface at the load or unload 
escapement, the skid supplier shall verify their design interfaces properly with the 
other tooling equipment. 
3.10.2.11 
All change and deviation requests shall be submitted to Ford Motor Company. 
3.10.2.12 
The skid supplier shall maintain a log of all skid changes requested and 
approved. 
3.10.2.13 
The skid supplier is to take the lead on any 8D's relating to the skid throughout 
the skid life cycle. 
3.10.3 
Skid Supplier Responsibilities 
3.10.3.1 
The skid supplier shall supply two pre-production skids minimum for tryout and 
validation at the plant. The pre-production skid shall allow the skid to function in a 
manner identical to the final production skid in the assembly plant. 
3.10.3.2 
The skid supplier shall construct a critical path to monitor program conformance 
to delivery timing. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 240 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.10.3.3 
The skid supplier shall certify the body rest and locator tooling using the 
certification fixture furnished by the Tier1 Integrator.  
3.10.3.4 
The skid supplier shall buy-off the Tier1 Integrators' integration of the skid into 
their system prior to the Ford Motor Company BCE buy-off of the equipment. 
This shall be documented and included in Tab 15 of the Tier1 Integrator’s Buy-
Off Book. 
3.10.3.5 
The skid supplier shall oversee the proper handling of the skids to the final 
receiving facility.  
3.10.3.6 
The skid supplier shall report any damage to the program team. 
3.10.3.7 
The skid supplier is responsible for any cost incurred for repairing damage to the 
skid. 
3.10.3.8 
After completing repairs to a damaged skid, the program team shall buy-off the 
acceptance of repairs. 
3.10.4 
Paint Skid Mechanical 
NOTE:  Refer to 3.10.5 for Body skid mechanical details. 
3.10.4.1 
The following mechanical components are required for skid construction see 
Figure 3.10.4: 
1. Laser cut Skid Number on left hand side of the skid (prototype) – 1 inch in 
height.  
2. Laser cut bar code to the approved height (production skid) that will 
accommodate the receiving plant.  
3. Skid rails shall be “I” beam constructions; dimensions are 4.00 inches (depth) 
x 2.663 inches (width) x 0.326 inches (web thickness “Billy beam”) 
4. Vehicle body locating pins: 
• For HSS production steels the locating pins shall be SAE 4140 pre-
heat treated to no less than 30 HRC. 
• For production ultra-high strength steels with over 60 HRC, skids 
supplier shall use locating pins made of a steel substrate that is able 
to withstand the interface of these types of materials. 
5. Tie bar (when required); 
Tube in a tube (welded construction) 
Holds skid rails together during transfer 
1 or 2 tie bars required (application specific) 
6. Stake tie bar locator – forging 
7. End plate - forging; shall contain the following: 
Holes for chain attachments required for “e-coat” process. 
Steel ribs for strength

8. End plate - forging; shall contain the following: 
Holes for chain attachments required for “e-coat” process. 
Steel ribs for strength 
9. Risers (forging for production skid and weldment for prototype skid) 
10. Locator block steel or forging (if applicable); locates skid to skid cart at 
unload skid station. 
11. Locators and rests (forging for production skid and steel for prototype skid) 
NOTE: contact surface to part must not exceed an area of 2500.0 square 
mm (50.0 mm x 50.0 mm). Any deviation must be approved by VOME and 
VO paint department. 4.8 mm grooves required in top of rest surface to 
dissipate heat loss. 
Grooves on rests to be machined for pre-production, forged for production.

Option 1 – hook clamps (forging for production skid and weldment for 
prototype skid). Skid supplier shall use a steel substrate as defined in 
3.10.4.1 point 4. 
Option 2 – eye bolts (56ZF-xxxx)

How it holds vehicle body to skid during “e-coat” process

3.10.6 
Buy-Off for Skids 
3.10.6.1 
There shall be a buy-off event at the skid supplier’s facility prior to shipment to 
the plant. 
3.10.6.2 
The buy-off shall be the same as GVOSS Section 1.2.6 with the following: 
3.10.6.3 
Buy-Off Book Tabs 1, 3, 6, 7, and 24 are required. 
3.10.6.4 
The buy-off event shall consist of a Buy-Off Book review. 
3.10.7 
Certification Fixture 
3.10.7.1 
General 
3.10.7.1.1 
The skid supplier shall design and build the certification fixture. The purpose of 
the fixture is: 
• 
Certify skid rails for straightness and flatness.  
• 
Certify body rests and locators for location. 
3.10.7.1.2 
The basic portion of skids checking fixtures are to be painted Safety Blue 2.5PB 
3/10. Ref. VOSS Sections 1.4.8. 
3.10.7.1.3 
All changes and deviations shall be submitted to Ford Motor Company BCE. 
3.10.7.1.4 
The skid supplier shall maintain a log of all certification fixture changes requested 
and approved. 
3.10.7.1.5 
The skid supplier, upon receiving a change request shall provide a definitive 
response within five days. 
3.10.7.1.6 
There shall be a buy-off event of the certification fixture at the skid suppliers prior 
to shipment to the skid/pallet build shop. 
3.10.7.1.7 
The skid supplier shall oversee the proper handling of the certification fixture to 
the skid build shop. 
3.10.7.2 
Skid certification process 
3.10.7.2.1 
Use 1.5 mm, 3.0 mm, and 6.0 mm feeler checks where possible to check critical 
surfaces/pins where the skid contacts the body. 
3.10.7.2.2 
Skid rail is netted at the front-end cap to the net plate on the skid certification 
fixture and measured at the rear end cap to validate length via a feeler gauge. 
 
1. The rear plate for the skid certification fixture    must be designed to allow 
for certification of maximum and minimum length. 
 
2. An 8mm feeler for minimum length (shortest allowable skid at 3mm short) 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 246 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
to be utilized at the rear end cap.  
 
3. A 2mm feeler for maximum length (longest allowable skid at 3mm long) to 
be utilized at the rear end cap.  
3.10.7.2.3 
Three net blocks (front middle, and rear) are to be utilized on the bottom of the 
rail (the middle one has 1.5 mm feeler) to check for straightness. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

Net plate

3.10.8 
Skid Pre-Production Verification 
3.10.8.1 
Skid pre-production verification shall take place at the plant during the VP build 
event. Pre-production skids that are built to production design intent shall be sent 
to the plant, along with VP level sheet metal bodies built-up to the level required 
to run through impacted areas of the plant. All major model variations (i.e. 
coupe/sedan, super cab/crew cab) must be tested. 
3.10.8.2 
Pre-production verification skid shall be run through the entire plant: 
• 
Body (as applicable, including closures) 
• 
Paint 
• 
Final 
3.10.8.3 
The appropriate manufacturing engineer will have responsibility to co-ordinate 
the pre-production verification event. Representatives from body, paint and final 
(VOME and plant) must be present when the skid is run through their respective 
systems. 
3.10.8.4 
Upon completion of the skid verification run, the representative from each area 
will provide the BCE manufacturing engineer with sign-off of skid acceptance. If 
the skid cannot be run through the plant, the representative from each area will 
review the body on the skid and provide sign-off of skid acceptance. 
3.10.8.5 
During the VP emphasis build, any operation that will be complete in production 
while the body is on the skid shall be completed with the body on the pre-
production skid. Results of the VP emphasis build shall be included in the skid 
acceptance sign-off.  
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.11 
FLOOR MOUNTED STRUCTURES 
3.11.1 
General 
3.11.1.1 
A floor mounted structure is any structure used for equipment and operators that 
is not attached solely to building structural steel. This shall include any structure 
integrated into or necessary for equipment function. 
3.11.1.2 
When support structure is required for overhead tooling, preferred method is to 
provide a floor mounted structure instead of truss-hung white steel. Consider 
cost, destination plant accessibility, prove-out at OEM, and buyoff feasibility 
when choosing support steel method. The Ford Motor Company VOME 
Engineer, BCE Engineer or VIS, shall determine application of the requirements 
of this standard. 
3.11.1.3 
The use of Commercial Beam or Girder Clamps is prohibited for the joining of 
structural steel elements. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.11.1.4 
Beam Clamps shall be used for underhung steel connections. Beam Clamp 
design and installation shall comply with Ford Motor Company drawing BACO-
1001. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 251 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.11.1.5 
The connections between the vertical structural members which are anchored to 
the floor shall be bolted through flanges to the horizontal structural members. 
The joint shall be in compression. Beam Clamps are prohibited for these 
connections. 
3.11.1.6 
Structures shall be braced to reduce sway. 
3.11.2 
Structural Fasteners 
3.11.2.1 
All bolt holes must be drilled. The burning of holes shall not be permitted. 
3.11.2.2 
Snug-tightened joints are prohibited unless specified by a professional engineer 
on the drawings. 
3.11.2.3 
Unless specified otherwise by a Professional Engineer all bolts shall be 
fabricated per the w-OETS Standard (ASTM F3125/F3125M) with proper 
identification on bolt. 
 
                                             3.11.2.3 Example 
3.11.2.4 
For FNA and FAP plants: When bolts cannot be properly pre-tensioned (torqued) 
using a calibrated wrench (torque wrench) the bolts shall be twist-off-type 
Tension Control Bolts that meet ASTM F1852 (A325 TC) with proper 
identification on the bolt. Nuts shall be tightened using an electric or air shear 
wrench specifically designed for use with Tension Control Bolts. 
 
Approved Manufacturers are:  
• 
LeJeune Bolt Company "LeJeune Tension Control Bolt". 
• 
TCB (Tension Control Bolt Limited) 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 252 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
Description of the fastener and function: 
 
Fastener size selection example: 
 
Alternately, Direct Tension Indicating washers manufactured to ASTM F959: 
Specification for Compressible-Washer Type Direct Tension Indicators for Use 
with Structural Fasteners are acceptable. 
 
 
3.11.2.5 
Hex nuts shall be ASTM A563, Grade C, plain finished. 
3.11.2.6 
Flat circular washers and square or rectangular bevel washers shall conform to 
the current requirements of the ASTM for hardened steel washers, ASTM F436. 
3.11.2.7 
Fasteners for floor mounted structures do not require safety wiring. 
3.11.2.8 
Structural type bolts may not be reused without the approval of a Professional 
Engineer. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 253 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.11.2.9 
Bolts mounted overhead in the vertical plane shall be "bolt head down" so that in 
the event of a bolt failure, there will be a visible unfilled hole in the structure. 
3.11.3 
Professional Engineer Approval of Design 
3.11.3.1 
Structures that are overhead of aisles, operator work areas, have platforms used 
for operator or maintenance access requires design approval of a Professional 
Engineer. 
3.11.3.2 
Permanent lanyard anchorage points require the design approval of a 
Professional Engineer. 
 
3.11.4 
Welder Qualification 
3.11.4.1 
Only welders qualified in the type of structural welding specified shall perform 
welding on structures. 
3.11.4.2 
Welders shall be qualified by an AWS Certified Weld Inspector (CWI) as 
prescribed in the American Welding Society AWS D1.1/D1.1M Section 4. 
• Canadian Standard CSA W47.1-1983 for equipment being installed in 
Canada. 
• Equivalent country and local Welder certification qualifications shall apply. 
3.11.4.3 
Copies of the welder's qualification test record shall be made available upon 
request. 
3.11.5 
Lanyard Anchorage Points 
3.11.5.1 
See also FAS08-108: Working at Heights and Fall Prevention – Safe Practices, 
for additional information on Ford Motor Company requirements for personnel. 
3.11.5.2 
All structures shall have lanyard anchorage points identified when the following 
condition exists: 
• 
Maintenance personnel are required to be over 6 feet (1.8m) above floor 
level. 
• 
Maintenance platforms with handrail do not exist, handrail does not exist, or 
removal of handrail is required for maintenance. 
Requirements for permanent anchorage points on equipment structures shall be 
determined and agreed upon by the equipment supplier, VOME and the plant. 
3.11.5.3 
Permanent Lanyard Anchorage 
3.11.5.3.1 
The equipment supplier is responsible for providing permanent lanyard 
anchorage points when they are mounted to floor mounted structures they 
supply. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 254 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.11.5.3.2 
All anchors shall comply with 
• ANSI Z359; Fall Protection Code 
• ISO Standards 13.340.60: Protection against falling and slipping. 
3.11.5.3.3 
Permanent anchorage shall be oriented and mounted in accordance with the 
manufacturers’ specifications. 
3.11.5.3.4 
Permanent anchors shall be bolted D-Plate or bolted D-Ring style. See examples 
below. 
            
 
3.11.5.3.5 
Each permanent anchor shall have a warning label identifying the number of 
persons allowed to be attached and the maximum allowed weight. See example 
below: 
 
3.11.5.4 
Lanyard anchorage points attached to building structure shall be the 
responsibility of Ford Motor Company Facilities Engineering.

Revision Date: June 08, 2025

3.11.6 
Horizontal Lifelines 
3.11.6.1 
Where maintenance personnel are required to travel beyond the envelope 
allowed by D-Ring lanyard anchorage, the use of Horizontal Lifelines are 
permitted. See example below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.11.6.2 
Horizontal Lifelines shall meet all applicable OSHA, ANSI, CSA or other national 
requirements. 
3.11.6.3 
Horizontal Lifelines shall be purchased from a manufacturer of Horizontal 
Lifelines. 
3.11.6.4 
Horizontal Lifelines shall be purchased as a system from the manufacturer, see 
example below. 
 
 
 
 
 
3.11.6.5 
Horizontal Lifeline systems shall be designed in accordance with the 
manufacturers’ specifications. 
3.11.6.6 
Horizontal Lifelines shall be designed for minimum of (2) persons. 
3.11.6.7 
Horizontal Lifeline travelers (shuttles) shall be used and shall pass over any 
intermediate brackets without requiring lanyard catch removal from the lifeline. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 256 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.11.6.8 
Horizontal Lifeline end anchor and intermediate bracket attachment points of the 
structure shall have a 5:1 design factor (safety factor) to the Horizontal Lifeline 
system rated capacity. 
3.11.6.9 
Horizontal Lifeline systems shall be installed in accordance to the manufacturers’ 
installation specifications. 
3.11.6.10 
Each Horizontal Lifeline system shall have a warning label identifying the number 
of persons allowed to be attached and the maximum allowed weight. See 
example below:

3.12 
LUBRICATION 
NOTE:  See also GVOSS section 1.4; "VISUAL FACTORY REQUIREMENTS 
FOR NEW EQUIPMENT" which defines additional standards and requirements 
for equipment lubrication identification. 
3.12.1 
The design of all lube systems must conform to Ford Motor Company w-LA0 
Manufacturing Standards. See also ISO 5170 standard/SAE1751 
3.12.2 
Grease Lubrication  
3.12.2.1 
The plugging of grease holes in bearings after lubrication is prohibited. 
3.12.2.2 
Grease fittings (zerk fittings) shall be either standard or button head with detent. 
The equipment supplier shall identify plant preference at the start of the program. 
• 
Zerk fittings shall be threaded style. 
• 
Drive (press-in) style zerk fittings shall be prohibited. 
                  
 
 
Typical Zerk Fittings 
Button Head Grease Fitting 
3.12.2.3 
All grease fittings (zerk fittings) shall be accessible for maintenance. 
3.12.2.4 
To improve access to grease fittings, either a long fitting may be used, a 
combination of a single elbow and maximum 2 inch (50 mm) straight tube or 
hydraulic hose that is secured in place to prevent chafing. 
3.12.2.5 
Wherever possible, following the requirements of this standard, hydraulic hose 
shall be used to group lubrication points to a single bracket. 
NOTE: Multiple bracket groupings on a machine are permitted. 
3.12.2.6 
Hydraulic hose, when used, shall be a minimum 1/4-inch (6 mm) ID. 
3.12.2.6.1 
Hydraulic hose used for lubrication shall be identified with white tape at both 
ends. 
3.12.2.7 
Individual lubrication lines shall be grouped together into lubrication junction 
blocks with grease fittings. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 258 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
   
 
3.12.2.8 
Lubrication junction blocks shall be painted Safety White and the material and 
TOX number shall be identified on a tag at each lubrication block. 
3.12.2.9 
Lubrication materials shall not be “mixed” on a single lubrication junction block. 
Use multiple blocks. 
3.12.2.10 
Lubrication junction blocks shall be located in areas on the machine, so they are 
readily accessible by maintenance personnel. 
3.12.2.11 
Single point lubricators shall be battery operated. 
NOTE: Battery operated units have consistent dispensing pressure. 
3.12.2.12 
When multiple lubrication points are mounted to a single bracket, they shall be 
spaced to allow for the use of single point lubricators at a later date. See 
example below. 
 
3.12.2.13 
The brackets shall be located in such a manner as to allow for the addition of 
battery-operated single point lubrication unit equipped with a 250 ml cartridge 
without interfering with the equipment function. 
3.12.2.14 
To prevent contamination, all grease fittings shall have grease fitting covers that 
are tied to the grease fitting. Grease fitting covers shall be white. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 259 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.12.2.15 
All grease lines shall be purged of air and filled with grease prior to shipment to 
the plant. 
3.12.2.16 
The equipment supplier shall validate that all grease lines shall have no leaks 
after equipment installation at the plant. 
3.12.3 
Oil Lubrication 
3.12.3.1 
All components requiring oil lubrication, such as chains, shall have an automatic 
oil lubrication system. 
3.12.3.2 
Oil lubrication systems can be multipoint where feasible. 
3.12.3.3 
Chain lubrication systems for small parts conveyors shall use "Oil Brushes" to 
apply the oil onto the chain. 
3.12.3.4 
To reduce the number of different lubrication systems in the Ford Motor 
Company facility, VOME standards, the plant and equipment supplier shall agree 
upon the automatic lubrication system to be used in the equipment. 
3.12.3.5 
Oil reservoirs shall be located where they can be safely filled while the equipment 
is operating.  
3.12.3.6 
Refillable oil reservoirs shall be marked with the normal working, maximum and 
minimum levels and the total reservoir capacity shall be clearly indicated. 
3.12.3.7 
All oil reservoirs with capacities greater than 0.5 L (31 in3) shall be fitted with a 
visual level indicator so that the actual level in the reservoir can be easily 
checked at any time from the maximum to the minimum level. The visual level 
indicator shall be visible with the equipment running production. 
3.12.3.8 
Automatic oil lubrication systems shall be set-up to dispense the correct amount 
of oil at the proper frequency for the application. When properly set up oil should 
not drip from the lubricated component onto the plant floor. 
3.12.3.9 
The equipment supplier shall validate the set-up and function of the oil lubrication 
system to the point of application after equipment installation at the Ford Motor 
Company facility. 
3.12.4 
Lubrication of Linear Bearings (Excludes NSK Bearings with K1 & K1-L) 
3.12.4.1 
Linear bearings shall have lubrication lines terminated at a lubrication junction 
block. 
3.12.4.2 
Lubrication line length shall be as short as possible. 
3.12.4.3 
Lubrication junction blocks shall be accessible for maintenance. 
3.12.4.4 
Lubrication junction blocks shall be mounted to the same machine element as 
the bearing blocks. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 260 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.12.5 
Lubrication Materials 
NOTE: Materials manufactured by Chevron Products Company are prohibited as 
Ford Motor Company is unable to obtain TOX numbers for their products. 
3.12.5.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator along with support from VOME is responsible for 
compliance to this section for all equipment they supply on their systems. 
3.12.5.2 
All materials shall have a Ford Motor Company TOX number in compliance with 
FAP03-132. 
3.12.5.3 
Approved materials are identified in the file GVOSS – Lubricants and TOX 
Numbers. 
3.12.5.4 
Materials not on the approved materials list shall be allowed only after a GVOSS 
"Action Request" form for a deviation is submitted and approved. 
3.12.5.5 
Suppliers should make every effort to use a minimum number of different 
materials in equipment supplied to Ford Motor Company. 
3.12.5.6 
At the beginning of a program the Tier 1 Integrator shall compile a list of all 
lubrication materials to be used by Tier 1 Integrator and Tier 2 suppliers on the 
program. Use the Check Sheet in the file GVOSS – Lubricants and TOX 
Numbers. 
3.12.5.7 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall contact and present the plant Launch Team and 
Environmental Engineer with the list of lubrication materials to be used on the 
program for concurrence and GMAP review for materials new to the plant. 
3.12.5.8 
New materials, which do not have a Ford Motor Company TOX number, must be 
identified and have the Action Request – Deviation form submitted at least 6 
months in advance of use. 
NOTE: Lubrication materials without a TOX number are PROHIBITED on Ford 
Motor Company property. 
3.12.5.9 
The supplier requesting a new material shall notify the M.E. for the program and 
supply the MSDS Sheet along with the Material Data Sheets. 
3.12.5.9.1 
For new materials that do not have a TOX number, the requesting supplier shall 
provide the program M.E. with the new material manufacturer. 
3.12.5.9.2 
The requesting supplier shall provide the program M.E. with the estimated 
amount of the new material used in a 1-year period in accordance to the 
equipment PM M tasks. When the frequency of use is greater than 1 year the 
estimate shall be the volume and time period first used. For example, 500 ml at 
3-year intervals.

3.12.5.10 
Material Commonization at the Plant.  
3.12.5.10.1 If the plant requests a material change for commonization and the equipment 
supplier agrees, the equipment supplier shall notify and coordinate all Tier 2 
suppliers of the material change. 
3.12.5.10.2 If the plant requests a material change for commonization and the Tier 1 
Integrator or Tier 2 supplier disagrees, the Tier 1 Integrator shall document why 
the change of material is not recommended. The documentation shall be 
available on request from Ford Motor Company. 
3.12.5.11 
Material Compatibility 
3.12.5.11.1 Material compatibility issues usually arise in greases. See also the Grease 
Compatibility Chart and page 2 of the Mobil Synthetic Lubrication Specifications 
for reference information. 
3.12.5.11.2 Ford Motor Company uses lithium-based greases as the "General Purpose" 
grease. 
3.12.5.11.3 When grease that is incompatible with Lithium grease is specified by the Tier 1 
Integrator, Tier 2, or FTF supplier the following label shall be attached below the 
material toxicology label (GVOSS 1.4.7). 
 
 
3.12.5.11.4 The notice above shall also be added to the 1A9 Lubrication Sheet (GVOSS 
1.4.11.4)

3.13 
PREVENTING SHAFT FRETTING 
3.13.1 
Fretting 
Fretting is a complex mechanical corrosion and wear process that leads to 
fatigue of materials from the shear cracks that develop from the fretting scars. 
Fretting corrosion and wear occurs where metal-to-metal contact points exist in 
equipment. Fretting can significantly reduce shaft material endurance limits and 
be detrimental to equipment maintainability. See the examples below. 
The Agg-Net (The Aggregates & Recycling Information Network) article “Taking 
the Fretting out of Direct-mounted Gearboxes” provides a good overview of the 
fretting process. 
   
 
3.13.2 
Preventing Fretting 
3.13.2.1 
To prevent Fretting Dow Corning® G-n Metal Assembly Paste shall be applied to 
all shafts and pins where metal-to-metal contact points exist. This includes but is 
not limited to:  
• 
Bearings 
• 
Couplings 
• 
Sprockets 
• 
Shaft Locks 
• 
Shaft Clamp Collars 
• 
Hollow output shaft gearboxes 
3.13.2.2 
G-n Metal Assembly Paste is Ford Motor Company TOX# 002116 
3.13.2.3 
Dow Corning® G-n Metal Assembly Spray can be used in situations where 
application of the G-n Metal Assembly Paste is difficult or impossible. The G-n 
Metal Assembly Spray has the additional characteristic that it will wick into an 
assembled joint. 
3.13.2.4 
Some components have special requirements for cleanliness and preparation. 
Dow Corning® G-n Metal Assembly Paste or spray may not be suited for use in 
some applications. Special care should be taken to follow the component 
manufacturer's installation instructions. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 263 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.13.2.5 
Other compounds to prevent fretting shall be permitted when specified by the 
component manufacturer. For example, manufacturers of hollow output shaft 
speed reducers will often specify specific anti-fretting compounds. 
3.14 
TURN TABLES TOOL TRAYS AND TRUNNIONS 
This section pertains to turn tables, tool trays and trunnions using either index 
drives or flexible drives. 
3.14.1 
Design 
3.14.1.1 
All turn table, tool trays and trunnion sizes shall be verified to the FIDES inertia 
model for the tooling. 
3.14.1.2 
The equipment supplier shall be responsible for providing the FIDES inertia 
model to the turn table, tool tray and trunnion manufacturer. 
3.14.1.3 
The inertia data shall be signed off by the equipment supplier and the turn table, 
tool tray and trunnion manufacturer. 
3.14.1.4 
The signed inertia model shall be attached to the “Commercial Component Sign-
Off Form” for the turn table, tool tray and trunnion. 
3.14.1.5 
The use of shot pins in the turn table and trunnion design is prohibited. 
3.14.1.6 
Unbalanced load conditions are prohibited on trunnion drives. 
3.14.1.7 
Trunnions shall be rotationally balanced. 
3.14.1.8 
Counterweights shall be provided for any unbalanced load conditions on all 
trunnion fixtures. 
3.14.1.9 
Tool Construction 
3.14.1.9.1 
Tools shall be constructed in such a manner that the manual change-out will not 
result in component wear. 
3.14.1.9.2 
Tooling shall be weighed and stenciled with the “as built” weight prior to hanging 
to the turn table, tool tray or trunnion. 
3.14.1.9.3 
Rigging points shall be clearly identified. 
3.14.1.10 
Index Cycle Time 
3.14.1.10.1 The Index cycle time for a Turntable, tool tray or Trunnion shall include the time 
necessary to establish the magnetic flux in the motor. This is the time from the 
motion start initiation signal to the motion stop signal. DO NOT use physical 
motion start to physical motion stop. 
3.14.1.10.2 Standard cycle times for 180 degrees of rotation shall be 5.5 seconds or 10.0 
seconds predicated on Turntable or Trunnion inertial loading. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 264 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.14.1.10.3 Cycle times for other increments of rotation shall be provided by the Turntable or 
Trunnion manufacturer. 
3.14.2 
Controls 
3.14.2.1 
Turn tables, tool trays and trunnions shall use in Next-Gen controls designs the 
existing Ford Standard FTF 70ZF Panel designs and in GVOSS controls designs 
the FTS design examples. 
3.14.2.2 
The turn table tool tray and trunnion manufacturer shall confirm the suitability of 
the application for use within Next-Gen controls designs with the Ford Standard 
FTF 70ZF panel designs (see GVOSS Section 8) or for use within GVOSS 
controls designs with the Ford Standard FTF designs (see GVOSS Section 14), 
controls sections. 
3.14.2.3 
All index drives shall be capable of continual indexing in one (1) direction. 
3.14.2.4 
The index drive manufacturer is responsible to present Ford Motor Company with 
the Controls architecture to function the index drive within the manufacturer’s 
specified positional accuracy and without positional drift. 
3.14.2.5 
All index drives shall utilize slip rings for communication between the tooling and 
the station controller. 
3.14.2.6 
The turntable / tool tray / trunnion ‘in position’ switch tree setup shall be designed 
in a staggered fashion with unique flag brackets per tool face such that flags for 
different faces are unable to actuate switches that aren’t intended for that face 
(This is to avoid PLC safety instruction faults). Switches shall be barrel type. See 
example below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
   
                              
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 265 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.14.3 
Mechanical 
3.14.3.1 
Power transmission belt guards shall be easily removable for maintenance 
access. This includes inspection, measuring belt tension and belt replacement. 
3.14.3.2 
The area adjacent to the drive belt tension adjustment mechanism shall be clear 
of mounted obstructions that will interfere with adjustment travel. 
3.14.3.3 
Index and flexible drives for trunnions shall use a double disc coupling to connect 
the index drive to the trunnion shaft. 
3.14.3.4 
Any tooling plates mounted to the index plate shall not obstruct access covers for 
turn table and trunnion internal components. The use of access holes is 
acceptable. The turn table and trunnion manufacture shall concur with the 
suitability of the provisions to access components for maintenance or 
replacement. 
3.14.4 
Installation 
3.14.4.1 
Where applicable, after installation of the equipment at the plant, the turn table 
and trunnion manufacturer shall be responsible to validate in writing: 
• 
Belt tension 
• 
Belt alignment 
• 
Index drive operation in dwell for high speed (when used) 
• 
Index drive operation in dwell for low speed (when used) 
3.14.4.2 
After installation the Tier 1 Integrator shall take the following measurements to 
establish base line data: 
• 
Motor current signature for all 3 power phases 
• 
Index drive dwell switch timing (CW, CCW and Dwell Confirmation) in both 
the CW and CCW rotation directions (when used). 
Measurements shall be delivered to the Launch Leader, Body M.E. manager and 
VOME for their records. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 266 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.15 
RACK LOCATING AND INDEXING RACKING SYSTEMS 
3.15.1 
Precision Rack Locating 
3.15.1.1 
Precision rack locating shall be required when automation loads and/or unloads 
the rack. 
3.15.1.2 
Precision rack location shall be required for both static rack set-down locations 
and automatic rack indexing systems. 
3.15.1.3 
VOME shall identify program applications and requirements for precision rack 
locating to the Ford MP&L Rack Development Engineer, prior to designing the 
automation equipment. 
3.15.1.4 
VOME shall identify the type of automation used for automated rack unloading. 
3.15.1.5 
Precision rack dimensions and receiver designs are identified in the 2-WAY/4-
WAY LOCATOR LOCATION DRAWING. VOME and the Tier 1 Integrator shall 
contact the MP&L Rack Development Engineer to discuss 2-way/4-way 
tolerances. 
3.15.1.6 
MP&L shall provide rack-specific receiver locations used on the rack to be 
precision located. 
3.15.1.7 
Standard 2-way and 4-way receivers shall be used on the racks. 
3.15.1.8 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible for the proper design and location of 
the pin units used with the rack receivers. The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible to 
retrieve the latest rack data from Team Center. 
3.15.1.9 
lamicoid tags used to identify sensors inside the system/stand need to be 
legible/large enough for ease of viewing from outside the system/stand i.e. 3”x”5 
(76.2mm x 127mm.).  
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 267 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.15.2 
Rack Indexing Systems 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.15.2.1 
General 
3.15.2.1.1 
When a rack indexing system has a manual operator interface at the unload 
escapement, the Tier1 Integrator shall design the unload area in accordance with 
all GVOSS ergonomic requirements. Note: A rack lift table may or may not be 
necessary depending on the system design. 
3.15.2.1.2 
When a rack indexing system has a robotic interface at the unload escapement, 
the Tier1 Integrator shall verify their end effector design interfaces properly with 
the rack design. 
3.15.2.1.3 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for the rack indexing system logic. 
3.15.2.1.4 
The Tier1 Integrator shall be responsible for the Rack Indexing System HMI 
screens and logic. 
3.15.2.1.5 
The rack indexing system supplier shall verify the center of mass of the rack will 
function properly on the rack indexing system. 
3.15.2.1.6 
The rack indexing system supplier shall be responsible for "Try-Out" logic to 
satisfy the requirements of this standard. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 268 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.15.2.1.7 
The rack indexing system supplier shall furnish the Tier1 Integrator with proper 
handling, shipping and installation instructions for the rack indexing system. 
3.15.2.1.8 
The rack indexing system supplier shall buy-off the Tier1 Integrators' integration 
of the rack indexing system into their system prior to the Ford Motor Company 
buy-off of the equipment. This shall be documented and included in Tab 15 of the 
Tier1 Integrator’s Buy-Off Book. 
3.15.2.1.9 
The rack indexing system supplier shall provide technical support for the rack 
indexing system integration at the Ford Motor Company receiving plant. 
3.15.2.1.10 The rack indexing system supplier shall buy-off the installation of their rack 
indexing system at the Ford Motor Company receiving plant prior to cycling the 
rack indexing system. 
3.15.2.2 
Drives 
3.15.2.2.1 
Linear transfer drives shall be SEW Eurodrive utilizing Movi Mot and Movi FIT 
VFD technology.  
3.15.2.2.2 
Motors shall be braking motors where required. 
3.15.2.2.3 
See GVOSS Section 3.3: MOTORS, GEAR MOTORS AND SPEED REDUCERS 
for additional requirements. 
3.15.2.2.4 
A deceleration switch shall be used to decelerate the rack to “in-position”. 
3.15.2.2.5 
An “in-position” sensor or switch shall be used to identify when the rack is “in- 
position”. 
3.15.2.2.6 
When brake motors are used, they shall only be used as a holding brake or 
E-Stop brake when an E-Stop condition exists. 
3.15.2.3 
General Mechanical 
3.15.2.3.1 
The machine and all components are to be modular in design and construction. 
Common assemblies and components shall be used. 
3.15.2.3.2 
The machine should be designed such that the whole assembly can be broken 
down to a minimum number of sections for ease of reassembly on site. 
3.15.2.3.3 
The machine shall be constructed from welded standard structural steel 
members as required to support the maximum design loads, consistent with 
commonly accepted industry design standards.  
3.15.2.3.4 
Primary bolted construction is not acceptable. 
3.15.2.3.5 
Bolted construction to join modular elements at the break points is allowed. 
3.15.2.3.6 
Minimum container-to-container clearance of 6 inches is required. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 269 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
Deflection 
Angles 
Wheel 
Chocks 
3.15.2.3.7 
Mechanical stops must be provided to contain all moving assemblies within the 
machine in the event of a failure of position sensors or machine controls.  
3.15.2.3.8 
Maintenance platforms shall be provided where maintenance access requires 
personnel to climb over 6 feet (1.8 m) from the floor. The maintenance platform 
should provide access to the drive, head shaft and other components.  
3.15.2.3.9 
The fork truck end protection plates will be constructed from minimum 3/4-inch 
(20 mm) plate.

must be affixed to the floor to prevent potential damage by fork lifts. See example 
below.

3.15.2.4 
Guarding 
3.15.2.4.1 
All guarding shall be constructed from sheet metal, using expanded metal for 
visual inspection ports. Any guarded point that needs to be accessed for problem 
diagnostic purposes, preventative maintenance activities and viewing the 
operation of the conveyor shall be viewable without requiring guard removal. 
3.15.2.4.2 
All guards shall be bolted in location. 
3.15.2.4.3 
All machine guards shall be designed and constructed for ease of operation and 
handling by one person. 
3.15.2.4.4 
Safety fence mesh shall be ¾”-16 gage flattened expanded metal with the 
diamond pattern orientation vertical. 
3.15.2.4.5 
Fixed side guard fencing shall be used for sections exposed to production and/or 
material handling operators.

3.15.2.5 
Light Screens 
3.15.2.5.1 
Light screens shall be provided at the operator pick position. 
3.15.2.5.2 
Light screens shall be provided at the fork truck loading area. 
3.15.2.5.3 
Light screens shall be adjustable so that they can be easily aligned. 
3.15.2.5.4 
All external light screens at the fork truck loading area shall be floor mounted and 
guarded to prevent damage from a fork truck.

3.15.2.6 
Controls 
3.15.2.6.1 
Control component manufacturers used on rack indexing system shall be 
consistent with those specified by the “Approved Controls Components” 
document supplied by VO Controls. If there is no approved component for the 
application, VO Controls Engineer or VIS for the program shall identify approved 
component manufacturers. 
3.15.2.6.2 
The Ford Motor Company VO Controls Engineer for the program shall identify 
the applicable Standard Ford controls reference drawings to be used. 
3.15.2.6.3 
Rack indexing system control boxes shall be accessible from floor level or 
maintenance platform while the rack indexing system is running. 
3.15.2.6.4 
Rack indexing system valves shall be accessible from floor level or maintenance 
platform while the rack indexing system is running. 
3.15.2.6.5 
All rack indexing systems shall be equipped with a run-bar at the fork truck 
loading area. The run-bar shall have a button for each of the following: 
• Loading a rack (light screen muting) 
• Restarting the rack indexing system (un-muting the light screen) 
• Emergency stop 
 
3.15.2.6.6 
Run-bars shall be mounted on a post as shown below:

3.15.2.7 
Part Unload from Rack 
3.15.2.7.1 
In the operator manual unload position, the rack must have the ability to 
incrementally lift so that the container is always at an ergonomically correct 
height.   
3.15.2.7.2 
In the operator manual unload position for Over-Under Rack Indexing systems a 
three-sided bellows or roll up screen shall be utilized on the lift to prevent egress 
by the operator into the lifter area while the lifter is in the “UP” position. 
3.15.2.7.3 
A three-sided bellows or roll up screen is not required in robotic pick applications. 
3.15.2.8 
Buy-Off 
3.15.2.8.1 
Requirements for holding a buy-off at the rack indexing system supplier shall be 
determined by the VO ME. The buy-off event should be with the ME, VIS, Tier1 
Integrator, rack indexing system supplier and plant (optional). This section 
provides guidelines for the buy-off event, when it is required. 
3.15.2.8.2 
There shall be a buy-off event at the rack indexing system supplier’s facility prior 
to shipment to the Tier1 Integrator's system integration facility. 
3.15.2.8.3 
The rack indexing system shall be connected to all air and electrical services 
required for operation of the equipment. 
3.15.2.8.4 
Air pressure shall be set at maximum 65 psi. 
3.15.2.8.5 
Rack indexing system controls shall be connected to a PLC loaded with the rack 
indexing system logic and fault messages. 
3.15.2.8.6 
The rack indexing system logic shall be modified to operate the rack indexing 
system in an automatic repeat mode. 
3.15.2.8.7 
The automatic repeat mode shall simulate rack indexing system function in the 
manufacturing environment of the final installation. 
3.15.2.8.8 
The buy-off shall be the same as GVOSS Section 1.2.6 with the following 
exceptions: 
• Only Buy-Off Book Tabs 3, 5-8, 15, 16, 18 and 21 apply. 
• The buy-off event shall consist of a Buy-Off Book review and GVOSS Section 
1.2.6.5 Phase I. 
3.15.3 
Over-Under Rack Indexing Systems 
3.15.3.1 
Rack Control - Mechanical 
3.15.3.1.1 
Load stop plates shall be used between the load position and position two (2) to 
prevent the fork truck from hitting the 2nd position rack while loading a rack. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

3.15.3.1.2 
A one-way mechanical dog/flipper shall be placed between the lift and 1st 
outbound position to prevent the container from being moved back into the lift 
position accidentally when the lift carriage is not present.

3.15.3.2 
Lift Mechanism for Over-Under Systems 
3.15.3.2.1 
The up and down motion shall be accomplished by driving the two strands of 
roller chain with a shaft-mounted gearmotor.  
3.15.3.2.2 
Gear couplings shall be used to connect the gearmotor to the head shaft. 
3.15.3.2.3 
The head shaft shall be sized to lift a full load rack. 
3.15.3.2.4 
The lift mechanism shall consist of a lift platform connected to two strands of 
roller chain.  
3.15.3.2.5 
Counterweight usage shall be based on manufacturers recommendation. 
3.15.3.2.6 
The free ends of the lift mechanism roller chains shall be connected to contained 
counterweights.  
3.15.3.2.7 
The counterweights shall be contained in a min. W6 x 25 lbs. /ft. (approx. 37 
kg/m) wide flange beam. 
3.15.3.2.8 
The W6 x 25 lbs. /ft. (approx. 37 kg/m) wide flange beam shall act as the lift mast 
columns as well as the counterweight containment. 
3.15.3.2.9 
The counterweight and lift carriage shall be safety cabled to the roller chain as 
shown in the example image below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 278 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.15.3.2.10 Broken-chain detection shall be provided for both the counterbalance and 
carriage side of the chains. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.15.3.2.11 The lift carriage shall be fully guided side to side and front to back at the four 
corners of the carriage. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Guide
Guide

3.15.3.2.12 Manual safety pins shall be provided to lock the carriage for maintenance. See 
image below. 
3.15.3.2.13 Manual safety pins shall be a minimum of 1-½ inch (38mm) in diameter. 
3.15.3.2.14 Removed 
 
 
 
 
3.15.3.2.15 Multiple manual safety pin receivers shall be located so that the carriage can be 
locked out anywhere in its vertical travel. 
3.15.3.2.16 NextGen Controls Architecture shall use a proximity switch to verify the safety pin 
is in its holder. 
3.15.3.3 
Fall Arrest for Over-Under Systems 
3.15.3.3.1 
The lift carriage shall utilize an “elevator style” mechanical braking system when 
the lift is descending too quickly (or free fall) as identified in the red box in the 
example image below.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.15.3.3.2 
An over-speed governor shall be used to detect over speed lift carriage. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 280 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.15.3.3.3 
The over-speed governor shall be bi-directional rotation and shall only activate in 
the lowering direction. 
3.15.3.3.4 
The over-speed governor shall be capable of manual reset by raising the lift after 
the condition that resulted in the over-speed has been corrected. 
3.15.3.3.5 
The over-speed governor shall be equipped with a secondary sheave to allow 
testing of the Fall-Arrest system. 
3.15.3.3.6 
When the over-speed governor trips it shall lock and arrest the cable motion. 
3.15.3.3.7 
When the cable motion is arrested by the over-speed governor, unidirectional 
instantaneous safety gear shall be actuated to arrest the carriage descent. 
3.15.3.3.8 
The instantaneous safety gear units shall be located on both sides of the carriage 
(right and left) and interlocked together. 
3.15.3.3.9 
Each instantaneous safety gear units shall each have an engagement plate or 
rail for arresting the carriage motion. 
3.15.3.3.10 The over-speed governor is not required in robotic pick applications. 
3.15.3.4 
Maintenance Safety – Working at Heights 
3.15.3.4.1 
A 6’-0” wide area the full width of the upper level at the head shaft area of the 
rack indexer shall be plated with a “Slip Resistant” steel floor material. 
3.15.3.4.2 
Permanent lanyard anchorage points or Horizontal Lifeline shall be provided to 
service the lift drive, bearings and any other components related to the lift 
mechanism.  
3.15.3.4.3 
When used the number of permanent anchorage points and their locations or 
location of the Horizontal Lifeline shall be agreed upon by VOME, the plant and 
the equipment supplier. 
Note: As a guideline use six permanent anchorage points distributed as follows; 
• (2) at each head shaft bearing. 
• (1) at each side of the drive, for a total of (2). 
• (1) at intermediate points between each head shaft attachment and the drive 
attachment for a total of (2) when the drive is center-mounted. 
This will provide fall prevention for (2) plant maintenance personnel to service the 
components of the lift mechanism. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 281 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.15.4 
Floor Mount Rack Indexing Systems 
 
3.15.4.1 
Rack Control - Mechanical 
3.15.4.1.1 
Goal post stop tube shall be used between the load position and position two to 
prevent the fork truck from hitting the 2nd position rack while loading a rack. 
(Goal post may be omitted providing there is sufficient space, determined on a 
project-to-project basis, between the load position and position two). 
 
Goal Post

3.15.4.1.2 
Unload back stops shall be used between the unload position and the position 
prior to the unload position to prevent the fork truck from hitting the rack in the 
position prior to the unload position when unloading a rack. (Unload back stops 
may be omitted providing there is sufficient space, determined on a project to 
project basis, between the unload position and the position prior to the unload 
position). 
3.15.4.1.3 
Heavy duty fork truck unload side guides shall be utilized to prevent the fork truck 
from swing the unload rack into the load position or safety fence.

3.15.5 
Static Rack Stand Systems 
3.15.5.1 
Mechanical 
   
 
 
  
  
     
 
        
    
 
 
 
 
 
    
   
             
 
                                 Static Rack Stand References 
3.15.5.1.1 
In addition to general mechanical design guidelines for Rack Indexing Systems, 
see GVOSS section 3.15.2.1 & 3.15.2.3, mechanical design for Static Rack 
Stands shall follow below requirements. 
3.15.5.1.2 
When Forklift truck forks are engaged with a container, static stand shall be 
designed to ensure the forks cover two-thirds of the container. If the container 
has full-length fork tubes, fork coverage must be 51% of the container length or 
more. (Ref. SFT-16-008) , Ford Truck Capacity and Load Center SPL #175, 
Material handling 101. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 284 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.15.5.1.3 
Forklift manufactures typically use a 24” load center. In order to ensure 
requirements from GVOSS 3.15.5.1.2 are met, Forklift Masts shall be positioned 
at a max of 24” from the rack positioned in Rack Static Stand. This is to be 
accomplished by positioning of the wheel chocks, (see Forklift wheel chocks 
drawings in TCE 056ZF-20044382), to stop the Forklift at the required distance 
from the rack.   
 
                                             
 
3.15.5.1.4 
Static Rack Stand floors shall be painted Safety Red to provide hazard 
awareness. 
3.15.5.1.5 
Two story Rack Static Stand design shall ensure that Forklift truck Mast shall not 
break lower light screen when loading top rack and vice versa. 
3.15.5.1.6 
Rack locating pins shall be protected from damage caused by the loading and 
unloading of racks. 
3.15.5.1.7 
Screw retainers for Rack locating pins and brackets shall be of robust design to   
withstand frequent rack hits during load. 
3.15.5.1.8 
The entrance to Static rack stands shall be flush with perimeter guarding.       
3.15.5.1.9 
 Fork truck ride up plates and rack deflection angles shall be utilized on static 
stands systems to guide forklift load. See example below.

3.15.5.2 
Light Screens 
3.15.5.2.1 
In addition to general Light Screens design guidelines for Rack Indexing 
Systems, see GVOSS Section 1.3.20, Light Screens design shall follow below 
requirements. 
3.15.5.2.2 
An additional set of light screens shall be provided on unload (internal) end of the 
static stand when the load or front (external) end of the static stand does not 
provide physical barrier higher than 40” or as determined by Risk Assessment. 
See picture below.  
 
                                            
 
3.15.5.2.3 
For all rack load/unload applications, the Probable stopping times from the EL4 
calculator may be used for engineering purposes. This method shall be used in 
conjunction with robot speed monitoring with EL10 verification during integration. 
An approved risk assessment is required. 
 
Reference the latest version of the EL4 light screen worksheet and robot safety 
system prove out guide. Light screen worksheet is available in Teamcenter.  
 
Light screen distance must be validated using safe distance video method. Any 
changes made to the speed monitoring DCS parameters must also have safe 
distance revalidated using the video method. 
3.15.5.3 
Controls 
3.15.5.3.1 
In addition to general controls design guidelines for Rack Indexing Systems 
controls design, see GVOSS section 3.15.2.6, should follow below requirements. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 287 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.15.5.3.2 
“Rack Present” sensors or switches shall be used to identify when the rack is 
loaded correctly. The yellow operator stack light shall be solid when Rack itself 
(with our without parts in the rack) is present with all Rack Present switches 
actuated. 
 
3.15.5.3.3 
A “last part” sensor or switch shall be used to identify when the last part in the 
rack is present. The number of these switches depends on the number of part 
rows and/or columns in the rack. 
3.15.5.3.4 
Additional switches for rack ID and/or rack/parts special features may be required 
and depend on number of these features. 
3.15.5.3.5 
lamicoid tags used to identify sensors inside the static stand need to be legible 
for ease of viewing from outside the stand i.e. 3”x 5” (76.2mm x 127mm.).

3.15.5.3.6 
When designing Part Present Switch positions all efforts shall be taken to ensure 
switches are protected from Forklift and dunnage rack. See example below. 
 
                        
 
                    
3.15.5.3.7 
Static stand design shall conform to the requirements for safe distance 
calculations, see GVOSS Section 1.3.11.2.3 Point of Danger, when using a 
safety rated monitoring device (light screens etc.). 
3.15.5.3.8 
DCS model for robot arm and robot end effector, with part, shall be as tight as 
possible to robot arm and shape of part/end effector. 
3.15.5.3.9 
Robots shall not be given the clear to enter a DCS zone with light screen violated 
(i.e. program a wait position just outside of DCS boundary). 
3.15.5.3.10 Robots shall utilize a side mount dress package when not using internal 
dressing. 
3.15.5.3.11 Each rack area with a light screen shall be programmed as an individual operator 
shared workspace. 
3.15.5.3.12 Rack descriptions shall be common on HMI, PLC program and Robot program.

Rack Locks 
3.15.6.1 
Robotic rack loading and unloading systems whether stationary, indexing or 
over/under system shall include failsafe controls detection to verify any 
mechanical locks of the rack are in the unlocked position.
