3.16 
FASTENER FEEDING SYSTEMS 
3.16.1 
General 
3.16.1.1 
The Tier 2 Equipment Supplier will provide all necessary air requirements, 
including, but not limited to: Air quality, hose sizes, feed tube routing/lengths, and 
pressure settings, etc. 
3.16.1.1.1 
Where the Tier 2 requirements are not aligned with the GVOSS the Tier 2 is 
responsible for conditioning the incoming air to meet their requirements. 
3.16.1.2 
All feed systems to be stenciled with station and operation number. 
3.16.1.3 
An engraved tag shall be provided on each feed system with the following: 
• 
Weld fastener number 
• 
Machine manufacturer ID number 
NOTE:  A sample of the fastener shall be mounted beside the fastener number 
tag. 
3.16.1.4 
Pneumatic components shall be permanently lubricated. 
3.16.1.5 
Control architecture shall be the identified by the Ford Motor Company VOME 
controls engineer for the program. 
3.16.1.6 
All components shall follow the approved component list for the program. 
3.16.2 
Feed Systems 
3.16.2.1 
For proper bowl operation and to prevent satellite vibration, all vibratory 
components must be mounted to a rigid framework. The top plate shall be a 
minimum 19mm plate. 
3.16.2.2 
Vibratory feed stands shall be equipped with leveling holes for proper field 
installation. 
3.16.2.3 
Feed systems must be completely surrounded with a sound and dust enclosure. 
3.16.2.4 
Feed systems shall comply with the requirements of the FAS08-212, w-SX1 
Noise Abatement Specification. 
3.16.2.5 
Enclosures will utilize clear polycarbonate panels where practical for visual 
inspection and access to the feed system components. 
3.16.2.6 
All feed system enclosure panels will be hinged or “bolt on”. 
3.16.2.7 
Lift-off style enclosure panels on feed systems are prohibited. 
3.16.2.8 
All vibratory feed bowls shall be constructed of minimum 11-gauge (3mm), 304 
stainless steel, or of other suitable wear resistant material. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 290 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.16.2.9 
Bowl tooling areas with high wear characteristics shall be manufactured with 
replaceable tooling. 
3.16.2.10 
Vibratory feed bowls shall be constructed with “dirt escape” slots or holes to allow 
dirt and debris to flow out of the bowl prior to reaching the bowl discharge. This is 
to prevent contaminating the discharge system. 
3.16.2.11 
A “fail safe” feature shall be designed within the bowl to prevent upside down 
parts from escaping the bowl.  
3.16.2.12 
In cases where implementation of a "fail safe" feature within the bowl is not 
feasible an external probe and eject mechanism shall be employed. 
3.16.2.13 
Vibratory drive units for feeder bowls shall utilize two coils for operation.  
3.16.2.14 
Vibratory controllers shall incorporate a manual switch for settings of vibration at 
either 3600 or 7200 VPM. 
3.16.2.15 
Vibratory controller enclosures shall conform to NEMA 12 in construction. 
3.16.2.16 
Vibratory feed bowls shall be fed from a vibratory supply hopper.  
3.16.2.17 
The supply hopper shall have a minimum capacity of an 8-hour supply of 
fasteners. For large parts a 4-hour supply may be permitted after an "Action 
Request Form" for a deviation to the 8-hour requirement is submitted and 
approved. 
3.16.2.18 
The supply hopper shall be equipped with a “no contact” level switch to signal a 
low condition to the operator. Mechanical type switches shall be prohibited. 
3.16.2.19 
The supply hopper shall be equipped with a stack light to signal a low-level 
condition.  
3.16.2.20 
The on/off operation of the supply hopper will be controlled by means of an 
optical or proximity part level switch in the feed bowl.  
3.16.2.21 
To prevent the bowl from being over filled, the on/off operation switch shall be 
interlocked with the feed bowl power source so that the supply hopper can never 
be turned on unless the bowl is operating. The bowl must be on to move loaded 
parts around the perimeter and actuate the bowl "full" level switch to turn off the 
supply hopper. 
3.16.2.22 
The load height of the supply hopper shall be no more than 42 inches (1060mm) 
from the floor unless otherwise specified by the ergonomic study. 
3.16.2.23 
The part escapement cover shall be easily removable without the use of tools to 
permit maintenance personnel to readily inspect the escapement and resolve any 
"jammed" conditions, 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 291 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.16.2.24 
Chutes and part conveyance conduit connections shall be easily disassembled 
for maintenance and troubleshooting. 
3.16.2.25 
Feed chutes shall be flexible, one-piece construction to eliminate contamination 
and prevent air leakage during the feed cycle. 
3.16.2.26 
Feed chutes shall be constructed of vinyl or urethane material for durability. 
3.16.2.27 
Chutes shall be transparent or translucent to facilitate visual monitoring of the nut 
location in the chute. 
3.16.2.28 
Chutes must be supported in a suitable manner at both the feed system end and 
the placement unit end. 
3.16.2.29 
Where necessary, chutes shall be supported to prevent kinking and maintain the 
minimum allowable bend radius. 
3.16.2.30 
Part escapements shall have pneumatic needle valves to control both the speed 
of the escapement and the volume of air for conveying the nut through the feed 
chutes and to the placement unit.  
3.16.2.31 
In the case of multiple discharge feed systems, the system shall be capable of 
feeding each fastener independently for individual operation. 
3.16.2.32 
Each feed system shall be supplied with a separate pneumatic filter, regulator 
and pneumatic lockout valve for integration with the plant or tooling air supply. 
This requirement is satisfied when the air supply is fed from a robot air supply 
with the feed system tap after the robot pneumatic filter, regulator and pneumatic 
lockout valve.  
3.16.3 
Placement Units 
3.16.3.1 
Each placement unit shall have a switch to sense "placement unit returned". 
3.16.3.2 
On spear tip style systems, the spear tip shall be separate from any actuator rod 
and easily replaceable. Component replacement shall not require major 
disassembly of the placement unit.  
3.16.3.3 
Spear tip style placement units shall have a maximum 12-inch (300mm) stroke. 
3.16.3.4 
Each placement unit shall have a separate pneumatic operating valve, hoses, 
regulator, and gauge.  
3.16.3.5 
Details contacting the part to be fed shall be made of wear resistant materials 
and heat treated. 
3.16.3.6 
All other details shall be plated or be of non-ferrous material to prevent corrosion. 
3.16.3.7 
A rigid mounting stand shall be provided for each placement unit.  
3.16.3.8 
Stands shall be easily adjustable for three axis movement and prevent machine 
vibration from affecting the proper operation of the placement unit. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 292 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.16.3.9 
Each mounting stand must be supplied with tapping pads for installation.  
3.16.3.10 
Weld nut placement units shall have a part-present switch at or near the 
placement unit head assembly to detect that a nut is present.  
3.16.3.11 
The switch shall monitor the part level and the system will operate on a “one in, 
one out” basis. 
 
3.17 
LINE SIDE EQUIPMENT 
3.17.1 
Manually Operated Equipment 
3.17.1.1 
All manually operated equipment requiring pneumatic service shall be equipped 
with: 
1. Lock-Out Exhaust valve 
2. Filter/Regulator or Flow Control 
See examples below: 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 293 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.17.1.2 
An air gage shall be provided after manual valves to verify pressure is released 
from the cylinder prior to performing maintenance. See example below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.17.1.3 
The manual valves shall have a “RP” (Release Pressure) ECPL icon when there 
is trapped air between the valve and the point-of-use after the Lock-Out Exhaust 
valve is actuated to exhaust the machine air. 
3.17.2 
Turn Tables 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 294 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.17.2.1 
All "Air Float" type rotating turntables used for part load/unload shall incorporate 
a cycling hard stop that pivot in to provide location accuracy for the load/unload 
positions. The hard stop pivots out prior to the next rotation of the turntable. 
3.17.2.2 
All modifications to existing turntables shall be approved by the manufacturer. 
3.17.3 
Lift and Tilt Tables 
3.17.3.1 
General 
3.17.3.1.1 
Lift/Tilt Tables shall have the manufacturer name and rated load capacity 
identified and a stamped manufacture date on the device. 
3.17.3.1.2 
Lift/Tilt Tables shall have the proper safety signal and hazard labels. Ref. 1.4.2 
3.17.3.1.3 
Lift/Tilt Tables shall be equipped with protective skirting/guarding. 
3.17.3.1.4 
Lift/Tilt Tables shall have a secondary safety retention device to prevent over 
extension of the tilt and/or lateral over extension of the vertical lift. 
3.17.3.1.5 
Lift/Tilt Tables shall have all functions labeled on the controlling device. (i.e. 
up/down labels on the hand or foot controller). 
3.17.3.1.6 
Lift/Tilt Tables shall have lockout devices for each movement. 
3.17.3.1.7 
Lift/Tilt Table lockout devices shall be located outside of the mechanism. 
3.17.3.1.8 
Reuse of existing lift/tilt tables: 
• The tables shall be updated to this standard before released to production.  
• Contact the manufacturer for the approved upgrade kit.  
3.17.3.2 
Lift/Tilt Tables Refurbishment 
3.17.3.2.1 
General 
3.17.3.2.2 
Lift/Tilt Table Refurbishment shall only be completed by the original 
manufacturer. 
3.17.3.2.3 
Lift/Tilt Table Refurbishments shall be recertified for stated load capacity, 
structural integrity, safety rating and 3.17.3. 
3.17.3.2.4 
Lift/Tilt Table Refurbishments shall have the refurbishment date stamped on the 
unit. 
3.17.4 
Production Personnel Lift 
3.17.4.1 
Lift designs are required to have a (1/2 -Ton capacity minimum.) 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 295 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.17.4.2 
Lift shall be engineered with a failsafe system to prevent premature lift drop in 
event of mechanical, pneumatic or electrical failure. 
3.17.4.3 
Lift shall meet safety requirements to control gravity. (Ref. 1.3.13) 
3.17.4.4 
Lift shall be delivered at the Ford Standard pneumatic pressure. 
3.17.4.5 
Lift mechanisms shall be completely protected to prevent access to the lift 
mechanism. 
3.17.4.6 
Lift mechanisms shall be completely protected to prevent debris and 
contamination. 
3.17.4.7 
Lift shall be identified with all visual factory requirements. (Ref. 1.4) 
3.17.4.8 
Lift shall be P.E. certified. 
3.17.4.9 
A Risk assessment shall be performed per application. 
3.17.4.10 
Lifting mechanisms are to be mechanical in design and electrically driven. 
3.17.4.10.1 Pneumatic Lift systems require VOME VIS and Safety approval. 
3.17.4.10.2 Lift Belt Drive systems require VOME VIS and Safety approval. 
3.17.4.10.3 When belts are used in the drive design, belt sensing is required. This sensing 
shall shut down the lift when a belt failure occurs and only allow it to be lowered 
manually. 
3.18 
ROBOT END EFFECTORS, ROBOT RISERS AND HOLD TABLES  
3.18.1 
General 
3.18.1.1 
For the purpose of this standard the following definitions shall be used: 
• Tooling Nest: Tooling used for part presentation and set-down 
• Part Nest: End effector, hold table and cart tooling used in moving the part 
between tooling nests. 
3.18.1.2 
All end effectors are to be of an efficient design, lightweight, structurally sound 
and with minimal antlers and/or clamping. Clamping is to be minimal and specific 
to the operation. 
3.18.1.2.1 
All aluminum welded end effectors and structures shall require an approved 
deviation.  
3.18.1.3 
All dispense end effectors shall be designed to provide adhesive/sealer 
placement to +/- 0.5 mm repeatability. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 296 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.18.1.4 
All proximity switches used to sense product presence are to be design centered 
within the manufacturers' recommended target range and located within 75 mm 
of the pin, pin clamp, gripper or power clamp. 
3.18.1.5 
All EOAT applications shall be implemented utilizing the robot manufacturers 
“collision guard” software. Ford shall provide a specification for minimum setup 
for a given robot manufacturer.  Functionality shall be validated with Ford 
personnel (VIS or Controls Engineer). Use of “break away “devices on critical 
applications will be considered with signed approval by Ford VO Commodity and 
/or Tool/Equipment LL5. 
3.18.1.6 
All end effectors are to be signed off by Ford personnel (M.E.) during the concept 
phase and prior to beginning the actual design. 
3.18.2 
Mechanical 
3.18.2.1 
Any mechanical adaptor bracket between an end effector and the robot tool face 
shall be welded steel construction. 
3.18.2.2 
Frame joints shall not fatigue or loosen over time. 
3.18.2.3 
Clamps and Grippers 
3.18.2.3.1 
All non-NC block and finger part holding shall use grippers. 
 
3.18.2.3.2 
Dual action grippers and clamps shall be prohibited.  
3.18.2.3.3 
Any clamp that contacts a Class A surface shall use NC blocks. 
3.18.2.3.4 
All NC blocks and fingers shall use power clamps. 
3.18.2.4 
End effectors that transfer, and hold tables that hold product assemblies while 
they are being worked on, shall have at a minimum: 
• 
One 4-way locating pin 
• 
One 2-way locating pin 
• 
Three NC part nests 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 297 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.18.2.5 
End effectors and hold tables shall utilize "wipers" as necessary to properly 
locate parts during transition from end effector to and from hold tables and any 
other types of part nests. 
3.18.2.6 
Modular components manufactured from non-ferrous materials do not require 
painting. 
3.18.3 
Certification 
3.18.3.1 
The VOME ME will determine when an end effector or hold table requires locator 
certification. 
3.18.3.2 
End effectors and hold tables shall have permanent datums established and 
clearly identified for certification purposes using laser tracking equipment. Unless 
otherwise specified, the set-up method will use 3-2-1 alignment. The VOME ME 
and Tier1 Integrator will agree upon the placement of datums and type. 
Recommended datum types are: 
• 
Tooling ball receiver. 
• 
Reflective tooling ball mount. 
3.18.3.3 
A certification report shall be generated after the locators are certified. 
3.18.4 
Controls 
3.18.4.1 
The Ford Motor Company VO Controls Engineer for the program shall identify 
any applicable Standard Ford controls drawings to be used. 
3.18.4.2 
End effector terminal and junction boxes shall use receptacles for control cables. 
Hard wiring for control cables is not allowed. 
3.18.5 
Docking End Effectors 
3.18.5.1 
Docking end effectors are used to set part geometry during a welding process. 
Docking end effectors shall use "Trailer Hitch" locating details: 
• 
056ZF-20040680 - standard locating ball 
• 
056ZF-20040681 - standard 2-way locator (receiver) 
• 
056ZF-20040682 - standard 4-way locator (receiver) 
The Tier1 Integrator is responsible for the receiver details. The end effector 
supplier is responsible for the locating ball details. 
 
3.18.6 
Pedestal Welding End Effectors 
3.18.6.1 
Pedestal welding end effectors shall be designed to withstand the stress and 
strain induced by the high frequency of impulse shock loads caused by weld gun 
equalization. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 298 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.18.6.2 
Pedestal welding end effectors shall have mounting surfaces and structural 
design sufficient to accommodate a tip dresser when specified. 
3.18.7 
Hold Table Construction 
3.18.7.1 
The preferred base is a steel pedestal type mount. 
3.18.7.2 
The top of the pedestal shall have receivers to locate the table nest. 
3.18.7.3 
A minimum of four fasteners shall be used to secure the table part nest to the 
pedestal. 
3.18.7.4 
The table part nest shall conform to the requirements for robot end effectors. 
3.18.7.5 
Robot Risers 
3.18.7.5.1 
Robot risers utilized shall be the new approved NAAMS round riser design. 
3.18.7.5.2 
Robot Kgs that are over the approved NAAMS standard riser design must be 
designed by the integrator and shall include: 
• Fork Pockets 
• FEA 
• PE Certification 
• Hilti Anchoring Approval. 
• Pull Out/Inspection Cart Construction  
3.18.7.6 
General 
3.18.7.6.1 
The purpose of pull-out/insert carts is to facilitate the removal and re-introduction 
of parts in a process. Unless otherwise specified by Ford Motor Company the 
components used for construction of the pull-out/insert carts shall be consistent 
with those used for the end effectors and hold tables for the program.  
3.18.7.6.2 
Carts shall be repeatable in positioning when reinserted into the station.  
3.18.7.6.3 
Carts shall be interlocked to the station controls to identify whether it is present or 
not.  
3.18.7.6.4 
The cart opening shall be guarded in such a manner that egress into a station is 
prevented when the cart is removed from the station. 
3.18.7.7 
Cart Classifications 
3.18.7.7.1 
Drawer Style Cart 
3.18.7.7.1.1 The cart is permanently located to station where used. See below picture.

3.18.7.9 
Ergonomics 
3.18.7.9.1 
1 Mechanical design shall take into consideration Initial and Sustained Push 
Force Ergonomic requirements. 
3.18.7.9.1.1 Initial Push Force is required force or human physical effort to start the cart into 
motion (i.e. force required to accelerate the cart) where the force is in front of the 
body and directed towards the body as the body stands or moves backwards. 
Assumed force is applied by two hands and is a whole-body force exertion (i.e. 
while standing and walking) and the force is applied in front of the operator. 
Maximum acceptable initial push/pull force is 200 N (45 lbs.*) 
3.18.7.9.1.2 Sustained Push Force is required force or human physical effort to maintain the 
movement of the cart. Once the cart is in motion, the sustained push force is 
relatively constant and is required to keep the cart moving. Assumed force is 
applied by two hands and is a whole-body force exertion (i.e. while standing and 
walking) and the force is applied in front of the operator. Maximum sustained 
push/pull force is 111 N (25 lbs.*) 
3.18.7.9.1.3 Ergonomic cut point is based on Snook & Ciriello Tables, Liberty Mutual Manual 
Material Handling Design Goal Tables and accommodates for 75% of the female 
population. 
3.18.7.9.2 
Controls 
3.18.7.9.2.1 For Ford Standard Controls set-up see VOSS Standard 070ZF-20016007 & 
070ZF-20013140. It covers pull-out “Drawer Cart type” with switches for Cart in 
position. 
3.18.7.9.2.2 The following switches are typically required: 
                     1. 
Part Present switches 
                     2. 
Model detect switches 
                     3. 
Cart in position switches 
                     4. 
Switch for Cart Locking device in position

3.19 
VEHICLE TIRE GUIDES 
3.19.1 
Equipment Tire Guide Lead-In Assembly (Drive-On Section) 
3.19.1.1 
Ball bearing type lead-ins are not acceptable. 
3.19.1.2 
The lead-in design shall consist of a series of UHMW rollers mounted on a steel 
shaft. The roller axles for wet or corrosive environment applications shall be 
fabricated from 316 stainless steel material. Both ends of the UHMW roller shall 
incorporate a machined radius to eliminate sharp corners. 
3.19.1.3 
Both ends of the UHMW roller mounting shaft shall be machined to incorporate a 
"keeper" plate. The "keeper" plates shall be tapped to the lead-in assembly steel 
frame structure. 
3.19.1.4 
The lead-in section of the tire guide assembly shall incorporate a "bullet nose" 
transition to prevent tires from contacting the UHMW roller support frame. The 
bullet nose shall be designed from UHMW Transition Roller and Side Guide 
Rollers as shown in Figure No. 3.19.4.1 The lead-in assembly shall be designed 
such that it is impossible for the tire to contact the steel frame structure of the 
initial UHMW roller assemblies. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 302 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.19.1.5 
Each transition from the angled lead-in rollers to the parallel side guide roller 
sections, the "tangent point" of the lead-in, shall consist of a vertical, stud 
mounted, tapered UHMW roller. The tapered roller mounting stud for wet or 
corrosive environment applications shall be fabricated from 316 stainless steel 
material. The tapered roller assembly shall be designed such that it is impossible 
for the tire to contact the steel frame structure of adjacent UHMW roller guide 
assemblies. 
3.19.1.6 
All sharp edges that could contact a tire or wheel shall be removed or covered by 
a split, smooth, round tube or pipe. 
3.19.1.7 
The lead-in assembly steel frame structure shall be painted Ford Safety Yellow. 
3.19.1.8 
Clearance from the top of the lead-in to the underbody of the vehicle shall be 
determined by reviewing all vehicle variants in Team Center. The design 
contractor is to also consider the dynamic loading (vehicle bounce) of the 
vehicles as they are driven onto the equipment past the lead-in. At a minimum, 
1.125” for unibody, and 2.5” for body on frame, clearances from the lead-in to the 
underbody shall be provided to take into the account this dynamic loading. It is 
unacceptable for any underbody component to come into contact with the lead-in 
assembly. 
3.19.1.9 
Lead-in rollers shall be tied together with a cover plate. 
3.19.1.10 
Cover plate cannot interfere with function of equipment. 
3.19.1.11 
Cover plate shall be bolt on, no welded cover plate allowed (use countersunk 
fastener, reference design drawing). 
3.19.1.12 
Cover plate shall be painted Ford Safety Yellow with non-slip paint, unless 
specified otherwise in equipment statement of work (SOW). 
3.19.1.13 
Outside lead-in guide roller structure and brackets shall be covered or designed 
so that no sharp edges are exposed on back side of roller structure. 
3.19.1.14 
No sharp exposed fasteners, including but not limited to bolts and threaded rods, 
are allowed in any area that can come into contact with the tire, this includes 
wheel stops, rumble strips, etc. 
3.19.2 
Equipment Tire Roller Guides (Parallel Roller Section) 
3.19.2.1 
Ball bearing type tire guides are prohibited. 
3.19.2.2 
The entrance and exit ends to all tire guides shall be oriented such that it is 
impossible for the tire to contact the blunt end of the guide. If it is not possible to 
orient the guide entrance or exit to avoid all possible tire contact, then the end of 
the guide shall curve toward the floor, ending with the blunt end of the guide 
toward the floor. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 303 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.19.2.3 
The tire guide design shall consist of a series of UHMW rollers mounted on a 
steel shaft, as shown in Figure No. 3.19.2.3. Both ends of the UHMW roller shall 
incorporate a machined radius to eliminate sharp corners. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.19.2.4 
Provide support legs at all tire guide splices as shown in Figure No. 3.19.2.4. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
                   
 
3.19.2.5 
No butt welds are allowed for all tire guide splices. 
3.19.2.6 
All sharp edges that could contact a tire or wheel shall be removed or covered by 
a split, smooth, round tube or pipe. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 3 - MECHANICAL 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 304 of 304 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.19.2.7 
If there is a gap greater than 8” between parallel side guide roller sections, the 
leading edge of the next roller section shall consist of a vertical, stud mounted, 
tapered UHMW roller. The tapered roller mounting stud for wet or corrosive 
environment applications shall be fabricated from 316 stainless steel material. 
The tapered roller assembly shall be designed such that it is impossible for the 
tire to contact the steel frame structure of adjacent UHMW roller guide 
assemblies. 
3.19.2.8 
Parallel guide roller sections shall be tied together with a cover plate unless the 
cover plate interferes with underbody process operations, such as wheel 
alignment, underbody inspection, water test sprayer nozzles. 
3.19.2.9 
Vehicle guides shall not interfere with underbody assembly or process 
operations. 
3.19.2.10 
No sharp exposed fasteners, including but not limited to, bolts and threaded rods, 
are allowed in any area that can come into contact with the tire, this includes 
wheel stops, rumble strips, etc. 
3.19.3 
Equipment Tire Pipe Guides 
3.19.3.1 
Pipe style tire guides are only allowed for conveyor applications. 
3.19.3.2 
Specifications for Tire Pipe Guides can be found in Section 9. 
3.19.4 
Thrust Rollers, when specified in equipment scope of work. 
3.19.4.1 
Thrust rollers shall be made from SAE 316 stainless steel.  Entire assembly must 
be corrosion resistant. 
3.19.4.2 
Thrust rollers shall be located at transition point where lead-in meets the parallel 
roller section.  Location shall be sufficient that vehicle tire is completely on the 
thrust rollers for a minimum of 12in (30cm) prior to intersection and remains on 
the thrust rollers as the tire passes the transition point. 
3.19.4.3 
Thrust rollers shall have the ability to be cleaned out of debris from below.

5.1 
GENERAL 
5.1.1 
Where existing equipment is to be modified, the Standard shall be in accordance 
to the "actual" Standard on site, except for any items mentioned in the Statement 
of Work for that project. After that, the requirement of this Specification becomes 
effective. New equipment and lines must comply with this specification. 
5.1.2 
Any reference in this document to ‘Tier 1 Integrator is deemed for the ‘Line 
builder that received the main contract from Ford Motor Company’. 
5.1.3 
Any reference in this document to ‘Tier 2 Equipment supplier’ is deemed for 
equipment supplier to the Tier 1 Integrator, including any Ford to furnish items. 
5.1.4 
For any reference required within this document to any voltages and electrical 
frequencies then the following is used: 
• Country Electrical Frequency 
• 3 Phase Voltage 
• Single Phase Voltage 
• Machine Control Voltage 
5.1.5 
For any reference required within this document to any input/output controls bus 
system then the following is used: 
• Controls Network 
5.1.6 
For any reference required within this document to any data network system then 
the following is used: 
• Data Network 
5.1.7 
For any reference required within this document to any Mechanical Design, 
pipe/hose colours, sizes etc. then refer to the GVOSS ‘Design’ Section. 
5.1.8 
For any reference required within this document to any Mechanical Installation 
then refer to the GVOSS ‘Mechanical’ Section. 
5.1.9 
Any specific components required within any project can be found within the 
project specific Approved Source List.

5.2 
DRESS PACKAGES 
5.2.1 
General 
5.2.1.1 
Unless specified otherwise in this section, Robot Dress requirements shall 
conform to the standards and specifications set forth in the Ford Motor Company 
VOSS. 
5.2.1.2 
The Ford Motor Company VOME, P&SD Department for each program shall 
provide the information necessary to quote the robot dress components to meet 
the <PA> cost target. This includes, but is not limited to: 
• 
part drawing (part number and description) 
• 
process (where used in the manufacturing process) 
• 
type of end of arm tooling dress packages required: 
– 
material handling end effectors 
– 
docking end effectors 
– 
welding (single or dual gun, AC or MFDC, constant heat or constant 
current control, pneumatic or servo, equaling or non-equalizing weld gun) 
– 
pedestal welding end effectors 
– 
sealing 
– 
laser welding 
– 
laser cutting 
– 
stud welding 
– 
piercing 
• 
process cycle time 
• 
robot type and model 
• 
weld gun manufacturer 
• 
end effector manufacturer 
• 
tip dresser requirements 
5.2.1.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator will develop and maintain a BOM (Bill of Material) that will 
contain the standard components that comprise the dress of each robot 
contained in their tools and include separately the necessary components on the 
Industrial Materials lists. 
When using a Tier 2 robot dress supplier the Tier 2 robot dress supplier shall 
provide the information noted above to the Tier1 supplier. 
5.2.1.4 
The Tier 2 robot dress components supplier shall be responsible for having their 
product line in FIDES to aid the tooling suppliers in doing their Process Simulate 
studies.   

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 4 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.2.1.5 
The Tier 2 robot dress component supplier shall be responsible for providing to 
the Tier 1 Integrators their installation requirements including torque 
specifications, visual management requirements, preventative maintenance 
requirements, acceptable dress package, bend radii and twist angles.   
5.2.1.6 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible for the processing, construction and 
integration performance of the end of arm tooling with the robot dress component 
packages on the robots in the tooling system they have been contracted to 
supply. 
5.2.1.7 
The Tier 2 robot dress component supplier and robot manufacturer shall buy off 
on the integration (application and installation) of their components at the Tier 1 
Integrator's facility.  
5.2.1.7.1 
Robot manufacturers providing internal dress solutions shall buy off on the 
integration (application and installation) of their components at the Tier 1 
Integrator's facility.  
5.2.1.8 
The Tier 2 robot dress component supplier and robot manufacturer are 
responsible for delivering the recommended preventative maintenance tasks per 
the VOSS MFM standard to the Tier 1 Integrators for the given program. (Ref. 
GVOSS 1.2.8.3.6) 
5.2.1.8.1 
Robot manufacturers providing internal dress solutions are responsible for 
delivering the recommended preventative maintenance tasks per the VOSS MFM 
standard to the Tier 1 Integrators for the given program. (Ref. GVOSS 1.2.8.3.6) 
5.2.1.9 
Prior to the 2500 cycle run, the Tier 1 Integrator is responsible for match-marking 
the robot dress rotational and translational relations of the straps, protection 
donuts and brackets relative to the umbilical to ensure that the dress package 
can easily be visually checked for the loosening of such components as a 
preventative maintenance task. This will enable the "as bought" dress package 
configuration to be sustained throughout its life cycle. It will also reduce the Mean 
Time To Repair the dress package when required.    
5.2.1.10 
The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible for addressing any dress package issues that 
arise during the 2500 cycle run. This includes the loosening of the match-marks 
described above and cuts to the dress package umbilical, hoses or cables. 
5.2.1.11 
Prior to buy-off, the Tier 1 Integrator shall complete the Dress package check list. 
Once completed by the Tier 1 Integrator, the robot dress component supplier 
shall buy-off (application and installation) their components at the Tier 1 
Integrator's facility. 
5.2.1.12 
The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible for preventing damage to the dress package 
components that may occur from manipulating the robot into its shipping position.  

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 5 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.2.1.13 
Utilizing the match-marks mentioned above, the Tier 1 Integrator is responsible 
for ensuring that the "as bought" robot dress package configuration is 
reconstructed at final installation at the plant. 
5.2.1.14 
The Tier 2 robot dress component supplier shall provide support for the robot dress 
integration at the Ford Motor Company receiving plant. 
5.2.1.15 
Prior to buy-off, the Tier 1 tooling supplier and Tier 2 robot dress component 
suppliers shall complete the Dress package check list.  Once completed by the 
Tier 1 tooling supplier, the robot dress component supplier shall buy-off 
(application and installation) their components at the Tier 1 tooling supplier's 
facility. The Tier 1 Integrator and Tier 2 robot dress component supplier shall 
review all dress packages prior to <MP2> to review for potential failure(s) and 
create the Dress Package Datasheet to be included as part of the line side 
documentation i.e. 1A4.  This will provide documentation of the dress package as 
approved by the tier 2 supplier and include any modifications to the dress 
package during the pre-builds. 
5.2.2 
No robot dress bracket mounts shall be welded to the robot. 
5.2.3 
Robot dress brackets shall be specified to withstand the stress and strain 
induced by the robot in motion for each of the robot’s programs. 
5.2.4 
Robot dress brackets and related hardware shall be comprised of standard "off 
the shelf" components within the robot dress component supplier's product line.  
5.2.5 
Robot dress brackets shall be bolted onto their mounting brackets. 
5.2.6 
Robot dress brackets shall be free of burrs and sharp edges. 
5.2.7 
Robot dress shall not contact sheet metal. 
5.2.8 
"Saddles" are to be used when robot dress hoses and cables are routed from 
elevated cable tray to the robot. 
5.2.9 
The dress bracket liners must be compliant enough to hold the hoses and cables 
in position without pinching off the supply of fluids to the end-of-arm tooling. In 
addition, the dress bracket clamps shall hold the hoses and cables in a way to 
prevent any wear that may be caused from relative rubbing motions between the 
hoses, cables and clamp liners.   
5.2.10 
Straps utilized to gather the dress package hoses and cables shall have the 
strength to endure the forces generated during the given applications throughout 
the life cycle of the dress package. They must be readily removed and reinstalled 
as required. They shall have the frictional and strength characteristics required to 
maintain the positions at which they were originally delivered and signed off on.  

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 6 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.2.11 
If conventional (no umbilical or sheathings) dress packages are specified for the 
program, standard "off the shelf" covers from the robot dress component 
supplier's product line will be utilized to protect the hoses and cables from any 
rubbing with the product and/or surrounding tooling. 
5.2.12 
If dress packages with umbilical or sheathing are specified for the program, 
standard "off the shelf" umbilical or sheathing from the robot dress component 
supplier's product line will be utilized.  
5.2.13 
If dress packages with umbilical or sheathing are specified for the program, the 
hoses and cables must have a MTTR (Mean Time to Replace) of no more than 
0.5 hours. The Tier 1 Integrator shall demonstrate this to the plant. 
5.2.14 
All robot applications shall be processed to minimize any flex of dressing.  
5.2.15 
Robot end effector dressing shall not hit or rub any robot motor, cable or fitting 
throughout range of motion on each program utilized. 
5.2.16 
"Strain Relief" shall be used where required to reduce the frictional, gravitational 
and inertial forces on the hoses and cables. 
5.2.17 
Thick non-flammable synthetic reusable straps shall be utilized instead of tie-
wraps for “bundling” cables and hoses together. 
5.2.18 
Cables and hoses shall not contact the floor. 
5.2.19 
Appropriate hose quick disconnect couplings shall be used as specified for the 
program. 
5.2.20 
Robot control cable track shall not be used to hold robot dress cables and hoses. 
5.2.21 
Robot dress cables and hoses shall not be attached to robot control cable track. 
5.2.22 
Soldered connections are prohibited on any self-confectioned cable. 
5.2.23 
If conventional (no umbilical or sheathing) dress packages are specified for the 
program, any servo weld gun power, communication or I/O cables required shall 
be ordered with individual corrugated sheathing (tubing) to protect them from the 
other utilities within the dress package. 
5.3 
INTERNAL DRESS PACKAGE REQUIREMENTS 
5.3.1 
Minimum Requirements 
5.3.1.1 
Robot control to Axis 3 (i.e. includes necessary cables from robot control to Axis 
3 where applicable, unless otherwise noted 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 7 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.3.1.2 
All internal wiring to cover welding, riveting and handling options shall be part of 
the base robot. 
5.3.1.3 
All Controls Network cables and connectors along with the 4-pin mini power 
cables and connectors shall be Ford to Specify and listed on the approved 
components list. 
5.3.1.4 
All 3 Phase Voltage connectors mounted on the robot shall be isolated from robot 
arm- Robot Mfg. shall be responsible. Intent is to keep any stray ground currents 
from robot arm and possible I/O connections on arm. True safety for the 
connector is the ground pin and connector connected to the power ground at the 
source. 
 
5.3.2 
Material Handling (MH), Protection for Tool Changer (TC) 
5.3.2.1 
Optional - One ¾-inch Air hose with the color as defined in ‘Section 2 – Design’ 
(used only for current applications). NOTE: This is in addition to the two airlines, 
with two ½-inch G-ports, provided by robot manufacturer as part of base robot. 
5.3.2.2 
Cable between robot control and Axis 1 provided by robot manufacturer; cable 
can be a combination cable to minimize multiple cables between Axis 1 and Axis 
3. (Provides power for DCIO and DCA connectors.) 
5.3.2.3 
Tool changer, if required, shall be installed by the Tier 1 Supplier. 
5.3.3 
Spot Welding (SW) Carried Gun and/or Pedestal Weld Gun (No Tool Changer) 
5.3.4 
Three ½-inch water lines with the colour as defined in ‘Section 2- Design’ shall be 
provided, from Axis 1 to Axis 3, by the robot manufacturer, with the following 
requirements: 
• Two ‘water out’ hoses with: 
o Axis 1 water junction block shall be one ¾-inch G-port common to two ½-
inch G-ports. 
o Axis 3 water junction block shall be two 1/2-inch G-ports on one side and 
two ½-inch G-ports on the other side all common to each other. 
• One ‘water in’ hoses with: 
o Axis 1 water junction block shall be one ¾-inch G-port common to a one 
½-inch G-port on the other side.  Axis 3 water junction blocks shall be one 
½-inch G-port common to one ½-inch G-port on the other side. 
5.3.5 
The Tier 1 Supplier shall provide water hose and fittings from weld control stand 
to base of robot.

• Two 3/4-inch G-ports for water located at Axis 1 base (water in/water out)

20013210).

70ZF–20013210).

robot arm, then only need connector on axis 3) (ref. 70ZF–20013210).

5.4 
CONTROLS 
5.4.1 
For Body Construction Engineering, controls for any dimension-setting robot 
station shall include a “Clamp and Hold” selector switch, switch shall inhibit robot 
initiate and display message “Clamp and Hold Enabled, Robots Disabled”.  
5.4.2 
If the Robot system does not support 'auto power off', then the Robots shall be 
programmed to remove drive power after five minutes of no motion 
(environmental timer set to five minutes).  
5.4.3 
For Body Construction Engineering, all robot load operations shall have an Abort 
Pick-up Mode of the Operation: 
5.4.3.1 
When a fault occurs, a button will be depressed, and robot will return part to nest 
or drop to floor (Model dependent magazine operation would also pick up correct 
part as part of the abort routine).  
5.4.3.2 
For operator/robot shared load stations, continued pressure on the yellow 
“Reset” button will “Unclamp” the tooling and return the robot to the “Pounce” 
position.  
5.4.3.3 
In addition to the yellow "Reset" button, there shall be a selection on the HMI to 
execute the abort pick-up mode of operation. 
5.4.3.4 
Where the process allows and agreement is defined from VOME engineers 
within Body Construction Engineering, all model-dependent robot load operations 
shall have a “Model Mismatch” Abort Mode of the Operation. When a mismatch 
occurs, a button will be depressed, and robot will return part to nest or drop to 
floor (Model dependent magazine operation would also pick up correct part as 
part of the abort routine). 
5.4.4 
Where the process allows and agreement is defined from Ford Manufacturing 
Engineers, all material handling robots placing a part under a sealing, mastic or 
adhesive dispense system shall require an inspection method that may consist of 
the following functions: 
• Accept 
• Reject: This may be replaced with ‘Swap, Scrap, Eject, Remove Now, remove 
later etc.  based on process. 
• Re-Seal 
5.4.4.1 
Pushbuttons shall be available on the nearest HMI and Robot Teach pendant to 
permit acceptance or rejection of the sealed part with manual intervention. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 13 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.4.4.2 
When the sealer, mastic or other dispense system is interrupted any time during 
the automatic “dispense cycle” for any reason (E-stop, low material flow, etc.) a 
bit shall be latched on and the logic will require the part to automatically be 
presented (after the dispense cycle is completed) to the fence line in as clear 
view as possible for an operator to view the dispensed material coverage on the 
part. 
• The part will remain presented to the operator until a three-position selector 
switch (spring return to center) is activated to the “Accept” or “Reject” 
position. 
• If “Accept” is selected, the part shall continue to the next station. If “Reject” is 
selected, the part shall either be identified as a rejected part in the shift 
register or dropped on a specific location inside the cell. 
5.4.5 
Load capacity of the robot shall be determined according to manufacturer’s 
specifications. Final designs shall be reviewed by the robot manufacturer for 
approval to ensure that specific design conditions have been met. 
5.4.6 
Any overlap of adjacent robot work zones shall be shown and shall have proper 
interlocks added to prevent robot crashes during program cycle. This will be 
accomplished by programming interlocking waits and outputs. 
5.4.7 
Robots shall be supplied with interface to Ford Specifications. The supplier shall 
provide design and equipment required for a fully functional robot station, 
including requests for Ford to furnish items. 
5.5 
ROBOT SIZING 
5.5.1 
Tooling supplier to verify robot size to the application utilizing design programs 
provided by robot manufacturer prior to ordering robots. Late changes regarding 
the size of the robot shall require an approved deviation defining how it was 
missed in the process, prior to ordering a larger robot. Tooling supplier shall be 
responsible for added cost and timing due to design errors resulting in a larger 
robot. 
 
5.6 
TOOL CHANGER APPLICATION  
5.6.1 
Tool adapter holding fixture shall have an RFID safety rated switch indicating tool 
adapter is in position that is plugged directly into the tool changer for hardwired 
operation to release tool. In addition to the RFID safety switch, a separate “tool in 
stand” switch shall be tied to PLC input(s) that shall be mapped to the robot. 
5.6.2 
Tool adapter holding fixture shall have compliant die springs allowing for vertical 
and radial tolerance if required for tool changer. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 14 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.6.3 
Tool adapter holding fixture shall have locating pins undersized by 0.5 mm. 
5.6.4 
Tool adapter holding fixture shall have a dust cover which prevents dust, weld 
slag and water contamination of 480V and 110V contacts of tool adapter.  
• For backup guns or batch build tool change processes or other processes 
where cycle time is not a concern, the dust cover shall be movable from 
tool to tool via the robot master tool changer. 
•  Where cycle time requires a fast tool change process the tool adapter 
holding fixture shall have a cylinder actuated cover. All cylinders (when 
required) shall be controlled by one valve. Valve shall be controlled by 
station PLC and robot interlocks. 
5.6.5 
Each “cover” cylinder shall have a switch showing “cover” clear to accept/remove 
tool adapter. Switch shall be wired to station control panel and interlocked to 
robot showing all covers clear. (Reference robot manufacturer’s interface 
drawing and node adapter bit map.) 
5.6.6 
Tool adapter holding fixture shall not clamp tool adapter in place. 
5.6.7 
A tool changer stand may not have more than one dedicated gun assigned to its 
operation. 
5.6.8 
Each tool adapter holding fixture shall be labelled with the assigned gun number 
with colors as defined within the drawing ‘70ZF20013205’. Numbers shall be a 
minimum of 203mm or eight inches high and shall be legible from outside 
guarded area. If model specific, then they must be color coded to match SOW 
model color coding requirements. 
5.6.9 
Tool adapter holding fixtures must be contained within the perimeter guarding. 
Fixtures must also be in safe position; safe position determined on application 
basis (i.e., when gun is designed in fixture, maintenance personnel must have 
access to do electrode maintenance to gun). 
5.7 
ROBOT END EFFECTOR 
5.7.1 
The robot shall have a "Qualification Part verification program" that will move the 
robot into a position that is an appropriate distance short of contact with the 
qualification Part, so that maintenance personnel can move the robot in teach 
mode the remaining distance. This is to verify positions of the end effector 
product and docking locators to the part. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 15 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.8 
ROBOT END OF ARM TOOLING CHANGER REQUIREMENTS 
5.8.1 
This section provides guidelines to protect for the addition of tool changers in 
robot applications. 
• This shall apply to all material handling end effectors, including docking and 
geometry setting end effectors. 
NOTE: This does not apply to welding or sealer robots. 
5.8.2 
In order to protect for future flexibility, material handling end effectors shall be 
capable of being retrofitted with tool changers to support future model 
requirements.  The auto changers shall be protected for but not provided until 
they are required. 
• Process to Protect for future addition of tool changers: 
− The tool changer weight shall be accounted for. 
− The tool changer offset to the robot end effector shall be accounted for. 
− The robot sizing shall be calculated with the weight and offset of the 
changer included. 
− A Virtual modelling program as approved by VOME model using the tool 
changer offset shall be used to validate the feasibility of adding the future 
tool changer. 
− SPACER ARE REQUIRED between the robot face plate and the end 
effectors to protect for the addition of a TOOL changer. 
 
5.9 
WELDING ROBOT BACKUP PROCEDURE FOR PLC-5 LEGACY SYSTEMS 
NOTE: This procedure is for robots dedicated for backup only. Any other robotics 
backup scenario shall be approved on an application basis. 
NOTE: This section does not mandate there be robots dedicated for backup, it 
only explains the backup procedure. 
5.9.1 
Robot in line is bypassed (i.e., Station #2, Robot #1, bypassed code = 21). 
5.9.2 
Plant personnel will read robot bypassed code and information at the bypassed 
robot and determine if manual backup is required. 
5.9.3 
If robot backup is possible, plant personnel will proceed to backup robot station 
control panel, put station in manual mode and enable the correct backup robot 
when first part requiring backup welds reaches station (i.e., “Enable Backup 
Robot” selector switch to the enabled position). 
5.9.4 
When Backup Enabled selector switch is actuated, cycle complete for backup 
robot and station will be reset. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 16 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.9.5 
Plant personnel will then verify that backup robot is bypassed and enter proper 
“Robot Bypassed Code” at thumbwheel on backup robot control panel. 
5.9.6 
Thumbwheel value will be sent to station PLC which will compare with transfer 
information to verify that robot selected is one of the robots which is currently in 
bypass. Once verified, backup robot station PLC will retain this value until a new 
code is accepted. Backup station PLC will not accept a new “Robot Bypassed 
Code” until robot is bypassed, and backup station is in manual mode. 
5.9.7 
When a backup robot is taken out of bypass, station auto is initiated and 
“Verification Signal” is sent from transfer. Station PLC will select proper robot 
program. 
5.9.8 
Plant personnel shall verify operation of the robot through the completion of the 
first welded part. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 17 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.10 
ROBOT “CONTROLS” WELD – NO-WELD INTERLOCKING 
5.10.1 
The following defines the performance expected in an automation system with a 
welding robot: 
a. Condition: Weld Control placed in a “No Weld Mode” (Internal No Weld) 
through the Timer Data Entry Panel or through its own faceplate. 
– 
Performance Expected: Robot will initiate a weld, gun will close and NOT 
fire, robot will turn on the #1 Time Delay Exceeded (Weld Fault) light, 
robot will issue a “Re-weld” command, gun will close and NOT fire, robot 
will sit with the gun in the position of the spot weld waiting for a “Weld 
Complete” from the weld control. 
– 
Requirement To Clear Fault: Operator put the weld control into the “Weld 
Mode” through the timer data entry panel or through its own faceplate, 
operator press the “Manual Weld” pushbutton on the Robot Interface 
Panel which initiates a weld, gun will close and fire, robot will receive the 
“Weld Complete” from the weld control and proceed to the next spot 
location. 
b. Condition: “Weld / No Weld” switch on the Robot Interface Panel is placed in 
the “No Weld” position while the robot is in its Start Position. 
– 
Performance Expected: In the Production Mode with Parts, the robot will 
NOT leave the Home position if the switch is in the “No Weld” position. 
The Smart Display for the station will display a message stating that 
“Robot Timer is Not in the Weld Mode”. 
– 
Requirement to Clear Fault: Placing the selector switch to the “Weld” 
position will allow the cycle to begin. 
NOTE: In the “Auto Repeat”, the position of the “Weld/No Weld” selector 
switch on the robot interface has no significance. “Auto Repeat” will run 
in either position. 
5.10.2 
The following defines the performance expected in an Automation System 
with a pedestal welding robot: 
a. Condition: “Weld/No Weld” switch on the Robot Interface Panel is placed in 
the “No Weld” position during a Welding Program. 
– 
Performance Expected: If, during the welding program, an Operator puts 
the selector switch in the “No Weld” position, the robot will continue its 
welding program with the weld timer in external no weld and no weld 
faults will be detected by the robot unless the timer actually faults in the 
no weld mode. The PLC controls will not set a cycle complete if the 
switch was in the “No Weld” position at the last weld of the robot due to a 
“Timer Fault” signal from the weld control. A message will be on the 
Smart Display indicating “Robot Cycle Overtime” or “Robot Timer Fault; 
Cycle Will Rerun”. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 18 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
– 
Requirement to Clear Fault: An operator will be required to reset the 
timer fault, normally by manually initiating the weld control through the 
robot interface panel with the switch in the “Weld” position. Once the fault 
is cleared, the robot will be told by the PLC to run the welding program 
again. 
NOTE: In the “Auto Repeat”, the robot will be instructed to run as if all welding 
was completed without a timer fault. 
a. Condition: “Weld/No Weld” switch on the Robot Interface Panel is placed in 
the “No Weld” position during a welding program on the combination 
pedestal welding material handling robot system. 
– 
Performance Expected: Robot will continue through the welding portion 
of its program and no weld faults will be detected by the robot unless the 
timer actually faults in the no weld mode. The robot will have an external 
wait signal programmed at the drop off location so the robot will not 
unload the part if the welding program ended with a timer fault caused by 
the switch in the no weld position. The smart display will show a 
message similar to the above condition. 
– 
Requirement to Clear Fault: An operator will be required to perform a 
wait override through the robot control panel or manually initiate a weld 
through the robot interface panel to clear the fault and allow the robot to 
continue with the part unload portion of its program. That operator is to 
assure that the part has been fully welded or remove the part and have it 
recycled through the automation. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 19 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.11 
ROBOT – TEACH AND MANUAL MOTION CONTROL OPERATIONS 
5.11.1 
Teaching Robots at Multiple Levels for PLC-5 Legacy Systems 
5.11.1.1 
General rules are listed below: 
a. If the upper robot is to be taught from the lower level, the robot must be first 
put in “Teach” and then the station put in “Manual”. 
b. Proceed to the lower gate where the upper robot teach pendant is stored and 
place the lower robot, which is normally accessed through that gate, in 
“Teach”. The lower robot will not be allowed to go into “Teach” because the 
upper robot is already in “Teach”, but this will allow you to enter through that 
gate without dropping mode in the station. 
c. Pick up the teach pendant for the upper robot and proceed into the station 
and proceed with the programming. If any of the upper gates are opened 
while the robot is being programmed from the lower level, the robot will be 
“E-stopped”. If any lower gates are opened while a robot is programmed from 
the upper level, the robot will be “E-Stopped”. 
5.11.1.2 
The Body Flex System (a.k.a. Sullivan Hockey Puck Compliance) is not to be 
used with docking end effectors. All docking end effectors will require the 
following robot manufacturer systems to be used:  
• Fanuc – Soft Float System  
• Kawasaki – Soft Servo System 
Tool Integrator shall assure that the docking fixture tooling is the only force 
locating the EOAT in the docking fixture and that the robot has no influence on 
the dimensional location of EOAT and /or Product in the docking fixture.  
Functionality shall be validated with Ford personnel (VIS or Controls Engineer). 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 20 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.12 
FORD ROBOT PROGRAMMING GUIDES 
5.12.1 
General 
5.12.1.1 
Moved to section 5.8.1.2.2. 
5.12.1.2 
Ford robot programming guides have been developed for all robot program 
variations. The robot program shall use the programming guide for every robot 
program. VO Controls and Standards shall be notified if a new application is 
required. Any required new program guides will be developed by VO Controls 
and Standards and supplied to the equipment providers.  
5.12.1.2.1 
Contact VOME Controls for the latest Kawasaki Programming guides. 
5.12.1.2.2 
Contact VOME Controls for the latest Fanuc Programming guides. 
5.12.1.3 
Absolutely no AS programming unless approved by Ford VOSS representative.  
5.12.1.4 
The robot program start point shall be the same physical location as the robot 
program end point. Robot tool center points must be validated and demonstrated 
to VOSS personnel prior to tool shipment.   
5.12.1.5 
For turntables / trunnions / tool trays; the same robot program shall be used if the 
tooling is identical and the model is identical on each face. 
5.12.1.6 
First step after “Move to Pounce” shall be off of home position. 
5.12.1.7 
Robots shall be programmed to remove drive power after five minutes of no 
motion (environmental timer set to five minutes). 
5.12.2 
Joining Robots 
5.12.2.1 
Weld Schedules with Retract Cylinders.  Robots using pneumatic weld guns with 
retract cylinders shall have the robot program set up such that the retract is 
closed prior to closing the weld stroke.  If the retract needs to be fully open to 
reach the first weld spot, the program shall have a "stacked" step at the weld 
location. The robot program will reach the location, fire Clamp 2, which closes the 
retract.  Wait for the gun to reach the retract closed position.  The timer feature in 
the robot will be used to accomplish this task.  Then fire Clamp 1 along with a 
weld schedule.   
5.12.2.2 
Weld Complete Delay. To prevent damage to pneumatic weld guns. A “Weld 
Complete Delay” programmed in the weld timer shall be ten cycles or greater. 
(Not required with servo weld guns.) 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 21 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.12.2.3 
Each model will receive its own robot program in the robot controller.  When 
joining is common (i.e., part numbers and joining method (weld, rivet, clinch, etc.) 
numbers identical between two or more models), then a common robot program 
can be used. 
5.12.2.4 
Robot program numbers shall be consistent across all commodities. 
5.12.3 
Sealer Robots 
All robots used for sealing applications must have their linearization tables 
validated and signed off by VOSS personnel. 
5.12.4 
Robot Zones 
5.12.4.1 
Robot Zone Documentation.  
Robot Zones shall be documented in the 1A5 Drawing Controls Application 
Drawing package (Sheet 1A5-200) for each PLC. For each robot, each and every 
interference zone between the robot and the adjacent robot/equipment must be 
implemented and included in the Robot Zone Drawing and Robot Zone 
Verification sheet. This documentation will be an aid to PLC programmers and 
robot programmers as well as the people responsible for troubleshooting.  An 
example of a robot zone layout is shown in Figure 5.12.4.1a. The Robot Zone 
Validation Sheet must be completed and signed off for every robot – Robot Zone 
Validation Sheet.

 Printed copies are uncontrolled

Robot zone numbers shall follow the guidelines shown in Figure 5.12.4.2.a. 
which depend on the application of the robot. An example of carried robot 
application zones for a pallet transfer station is shown in Figure 5.12.4.2.b.

5.12.4.3 
Robot Zone Logic.  
 
The purpose of robot zone logic is to prevent more than one robot sharing the 
same physical space at the same time (crash).  Complete robot zone logic 
resides in two locations, the robot AND the PLC, both locations MUST comply to 
the Ford Standard.  A description of each part of the zone logic will follow.  
While entering the robot programs, the programmer must adhere to the robot 
zone number definitions as designated on the Control Drawings as described in 
Figure 5.12.4.3.a. PLC programmers MUST adhere to the Standard PLC Zone 
Logic.  An example of Zone #1 is shown, the same scenario exists for up to 14 
Zones. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 24 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
a. In the robot:    
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
  
 
       
 
 
 
          
 
            
  
       
    Figure 5.12.4.3.a.  Robot Zone Program Flowchart 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 25 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
b. In the PLC: 
Figure 5.12.4.3.b has examples of robot zone logic for a number of 
situations. All follow the same general convention.  
Permission to enter a zone is controlled by an assigned latch in the PLC.  
The robot output for clear of a specific zone (zone # 1 for example would be 
robot output #5) is used to unlatch the coil of an assigned binary latch. 
Nothing else is in that line of logic. 
Since the robot clear of the zone output is turned on most of the time, the 
latch is inhibited from latching up.  
The logic to drive the above latch coil is simply the NOT of robot output (#5 
in this case) in series with the NOT of any other robot that shares the same 
zone’s latch coil (in parallel with the same robot being in bypass). 
If three robots share the same zone, there would be three sets of parallel 
contacts in series with the Not of Robot #1 clear of the zone.  
If none of the robots sharing a zone need to enter the zone, all the latch coils 
are inhibited from latching up. All robots have equal access to the zone 
control. The first robot to "grab" control in the zone then sets the zone clear 
latch, thereby locking all the other robots from entering the shared physical 
space.  When the first robot to gain access to the zone completes their task 
that robot then turns on their output # 5 which resets their specific latch and 
allows the next robot to gain access.   
Robot Zone ControlLogix Logic shall adhere to the examples shown in 
Figure 5.12.4.3.b.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure 5.12.4.3.b.  Robot Zone Logic Sample 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 26 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
5.12.4.4 
Robot Zone Logic Prove-out.  
The critical nature of robot zones programmed correctly requires a thorough 
check-out prior to shipment of the new tooling.  
Run line in auto repeat 
– 
Starting with one robot at 10% and watch for it to enter first interference 
zone. 
– 
Once robot has entered the zone, pause/hold the robot while maintaining 
auto mode. 
– 
Allow surrounding interfering robots (one at a time) to begin motion also 
at 10% and ensure that the robot stops and waits for a signal from the 
PLC to allow it to enter the interfering zone. 
– 
If the robot does not wait, put it in hold and check PLC logic and 
add/verify that the interfering robot’s corresponding zone number is 
correctly preventing the robot from receiving its request approval. Restart 
with all robots back at home. 
– 
Repeat this process by allowing whichever robot entered the shared 
interfering zone 2nd, to now enter the shared interfering zone 1st and 
repeat the ‘pause/hold’ method listed above to back check that the zones 
work in both directions for both scenarios as robots always have 
potential to enter the interfering zone at different times in any given 
machine process. 
– 
Repeat the entire process for each robot and every zone throughout the 
machines process for each possible robot program select and each 
possible robot program jumps (I.E all program loops, return to home 
scenarios, return from pounce, tip dress, maintenance, cut tape, etc.) 
within each program select while also verifying clearances and cable 
routing. 
– 
Increase speeds of robots one by one from 10% to 100% incrementally 
(10%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%). Increasing the speed of the robots 
individually will give you situations that under “normal circumstances” 
wouldn’t occur. 
– 
Once each zone has individually been verified it is to be signed off by 
both the PLC and Robot programmer performing the validation on the 
Robot Zone Validation Sheet. 
– 
After completion of the Robot Zone Validation Sheet, the signed sheet is 
to then be brought to the Ford VO controls design follow up for review 
and approval. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 27 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS

5.12.5 
Location of Maintenance Positions  
5.12.5.1 
Tip Dress/Minor End Effector Maintenance Position. The goal of minor end 
effector maintenance and tip dress is to enhance throughput. This procedure is 
permitted because it is defined as a routine and repetitive process performed by 
properly trained and skilled individuals.  Proper orientation of the weld gun is 
paramount. 
The robot must complete the last two feet (60 cm) towards the maintenance 
position at a speed of not more than five inches (130 mm) per second. 
Ideally the Robot simulation should be done with the robot extended towards the 
gate with the weld caps about three feet (1 M) from the concrete floor and 
oriented at the tangent of a plane such that any motion of the robot would be 
AWAY from the operator, minimizing exposure to mechanical hazards. After this 
ideal condition is obtained, THEN the robot gate should be positioned on the 
layout. A dutch door shall be provided for all applications except for single 
function end effectors that are used for part handling only. Figure 5.8.5.1 shows 
an example of this positioning.   
Figure 5.8.5.1.  Robot Tip Dress/Maintenance Position 
5.12.5.2 
Pulled Tip Bypass. When the weld water is shut off, the robot shall automatically 
stop closing the weld gun as it proceeds through the rest of the welding program. 
The robot will move to the maintenance position where the skilled trades are to 
replace the weld caps.  Once the water is turned back on, the robot will return the 
part and start the weld process from the beginning. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 28 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.12.5.3 
For any pedestal application and/or minor end effector maintenance position for 
material handling robots, the robot shall be positioned such that the robot end 
effector and associated equipment can be reached by the maintenance 
personnel and worked on in a safe manner.  The robot shall also be positioned 
such that the part and end effector are no closer than 60 inches from any 
pedestal application (if present). The system integrator shall ensure that the 
maintenance personnel have a clear path from the gate to the pedestal 
application and from the pedestal application to the robot MH end effector.  
5.12.6 
Servo Weld Guns  
NOTE: Robot servo gun cables for robot to servo gun shall be provided by robot 
manufacturer. 
Each weld servo gun has a relationship between the servo motor current and 
weld tip force depending on a number of factors.  To that end each weld gun 
must utilize the equivalent of a "look-up table" for proper operation of the servo 
weld gun. A calibration procedure must be performed to establish values of servo 
motor current to weld tip force. Each servo weld gun must have this data stored 
in a robot table.   
Contact VOME Controls for the latest Kawasaki programming guide. 
Contact VOME Controls for the latest Fanuc programming guide.  
5.12.6.1 
The recommended procedure developed by the robot OEM must be followed for 
force setup. Actual weld force measured shall be with +/- 5% of the intended 
weld force for any given weld schedule. 
 
5.12.6.2 
Tip Wear Correction Logic for Non-Equalizer Servo Guns Procedure: 
1. PERFORMING MEASURE 1 CONDITION: Robot will determine the 
difference in caps (Value A) as shown below. 
 
2. PERFORMING MEASURE 2 CONDITION: Robot will determine the 
difference in the moving cap (Value B) by touching the reference plate (also 
referred to as the servo calibration block, see 6.3.6.3.1) with moving cap as

Due to the tip wear, the final correction involves moving the gun axis by “B” and 
fixed cap by value “C” as shown below. Note that robot moves up to compensate 
for fixed cap wear.

5.13 
RE-DEPLOYMENT OF ROBOTS 
5.13.1 
Only robots that are in full compliance with ANSI/RIA -1506-1999 may be used 
for new processes, programs, and/or new applications.  The reason for this is 
that the robot "Teach" mode must be "Control Reliable". When a robot is placed 
in "Teach" mode, we bypass an already "Control Reliable" circuit used for the 
safety gate. Both ANSI B11.19-2003 and ANSI/RIA 1506-1999 require any 
electrical circuit bypassing a "Control Reliable" circuit, that the circuit used to 
bypass the "Control Reliable" circuit must in itself also be "Control Reliable" to 
maintain system integrity. In general robots manufactured prior to the 
incorporation of this standard (in 1999) do not comply with this "Control Reliable" 
requirement. Also, the teach pendant must be the three-position type to comply 
with ANI/RIA 15.06. 
5.13.2 
Specifically, the Kawasaki "AD" Controller and earlier models are NOT compliant. 
The "C" Controller and later Controllers are. In some cases, the earlier "C" 
Controllers must have their teach pendants upgraded to bring the teach pendant 
into compliance.  
5.13.3 
Older robots may be re-deployed for exact-like kind robots used for the same 
applications when they are exactly replacing another robot of the same vintage or 
later vintage. This basically constitutes a repaired robot. The electrical circuit 
remains the same; however, care must be taken to ensure we do NOT EXPAND 
the existing robot envelope. In cases where a newer model (i.e. using an "AD" 
Controller) robot with a greater reach is used to "repair" the older robot, hard 
stops must be used to contain the robot envelope to the original envelope 
dimensions.     

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 31 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.14 
OPERATOR LOADS DIRECTLY TO ROBOT APPLICATION 
5.14.1 
In an operator load to robot application, the robot end of arm tooling becomes the 
part nest/load tooling. The robot and the operator share the same workspace at 
the same time. Power shall be removed from all devices that create a hazard to 
the operator. 
5.14.2 
An operator will be permitted to load directly to a robot provided ALL of the 
following conditions are met: 
• 
The robot is in a taught reference position for the load position. 
• 
The output power to the robot end effector is interrupted (control reliable). 
• 
The robot servomotor power is disabled and verified (control reliable). 
• 
It is verified the robot is still in the reference position (within 5mm) after motor 
power is disabled. 
5.14.3 
If an operator enters the shared space when any of the above conditions are not 
met, an e-stop to that robot occurs. 
5.14.4 
If fixed perimeter guarding is not used in the load area.  Perimeter controls will be 
added to prevent unauthorized access to the entire cell.  A violation of the 
perimeter controls during any part of the operation will result in stopping of the 
entire cell. 
5.14.5 
The robot end effector shall be designed such that part retention is maintained 
upon loss of power. (Reference VOSS Section 2.4.3 Pneumatic Design) 
5.14.6 
The controls shall be designed to provide at least three reference positions.  
Separate reference positions can be used for load height and/or EOA orientation 
if multiple models are required. 
5.14.7 
The robot reference position limits shall be set in the robot such that the robot will 
not drift more than 5 mm in any direction after motor power is disabled.  A loss of 
the reference position signal after motor power is disabled is an indication that an 
axis brake is failing  
5.14.8 
The operator shall be able to change between reference positions.  If a new 
reference position is selected while the robot is in motion, the robot will complete 
its program and return to the new reference position after its cycle is completed. 
5.14.9 
The robot controller e-stop shall be monitored via the PLC.  An e-stop to the 
robot controller shall stop the motion of the rest of the cell.  
5.14.10 
If a tool changer application is required, the design and robot program shall be 
such that an unintentional decouple in the operator load position will not allow 
gravity to drop the end effector to the floor. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 32 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5.14.11 
An additional stack light shall be provided (blue) at the operator load station to 
indicate robot motor power is disabled.  This will serve as a visual check that an 
operator can perform as part of the daily safety start up. 
5.14.12 
Location of Light Curtains/Scanners shall be compliant to w-EL4. 
5.15 
ROBOT LOADING AND UNLOADING TO A PRESS OR HEMMER 
5.15.1 
Any robot loading or unloading a press/hemmer/roll hemmer shall have a light 
screen tied to a press/hemmer tower-enable circuit that only enables motion 
when the robot is clear of the press/ hemmer tower. The light screen is to be 
wired to standard I/O. This application of the light screen is the same as a limit 
switch, it is not required to be a safety circuit. However, a light screen is not 
necessary if the robot entering the press/hemmer tower has safety zones (DCS / 
Cubic-S) that can be used to verify that the robot is clear when press/hemmer 
tower motion is permitted. 
5.16 
FORCE SENSTIVE ROBOTS (COLLABORATIVE OR COBOTS) 
5.16.1 
All Force Sensitive Robot applications must have a plan and that plan must 
reviewed with VO safety before any engineering of the robot can take place. 
5.16.2 
Only Force Sensitive Robot on the w-E1-QSSL (Electrical Qualified Safety 
Source list) document can be used. No other robots will be permitted in VO Final 
Assembly operations. 
5.16.3 
All Force Sensitive Robots fall under and must comply with FAS08-131. Along 
with FAS08-131 attachment 1 “Collaborative Robots and Robotic Systems”. 
5.16.4 
Every Force Sensing Robot application will have its own ECPL Placards. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005

Revision Date: June 08, 2025

                                      Figure 5.16.4 
5.16.5 
Every Collaborative robot will have its own risk assessment. This shall be 
separate of any system it may be part of. 
5.16.6 
The integrator will be responsible to provide any awareness barriers needed for 
the application. 
5.16.7 
The integrator will be responsible to provide all the collaborative robot signs need 
for the area. 
5.16.8 
The integrator will provide and ensure there is proper marking on the ground 
around the collaborative space. 
5.16.8.1 
Ground marking will adhere to FAS08-131 attachment 1 requirements.

5.16.9 
There are two types of approved Force sensitive Robot applications allowed 
within Ford Final Assembly Presence Sensing and Force Limiting. 
5.16.9.1 
A Presence Sensing Collaborative Robot application will use both the Robot and 
other w-E1 QSSL presence sensing device such as scanners, floor mats and 
light curtains. This typical setup will either slow the robot down or stop the robot 
when personal get close to the robot. 
5.16.9.2 
A Force limiting application lets operators get close to the robot and the robot will 
stop if it strikes something in its path. 
5.16.9.2.1 
When setting up a Force limiting design the tooling integrator will need to assess 
the possibility where a Cobot my strike a person and adjust the force in that 
motion to comply with acceptable standards. The integrator will need to have 
Ford safety and ergo study the path and take strike measurements. 
5.16.10 
The integrator will document how the Cobot safeties are setup and how the 
integrator will protect the safety setup from unintended change. Example: extra 
passwords in the robot, map of where the area scanner scans, witness marks of 
where and how safety devices are mounted. 
5.16.11 
All Force sensing robots will interlock to a PLC. 
5.16.11.1 
Every application will have the following PLC to Robot interlocks as a minimum. 
Robot to PLC 
• Robot at home 
• Robot at work 
• Robot Faulted 
• Robot has Force Fault 
• Robot Ok to Run 
• Robot Process complete 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 5 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 35 of 35 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• Robot clear of zone 
 
PLC to Robot 
• Initiate robot 
• Robot Stop 
• Robot Ok to proceed 
• Fault Reset 
• Reset process complete 
5.16.12 
Dress package for EOAT on a Cobot 
5.16.12.1 
The integrator shall be responsible to ensure that the Dress package for EOAT 
must not cause faults during Cobot operations.  
5.16.12.2 
The dress package must comply to Collaborative robot standards. Meaning as 
the robot does its operation the harness cannot have the potential to stab, cut, 
maim, or pinch an operator. 
5.16.12.3 
Robot Pedestals shall be designed so that the robot safeties will work per the 
manufactures design and the pedestal itself will not cause the robot to be unsafe.
