6.1 
INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE 
6.1.1 
Introduction and Purpose 
6.1.1.1 
The purpose of this welding section is to describe requirements, guidelines and 
performance expectations of welding and joining equipment and processes. The 
equipment may be supplied as components or as a complete system. Application 
of these standards will reduce the need for special equipment and tools. In 
addition, the time required for inquiries, purchase orders and approvals will be 
reduced. 
6.1.1.2 
 Joining equipment shall meet these GVOSS specifications and any deviations 
must have a GVOSS Action Request Form, outlined in Section 1.1.2, approved in 
E2KS prior to Design Approval. Proposals to deviate from this standard should 
start with a review with the Ford BCE Joining Engineer assigned to the project. 
6.1.2 
Reference Specifications and Standards  
6.1.2.1 
Regional Vehicle Operations Operating Procedures which may be referenced in 
this section: 
• VOP SSN-004 Nondestructive Testing of Resistance Spot Welds in Steel and 
Aluminum Body Structures 
• VOP SSN-006 Nondestructive Testing of Adhesive Joints for Steel & 
Aluminum Body Structures & Closures 
• VOP SSG-008 Joining Quality Program 
6.1.2.2 
Applicable Ford Engineering Specifications are defined on program-specific basis 
by Product Development. The applicable Engineering Specifications released by 
PD for a specific program are listed in CAD. 
 Controlled copies are available in FordDoc:  http://forddoc.secure24.ford.com 
Engineering Specifications are continuously being created or updated, so it is 
important to check with Ford Body Construction Engineering Joining regarding 
those which apply to a given program. 
6.1.3 
Definitions and Terminology 
The following joint information shall be provided for all welding and joining 
equipment: 
ADAPTIVE CONTROL - The adaptive control system measures the current and 
voltage at the electrode in real time. Mathematical algorithms calculate resistance 
curves and energy balance for the given weld. The weld current is then adjusted 
to compensate for small variances from the reference curve as welding 
continues.                                                    

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 5 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
LASER WIRE FEEDER SYSTEM – The filler wire the system uses to weld the 
roof panel to the body together is fed to the Laser Head with the Laser Wire 
Feeder System.  
BUTTON - The part of a spot weld which tears out during destructive testing of 
welded steel. It may include all or part of the nugget, the heat affected zone and 
base metal. A hole is left in the mating sheet(s). (AWS D8.1M:2006)  
CLOSE OUT WELDS - Welds that cannot be inspected by in-process pry testing 
or other Nondestructive Test (NDT) methods at available inspections stations due 
to access restrictions. These welds are typically covered by parts that are loaded 
downstream of the welding operation.  
COMMON WELDS - Welds that are not specified as Control Welds.   
CONTROL WELDS - Welds critical to structural integrity, safety or Federal 
Compliance (ref ES-1N261-A). The welds are identified by an inverted delta 
symbol (   ) on engineering documentation.  
DELTA WELDS ( 
 ) – Also known as INVERTED DELTA WELDS.  See 
CONTROL WELDS.  
ELECTRODE - The generic term for any current-carrying portion of a welding 
machine that comes in contact with the welded area of the material.  
ELECTRODE ADAPTER - Used in Resistance Spot Welding (RSW). The 
transition piece that allows current and water to flow into the electrode cap. The 
electrode adapter may be attached directly to the welding gun body by means of 
a clamp, or to an electrode holder.  
ELECTRODE CAP - The replaceable point of contact between the resistive 
welding equipment and the material being welded. The electrode cap conducts 
the current to the metal to be welded and is held in place by the electrode 
adapter.   
ELECTRODE HOLDER ASSEMBLY - Used in RSW. Current-carrying 
components that allow an electrode adapter to be attached to the “working” end 
of a cylinder.  
FIXTURE (HARD AUTOMATION) WELD GUN - See RESISTANCE WELD 
GUNS.  
FLOOR MOUNTED (PEDESTAL) WELD GUN - See RESISTANCE WELD 
GUNS.  
LASER WELDING HEAD - The Laser Processing Optics used in the Laser Head 
efficiently transfer the laser beam from the fiber cable to the focus head. Laser 
Head properties should exhibit minimal spherical aberration, optimal beam spatial 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 6 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
profile, and a precision beam diameter. This Laser Welding Head has two options 
installed to track weld joints – the tactile finger and the pressure wheel.  
INTEGRAL FIXTURE (HARD AUTOMATION) WELD GUN - See RESISTANCE 
WELD GUNS.  
INVERTED DELTA WELDS (  ) - See CONTROL WELDS.  
LASER BOOTH ENCLOSURE - The laser booth enclosure is a safety protective 
device. It is light tight to protect persons outside from welding laser radiation 
inside the booth.  
LASER CLASS 4 - Class 4 Lasers emit radiation from the 302.5 to 106 nm range 
at a power that exceeds 500 mW. Observing Class 4 laser radiation is hazardous 
to both the eyes and skin, and even observing diffuse reflections can be 
hazardous. There is also a risk of fire and/or explosion with Class 4 laser 
radiation.  
LASER BEAM WELDING (LBW) - Laser beam welding is a welding technique 
used to join multiple pieces of metal through the use of a laser. The beam 
provides a concentrated heat source, allowing for narrow, deep welds and high 
welding rates. Welding lasers used at Ford Motor Company are Class 4 Lasers.  
LASER SAFETY OFFICER - A laser safety officer must be appointed by a written 
document before the laser equipment is set into operation. The laser safety 
officer is responsible for training (at least once a year) the operators of the laser 
equipment with regard to the hazards caused.  
NON-PRYABLE WELDS - Welds that cannot be inspected in-process with the 
pry test (also known as pry check or non-destructive chisel test) method. 
Potential reasons for welds to be considered non-pryable are documented in 
Resistance Spot Welding Engineering Specifications.  
PRESSURE WHEEL - The pressure wheel at the end of the laser head presses 
one metal panel end down to other to get metal-to-metal contact for the weld.  
PROCESS BACKUP - The means of production when the primary production 
equipment is offline.  
RESISTANCE WELD GUNS - As used in other parts of this specification:  
INTEGRAL FIXTURE (HARD AUTOMATION) WELD GUN - A spot welding gun, 
installed as hard tooling, which combines the transformer and the welding gun 
into one unit. Careful consideration must be given to accessibility for 
maintenance when using integral guns in hard automation applications.  
FIXTURE (HARD AUTOMATION) WELD GUN - A spot welding gun, installed as 
hard tooling, which utilizes a remotely located transformer. Secondary 
connections to the gun are generally made with air cooled cables.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 7 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
FLOOR MOUNTED (PEDESTAL) WELD GUN - A spot welding gun, mounted in 
a fixed position that is used in conjunction with a material handling robot or other 
means of part positioning to produce multiple welds per part.  
PORTABLE WELD GUN (MANUAL) - A hand positioned and operated 
spotwelding gun.  
ROBOT WELD GUN - A spot welding gun that is positioned by means of a robot.  
SECONDARY (STANDBY) EQUIPMENT - Additional equipment supplied with 
the original complement of tools to help maintain full production rates.   
SHUNTING CURRENT - The current that is diverted away from the desired weld 
location. This can result from either another spot weld or any other conductive 
material that “shorts” across the sheet metal being welded.  
TACTILE FINGER - The tactile finger at end of laser head is a metallic probe that 
facilitates tracking of welding joint during laser beam welding.  
WELD BEAD - A weld deposit resulting from a single longitudinal progression of 
a welding operation along welded joint.  
WELD EXPULSION - Weld Expulsion is forceful ejection of molten metal from the 
forming weld. Expulsion is caused by lack of control of the expanding molten 
material between the electrode tip faces.  
WELD INDENTATION - The impression made by the welding electrodes or 
electrode caps into the surface of the metal during the spot-welding process.  
WELD SCHEDULE (WELD PARAMETERS) - Weld Schedule/Parameters is a 
record of all welding machine settings plus identification of the machine needed 
to produce a weld for a given material of a given size and finish.  
6.2 
 TIER 1 INTEGRATOR RESPONSIBILITIES 
6.2.1 
General Responsibilities 
6.2.1.1 
Tooling suppliers shall provide joining equipment engineered to produce the 
number of joints required to sustain continuous quality levels at rates required by 
the engineered process and for periods consistent with planned maintenance 
timing. Nonexpendable (also known as non-consumable) items for joining guns 
shall be designed for a minimum of 10 million cycles under production conditions. 
6.2.1.2 
Only approved Tier 2 sources of joining equipment, specified on the Approved 
Source List in the Scope of Work, shall be used by the tooling suppliers. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 8 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.2.1.3 
Joining process capability validation shall be performed on the completed tool as 
it will be used in production prior to shipment from the tooling supplier facility. If 
actual prototype/production-intent sheet metal parts are not available for buy off, 
coupon material representative of the substrate material, coating type and 
weight, and thickness intended for production shall be used. Section 1.2.6 
defines the requirements as part of Tier 1 Integrator Buyoff responsibilities. 
6.2.2 
Project Documentation 
6.2.2.1 
The following joint information shall be provided for all welding and joining 
equipment: 
• A Weld Information Spreadsheet (WIS) or Cross-Reference List (CRL) in an 
Excel format shall be completed by the Tier 1 Integrator no later than Tool 
Buy-Off and shall be updated immediately after any modifications are made to 
the tool through 45 days past Job 1. At a minimum, the WIS must be submitted 
to the SPL/WIM Team and Ford Body Construction Engineering (BCE) Joining 
once per month to ensure documents are kept up to date.  Any changes 
compared to the prior submission must be highlighted.  Contact BCE Joining 
for the exact format of spreadsheet to be used on a specific program. 
• A 1A4 Datasheet in either an Excel format or within an on-line 1A4 database 
shall be completed prior to tool buy-off for every joining application. The 1A4 
encapsulates all weld timer parameters, pertinent robot welding parameters, 
weld gun, tip dresser consumable part numbers, metal stack-ups, joining 
sequence illustrations, rivet gun, rivet gun consumables, rivet/die information, 
rivet sequence, rivet program number, tool details and IP address. Tier 1 
Integrators are responsible for making all updates to the 1A4 Data Sheets 
through Job 1 + 45 Days. Contact BCE Joining for the 1A4 template to be 
used on a specific program. At buy-off, a hard copy shall be placed at each 
operation in clear reference pockets (Tarifold ® #TFIW291, Ford MCC# 
1011719181 or equivalent). The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible to conduct a 
physical audit of the 1A4’s for each milestone (TT, PP, MP1, and MP2) of the 
program. The Tier 1 integrator shall update the JSVCS with the audit status 
and send the update to the BCE Joining and plant-joining engineer.  Open 
issues found shall be documented in the open issue list in the JSVCS.  
 
• A Joining Matrix in a excel format shall be completed by the Tier 1 integrator 
no later than tool buy-off and shall be updated immediately after any 
modifications are made to process or product through 45 days past Job 1. 
Contact BCE Joining for the exact format of the matrix on a specific program.  
• Joining System Validation Check Sheet (JSVCS): This is an excel matrix that 
encapsulates the key setup requirements associated with each joining 
process. The JSVCS shall be completed both prior to buyoff (refer to Section 
1.2.6.3.11) and after installation at the manufacturing site. Contact BCE 
Joining for the latest template. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 9 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• The Global Aligned Weld Number label format shall be used in all documents 
to describe all joints: welds, weld nuts, weld bolts, rivets, studs, etc. 
 
 
SPECIAL NOTE for programs with carryover tooling, the Tier 1 Integrator for that 
tooling subsystem shall be responsible for ensuring that WIS and 1A4’s are 
created for any carryover welding applications within the subsystem being kitted 
that don't already have them. Physical audits may be required to confirm 
processed joint locations and joint equipment information. The supplier shall 
include this cost within the request for quote that they submit for the statement of 
work affecting the given subsystem.   
 
SPECIAL NOTE: For programs without a dedicated BCE Joining Engineer -- The 
Tier 1 Integrator Joining Engineer will be responsible for ensuring all Section 6 
deliverables are met and will report to the program BCE Launch Leader as 
necessary. Contact BCE Joining group for any questions regarding whether this 
applies to a given program. 
6.2.2.2 
Robot Controller Programs shall use an expanded weld label for every spot weld 
based on the Global Aligned Weld Label.  It must consist of the Weld number as 
an exact reference to CAD supplemented by various suffices such as Stackup, 
Variant etc. The correct format is program dependent and must be aligned with 
the Dedicated Joining Engineer prior to program. 
Example: 80011-02-R_M_P703_CC_A_3T 
Joining Controller Programs (schedules) will utilize only the Global Aligned Weld 
Number label for each joint. 
6.2.2.2.1 
Every robot controller shall have the robot label programmed into the controller.  
The robot label must include the following: IE Group, three-digit station number, 
hand L or R where applicable, and two-digit robot number.   
 
Fanuc Example:  
9B-020-06     programmed into HOSTNAME 
Kawasaki Example: S9B-020-06 programmed into the FTP Server Setting, User 
Name field, found in AUX 0815 field 
6.2.2.3 
Each unique joining method (weld, rivet, clinch, etc.) number will receive its own 
program (schedule) in the applicable controller. The only exception is for “half 
common” (i.e., different part numbers, but same XYZ location and metal stack-
up) situations, where a common program or common schedule call can be 
utilized if it does not adversely affect the quality of the result. However, if the 
joining condition requires individualized parameters to achieve the desired quality 
and stability, it should be assumed that the half common weld no longer qualify 
for exemption. In such circumstances, programs should be set up to allow for 
unique weld calls to be introduced for these cases.

6.2.2.4 
Documentation Verification 
WIS and Robot Programs will be verified for correct weld labels and spot location 
by utilizing the Automated Weld Label and Location Tool.   
WIS files and robot backups will be provided to the dedicated BCE Joining 
Engineer for execution of the verification. 
To perform spot location verification the below must be completed. 
• User frame and tool frame [Kawasaki: Tool,FTOOL, and WORK]: must be 
set correctly to the process.   
For Kawasaki depending on the standard revision the (WORK) maybe provided 
in a separate file.   
6.2.3 
Coupons 
6.2.3.1 
This section identifies the coupon requirements for aluminum and some high 
strength steels. In addition, adaptive weld controls may require coupons to 
execute weld schedule set-up.   
6.2.3.2 
When preparing for any joining or cutting tool tryout event such as resistance, 
projection, laser, self-pierce rivet, clinching, GMAW, etc., some amount of 
coupon material is generally required to validate these processes and comply 
with GVOSS Section 1.2.6, which addresses Tier 1 Integrator Buyoff 
responsibilities. 
6.2.3.3 
It is the responsibility of the Tier 1 Integrator to procure all coupons necessary to 
satisfy these needs. The coupon material must match the material specification 
of the production part the coupons are representing and come from Ford’s 
approved material suppliers. Situations may arise where surrogate material is 
acceptable. For questions regarding this requirement, contact the appropriate 
Ford Joining Engineer. 
6.2.3.4 
Steel  
For steels with minimum ultimate tensile strength greater than or equal to 590   
MPa, the coupon material must come from the same steel source of the 
production part. 
6.2.3.5 
Aluminum 
6.2.3.5.1 
Per the material list acquired via Teamcenter in conjunction with direction from 
the Ford Joining Engineer, the methods in this section shall be used to acquire 
material for gun certification and joint feasibility testing.   
6.2.3.5.2 
For unique gauges and grades of aluminum sheet metal which are not currently 
used in production at Ford, Tier 1 Integrators shall procure such material directly 
from Ford’s aluminum suppliers, e.g., Aleris, Novelis and Alcoa.   

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 11 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.2.3.5.3 
For extrusion and hydroform materials, Tier 1 Integrators must procure such 
material directly from Ford’s suppliers, e.g., SAPA, Kaiser and Schuler. Special 
attention shall be noted regarding the temper.   
6.2.3.5.4 
For joints which include steel applications, steel shall be procured directly from a 
steel supplier and not via warehouse or open market. The metal certification 
must be included with the steel.   
6.2.3.5.5 
For gauges and grades of aluminum sheet metal which are currently used in 
production at Ford, Tier 1 Integrators must acquire such material from the 
applicable Ford Stamping Plants. The Joining Engineer shall coordinate these 
activities with the Tier 1 Integrator and appropriate plant personnel.   
6.2.3.5.6 
Tier 1 Integrators are responsible for managing all material with the following 
details: Gauge, Grade, Temper, Supplier, Coil Number, and in the case of 6xxx 
series material the Continuous Anneal Solution Heat-treat date (i.e., CASH date).   
6.2.3.5.7 
6xxx series material must be scrapped after CASH date + 180 days and may not 
be used for testing.   
6.2.3.5.8 
Tier 1 Integrators are responsible for heat treating material, as required. 
 
 
6.3 
 RESISTANCE SPOT WELDING EQUIPMENT 
6.3.1 
Process Requirement 
The direct current welding shall be employed for all resistance welding  
applications. Push/pull or in-series welding methods shall not be used. 
6.3.2 
Weld Schedules and Steppers 
6.3.2.1 
General Recommendations 
6.3.2.1.1 
The weld schedules included in this section are Nominal Design Resistance 
Welding Schedules. Use of these guidelines will result in welding equipment that 
accommodates a wider scope of materials and machine characteristics. 
 
NOTE: Design Schedules are intended for equipment sizing only. Tier 1 
Integrators are responsible for determining optimized parameters to be used in 
the weld controllers. These parameters must result in spot welds meeting all 
engineering specification requirements while achieving flash free performance in 
adaptive control mode. BCE Joining may assist inexperienced Integrators when 
requested by suggesting parameter starting points for stackups where an 
optimized schedule is unknown. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 12 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.3.2.1.2 
The timer heat shall never (at the end of the heat stepper table) exceed 85% of 
his power capacity to allow proper Constant Current Regulation. The Tier 1 
Integrator has to provide a sheet showing the measured current on all guns with 
85% heat (timer in non-Constant Current mode) and the requested current acc. 
Weld Study. The measured current at 85% heat must be minimum 20% above 
the current of the Weld Study. 
6.3.2.1.3 
Nominal Design Resistance Welding Schedules may be determined using the 
Resistance Welding Data Weld Schedule Selection Flowchart as shown in Figure 
6.3.2. The flowchart refers to an adjustment chart (Table 6.3.2.a) and four 
material-based charts (Tables 6.3.2.b through 6.3.2.d). These charts may be 
used to determine weld parameters based on material and Governing Metal 
Thickness (GMT). 
6.3.2.1.4 
For determining nominal weld schedules, all metal stack-ups shall be considered 
loose. 
6.3.2.1.5 
Weld hold time equal to 50% of the prescribed weld time is to be included in 
design assumptions.  
6.3.2.1.6 
BCE Joining shall provide initial stepper program and tip dress counter values. 
6.3.2.1.7 
During TTO and TT build phases, weld parameters must be validated for 
accuracy and consistency across all variants and paired tooling operations by the 
Tier 1 Integrator.

SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 13 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.3.3 
Flash Free and Adaptive Weld Controls Implementation 
6.3.3.1 
Flash Free Implementation  
6.3.3.1.1 
Every resistance spot weld shall be optimized to eliminate expulsion, burrs, and 
slag which are unacceptable without compromising the required satisfactory weld 
button size. Tool suppliers are responsible for flash-free performance according 
to the following schedule:  
TT: Joining System Validation Check Sheet completed  
PP: Baseline flash free percentage established  
MP1: 50% Flash Free  
OKTB: 75% Flash Free  
MP1 + 90 Days: 90% Flash Free  
Achieving 90% Flash Free at MP1 + 90 Days assumes a sufficient quantity of 
subassemblies for each model have run through a given line.  If the quantity is 
deemed insufficient by the BCE program implementation team, that team and the 
Tier 1 Integrator shall develop a modified timing plan to deliver 90% Flash Free.  
Note:  Although all build areas are required to meet the metrics above, priority 
must be placed on those areas where flash on the body would be visible to a 
customer. The prioritization plan will be agreed between the BCE program 
implementation team and Joining Engineer.  
6.3.3.1.2 
The following documents shall be used at a minimum to ensure flash free 
implementation processes are standardized globally.  Regions may add to this as 
desired to support their own efforts. 
6.3.3.1.2.1 
Flash Free Daily Update 
6.3.3.1.2.2 
Flash Free Tracking and Buyoff Sheet 
6.3.3.1.2.3 
Flash Free Work Plan 
6.3.3.1.2.4 
Joining System Validation Check Sheet (refer to Section 1.2.6.3.11)   
6.3.3.1.3 
For new equipment launch, evidence of the work to attain flash free shall be 
captured in the Joining System Validation Check Sheet in GVOSS 1.2.6.3.11.  It 
is the Tier 1 Integrator’s responsibility to identify and close. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 14 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.3.3.1.4 
Welds that still show expulsion in spite of this work shall be identified and 
documented with the root cause in the comments section of the check sheets, 
then reviewed with the given program's Body Leader, BCE Joining Engineer, the 
affected plant's Weld Engineer and Manufacturing Engineering Manager for 
consideration as exceptions to this objective. 
6.3.3.1.5 
For kit work/minor launch: It is the Tier 1 Integrator’s responsibility to achieve 
expulsion reduction for new items provided through the kit work. It is their 
responsibility to close items on the Joining System Validation Check Sheet 
relating to the kit work.  Items that are identified as ongoing maintenance issues, 
that were not part of the kit work, are the responsibility of the plant.  These items 
are to be identified and documented to the launch team for disposition to the 
responsible plant personnel. 
6.3.3.1.6 
For kit work that requires robot reprogramming: Tier 1 Integrator is responsible 
for all checklist items that relate to robot programming (square to metal, no edge 
welding, etc.). Tier 1 Integrator is not responsible for worn cutter blades, mis-
aligned adaptors or gun maintenance. These items are the responsibility of the 
plant. 
6.3.3.1.7 
For kit work requiring weld gun modification or replacement: Tier 1 Integrator is 
responsible for all checklist items relating to robot programming, tip dress 
programming, gun mechanical issues, etc.  Tier 1 Integrator is not responsible for 
worn cutter blades unless the weld gun modification includes a change to a 
different cutter based on adaptor design. 
6.3.3.1.8 
The use of anti-spatter oils and sprays on body components, subassemblies, and 
assemblies to try to mitigate weld flash adherence to the sheet metal is 
prohibited. 
6.3.3.2 
Adaptive Weld Control Implementation  
6.3.3.2.1 
All weld controls shall be capable of adaptive (also known as "regulation") 
welding defined by the ability to adjust welding parameters during the weld 
process based on the secondary voltage being measured at or near the 
electrode adaptors. 
6.3.3.2.2 
BCE Joining will determine the weld controller types that meet adaptive welding 
criteria. 
6.3.3.2.3 
All RSW systems shall be prepped (wired) adaptive ready.  Following are the 
responsibilities of suppliers relative to secondary voltage wiring: 
6.3.3.2.4 
Weld gun supplier provides connection points for wires.   The contacts for 
secondary voltage wires must be at the last weld current conducting part before 
arm/adapter connection. A fixation clip with strain relief function must be used. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 15 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
 
( 
 
 
 
Figure 6.3.3.4 Example of adaptive wire attachment to gun 
6.3.3.2.5 
Robot supplier provides secondary voltage wires in internal and external dress 
packages.  For Next-Gen controls refer to Section 5 Robots or for GVOSS 
controls refer to Section 13 Robots to see provision of the secondary voltage 
wires in the robot dress pack. 
6.3.3.2.6 
Tier 1 Integrator connects wires to gun and to weld controller.  Care must be 
taken to ensure wires are secure, but not so tight that they will bind with gun and 
robot movement.  Note:  additional wire has to be added by the Tier 1 Integrator 
from base of robot to weld controller. 
6.3.3.2.7 
Weld control supplier works with Tier 1 Integrator to approve and buy-off on 
dress package for all wiring associated with adaptive welding. 
6.3.3.2.8 
Tool suppliers must begin to optimize welding to avoid weld expulsion during the 
TT Build phase.  Tool suppliers are responsible for flash-free performance 
according to the following schedule: 
• PP: 25% active    
• MP1: 50% active   
• OKTB: 75% active  
• MP1 + 90 Days: 100% active  
6.3.3.2.9 
Tier 1 Integrators shall have qualified welding engineers at the launch site to 
support conditional weld certification, flash free and adaptive control 
implementation, as well as open issue resolution through validation. Ford, at their 
discretion, may require a given Tier 1 Integrator to furnish qualified joining 
support to resolve joining-related open issues through launch exit (at least MP1 + 
90 days) at no expense to Ford Motor Company.

Note 2: Welding condition becomes more critical as outer metal thickness ratio (X/Z) increases. 
Note 3: Gauge ratio limits are described in ESB5MA-1N261-AA Resistance Spot Welding.

Page 21 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.3.4 
Weld Gun Requirements 
6.3.4.1 
General Requirements 
6.3.4.1.1 
The only approved weld guns are as noted on the Approved Source List / 
Statement of Work for a given program. 
6.3.4.1.2 
All weld gun designs shall be created from the standard libraries in FIDES.  
Previously approved non-standard component designs shall not be reused for 
new tooling without approval from BCE Joining.   
NOTE:  To promote commonality and avoid unnecessary unique weld gun 
designs, symmetrically opposite (RH Shown/LH Opposite) weld gun designs are 
prohibited. 
6.3.4.1.3 
Legacy non-standard component designs shall not be used.  
6.3.4.1.4 
Gun designs shall be sent to the gun manufacturer for review.  The gun 
manufacturer is responsible for coordinating gun designs with all tool suppliers 
for each plant with the objective of minimizing the number of component and gun 
designs for each plant. 
6.3.4.1.5 
All welding guns, pivot units, slide units, clamps and other moving devices must 
be shown in the open and closed condition, and/or in any index position required.  
6.3.4.1.6 
Solid, bent, or offset electrode designs shall be prohibited. 
6.3.4.1.7 
No welding system shall be permitted which allows the energizing of a 
transformer primary circuit when any part of the secondary circuit or circuits are 
open. 
6.3.4.1.8 
For pinch or X-type guns, the welding work-plane shall intercept the gun arm 
pivot pin when in the welding position and with the tips normal to metal. 
6.3.4.2 
Weld Gun Forces 
The required gun force is related to the electrode size calculated according to the 
Resistance Spot Welding Engineering Specification. The following minimum 
operating forces at the caps have to be achieved:  
 
• 13 mm electrodes: 2,7 kN 
• 16 mm electrodes: 4,5 kN 
• 20 mm electrodes: 5,5 kN for GMT <= 2mm; 6,5 kN for GMT > 2 mm 
 
Deviations require approval from Ford BCE Joining. 
NOTE:  Actual weld forces shall not exceed 90% of the maximum force capability 
of the weld gun. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 22 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.3.5 
Water Flow Requirements for Weld Guns   
6.3.5.1 
Cooling circuit diagrams for each gun type have to provided, showing cooling 
layout and required flow rates for the gun components.     
6.3.5.2 
A water flow certification shall be performed by the weld gun manufacturer with a 
pressure differential at the gun main ports of 0.15 MPa.    
6.3.5.3 
The minimum required flow rate on each electrode cap at this pressure 
differential is 4 l/min. Specific designs may require less than 4 l/min according to 
gun manufacturer specifications. In these cases, the required flow rate shall be 
documented in the Weld Gun Flow Check-Off Sheet (Ref Section 1.2.5). 
Additional flow for the transformer and servo drive has to suit gun manufacturer 
requirement.   
6.3.5.4 
The flow measurements have to be documented in the Weld Gun Flow Check-
Off Sheet (Ref Section 1.2.6). Results have to be supplied digitally to the Tier 1 
Integrator(s). After installation of the weld gun, the Tier 1 Integrator has to re-
measure all values at the same pressure differential and add into the Check-Off 
Sheet. The recorded values shall match with a tolerance of 10%. 
6.3.5.5 
The Tier 1 Integrator is expected to design and provide a weld cooling water 
distribution system capable of delivering at least 0.15MPa differential pressure at 
the gun main manifolds during full production.  
6.3.5.6 
The station supply pressure required to achieve the above water flow shall not 
exceed 80% of the maximum facility supply pressure at the tool or 0.55 MPa 
(whichever is lower). The facility supply pressure at the tool must be determined 
by field survey or as provided by facility engineering. 
6.3.6 
Electrode Caps   
6.3.6.1 
The weld caps used for Joining Capability Validation at the Tier 1 Integrator shall 
be the same manufacturer and alloy used for welding certification at the plant. 
6.3.6.2 
Refer to specification WC1-004 "RESISTANCE SPOT WELDING ELECTRODE 
CAPS" for selecting weld caps. All new programs will use: 
6.3.6.2.1 
Type F domed electrode caps for robot, pedestal, and manual applications. 
6.3.6.2.2 
Type B truncated electrode caps for fixture and hard tool applications. 
6.3.6.3 
Flat caps shall not be used. If radius caps are allowed by deviation, ISO 5821 
Type A caps shall be used, but this is restricted two-metal weld stack-ups and 
the cap can only be used on the visible side of the metal stack-up.  Type A caps 
require automatic tip dressing every 50 welds.  To account for the frequent cap 
changes that will be necessary, tip changers are also required. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 23 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.3.6.4 
Weld processes and metal stack-ups that require the use of different sized weld 
caps on the same weld gun are unacceptable. Cross cap conditions shall be 
avoided. 
6.3.6.5 
Weld caps will be supplied by the Tier 1 Integrator through the TT Build Phase, 
up to a maximum of six sets per weld gun, unless other arrangements are made 
with the receiving plant(s). 
6.3.7 
Robot and Pedestal Mounted Weld Gun Applications 
6.3.7.1 
Refer to Section 8 and 14 Control Logix for controls and electrical requirements 
for weld guns. 
6.3.7.2 
The following describes additional requirements specifically for robot and 
pedestal weld guns. 
6.3.7.3 
Integral MFDC weld guns shall be used.  The positive electrode shall be placed 
on the moveable side of the gun. 
6.3.7.4 
It is recommended that the weld gun negative cap be used for any non-standard 
cap (e.g. radius or offset angle). This is done to take advantage of the cooling of 
the negative electrode in DC resistance welding processes (Peltier effect). 
6.3.7.5 
Dual robot and pedestal weld guns shall have individual transformers for each 
weld gun secondary to allow for individual control of the secondary.  
6.3.7.6 
For Next-Gen controls refer to Section 5 Robots or for GVOSS controls refer to 
Section 13 Robots: to see robot End-of-arm Tooling (EOAT) requirements. 
6.3.7.7 
Minimum tip opening shall be 40 mm for automatic tip dressing. 
6.3.7.8 
Robot maintenance programs shall position the weld gun at an attitude that 
prevents contamination of the actuator by water or debris when the electrodes 
are changed or dressed. 
6.3.7.9 
Pedestal mounted weld guns shall be mounted as shown below to prevent 
contamination of the actuator by water and debris when the electrodes are 
changed or dressed. If the weld gun cannot be mounted as shown, a 
contamination shield must be added to protect the actuator. The shield must be 
approved in writing by the weld gun manufacturer. 
6.3.7.10 
Pedestal mounted weld guns shall be mounted to prevent excessive gun 
movement that may affect weld or tip dress quality.  Example shown below uses 
gussets to accomplish this requirement. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 24 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled

axis perpendicular 
to floor.
C Gun – Tip axis 
parallel to floor.
Pinch Gun – Tip 
axis perpendicular 
to floor.
C Gun – Tip axis 
parallel to floor.
 
Figure 6.3.7.10 Pedestal-Mounted Weld Gun Examples 
 
6.3.8 
Fixture Weld Gun Applications  
The following describes additional requirements specifically for weld guns 
mounted in fixture tooling or presses.  These types of guns should be avoided. 
6.3.8.1 
Integral weld guns are preferred. 
6.3.8.2 
The weld gun shall be insulated from ground. 
6.3.8.3 
Simultaneous welding of guns in a fixture is not permitted. 
6.3.8.4 
Weld guns are to be located to design position at the mean of their adjustment 
range. 
6.3.8.5 
Evaluate surrounding tooling conditions to determine accessibility of gun 
(particularly leaf shunts on integral guns) for maintenance. All welding guns, 
secondary conductors, water lines, transformers, tooling components and all 
other apparatus within a specific fixture and/or machine shall be readily 
accessible for maintenance without the removal of any other piece of equipment. 
Transformer and pneumatic cylinder replacement shall require a maximum of 30 
minutes and shunt replacement shall require a maximum of 15 minutes. 
6.3.8.6 
Fixture weld guns shall make no more than two welds per job. A single weld is 
preferred. 
6.3.9 
Manual Weld Gun Applications 
6.3.9.1 
This section describes additional requirements specifically for manual operated 
weld guns and associated stations components. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 25 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.3.9.2 
Manual resistance spot welding gun design and sizing shall conform to the 
General Weld Gun Requirements of Sections 6.3.4.1 through 6.3.5.  Manual 
applications shall be MFDC integral transformer type having adaptive control 
capability. 
6.3.9.3 
Guideline for manual welding processes is to allow the following grouping of spot 
welds for one gun into three categories: 
• Thin Gauge welding (2-T and 3-T welds with a total stack up from 1.3 to 
4mm) 
• Heavy Gauge welding (2-T and 3-T welds with a total stack up from 3.5 to 
6mm) 
• Boron Steel spot welds 
 
Note:  A 6.0 mm total stack up is the maximum permitted for manual weld gun 
applications. 
6.3.9.4 
Manual Weld Gun Schedules 
One weld button is permitted in addition to the retract button.  Weld schedule 
selection and equipment sizing will follow 6.3.4.2.  The force range and 
associated air pressure will fall within the weld gun manufacturer’s 
recommendation and the receiving plant’s compressed air service stand at 90% 
efficiency.  Deviations to the design weld schedules will be made in writing and 
agreed to by the BCE Joining Engineer or receiving Plant Joining Engineer. 
6.3.9.5 
Ergonomics and Safety for Manual Welding 
6.3.9.5.1 
Operator ergonomics studies shall be performed and satisfy all requirements and 
reporting described under Section 1.6.  The ergonomic studies will consider all 
motion expected of the operator including obtaining the weld gun, orienting the 
weld gun and presenting the electrodes to the first spot, position and posture with 
hands on the weld gun and buttons, performing any subsequent repositioning 
and welding, forces of gravity and the effect of the balancing system, performing 
manual tip dress, returning the weld gun to the rest position, as well as any other 
operator tasks. 
Note:  If the Ergo-Study does not show green conditions, a handling aid has to be 
provided or the process has to be changed. 
6.3.9.5.2 
Hoists, Jibs, Bridge Cranes and Related Equipment shall conform to Section 
1.3.17.  Elevation drawings will be created to illustrate that the highest and lowest 
weld positions can be accommodated by the overhead positioning equipment 
when considering the weld gun brackets, balancer, hooks, and insulators along 
with the part with tooling and operator floor position.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 26 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.3.9.5.3 
Weld gun push buttons shall be the guarded type to prevent accidental firing.  
 
 
 
Figure 6.3.9.5.3 Guarded Firing Buttons 
6.3.9.5.4 
Weld timer electrical circuit shall include a ground continuity monitoring and 
residual current monitoring system for operator safety. 
6.3.9.5.5 
The need for local operator fume extraction will be considered by the Tier 1 
Integrator and Ford Facilities Engineering.  The need for fume extraction, in 
addition to the planned plant facility building ventilation, will be determined by the 
VOME Safety Engineer assigned to the program. 
6.3.9.6 
Other Manual Weld Gun Station Requirements 
6.3.9.6.1 
A Gun stand or rest point for each gun will be provided. 
6.3.9.6.2 
A stationary tip dresser, engineered for manual tip dressing, will be provided for 
use by the operator and will include tip sensing and a guarded foot pedal. 
6.3.9.6.3 
Gun guides will be provided to assist the operator in spot placement.  Gun guide 
designs will be agreed to by the BCE Joining engineer or the receiving plant 
welding engineer for every spot weld.  Weld gun arm insulating armor will be 
specified when needed to avoid the possibility of shunting to the tooling or sheet 
metal. 
6.3.10 
Weld Gun Equalization Requirements 
6.3.10.1 
Fixture weld guns shall have a single-attitude pneumatic equalizer. 
6.3.10.2 
Pedestal weld guns shall have a single-attitude pneumatic equalizer with robot-
initiated Measure 1 (tip-to-tip measurement) for tip wear compensation. 
6.3.10.3 
Robotic weld guns shall not have an equalizer and must adhere to the following: 
6.3.10.3.1 
Robot-initiated Measure 1 and Measure 2 (tip-to-calibration plate) for tip wear 
compensation. 
6.3.10.3.2 
Robot-initiated compensation for gun deflection 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 27 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.3.10.3.3 
Manually initiated EASYTOUCH (or equivalent) robot software tool for periodic 
programming adjustments. 
6.3.10.4 
Specific robot applications may require equalization based on application (e.g. 
end effector welding, where the part is held by an end effector instead of fixture), 
gun arm size, and weld class designation. These applications will be managed 
on an exception basis, subject to the review and advisement of the program 
Joining Engineer. 
6.3.11 
Weld Gun Servos and Pneumatic Cylinders 
Weld stroke shall provide a minimum of 30 mm of travel remaining at the weld 
caps to allow for tip wear, bending, adapter taper wear, and other factors.  
6.3.11.1 
Servo Weld Guns 
6.3.11.1.1 
Non-equalizing robot mounted weld guns must have a servo calibration block 
which shall be mounted to the tip dresser stand.  The block, mounting, and 
measurement location must be set up to meet the requirements of the robot 
supplier to ensure sufficient measurement accuracy.  
6.3.11.1.2 
NOTE: Self-equalizing guns do not require a calibration block. 
6.3.11.1.3 
Servo pedestal guns shall not use a calibration block.  Servo pedestal robots will 
be programmed to estimate 50% of total tip wear on each tip. Through launch 
acceleration, this estimate will be monitored and adjusted to best suit each 
application. The tip wear ratio is not to exceed a 40% / 60% wear ratio.   
6.3.11.1.4 
For carried servo guns only, total tip wear and individual tip wear (tip to 
calibration block) measurements shall be made after every tip dress. Pedestal 
mounted weld guns shall only perform a tip to tip measurement after every tip 
dress. 
6.3.11.2 
Pneumatic Weld Guns (Deviation required for all uses)   
6.3.11.2.1 
A Weld Complete Delay is required to prevent damage to pneumatic weld guns.  
This Weld Complete Delay, programmed in the weld controller, shall be a 
minimum of ten cycles. Alternative: position switches for the moving gun arm 
shall be used. 
6.3.11.2.2 
Pneumatic weld guns will use pneumatic equalizing. Weld guns having more 
than 100 degrees of orientation change during a tooling cycle shall utilize a multi-
pressure valve or programmable pressure valve that is controlled by the robot. 
6.3.11.2.3 
Flow controls or "low impact" valves are recommended for cylinders with total 
stroke over 75 mm. 
6.3.11.2.4 
Clean, dry air shall be supplied to pre-lube cylinders for maximum seal life. Air 
line lubrication may contaminate the permanent lubricants reducing cylinder life. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 28 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.3.11.2.5 
Weld gun suppliers are responsible for designing guns that produce the required 
force for a given application based on available plant air pressure.   
6.3.11.3 
Cylinder Stroke 
6.3.11.3.1 
Retract cylinders shall be used on robot and portable guns with a weld stroke 
greater than 75 mm. 
6.3.11.3.2 
Cylinders shall bottom out on the return (fully open) stroke. 
6.3.11.3.3 
Touch and lock, air-over-oil, or blocking type cylinders shall not be used on weld 
gun units. 
6.3.11.3.4 
All retract type welding cylinders shall have flow restrictors for retraction. Retract 
weld guns shall have proximity or other sensors to sense the gun arm in the open 
position. 
6.3.11.4 
Transformers 
6.3.11.4.1 
Weld gun transformer type and size are defined by Ford BCE and the approved 
Source Weld Gun Manufacturer for the program. Only one standard MFDC 
transformer shall be used for all weld gun applications. 
6.3.11.4.2 
Refer to Section 2 for tagging requirements. For Next-Gen controls tagging 
requirements see also Section 8. For GVOSS controls tagging requirements see 
also Section 14. 
6.3.11.4.3 
For integral weld guns, leaf (e.g. roll) shunts are preferred over air-cooled or 
water-cooled cables. 
6.3.12 
Automatic Tip Dressers 
6.3.12.1 
General Requirements   
6.3.12.1.1 
All new programs and kits where new robots and/or weld timers are being 
furnished shall have either robot-controlled servo-electric or electric automatic tip 
dressers.   Servo dressers are required for North America and preferred in all 
other regions. 
6.3.12.1.2 
Pneumatic dressers are allowed by deviation only after an Action Request Form 
is submitted and approved. 
6.3.12.1.3 
Servo motors shall be provided by the robot manufacturer, as part of the robot 
purchase, to the tip dresser manufacturer.  
6.3.12.1.4 
If electric dressers are selected, electric motors shall be specified according to 
regional electrical standards. Following are additional requirements for electric 
motor tip dressers:  
6.3.12.1.4.1 Motor enclosures and bearings shall have a minimum protection of IP 54. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 29 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.3.12.1.4.2 The power cable from the weld control to the tip dresser motor terminal box shall 
be a single continuous cable. 
6.3.12.1.4.3 Control of the tip dress motor shall be from a motor starter that is an integral part 
of the weld control. 
6.3.12.1.5 
After installation the direction of blade rotation shall be verified to match the 
arrow provided on the dresser. 
6.3.12.1.6 
Approved tip dresser and cutter manufacturers can be found on the preferred 
components list. The manufacturer and model numbers approved for use on a 
specific program and plant shall be specified by regional BCE Joining groups. 
6.3.12.2 
Tier 1 Integrator Responsibilities   
6.3.12.2.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible for programming a tip resistance check 
following tip dressing as a means of tip dress verification. 
6.3.12.2.2 
Removed 
6.3.12.2.3 
Regarding tip dress and change requirements: 
• First, it is assumed that weld tip changes can be scheduled to occur 
during breaks or off-shift times. 
• Second, if the weld tip changes must be accomplished during productive 
time, automatic changing equipment is strongly recommended. 
• Third, if automatic changing equipment is not purchased, then the 
changes will be included in the simulation on a case-by-case basis.   
 
Following are the assumptions for tip change frequencies: 
• 13mm Tips = 5000  
• 16mm Tips = 8000 
• 20mm Tips = 6000 
 
 
Note: The tip life of non-standard tips (mushrooms tips) and electrode 
holders/adapters >0° is lower and must be approved from Group staff 
responsible Joining Engineer. 
6.3.12.2.4 
Weld parameters are only to be adjusted to achieve required weld quality. To 
achieve cycle time, neither weld quality nor the weld process with zero or 
minimum weld spatter occurrence are to be compromised. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 30 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.3.12.2.5 
If included for electric tip dressers, the Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible for 
verifying the proper size and function of the tip dresser motor current sensor and 
thermal overload in the weld control per the weld control manufacturer's 
specifications. This will be verified prior to VTTO. 
6.3.12.2.6 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible for mechanical pivot units used in 
pedestal welding applications. Tier 1 Integrators shall use a common pivot unit 
and basic dump unit design, either as provided by the Tier 2 Equipment Supplier 
or selected from the FIDES library. 
6.3.12.2.7 
Prior to Vendor Tool Tryout (VTTO) Buy-Off, the Tier 1 Integrator shall 
demonstrate to the satisfaction of the Tier 2 Equipment Supplier that each tip 
dress system is set up correctly. The Tier 2 Equipment Supplier shall not sign off 
in the component sign-off book until this is completed.  
6.3.12.3 
Tip Dresser Manufacturer (Tier 2 Equipment Supplier) Responsibilities   
6.3.12.3.1 
The Tier 2 Equipment Supplier shall be responsible to provide the Tier 1 
Integrator with all information and support to properly select, install, and operate 
their equipment. 
6.3.12.3.2 
Upon Ford Motor Company or Tier 1 Integrator request, the Tier 2 Equipment 
Supplier shall provide on-site support for the tip dressers integration at the Ford 
Motor Company receiving plant.  
6.3.12.3.3 
The dresser shall have a compliance device that provides an axis of motion 
parallel to the axis of cutter rotation. 
6.3.12.3.4 
The tip dresser shall be labeled with an arrow for correct direction of rotation. 
6.3.12.3.5 
A matrix shall be developed and maintained showing robot number, dresser 
model, cutter, mounting, and options.  
6.3.12.4 
Tip Reference Plate 
The tip reference plate (fix device) shall be capable for 1,5kN force with a max. 
deflection of 0.1mm. The default mounting position is on the stand of the unit. A 
CAE calculation of the deflection in this position at 1,5 kN Force shall be 
provided. 
6.3.12.5 
Initial Tip Dresser Set-Up   
6.3.12.5.1 
Initial tip dress parameters for cut time and force shall be set according to the Tip 
Dresser Supplier's recommendations and concurred by BCE Welding 
Engineering.  
6.3.12.5.2 
Dress force for servo weld guns shall be set between 1.3 and 1.8 kN. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 31 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.3.12.5.3 
Dress frequencies and maximum number of dresses before tip change shall be 
provided by BCE Joining and programmed by the Tier 1 Integrator. See 
6.3.12.2.3 for initial assumptions for tip change frequencies defined by total weld 
count. 
6.3.12.6 
Tip Dresser Mounting   
6.3.12.6.1 
For applications where the weld gun is mounted to the robot, the preference for 
mounting system is indicated below. All designs are available in the FIDES 
library: 
• 1st preference is to mount the dresser on a stand attached to the floor. 
• 2nd preference is to mount the dresser to the robot base using the robot base 
mounting system. 
6.3.12.6.2 
Balcony robots shall have the dresser mounted in a location which is suitable for: 
• robot gun access 
• maintenance access 
6.3.12.6.3 
For applications where the weld gun is mounted on a pedestal, the tip dresser 
shall be mounted on a dump unit. All tip dresser dumps shall be in the horizontal 
position (preferred) or vertical position. Angular swing of a tip dresser is 
prohibited. 
6.3.12.6.4 
All tip dresser stands, when mounted to the floor, shall use four anchors as 
specified by the tip dresser manufacturer.  See Section 1.1.24 for Floor Anchor 
Requirements. 
6.3.12.6.5 
Once tip dresser stands are permanently placed in the plant and dressers are 
properly aligned for the weld gun, the dresser mounting plate shall be 
permanently fixed in position on the stand. 
6.3.12.6.6 
Where possible, the stand or post shall be welded to the supporting structure. 
6.3.12.6.7 
The dresser shall be mounted such that the blade is visible and accessible for 
replacement. The tip dresser shall not be mounted more than 1.4 m above the 
floor or balcony where it can be serviced. 
6.3.12.6.8 
The tip dresser shall be mounted such that weld gun equalization functions 
correctly when tip dressing. A weld gun with an equalizer that has been adjusted 
for welding in a particular attitude shall have a tip dresser mounted to permit 
dressing in that same attitude. 
6.3.12.6.9 
Tip dresser location on the mounting bracket and any associated slip planes shall 
be match marked prior to shipping as shown below in Figure 6.3.12.6.9. The 
purpose of this is to provide a visual indicator in the event the tip dresser has 
moved on the mounting bracket during shipping.

6.3.12.7.1 
A vacuum system for chip containment and collection is preferred for automatic 
tip dressing applications. The vacuum system shall be supplied by the tip dresser 
manufacturer.  See Figure 6.3.12.7.1.

6.3.12.7.2 
The chip container for the vacuum system shall be mounted to the tip dresser 
stand and shall be removable for periodic emptying of chips. 
6.3.12.8 
Chip Removal and Containment – Lubricated Air Blow-Off System 
(Deviation required for all uses)  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 33 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.3.12.8.1 
At the discretion of the Program Manufacturing Engineering and Joining 
Engineering personnel, blow off systems may be used in lieu of vacuum systems. 
For example, some specific applications, which may have excessive adhesive 
buildup may choose lubricated blow off system to keep tip dress cutter clean. 
The requirements that follow shall apply to the specific applications where a 
blow-off system will be used. 
6.3.12.8.2 
All applications with blow-off system in lieu of vacuum will have a standard debris 
container (aka Chip Tray) supplied by the tip dresser manufacturer. The tray shall 
be attached to the stand under the tip dresser but must be removable for periodic 
emptying of debris. The tray shall have the following dimensions: 350 mm 
diameter and 100 mm deep, as shown in Figure 6.3.12.8.2. Note that stand and 
tray colors are to be Ford Standard Brown. 
 
 
Figure 6.3.12.8.2 Chip Tray 
6.3.12.8.3 
The debris container shall be held such that it can be removed without the use of 
tools for replacement or disposal of debris. 
6.3.12.8.4 
At a minimum, the blow-off will be turned on as the cutter rotation is started and 
turned off after rotation has stopped.  It shall have the capability from the robot 
teach pendant to extend spinning and blow off time beyond end of tip dress 
without affecting the tip dress motor fault calculations and fault circuit. It shall be 
independent of servo gun measurement operation and shall be independent of 
robot motion so as not to add cycle time to tip dress operation. 
6.3.12.8.5 
Blow-off systems shall meet current noise abatement objectives and have a 
single point lubricator to apply oil or other approved lubricant/solvent through the 
blower orifice to promote removal of sealer/adhesive and extend blade life.

6.3.12.9 
Tip Dresser Cutter   
6.3.12.9.1 
Cutters shall be of the face cutting type and are required to last a minimum of 
10,000 dress cycles. 
6.3.12.9.2 
Other cutter features such as number of flutes on the cutter will be specified by 
BCE Joining on a program and plant basis.  
6.3.12.9.3 
The cutter shall not require external guides for weld gun cap adapters to dress 
effectively. 
6.3.12.9.4 
The tip dresser cutter shall be error-proofed for installation to assure proper 
cutter orientation when replaced. 
6.3.12.9.5 
The tip dresser cutter shall have an identification system that identifies each 
cutter type.

6.3.13 
Automatic Tip Changers 
6.3.13.1 
General Requirements   
6.3.13.1.1 
Automatic tip changer systems have to be selected from the Approved Source 
List / Statement of Work for a given program.  
6.3.13.1.2 
The automatic tip changer should be a modular system. 
6.3.13.2 
System Variants 
6.3.13.2.1 
Tip dresser and changer systems are available in following configurations: 
 
• Combined automatic dresser / changer (modular system) 
• Standalone automatic tip dresser  
• Standalone automatic tip changer

6.3.13.3.1 
The combined automatic tip changer system shall have the following components

6.3.13.4 
Tip Removal Unit 
6.3.13.4.1 
The cap shall be detached/removed from the holder by a linear movement (no 
turning of the cap) 
6.3.13.4.2 
The removal unit shall be placed close to the magazine to minimize robot 
movements.  
6.3.13.4.3 
The removal mechanism shall be capable of removing tips that were in operation 
with 6.5 kN weld force.  
6.3.13.4.4 
The removal mechanism shall be able to operate with following support surface 
dimensions and gaps to the electrode holder:

The tip magazine shall have capacity for min. 10 caps.  A poke yoke feature 
required to ensure correct tips in magazine.  
6.3.13.6 
Sensors 
The automatic tip changer shall have sensors for magazine identification and tip 
detection. The sensors shall be wire break proof. 
6.3.13.7 
Tip Hammer Plate 
6.3.13.7.1 
The function of the tip hammer plate is to press/set the tip on the cone before 
dressing. 
6.3.13.7.2 
A guiding geometry is required (e.g. cone) to allow a gun closing with maximum 
force and new, undressed tips.

6.3.14 
Adjacent Tooling Requirements 
For fixture welding applications, locators, rough locators, and pins shall be 
insulated as necessary to prevent grounding and shunting of secondary current 
during welding. Insulation is done as close as possible to the pin. The clamp 
finger is insulated to prevent current from traveling through the clamp cylinder. 
The locating block is not insulated. Such insulation is not required for robot or 
pedestal welding applications.

6.4 
FASTENER PROJECTION WELDING EQUIPMENT 
6.4.1 
General Requirements 
6.4.1.1 
Projection welding equipment shall meet the requirements described in Sections 
6.3.1, 6.3.4, and 6.3.14.  Exceptions are noted in this section. 
6.4.1.1.1 
Where the Tier 2 requirements are not aligned with the GVOSS and Ford Land 
Standards, the Tier 2 is responsible for conditioning the incoming air to meet their 
requirements. 
6.4.1.2 
Nominal design weld schedules for projection welding are typically provided by 
equipment manufacturer.  
6.4.1.3 
Certain fastener projection weld applications (i.e. boron steels) require “fast rise” 
of the weld current.  The minimum requirements are that the transformer and 
weld controller be capable of reaching targeted current in 15 ms or less with an 
additional external secondary load resistance of at least 150 micro-ohms.  BCE 
Joining Engineering shall determine applications that require fast-rise equipment. 
6.4.1.4 
The Tier 1 Integrator will provide controlled air to the Tier 2 Equipment Supplier’s 
projection welding electrode to meet the Supplier’s requirements for projection 
weld nut pin movement and projection weld bolt electrode blow off. 
6.4.1.5 
Robot end effector locating blocks, fingers, and clamps do not need to be 
insulated.  Adherence to 6.5.1.5 will ensure that the ground goes to the part 
rather than to the bolted connection to the end effector. 
6.4.2 
Product Design Requirements 
6.4.2.1 
The following requirements are to be reviewed by the Ford Body Construction 
Engineering and the Tier 1 Integrator. Non-conforming conditions are to be 
identified and resolved as soon as they are identified. 
6.4.2.2 
Projections shall be sized correctly for the gage and material being welded. Refer 
to the weld fastener engineering drawing or the program-applicable Engineering 
Specification for recommended projection sizes. 
6.4.2.3 
Non-piloted, unthreaded weld fasteners with three round projections are 
preferred. 
6.4.2.4 
Current stepping and tip dressing are not to be used for projection welding 
applications. 
6.4.2.5 
Projection welds shall meet the Destructive Test Requirements of the program-
applicable Engineering Specification unless expressly designated otherwise by 
product engineering and concurred by Ford Body Construction Engineering. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 40 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.4.3 
Error-Proofing of Projection Fasteners 
6.4.3.1 
All projection welded fasteners require error-proofing integral to the tool which 
applies the fastener.  
6.4.3.2 
This error-proofing shall be capable of determining correct fastener presence, 
location and orientation. Fastener type and size shall be error-proofed by the 
given feed mechanism(s). 
6.4.3.3 
This error-proofing shall include the use of one or multiple electrodes per gun, of 
such design that they do not pass weld current when (a) the weld nut is missing; 
and/or (b) the weld nut is off location; and/or (c) the weld nut is upside down, 
supplied by a manufacturer approved by BCE Joining. 
6.4.3.4 
If such error-proofing for a specific nut configuration has not been previously 
deployed, the Tier 1 Integrator is authorized to use standard electrodes as an 
interim measure while jointly working with the electrode supplier to ensure 
delivery of a tool meeting the above requirements. 
6.4.3.5 
Closed out fasteners or those which cannot be easily repaired shall be error-
proofed at the point of load (where the part with the fastener gets loaded) or prior 
to closing out. This requirement may be waived with written direction from the 
Body Construction Engineering manufacturing engineer assigned to the given 
subsystem on the given program at the given plant specifically identifying the 
affected fasteners. Fasteners installed by a supplier (PIA) shall be error-proofed 
by the given supplier not at the receiving plants. If the supplier fails to 
demonstrate the capability of delivering the given part with the fasteners attached 
properly then they shall pay the given plant to implement whatever error-proofing 
technique that the given plant suggests will work and repair or pay the given 
plant to repair any non-conforming parts that may be detected at the given plant. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 41 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.5 
DRAWN ARC WELDING EQUIPMENT 
6.5.1 
General Requirements 
6.5.1.1 
The Tier 2 Equipment Supplier will provide all air requirements, including, but not 
limited to: Air quality, hose sizes, feed tube routing/lengths, and pressure 
settings, etc. 
6.5.1.1.1 
Where the Tier 2 requirements are not aligned with the GVOSS and ford land 
standards, the Tier 2 shall be responsible to condition the incoming air to meet 
their requirements. 
6.5.1.2 
Drawn arc welding applications (stud, nut, or bracket) shall be reviewed by Ford 
Body Construction Engineering (BCE) and the Tier 1 Integrator for conformance 
to the program-applicable Engineering Specification and the product weld 
fastener engineering drawing to ensure suitability of the fastener to the intended 
application. Drawn arc welds shall meet the destructive test requirements of the 
engineering specification. 
6.5.1.3 
Secondary (standby) drawn arc welding equipment requirements, needed to 
minimize production losses in the event of equipment failures, are defined in GVOSS 
Section 6.12.4.2. 
6.5.1.4 
Robotic drawn arc stud welding applications shall have fixed pedestal-mounted weld 
guns.  Robot-carried applications have lower equipment availability and require a 
signed tooling deviation. 
6.5.1.5 
Part fixtures and end effectors shall provide sufficient support to the sheet metal 
such that there will be no part deflection during the drawn arc welding process. 
6.5.1.6 
Grounding methods shall ensure constant positive contact to the part and be 
independent for each weld power supply. The grounding connection detail must be 
insulated at the mounting hardware to ensure the weld ground goes predominantly 
to the part rather than to the bolted connection to the end effector.  The Tier 1 
Integrator shall consult with the Tier 2 Equipment Supplier during tool design to 
ensure satisfactory grounding is achieved. The grounding circuit must be reviewed 
by the Tier 2 Equipment Supplier and must conform to that Supplier’s standards. 
6.5.1.7 
 The Tier 2 Equipment Supplier shall specify weld polarity for each application and 
deliver the system to the Tier 1 Integrator configured to the correct polarity.  To 
facilitate a polarity, change in the field should it be determined that a change is 
required, jumpers or other components necessary for conversion shall be provided 
with each system. 
6.5.1.8 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall develop and utilize programming aids to ensure stud 
location accuracy.  Examples include templates, bulls-eye panels, and part 
overlays.  The specific type can be determined by the Integrator based on what is 
best-suited to the application.  This requirement applies whether programming is 
done at-Integrator or in-plant (e.g. kit job). 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 42 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.5.1.9 
Drawn arc welding applications shall provide means to prevent direct viewing of the 
arc by anyone outside of the stations. Welding curtains shall be provided and 
installed by the Tier 1 Integrator around the drawn arc welding stations. 
6.5.1.10 
Consumable collets shall be supplied by the Tier 1 Integrator through the TT Build 
Phase unless other arrangements are made with the receiving plant(s). 
6.5.1.11 
Drawn Arc Bracket Welding 
6.5.1.11.1 
Any new drawn arc bracket welding applications shall be approved by Ford BCE 
Joining Engineering.  
6.5.1.11.2 
Drawn arc bracket welding stations shall incorporate a test coupon stand and gas 
nozzle reamer. Argon gas bottles shall be supplied by the Tier 1 Integrator 
through the TT Build Phase unless other arrangements are made with the 
receiving plant(s).  
6.5.1.11.3 
Drawn arc bracket welding processes shall have manual inspection and repair 
stations planned and equipped for the operation.  
6.5.1.11.4 
Hand-apply templates for manually locating the drawn arc brackets and gas metal 
arc welding equipment shall be provided for manual repair of the brackets. 
6.5.1.12 
The Tier 2 Equipment Supplier shall be responsible for reviewing and approving 
the drawn arc welding application, tooling design, and integration of their 
equipment at the tool shop and final installation at the receiving plant. Sign-off 
approval shall be presented in writing to the Tier 1 Integrator for inclusion in 
Buyoff Books. 
6.5.2 
Error-Proofing of Drawn Arc Fasteners 
6.5.2.1 
Drawn arc welded fasteners require error-proofing integral to the welding system 
and shall not depend on post-process part detection. Fastener type and size 
shall be error-proofed by the given feed mechanisms. 
6.5.2.2 
Drawn arc process parameter windows shall be programmed to the equipment 
manufacturer’s recommendations and set to stop the operation at the point of 
application for any process parameter faults that indicate a suspect weld. 
Parameter monitoring must be ON at all times from tool buy off through MP1 +90 
days. Examples include:  
• No stud on work piece. 
• Low/no current or voltage or weld time detected. 
• No arc achieved.  
6.5.2.3 
When the weld controller creates a fault requiring manual intervention, the part 
shall be sent to the take-out station for stud integrity verification whenever:  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 43 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• The “Reject Part” or the “Reject No PG Recall” buttons on the robot teach 
pendant is pushed.  
• The weld complete is manually set.  
6.5.2.4 
An inspection take-out station shall be provided at the end of the sub-assembly 
line for manual inspection and repair or swap-out, including the capability for 
reintroduction. 
6.5.3 
Post Process Error Proofing of Drawn Arc Fasteners 
6.5.3.1 
Post-process error proofing is not necessary unless it is specifically identified as 
required for an application in the Statement of Work. Error-proofing at the point of 
welding or load (where the part with the fastener gets loaded) or prior to closing 
out is preferred. 
6.5.3.2 
A discrepancy found at the stud/nut/bracket error-proofing location will be routed 
to the take-out station. 
6.5.4 
Purchased-in-Assembly Parts 
Fasteners installed by a supplier (PIA) shall be error-proofed by the given 
supplier and not at the receiving plants. GVOSS Section 2.7.4.10 prohibits the 
inspection of incoming parts for completeness, including presence of fasteners, 
whether the part is PIA or manufactured elsewhere in the plant on a 
subassembly tool.

6.6 
GAS METAL ARC BRAZING AND WELDING EQUIPMENT 
6.6.1 
General Requirements 
This section is intended to provide minimum content for equipment specification 
and processing of gas metal arc brazing and welding operations. Robotic 
applications are assumed, but this standard is also to be used for manual 
applications where applicable. Detailed requirements for each program regarding 
gas metal arc applications are to be jointly developed by the Tier 2 Equipment 
Supplier, Tier 1 Integrator, and Ford Body Construction Engineering. For process 
monitoring 
requirements, 
refer 
to 
the 
program-applicable 
Engineering 
Specification. 
6.6.1.1 
A crash box or clutch must be installed between the welding torch and the robot.  
A fault must be generated if a collision is detected. 
6.6.1.2 
A pusher is required if there is any possibility of sheet metal gaps existing at the 
locations where brazes or welds will be made. 
6.6.1.3 
Pushers, locators, clamps, etc. near the welding area must be protected from 
spatter and must be made of bronze or non-conducting material to reduce spatter 
build-up. 
6.6.1.4 
The base sheet metal being welded may require counter support to prevent 
deflection from the pusher if it has low stiffness. Protect for adding this support if 
it is found to be required during Production Validation. 
6.6.1.5 
A touch-sensing or vision system may be required if there are slots that require 
100% fill. 
6.6.1.6 
An automatic torch-cleaning process must be installed. Frequency of cleaning is 
to be set at every 10 jobs initially but may be changed as required to keep the 
torch clean. 
6.6.1.7 
Removed 
6.6.1.8 
Engineering-released wire for welding equipment will be the responsibility of the 
Tier 1 Integrator to procure and will be included in Tier 1 Integrator’s quote.  Wire 
will be supplied by the Tier 1 Integrator through the TTO Build Phase, unless 
other arrangements are made with the receiving plant(s). Wire must be in barrels.  
6.6.1.9 
For gas metal arc brazing applications, the silicon bronze wire diameter used 
must be a minimum of 1.0 mm to avoid kinks and tangles. 
6.6.1.10 
Gas bottles will be supplied by the Tier 1 Integrator through the TTO Build Phase 
unless other arrangements are made with the receiving plant(s). A method to 
safely secure the compressed gas bottle at the station will be provided. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 45 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.6.1.11 
Two means of ensuring gas flow must be installed. One is a pressure switch prior 
to the gas regulator outside the cell, to ensure adequate gas is being supplied 
through the bulk system. The other is at the feeder, to detect a low gas flow 
condition between the regulator and the feeder. A gas regulator and flow meter 
will be provided at each application. 
6.6.1.12 
The grounding method must ensure constant, positive contact to the body and 
must be independent for each weld power supply (i.e. no looping RH ground 
wiring with LH ground wiring). Resistance must be kept as low as possible. Tier 1 
Integrator must consult with the equipment manufacturer during design to ensure 
satisfactory grounding is achieved. Grounding clamps must be designed and 
implemented with as minimal of an impact force as possible in order to ensure 
the prolonged life of the clamping components. 
6.6.1.13 
The Tool Center Point (TCP) verification fixture/tool location must be in a safe 
area with rigid mounting brackets/stands. If necessary, additional protection must 
be put in place to avoid potential for collisions. 
6.6.1.14 
The wire conduit that runs from the wire barrel outside the cell to the wire feeder 
on the robot arm must have as few bends as possible. The wire conduit must be 
of an ultra-low friction push-type, such as a Rolliner NG, MasterLiner, or 
equivalent.  The wire conduit shall not be tied directly to the rest of the robot 
dressing, to allow for more freedom to minimize the number of bends. The wire 
conduit shall be of a one-piece design, i.e. no splices or junctions allowed. Wire 
conduit routing must be reviewed and approved by all relevant Tier 2 Equipment 
Suppliers. 
6.6.1.15 
For drawn arc and gas metal arc welding applications only, weld curtains (Light 
amber color PVC curtains, 1.0 mm (0.040") thick, compliant to NFPA 701), shall 
be procured and installed by the tooling OEM to prevent direct viewing.  
6.6.1.16 
Manual applications shall have a fume extraction system to prevent any harm to 
the operator from the welding fumes. 
6.6.1.17 
An inspection station shall be provided as close as possible to the gas metal arc 
brazing or welding station exit, to allow for immediate inspection, performance 
feedback, and repair if necessary. 
6.6.2 
Tier 2 Equipment Supplier Responsibilities 
6.6.2.1 
Equipment shall be capable of welding in either constant voltage or pulsed arc 
modes. 
6.6.2.2 
The Tier 2 Equipment Supplier shall be responsible to provide the Tier 1 
Integrator with all information and support to properly select, install, and operate 
their equipment. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 46 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.6.2.3 
The Tier 2 Equipment Supplier shall be responsible for reviewing and approving 
the application and installation of their equipment.  Approval must be presented 
in writing to the Tier 1 Integrator for inclusion in Buyoff Books.  
6.6.2.4 
Upon Ford Motor Company or Tier 1 Integrator request, the Tier 2 Equipment 
Supplier shall provide support for the equipment integration at the Ford Motor 
Company receiving plant.  
6.7 
SELF-PIERCE RIVETING EQUIPMENT 
6.7.1 
General Requirements 
This section pertains to self-pierce rivet equipment. The requirements for each 
program regarding self-joining applications are to be jointly developed by the 
technology supplier and the Ford BCE Joining team. 
The following requirements extend to joints that are produced using manual, 
automatic 
and 
robotic 
equipment, 
and 
contain 
pneumatic, 
hydraulic, 
Servo/electric and hybrid drivers. The aforementioned equipment and drivers 
must be approved for use by Ford BCE Joining for any given application. 
Additionally, these requirements are to be reviewed by the Ford BCE team and 
the Tier 1 Integrator. Non-conforming conditions are to be identified and resolved 
as soon as they are identified. 
6.7.2 
Product Design Requirements 
6.7.2.1 
The self-pierce rivet technology manufacturer shall be responsible for 
acceptance testing and rivet schedule development according to FLTM BA 157-
01 (Consult Ford BCE Joining to determine if acceptance testing has already 
been completed for the released material combinations). This shall be completed 
prior to the shipment of the equipment to the Tier 1 Integrator.  The tooling is to 
be shipped with the combinations released by Ford Product Development and 
agreed upon by Ford BCE Joining, as designated by the applicable vehicle 
WIMS.  
6.7.2.2 
The self- pierce rivet technology manufacturer and Tier 1 Integrator shall jointly 
be responsible for adapting the rivet schedules to the production tooling and 
Vendor Site Tool Certification. 
6.7.2.3 
After equipment installation at the designated Ford Motor Company location, the 
self-pierce rivet technology manufacturer and Tier 1 Integrator shall jointly be 
responsible for adjustment and recertification of the rivet equipment. 
6.7.2.4 
SPR equipment shall be equipped with a built-in parameter control and 
monitoring unit. The below items shall be monitored by the system. 
• Pre-Clamp Force or Pressure 
• Rivet Setting Parameter (Displacement, Force, Velocity)
