• Punch Travel 
6.7.2.5 
Tool changers should be avoided as part of the normal process. If tool changers 
are required, approval from BCE Joining and the equipment manufacture is 
required. 
6.7.3 
SPR Robot Commonization 
The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible for working with the BCE Joining team to 
commonize rivet and die combos to eliminate unnecessary equipment. See the 
attached SPL below for the process flow. 
Commonization Request Process Flow Chart Rev2 
6.7.4 
Fault Recovery  
6.7.4.1 
All SPR processes shall stop at the point of application for various process       
parameter faults that may indicate a suspect joint. 
6.7.4.2 
When an SPR fault is identified by the SPR controller the part will be sent to the 
take-out station for SPR integrity verification whenever: 
• The “skip once” button is pushed 
• The “joint complete” is manually set. 
6.7.4.3 
See also Recovery Mode SPL for Rivet, Clinch and Flow-screw Applications. 
6.7.4.4 
Take-out stations will be provided at the end of the sub-assembly line for manual 
inspection, repair or swap out. 
6.7.5 
SPR Air Requirements 
6.7.5.1 
Separate drops from the system header are to be provided for the cubes/hoppers 
and the tools, i.e. one drop for each individual feed cube and one drop for the 
gun. An air drop with a quick disconnect is required at the locations where the 
SPR spools will be changed.  
6.7.5.1.1 
Where the Tier 2 requirements are not aligned with the GVOSS the Tier 2 is 
responsible for conditioning the incoming air to meet their requirements. 
6.7.5.2 
The Tier 2 Equipment Supplier will provide all necessary air requirements, 
including, but not limited to: Air quality, hose sizes, feed tube routing/lengths, and 
pressure settings, etc. 
6.7.6 
SPR Error Proofing 
6.7.6.1 
The Self Pierce Riveting equipment supplier must provide a method for error 
proofing the rivet and die to prevent the installation of the incorrect components.

6.7.6.2 
The Self Pierce Riveting equipment supplier must also provide an optional 
feature that is able to detect broken dies. 
6.7.6.3 
SPR operations that use multiple feed tubes and feeders. The equipment 
supplier shall be responsible to provide labels for the feed tube and the feeder 
associated with each feed tube. I.e. The feed tube would be labeled “Feed tube 
A” the feeder would be labeled “Feeder A “. The Feed tube label shall be placed

feeder lid.

6.8 
CLINCHING EQUIPMENT 
6.8.1 
General Requirements 
6.8.1.1 
This section pertains to clinching equipment. The requirements for each program 
regarding clinch applications are to be jointly developed by the technology 
supplier and the Ford BCE Joining team. 
6.8.1.2 
The following requirements are to be reviewed by the Ford Body Construction 
Engineering and the Tier 1 Integrator. Non-conforming conditions are to be 
identified and resolved as soon as they are identified. 
6.8.2 
Product Design Requirements 
6.8.2.1 
The clinching equipment manufacture shall be responsible for acceptance testing 
and clinch schedule development according to FLTM BA 114-01 (Consult Ford 
BCE Joining to determine if acceptance testing has already been completed for 
the released material combinations). This shall be completed prior to shipment of 
the equipment to the Tier 1 Integrator. Tooling to be delivered with the 
combinations that have been released by Ford Product Development and agreed 
upon by Ford BCE Joining, as designated by the applicable vehicle WIMS. 
6.8.2.2 
The clinching equipment manufacturer and Tier 1 Integrator shall jointly be 
responsible for adapting the clinch schedules to the production tooling and 
Vendor Site Tool Certification. 
6.8.2.3 
After equipment installation at the designated Ford Motor Company location the 
clinching equipment manufacturer and Tier 1 Integrator shall jointly be 
responsible for adjustment and recertification of the clinch equipment. 
6.8.3 
Clinch Gun Requirements 
6.8.3.1 
This section pertains to clinching equipment. The requirements for each program 
regarding clinching applications are to be jointly developed by the technology 
supplier and the Ford BCE Joining Section. 
6.8.3.2 
Clinching equipment shall be equipped with a built-in parameter control and 
monitoring unit. Clinching equipment must be able to monitor and provide punch 
travel feedback. Some examples of parameters that could be monitored by servo 
equipment are shown below: 
• Pre-clamp force or pressure 
• Set force or pressure 
• Velocity 
• Punch travel 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 53 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.8.3.3 
Clinch Joints may require lubrication to the punch and die side of the joint. The 
requirement of lubrication will be identified through the FLTM acceptance testing. 
All clinch tooling must be provided with the capability to apply lube. Contact the 
BCE Joining engineer to identify the locations that require lubrication. For the 
tools that utilize lubrication a drip tray shall be provided. 
6.8.3.4 
Strippers are to be provided with each clinch gun to prevent from deformation to 
the local material and damage to the clinch joint when the punch and die are 
withdrawn from the material.  
6.8.3.5 
Multiple point clinch tooling must be capable to allow adjusted for each point 
without impacting the performance of the other points. It is recommended to have 
a maximum of two points for multiple point tooling. Multiple point clinch tooling 
design must be signed off by BCE Joining Engineering.   
6.8.3.6 
Clinching equipment supplier must provide a method of error proofing the punch 
and die to prevent the installation of the incorrect components.  
6.8.3.7 
Clinching equipment supplier must provide the option to detect broken dies.  
6.8.3.8 
It is the Clinch equipment supplier and Tier 1 Integrator to provide the pneumatic 
requirements.  
6.8.3.9 
The clinch equipment supplier shall provide a Joint Process tag with each 
system. An example of a tag is shown below. Tag content will be agreed to 
between the equipment manufacturer and BCE Joining Engineering.

6.9 
FLOW DRILL FASTENING 
6.9.1 
General Requirements 
NOTE: This section shall apply until equipment availability has been established. 
6.9.2 
Main Line Stations vs. Off-line Tooling Flow Drill Screw (FDS) Systems: 
6.9.2.1 
Main Lines Stations – Each flow drill gun requires a secondary gun stationed 
inside the station. 
6.9.2.2 
Off-line Tooling: 
6.9.2.2.1 
Each flow drill gun requires a secondary gun stationed inside the cell (hosed and 
wired). The changeover process shall be automatic in the station. "Quick-
change" plates, slides and/or connections shall be used for easy removal and 
maintenance/repair. 
6.9.2.2.2 
If an existing robot, within the same system, can be programmed to back-up 
another robot, without negative impact to the dress package, a secondary gun is 
not required. 
6.9.3 
Fixed Gun PED (Pedestal) vs. Robot-Mounted FDS 
6.9.3.1 
Fixed Gun PED (Pedestal) FDS System: 
6.9.3.1.1 
Pedestals shall be in the tooling cell. 
6.9.3.1.2 
Controllers, feeders, and guns shall be equipped with quick change connections.  
6.9.3.1.3 
Each gun shall have its own pedestal. 
6.9.3.1.4 
Each pedestal shall have its own dress package. 
6.9.3.1.5 
Backups for Pedestal FDS systems are not required. 
6.9.3.2 
Robot Mounted FDS: 
6.9.3.2.1 
The change-out process shall use an automatic tool changer.  
6.9.3.2.2 
Controllers shall be equipped with quick change connections.  
6.9.3.2.3 
Feeders shall be equipped with quick change connections.  
6.9.3.2.4 
Robot dress packages shall be equipped with an automatic tool changer on the 
robot face with quick change connections at the controller and feeder ends.  
6.9.3.2.5 
Dress packages shall contain (2) individual blow feed system tubes for use with 
Gun A or Gun B. Each shall be dressed to the robot side of the tool changer.   
Hoses shall be labeled “A” and “B”. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 56 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
6.9.3.2.6 
Dress packages shall contain (1) electrical and controls common cable set for 
use with both Gun A & B. Each shall be dressed to a common connection on the 
tool changer.  
6.9.3.2.7 
Guns (Gun A & Gun B) shall be on automatic tool changers.  
6.9.3.2.8 
The gun side of the A & B tool changers shall use common electrical and controls 
connections.  
6.9.3.2.9 
The gun side of the A & B tool changer shall use only the blow feed tube 
assigned to it (A or B). 
6.9.3.2.10 
Guns and tool changers shall be labeled Gun A & Gun B. 
6.9.4 
Product Design Requirements 
6.9.4.1 
Tooling combinations shall be determined through FLTM BA 115-01: Flow Drill 
Screw Schedule Development Acceptance Test for Aluminum, Low Carbon Steel 
Sheet and Extrusions. Consult Ford BCE Joining Engineering to determine if 
acceptance testing has been completed for the released material combinations.  
6.9.4.2 
Tooling will be delivered with the combinations that have been released by 
Product Development Engineering and agreed to by BCE Joining Engineering. 
The released combinations can be found in the Weld Information Masters 
Sheets. 
6.9.4.3 
The screw equipment manufacturer shall be responsible for performing the FLTM 
BA 115-01 acceptance testing and the FDS schedule development. This shall be 
completed prior to shipment of the equipment to the Tier 1 Integrator.  
6.9.4.4 
The FDS equipment manufacturer and Tier 1 Integrator are jointly responsible for 
adapting the FDS schedules to the production tooling and Vendor Site Tool 
Certification. 
6.9.4.5 
After equipment installation at the Ford Motor Company receiving plant is 
complete, the screw equipment manufacturer and Tier 1 Integrator shall jointly be 
responsible for adjustment and recertification of the FDS equipment. 
6.9.5 
Fault Recovery 
6.9.5.1 
All flow drill processes shall stop at the point of application when a process 
parameter fault occurs that may indicate a suspect joint. If the fault occurs during 
the screw process, then the process will be allowed to complete before the 
system is stopped. 
6.9.5.2 
When a flow drill fault is identified by the flow drill controller the part will be sent 
to the take-out station for flow drill screw integrity verification whenever: 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 57 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• The “skip once” button is pushed.  
• The “joint complete” is manually set. 
See also Recovery Mode SPL for Rivet Clinch and Flow-screw Applications. 
6.9.5.3 
Take-out stations will be provided at the end of the sub-assembly line for manual 
inspection, repair or swap out. If the flow drill screw is closed out before the end 
of the line, an inspection point needs to be provided before the joint is closed out. 
6.9.6 
Flow Drill Screw Air Requirements 
6.9.6.1 
The Tier 2 Equipment Supplier will provide all necessary air requirements, 
including, but not limited to: Air quality, hose sizes, feed tube routing/lengths, and 
pressure settings, etc. 
6.9.6.2 
Where the Tier 2 requirements are not aligned with the GVOSS and Ford Land 
Standards, the Tier 2 is responsible for conditioning the incoming air to meet their 
requirements. 
6.9.6.3 
Separate drops from the system header are to be provided for the cubes/hoppers 
and the tools, i.e. one drop for each individual feed cube and one drop for the 
gun.  
6.9.6.4 
For additional specific requirements— hose sizes, feed tube routing/lengths, and 
pressure settings—refer to the applicable Tier 2 Equipment Supplier’s drawings 
and specifications. The supplier will provide the required air specifications to 
ensure alignment to what the plant can supply.

6.11 
LASER WELDING, BRAZING, AND CUTTING PROCESSES EQUIPMENT 
6.11.1 
General Requirements 
6.11.1.1 
This section is intended to provide minimum content for equipment specification 
and processing of laser welding, brazing, and cutting operations. Detailed 
requirements for each program regarding laser joining applications are to be 
jointly developed by the technology supplier and Ford Body Construction 
Engineering. 
6.11.1.2 
Tier 1 Integrator shall comply with the safety requirements noted in the latest 
edition of Ford Motor Company's FAS08-211 "Laser Safety Standard". See 
Section 1.2.9.10 for additional requirements. 
6.11.1.3 
Tier 1 Integrator shall have an established laser safety program at their facility 
where the laser tooling and equipment are integrated and validated. As part of 
this program, the Tier 1 Integrator shall have a designated Laser Safety Officer 
(LSO) on staff with the responsibility of monitoring, validating, and enforcing 
compliance with the Standards noted in this section, as well as other pertinent 
safety regulations. 
6.11.2 
Laser Booth Requirements 
6.11.2.1 
Product Entrance and Exit Doors 
6.11.2.1.1 
The laser booth shall have automatic product doors (centered) on each booth 
end with a sufficiently wide opening for the product to enter and exit. 
6.11.2.1.2 
The automatic doors shall be electric motor controlled. 
6.11.2.1.3 
Motor control system shall be defined for the program as part of Rev Level of 
controls design for the specific program. 
6.11.2.1.4 
The motor controlled auto-doors shall open and close within 6 seconds. 
6.11.2.1.5 
Automatic entry and exit doors shall be of light-tight construction. 
6.11.2.1.6 
Each door shall have an individual proximity switch indicating door open. 
6.11.2.1.7 
Each door shall have two safety-rated switches for indicating door closed wired 
to safe inputs, as defined by the Ford Standard Electrical Component list for the 
specific program. 
6.11.2.2 
Operator Doors 
6.11.2.2.1 
Standard manual, steel or aluminum and light-tight doors with steel or aluminum 
frames shall be installed for booth egress. 
6.11.2.2.2 
The door shall swing on the outside of the booth. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 62 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.11.2.2.3 
One door shall be required for each side of the line and each level of booth. 
6.11.2.2.4 
A light stack indicating laser status shall be installed outside the booth, next to, or 
over each entrance operator door to the laser enclosure. At a minimum, a stack 
shall include a "LASER BOOTH IN CYCLE" and "LASER BOOTH NOT IN 
CYCLE" status lights, i.e. red and green lights respectively. 
6.11.2.2.5 
Each door shall also include a safety-rated gate switch as defined by the Ford 
Standard Electrical Component list for the specific program.  
6.11.2.3 
Lighting and Receptacles 
6.11.2.3.1 
L.E.D. light fixtures are required.  There shall be a minimum of four L.E.D. light 
fixtures in each laser booth. The specific requirements for minimum lumens can 
be found in GVOSS Section 2. 
6.11.2.3.2 
GFCI power receptacles are required in each laser booth.  The minimum number 
of GFCI receptacles is one receptacle per wall.    The GFCI receptacles shall be 
110 VAC 15 Amp / 220 VAC 7.5 Amp. GFCI receptacles shall be installed per 
level of laser cell. (i.e. for balcony-mounted systems double the amount of lights 
and receptacles). Follow approved regional requirements. See the Ford Standard 
Electrical Component list for the specific program. 
6.11.2.3.3 
Lighting and GFCI receptacles shall be controlled via an approved lighting power 
distribution system as specified in Ford Standard Electrical Component list for the 
specific program. 
6.11.2.4 
No Step Signs 
Tier 1 Integrator shall provide "NO STEP" signs posted around outside of laser 
booth ceiling. 
6.11.2.5 
Booth Wall Construction 
6.11.2.5.1 
General construction of laser booth shall provide for light-tight installation. 
6.11.2.5.2 
Construction of walls of laser booth shall consist of modular panels of polycore 
substrate, with either steel or aluminum interior and exterior. All panels shall be 
removable for maintenance.  Any time a panel is removed for maintenance, laser 
enclosure shall be re-certified to meet light-tight requirement by certified Laser 
Safety Officer. 
6.11.2.6 
Fire Protection 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall coordinate with the Ford VO Plant Facilities Contractor 
the design and installation of fire sprinkling systems required for the specific 
booth design requirements.

6.11.2.7 
Laser Booth Ventilation 
6.11.2.7.1 
Tier 1 Integrator shall coordinate with the VO Plant Facilities Contractor design 
and installation of ventilation equipment and ductwork routing for the specific 
booth ventilation design requirements. The design shall include but is not limited 
to: 
• Air Change Rate (ACR)  
• Sufficient make-up air to properly balance the system. 
• Prevent negative pressure in the booth. 
• Make-up air inlets shall be placed to properly vent the booth. 
6.11.2.7.2 
Ventilation equipment shall be provided by Ford VO Plant Facilities Installation 
contractor. 
6.11.2.7.3 
Tier 1 Integrator shall provide and install 3-Phase AC components to control the 
ventilation system. Follow approved regional requirements. 
6.11.2.7.4 
Ventilation system shall run continuously, with a shutdown timer set for 10 
minutes. 
6.11.2.7.5 
If there is no cycle start after ten minutes the ventilation system shall shutdown. 
6.11.2.7.6 
After shutdown by the timer, the next cycle start shall reset the timing circuit and 
restart the ventilation system. 
6.11.3 
Laser Chillers 
6.11.3.1 
A self-contained, closed loop chiller and heat exchanger system shall be 
provided.  
6.11.3.2 
All fittings and valves installed in the optical deionized (DI) water loop must be 
constructed of stainless steel. Galvanized steel, “black pipe”, or brass type 
components are not allowed in the DI water loop. 
6.11.3.3 
Operating temperature and pressure ranges for both the “tap” and “DI” water 
loops shall be marked in green on the corresponding external gage indicators. 
6.11.3.4 
Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible for providing pipe insulation for chiller piping 
to minimize condensation dripping on floor or balcony surfaces.  
6.11.3.5 
Access for placement of DI cooling water containers   
6.11.3.6 
A clear path for Fork Truck access to the Laser Chiller shall be made available 
for the placement of DI cooling water.

6.11.4 
Closed Circuit Laser Booth Monitoring Video 
6.11.4.1 
Closed circuit monitoring video network cameras must be installed inside the 
laser booth with the real-time image displayed for viewing on a monitor outside 
the cell. These cameras are to be installed regardless of whether a viewing 
window exists and are required to enable remote viewing of the laser process. 
The number of cameras required is to be determined based on field of view 
coverage of the entire laser processing area. Each camera interface will include 
means of manipulating zoom viewing angles and switching between camera 
views. Camera resolution must be sufficient to enable detailed inspection of the 
laser process at the given lighting conditions inside the booth. Minimum 
specifications for these fixed dome type cameras are 1.3 Megapixel resolution, 
0.5 LUX illumination, and 29X optical and 12X digital zoom with autofocus 
capabilities.  
6.11.4.2 
The video system shall have recording and storing capabilities. Closed circuit 
camera system and monitor shall be specified on Standard Components list for a 
given program. 
6.11.5 
Laser Power Meter 
6.11.5.1 
A water-cooled laser power monitor shall be installed inside the laser booth and 
within the reach of each of the laser processing robots. Such a device may be 
mounted onto a stand-alone fixture, or existing stands such as coupon fixture or 
wire trimmer stand. Laser power shall be sampled and verified at a power level 
representative of production level.  Initial assumption for cycle time planning is 
once per 20 cycles. This can be modified as required during launch depending 
on the needs of a specific application. 
6.11.5.2 
The ten most recent laser power readings shall be displayed along with the 
corresponding date and time stamps on the laser booth HMI. A laser power 
output that falls below 3% of preset programmed power value shall be displayed 
as a warning on the HMI screen. A laser power output that falls below 5% of 
preset programmed power value shall generate a system fault. 
6.11.6 
Coupon Stand 
6.11.6.1 
A weld coupon stand must be installed inside each laser booth. The stand will be 
used to run verification welds on coupons to ensure system capability. It is 
expected that a stand will be located within the reach of each processing robot in 
the laser booth. The stand is to be designed to hold the coupons in the same 
orientations as joints being welded or brazed on the body. 
6.11.6.2 
The geometry of the test coupons shall be representative of the joint geometry 
welded in production. Material grade and coatings must be identical to the 
production intent materials. Any deviations must be communicated in writing to 
the BCE Joining Engineer. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 67 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.11.7 
Filler Wire Trim Station 
6.11.7.1 
A filler wire trimming station shall be installed in each laser station where the 
process requires a filler wire. A wire trimmer must be within the reach of each 
welding or brazing robot in the booth. Each trim station shall have means of 
validating wire stick-out after trimming action is completed.  
6.11.7.2 
All wire trimming stations shall be equipped with a debris container attached to 
the stand below the trimmer and must be removable for periodic emptying of 
debris. The debris container shall be held such that it can be removed without the 
use of tools for replacement or disposal of debris. 
6.11.8 
Shield Gas 
6.11.8.1 
When either shield or assist gas is required for a laser process, two means of 
ensuring gas flow must be installed. One is a pressure switch prior to the gas 
regulator outside the booth, to ensure adequate gas is being supplied through 
the bulk system. The other is at the feeder, to detect a low gas flow condition 
between the regulator and the feeder. 
6.11.9 
Filler Wire Pre-Heat Equipment 
6.11.9.1 
For laser brazing applications, filler wire pre-heating may be required with the 
wire feed system. This is typically achieved through interfacing between a 
portable welding power supply and the laser head/wire feed system. Any 
requests for deviations must be communicated in writing to the BCE Joining 
Engineer. 
6.11.9.2 
In applications where an energized filler wire is required, either a retractable or a 
swing-type grounding clamp shall be incorporated into the booth design and must 
be in a position to establish a positive contact with the vehicle being laser 
processed. 
6.11.9.3 
In roof welding and brazing applications, to account for possible variations in roof 
position, all such applications should have means of determining roof location 
and orientation prior to the start of laser cycle. Devices that may be used to 
determine roof location include an edge finder mounted on the laser robot, or a 
global sensor such as a booth-mounted camera. Each method must be capable 
of interfacing with the laser robot controller in order to offset the robot’s position 
and determine laser head start position. Robot offsetting vision system should be 
installed in addition to the laser head integrated seam tracker (see Section 
6.11.11.2) 
6.11.9.4 
Filler wire drums (when applicable) must be shipped to the production facilities 
with matching drum hoods. All drum hoods must be equipped with proper 
hardware for filler wire liner attachment. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 68 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.11.9.5 
All applications involving a filler wire shall have an "End of Wire" indicator 
installed on each of the wire packaging/containers feeding the laser booth. Such 
indicators shall be interlocked to the PLC to alarm the laser cell operator that a 
wire packaging/container change is required. 
6.11.10 
Roof Hold Down Fixture 
6.11.10.1 
In roof laser welding and brazing applications, pressure blocks mounted on a roof 
hold-down fixture shall be constructed of Teflon type material (Du Pont brand or 
equivalent) with black oxide coated sheet steel heat shield plates attached to the 
blocks and facing the Laser process. Such shield plates shall not come in contact 
with the BIW during the hold-down cycle. 
6.11.10.2 
Each pressure block shall have the capability to be individually controlled via a 
pneumatic valve, enabling the option to independently control the amount of 
pressure exerted by a specific block on the roof. 
6.11.11 
Laser Head Requirements 
6.11.11.1 
Each laser head shall have power monitoring capabilities built in the focusing 
optics head. This internal power monitoring requirement is in addition to the 
external device. 
6.11.11.2 
In edge weld applications, a seam tracker shall be integrated with the weld head 
to locate the edge of the part being welded. The seam tracker shall be either 
optical (non-contact) or a tactile sensor-based (mechanical) system. 
6.11.11.3 
An optic cover lens life indicator and a corresponding fault message shall be 
provided as part of the station controls for each laser weld head. The protective 
shield glass (lens cover) shall be monitored by sensing its temperature and/or 
reflected light through it. Such a device shall be capable of detecting an 
excessively contaminated lens and/or a damaged cover lens. All monitoring 
information must be fed to the HMI for possible manual intervention. Monitoring 
acceptance limits shall be preset and calibrated prior to weld verification and are 
the responsibility of the Tier 1 Integrator. 
6.11.11.4 
A crash protection device or clutch must be installed between the laser head and 
the robot. A fault must be generated if a collision is detected. 
6.11.11.5 
For laser heads fitted with a pressure wheel, prior to system buy-off at the Tier 1 
Integrator facility, the pressure wheel load characteristics shall be determined by 
the Tier 1 Integrator. This characterization will be represented in the form of a 
graph correlating the head's electrical system output versus actual force (weight 
measured by a scale) applied by the wheel. The correlation curve will be 
presented to the BCE Joining Engineer prior to system buyoff.

6.11.11.6 
The optical loop water flow rate shall be validated and recorded for each laser 
and robot pair after the completion of laser system integration on the assembly 
plant floor. The recorded readings shall be affixed to the inside of the laser 
generator panel door next to the water flow valves. 
6.11.11.7 
Water flow rate shall, at a minimum, match the recommended flow rate 
established individually by the Tier 2 Equipment Suppliers of the laser system

6.11.12 
Fiber Optic Cables 
6.11.12.1 
Fiber Optic cable routing must be reviewed and approved by the laser 
manufacturer, as well as all other relevant Tier 2 Equipment Suppliers. 
6.11.13 
Process Related Requirements 
6.11.13.1 
If an E-Stop occurs during welding or brazing cycle, a fault recovery shall 
automatically provide an uninterrupted weld and a minimally interrupted weld or 
braze seam. This will account for the skip that was created by any delay between 
the laser stopping and the robot stopping. Fault recovery capabilities must be 
demonstrated during <PP> build phase and validated during Laser booth buyoff.  

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 70 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.11.13.2 
For applications involving a filler wire, following any interruption in the process, 
such as a manual intervention or the application of an E-Stop, each robot shall 
subsequently move to a wire trimming position and have the wire extension 
trimmed prior to re-starting the welding/brazing cycle. Fault recovery capabilities 
must be demonstrated during <PP> build phase and validated during Laser 
booth Buyoff.  
6.11.13.3 
Prior to shipping the integrated laser booth to the production facility, the Tier 1 
Integrator shall validate operation of each laser head by welding or brazing the 
intended parts, subassembly, or vehicle. If not available, with a written 
permission of the BCE Joining Engineer, a set of coupons may be substituted for 
the actual parts. This validation must be performed to ensure proper laser 
function, beam delivery through the head optics, and controls interface. Weld 
integrity of the coupons must be validated via both a coupon peel and 
metallurgical section analysis. 
6.11.13.4 
Each laser installation shall have the provision to display primary process 
parameters such as, at a minimum, laser output power, robot travel speed, and 
filler wire feed speed (when applicable), and filler wire pre-heat current (when 
applicable). Such display will be available for viewing on either a PC workstation 
or an HMI station located outside the laser booth. 
6.11.13.5 
The validated optimum process parameters for each of the laser processes 
carried out inside the laser cell must be engraved on a placard and fixed to the 
exterior wall of the laser booth in a readily visible location. At a minimum, the 
display will include the required laser power, robot travel speed, pressure wheel 
load, fiber optic cable length and diameter, wire feed speed (when applicable), 
shielding gas type and flow rate (when applicable). 
6.11.13.6 
The wire conduit that runs from the wire packaging/container to the wire feeder 
on the robot arm must have as few bends as possible. The wire conduit must be 
of an ultra-low friction push-type, such as a Rolliner NG, MasterLiner, or 
equivalent. The wire conduit shall be of a one-piece design, i.e. no splices or 
junctions allowed (see figure below). Wire conduit routing must be reviewed and 
approved by all relevant Tier 2 Equipment Suppliers.

6.11.13.7 
All laser process-related consumables such as cover lenses, contact tips, filler 
wire, filler wire feed liners, chiller filters, and braze finishing abrasive wheels 
(where applicable) shall be supplied by the Tier 1 Integrator through the 
completion of the <TT> build phase. 
6.11.13.8 
During laser system buyoff, the Tier 1 Integrator shall submit a list of spare parts 
(see GVOSS Section 1.2.8 Non-Production Industrial Materials for Equipment) to 
be maintained by the production facility. The spare parts list shall cover all lasers, 
laser heads, chillers, wire feeders and delivery (when applicable), gas delivery 
and monitoring hardware (when applicable) and all other pertinent auxiliary 
equipment. This list must be prioritized relative to the expected frequency of 
usage and criticality to the process as recommended by all relevant Tier 2 
Equipment Suppliers. 
6.11.13.9 
An in-line inspection station shall be provided immediately following the laser cell 
exit to allow for timely inspection, performance feedback, and repair if necessary. 
The station will facilitate operator access for the purpose of visual inspection of 
both sides of the vehicle. 
6.11.13.10 
During new model launch integrated build of aluminum laser welded assemblies: 
See also Aluminum Laser Weld Quality Enablers for New Model Launch 
6.11.14 
Laser Cutting 
6.11.14.1 
When utilizing compressed air as the assist gas for laser cutting, the Tier 1 
Integrator shall supply compressed air at the pressure level necessary to achieve 
the required cut quality and slug removal. The air pressure required for cutting 
may necessitate the installation of a stand-alone air compressor. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 72 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.11.14.2 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide means of filtering and drying the compressed 
air supplied to the laser cutting heads. Compressed air quality shall satisfy the 
minimum requirements established by the cutting head manufacturer relative to 
oil content, condensation level, and dust particle size and concentration. 
6.11.14.3 
Compressed air assist gas will require an analog valve to control flow in three 
states, standby, pierce, and cutting. 
6.11.14.4 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall ensure proper clearance underneath the cutting 
surfaces to avoid damage to adjacent objects during the cutting cycle. No 
exposed equipment, tooling, electrical wiring, hoses, etc. shall be located at close 
proximity to the sheet metal parts being laser cut. As the clearance requirement 
is based on specific process parameters, the Tier 1 Integrator shall establish and 
validate clearance prior to finalizing system design. 
6.11.14.5 
Debris container or collection equipment shall be equipped where applicable. It is 
required to be integrated with cutting production line by Tier 1 Integrator and 
must be removable for periodic emptying of debris. The container shall be held 
such that it can be removed without the use of tools for replacement or disposal 
of debris.

Page 73 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.11.15 
Remote Laser Welding 
6.11.15.1 
The laser head controller box must be mounted outside of the booth so that it can 
be accessed while the station is in auto. 
6.11.15.2 
Layout of the station must be so that maintenance can access all parts of the 
fixtures without having to step on or over obstructions that should not be stepped 
on (i.e., cable trays, 7th-axis tracks, cables, airlines, etc.). 
6.11.15.3 
The clamp tooling scheme shall be designed around every single remote laser 
weld.  Clamps should be designed as close to the welding surface as possible 
(approximately 5 mm) without obstructing the laser beam path from the welding 
head. 
6.11.15.4 
All clamp fingers must be flat and evenly pressed along the part.  Clamp 
shimming can initially be set up without metal.  A good starting point for this is to 
measure the distance between the net blocks and clamp fingers and set this 
distance to the stack-up thickness minus 0.25 mm to ensure a press from the 
fingers.  Once this is established, parts should be put into the fixture and every 
clamp shall be tested with feeler gauges between the clamp and the part.  This 
must always be a zero-gap condition to ensure the clamp is pressing down on 
the part.  Then feeler gauges shall be used to measure between the two sheets 
of metal for the surface where the weld is located.  This target gap is between 0 
and 0.1mm. 
6.11.15.5 
The clamp fingers should extend the length of the flange to the trim edge in order 
to ensure a consistent, flat and gap-free welding surface. 
6.11.15.6 
Plume suppression shall be set up to blow the airflow across the weld and not 
aimed directly at the weld.  Development has shown that the most robust results 
come with a plume suppression of about 1500 FPM.  The target window should 
be between 900 – 2000 FPM. Plume suppression shall be measured at the weld 
using an anemometer in feet per minute (FPM). 
6.11.15.7 
All the Remote Laser Heads in a station shall be networked to a single computer 
and robots properly identified in the remote laser weld head software. 
6.11.15.8 
Each Remote Laser Head robot must have its own coupon stand and power 
meter. 
6.11.15.9 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall scribe mark mounting plates on remote laser welding 
robots to ensure proper orientation when replacing a remote laser welding head. 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 74 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.12 
SECONDARY (STANDBY) EQUIPMENT AND PROCESS REQUIREMENTS 
6.12.1 
General 
6.12.1.1 
This section pertains to robot, weld gun and other body assembly joining 
systems. These include nut runners, stud weld guns, laser weld, laser cut, 
plasma cut, drawn arc brackets, adhesive, sealer, self-piercing and pop rivet 
systems.  
6.12.1.2 
The objective is to establish a uniform starting point for secondary (standby) 
equipment requirements. Deviations to the guidelines must be documented and 
agreed to by VO, plants and Tier 1 Integrators prior to <PA>, verified at VTTO 
and validated on the plants’ floor at TTO. 
6.12.1.3 
Unless specified otherwise in this section, all requirements shall conform to the 
standards and specifications set forth in the Ford Motor Company BCE GVOSS. 
NOTE:  Special attention shall be paid to providing information, documentation 
and processes necessary to support Ford Motor Company Equipment R&M 
efforts and policies. 
6.12.2 
Requirements 
6.12.2.1 
This section pertains to process and equipment requirements to prevent or 
minimize production losses in the event of robot, weld gun or other body 
assembly joining system failure. This includes sub-assembly equipment. 
6.12.2.2 
A robot bypass shall be implemented to give the plants the option to bypass any 
joining robot in the event of a major breakdown. 
6.12.2.2.1 
Robots that perform geometry or closed out joining (includes dispensed material) 
shall not be bypassed. 
6.12.2.2.2 
Prior to bypassing any robot, an approved repair standard, which includes welds, 
rivets, clinch, and flow drill must be signed off by the appropriate PD engineer 
and entered GSPAS. 
6.12.2.2.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall develop and provide a list of proposed robots with 
bypass functionality for review during the 50% Mechanical Design review. 
6.12.2.3 
Automated secondary equipment and processes shall be reduced/eliminated with 
any one of the following enablers: 
6.12.2.4 
Modular system designs are utilized, or any part can be replaced or changed 
over with reduced downtime as compared to alternative designs. 
6.12.2.5 
Feedback controls are utilized to continually monitor equipment status to foster 
predictive maintenance and reduce unplanned downtime. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 75 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.12.3 
Manual Systems 
6.12.3.1 
Tier 1 Integrator responsibility at VTTO – Supplier must adhere to the agreed-
upon design and build secondary system requirements, demonstrate and 
achieve sign-off of both the primary and secondary systems and related robot 
programming prior to tool shipment (VTTO Verification) with validation on the 
Plant floor at TTO. 
6.12.4 
Exceptions 
6.12.4.1 
Based on the availability of the stud weld process, the following standby 
equipment is required: 
6.12.4.2 
Each pedestal and/or robot-mounted stud weld gun shall have a secondary gun 
present complete with cables and feed tube inside the fence line.  The 
changeover process should be designed to use "quick-change" weld head 
mounting plates, slides and/or connectors.  Only routine preventative 
maintenance (cleaning / collet change, etc.) is to be performed when the weld 
gun is connected.  Note: the energy sources (electrical power and air) must be 
turned to the off position before the weld head is removed from or installed to the 
supplied cable package.   
6.13 
MECHANICALLY INSERTED FASTENER EQUIPMENT 
6.13.1 
General Requirements 
6.13.1.1 
This section pertains to fastener insertion press equipment that is used for the 
installation of studs and nuts that are released to the most recent Clinch or 
Pierce-and-Clinch Stud and Nut Engineering Specifications (ES). Mechanically 
inserted studs, bolts and nuts shall meet the test requirements of the applicable 
standard.  
6.13.1.2 
Validation testing of fastener insertion equipment must be completed by the Tier 
2 Equipment Supplier as follows, regardless of whether the fastener being 
installed is new to Ford.  Results are to be reviewed by the Ford BCSE Joining 
team and Tier 1 Integrator.  Non-conforming conditions are to be identified and 
resolved immediately. 
• The fastener feed device (hopper, bowl, escapement) shall be tested all 
the way to the press fastener insertion tooling with cycling of the press.  
This testing is done without parts or coupons. 
 
o For studs and bolts, this can be accomplished using a hollow die to 
allow the studs or bolts to fall through when the tooling collapses. 
o For nuts, this can be accomplished using a gripper to allow the nuts 
to avoid being pressed into the die. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 76 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• All tooling must pass fastener feed and press cycling tests prior to being 
delivered to the Tier 1 Integrator or plant location as indicated: 
 
o Newly developed tooling must pass a 5,000-fastener feed test. 
o Previously developed tooling must pass a 2,500-fastener feed test. 
• All tooling shall demonstrate that 100 fasteners can be inserted into parts, 
plates, or coupons. 
• A test run check sheet shall be delivered to the Tier 1 Integrator and Ford 
BCSE Joining upon completion, prior to tool shipment. 
 
The Tier 2 Equipment Supplier must procure all fasteners required for testing.  
Ford can help as needed with the procurement of the material for testing.  
While the fasteners need to be as specified for production, the material 
(parts, plates, or coupons) used for the 100-fastener insertion do not have 
to be exactly production representative since the main purpose is to 
validate the equipment functionality. 
 
6.13.1.2.1 
All newly developed tooling must pass a 5,000-fastener feed test prior to be 
delivered to the Tier 1 Integrator or plant location. All previously developed 
tooling must pass a 2,500-fastener feed test prior to be delivered to the Tier 1 
Integrator or plant location. 
6.13.1.3 
Presses shall be delivered with a method for ensuring the setting head and die 
alignment is correct. 
6.13.1.4 
The Tier 2 Equipment Supplier and Tier 1 Integrator are jointly responsible for 
developing and testing the controls interface logic prior to equipment buyoff and 
shipment to the installation facility. 
6.13.1.5 
For any given subassembly with multiple fastener insertion presses for the same 
fastener, the Tier 1 Integrator should design the line in a manner which ensures 
that there are no fasteners that only one press can insert. For example, a press 
can be oversized for an application and designed for the worst-case access 
scenario, if it allows for an additional station where nuts assigned to a unique 
press can be inserted. Contact BCE Joining to see if this applies to any given 
subassembly. 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 77 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.13.2 
Product Design Requirements 
6.13.2.1 
The Tier 2 Equipment Supplier shall be responsible for performing FLTM BA-
119-01 Self-Pierce and Clinch Fastener Acceptance Test for Aluminum and 
Steel. (Consult Ford PD or BCE Joining to determine if acceptance testing has 
already been completed for the proposed fastener and material combination.) 
This testing shall be completed with production intent representative equipment 
and material. Testing shall be completed prior to design approval of the 
equipment. 
6.13.2.2 
The equipment manufacturer and Tier 1 Integrator shall be jointly responsible for 
establishing and setting the process windows and parameters to allow the 
fastener to meet the applicable standard to the production tooling. 
6.13.2.3 
The Tier 2 Equipment Supplier shall immediately notify Ford BCE Joining when a 
system is requested for a fastener that requires the development of a new setting 
head, i.e., first application at Ford or first application for Tier 2. 
6.13.2.4 
Multi-point fastener insertion press applications where more than one fastener is 
installed under one drive, the Tier 2 Equipment Supplier, Fastener Manufacturer, 
Tier 1 Integrator and BCE Joining must review the DFMEA and approve the 
process design. The design of a multi-point insertion press must include 
independent adjustment of the setting head and die for the purpose of proper 
alignment. 
6.13.2.5 
The Tier 1 is responsible for providing the dress package to properly support and 
route the feed tube for the system. The Tier 1 and Tier 2 shall work together to 
design the proper support and routing strategy. The Tier 2 must sign off on the 
design of the feed tube routing and dress package. 
 
 
6.13.3 
Press Requirements 
6.13.3.1 
The press shall be equipped with in-station process controls, which will provide 
force and position feedback at a minimum.  The equipment shall have the 
capability to set process windows around force and position.  
A method for viewing and modifying the process windows must be provided, i.e., 
HMI or panel view screen. The ability to record and save the results must be 
provided. 
6.13.3.2 
BCE Joining must approve the suppliers of the individual main components of the 
press such as the feeder, setting head, etc. prior to final design approval by the 
Tier 2 Equipment Supplier 

 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 6 – WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 78 of 80 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
The fastener manufacturers setting head shall be used except when noted by 
BCE Joining. Approval from BCE Joining along with the completion of production 
validation is required to use an alternate setting head.  
An alternate setting head must provide improved availability, reduced 
maintenance, industrial materials complexity, or cost compared to the fastener 
suppliers setting head to be considered for approval. 
6.13.3.3 
All presses shall be provided with position detection with the minimum 
requirements of detecting the following conditions: 
• Fully retracted 
• Advanced and ready to intensify (all setting conditions met)  
• Overtravel (no fastener and/or part present) 
6.13.3.4 
A container shall be provided to capture slugs that are removed for pierce nut 
applications. 
6.13.4 
Inspection / Repair Requirements 
6.13.4.1 
Take-out stations are to be provided, at the end of the sub-assembly line for 
manual inspection, repair or swap out. If the fastener is closed out prior to the 
end of the line, an inspection point needs to be provided before the joint is closed 
out. 
6.13.5 
Error Proofing 
6.13.5.1 
All Mechanically installed fasteners require error-proofing integral to the tool, 
which applies the fastener. 
6.13.5.2 
This error proofing shall be capable of determining correct fastener presence, 
location and orientation. Fastener type and size shall be error-proofed by the 
given feed mechanism(s). 
6.14 
REPAIR REQUIREMENTS 
6.14.1 
General Requirements 
6.14.1.1 
These requirements apply to programs where new lines are being installed or 
where significant joining processes are being incorporated in existing lines. 
6.14.1.2 
In-line weld repair stations included in process layouts under the process defined 
under the process described in GMR-571 Weld Inspection, Containment, and 
Repair. 
6.14.1.3 
Basic facilities requirements for a repair area include: 
• 3 phase AC – example 480V / one 200A drop with one outlet on each side 
for portable welder.

6.15 
INDUSTRY 4.0 FOR WELDING / MECHANICAL FASTENING EQUIPMENT 
6.15.1 
General Requirements 
6.15.1.1 
This section is intended to cover Industry 4.0 (Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT), 
Factory of Tomorrow (FoT)) software for all joining equipment covered in Section 
6. Tooling manufacturers are required to offer to Ford Industry 4.0-capable 
tooling, including a dashboard, interface, or HMI to visualize real-time production 
data to improve and perform tasks related to root cause analysis, improved 
automation and fault recovery, and predictive analytics and maintenance. 
6.15.1.2 
The tooling manufacturer can meet this requirement by offering their own 
Industry 4.0 software or by working with Ford to develop software and an 
interface that adheres to the data sharing standard outlined in GVOSS Section 
1.8. 
6.15.1.3 
Industry 4.0 offerings must be reviewed by the BCE Joining Engineer who is 
leading the program being quoted. New joining Industry 4.0 technologies being 
considered for incorporation into a program must have a detailed project 
proposal having the complete project content and associated cost including 
impact to Ford-to-Furnish items as well as any agreement terms related to the 
offering. 
6.16 
EQUIPMENT OBSOLESCENCE 
6.16.1 
Ford requires that the Tier 2 Equipment Suppliers’ components remain in 
production for at least 14 years. For any components in the supplier’s supply 
chain that become obsolete or no longer available, it is the responsibility of the 
supplier to: 
 
1. Formally notify Ford Motor Company within 5 years, 3 years and 1 year 
prior to component obsolescence 
 
2. Provide an alternative component to replace the obsolete component 
having the same functionality and cost. 
 
3. If an alternative component cannot be provided prior to the time of 
component obsolescence, then the supplier shall provide Ford a

7.1 
INTRODUCTION  
7.1.1 
This section applies to new, global standard tooling only. For kits, re-use, and 
existing applications use existing specifications and/or requirements to which the 
equipment was initially installed. 
7.1.2 
The selected Approved Sealer System supplier is responsible for 100% 
compliance to all aspects of this specification unless otherwise specified. Within 
this specification the word “sealer” represents all pumpable materials used in 
BIW such as sealer, adhesive, mastic, bulk applied sound deadener and metal 
reinforcement adhesive. This specification contains all general requirements for 
automated application systems or manual application systems used in BIW, as 
shown in Figure 1. For automated systems, two different variants are included in 
this specification, a fixed mounted applicator/doser, and a robot mounted 
applicator/doser, including hoses. The requirements are general, and they apply 
to all installations, irrespective of the robot supplier. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
               Figure 1. Automated and Manual Application Systems 

 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 7 – ROBOT AND MANUAL APPLIED SEALERS, 
ADHESIVES, MASTICS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
 
Page 3 of 37 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
Printed copies are uncontrolled 
7.1.3 
When a Tier 2 Equipment Supplier wants to include a component or piece of 
equipment within their system, which has not been previously approved and used 
within Ford, a design review must be held.  Examples include using a new 
servomotor on a doser or using a new PLC within the control. This design review 
is to be scheduled by the Tier 2 Equipment Supplier to include VOME Joining 
and Controls personnel as required, as soon as a deviation from prior program 
equipment installations is known to be desired or needed.

7.2 
TERMS 
7.2.1 
Equipment Terms for Automated and Manual Systems 
Refer to Figure 1 for the items below. List item numbers (when present) indicate 
the corresponding component in Figure 1. 
1. Pump Set: An assembly of two material pumps that includes an air motor, 
pneumatic ram assembly, control unit, follower plates, outlet hoses, crossover 
valve, and communication cables. 
1.1. Follower Plate: A metallic disk attached to the bottom section of the 
material pump containing an encircling O-ring to seal the material in the 
container from leaking into the plant environment. 
1.2. Material Pump: A mechanical device, usually pneumatic, that transfers 
material from a container such as a pail or drum through a series of hoses 
to a doser or mastic regulator. 
1.3. Air Motor: The pneumatic cylinder assembly located above the material 
pump that drives the piston of the pump which moves sealer out of the 
container. 
1.4. Ram Assembly: A set of pneumatic pistons that raise and lower the 
follower plate and material pump in an out of the container. 
1.5. Pump Crossover Valve: A pneumatic, three-way ball valve located 
between the active and inactive material pumps. 
1.6. Pump Outlet Hose: The hose from the material pump to the crossover 
valve. 
2. Sealing System Control Unit: A controller panel containing a pump control 
unit, doser controller, heating zone controller, and human-machine interface. 
2.1. Pump Control Unit: An electro-pneumatic device that operates the 
material pump and monitors its status. 
2.2. Doser Controller: An electric device that controls the parameters of a 
sealer application and the applicator/doser. Dosing pumps often have a 
controller which enables the sealer flow to be monitored and adjusted 
easily. 
2.3. Heating Zone Controller: An electric device that provides power and 
controls the heat zones. 
2.4. Human-Machine Interface: A means for interacting with the sealing 
system. 
3. Sealing Robot System: “Sealing robot” is an industry term for a robot that 
has additional fluid handling software integrated into the robot that 
differentiate it from all other standard industrial robot applications. 

 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 7 – ROBOT AND MANUAL APPLIED SEALERS, 
ADHESIVES, MASTICS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
 
Page 5 of 37 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3.1. Sealing Robot: For robot applied sealers, the device which carries the 
doser and applicator. 
3.2. Robot Controller: An information processing device whose inputs are 
both the desired and measured position, velocity or other pertinent 
variables in a process and whose outputs are drive signals to a controlling 
motor or actuator. 
4. Pedestal: A support structure for fixed applicator/doser systems to apply 
material on a part carried by a material handling robot. 
5. Hosing and Controller Cables: Hosing configurations may be shared or 
unshared depending on the nature of the process. 
5.1. Material Hose (shared): The material hose delivers material from the 
pump crossover valve to the doser. In shared pump applications the 
shared material hose will terminate at a tee or Y fitting. 
5.2. Tee or Y fitting (shared pump applications): In shared pump 
applications the tee or Y fitting bridges the shared material hose to the 
unshared material hoses. 
5.3. Material hose (unshared): The hose or hoses that provide material from 
the pump crossover valve to a single doser. 
5.4. Doser controller cable: The connection between the doser controller 
and the doser. 
5.5. Pump controller cable: The cable interfacing the robot controller (if 
used) to the sealing system control unit. 
6. Automated 
Dispensing 
Components: 
The 
dispensing 
components 
represent the components located on the robot or pedestal that will dispense 
the material. 
6.1. Purge Valve: Manually actuated ball value used in order to bleed air out 
of the hose more rapidly. 
6.2. Doser Mounting Bracket: For the connection of the doser to the robot or 
pedestal. 
6.3. Doser: A servo-electric motor driven material dosing system that controls 
the flow rate and volume of the material dispensed from an applicator gun 
valve and nozzle. 
6.4. Heated Outlet Hose to Applicator Valve: The connection between the 
doser and the applicator called “divorced application” (used in case the 
doser weight exceeds the robot’s pay load). 
6.5. Applicator: Valve to control the start and stop of material flow from the 
nozzle. It is sometimes referred to as a “gun.” 

 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 7 – ROBOT AND MANUAL APPLIED SEALERS, 
ADHESIVES, MASTICS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
 
Page 6 of 37 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
Printed copies are uncontrolled 
6.6. Nozzle: A projecting part with an opening, as at the end of a hose, for 
regulating and directing a flow of fluid or sealer. 
6.7. End of Arm Bracket: The connection between the doser or the 
applicator and the end point of the pedestal/robot. 
6.8. Drip Pan: A container for catching material that drips from above. Usually 
a shallow, rectangular metal pan. 
7. Manual Application System: Manual applicators have many fewer 
components. 
7.1. Manual Station Post: A fixed structure to support the hose and 
applicator assembly. 
7.2. Manual Applicator: Valve to control the start and stop of material flow 
from the nozzle. 
7.3. Regulator: A pneumatic valve that controls the material flow rate from the 
pump. 
7.4. Balancer: A device to counter balance the weight of the filled hoses and 
applicator to enable an ergonomic dispensing operation. 
7.5. Guiding Clip: Used to route the hose from the balancer to the applicator. 
7.2.2 
Material Terms 
Adhesive: A weld through, non-expandable organic material applied as an 
extruded bead and compressed between two metal flanges via spot welds or 
rivets for the purpose of transferring load across the flange to improve stiffness or 
durability. Adhesives are not designed for primary sealing applications. 
Bulk Applied Sound Deadener: A non-weldable organic material with no 
expansion properties extruded as a rectangular patch on a metal surface such as 
a closure outer, roof or bodyside for the purpose of dampening road noise or 
closure closing sounds. 
Bulk Applied Stiffeners: A non-weldable organic material with no expansion 
properties but highly filled with reinforcement particles or fibers for the purpose of 
localized stiffening of thin sheet metal areas such as any class A surface. 
Mastic: A non-weldable organic material with expansion properties applied as an 
extruded bead either half round or triangular in cross-section designed to bridge 
a gap between two metal surfaces for the purpose of improving NVH (anti-flutter) 
of roof and closure panels. 
Sealer: A weld thru expandable organic material applied as an extruded bead 
and compressed between two metal flanges via spot welds or rivets for the 
purpose of impeding the ingress of water and/or air. 
7.2.3 
Application Terms 

 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 7 – ROBOT AND MANUAL APPLIED SEALERS, 
ADHESIVES, MASTICS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
 
Page 7 of 37 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
Printed copies are uncontrolled 
Flow Rate: Volume of dispensed material as a function of time. Expressed in 
cubic centimeters per second (cm3/s). 
Material Bead Length: Segment distance from start point to end point of a linear 
application of sealer. Expressed in millimeters (mm). 
Pre-Pressure: Prior to opening the applicator valve, the doser compresses the 
material causing a flow immediately at the bead start without an encoder 
movement. 
Program: A set of parameters that generates a certain volume stream of the 
sealer during a given Tool Center Point (TCP) velocity and application distance. 
Purging: Emptying of the doser or applicator, or manually extruding out material 
in a bucket. 
Section: Distance between two teach spots of a robot program. 
TCPP: Tool Center Point Protocol. 
Volume Per Length: A defined volume per linear distance. It represents the 
cross-sectional area of the applied bead, expressed in either square centimeters 
(cm2) or millimeters (mm2).  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
             Figure 2. Example of some Application Terms

7.3 
MILESTONES 
7.3.1 
Ford-to-Furnish Programs 
7.3.1.1 
Documented performance at Tier 2 sealer equipment supplier. 
• Tier 2 supplier shall demonstrate entire scope of work to the Tier 1 
supplier and VOME representation to ensure Ford-to-Furnish content has 
been designed as specified by the Tier 1. 
• All systems and components shall be available at the Tier 2’s location and 
in an accessible location to ensure that the system and components 
match the scope in the previous paragraph. 
7.3.1.2 
Documented performance at integrator/tooling Tier 1 Integrator. 
• Sealers to be supplied for VTTO builds per GVOSS Section 1. 
• Complete functioning in the simulated production environment running 
prototype or production parts by the Tier 1 integrator.  
• Production pumps with 20-liter follower plates shall be used for VTTO. 
Satellite pumps will only be used for Magna drums. Only one pump from 
each crossover set will be used. 
7.3.2 
All Programs 
7.3.2.1 
Material Supply and Disposition 
• Consult local VOME Joining Engineering for supply and disposition of 
sealers in each plant. In general MP&L assumes responsibility from TT in 
FNA, and New Model Programs assumes responsibility from TT until MP1 
in FOE. 
7.3.2.2 
Final acceptance of the sealer equipment installation at production area 
• The sealer equipment supplier is responsible for the following up to MP1: 
• Update parameter sheets. 
• Verify all components of the sealer system are working correctly. 
• Verify Tier 1 Integrator has correctly installed and wet out the sealer system.  
• Verify Tier 1 Integrator has correctly dressed out all robot carried systems. 
Tier 2 must sign off on all robot dress out of their systems at MP1.

7.4 
SEALER ROBOT PROGRAMMING 
7.4.1 
Material handling robots must be programmed using remote tool center point 
(RTCP) for fixed pedestal systems. 
7.4.2 
All seal schedules must use TCPP Volume (volume per length, e.g., cm3/meter) 
and not fixed voltage or Bead Width (BW) flow types. The seal schedule is the 
cross section of the applied bead in mm2. 
7.4.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator must follow the Ford Programming Guide and document the 
program seal path on a “node map” as defined in the buyoff requirements listed 
in GVOSS Section 1. 
7.4.4 
Initial parameters are to be determined by the supplier of the sealer package for 
materials used in the plant, defined by Vehicle Product Engineering 
Specifications, considering the material and equipment supplier requirements, 
and prevailing plant conditions. 
• Sealer / Adhesive Flow = cm3/s 
• Gun Pre-open Time = ms (if applicable) 
• Gun Pre-close Time = ms (if applicable) 
• TCP Velocity = mm/s 
• TCP Application Distance = mm 
• Pre-Pressure = bar (if applicable) 
7.4.5 
To fully validate the effectiveness of the applications, the Tier 1 Integrator must 
complete the Sealer/Adhesive/Mastic Teardown Report in Section 1.2.6.3.19. 

 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005

7.5 
GENERAL DEMANDS 
7.5.1 
All material in the function package must be chemically resistant to and not react 
to the sealers in use. 
7.5.2 
The function package must be compatible with materials containing up to a 
maximum of 10% (by weight) of 0.25 mm glass beads. 
7.5.3 
Extreme caution shall be exercised to ensure that no silicone-containing 
materials or lubricants are used in the supplied equipment. Specific items include 
lubricants inside all valves, pipe drops, wire pulling lubricants, O-ring lubricants, 
aerosol sprays for lubrication and waterproofing, and hand lotions. In the event 
that any contaminated components, materials, or construction aids are 
discovered, the supplier is required to replace all construction possibly affected at 
no expense to Ford Motor Company. 
7.5.4 
All components used in the manufacturing of this equipment must comply with 
“Approved Source List” provided with the request for quotation (see 
GVOSS Section 2). 
7.5.5 
Preferred Dispensing System List can be found in GVOSS Section 2. These 
systems are preferred for use as they have passed the Verification Test as 
outlined in this GVOSS section. 
7.5.6 
Additional Tier 1 Responsibilities  
7.5.6.1 
A selector switch shall be provided by the Tier 1 Integrator to bring the completed 
part to the gate for view before transferring to the next station. If the dispensed 
bead cannot be evaluated from outside the fence area for skips by a reasonable 
person per the engineering specification (ESBU5A-1B301-AA or 1B302-AA) then 
a video camera and monitor must be installed by the Tier 1 integrator per the 
Remote Viewing specifications outlined in GVOSS Section 10. This applies for 
both pedestal and Robot carried systems.  
7.5.6.2 
For Ford-to-Furnish programs, the Tier 1 shall provide each doser with its own 
independent air supply, lockout, regulator, and pressure switch consistent with 
GVOSS Section 2.3.7. 
7.5.7 
Warranty 
7.5.7.1 
A three-year warranty (parts and service) on all components (except dispense 
nozzles) must be guaranteed starting at Job 1. 
7.5.7.2 
All material seals (gun valve, inlet valve, shotmeter, and pumps) shall remain 
leak free for a minimum of 500,000 cycles. 
7.5.8 
Sealer Robot Speed

7.5.8.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall evaluate the composite (average) robot speed during 
the process phase to maximize robot accuracy and minimize part movement. 
7.5.8.2 
300 mm/s shall be used for the composite speed at the process phase to develop 
station cycle time. See Section 7.19 for maximum dispense speeds per 
application.

7.6 
SEALER APPLICATION UNIT (DOSER/APPLICATOR FUNCTIONS) 
7.6.1 
No dripping or leaking of sealer is allowed when the applicator/doser is switched 
off. 
7.6.2 
The flow and pressure of the sealer must be closed-loop-controlled during 
application of the doser in order to adapt to changes in rheology. 
7.6.3 
The doser shall be filled up at a given input signal. The time for filling a doser 
must be monitored. If the time is out of limit, an alarm shall be activated. Filling of 
the doser shall not take away any time from the production cycle. 
7.6.4 
An automatic purging function shall be available. Purging shall be done when the 
system has been standing still for a certain time. Purge time is material 
dependent and specified by the dispensing equipment supplier. The purging 
function shall be controlled via a programmable timer (e.g. in the line PLC). This 
is especially important for 2K material. 
7.6.5 
A function must be provided so that the applicator/doser can be switched off 
manually and the robot can run without applying any sealer (flow/no-flow). 
7.6.6 
Applications shall be standardized (e.g., type of nozzle, doser, controls, software) 
whenever possible so as to reduce spare parts inventory complexity and to 
prevent potential misapplication of spare parts. 
7.6.7 
Greases and oils used for lubrication of the system must be compatible with the 
sealer applied. All lubricants/coolants must be shown to be non-reactive in the 
paint system and have Tox Number on record. Non-lubed equipment is 
preferred. 
7.6.8 
All servo dozers shall utilize encoders which do not require remastering after loss 
of system power. 
7.6.9 
Nozzles 
7.6.9.1.1 
Robot carried dispensers that apply material to vertical part sections (within ±15°) 
shall use streaming. 
7.6.9.1.2 
For Ford-to-Furnish Programs, streaming shall be the preferred method of 
application for adhesives and non-topical body sealers. 
7.6.9.1.3 
Extruded applications require breakaway or spring-loaded nozzles compatible 
with lower pressure fault detection functions. 
7.6.9.1.4 
All swirl nozzles shall be of the electrical variety, e.g., e-swirl. Pneumatic swirl is 
not permitted. 
7.6.9.1.5 
For Ford-to-Furnish Programs, electric swirl streaming (e-swirl) shall be the 
preferred method of application for hem adhesives. 

 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 7 – ROBOT AND MANUAL APPLIED SEALERS, 
ADHESIVES, MASTICS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
 
Page 14 of 37 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
7.6.9.2 
Multi daub mastic systems 
7.6.9.2.1 
Multiple mastic ejectors affixed to a stationary plate that apply mastic 
simultaneously. 
References are in the SCA 702 HOOD Mastic Apply Stand  Example. 
• The plate height shall be between 48 and 52” from floor to allow access 
from below and above for maintenance and adjustment.  Dimension “A” in 
picture #2. 
• The ejector valves shall no higher than 8’ from floor.  See Dimension “B” in 
Picture #2. 
• A maintenance platform shall be provided to access the ejector 
valves 
• The I/O blocks controlling the ejector valves shall be placed such 
that status indicators are visible while servicing the ejector valves.  
See example in Picture #3. 
7.6.9.2.2 
Plate Design 
• Mastic ejectors shall have adjustment in (2) directions. 
• 25 mm perpendicular to the flange the mastic will contact 
• 6 mm parallel to the flange mastic will contact. 
• The adjustment shall be accomplished with a slotted hole with the long 
axis perpendicular to the flange the mastic interfaces with. 
• A drip plan shall be provided to capture purge material.
