Page 15 of 37 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
Printed copies are uncontrolled 
7.7 
 CONTROL AND POWER UNIT 
7.7.1 
The application instruction in the robot program shall supervise the whole 
application sequence. At the end position when the applicator is turned off, an 
alarm check shall be made. 
7.7.2 
It shall be possible to initiate filling and purging of the doser from the user 
interface. 
7.7.3 
It shall be possible to operate two independent dosers from one robot controller. 
Each independent doser must be capable of operating two separate applicators, 
but not simultaneously. If two dosers are controlled by one robot, a dual dispense 
controller is recommended. The dispense controller should be designed to fit 
above the robot controller. 
7.7.4 
The working temperature of the sealer to be applied shall decrease to resting 
temperature (25oC or as recommended by the sealer supplier) when production 
has been standing for a given, programmable time. A separate timer must also 
be provided for complete heat shut down. 
7.7.5 
The signal for increasing the sealer temperature from resting temperature to 
working temperature must be handled within the sealer function package. When 
this signal is triggered, the heating of the sealer shall begin so that the sealer 
reaches working temperature. This may be known as “remote start.” 
7.7.6 
The working pressure of the material to be applied shall be relieved when 
production has been standing for a given, programmable time. Regardless of 
what dispensing technology is used, the entire system shall be depressurized. A 
"Remote Start" feature shall return the dispense system to the state/mode it was 
in prior to initiation of auto-depressurization. The "Remote Start" shall also re-
start the temperature conditioner, if applicable. This communication shall come 
from the PLC through the robot to the dispense controller. Material pressures 
shall not be permitted to exceed 300 bar. The dispensing system must report to 
the PLC when in “sleep” or “energy saving mode”. 
7.7.7 
Operating pressure at the applicator is not to exceed the disposable static mix 
tube burst pressure for "2K" systems. 
7.7.8 
The sealer volume flow shall be monitored during without a negatively influence 
to robot movement or cycle time. 
7.7.9 
The following parameters shall be available to display in real time while 
dispensing. Both set and actual value: 
• Sealer Flow Rate (cm3/s) 
• Sealer Pressure (bar) 
• Sealer Temperature (C) 

 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 7 – ROBOT AND MANUAL APPLIED SEALERS, 
ADHESIVES, MASTICS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
 
Page 16 of 37 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• Mix Ratio (2K only) 
• Dispensed Total Volume (cm3) 
• Tolerance of dispensed Total Volume (%) 
• Each individual bead segment volume (cm3) 
7.7.10 
A logging function shall be available to log the following parameters: 
• Sealer Temperature (C) 
• Sealer Pressure (bar, min., max., and avg.) 
• Alarms (Faults) 
• Total Material Deposition/Cycle (Gun on Time (sec.)) 
• Mix Ratio (2K only) 
• Dispensed Total Volume (cm3) 
• Dispensed Segment Volume (cm3) 
• Compressibility (cm3) 
• Volume deviation to set values (%) 
7.7.11 
It shall be possible to log these parameters for each produced component or 
body with a minimum of 20,000 data points using a FIFO Buffer. Both graphic 
and digital display shall be available. The content in the logger shall be possible 
to transfer over USB in a non-proprietary data format. 
7.7.12 
Parameters having the potential for manual changes shall be logged when 
changes are made. 
7.7.13 
The I/O interface shall be capable of providing minimum 8-bit style select 
regardless of current Controls standards.  

 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 7 – ROBOT AND MANUAL APPLIED SEALERS, 
ADHESIVES, MASTICS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
 
Page 17 of 37 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
Printed copies are uncontrolled 
7.8 
MATERIAL SUPPLY UNIT (PUMP FUNCTIONS) 
7.8.1 
Material Pump Brand: It shall be possible to use different brands of material 
pumps within the function package. Pump brand to be specified for each 
purchase. 
7.8.2 
Drum Change: It shall be possible for one person with a manual device to 
change a 200-liter drum.  
7.8.3 
Automatic Change-Over: Double material pump and system shall have an 
automatic change-over. The low-level warning of one of the pumps must start the 
heating of the second pump. Thereafter the empty level switch shall activate 
automatically the second pump. 
7.8.4 
Air Evacuation: Separate, easy, air evacuation of the pump must be possible. It 
must be possible to run the pump separately in manual mode. 
7.8.5 
System Purge: It must be possible to purge the complete system from pump to 
doser without refilling the doser. No doser-only pump action is permitted. 
7.8.6 
Fittings: 90° fittings on material hoses for pumping or metering systems shall be 
avoided if possible. 
7.8.7 
Low Level: Low level of material in the material barrel shall be indicated when a 
certain level is reached. This level must be adjustable. 
7.8.8 
Material packaging: Pumping systems must be able to adapt to accept mini 
totes, conventional barrels and 20-liter pails. 
7.8.8.1 
Minitote Dimensions 
7.8.8.2 
Supplier Requirements 
7.8.8.3 
The system shall have the opportunity to install a cartridge filling device (e.g., for 
backup and/or repair processes). The number of cartridge filling devices, 
cartridges, and manual applicators to be agreed between VOME and Tier 1 
Integrator during Design Review, with one per material minimum per facility. 
7.8.8.4 
The barrel shall be fixed to the base plate with suitable components (e.g., hold-
down brackets or guides) matching to the barrel type (e.g., with/without venting 
hole in the bottom of the barrel). If different barrel types require a different barrel 
exchange process, a warning sign together with a respective hint must be 
attached to the Pump Control Unit. 

 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 7 – ROBOT AND MANUAL APPLIED SEALERS, 
ADHESIVES, MASTICS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
 
Page 18 of 37 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
Printed copies are uncontrolled 
7.9 
MEDIA CONTROL AND TRANSFER PACKAGE (AIR, WATER, POWER, ETC.) 
7.9.1 
All hoses on the function package shall be mounted (installed) so that strain and 
sharp bends are avoided. 
7.9.2 
It must be possible to temperature control the material in the hoses and pipes for 
material supply. The sealer/adhesive package shall also contain necessary 
hardware and software for temperature control. Temperature control and 
monitoring supervision shall be available from the material barrel to the nozzle.  
7.9.3 
The heating equipment shall be capable of providing an adhesive/sealer 
application temperature of +30 to +60 ºC. 
7.9.4 
The heating method shall not adversely affect the material properties. 
7.9.5 
It shall be possible for the system to detect if a material hose on the 
sealer/adhesive package is broken or leaking material.  
7.9.6 
Material Hoses 
7.9.6.1 
Material hoses shall have a burst strength that meets or exceeds 1400 bar and a 
working strength that meets or exceeds 350 bar. 
7.9.6.2 
Total length of supply hoses from the pump crossover to the doser must be 
provided by the Tier 1 Integrator at least one month prior to Tier 2 equipment 
buyoff. Total length is not to exceed 15 meters. Extensions of the total lengths 
can be granted via written approval of VOME, pending a design review of the 
justification, materials, and application details. 
7.9.6.3 
When one pump set is supplying material to two separate dosers (defined as 
pump share), the additional hose from the “T” or “Y” fitting to the second doser 
must be agreed to by the tier 2 equipment supplier in writing.  
7.9.6.4 
No more than 2 dosers shall be supplied by one pump set. 
7.9.6.5 
Swivel connections shall be used for the heated and non-heated material hoses 
to prevent premature wear when robot carried. 
7.9.7 
The Tier 2 equipment supplier is responsible to design and build a standard Ford 
bracket (see also standard Ford Bracket example) both for Pedestal and Robot 
carried applications. The brackets are identified in Figure 1 as an End of Arm and 
doser mount. If the standard is not accepted by the Tier 1 Integrator, then it is the 
responsibility of the Tier 1 Integrator to design and build the non-standard 
brackets. Any changes to the brackets after the FTF have been submitted are 
solely the responsibility of the Tier 1 Integrator including any added costs unless 
otherwise directed by Ford. 
 

 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 7 – ROBOT AND MANUAL APPLIED SEALERS, 
ADHESIVES, MASTICS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
 
Page 19 of 37 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
Printed copies are uncontrolled 
7.9.8 
Pedestals 
7.9.8.1 
Adhesive/seal pedestals shall be processed and designed such that the nozzle 
shall not exceed a height of 2.3 m from the ground. The doser assembly shall be 
mounted on the cross boom such that access for maintenance and repair can be 
accomplished via a portable platform no taller than 500 mm. Pedestals requiring 
heights in excess of this direction will require a deviation signed by VOME and 
Safety. 
7.9.8.2 
The pedestal shall be of a modular, four-piece, 10 cm square tube design that 
includes a base (fixed), boom (adjustable), gusset (fixed t-section), and shaft 
(adjustable). See Fig. 4 below for common examples. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
                         Figure 4. Pedestal Examples 
7.9.9 
FILTERS 
7.9.9.1 
Should the Application System contain a nozzle with an inner diameter smaller 
than or equal to 1.0mm, a filter system with a suitable mesh size (e.g. 0.5mm) 
shall be inserted between Pump Crossover Valve and Doser. It should be placed 
into a position which allows maintenance in a safe and ergonomic way. 
7.9.9.2 
Remaining applications on a program basis must be confirmed with VOME. 
 

 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 7 – ROBOT AND MANUAL APPLIED SEALERS, 
ADHESIVES, MASTICS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
 
Page 20 of 37 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
Printed copies are uncontrolled 
7.10 
TOOL FUNCTIONS AND ACCESSORIES 
7.10.1 
The applicator/doser center of gravity shall be as close as possible to the robot 
mechanical interface/face plate. 
7.10.2 
The applicator or doser shall be mounted on an adapter for different applications. 
Adapter is a modular piece (on the applicator/doser) that allows different lengths 
and angles on the applicator/doser. The length and angle specification will be 
decided at purchase. 
7.10.3 
Joints between applicator/doser, adapters and robot/fixed stand shall be provided 
with alignment devices (e.g., locating pins). 
7.10.4 
It shall only be possible to mount the nozzle in one specific, locked position on 
the applicator/doser. 
7.10.5 
The TCP calibration and check can be a manual solution. To be approved by 
Vehicle Operations; otherwise automated means must be provided when 
possible. 
7.10.6 
Extruded applications shall require the use of an automated bent/broken nozzle 
detection utilizing a sensor/switch or other suitable and effective means of 
ensuring presence of the functional nozzle. This check must be performed 
between cycles.  
7.10.7 
The distance between the applicator and doser should be minimized to increase 
response performance. It is ideal to directly mount the applicator to the doser 
without a hose. If a hose is required it should be no longer than 1.5 meter with 
heat if required. 
               
 
            Figure 7.10.6 Example of Extruded Nozzle Monitoring Sensor Setup

7.11 
SAFETY FUNCTIONS 
7.11.1 
The function package must comply with ANSI RIA 15.06 / NFPA 79, with the 
following deviations and amendments. 
7.11.1.1 
There shall be separate main switches for the robot and the application 
equipment in the function package. 
7.11.1.2 
Emergency and safety stop strategy: The robot shall stop in its path in a 
controlled way (without leaving the path) and the applicator/doser shall switch off. 
Restart shall be done in such a way that it gives no gap in the applied bead and 
with a maximum overlap of 5 mm (e-stop recovery program supplied within robot, 
but timer parameter adjusted by integrator). 

 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 7 – ROBOT AND MANUAL APPLIED SEALERS, 
ADHESIVES, MASTICS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
 
Page 22 of 37 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
Printed copies are uncontrolled 
7.12 
SERVICE AND MAINTENANCE 
7.12.1 
The sealer/adhesive robot dress package must be designed in such a way that it 
will minimize wear of all components within the package. The final dress-out 
design must be validated by the Tier 2 dispensing equipment supplier.  
7.12.2 
The function package shall be modularized to enable quick change of 
components and modules within the package. 
7.12.3 
Every separate heating circuit shall be supervised so maintenance can easily 
trace the source of a ground. 
7.12.4 
The interface between applicator/doser and adapter shall be of quick 
disconnecting design. It shall take a maximum of 15 minutes to change an 
applicator/doser, to be demonstrated at the integrator. 
7.12.5 
The cabling from the robot to the applicator/doser shall be designed so the 
maximum replacement time is 15 minutes, including air and signals. This shall be 
demonstrated at the integrator. 
7.12.6 
It shall take a maximum 30 minutes to change a material hose and shall be 
demonstrated at the line builder. 
7.12.7 
A tray shall be included with each system and be fitted with disposable liners to 
contain purged materials used in set up and purging.

Page 23 of 37 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
Printed copies are uncontrolled 
7.13 
ERROR HANDLING 
7.13.1 
The following errors shall result in the completion of the current sealing cycle, the 
system coming to a stop, and a fault being issued: 
• too much or too little material (max/min volume of dispensed bead) 
• alarm from the temperature monitor 
• over/under pressure 
• off 2k ratio 
• empty barrel 
• pump runaway 
• doser error (motion stop) 
7.13.2 
Excessive stroking (pump runaway) of the material pump must be prevented by 
including runaway protection in the function package controls. If pump runaway is 
detected, the cycle completes, and a fault is issued. 

 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005

7.14 
SHIPPING  
7.14.1 
Follow the procedure below to prepare equipment for shipping to the 
manufacturing facility: 
• Ensure the system has been depressurized. 
• Seal off any exposed hoses and/or piping containing material with pipe 
 
plugs and/or caps. 
• Tier 2 equipment supplier will verify that all fluids (grease and oils) are at   
       the correct level, all hoses and cables are correctly installed, and the    
       pumps and dispensing unit are correctly functioning at final install at the     
       plant.

7.15 
PERFORMANCE 
7.15.1 
Heat up time of the hoses, pipes and doser/applicator for material supply from 
room temperature (25°C) up to application temperature should not exceed 30 
seconds/degree for all heating zones. 
7.15.2 
Filling rate of the doser shall not be less than 7cm3/s. 
7.15.3 
The material flow shall be supervised with maximum and minimum flow levels. A 
deviation of +5%/-10% shall give an alarm. 
7.15.4 
Flow performance shall be: 
• Flow of 10 cm3/sec for a doser up to 100 cm3 
• Flow of 15 cm3/sec for a doser larger than 100 cm3 
7.15.5 
System performance shall be: 
• Volume per VPL and Total Volume, compared to Target Values 
maximum ±5% 
• Actual Flow, compared to Target Value maximum ±8% 
• Mix Ratio, compared to Target Value maximum ±10% during entire shot as 
measured by Ferro-Magnetic Tagging Unit. 

 
Issue Date: JAN. 13, 2005 
SECTION 7 – ROBOT AND MANUAL APPLIED SEALERS, 
ADHESIVES, MASTICS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
 
Page 26 of 37 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
Printed copies are uncontrolled 
7.16 
VERIFICATION TEST 
NOTE: Reference sealer adhesive methods (Section 7.18) and 
Application Test (Section 7.19). 
7.16.1 
EACH DISTINCT DRESSED-OUT ROBOT SHALL RUN FOR A MINIMUM OF 
40 HOURS FOR TESTING WITHOUT REQUIRING DRESS ADJUSTMENT. 
THE RUNNING SHALL BE CONTINUOUS AND BE AS CLOSE TO THE 
ACTUAL ROBOT PATH AND SPEED AS POSSIBLE. NEW DRESSING 
VARIATIONS SHALL BE THOROUGHLY TESTED AND REVIEWED PRIOR TO 
APPROVAL. IF DRESS ADJUSTMENTS ARE NECESSARY DUE TO HOSE 
BREAKAGE, EXCESSIVE WEAR, ETC., THE FIX SHALL BE INSTITUTED AND 
THE 40-HOUR TRIAL RE-STARTED AT THE BEGINNING. 
NOTE: If there is any wear on the dress out package beyond the 
40-hour test, it is the responsibility of the supplier to correct the 
problem free of charge. 
7.16.2 
For 2K Dispense Systems, the mix ratio of the system may not vary more than 
±10% throughout the proposed application. The dispense system supplier is 
responsible for assuring that the mix ratio is within tolerance. 
7.16.3 
For each new equipment design or unique equipment/adhesive/sealer 
combination, a 1000-hour runoff test for durability shall be performed. This test 
will involve re-circulating material through the system continuously at the rate and 
shot size deemed reasonable for the application being bid. The proper hose 
lengths, conditioning equipment, material unloading equipment, etc. shall be 
used. This test is in addition to Section Error! Reference source not found. 
above and does not involve a robot. It is to be run continuously for 1000 hours. 
The equipment will be dismantled and inspected after the test. Any wear is to be 
noted and excessive wear is to be remedied at the expense of the supplier. Any 
failure, leak, etc. during the 1000 hour run requiring the system to be shut down 
will automatically results in the test being deemed a failure, the problem getting 
resolved, and the test being re-started at time = 0. All equipment must run for 
1000 hours continuously to be approved.

DOCUMENTATION

Items to be Supplied with Quotation 
• Reliability and maintainability documentation to be provided with quotation. 
• Recommended spare parts, per units in service, to be provided with 
quotation. 
• MTBF and MTTR data required with quotation. 
7.17.2 
The following information must be posted at each pumpable sealer/adhesive 
application prior to the TT build: 
• Set Point Parameter Sheet 
• Visual Build Sheet 
• Application Control Plan Sheet 
• Fault Recovery Sheet

7.19 
APPLICATION TESTS 
7.19.1 
Sealer Application Test 1 
7.19.2 
Test 1 shall show the ability of the robot and dispensing system to cope with a 
wide variation in dispense speed and Volume per Length (VPL) that might be 
required to meet product specifications. The test shall be performed as follows: 
• 14 different VPLs with the length of 600 mm shall be applied on a metallic 
substrate. (See Figure 7.19.a for a sample of the test.) 
• All programmed positions must be clearly marked on the substrate 
(programmed start of line, programmed gaps, and programmed end of line). 
• All VPLs shall start from the left side. 
• If it is possible to perform the test at higher speeds, the supplier shall inform 
this to Vehicle Operations, and can increase the speed for all paths. 
• If it is not possible to run the test at the prescribed speed, run the test at a 
slower speed and inform Vehicle Operations of this. 
• The material, flow rate, and nozzle orifice selected for this test are listed in 
Table 7.19.1. 
• The Volume per Length can then be calculated for each of the values 
specified in the test as well as bead height and diameter. 
• The vendor should use the parameters stated in Table 7.19.1. 
• Line 14 is used to show system performance with equipment emergency stop 
and restart. It is expected that upon restart, the sealing robot will resume and 
complete the path without gaps in coverage.

Figure 7.19.a. Test 1 Sample 
7.19.3 
The vendor shall provide Vehicle Operations with a high-resolution digital 
photograph documenting dispense results. All programmed positions must be 
clearly marked as defined below: 
• The VPL start and end position relative to the programmed position should be 
measured for all 14 lines. 
• Start and end bead height and width relative to steady state bead will be 
assessed. 
• Programmed gaps in VPL #11 and stitch performance in VPL #12 and #13 
will be assessed. Measure to the closest mm. 
• Gap or bead overlap in VPL #14 after Emergency Shutdown should be 
measured. 
• The vendor shall also provide a copy of the robot program and sealer control 
program, with all Seal Schedules used to generate the test pattern, in order to 
assess whether the test pattern was generated with standard programming 
tools or required program enhancement (custom tweaking) for each unique 
VPL.

7.19.4 
Sealer Application Test 2 
7.19.4.1 
Test 2 will be used to assess the repeatability and accuracy of the robot and 
dispensing system. 
• The pattern is to be dispensed on the substrate as in Test 1 (using the same 
robot and sealer program). 
• The following test pattern beads are to be measured by height and width: 
o #1 @ 150 mm and 450 mm 
o #2 @ 150 mm and 450 mm 
o #3,4,5 as a single unit @ 150 mm and 450 mm 
o V#9 @ 150 mm 
o #10 @ 150 mm 
• Reporting and Analysis (deliverables): 
o The test pattern is to be run five times each. 
o Measurement data is to be collected on one test in an Excel spreadsheet 
for a repeatability assessment. (Table 7.19.2 below is a sample of what 
the spreadsheet should look like click here to link to the complete Excel 
file.) 
 
 
 
 
 
                   Table 7-17.2. Reporting and Analysis Chart 
• The vendor shall provide Vehicle Operations the raw data as well as 
summary and comparison results. 
• The following test pattern VPLs are to be measured by the compressed bead 
method: 
o VPL #1 @ 150 mm and 450 mm 
o VPL #2 @ 150 mm and 450 mm 
o VPL #3,4,5 as a single unit @ 150 mm and 450 mm 
o VPL #9 @ 150 mm 
o VPL #10 @ 150 mm

• Compressed Bead Method: A glass slide approximately 75mm wide x 50mm 
wide by 1mm thick should be placed on top of the dispensed bead to 
compress. Place the glass slide in the center of the dispense path as 
indicated in Figure 7.19.b. Bond line spacing is controlled by sprinkling some 
0.25mm diameter glass spheres (spacers) prior to the compression. (For lines 
3, 8b, 9b and 10a, use 1 mm glass beads). 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
• Measure the compressed width of the bead in center of the glass slide to the 
nearest mm. The VPL can be calculated by multiplying the width by the bond 
line thickness (0.25 mm). VPL (sq. mm) = W (mm) x 0.25 mm

• Reporting and Analysis (deliverables): 
o The test pattern is to be run five times each. 
o Measurement data is to be collected in an Excel spreadsheet for a 
repeatability assessment.  
7.19.5 
Sealer Application Test 3 
7.19.5.1 
No change in test as specified, except make side walls of the test substrate <90º. 
• Reporting and Analysis (deliverables): 
o The vendor shall provide Vehicle Operations with a high-resolution digital 
photograph of the test substrate. 
o The vendor shall also provide a copy of the robot program and sealer 
control program, with all Seal Schedules used to generate the test pattern, 
in order to assess whether the test pattern was generated with standard 
programming tools or required program enhancement (custom tweaking) 
for each unique VPL. 
7.19.6 
The preferable reporting method is in electronic form (any combination of .pdf, 
.jpg, PowerPoint, Word, or Excel). 
7.19.7 
The report shall also document test equipment and conditions: robot model, 
dispense equipment model and type, material used, nozzle orifice diameter, 
mean bead diameter along with recommended minimum and maximum for this 
set-up, temperature during test run, and whether or not heater was used for 
material. Any variations in test method should be reported. Copies of the robot 
and sealer program with seal schedules for test are expected to be included in 
the report package. 
7.19.8 
Evaluation. The height and width of the VPL shall be even and no lumps of 
adhesive / sealant or thin parts should occur. The programmed position (d) and 
the actual position of the VPL will also be evaluated. 
7.19.9 
Application tests may be modified as required by agreement with VOME in order 
to accommodate special or unusual conditions, such as limitations of robots or 
the introduction of new dispensing methods such as e-swirl.

7.20 
TRAINING 
NOTE: The suppliers shall quote training as an option. 
7.20.1 
The supplier shall provide formal in-plant and/or off-site training for all three 
shifts, as required. Include the following information for both in-plant and off-site 
Training: 
• Course Duration: ___________ Example (5 days, 8 hours / day) 
• Class Size: ___________ Students / Class 
• Cost per Student (include Books / Manuals): $ ___________ 
• Equipment Required for In-Plant Training: ___________ 
7.20.2 
The supplier shall provide informal, on-the-job training, as required. 
7.20.3 
Formal training shall include: 
• dispensing and supply pump system 
• pump/meter teardown and re-build, operation, electrical and mechanical 
troubleshooting and PM procedures. 
• robot programming, with respect to sealer and adhesive operation and 
interfacing (only). 
• providing Ford VIS and the manufacturing plant with a detailed lesson plan 
and/or course outline, prior to the start of training.

7.21 
CONTROL PLAN 
7.21.1 
The Adhesive and Sealer Control Plan Strategy defines Ford Motor Company’s 
strategy for the set-up and inspection methodologies necessary to assure sealing 
quality. 
7.21.2 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall work with VOME, plant and sealing system supplier to 
determine the control plan content that is necessary to be integrated into the 
assembly system by the Tier 1 Integrator. This content shall be identified, and 
costs developed to support <PA>. This will be primarily, but not limited to, 
satisfying the requirements for "Common Checks on a Regular Basis". Examples 
of the various methods available are outlined in the Adhesive and Sealer Control 
Plan Strategy. 
7.21.3 
Applications 
of 
Significant 
Water 
Seals 
(SWS) 
and 
Control 
Bonds 
(CB/Control/ ), plus any additional applications directed by the vehicle program, 
shall be provided with a vision system capable of detecting conditions discrepant 
to the applicable product specification.  See GVOSS Section 10.  
Dispensing tooling with vision systems must have the same vision functions 
programmed and activated for all beads on the application, and not just the 
beads driving the vision system requirement.  For example, an application with 
one Significant Water Seal (SWS) and two Common Water Seals (CWS) must 
have a vision system that functions for all three beads. 
The Tier 1 Integrator is responsible for reviewing CAD, Visual Build Sheets, and 
the vehicle program Statement(s) of Work for sealer and adhesive classifications 
and directions as described above, to ensure lines are processed correctly. 
7.21.4 
Roles and Responsibilities for the implementation and ongoing execution of the 
Sealer Control Plan are identified in the Plant Control Plan. 
• 1K Adhesive and Sealer Control Plan Template

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
8.1 
QUALIFICATIONS 
8.1.1 
This section is for new tooling only.  No kits, reuse of robots, weld controls, 
sealers and riveters or like equipment. 
8.1.1.1 
Robot Bypass Capability and Functionality 
8.1.1.1.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall develop and provide a list of the proposed robots with 
the enabled bypass functionality during the 50% Mechanical Design review. The 
proposed list of robots with the bypass functionality enabled must be reviewed 
and approved by the program ME or Launch Body Leader.  The Tier 1 
Integrators then implement and enable the bypass functions for each robot on 
the approved list. The bypass functions on the approved list will be turned off (by 
turning off the visibility on the PanelView HMI file) prior to the tool buy-off, unless 
directed otherwise by Launch Body Leader.  
8.1.1.1.2 
Prior to bypassing any robot, an approved repair process, which includes welds, 
rivets, clinch, and flow drill must be followed. Plant management shall be 
responsible to ensure that the bypassed robot process can and will be picked up 
plus set up the control plans for repair joints within the appropriate system. 
8.1.1.1.3 
For each robot and every robot, the bypass capability and functions shall be 
included and tested for each robot’s individual application (e.g. MH with Seal 
APP + Rivet APP) as well as each robot itself (bypassing the entire robot and its 
application(s)). 
8.1.1.1.4 
Robots that perform geometry or closed out joining (includes dispensed material) 
plus robots not listed in the 8.1.1.1.1 bypass list shall not be bypassed after 
function testing in 8.1.1.1.3 with their robot bypass PB visibility turned off before 
tool buyoff. 
8.1.1.1.5 
Carried applications can be tested with or without parts. Ped applications shall be 
tested with parts. 
8.1.2 
Any reuse of tooling such as conveyors, turntables, etc., will require removing 
existing controls components and installing all new to Control Logix standards. 
8.2 
PROCESS 
8.2.1 
Robots 
8.2.1.1 
Maximum of three external applications associated with a robot (ex: 1 ped weld 
and 2 ped dispense). 
8.2.1.2 
Maximum of two single timer weld controls per robot. 
8.2.1.3 
Material handling robots shall have valves for end effector tooling mounted on 
the EOAT. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 5, 2009 
SECTION 8 - CONTROL LOGIX 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 3 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
8.2.1.4 
NextGen Revision 1 and Revision 2 architecture shall have a maximum of 9 
valves and 48 inputs for material handling robot EOAT. NextGen Revision 3 
architecture shall have a maximum of 5 amps at 24 VDC inputs or outputs, 6 
amps maximum combined inputs and outputs for material handling EOAT. 
8.2.1.5 
Maximum of eight weld guns / EOAT for a tool changer application. 
8.2.1.6 
No robot pneumatic weld guns. 
8.2.1.7 
No dual tip dressers. 
8.2.1.8 
When using pneumatic equalizers on robot-controlled servo guns, if required, the 
control package including valve for the equalizer shall be an integral part of the 
weld gun. Valve manufacturer shall be program specific. 
8.2.1.9 
No "dutch" doors. 
8.2.1.10 
No back up weld robot stations. 
8.2.1.11 
No retractable hard stops. 
8.2.1.12 
Ring Switches (or an equivalent approved software solution) are required for 
Operator Shared Workspaces. 
8.2.1.13 
No electric tip dressers mounted on robot EOAT. 
8.2.1.14 
Ethernet/IP (EtherNet Industrial Protocol) 
8.2.1.14.1 
All external robot applications shall have two independent EtherNet/IP 
connections, one shall be EtherNet/IP the other shall be DATA. Both connectors 
shall be M12 EtherNet/IP micro connectors. The DATA port is on a different 
network (port must be isolated from the EtherNet/IP port) to provide archiving, 
programming support, and statistical data gathering and report software on a 
local programming workstation. 
8.2.1.14.2 
All external applications shall have a control reliable circuit to accept control 
reliable e-stop contacts from robot control. 
8.2.1.14.3 
EtherNet/IP shall be configured for auto negotiate and have the capability to 
deliver 100 mbs and full duplex. 
8.2.1.14.4 
Data port shall be an isolated port with externally mounted M12 connector (ref 
70ZF 20013210 Dwg for pin-out and mfg. specs).  
8.2.1.14.5 
External application supplier shall supply non-copy-protected software to CRW 
programming workstation supplier as part of the program. 
8.2.1.14.6 
Software shall be capable of providing online and off-line program edits of device 
on network, provide diagnostic tools, SPC trend analysis tools, archiving, and 
program upload and download capabilities. 
8.2.1.14.7 
Data port shall be 10 mbs, TCP/IP. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 5, 2009 
SECTION 8 - CONTROL LOGIX 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 4 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
8.2.1.14.8 
Any application requiring AIS box connection to Ford QLS system shall have a 
third isolated port and M12 externally mounted connector to support the AIS box 
connection. 
8.2.1.14.9 
All external robot applications (weld controls, sealer controls, rivet controls, etc.) 
shall be an adapter to the robot. 
8.2.1.14.10 All externally dressed applications shall require a break point at base of robots 
for all hoses and cabling (excluded are feeder blow tubes that must be 
contiguous). Tool integrator shall be responsible for working with the external 
application supplier to provide this. 
8.2.1.14.11 All external applications shall receive their own 480VAC, 60 Hz power supply.  
Applications that need hot power to maintain proper function when the robot 
control power is locked out; such as Sealer controller, shall receive their 
120VAC, 60 Hz separate from the robot.  All other exceptions shall require an 
approved deviation prior to design, testing and implementation. 
8.2.1.14.12 Any external robot application not meeting these specifications shall require an 
approved deviation prior to design and implementation. 
8.2.1.14.13 All wiring within any robot external application shall have wiring labeled with 
stick-on “Brady “labels (legible, non-removable labels), labels shall reflect 
schematic drawings. 
8.2.1.14.14 All servo-driven external robot applications shall require absolute encoders 
and/or battery backup to prevent loss of mastering data when power is locked 
out. 
8.2.1.14.15 Tool integrator shall provide ENET Data drops to all external robot applications 
and manual applications (spot weld, rivet, stud weld, clinch, flow drill screw, 
sealer, etc.) to CRW for Programming/Archiving purposes. 
8.2.1.15 
Ford Standard convention for servo gun closing is x=-(negative). I.e. convention 
is that closing the gun is always in the negative direction. x could be different 
based on application and robot mfg. 
8.2.1.16 
Interface Limitations. Any application that exceeds the capability of the Ford 
Standard Control Logix Robot bit map shall require an approved deviation prior to 
beginning design. 
8.2.1.16.1 
Maximum of two identical external applications per robot. 
8.2.1.16.2 
Maximum of two spot welding applications per robot. 
8.2.1.16.3 
Maximum of two sealing applications per robot. 
8.2.1.16.4 
Maximum of one stud welding application per robot. 
8.2.1.16.5 
Maximum of two nut-welding applications per robot. 
8.2.1.16.6 
Maximum of one MIG welder per robot.

8.2.1.16.7 
Maximum of one nut runner application per robot. 
8.2.1.16.8 
Maximum of eight tools on any tc application. 
8.2.1.16.9 
Maximum of two pierce rivet applications per robot. 
8.2.1.16.10 Maximum of two servo clinch applications per robot. 
8.2.1.16.11 Maximum of one Flow Screw application per robot. 
8.2.2 
Roll Bed Cells 
8.2.2.1 
Maximum of eight stations per safety zone scope. Multiple safety zone scopes 
may be contained in a single processor depending on processor selection and its 
capabilities. 
8.2.2.2 
Maximum of eight robots per station. 
8.2.2.3 
For each operator workstation the station proceeding shall utilize cylinder 
operated pallet stop.   
8.2.3 
Body Framer 
8.2.3.1 
Maximum of 6 tooling gates per side. 
8.2.3.2 
Maximum of 40 valves and 96 proximity switches per tooling gate. 
8.2.3.3 
Each side of the Framer station shall have an HMI panel inside the cell to provide 
clear line of sight to the gate tooling. The HMI panel location must not interfere 
with future gate change storage and transfer. 
This HMI must only be capable of actuating minor motions. It must not allow any 
major motions (ex. no drive motion). Major motions must only be actuated from 
HMIs outside of the cell with the perimeter safeties reset. 
8.2.4 
Hard Tool Welders 
8.2.4.1 
One DC Robot weld control per gun. 
8.2.4.2 
Pneumatic weld guns shall be utilized. 
8.2.4.3 
Maximum of SIX welding applications per weld station. 
8.2.5 
Projection Welders 
8.2.5.1 
Smart Electrodes shall not be utilized. 
8.2.5.2 
MTS feeders shall be utilized. 
8.2.5.3 
The air/oil weld gun shall be robot controlled. Feeder shall be GLX controlled. 
8.2.5.4 
Maximum of SIX welding applications per weld station.

8.2.7.1 
Maximum of 8 cylinders per valve.

8.2.7.1.2

8.2.7.1.3 
The valve island and M12 connector version shall utilize the same valve /

Example: Applications with 1 pneumatic valve (I.E. date stamp, air prep stand,

connectors connected to a IP6x block I/O shall be used, utilizing the same valve /

between valve island version.

robot’s EOAT.

8.3 
CONTROLS DESIGN 
8.3.1 
Hardware Application 
8.3.1.1 
Architecture shall be Ethernet I.P. 
8.3.1.1.1 
Exception for Rev. 1 and Rev. 2 designs only: DeviceNet shall be utilized for 
quick-change tooling. 
8.3.1.2 
Each Allen-Bradley L6 Series Processor shall have a maximum of: 
• 
16 carried SW robots or 12 MH robots. 
• 
4 PanelView Plus 1500. 
• 
4 PanelView Plus 600. 
Each Allen-Bradley L7 Series Processor shall have a maximum of:  
• 
32 carried SW robots or 24 MH robots.  
• 
8 PanelView Plus 1500.  
• 
8 PanelView Plus 700. 
• 
Each Allen-Bradley L8 Series Processor shall have: 
• 
For initial design / installation: 
• 
PLC processing memory, bandwidth, CIP connections and node counts shall 
be 80% capacity or less. 
• 
80ms max scan time. 
• 
Ethernet switches shall have a minimum of 2 spare ports. 
• 
For dedicated or reserved Ethernet ports see the latest Global Logic 
Programming Guide. 
• 
For Kit work: 
• 
PLC processing memory, bandwidth, CIP connections and node counts shall 
be 90% capacity or less. 
• 
90ms max scan time. 
• 
Ethernet switches shall have a minimum of 1 spare ports. 
• 
For dedicated or reserved Ethernet ports see the project Global Logic 
Programming Guide. 
8.3.1.2.1 
For any new system, all processors are required to be L8 series. L8 safety 
partner not to be used where system risk assessment determines PLd / SIL 2 / 
Cat 2 (or Cat 3). The only time a safety partner is required is if the system risk 
assessment deems the need for PLe / SIL 3 / Cat 4. 
8.3.1.2.2 
Where possible, separate adjacent safety zones will be combined into one L8 
processor to take advantage of processor capabilities and reduce the overall 
number of processors required. Integrator shall meet with their VO Controls 
Engineer to review system layouts prior to ordering processors. 
8.3.1.3 
Removed.

Page 8 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
8.3.1.4 
Ford standard 70ZF panel designs shall be utilized.  
8.3.1.4.1 
All standard 70ZF panels are stored in Team Center. 
8.3.1.4.2 
Suppliers are required to have Team Center access for retrieving controls 
standards. 
8.3.1.4.3 
All control panels on Next Gen Controls applications will be Ford to Furnish 
(FTF). 
8.3.1.4.4 
All vendors are to ensure their timing charts properly account for the 12-week 
lead time for all control panels. 
8.3.1.4.5 
Any unique designs shall be the responsibility of the installer/integrator to create. 
All designs will then be approved by VO controls and documented with a 70ZF 
number. 
8.3.1.5 
One controls design per line or sub assembly cell. 
8.3.1.6 
Controls design will consist of all interconnects of standard panels, product 
specific tooling controls, power distribution, bill of materials, interlocks to adjacent 
tools and device tags. 
8.3.1.7 
Ford standard panel designs will stand alone and shall not be merged with the 
application design AutoCAD files. 
8.3.1.8 
Supplier shall use Ford supplied Light Screen Worksheet to determine safe 
distance. 
8.3.1.9 
Supplier will use Ford supplied Ethernet calculator to determine processor and 
Ethernet network capacity. 
8.3.1.9.1 
Tooling shall be compliant with the limitations of the Ethernet calculator at time of 
delivery. 
8.3.1.9.2 
Connection total shall be 80% or less. 
8.3.1.9.3 
Bandwidth shall be 80% capacity or less. 
8.3.1.9.4 
Removed. 
8.3.1.9.5 
Removed. 
8.3.1.10 
The vendors are responsible to update all hardwire designs for revisions and/or 
fixes as part of their base quote. 
8.3.1.11 
IBSS panels are to be included in the design packages and will be part of the 
FTF order. 
8.3.1.12 
Tool integrators shall provide a water flow switch wired to PLC to indicate that 
any chiller system for any welding or laser application is flowing properly. A flow

switch is required for each water circuit, if chiller has multiple circuits each shall

8.3.1.13 
Any robot loading or unloading a press/hemmer/roll hemmer shall have a robot 
cube type (global) clear zone interlock to the press/hemmer for motion clear 
when the robot is clear of the press/hemmer tower. See also Global VOSS 
Section 5 Robots. 
8.3.1.14 
Drive Usage and Sizing Requirements. 
8.3.1.14.1 
Tier 1 Integrators shall use components listed on the approved components list 
identified in the Program SOW. Exceptions shall require a deviation prior to 
design, testing and implementation. 
8.3.1.14.2 
Tier 1 Integrators shall consult with the Drive and equipment suppliers for proper 
usage, design and sizing.  For Rockwell VFD application, reference the Ford VO 
- PowerFlex Drive Specification Rev 3.04. 
8.3.1.14.3 
Tier 1 Integrators shall also follow any additional local safety requirements. 
 
8.3.1.15 
Weld Power Distribution Design 
8.3.1.15.1 
For new body shop installs: The Ford standard Weld PDPs (Power Distribution 
Panels) (as specified in the Electrical Approved Component list) shall be used to 
provide weld power to (4) or more weld timers for new lines/sub-assemblies.  For 
(3) weld timers or less, the current standard Weld Distribution Box (70ZF-
20013174) shall be used until smaller Weld PDPs become available. When using 
the 70ZF- 20013174 panel, the maximum cable length from the bottom of the 
weld PDP to the top of the weld timer must not exceed 25ft and be within line of 
sight per NEC 240.21.(B) 
            
                     Integrators shall use the Ford-provided Weld PDP excel calculator when sizing                      
 
 
The Weld PDP. 
• The Weld PDP calculated current (A) plus the welding type % shall be less 
than or equal to 80% of the plant drop; to provide the 20% spare.   
• Use of 600A Weld PDPs shall require approval from the VO controls 
Design/Follow-up engineer.  Review the calculation with the VO controls 
engineer.  Integrators are responsible for the accuracy and function of the 
power calculation for the Weld PDP. 
 
8.3.1.15.2 
For kitted tooling that did not already utilize the standard Weld PDPs:  consult the 
program controls lead for direction. 
8.3.2 
Software Application – Logic and PanelView Plus 
8.3.2.1 
All designs shall utilize the Ford Next Gen tag naming convention. Please 
reference the Guardlogix Programming Guide. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 5, 2009 
SECTION 8 - CONTROL LOGIX 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 10 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
8.3.2.2 
All designs shall utilize the Ford Next Gen I.P. address requirements. Please 
reference the Guardlogix Programming Guide. 
8.3.2.3 
The only alias tags that are allowed are those for processor generated point I/O 
tags used in the input mapping routines. 
8.3.2.4 
Use of a periodic task shall be limited to safety tasks.  Any change requires an 
approved deviation. 
8.3.2.5 
All software and components shall comply with the Ford issued revision level 
matrix for the given plant/program. 
8.3.2.6 
Ford standard UDT's (User Defined Tags) and logic routines shall be used to 
compile the Guardlogix processor program.  Please reference the Guardlogix 
Programming Guide. 
8.3.2.7 
Only the routines designated with "_edit" will be allowed to be modified. Any 
issues with a file which is not identified with "_edit" are to be brought to the Ford 
BCE controls dept attention for resolution.  Please reference the Guardlogix 
Programming Guide. 
8.3.2.8 
Ford supplied logic template shall be copied into the application processor file. 
The non safety file construction will be done by copying routines and tags of the 
desired entities and deleting unused programs. These files can then be 
connected to real world inputs/outputs in the "input mapping edit" and "output 
mapping edit" routines. The sequence and associated machine interference 
zones will be accommodated by completing the "motion edit" routine for that 
entity. Please reference the Guardlogix Programming Guide. 
8.3.2.9 
The entire Ford supplied safety task is to be copied into the application 
processor. Delete tags and routines that are not required for the application. The 
safety routines indicated with "_edit" will then need to be modified for the 
available options used for application. Please reference the Guardlogix 
Programming Guide. 
8.3.2.10 
The entire standard PanelView plus routines and tags shall be copied into the 
application processor file. Then delete screen specific routines which do not 
apply to the application. Modify the "_edit" routines to attach to the machine 
programming and turn on or off visibility of entities on the screens as applicable 
for the process. Screen specific tags are part of the PanelView plus UDT are not 
to be deleted or modified, they are just not used.  Please reference the 
Guardlogix Programming Guide. 
8.3.2.11 
The Ford standard FIS and PCB tags and routines shall be copied into the 
application file for each asset. The associated "_edit" file can then be modified for 
the appropriate triggers. Please reference the Guardlogix Programming Guide. 
8.3.2.12 
The software I/O tree structure shall be the same as was qualified with the 
Ethernet calculation tool software. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 5, 2009 
SECTION 8 - CONTROL LOGIX 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 11 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
8.3.2.13 
For the PanelView, Ford standard screen shall be loaded into the application 
PanelView file.  Only the screens appropriate for that application are to be used.  
8.3.2.14 
PanelView local messages are to be completed for both screen indicators and 
banner fault messages appropriate to the application.  
8.3.2.15 
Specialty designs of which there is not a Ford standard routine(s) developed 
(Hemmer, etc.) will require the supplier to develop under Ford BCE controls dept 
direction. 
8.3.2.16 
Supplier shall utilize Ford Standard Abbreviation list for all messages, descriptors 
and tags. 
8.3.2.17 
Standard and Safety Task Settings 
8.3.2.17.1 
Safety task scan time shall be equal to or less than 20 milliseconds. 
8.3.2.17.2 
Safety task period shall be equal to 50 milliseconds. 
8.3.2.17.3 
Safety task priority shall be set to 1. 
8.3.2.17.4 
Safety task watchdog shall be set to 22 milliseconds. 
8.3.2.17.5 
Standard task scan time shall be equal to or less than 70 milliseconds. 
8.3.2.17.6 
Standard task watchdog shall be equal to 500 milliseconds. 
8.3.2.17.7 
System overhead time slice shall be set to 20%. 
8.3.2.17.8 
The system integrator is responsible for updating all components’ firmware, as 
required, to comply with Model Program Revision Level, including MoviFIT 
programs. 
8.3.2.18 
The vendors are responsible to update all software designs for revisions and/or 
fixes as part of their base quote. 
8.3.2.19 
Control Software Task Update Process on a Program Basis: 
8.3.2.19.1 
For each launch program, the lead controls engineer will communicate to the 
vendors the initial controls template revision level to be downloaded from 
Teamcenter. Timing: no later than start of controls software design per the 
integrator timeline. 
 
8.3.2.19.2 
The lead controls engineer will communicate via directional letter, the task level 
updates that are required prior to buy-off. Timing: no later than power on dates 
per the integrator timeline. 
8.3.2.19.3 
Any task level update identified after power on will be held and implemented after 
the tool ships.   
Exceptions:

• Updates required that affect the safe operation of the tool must be 
completed before buy-off 
• Updates required to facilitate an effective buy-off (including 2500 cycles) 
8.3.2.20 
Batch build systems are required to have rolling changeover. 
 
 
8.3.3 
Controls Build – General 
All components shall comply with the approved components list identified in the 
Program SOW. 
8.3.3.1 
No more than six (6) midline Ethernet bulkhead type connections from switch to 
switch or switch to device. 
8.3.3.2 
Ethernet connectors shall be D-coded M12 exterior to control enclosure and 
RJ45 within all control enclosures. 
8.3.3.3 
Only one Ethernet cable shall be used to connect two devices.  Extension cables 
or feed-thru bulkheads are not authorized for use. 
8.3.3.4 
The maximum stranded cable length is limited to 85 meters, including patch 
cords. 
8.3.3.5 
Patch cords (internal cord sets) shall not exceed 5 meters. 
8.3.3.6 
All cable extensions shall be reviewed and approved by the lead controls 
engineer. If connections are in the cable tray, then the cover shall be marked 
such that the plant is aware a connection is present.  
8.3.3.7 
Cable tray (floor, fence, overhead, etc.) shall be sized to comply with all project 
site local laws, specifications, electrical codes and fill requirements with at least 
20% reserved fill space per channel. Communication cables (Ethernet, 
DeviceNet, etc.), control cables, ground / bond cables and high voltage cables 
shall be separated with dividers. See drawing 70ZF-20013207 Grounding, 
Bonding and Cable Tray Installation Examples for GVOSS examples. See also 
GVOSS Section 1 General Requirements and Section 2 Design for additional 
requirements. 
8.3.3.7.1 
Hook and loop tie wraps shall be used for ALL electrical cables including 
Ethernet cables (see example below) unless local code prohibits hook and loop 
tie wraps. Buckle style shall be prohibited. See the individual program 
"GuardLogix Approved Components List" for additional information on approved 
manufacturers and models. 
A. For Canada, hook and loop tie wraps shall only be used on Ethernet and 
Communication cables. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 5, 2009 
SECTION 8 - CONTROL LOGIX 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 13 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
B. The exceptions to this requirement are FTF Panels, Tier 2 standard products, 
standard panels and the Robot RTU unit and systems that require specific cable anchor 
point securing methods by the manufacturer. i.e. (Cat Track entrance and exit points, 
Service Loops on VDLs, Indexing Slides, Dumps etc.).

8.3.3.12 
Grounding and Bonding 
8.3.3.12.1 
Grounding and bonding of electrical systems shall be applied as shown on 70ZF-
20013207. System grounding and bonding shall be documented as part of the 
Controls Application Package. Good Ground Points need to be identified and 
approved by Ford Land before used. Grounding and bonding of electrical 
systems shall meet any regional and local requirements.   
8.3.3.12.1.1 The complete grounding system shall not exceed 5 ohms to ground. 
8.3.3.12.2 
All materials, surface preparation and labor shall be supplied by the equipment    
Installer.   
8.3.3.12.3 
Grounding and bonding of electrical systems shall be installed utilizing one of the 
two approved methods. 
• Hilti XBT/SBT  
• Exothermic Welding (CAD)

8.3.3.17 
Control Logix BCE Wire Color Codes 
8.3.3.17.1 
Power Circuits: 
8.3.3.17.1.1 Black – 3-Phase AC. 
8.3.3.17.1.2 Black with Orange Stripe – 3-Phase AC which remains energized with enclosure 
disconnect off. 
8.3.3.17.2 
Control Circuits: 
8.3.3.17.2.1 Red – Single phase AC line side fed from Control Power Disconnect. 
8.3.3.17.2.2 White – Single phase AC neutral fed from Control Power Disconnect. 
8.3.3.17.2.3 Orange – Single phase AC line side which remains energized with Control Power 
Disconnect off. 
8.3.3.17.2.4 White with Orange stripe – Single phase AC Grounded which remains energized 
with Control Power Disconnect off. 
8.3.3.17.2.5 Blue - +24 VDC ungrounded fed from Control Power Disconnect. (Not Light 
Blue). 
8.3.3.17.2.6 Blue with white stripe – 0 VDC ungrounded. 
8.3.3.17.2.7 Blue with Orange stripe - +24 VDC ungrounded which remains energized with 
Enclosure Disconnect off. 
8.3.3.17.2.8 White with Blue stripe – 0 VDC grounded. 
8.3.3.17.2.9 Green with Yellow stripe – Ground / Bond (PE). 
8.3.3.18 
Vendors shall provide the stand for the Control PDP panels. 
8.3.3.19 
AVS logic and HMI screens shall reside in tooling PLCs & HMIs. Integrator to 
coordinate with the AVS Controls Engineer to determine processor memory 
requirements and take this into consideration with processor sizing. 
8.3.3.20 
Vendors are to supply generic arc flash labels consistent with the requirements 
spelled out in the National Electrical Code. This shall apply to all panels which 
contain 120 volts and above.

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
8.3.3.21 
Supplier shall provide a control power distribution panel custom design where the 
Ford standard does not exactly support the application. 
8.3.3.22 
All applications shall be engineered such that the voltage drop due to cable 
length does not compromise component operation requirements. 
8.3.3.23 
Conduit and Flexible Conduit Runs 
8.3.3.23.1 
Where flexible or rigid conduit enters a box, it shall be attached with double 
locknuts (one inside, one outside) with an insulating bushing at conduit 
termination. If the conduit fittings already have locking nuts built-in with sealing 
gasket, double locknuts are not required.  In all cases, installation of these 
conduit fittings shall not reduce the IP rating of the panel. 
8.3.3.23.2 
Running threads shall not be acceptable for any conduit run. 
8.3.3.23.3 
Nonmetallic flexible conduit shall not be allowed; any flexible conduit runs shall 
meet all applicable local codes and shall be metallic. 
8.3.3.23.4 
All service entrance conduit runs shall require a locknut and sealing ring on the 
outside and an NRTL listed grounding locknut and insulated bushing on the 
inside. 
 
8.3.3.23.5 
All exiting conduit runs shall require a locknut and sealing ring on the outside of 
the panel and a locknut and insulated bushing on the inside. 
8.3.3.24 
IP addressing of components on the I/O Network 
8.3.3.24.1 
For any components that require an IP address, the required address shall be set 
by using its dip switches or rotary dials.  
8.3.3.24.2 
Software can be used for setting an IP address when there are no physical 
means available. 
8.3.4 
Pallet Magazines / Conveyors Between Systems 
8.3.4.1 
Control of magazine/conveyor shall be independent of the two systems, stops 
shall be controlled by the zone control where they are located. 
8.3.4.2 
The magazine/conveyor shall continue to run if either system is shut down. 
8.3.5 
For inline Operator stations that are directly coupled with PRB/Skid 
transfer system such as those in Closure Hang and Operator 
Load/Inspection stations in mainlines, to improve throughput: 
8.3.5.1 
Operator light screens violation shall only stop and disable the Station PRB/Skid 
and each adjacent PRB/Skid if and only if the PRB/Skid is in in transfer and 
blocking the migration light screens between the station and any adjacent station. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 5, 2009 
SECTION 8 - CONTROL LOGIX 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 18 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
8.3.5.2 
Line/Station shall remain in automatic mode when Operator light screens are 
violated, unless otherwise specified in the risk assessment. 
8.3.5.3 
The stopped and disabled PRBs/Skids shall resume motions if the Operator 
lights screens are cleared and reset at the Station Operator run-bar (by pressing 
the Run or Reset button). 
8.3.5.4 
A safety risk assessment shall be required to identify associated hazards and 
countermeasures. 
8.3.5.5 
This risk assessment shall also document hazards and countermeasures for all 
crosswalks, if any required. 
8.3.6 
PIN CLAMP OPEN/CLOSE VERIFICATION 
8.3.6.1 
At each insertion station or insert/pull-off station, an RFID (if used on carriers), an 
HMI (or indicator lights if model complexity allows) and all necessary detection 
sensors shall be required to accurately detect and verify the exact model, option 
details and pin clamps in proper positions prior to enabling insertion process.  
Proofing method shall be rigid, repeatable and reliable without a need for regular 
adjustment or maintenance. 
8.3.6.2 
Chucking units shall not be provided in Pull off stations.

8.4 
CABLE REQUIREMENTS FOR GUARDLOGIX ARCHITECTURE 
8.4.1 
Approved Cable Manufacturers  
8.4.1.1 
Approved cable manufacturers are listed on the approved components list and 
referenced in the standard hardware drawings; any change shall follow the 
deviation process. 
8.4.2 
Control Cables 
8.4.2.1 
All receptacle wire insulation colors are to comply with wire colors shown in Ford 
standard drawings. Receptacle wires shall be tagged with pin number. 
8.4.2.2 
All cable assemblies shall be factory manufactured with molded connectors.  
8.4.2.3 
4-pin and 5-pin mini cords and receptacles shall be 16 awg, 600 V, UL rated for 
10.0 amps. Cord sets shall have connectors on both ends, yellow TPE outer 
jacket, TC-ER/FT4 rated.  
8.4.2.4 
4-pin and 5-pin mini tee connectors shall be yellow in color and UL rated for 10.0 
amps. 
8.4.2.5 
10-pin mini cords and receptacles shall be 16 awg, 600 V, UL rated for 8.0 amps. 
Cord sets shall have connectors on both ends, yellow TPE outer jacket, TC-
ER/FT4 rated. 
8.4.2.6 
12-pin mini cords and receptacles shall be 16 awg, 600 V, UL rated for 7.0 amps. 
Cord sets shall have connectors on both ends, yellow TPE outer jacket, TC-
ER/FT4 rated. 
8.4.2.7 
16-pin M23 cords and receptacles shall be 18 awg, 150 V, UL rated for 6.0 amps. 
Cord sets shall have connectors on both ends, yellow TPE outer jacket, 
ITC/PLTC/FT4 rated. 
8.4.2.8 
4-pin and 5-pin M12 Euro cords and receptacles shall be 18 awg, 250 V, UL 
rated for 4.0 amps. Cord sets shall have connectors on both ends, yellow TPE 
outer jacket, ITC/PLTC/FT4 rated. 
8.4.2.9 
Bulk cable for 120-vac power distribution. 4 conductor, 600V, yellow TPE outer 
jacket, no connectors, TC-ER/FT4 rated, individual conductor wire color to 
comply with Ford standard drawings. Wire gauge per load and distance 
requirements. 
8.4.2.10 
4-pin and 12-pin mini hi-flex cords shall be 16AWG, 600V. 
8.4.2.10.1 
4-pin rated for 10 amps, 12-pin rated for 7 amps, connectors on both ends, 
yellow TPE outer jacket, FT1 4 conductor 20 million flexes at 3 inches bend 
radius, 12 conductor 20 million flexes at 4.5 inches bend radius. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 5, 2009 
SECTION 8 - CONTROL LOGIX 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 20 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
8.4.2.11 
Control cable connections shall not be match marked similar to torque match 
marking.  If the integrator has an internal process that requires identifying if a 
cable has met an internal process control check, the method to identify this shall 
be a marking that shall not be confused with a torque match mark. (i.e. sticker, 
dot, tape) 
8.4.3 
DeviceNet Cables 
8.4.3.1 
DEVICENET SHALL ONLY BE USED FOR QUICK CONNECT TOOLING. 
Applies to Rev. 1 and Rev. 2 designs only. 
8.4.3.2 
DeviceNet cables shall be DeviceNet thin type, gray TPE outer jacket, 22 awg, 
300 V, manufacturer high flex rated for 10 million flexes, UL rated for 5.0 amps, 
ITC/PLTC/FT4 rated. DeviceNet cables shall have factory molded mini 
connectors on both ends and be ODVA compliant. 
8.4.3.3 
DeviceNet tee's and terminating resistors shall be gray in color. 
8.4.4 
Ethernet Cables 
8.4.4.1 
Each Ethernet assembly shall be factory assembled and tested to comply with 
TIA CAT5E requirements using a Fluke DSP-4000 analyzer. Test to include: 
Continuity, Propagation Delay, Delay Skew, Near End Crosstalk, Attenuation, 
Resistance, Return Loss, Power Sum Near End Crosstalk, Equal Level Far End 
Crosstalk, Power Sum Far End Crosstalk. 
8.4.4.2 
Ethernet cables exterior to enclosures: Minimum requirements shall be 24 AWG / 
0.25 mm² stranded, 300 Volts listed, 2 pair, twisted bonded pairs or equivalent, 
unshielded, TPE outer jacket, over-molded M12 D coded male connector on both 
ends, Rated Cat5e, CM listed.  
8.4.4.3 
Ethernet cables exterior to enclosures requiring high flex properties: Minimum 
requirements shall be 24 AWG / 0.25 mm² stranded, 300 Volts listed, 2 pair, 
twisted bonded pairs or equivalent, unshielded, TPE outer jacket, over-molded 
M12 D coded male connector on both ends, Rated Cat5e, CM listed.  
Manufacturer rated for 10 million cycles at 20 times the diameter bend radius.   
8.4.4.4 
Removed 
8.4.4.5 
Ethernet receptacle assemblies installed on the exterior of an enclosure: 
Minimum requirements shall be 24 AWG / 0.25 mm² stranded, 300 V, 2 pair, 
twisted bonded pairs or equivalent, unshielded, TPE outer jacket, M12 D coded 
female receptacle on one end and a RJ45 male connector on the other end, 
Rated Cat5e, CM listed.  
8.4.4.6 
Ethernet cables interior to enclosures: Minimum requirements shall be 24 AWG / 
0.25 mm² stranded, 300 V, 4-pair, twisted bonded pairs or equivalent, 
unshielded, TPE outer jacket, RJ45 male connector on both ends, Rated Cat5e, 
CM listed.   

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 5, 2009 
SECTION 8 - CONTROL LOGIX 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 21 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
8.4.4.7 
Ethernet cable outer jacket requirements: Blue shall be used for static 
applications for control & I/O networks. Orange shall be used for identifying High 
Flex rated cables for control & I/O networks. Orange cables may be used instead 
of blue. Teal shall only be used for data backup networks.  
8.4.5 
Power Cables 
8.4.5.1 
Welding Cable (weld control load side) to be NEC compliant, copper conductors, 
90 Deg C, FT4, TC-ER, UL approved, TPE/XLPE outer jacket, black numbered 
conductors with green ground static application.  
8.4.5.2 
Power Cable (Dist. panel to weld control, robot control, drive panel) to be NEC 
compliant, copper conductors, 90 Deg C, FT4, TC-ER, UL approved, 600V Min., 
TPE/CPE/TSE outer jacket, black numbered conductors with green ground static 
application. 
8.4.5.3 
Motor and Encoder Cable (VFD applications), NEC compliant, copper 
conductors, 90 Deg C, FT4, TC-ER, UL approved, 600V Min, Shielded, TPE 
outer jacket, black numbered conductors with green ground, both static and 
continuous flex applications. Motor only conductors and motor conductors with 
16awg signal pair composite. Encoder cable min 18awg. Bidder must provide 
data to show complete cross reference with the approved sources on the 
approved components list. 
8.4.5.4 
Motor Cable (motor starter applications), NEC compliant, copper conductors, 90 
Deg C, FT4, TC-ER, UL approved, 600V Min, TPE outer jacket, black numbered 
conductors with green ground, both static and continuous flex applications. 
8.4.5.5 
120V Distribution Cable shall be 90 Deg C, 600V, 5 conductor yellow TPE jacket, 
TC-ER, UL approved bulk cable with the following color code: red / white / yellow 
/ white with yellow / green (ground). 
8.4.5.6 
Items 1-4 outer jacket are to be black for static and yellow for flexing applications. 
8.4.5.7 
Supplier will work with OEMs to coordinate a proper forecast for the program to 
ensure timing and proper volume discount price is given. 
8.4.6 
Motor Control Cables 
8.4.6.1 
Beginning with the CD539/540, D471/472 program Ford Standard Next Gen Rev 
3 VFD and Motor Starter panels shall utilize 480vac motor connectors for SEW 
Eurodrive motor sizes 10HP and smaller. 
Reference Drawings: 
Cable Assembly: C-P30412-E revC 
Male Receptacle Assembly: C-P30545-H revB 
Female Receptacle Assembly: C-P30544-H revB

8.5 
ROBOT INTERNAL DRESS PACKAGE REQUIREMENTS 
8.5.1 
Minimum Dressing Requirements 
8.5.1.1 
Robot control to Axis 3 (i.e. includes necessary cables from robot control to Axis 
3 where applicable, unless otherwise noted - excludes hosing). 
8.5.1.2 
All internal wiring, to cover all options, shall be part of the base robot. 
8.5.1.3 
All ENET and DNET cables and connectors along with the 4-pin mini power 
cables and connectors shall be Ford to Specify and listed on the approved 
components list. 
8.5.1.4 
All 480 VAC connectors mounted on the robot shall be isolated from robot arm- 
Robot Mfg. shall be responsible. Intent is to keep any stray ground currents from 
robot arm and possible I/O connections on arm. True safety for the connector is 
the ground pin and connector connected to the power ground at the source. 
8.5.1.5 
Provider of dressing from axis 3 to axis 6 shall provide the EOAT fittings as a kit 
for the specific size hose /tubing being provided to adapt to ports being provided 
for a specific program. 
8.5.1.6 
All Water and Pneumatic ports at axis 1 and 3 shall be ISO 1179-1 (BSPP) port 
to accept ISO 228-1 (“G”) threads with seal face. 
8.5.1.7 
Provider of robot dress from axis 3 to axis 6 shall provide the EOAT fittings as a 
kit for the specific size hose /tubing being provided to adapt to ports being 
provided for a specific program. 
8.5.2 
Material Handling (MH), Protecting for Tool Changer (TC) 
8.5.2.1 
Tooling integrator to provide one ENET/DNET HARDENED GATEWAY per MH 
robot. Applies to Rev. 1 and Rev. 2 designs only. 
8.5.2.1.1 
Ford to Specify Manufacturer of ENET/DNET GATEWAY. 
8.5.2.1.2 
Ground wire for Ethernet/DeviceNet is no longer required. The Turck DeviceNet 
network requires a grounding terminator Turck #RSM 57-TR/CS12791 mounted 
at Gateway module. A standard terminating resistor 70ZF-20013210 TR-5-DN is 
to be used at other end of DeviceNet trunk line. 
8.5.2.2 
Optional - One 3/4-inch blue air line (used only for current applications). NOTE: 
This is in addition to the two air lines, with two G1/2-inch ports (ISO 1179-1), 
provided by robot manufacturer as part of base robot. 
8.5.2.3 
Cable between robot control and Axis 1 provided by robot manufacturer; cable 
can be a combination cable to minimize multiple cables between Axis 1 and Axis 
3. (Provides power from DCIO and DCA connectors.) 
8.5.2.4 
Tooling integrator shall supply and install the following from Axis 3 to Axis 6: 
8.5.2.4.1 
One ENET cable and associated connectors

8.5.2.4.2 
Two 4-pin mini power cables and associated connectors (connector DCIO, DCA), 
labeled and located on Axis 3. 
8.5.2.4.3 
One 5-pin mini DNET cable and associated connectors (cable and connector 
only required for MH EOAT); Cable is from ENET/DNET gateway and supplied 
by tooling integrator. 
8.5.2.5 
Tool changer, if required, shall be installed by tooling integrator.

Spot Welding (SW) Carried Gun and/or Pedestal Weld Gun (No Tool 
Changer) 
8.5.3.1 
Three 1/2-inch water lines shall be provided, from Axis 1 to Axis 3, by the robot 
manufacturer, with the following requirements: 
8.5.3.1.1 
Two black (water out) hoses with: 
8.5.3.1.1.1 
Axis 1 water junction block shall be one 3/4-inch NPT port common to two 1/2-
inch NPT ports. 
8.5.3.1.1.2 
Axis 3 water junction block shall be two G1/2-inch ports on one side and two 
G1/2-inch ports on the other side all common to each other (ISO 1179-1). 
8.5.3.1.2 
One green (water in) hose with: 
8.5.3.1.2.1 
Axis 1 water junction block shall be one G3/4-inch port common to a one G1/2- 
inch port on the other side. Axis 3 water junction block shall be one G1/2- inch 
NPT port common to one G1/2-inch port on the other side (ISO 1179-1). 
8.5.3.2 
Tooling integrator shall provide water hose and fittings from weld control stand to 
base of robot. Hose fittings shall be JIC 37-degree flare at hose /BSPP (ISO 
1179-1) at manifold. 
8.5.3.2.1 
Two G3/4-inch ports for water located at Axis 1 base (water in/water out) shall be 
provided with robot. BSPP (ISO 1179-1). 
8.5.3.2.2 
Hose manufacturer and color shall be as specified for the program (for external 
hose ONLY). 
8.5.3.3 
Optional – One 1/2-inch blue air line (used only for current applications, or where 
gun controlled by weld controller). 
8.5.3.4 
Transformer and servo gun motor thermal switch. 
8.5.3.4.1 
One 4-pin M12 micro connector shall be required for transformer and servo gun 
motor thermal switch. 
8.5.3.4.2 
Tooling integrator shall be responsible for cable from Axis 1 to robot control. 
8.5.3.4.3 
Wiring shall be as follows: 
8.5.3.4.3.1 
Pin 1 and 2 shall be for the Transformer Thermal Switch (ref. 70ZF–20013210).

8.5.4 
Spot Welding Carried Gun with Tool Changer (SWTC) 
8.5.4.1 
Same as NO Tool Changer (TC), with the addition of the following clarification of 
cabling requirements: 
8.5.4.1.1 
Robot supplier shall coordinate cable quantity with Ford specified Axis 3 to Axis 6 
dress supplier and provide cables to the dress out supplier prior to shipping date.  
This includes coordinating tool changer requirements and cables from Axis 3 to 
TC and from TC to weld gun. Thermal switches, secondary voltage wires, and 
any optional feedback connections for weld control shall "pass thru" the tool 
changer.  Tool changer manufacturer shall provide the appropriate connectors for 
these devices.

Robot Transfer Unit (RTU) (previously defined as 7th axis applications) 
8.5.6.1 
All items listed for non-RTU robot applications, where tooling integrator is 
responsible to provide the cable from Axis 1 to robot control and / or external 
application; the tooling integrator shall provide same cable from Axis 1 to entry of 
RTU cat track along with cable from entry of RTU cat track to robot control and / 
or external application.

required cable routing and lengths.

processor prior to Buy-Off and included in the Buy-Off Book.

Checklist and the Control Logix Build Checklist. See the Logic Comparison Tool 
Users Guide for more information. 
 
8.7 
INTELLIGENT DEVICES 
8.7.1 
Intelligent devices are those that contain a component with firmware or software.  
• 
Each FTF supplier is responsible for creating and maintaining an Intelligent 
Device Tracking Matrix. 
• 
Each Tier 1 Integrator is responsible for creating and maintaining an 
intelligent device tracking matrix for items not listed in the “Program Revision 
Master Matrix”.

8.9 
RADIO FREQUENCY IDENTIFICATION (RFID) TAGS 
8.9.1 
Palletized Systems using Power Roll Tables 
8.9.1.1 
The RFID Tag shall be placed on the LH side of the pallet forward of the pallet 
Center Line (along (X) axis). 
8.9.1.2 
The RFID Tag Reader shall be mounted on the Power Roll Bed corresponding 
with pallet RFID Tag placement, and shall have adjustment in all three (X, Y, Z) 
directions. 
8.9.2 
Conveyor Systems 
8.9.2.1 
Carriers (Any conveyor carrier including Small Parts, Facility P/F, Friction, EMS 
and similar conveyors using Part Hooks and Nest Tooling) shall have an RFID 
tag. 
8.9.2.2 
Conveyors shall include RFID Tag Readers at the insertion points, extraction 
points and diversion points and shall have adjustment in all three (X, Y, Z) 
directions. Framing and Underbody lines shall include RFID Tag readers in every 
station that is physically possible. 
8.9.2.3 
Tool integrators shall coordinate location of RFID tags on carriers to 
accommodate a maintainable location for the tag reader. 
8.9.2.4 
Data on each RFID tag shall be same as is currently on the Body Pallet RFID 
Tag. 
8.9.2.5 
Batch build, single model conveyors are excluded from this requirement if there 
is no IBSS data available. 
8.9.3 
Deviations to this section shall be allowed only after a VOSS "Action Request - 
Deviation" form is submitted and approved. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 5, 2009

Page 32 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
8.10 
PRIMARY, TRANSFORMER, AND SECONDARY CONDUCTORS 
8.10.1 
Welding Primary 
8.10.1.1 
Hard tool weld / manual guns: Transformer connectors (plug and socket type; 
i.e., RobiFix) shall be two-conductor + ground, flexible copper, 600 volts insulated 
with neoprene jacket, sized per welding current and duty cycle requirements; but 
will have a regional minimum of 2 AWG (N.A.) or 35 mm² (all other regions). 
8.10.1.2 
Robot weld / Robot ped weld: See GVOSS Section 5 Robots (Internal Dress 
Package Requirements). 
8.10.2 
Transformers 
8.10.2.1 
Only Ford Motor Company approved MFDC transformers shall be selected. Tier 
1 Integrators shall use for one transformer size for a given plant. 
8.10.2.2 
Transformers and secondary conductors shall be sized to provide the required 
secondary current specified by the nominal design weld schedule at maximum of 
80% of current output capacity as described in GVOSS Section 6. 
8.10.2.3 
Transformers with taps, the tap setting shall have the following settings: 
• Minimum of two tap settings to go. 
• Integral transformer with Hard Link Tap set to Tap 1. 
8.10.2.4 
All hard automation transformer brackets shall be identified with brass or lamicoid 
tags permanently attached and readily visible. Identification of transformer 
number shall be with 25 mm high letters and numbers on a 50 mm high tag. Tags 
shall be appropriately fastened. See Next-Gen 70ZF-20013205 Lamacoid 
Tagging Examples from Teamcenter. 
8.10.2.5 
Fixture type transformer mounting brackets shall be capable of supporting a 
transformer one size larger as defined below and have slots for ease of 
replacement to minimize downtime. See Ford Standard Drawing 56ZF- 25116. 
• Single Secondary B-1-S (50 KVA) with C-1-S (75 KVA) 
• Dual Secondary B-1 (85 KVA) with C-1 (100 KVA) 
8.10.2.6 
Transformers of different voltage ratings shall be controlled separate. 
8.10.2.7 
All Tier 1 Integrators shall use heat shrink tube on any cable lug utilized for 
connection to the weld transformer primary terminals. The Tier 1 Integrators shall 
also verify that all primary connections are fully clear and isolated from grounded 
such as the transformer terminal enclose surfaces. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 5, 2009 
SECTION 8 - CONTROL LOGIX 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 33 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
8.10.3 
Welding Secondary Conductors 
 
8.10.3.1 
Roll shunts are preferred over air-cooled or water-cooled cables. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
8.10.3.2 
When cables are used the following shall apply: 
• Secondary cables for fixture guns shall be approved dry-type jumpers 
unless otherwise specified and shall be as short as possible. 
• Secondary cable lengths shall not be longer than 24 inches (609.60 mm). 
• All secondary cables shall be tied together with leather straps or other 
approved devices to reduce air gap between jumpers of opposite polarity 
and prevent excessive jumper vibration when carrying current. Plastic tie 
wraps shall not be used. 
• The routing, length and area in MCM / mm² of welding shunts shall be 
shown on the design. 
• Welding transformer secondary loops shall be balanced so that the 
maximum current difference between the two secondaries of any one 
transformer and between transformers with similar welding conditions 
shall not exceed 5% of the nominal designed weld current. All secondary 
welding circuits connected to a specific welding system shall be 
electrically balanced to each other. 
• All single conductor cables passing through metal openings shall pass 
through in pairs (from the same phase opposite polarity) with no magnetic 
material between the cables. 
• Water-cooled cables shall be piped downstream of the transformer, 
electrodes, etc. in the water circuit. 
• Minimum size for air cooled welding cables shall be 750 MCM / 400 mm². 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 5, 2009 
SECTION 8 - CONTROL LOGIX 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 34 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
8.10.3.3 
For push/pull applications, loop areas of the upper and lower portions of the 
secondary circuit shall be balanced, gun forces shall be equal, and the same size 
and type of transformer shall be used for both the upper and lower. 
8.10.3.4 
Secondary conductors including air-cooled cables, water-cooled cables, kickless 
cables, leaf shunts and buss bars shall be sized appropriately for the worst case 
continuous current requirement.

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS
