9.1 
GENERAL 
9.1.1 
Specification Intent 
9.1.1.1 
This specification – including all referenced standards, specifications and 
drawings, etc. – is intended to dimension the minimum design requirements for 
Vehicle Operations assembly plant production and delivery conveyor systems. 
The conveyor Supplier is responsible for the final design, fabrication and 
installation necessary to provide a complete and workable system. 
9.1.1.2 
The GVOSS specification shall be used together with other Ford standards and 
specifications, applicable branch drawings and the project bid specifications as 
identified herein. Where discrepancies exist between the GVOSS specification 
and the detailed project specifications, other Ford standards and/or branch 
drawings, the GVOSS specification shall be used. 
9.1.1.3 
All production and delivery conveyors shall follow the standards and 
specifications set forth in the GVOSS. Deviations will be allowed only after the 
submission and approval of a GVOSS VOME Structures Standards Equipment 
Standards Action Request Form, and in compliance with the procedure outlined 
in GVOSS Section 2.8. The Ford Motor Company designated representative – 
a.k.a. the Owner – must approve all requested deviations from this specification 
before any work is started. 
9.1.2 
Reference Specifications, Codes and Standards 
9.1.2.1 
The conveyor Supplier shall ensure that all work performed is in compliance with 
the latest revision of federal, state and local codes and applicable industry 
standards including, but not limited to, the following:  
 
1. 
Occupational Safety and Health Act (U.S. and Canadian Plants)  
 
2. 
American Society for Testing and Materials  
 
3. 
American National Standards Institute  
 
4. 
American Standards Association  
 
5. 
National Electric Code  
 
6. 
National Board of Fire Underwriters Laboratories Inc.  
 
7. 
National Fire Protection Association  
 
8. 
Canadian Standards Association (Canadian Plants only)  

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 11 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.1.3 
Definitions  
9.1.3.1 
The following definitions apply as used within these specifications: 
 
1. 
Drawings and Specifications: the documents that outline the construction or 
installation to be completed pursuant to the purchase agreement.  
 
2. 
Goods: the parts, materials, machinery and equipment required to complete 
the work.  
 
3. 
Services: the direct and indirect labor required to complete the work, 
including all personnel, supervision, management and support.  
 
4. 
Contractor: the provider of the goods and services as described in the 
project bid specification and purchase agreement. 
 
5. 
Subcontractor: any entity that contracts with the Contractor, directly or 
indirectly, to furnish the goods and/or services as described in the project 
bid specification and purchase agreement.  
 
6. 
Work: the cumulative goods and services, including everything indicated by, 
or reasonably implied from, the drawings and specifications, required to 
complete the intent of the purchase agreement.  
 
7. 
Premises: the land or buildings of Owner or designee where the work of the 
purchase agreement is to be performed. 
 
8. 
Owner: Ford Motor Company 
 
9. 
Ford Motor Company: within these specifications refers to the department 
issuing these specifications. 
 
10. VOGO: Vehicle Operations General Office 
 
11. VOME: Vehicle Operations General Office, Manufacturing Engineering 
 
12. Assembly Plant, or Plant: the assembly plant where the work is to be 
performed for this project. 
 
13. Project Engineer: the VOME Project Engineer, or designated representative. 
 
14. Field Engineer: the VOME Field Engineer, or designated representative. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 12 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
15. Electrical Engineer: the VOME Electrical Engineer, or designated

9.2 
TOOLING SUPPLIER RESPONSIBILITIES 
9.2.1 
General 
9.2.1.1 
The Tooling Supplier shall provide the Conveyor Supplier with design parameters 
necessary to design the conveyor. This shall include but is not limited to: 
 
1. 
Tooling nest layout with significant dimensions and empty/loaded part nest 
center-of-gravity identified. 
 
2. 
Locator scheme for tooling nest. 
 
3. 
Stop locations and elevations at load/unload stations. 
 
4. 
Acceptable positioner tolerance and repeatability required for process 
interface. 
 
5. 
Tooling segment start- and end-roll location, reference elevation and speed 
profile. 
9.2.1.2 
The Tooling Supplier shall design, build and assemble the "part nest" tooling and 
details for part carriers. 
9.2.1.3 
Tooling nest qualification fixtures, or checking fixtures, are required and provided 
by the Tooling Supplier unless otherwise specified by Ford VOME. 
9.2.1.4 
The Tooling Supplier shall provide the part present-sensing and any mechanical 
units required to stabilize parts at the load and unload points. Part-present 
sensing shall also include error-proofing at the load point to identify an improperly 
loaded part and prevent it from reaching the unload point. 
9.2.1.5 
When a conveyor has an automatic or robotic interface at the load or unload 
point, the Tooling Supplier shall verify the end effector design interfaces properly 
with the tooling "nest" design. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 14 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.3 
CONVEYOR SUPPLIER RESPONSIBILITIES 
9.3.1 
General 
9.3.1.1 
The Conveyor Supplier – a.k.a., the Contractor – shall be responsible for the 
proper design, fabrication and installation of the conveyor at the final receiving 
facility. It is the responsibility of the Conveyor Supplier to ensure that the final 
conveyor design, whether proposed by the Conveyor Supplier or by the Owner, 
will meet the original design intent. All bidders must submit detailed 
layout/assembly drawings with their proposal for Vehicle Operations review and 
approval. 
9.3.1.2 
To minimize industrial materials kept in inventory by the plant, the Conveyor 
Supplier shall ensure that all mechanical and controls components provided are 
in compliance with the approved sourcing list. Deviations shall be submitted to 
the Implementation Supervisor and/or their designated representative using the 
GVOSS deviation request process. 
9.3.1.3 
To obtain approval for equipment or material as "Owner approved equal", the 
Conveyor Supplier must provide written performance testing results, life 
expectancy test results, Failure Modes Effects Analysis (FMEA) and all other 
baseline data for review and approval consideration by the Implementation 
Supervisor and/or designated representative. Sample FMEA forms and 
procedures are available from Ford Motor Company if needed. 
9.3.1.4 
The reliability and functionality of the equipment, including compliance with all 
applicable specifications, guidelines, and requirements referenced herein, are the 
responsibility of the Conveyor Supplier. It is the responsibility of the Conveyor 
Supplier to ensure that the final design shall meet all project requirements, 
guidelines, and applicable specifications, codes and regulations. 
9.3.1.5 
The Conveyor Supplier shall be required to provide reliability data for all new 
technology introduced for the first time to Ford Motor Company before the 
equipment is approved for installation in the plant. Reliability and durability data 
shall be collected and/or completed by the Conveyor Supplier for all components 
included as part of the new technology application. Reliability data shall be 
collected for all new hardware introduced, both electrical and mechanical, but is 
not needed for new software. Software shall be tried out and debugged in the 
Contractor shop during modular build, try-out and approval as applicable. 
9.3.1.6 
The Conveyor Supplier shall provide the Tooling Supplier with a carrier 
dimensional "envelope" and design parameters to use for carrier tooling "part 
nest" design. The Conveyor Supplier shall verify the center of mass of the carrier 
tooling "nest" and shall also verify the design will function as intended on the 
conveyor for both the loaded and empty carrier/pallet/skid return. The Conveyor 
Supplier shall provide the Tool Supplier with load bar design parameters for 
trolley conveyor applications. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 15 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.3.1.7 
The Conveyor Supplier shall provide the facility layout engineer and the Tooling 
Supplier with a conveyor dimensional "envelope" and design parameters to use 
for the purpose of determining clearances for material handling and production 
processes and with adjacent equipment. 
9.3.1.8 
The Conveyor and Tooling Suppliers shall have regular communications for the 
purpose of simultaneous engineering to exchange information relevant to the 
conveyor design. The Conveyor Supplier shall be responsible to ensure the 
Implementation Supervisor and/or their designated representative is made aware 
of the meeting schedule. 
9.3.1.9 
The Contractor work shall comply with GVOSS Section 9.4 Safety Requirements 
for Conveyors and Ford Motor Company Facility Construction Specification 
(FCS) 01100 Global Construction Safety. No proposal shall be accepted unless 
the cost of these requirements is included in the Contractor proposal and 
alternate costs. 
9.3.1.10 
The Contractor shall include in their proposal those Subcontractors which will be 
used for engineering and subcontracted portions of the work. It will be a condition 
of contract award that these identified Subcontractors will be used in executing 
the specified work. 
9.3.1.11 
All maintenance documentation shall be submitted in electronic format to be 
uploaded to the Engineering Information System (EIS) as defined in GVOSS 
sections 1.2 PROGRAM REQUIREMENTS, 1.4.11 Visual Aids and 1.5 
EQUIPMENT DOCUMENTATION. 
 
9.3.1.12 
The Contractor shall be responsible for contracting the Ford preferred EIS 
service provider to upload these materials to the EIS website; i.e., the EIS 
service provider will be a Subcontractor to the Contractor to upload the materials 
to the web site. The preferred EIS service provider is specified in GVOSS 
1.2.2.14.1.

Documents shall include only the fonts, symbols, shapes and graphics that come 
standard with the software application package used to create the document. No 
custom fonts, symbols, shapes, graphics or add-on libraries shall be used. 
9.3.3 
Document Submittal 
9.3.3.1 
All Contractor, Subcontractor and Supplier documents shall be reviewed, 
corrected, approved, stamped, and signed by the Contractor prior to each 
submittal to the Owner. The Contractor reviews and approvals of Subcontractor 
and Supplier documents shall be conducted by in-house Contractor personnel 
having the appropriate professional expertise. 
9.3.3.2 
All drawings shall be submitted to the Owner in electronic format; i.e., e-mail 
PDFs, CD, Exchange Server, etc. All non-drawing documents shall be submitted 
to the Owner in electronic format. 
9.3.3.3 
The Owner reserves the right to refuse acceptance of any document or drawing 
which is incomplete or non-legible. The Contractor shall complete and/or make 
new documents and drawings to replace non-acceptable documentation. 
9.3.3.4 
Documents and drawings shall be submitted to the Owner for approval at least 
two (2) weeks prior to start of any work; i.e., fabrication, erection, construction, 
installation, demolition, training, tryout, launches, etc. The Contractor shall not 
start any such work until approval is received from the responsible Owner or 
designated representative. 
9.3.3.5 
The Owner acceptance of any document or drawing does not relieve the 
Contractor from the responsibility for a proper, timely and workable installation. 
Any corrective action and rework of newly installed items as a result of errors 
and/or omissions on approved documents shall be the responsibility of the 
Contractor. 
9.3.3.6 
As installations are completed, as-built documentation shall be submitted to the 
Owner no later than 30 days after installation completion for review and

documents and drawings for each unit of work and deliver these transmittals to 
the Owner. As-built transmittals shall indicate the date of Owner receipt, the 
Owner designated responsible person's name and telephone number who is 
accepting the as-built documentation, and their signature. 
9.3.4

9.3.4.1 
The Contractor shall develop a Facility Launch Book and Baseline Data Sheets 
to describe, plan, schedule, record and document facility, equipment and system 
compliance with the Project Specifications. 
9.3.4.2 
The Facility Launch Book shall include information presented in matrix and 
graphical formats. All Facility Launch Book information and Baseline Data Sheets 
shall be copies of electronic documents. Key plans, digitized sketches, graphs, 
charts, standardize forms and CAD drawings shall be used to present information 
in a very readable and user-friendly format. 
9.3.4.3 
The Facility Launch Book is intended to be representative of the project “current” 
launch status. Therefore, documents shall be developed early in the project for 
the Owner review prior to the Facility Launch Phase. The scope, content and 
formats of the Facility Launch Book and Baseline Data Sheets shall be submitted 
to the Owner for approval well in advance of their scheduled use.

9.3.5 
Project Schedule 
9.3.5.1 
The Contractor shall develop and maintain a fully documented implementation 
schedule submitted in critical path format utilizing precedent sequence and bar 
chart format to reflect the target start and completion dates, intended manpower 
and percentage complete status for each unit. 
9.3.5.2 
The initial start and end date once established shall not be changed. The 
schedule shall record the actual start and end dates for each schedule item 
directly below the original start and end dates. 
9.3.5.3 
The schedule shall detail engineering, fabrication, delivery and installation 
activities on a per unit/week basis. Critical milestone dates shall be identified for 
all applicable units. 
9.3.5.4 
The weekend and downtime installation schedules shall show work activities 
broken down in half-shift increments.  
9.3.5.5 
The Contractor schedule shall comply with the assembly plant weekend and 
overtime work schedule. The scheduled workdays and work hours will be as 
defined in the project bid specification. 
9.3.5.6 
The schedule shall reflect the entire duration of the project from PO award 
through the standby period following the <Job1> date.  All site engineering, 
project management, and Supplier technical assistance for start-up, de-bug, 
standby, and training sequences shall be presented in man/week format and 
shall be shown separate of the installation activities. 
9.3.5.7 
Schedules shall be updated and issued to the Owner on a weekly basis to show 
the progress of the work as of the issue date. The date of issue shall be 
highlighted on the schedule such that review of the work progress is obvious. 
9.3.5.8 
Weekend and downtime schedules shall be issued daily to the Owner. 
9.3.6 
Design Review 
9.3.6.1 
The Contractor shall schedule and lead 30%, 60% and 90% Conveyor Design 
Review meetings. A minimum two (2) week advance meeting notice will be 
provided to the VOME standards engineers to facilitate travel request and 
planning as needed. Audio/online meetings are acceptable alternatives but still 
require a two (2) week notice.  

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 23 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.3.6.2 
The 30%, 60% and 90% Conveyor Design Review meetings shall be identified 
on the Contractor provided project planning milestone schedule.  
9.3.6.3 
Although the actual scope of work and implementation timing will vary with each 
project, the 30% Design Review shall minimally include the following elements: 
 
1. 
The 30% design review meeting shall be held no later than four (4) weeks 
following award of contract. The purpose of this initial meeting is to review 
each type of conveyor and each system layout for the work to be installed, 
reworked or relocated as part of the project to ensure compliance with 
applicable standard specifications. 
 
2. 
The Design Checklist shall be completed at the 30% Design Review 
Meeting. 
 
3. 
Area layouts and design concepts related to the overall conveyor path, 
cable tray configurations, conveyor-related structural systems, part 
clearances, and safety systems shall be reviewed at the 30% design review 
meeting. 
 
4. 
Conveyor speeds and cycle time calculations shall be reviewed at the 30% 
design review meeting. The Sequence Chart Template shall be used for the 
cycle time calculations. 
 
5. 
Mechanical and controls component layouts, assembly drawings, Safety 
Risk Assessments, FMEAs and/or concept sketches for standard and non-
standard equipment shall be reviewed at the 30% design review meeting. 
9.3.6.4 
The 60% Design Review shall minimally include the following elements: 
 
1. 
The 60% design review meeting is intended to provide detail design review 
and approval of all area layouts, mechanical and controls component 
assemblies and related shop drawings/documents. 
 
2. 
A point-to-point chain-pull or belt-pull calculation, with supporting data and 
layouts, shall be provided and reviewed/approved at the design review. 
 
3. 
The chain/belt pull calculations leading to motor sizing and selection shall 
be reviewed and approved at the 60% design review meeting. 
 
4. 
Confirmation 
of 
purchased 
components, 
including 
the 
completed 
Commercial Component Sign-Off Form, shall be reviewed and approved at 
the 60% design review meeting. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 24 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5. 
The conveyor system component assembly and shop drawings shall be 
reviewed and approved at the 60% design review meeting. 
 
6. 
The conveyor superstructure design including header steel and hanger/x-
bracing, falling parts guard, control panel platforms, repair spur platforms 
and mezzanines, including imposed building truss and/or floor loading, shall 
be reviewed and approved at the 60% design review meeting. 
 
7. 
The conveyor controls application packages shall be reviewed and 
approved at the 60% design review meeting. 
9.3.6.5 
The 90% Design Review shall minimally include the following elements: 
 
1. 
The 90% design review meeting is intended to provide review and approval 
of 
the 
conveyor 
system 
placarding, 
safety 
signage 
and 
other 
documents/processes related to skilled trades training, preventive 
maintenance and project close-out. 
 
2. 
The training program outline and related documentation shall be reviewed 
and approved at the 90% design review meeting. 
 
3. 
The Facility Conveyor Tool Profile Template and standard/non-standard 
preventive maintenance tasks, and ECSPMT and ECPL placarding shall be 
included in the 90% design review meeting. Ref. Section 1.2.3.12. 
9.3.7 
Conveyor System Installation 
9.3.7.1 
The successful bidder shall be responsible to furnish and set all anchor bolts for 
equipment to be installed. 
9.3.7.2 
The Contractor shall complete all phases of the conveyor system installation in 
compliance with the PROTAG Standard as described in GVOSS paragraph 
1.1.1.41. The VOME Conveyor Customer Checklist may be completed as part of 
the PROTAG approval process. 
9.3.7.3 
The on-site construction and installation phases shall be identified on the 
Contractor provided project planning schedule. The Contractor shall submit the 
detailed construction and installation schedule for approval one month, minimum, 
prior to any on-site construction and installation. 
9.3.7.4 
The Contractor is responsible to complete a Conveyor System Fault Log to 
identify equipment performance during facility launch, system fill, production 
start-up and standby periods. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 25 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.3.7.5 
The on-site construction and installation of the conveyor and related equipment 
shall be divided into the following five phases: 
 
1. 
Installation – period for construction of facilities and installation of equipment 
and systems. 
 
2. 
Debug – period for debug and adjustment of conveyors and related 
equipment. Equipment modifications and adjustments shall be 100% 
complete at the end of the Debug Phase. 
 
3. 
Tryout – period to tryout conveyor systems and related equipment to verify 
construction and installations comply with project requirements. This phase 
includes the 20-hour dry cycle test runs. 
 
4. 
Facility Launch – period to prove to the Owner that all equipment and 
systems operate in accordance with specified project parameters. This 
phase includes the 20-hour pallet/skid/carrier prove-out runs that simulate 
normal production conditions. 
 
5. 
Production Launch – period for the Owner to begin use of conveyors and 
related equipment for production and the Contractors period to provide 
equipment and systems training, system fill, start-up and standby. 
9.3.7.6 
Installation Phase: 
 
1. 
The Contractor shall be responsible for constructing the facility conveyors 
and related equipment and systems in accordance with all applicable 
project specifications. 
 
2. 
Following completion of the Installation Phase, the Contractor shall visually 
check and clean all overhead areas worked. Any loose debris (weld rods, 
wire ties, bolts, trash, etc.) shall be removed and a “Clean Sweep” Letter 
shall be submitted verifying the overhead areas are clean of any and all 
loose debris. 
9.3.7.7 
Debug Phase: 
 
1. 
During the Debug Phase, the Contractor shall check, verify, correct and 
adjust all equipment, components and systems utilizing production units, 
parts and materials under production conditions to ensure that all Project 
Specification requirements have been met. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 26 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2. The Contractor shall maintain a punch list of functional and operational 
problems and their corrective actions and issue it daily to the Owner for 
review. At the end of Debug Phase, all facility, equipment and system 
modifications shall be complete, and all punch list items and open issues 
closed. 
 
3. 
The Contractor shall utilize appropriate skilled trades, engineers and 
technicians to check motor rotations, verify field wiring and piping, check 
and adjust speeds, check and adjust sequences, check and adjust limit/prox 
switch position and operation, check and adjust equipment elevations, 
check and adjust interlocking equipment, etc. to ensure that all design 
requirements have been satisfactorily met. 
 
4. 
The Contractor must “Punch List” his own work and correct deficiencies as 
the job progresses. The Contractor is required to submit the Punch List on a 
PER UNIT basis for each phase of the installation. Failure of the Contractor 
to submit punch lists shall result in that portion of the work being considered 
"late". 
 
5. 
The Contractor shall provide all manpower, equipment, materials, skilled 
trades and system support required to operate and maintain all equipment 
and systems during the Tryout Phase. 
9.3.7.8 
Tryout Phase: 
 
1. 
The Contractor shall be responsible for the satisfactory tryout of all 
equipment and systems including equipment and systems provided by 
Subcontractors and Suppliers. The Tryout Phase will begin only after the 
equipment and systems are capable of operating as designed and in a safe 
manner. 
2. 
All safety equipment and interlocks shall be fully operational beginning with 
the Tryout Phase. The Owner may issue specific written approval to vary 
use of select safety equipment and interlocks, subject to approval of a 
Contractor submitted deviation request. 
 
3. 
The Contractor shall provide all manpower, equipment, materials, skilled 
trades and system support required to operate and maintain all equipment 
and systems during the Tryout Phase. 
 
4. 
The Contractor shall verify the following as a condition of completing the 
Tryout Phase (prior to the Facility Launch Phase): 
A. 
All equipment and systems shall be tested and determined “launch 
capable” by operating at minimum through maximum loads, limits and 
rates, checking all controls, safety devices and software programs. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 27 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
B. 
All equipment and systems shall be operated in the full manual mode 
and full automatic mode to prove operational capacity meets design 
criteria and parameters. 
C. 
All equipment shall be operated, including main and standby 
equipment, to prove operational capacity meets design criteria and 
parameters. 
D. 
Each piece of equipment shall be operated in “dry cycle” mode for two 
(2) consecutive days – i.e., a 20-hour run – without equipment 
malfunction or failure. 
E. 
All equipment and systems that require synchronization with other 
equipment 
and 
systems 
shall 
be 
tested 
to 
assure 
proper 
synchronization and operation. 
F. 
After each piece of equipment has been operated in the 20-hour dry-
cycle run, all equipment and systems shall be able to automatically 
circulate a group of units or parts to prove out the equipment and 
system interlocks, cycle times and synchronization. 
9.3.7.9 
Facility Launch Phase: 
 
1. 
The Facility Launch Phase is when the Contractor demonstrates to the 
Owner that all facilities, equipment and systems including all components, 
perform in compliance with the original design intent. 
 
2. 
The Contractor shall coordinate and provide all manpower, equipment, 
access tools and equipment, materials, skilled trades and system support 
required to run the entire system (and re-run after a malfunction or failure, 
as necessary), document all project requirements, and support the Owner’s 
random verification of Contractor information. 
 
3. 
Prior to acceptance of a system, the functionality and reliability of standby 
equipment under production conditions shall be demonstrated. 
 
4. 
The Contractor shall coordinate and complete a pallet/skid/carrier prove-out 
test that simulates normal production conditions. 
A. 
To demonstrate the system is “ready to run” in the production 
environment, the Contractor shall operate all equipment separately 
and as a system, for 20-hours under normal production conditions, 
with no interruptions due to conveyor or related equipment 
malfunction, fault, or failure. Following a malfunction, fault or failure, 
the prove-out test shall restart the 20-hour run until it is accomplished 
without interruption.  
B. 
The pallet/skid/carrier prove-out test shall consist of a 20-hour 
automatic 
run 
of 
continuous 
operation 
utilizing 
production 
pallets/skids/carriers and parts. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 28 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
C. 
All Facility Launch activities shall be coordinated and integrated. No 
individual conveyor system shall be launched until all related systems 
are ready to launch. No interdependent conveyor equipment or system 
shall be launched as a stand-alone component. 
D. 
The prove-out test shall be conducted with a minimum grouping of six 
(6) pallets/skids/carriers. The group of six units shall be run through 
the system together to prove-out conveyor transitions, cycle-times, unit 
spacing, sequencing, etc. 
E. 
One time during the 20-hour run and prior to acceptance, the 
equipment and/or systems shall be fully turned off and then restarted 
to insure proper re-start functions. Timing for this test shall be 
scheduled and pre-approved by the Owner. 
 
5. 
Training of plant operational, maintenance and technical personnel may 
begin during this phase. Facility Launch 20-hour runs will not be interrupted 
for training purposes. 
 
6. 
Once all equipment and systems are accepted in writing by the Owner, the 
Contractor shall be responsible for readjusting the equipment and systems, 
as needed, to comply with the plant production requirements. 
 
7. 
Following completion of the Facility Launch Phase, the Contractor shall 
visually check and clean all overhead areas worked. Any loose debris (weld 
rods, wire ties, bolts, trash, etc.) shall be removed and a “Clean Sweep” 
Letter shall be submitted verifying the overhead areas are clean of any and 
all loose debris. 
9.3.7.10 
Production Launch Phase: 
 
1. 
After the Facility Launch Phase is complete, there will be a period of time 
described as the Production Launch Phase. During this phase, plant 
production personnel will finish their equipment and system training, 
system-fill will take place, and production will begin. 
 
2. 
Tryout and Debug modifications and Facilities Launch adjustments and re-
testing are not acceptable during the Production Launch Phase. All 
adjustments and retesting must be complete prior to the Production Launch 
Phase. 
 
3. 
The plant personnel shall not have their attention diverted due to facility, 
equipment or system malfunctions, faults or failures. It is expected that plant 
maintenance personnel will not be proficient enough to keep the facility 
running under production conditions. The Contractor shall provide 
manpower to support plant maintenance personnel during the Production 
Launch Phase as indicated in the Start-Up/Standby unit in the project DED 
specification. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
4. 
The Contractor shall update the Facility Launch Book and all Baseline Data 
Sheets to document the status of all facility, equipment and system settings 
and indicators at the end of the Production Launch Phase. 
9.3.8 
Production Start-up and Standby Support 
9.3.8.1 
The Contractor shall provide start-up and standby support as defined in the bid 
specification DED for the purpose of sustaining production in the event of 
equipment failure. The Contractor manpower and equipment shall remain until 
such time as the VO Implementation Supervisor or designated representative 
approves the equipment is reliable and installed in compliance with the bid 
specification intent. 
9.3.8.2 
The production launch shall be free of any production interruptions caused by the 
Contractor standby support work. If any equipment or system malfunctions and 
interrupts production, the Contractor shall make all required modifications and 
with all associated costs paid for by the Contractor. 
9.3.8.3 
The Contractor standby support shall include as a minimum mechanical, 
electrical and operator skilled trades and controls engineers for each new piece 
of equipment and system provided. 
9.3.8.4 
The start-up and standby personnel shall be on site 1/2 hour prior to production 
start-up. 
9.3.8.5 
Any interruptions to production during the Production Start-Up and Standby 
period due to warranty issues, standby personnel intervention, etc., shall not be 
counted against the purchased start-up/standby period. Support for these type 
issues shall be at the Contractor expense. 
9.3.8.6 
The Production Standby period does not begin until the Contractor has corrected 
all punch list items. 
9.3.8.7 
For purchased start-up and standby periods, the Contractor shall provide the VO 
Implementation 
Supervisor, 
or 
designated 
representative, 
with 
weekly 
timesheets. The timesheets shall indicate personnel names, trades, start-
up/standby location and hours worked. Timesheets shall also include a copy of 
the standby person’s "Fault Log" for the respective time period. Timesheets and 
documentation shall be turned into the Implementation Supervisor on the 
Monday of the following week. The Contractor shall track the start-up/standby 
timed logged against the purchased amount. 
9.3.8.8

warranty replacement parts as needed to support installation, try-out, debug 
and launch implementation through final system buy-off.

9.3.9 
Baseline Data 
9.3.9.1 
Each conveyor and related production system shall be broken down into their 
component parts for compliance verification. All compliance information shall be 
collected to the level of detail as directed by the Owner and shall be documented 
by the Contractor on Baseline Data Sheet Forms. Baseline Data Sheet 
information shall be included in the appropriate section of the Operations and 
Maintenance Manual. 
9.3.9.2 
The Contractor shall complete the Baseline Data Sheet Forms for every 
conveyor installed, relocated or reworked. Baseline Data Sheets shall include the 
following types of component information displayed in check list and matrix 
formats: 
 
1. 
Description and identifier, design cross reference number, location, 
arrangements, model, tag and name plate details, installation and launch 
date, components, component brands, materials, Supplier contract, 
warrantee and guarantee information, etc. 
 
2. 
Design, simulation, performance, system and operational parameters, 
values, ranges, limits, indicators, speeds, timing, transfer rates, integration 
information, safety requirements, etc. 
 
3. 
Actual field operational data, readings, settings, range indicators, cycle 
times, operational, start/stop and interlock tests, launch and test durations 
and conditions, vibration, frequency and infrared signatures, alignment 
measurements, air balance reports, etc. 
9.3.9.3 
For each measurement and test, the Baseline Data Sheet shall indicate the test 
procedure, method of measurement, instrumentation used, the date, and the 
person collecting the field information. The intent is to ensure the integrity and 
repeatability of each measurement and test. 
9.3.9.4 
The Contractor shall ensure that all measurements and tests are performed 
under the supervision of a certified person in accordance with recognized 
industrial standards and procedures. All instrumentation shall be calibrated to the 
accuracy stated in the applicable Ford Motor Co. standards and procedures.

9.3.10.1

named in accordance with FAS08-212 and GMS02-727 Manufacturing Data

drawings shall be completed using the AutoCAD version currently used by the 
VO layout and planning engineers. All fonts, layers, levels and reference files, 
etc., used to create layout files that will be incorporated into the Owner overall 
plant layout drawing file shall be completed in compliance with the FCS 00 22 23 
CAD Specifications w/attachment. Shop drawing file fonts, layers and levels may 
be selected by the Contractor. However, no custom fonts, symbols, shapes, 
graphics or add-on libraries shall be used.  
9.3.10.3

an image file shall not be acceptable as as-built documentation. The Contractor 
shall be responsible for any document "clean-up" resulting from converting these 
files to AutoCAD.

All as-built documents shall be submitted in electronic format and organized in 
folders as outlined in the EIS File Structure Specification. An "As-Built Table of 
Contents" file shall be included with the file transfer media e.g., CD or external 
drive. 
9.3.10.5 
All non-drawing documentation shall be completed using the Owner standard 
Microsoft Corporation software application. The selection of the software 
application and its version for each project document type shall be as directed by 
the Owner. 
9.3.10.6 
All design drawings and specifications shall be in English units of measure. Non-
English units, when used, shall be bracketed “[ ]” and placed adjacent to the 
English units.

Sign-Off Sheet.

9.3.11 
Operation and Maintenance Manuals 
9.3.11.1 
The Contractor shall provide detailed Operational and Maintenance (O&M) 
Manuals for all equipment and systems as defined in GVOSS Section 1.5 
EQUIPMENT DOCUMENTATION.  
9.3.11.2 
The following equipment and system operating procedures shall be provided: 
 
1. 
Operational Start-Up Procedure – detailed step by step instructions for 
equipment and system start-up after short or long periods of non-use. This 
shall include, but is not limited to, checking lubrication points, fluid levels, 
pressure and vacuum settings, speed settings, switch positions, physical 
equipment locations, calibration requirements, and inspection of critical 
components, i.e. seals, belts, guards, positioners, etc. 
 
2. 
Operating Procedure – detailed step by step sequence of operations and 
monitoring instructions of operating parameters i.e. temperatures, 
pressures, fluid levels, etc. 
 
3. 
Shut-Down Procedure – detailed step by step instructions for the shut-down 
of the equipment and systems for both short and long periods of time. 
Length of shut down periods shall be submitted to the Owner for approval. 
9.3.11.3 
The following equipment and system service and maintenance procedures shall 
be provided: 
 
1. 
Inspection, Maintenance and Service Procedure - detailed step by step 
instructions for inspecting, maintaining, and servicing conveyor equipment 
and related systems during operating and shut-down periods. The 
procedure shall in include, but is not limited to, mechanical components, 
hydraulics, pneumatics, electrical, lubrication and position related 
requirements. The procedure shall specify inspection frequency 
requirements, e.g., daily, weekly, bi-weekly, monthly, etc. 
 
2. 
Troubleshooting Procedure - detailed step by step instructions to investigate 
and determine causes for equipment and system malfunctions. 
 
3. 
Repair Procedure – detailed step by step instructions for repairing various 
equipment and system components. 
9.3.11.4 
Procedures shall include instructions to operate, inspect, maintain, service and 
repair purchased components if instructions are not included in the 
manufacturers' literature. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

1.

9.3.12 
Training 
9.3.12.1 
The Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier/Contractor shall provide, document and conduct a 
task-orientated training program directed toward the Owner production, 
maintenance, and technical support personnel who will operate, utilize and 
maintain the new equipment and systems. 
9.3.12.2 
The training program shall be designed to instruct various levels of personnel in 
all areas relating to the new equipment and systems. Training program 
documentation and courses shall include and present the following: 
 
1. 
A complete understanding of all process, production and site-specific plant 
terminology. 
 
2. 
A complete understanding of all equipment, system and component 
operational and maintenance requirements and O&M procedures. 
 
3. 
A complete understanding of all equipment and system safety issues and 
requirements including step-by-step lockout and tag-out procedures for 
service, maintenance and repair. 
 
4. 
A thorough understanding of all O&M Manuals with hands-on field 
demonstrations including QPS Operator Instruction Sheets. 
9.3.12.3 
The Contractor shall submit outlines of training program documentation and 
courses to the Owner for review. Once approved by the Owner, the Contractor 
shall develop the complete training program and obtain Owner approval prior to 
Facility Launch. No equipment or system shall be launched without an approved 
training program. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 34 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.3.12.4 
The training program shall include at least the following three levels of personnel 
training: 
 
1. 
Operator Level Training - This training level shall be directed toward 
operator and supervisory personnel and include equipment overviews, basic 
equipment operation, start-up and shut-down procedures, instructions in 
manual and automatic modes of operation, instructions in the standby and 
bypass modes of operation, equipment safety features, equipment controls 
and methods for controlling abnormal operations and resuming normal 
operations. 
 
2. 
Electrical Maintenance Level Training - This training level shall be directed 
toward electrical skilled trades personnel and shall include a detailed review 
of the mechanical and electrical equipment and component identification, an 
explanation of the controls logic, an explanation of the equipment sequence 
of operations, and an explanation of trouble shooting, diagnostics, error 
checking, repair and preventive maintenance procedures. 
 
3. 
Mechanical Maintenance Level Training - This training level shall be 
directed toward mechanical skilled trades personnel and shall include a 
detailed review of the mechanical and electrical equipment and component 
identification, an explanation of the equipment sequence of operations and 
an explanation of trouble shooting, diagnostics, error checking, repair and 
preventive maintenance procedures. 
9.3.12.5 
All Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier/Contractor and manufacturer training instructors 
shall have a thorough knowledge of course materials and be able to effectively 
answer specific detailed questions. 
9.3.12.6 
The Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier/Contractor shall coordinate and provide training 
course times based on plant production schedules and trainee availability. The 
Contractor shall make accommodations to train personnel on all shifts. 
9.3.12.7 
The Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier/Contractor shall provide each trainee with one (1) 
training manual that includes the class schedule, course description, course 
materials including appropriate visual aids and additional reading material 
necessary to provide the trainee with the requisite understanding of the 
equipment. 
9.3.12.8 
All training documentation shall become the property of the Owner to be used for 
future plant training.

9.4 
SAFETY REQUIREMENTS FOR CONVEYORS 
9.4.1 
General 
9.4.1.1 
Conveyor machine safeguarding standards are intended to protect employees 
from crushing, pinching, shearing and impact injuries which may result from 
exposure to various aspects of machine operation and layout. The intimate 
understanding of equipment and its associated hazards should allow the 
Contractor to meet the intent of this standard. This understanding is a natural 
outcome of the risk assessment each provider shall conduct on both systems 
and individual conveyor elements with respect to machine layout, design and 
functions. 
9.4.1.2 
This conveyor/machine safeguarding specification is classified in five general 
areas, specific requirements for which will be detailed in this standard. 
 
1. 
Point of Operation Guarding  
 
2. 
Automation 
 
3. 
Pinch Points 
 
4. 
Fall Protection 
 
5. 
Safety Devices 
9.4.1.3 
All Contractors shall be responsible for being thoroughly familiar with the latest 
edition of all Ford Motor Company safety specifications and all applicable 
Federal, State (or Provincial), and Local safety standards, laws, codes, 
ordinances and regulations. 
9.4.1.4 
The Safety Risk Assessment is used to proactively identify potential hazards, 
estimate the degree of risk (severity of injury and probability of occurrence) and 
provide the adequate safety control measures to provide a safe work 
environment. The Risk Assessment documentation and completion process can 
be accessed from the following Ford Occupational Health and Safety Standard 
listed below: 
 
1. 
FAS08-036 Risk Assessment Requirements For The Design, Build, 
Modification And Commissioning of Equipment. 
9.4.1.5 
This Section is to be used in conjunction with the following Ford FCS sections.  
This Section shall supersede any conflicts between this Section and those listed 
below. 
 
1. 
FCS Section 00 21 13 (formerly 00200) Instructions for Procurement 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 37 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2. 
FCS Section 01100 Global Construction Safety 
 
3. 
FCS Section 01105 Site Safety Contractor Responsibilities 
 
4. 
FCS Section 01110 Dedicated vs. Dual Role Safety Engineer 
9.4.1.6 
This Section is to be used in conjunction with the following Ford GMS safety 
specifications.  This Section shall supersede any conflicts between this Section 
and those specifications listed below. 
 
1. 
w-OETS001 Overhead Equipment and Tooling Standard 
 
2. 
FAS08-212 Occupational Noise Standard 
9.4.1.7 
This Section is to be used in conjunction with the following Ford GMS mechanical 
conveyor specifications.  This Section shall supersede any conflicts between this 
Section and those specifications listed below. 
 
1. 
GMS CX2 Trolley Conveyors 
 
2. 
GMS CX10 Power and Free Conveyor 
 
3. 
GMS CGX1 Trolley Conveyor Stock Guards 
 
4. 
GMS CGX7 Overhead Conveyor Incline and Decline Safety Stops 
 
5. 
GMS02-720ME Conveyor Safety Devices Pull Wire Rope and Switch 
 
6. 
GMS U-CX003 Overhead Conveyors 
9.4.1.8 
This Section is to be used in conjunction with the following Ford GMS electrical 
specifications.  This Section shall supersede any conflicts between this Section 
and those specifications listed below. 
 
1. 
GMS w-EL4 Light Curtains and Presence Sensing Devices for Machine 
Safeguarding 
 
2. 
GMS w-EL10 Programmable Safety Systems 
9.4.1.9 
The Conveyor Supplier shall ensure the equipment provided is in full compliance 
with the identification and written procedures for labeling and control of 
hazardous energy as specified in the Ford Motor Company specifications and 
applicable Energy Control/Power Lockout (ECPL) and Energy Control for Setup 
and Permitted Routine and Minor Tasks (ECSPMT) standards, latest revision, 
and applicable Federal, State (Provincial) and Local safety standards, laws, 
codes, ordinances and regulations; e.g., OSHA 1910.147 and Subpart S 
(1910.300 - 1910.399). 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 38 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.4.1.10 
All identification labels and energy control/lockout labels must be installed in 
compliance with the timing requirements identified in the Ford VOME PROTAG 
process. 
9.4.1.11 
The Conveyor Supplier shall design product and personnel guarding to allow for 
as much shop pre-assembly as is possible. 
9.4.1.12 
For major program installations, pedestrian traffic flow must be studied during the 
design stage. The appropriate Ford Plant Engineer shall provide a layout 
identifying formally established and clearly designated routes to production 
operations as well as maintenance access to equipment. The Contractor shall 
ensure the final conveyor and related equipment layouts provide both production 
and maintenance access without exposure to hazards. 
9.4.1.13 
Where deviations to this Section are identified as necessary they shall be 
requested and reviewed using the standard deviation process identified within 
this GVOSS specification. Deviation requests will not be considered without 
completion of a thorough Risk Assessment for the subject request. 
9.4.2 
Point of Operation Guarding 
9.4.2.1 
Point of operation guarding includes guards necessary to prevent inadvertent 
access to pinch points created by the drivetrain. e.g., at the area of engagement 
of chains or belts with sprockets or sheaves, and at take-up and traction wheels 
that are accessible from floor levels, maintenance platforms and repair spurs.  
9.4.2.2 
Point of operation guards shall be provided at all moving, sliding and rotating 
parts including, but not limited to, the following: 
 
1. 
All moving parts of the power transmission system – i.e., drives, couplings, 
belts, sheaves, RC chain and sprockets, etc. – shall be guarded.  The 
power transmission guard shall be secured to the drive base and/or 
machinery framework either by fasteners or by retaining or nesting clips.  
 
2. 
Overhead Monorail (OHM) and Overhead\Inverted Power and Free (P&F) 
caterpillar drive sprockets and chain shall be fully guarded, except for the 
side toward the conveyor chain. 
3. 
Conveyor chain which is accessible from plant floors or maintenance 
platforms. e.g., Inverted P&F drives and/or take-ups located on the plant 
floor and OHM/P&F drives and/or take-ups accessed by maintenance 
platforms or in repair spurs. 
 
4. 
On OHM and P&F conveyors, the conveyor chain that travels over the 
platforms, through the drive(s) and the take-up shall be guarded. The 
guarding shall be provided on the sides and top of the drive and take-up to 
restrict access to all pinch points that can be accessed by a person while on 
the maintenance platform. 
 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 39 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
5. 
The head and take-up sections on floor conveyors.   
9.4.2.3 
Point of operation guarding is not required where conveyor equipment is located 
within automated cells surrounded by perimeter fencing which can be entered 
only via interlocked gates and or light screens which subsequently control power 
to the conveyor equipment. e.g., power roll beds, hold tables, lift tables, and 
cross-belts, etc. included in decouple zones located between respot segments 
within tooling cells. 
9.4.2.4 
Where there is potential to access the drive and/or take-up machinery from below 
– e.g., while standing in the screen guard or on a platform – the potential pinch 
points shall be located such that they are more than 7 feet (2134 mm) above the 
standing surface. Otherwise, these potential pinch points shall be guarded as 
specified herein 
9.4.2.5 
Point of operation guarding on conveyor equipment located in close proximity to 
production operations, and as defined by the Risk Assessment, must be secured 
with fasteners requiring hand tools for removal. In all other locations, fasteners 
are not required and "drop in place" guards can be used. 
 
1. 
The “drop in place” guards must be “secured” to the machine framework 
using hinges, tabs and\or clips, etc. to prevent movement due to vibration, 
otherwise fasteners shall be provided as necessary to keep the guarding 
securely in position. 
 
2. 
Where fasteners are used, they shall be secured to the drive base or 
machinery framework. 
9.4.2.6 
All point of operation guards shall be constructed of lightweight see-through 
mesh with 1/8" thick framing. The width of the framing is to be determined by the 
application; the intent is to keep the guarding light but able to hold shape during 
installation and removal. 
 
1. 
The size of the mesh openings shall depend upon the hazard. No part of the 
body should be able to touch the hazard with the guarding installed. 
 
2. 
The guarding is to be constructed with lifting handles. 
 
3. 
The guarding shall be designed such that no single piece is longer than 72 
inches (1828 mm), nor weigh more than 30 pounds (14 kg).  
9.4.2.7 
Point of operation guarding and safety related components shall be painted and 
stenciled as follows: 
 
1. 
All moving parts made of fabricated steel with one coat Safety Orange. 
 
2. 
All switch brackets, track switches (except moving parts), hand railing and 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 40 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
highway guard railing with one shop coat of Safety Yellow. 
 
3. 
All power transmission belt and chain guard framing and perimeter fence 
posts with one shop coat of Safety Yellow. 
 
4. 
All power transmission belt and chain guard expanded metal and perimeter 
fence guards with one shop coat of Ford Standard Black. 
 
5. 
Stencil pinch points on automatic greasers. 
 
6. 
Stencil direction of travel/rotation of belts and chains on all drivetrain 
guards. 
9.4.2.8 
Although point of operation guarding is not intended to serve as a step, platform 
or walking surface, these guards shall be designed accordingly where there is 
potential for plant personnel to step on them during routine or non-routine 
maintenance, production and/or other related activities. Refer to Sections 9.4.4 
and 9.4.5 of this specification for additional guard design requirements. 
9.4.2.9 
Where physical room permits, machinery guards shall be oversized by 15 
percent to accommodate future size increases of belts, sheaves, couplings, 
sprockets, etc. 
9.4.3 
Automation – Conveyor/AGV/AGC Systems 
9.4.3.1 
The conveyor layout shall be designed and configured such that crossing the 
conveyor to gain access to gearmotors, operator panels, etc. is minimized e.g., 
the gearmotors for mezzanine mounted power roll beds are located on the same 
side of the mezzanine as the mezzanine access stairs. 
9.4.3.2 
Conveyor AGV/AGC controls and control panel locations: 
 
1. 
Control Panels shall be located such that the motions of equipment 
controlled by a specific panel are visible when standing in front of the 
control panel. 
2. 
For floor conveyors, AGV/AGVs the control panels are to be located at the 
floor level. 
 
3. 
For floor conveyors installed at the mezzanine level, the controls panels 
shall also be located on the mezzanine. 
 
4. 
For overhead conveyors, AGV/AGVs the drive panels shall be located at the 
same level as the drives. 
 
5. 
For Vertical Drop Lifters, the main control panel shall be located at the floor 
level and a remote panel, or operator station, shall be provided at the upper 
level. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 41 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.4.3.3 
Vertical Drop Lifters (VDLs) with 6 feet (1828 mm), or greater, travel shall be 
designed as follows: 
 
1. 
All VDLs with vertical travel of 6 feet (1828 mm), or greater, shall be 
provided with perimeter fencing in compliance with GVOSS Section 1.3.5, 
including interlocked personnel access gates and light screens at the 
bottom (plant floor) level. 
A. 
Where the VDL travels between an elevated 2nd level – e.g., a 
conveyor mezzanine or basket guard – and a 3rd level, the entry (or 
exit) at the 2nd level shall be treated the same as if it were at the 
bottom (plant) floor level, i.e., the opening in the perimeter fence shall 
include light screens to prevent inadvertent access to the crush 
hazard. 
B. 
The perimeter fence shall be located to provide 36 inches (914 mm) 
running clearance to the moving carriage and/or power roll bed frame. 
C. 
In certain cases, silhouette guards may be used at lift entry and exit 
points where design allows the passage of the carriers and/or the 
product while preventing inadvertent personnel access. These cases 
shall be treated as standard deviations and shall be processed 
accordingly. i.e., the Contractor shall submit, and receive approval of, 
a standard deviation request including a completed Risk Assessment 
for the subject request. 
 
2. 
The VDL layout shall be designed to maximize visibility of the lifting and 
transfer operation for maintenance personnel observation from outside the 
perimeter fence area. 
 
3. 
Individual machine point of operation guards will not be required within the 
enclosed perimeter fence area except where hazards are accessible via 
maintenance platforms while the VDL equipment is running. 
4. 
When two or more VDLs of 6 feet (1828 mm), or greater, vertical travel are 
installed next to one another they must each be individually enclosed in 
their own perimeter fence areas. 
 
5. 
The maintenance access ladders shall be located outside of the perimeter 
fencing to allow equipment access for maintenance and observation while 
running. 
 
6. 
All VDLs with vertical travel of 6 feet (1828 mm), or greater, shall be 
equipped with handrail or perimeter fence around the upper level “floor 
opening”. An opening in the handrail or fencing shall be provided to allow 
the skid, pallet or carrier to transfer to/from the fixed mezzanine mounted 
conveyor from/to the moving VDL mounted conveyor. 
A. 
Fall hazard identification components – i.e., signage, rope curtain and 
tread plate, etc. – shall be provided as described in GVOSS section 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 42 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.4.7 Fall Protection. 
B. 
A suitable retractable lanyard shall be provided and connected to a 
fixed anchorage tie-off point. The lanyard shall be securely stowed 
near the transfer opening in a safe manner for maintenance personnel 
use. 
C. 
A “drop in” top and intermediate rail shall be stowed on “gun racks” 
located adjacent to the transfer opening. These “drop in” rails are 
intended to provide fall hazard protection for routine maintenance 
activities. The “gun rack” shall include prox switches to indicate the 
rails are in the “stowed “position to allow the skid, pallet, or carrier 
transfer to/from the VDL. 
D. 
For skid and pallet conveyors, the intermediate “drop in” rail is not 
required where the intermediate rail can be fixed in position such that 
the skid or pallet will clear during transfer to/from the VDL. 
 
7. 
All control panels, operator stations, motor disconnects, brake disconnects, 
air dump valves and energy control devices are to be located outside the 
safety fencing/railing and the 6’-0 (1828 mm) fall hazard area. 
 
8. 
Control Panels shall be so located as to allow observation of crush point in 
event of lift operation. Where this is not possible a remote panel, or operator 
station, shall be installed to fulfill this requirement. 
 
9. 
The perimeter fence gate plug shall be designed in compliance with 
applicable sections of the GVOSS and Global Manufacturing Specifications. 
9.4.3.4 
Vertical Drop Lifters with less than 6 feet (1828 mm) travel: 
 
1. 
For skid and pallet VDLs with vertical travel less than 6 feet (1828 mm), the 
crush hazard exposure shall be prevented using side skirting – e.g., 
accordion guard – to restrict access below the VDL mounted power roll bed. 
In the event such equipment is for other reasons located in a cell with 
protected perimeter fencing, the side skirting is to be considered redundant 
and shall not be required. 
 
2. 
All other VDLs shall be guarded as identified in paragraph 9.4.3.3. 
9.4.3.5 
Power Roll Beds: 
(This is also a generic guarding specification for other equipment, not just power 
roll beds.  It is referenced elsewhere to apply to other equipment): 
 
1. 
Delivery Power Roll Beds running up to and at 120 feet (36.6 meters) per 
minute shall be provided with "removable" perimeter guarding anchored to 
the plant finished floor. 
A. 
The typical perimeter guard shall be OSHA compliant handrail located 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 43 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
on both sides of the PRB conveyors. i.e., the handrail shall be 
constructed from 2" (25 mm) diameter tube steel with 3'-6" (1067 mm) 
high top rail, a mid-rail at 21" (534 mm) height and a continuous 4" 
(100 mm) kick plate. 
B. 
Alternatively, perimeter fence guards may be used. When perimeter 
fence guards are used, they must comply with the requirements 
identified in Paragraph 9.4.3.5.2. 
C. 
A "highway style" guard shall be provided when the PRB conveyors 
are located adjacent to aisles or material staging/storage areas. The 
highway guard may be used in lieu of the handrail guards but shall be 
provided when perimeter fence guarding is used. 
D. 
Self-closing spring-return access gates shall be provided for 
maintenance personnel access. The access gate shall include a 
warning sign indicating "Danger – Automated Equipment May Start at 
Any Time. Maintenance Access Only". 
E. 
The handrails, guardrails and access gate shall be painted Safety 
Yellow. 
 
2. 
Power Roll Beds running over 120 feet (36.6 meters) per minute shall 
include perimeter fencing, as specified in the GVOSS Section 1.3. 
A. 
A minimum of two (2) access gates with gate plugs, one on each side 
of the conveyor cell, shall be provided. 
B. 
An approved presence-sensing device shall be provided at the 
entrance and exit of the PRB conveyors to detect unauthorized 
access. 
3. 
Where there is a continuous line of Power Roll Beds in excess of 100 feet 
(30.5 meters) long, non-maintenance personnel crossovers shall be 
provided where required. The non-maintenance personnel crossovers shall 
include a floor mounted overhead catwalk with standard handrails 
accessible by OSHA compliant stair assemblies. The access stairs shall be 
configured such that they do not create a potential pedestrian/PMHV safety 
hazard by opening directly into adjacent aisles. 
9.4.3.6 
Turntables and Shuttle Carts 
 
1. 
All turntables and shuttle carts must have their direction/rotation area clearly 
marked with 12" (305 mm) wide, red color arch with directional arrows. The 
inside perimeter of this arch must start at the edge of the power roll bed 
mounted to the turntable or shuttle cart to indicate the clearance line, or at 
the end of the skid/pallet if it overhangs the power roll bed. 
 
2. 
All turntables and shuttle carts shall be provided with "removable" perimeter 
guarding anchored to the plant finished floor. 
A. 
The typical perimeter guard shall be a continuous handrail, as defined 
in paragraph 9.4.3.5 of this specification, and shall be located on the 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 44 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
full perimeter of the turntable and shuttle cart conveyors. 
B. 
The perimeter guardrail shall be located to provide 36 inches (914 mm) 
running clearance to the power roll bed frame. 
C. 
Alternatively, perimeter fence guards may be used. When perimeter 
fence guards are used, they must comply with the requirements 
identified in Paragraph 9.4.3.5.2, except only one access gate is 
needed. 
D. 
A "highway style" guard shall be provided when the PRB conveyors 
are located adjacent to aisles or material staging/storage areas. The 
highway guard may be used in lieu of the handrail guards but shall be 
provided when perimeter fence guarding is used. 
E. 
One spring-loaded, self-closing access gate shall be provided near the 
operator station for the turntable and shuttle cart. A warning sign that 
indicates "Danger-Automated Equipment May Start at Any Time. 
Maintenance Access Only" shall be posted on the access gate. 
 
3. 
As shown in Figure No. 9.4.3.6.3, the floor area below the access gate shall 
be “tiger stripe” painted – i.e. yellow and black diagonal lines – the full width 
of the opening and extending from the rotation arch beyond the outside of 
the perimeter rail a length equal to the access gate width. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure No. 9.4.3.6.3 – Typical Turntable and Shuttle Cart Design 
 
 
4. 
Openings shall be provided in the perimeter handrail to provide running 
clearance for the skidded/palletized unit to transfer to/from the adjacent 
charge and discharge conveyors. An 18" (457mm) high horizontal barrier 
rail shall be provided the full width of the transfer opening.  The barrier rail 
shall be painted Safety Yellow.

9.4.4 
Power Roll Beds used for Production Operation and Repair/Inspection 
Stations with Cross Over 
9.4.4.1 
General 
• Power Roll Beds used for production operation, inspection or repair 
workstations are specified in three different configurations based on speed 
of conveyors.  
• Although Power Roll Bed applications are specifically addressed within 
this section, these specifications are intended to apply to all conveyor 
types when utilized for discontinuous production, inspection or repair 
workstations operating at the speeds described.  
• A Risk Assessment is required for each application. The VOME and 
Safety engineers shall review and approve each production, inspection 
and/or repair workstation design before finalizing the conveyor design. 
 
9.4.4.2 
Conveyors surrounding the station are running < 82fpm (Ref drawing 3ZF-35670-
sheet#6 REV A.)

through

• Request to cross button is required on both ends of the cross-
through. 
• Pallet stop on the preceding conveyor is required 
• Crossover height to be at top of z-rail to allow egress onto adjacent 
covered PRB. 
• The PRB adjacent to the cross-over shall include cover plating for 
emergency egress. 
• An operator start/release PB stand as specified in section 2 shall be 
provided on each side of the conveyor where operators are 
present. The PB stand shall be located such that the operator must 
vacate the work zone to activate the start/release switch from a

9.4.5 
Pinch Points 
9.4.5.1 
Pinch points are created by the transition from one floor conveyor element to 
another. Examples are power roll bed to power roll bed, power roll bed to two 
strand conveyors, cross transfer to two strand conveyor and other similar 
configurations. 
9.4.5.2 
Pinch Point hazards are typically found to exist in the following configurations: 
 
1. 
Work platforms and pits with vertical faces perpendicular to the path of the 
conveyor. 
 
2. 
Crossover stairs with vertical faces perpendicular to the path of the 
conveyor. 
 
3. 
Conveyor electrical boxes, drives, devices, etc. that protrude out at 90 
degrees to the conveyor and within the path of the conveyor or walkway 
along the conveyor. 
 
4. 
Flooring with wide track slot openings, creating foot pinch point or 
entrapment hazards. 
 
5. 
Entrances to and exits from booths, body loader/unloader, caged 
equipment, through wall openings or silhouettes. 
 
6. 
Insufficient clearance between carrier paths and fixed objects such as 
building columns, railings, etc. Refer section 9.4.5.3 for required minimum 
clearances. 
 
7. 
Unguarded return conveyor chain through platforms or into pits creating foot 
pinch point or entrapment hazards. 
 
8. 
Low headroom clearance over the body or carrier creating the potential for 
decapitation. 
 
9. 
Where a skid/pallet transfers between adjacent conveyors. 
 
10. Power Roll Lift Tables shall have foot pinch point protection around the drive 
frame.  
 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 48 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 11.   Floor mounted stabilizers for overhead conveyors at load station.    
9.4.5.3 
A risk analysis will identify the necessary measures for hazard remediation.  
Typical methods used to eliminate these pinch point hazards include the 
following: 
 
1. 
Provide logical, safe pedestrian traffic paths by adding or removing 
conveyor crossover steps and reviewing zone-to-zone paths. 
2. 
Observe and maintain minimum safe spacing between the units in order to 
prevent employee injury: 
A. 
In a typical area of the plant where employees may be working in an 
open space beside or inside the vehicle body, there shall be a 
minimum of 20 inches (500 mm) between the moving and fixed/moving 
objects so as not to create the potential for a pinch point. 
B. 
Where employees are assigned to work between units, expected to 
move between units or where the working space between units 
becomes part of their access to a means of egress, the minimum 
spacing shall be 28" (711 mm) between units. 
C. 
Within vehicle body storage banks or similar areas, where there is 
proper safeguarding provided to prevent employee access, the 
minimum space required between units shall be 6" (153 mm). 
 
3. 
Provide barrier guarding (handrails, highway guard or perimeter fence) 
surrounding a specific floor area to prevent general access to large 
accumulating banks. 
 
4. 
Provide a continuous guardrail around the perimeter of all Cross Transfers 
that are not used for production. 
A. 
A continuous handrail, as defined in paragraph 9.4.3.5 of this 
specification, shall be used for skid cross-transfers perimeter guard 
rail. 
B. 
Alternatively, "highway style" guards shall be used when the conveyors 
are located adjacent to aisles or material staging and/or storage areas. 
C. 
Barrier guarding between the adjacent conveyors at both the charge 
and discharge of the cross-wise transfer conveyor shall be provided in 
compliance with the requirements specified within this document. 
D. 
Conveyors used as part of the process tooling – e.g., Body Shop 
decoupler X-belt conveyors, lift-tables, etc. – shall be guarded in 
compliance with the GVOSS section 1.3.5, latest revision. 
 
5. 
Barrier guarding, as shown in Figure No. 9.4.4.3.5 Typical Barrier Guard, 
shall be provided to prevent passage between adjacent pieces of conveyor 
equipment such as pallet power roll beds. 
 
6. 
Start/Stop Buttons may be used to control conveyors and allow safe 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 49 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
passage. 
 
7. 
Signage indicating "DANGER: PINCH POINT" and "RESTRICTED 
ACCESS" shall be posted and clearly visible where employees are exposed 
to the pinch point hazard. 
 
                     
 
 
 
Figure No. 9.4.4.3.5 – Typical Barrier Guard 
 
 
8. 
Paint platforms and floors to alert personnel of potential pinch point hazards. 
A. 
The floor painting shall include a Safety Red background with White 
stenciled labels indicating "DANGER PINCH POINT". 
B. 
The platform perimeter in the area near the pinch point hazard shall be 
painted with a 6" (153 mm) wide red stripe, with "DANGER" stenciled 
labels in yellow as shown in Figure No. 9.4.4.3.8. 
 
9. 
Umbrella shaped sheet metal shrouds as shown in Figure No. 9.4.4.3.9, or 
sloped-up ramp guards Figure No. 9.4.4.3.8, prior to high work platforms, pit 
walls and cross-over's with vertical surfaces perpendicular to the conveyor 
path. 
A. 
Installed the guard from the leading edge of the platform, cross-over or 
pit wall to a point 3 feet (914 mm) up-stream, preventing employees 
from standing close to the front edge of the platform, pit wall or cross-
over. 
B. 
The guard shall be of sufficient size to eliminate the pinch point hazard.

egress to and from work areas.

9.4.5.4 
Personnel conveyor cross-over shall be provided in four basic configurations: (1) 
Plant Personnel Cross-Over Bridge, (2) Plant Personnel Cross-Through (3) Line 
Level Maintenance Only Cross-Over and (4) Mezzanine Level Maintenance 
Cross-Over. 
 
1. 
Plant Personnel Cross-Over Bridge: The Plant Personnel Cross-Over 
Bridge shall consist of an elevated catwalk with access stairs to allow all 
plant personnel to safely cross a conveyor by going up and over the line. 
A. 
The Plant Personnel Cross-Over Bridge system assembly shall be 
complete with floor-mounted checker-plate catwalk, two (2) access 
stairs, minimum, and 42” (1066 mm) high handrail with mid-rail and 4” 
(100 mm) high kick-plate. 
B. 
For additional design requirements, refer to GVOSS Section 1.3.14 
Open-Sided Floors, Platforms and Balconies.

Page 53 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2. 
Plant Personnel Cross-Through: (Crossing in between the gap of 2 
conveyance systems). The Plant Personnel Cross-Through shall consist of 
a pushbutton request and presence-sensing control system, as shown in 
Figure No. 9.4.4.4.2(a), to allow all plant personnel to safely cross a line of 
conveyor by walking between adjacent conveyors or between static 
production units. 
A. 
The Plant Personnel Cross-Through system assembly shall include 
light screens and other similar Ford approved presence sensing 
devices, as shown in Figure No. 9.4.4.4.2(b) and (c) Plant Personnel 
Cross-Through Control Schematics, to control conveyor movement to 
allow safe passage between the conveyors or static units. 
B. 
The monitored/scanned area shall be sized sufficient to eliminate the 
potential pinch point hazard. 
C. 
The floor area shall be "tiger stripe" painted – i.e., yellow and black 
diagonal striping – to indicate the monitored area. 
D. 
The passageway shall be provided with two (2) pushbutton stands, one 
on each side of the cross-through, complete with a crossing request 
pushbutton, presence-sensing device reset button, green and red 
indicator light and audible alarm. 
E. 
The audible alarm will be controlled by a timer to initiate the alarm if 
the reset button is not pressed after a predetermined time following the 
request to cross the line. 
F. 
When an elevated cross-over surface is necessary, the cross-over 
walkway height shall be 1" (25 mm) below the top of chain, belt or 
roller elevation. 
G. 
The cross-through floor surface shall be in compliance with the 
requirements identified in FAS08-040 Safe Practices for Workplace 
Organization, Housekeeping and Walking Working Surfaces.

3. 
Line Level Maintenance Only Cross-Over: The Line Level Maintenance Only 
Cross-Over, as shown in Figure No. 9.4.4.4.3. shall be used to provide safe, 
quick maintenance access between pieces of equipment as necessary in 
areas designated only for plant maintenance skilled trades, maintenance 
supervisors and other similarly authorized personnel. The Line level 
Maintenance Cross-Over shall be properly identified. See Fig. 9.4.4.4.3. 
A. 
The Maintenance Only Cross-Over shall consist of an elevated cross-
over platform with steps when the platform elevation is greater than 
11"(280 mm). 
B. 
The edges of the platform and leading edge of the stair treads shall be 
painted Safety Yellow. 
C. 
The cross-over walkway height shall be 1/2" (12 mm) below the top of 
chain, belt or roller elevation. 
D. 
The “down-stream” conveyor shall include a full-length cover plate, for 
a minimum one skid/pallet length. 
E. 
The perimeter handrail shall be 42” above the downstream conveyor 
cover plate, starting at 2’-0 (610 mm) in front of the cross-over platform 
leading edge and shall extend the full length of the down-stream 
conveyor cover plate, both sides. Figure 9.4.4.4.3 
F. 
Line level maintenance only cross overs can be used as line level 
production personnel cross overs only after a risk assessment is 
completed. The finalized risk assessment shall determine the 
necessity of additional safeguards. i.e. plant personnel usage, request 
to cross or other safety components. Figure 9.4.4.4.3(F)

cover plate, starting at 2’-0 (610 mm) in front of the cross-over platform 
leading edge and shall extend the full length of the down-stream 
conveyor cover plate, both sides.

9.4.6 
Conveyor Equipment Pinch Point Guarding 
9.4.6.1 
A "pinch point" is defined as any point, other than the point of operation, at which 
it is possible for a part of the body to be caught between the moving parts of a 
conveyor and auxiliary equipment, or between moving and stationary parts of a 
conveyor and auxiliary equipment or facility, or between the material and moving 
part or parts of the conveyor and auxiliary equipment. All pinch points shall be 
guarded. 
FLOW 
Down Stream 
PRB w/ Full 
Length Cover 
Added Rail 
42” above 
PRB Cover 
Maintenance 
Only 
Cross-Over 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 59 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.4.6.2 
The return strand of conveyors utilizing fixtures shall be guarded. Where 
applicable, the return strand guard shall also be provided with safety gate 
interlocks as specified in this Section and applicable Ford Global Manufacturing 
Standards. When interlocks are required, all guarding must be secured with 
fasteners requiring tools for removal. 
9.4.6.3 
Casters and wheels which present potential pinch points shall be guarded. e.g., 
shuttle cart wheels shall be guarded as shown in Figure No. 9.4.5.3(a) and 
Inverted Power and Free carrier stabilizer wheels shall be guarded as shown in 
Figures No. 9.4.5.3(b). 
9.4.6.4 
Finger guards and pinch point guards shall be provided on all rail, roller turns, 
traction wheel turns and vertical curves wherever the bottom of the power rail on 
a monorail conveyor or bottom of the free rail on a power and free conveyor is 
below 7'-0" (2134 mm) measured from the plant floor or work platform. 
 
1. 
Where there is potential for personnel to step on the guard, either 
intentionally or inadvertently, the guarding structural steel bar/angle 
framework shall be capable of supporting a 250 lb. (114 kg) load located at 
the center of the unsupported frame length. 
 
2. 
Refer to 3ZF-20708 sheets 8 through 10 for standard monorail track and 
traction wheel finger guarding. 
9.4.6.5 
Finger guards and pinch guards shall be provided on both sides of inverted 
power and free tracks, closing the gap between the free track and the power 
track, at roller turns and traction wheels and along traffic areas where the 
elevation is below 7'-0" (2134 mm) measured from the plant floor, catwalk, 
basket guard, or work platform. 
9.4.6.6 
On Inverted P&F conveyor vertical curves, the track seal and stabilizer rail 
configuration shall extend up the vertical to a height of 8'-0" above the finished 
floor or platform elevation as shown in Figure 9.4.5.6. 
9.4.6.7 
Cross-over steps shall be provided to cross the line from one side to the other on 
"above floor" mounted Inverted Power and Free Delivery Conveyors located in 
screen guard and non-production areas. The cross-overs shall be located where 
required to provide safe employee egress and proper maintenance access (re: 
Figure No. 9.4.4.3.9).
