9.4.7 
Fall Protection 
9.4.7.1 
Falling parts guard, or screen guard, shall be provided under conveyors where 
necessary to prevent injury to employees in the event parts fall from carriers. 
9.4.7.2 
Where control devices, stops, track switches or operator control stations exist 
and no screen guard is present means of safe access to these items must be 
provided such as elevated platforms, service platforms and access ladders.  
9.4.7.3 
All screen guards at inclines and declines are to be brought to the floor level 
unless restricted by floor space.  In restricted spaces, the screen guard may start 
4 feet (1220 mm) above the floor level, subject to approval by the appropriate 
Ford conveyor engineering personnel. 
9.4.7.4 
Where screen guards are used as walkways to provide maintenance access, 
they must have 42" (1067 mm) high handrails extending 6'-0" (1828 mm) from 
the opening in the screen guard, where applicable, to provide protection against 
the fall hazard. 
9.4.7.5 
Falling parts guard openings that yield a difference in elevation equal to or 
greater than 6 feet (1828 mm), including those at vertical inclines/declines, are 
considered fall hazards and shall be identified as such to alert employees to the 
need for appropriate fall hazard protective measures. 
 
1. 
The hazard shall be identified in the basket guard via provision of specially 
prepared 16 gage thick, 3 feet wide small hole “Tread Plate Flooring". 
A. 
The tread plate flooring shall span the entire width of the screen guard. 
B. 
The tread plate flooring shall be installed 6 feet (1828 mm) from the 
edge of the screen guard. 
Finished Floor 
8'-0 
Top of Track Seal 
and Stabilizer Rail 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 62 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
C. 
The tread plate flooring shall be “tiger stripe” painted – i.e., yellow and 
black diagonal stripes. 
 
2. 
Warning signs, as shown in Figure No. 9.4.6.5.2 shall be provided to further 
alert personnel of the fall hazard. 
A. 
The warning signs shall be located at eye level no less than 6 feet 
(1828 mm) from the edge of the screen guard. 
B. 
The signs shall have text to indicate "Danger- Open Area. No 
Guarding. Fall Protection Required Beyond This Point. Authorized 
Personnel Only". 
C. 
Multiple signs shall be utilized where multiple approaches to the 
hazard are identified.  
 
3. 
A "rope silhouette curtain" shall be provided extending across the screen 
guard oriented perpendicular to the conveyor and located at the edge of the 
"tiger-striped" tread plate nearest the fall hazard. 
 
4. 
A suitable retractable lanyard shall be provided and connected to a fixed 
anchorage tie-off point. The lanyard shall be securely stowed a minimum 6 
feet (1828 mm) away from the fall hazard in a safe manner for maintenance 
personnel use. 
 
5. 
See also Figures No. 9.4.6.5.5(a) and (b) for illustrations of some typical fall 
hazards and associated fall hazard identification equipment.

Access ladders to basket guarding will be so positioned as to have a minimum

basket guard floor (re: Figure No. 9.4.6.5.5(a))

All access ladders shall be designed in compliance with the applicable sections 
of the OSHA specification, latest edition.

9.4.8 
Falling Parts Guard Design 
9.4.8.1 
Falling parts/basket guards shall be provided under all conveyors where the 
lowest portion of the conveyor or part carrying device is greater than 7'-0" (2133 
mm) from the plant floor or platform. The falling parts guard shall be designed, 
fabricated and installed in conformance with Ford GMS w-CGX1 and as specified 
herein. Where discrepancies between GMS w-CGX1 and this GVOSS 
specification exist, this specification shall take precedence. 
9.4.8.2 
The falling parts guard shall be 2" x 4" (50 x 100 mm) No. 2 gage welded wire 
mesh. (4'-4" wide X 10'-0" Lg, helixed to 2'-0" high side X 10'-0" Lg and (1) 2'-0" 
Lg vertical helix on (1) end) 
9.4.8.2.1 
Falling part-guarding orientation shall be oriented with 2” bar spacing facing up 
and 4” bar spacing facing down. 
 
                     
   
 
9.4.8.3 
All trimming of guard sections shall be done with wire cutters.  The joining of two 
(2) sections of the screen guarding in the field will be done only by using the 
guard manufacturer supplied spiral coils. Welding of screen guarding in the 
field is not permitted. 
9.4.8.4 
Welded modular sections of basket guard built in the fabrication shop are 
acceptable. 
 
1. 
All edges of the shop assembled basket guard sections must land on the 
structural steel members and may be shop welded to the structural steel. 
 
2. 
The side sections (vertical sections) shall be assembled to the horizontal 
sections with the guard manufacturer supplied 8 gage steel spiral coil, 
folded over and sent to the job site. 
 
3. 
In the field, the side panels shall be vertically secured to the hangers with 
the guard manufacturer supplied 8 gage wire or clips and fasteners. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 65 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.4.8.5 
All ends and/or edges of guarding shall rest upon and be field wired to a 
structural support member. Falling parts guard shall have L 4 x 3 x ¼ (LLV) 
running angles (minimum) on the outside perimeter of the guard panels. 
9.4.8.6 
As shown in Figure No. 9.4.7.6, falling parts guard sides shall be a minimum of 
2'-0" (610 mm) high from the bottom of the guard for overhead trolley conveyors, 
and 3'-6" (1067 mm) high for inverted trolley and floor-type conveyors, with 
running L 2½ x 2½ x ¼ angles 3'-6" (1067 mm) up from the bottom of the guard. 
9.4.8.7 
The minimum allowable clear headroom dimension from the bottom of the guard 
supports to the finished floor shall be 6'-8" (2032 mm) unless otherwise specified 
or approved. Refer to GVOSS paragraph 9.26.1.6 for minimum aisle clearance 
specifications. 
9.4.8.8 
Trolley and floor type conveyor falling parts guard shall be installed with a 
minimum or 24" (610 mm) clearance on both sides of the load, and a minimum of 
6" (152 mm) clearance under the load.

9.4.8.9 
Step treads with a non-slip surface, angle L 6 x 4 LLH x 18" (457 mm) long, 
minimum, shall be provided on 15" centers on all sloped guards on the side with 
the access walkway, to permit passage within the guard, clear of the load.  
Access ladders shall be provided at all vertical curves, on the side with the 
access walkway, where the screen guarding does not extend to the floor to 
permit easy access into the guard. 
9.4.8.10 
The installation of the falling parts guards and its support steel shall be such that 
the guards shall be capable of supporting the maximum part load, plus the 
maximum carrier load, plus the impact load of the falling part and carrier, plus the 
load of two maintenance personnel. The maximum carrier load may be excluded 
from this calculation where carrier load bar secondary restraints are provided and 
the secondary restraint “fall distance” is less than the clear distance between the 
bottom of the carrier and the top of the falling parts guard. 
9.4.8.11 
A 6" (152 mm) high vertical member shall be provided at the bottom of the open-
ended sloped sections, full width of the opening, with 6" (152 mm) high stock 
retainers on 4'-0" (1220 mm) centers, maximum, to retain fallen parts. 
9.4.8.12 
Guard panels shall not exceed 32 square feet (3 square meters) without 
structural steel support members. Guard hangers shall be spaced at 8'-0" (2438 
mm) maximum centers and shall be hung from superstructure headers.  All guard 
hangers and support steel shall be bolted construction. 
9.4.8.13 
A sheet metal pan shall be provided in the screen guard on all conveyors where 
loose chains or parts may hang up on the guard. This pan must be lapped in the 
2" x 4" Springport

direction of travel and shall not extend into the access way.  For example, E-coat 
conveyors with hang chains shall have sheet metal pans with 2" (50 mm) sides 
the entire length of the return to prevent the hang up of cables. 
9.4.8.14 
All control devices (pneumatic valve boards, etc.) in the screen guard shall be 
located on the side of the screen guard with 24" (610 mm) minimum clearance. 
Control devices shall not be located in areas of potential pinch points due to part 
or carrier travel, chording, etc. 
9.4.8.15 
The layout of screen guard shall be designed for ease of mobility between runs 
of screen guard and platforms. Connections between parallel runs and 
connections to drive, take-up and control panel platforms shall be provided to 
minimize the need for ladder access and excessive travel time through the 
guarding. 
9.4.8.16 
Stairs shall be located such that carriers do not accumulate at the entrance to the 
screen guard or service platform. 
9.4.8.17 
Traction wheels that are directly over the plant floor, over the regular 
maintenance pathways and over the repair spurs must be guarded with a "wheel 
catcher" frame.

9.4.9 
Pits 
9.4.9.1 
All open pits shall have a 18" (457 mm) wide yellow stripe around the entire 
perimeter.  
9.4.9.1.1 
Pit depths 48” or greater shall have an engineered safety system (i.e. safety 
netting) across the width of the pit as a means for fall protection during periods of 
planned downtime/non-usage/bypass. 
9.4.9.1.2 
Pits shall be identified with a 10x14 in “Danger OPEN PIT” signage. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
9.4.9.2 
Two Strand Conveyors 
9.4.9.2.1 
As shown in Figure No. 9.4.9.2, all center pits in Flat Tops and Two-Strand 
conveyors shall be protected with a presence sensing device, or "safety mat”, in 
compliance with GMS w-EL4 Light Curtains and Presence Sensing Devices for 
Machine Safeguarding. 
 
1. 
The mats shall be placed from the entrance of the pit to 6 feet (1828 mm) 
back. 
 
2. 
The minimum length of the mat is 6 feet (1828 mm) and the width shall be 
determined by the spacing between the conveyor strands. 
 
3. 
The width needs to cover the entire area between the strands. 
 
3. 
A local reset control operator station shall be provided in the vicinity of the 
safety mat but far enough that it can't be reached by a person standing on 
the mat. 
9.4.9.2.2 
Flat Top Conveyors 
1. The size of the safety mat shall be determined from the pit edge minus the 
tire guide lead in length to adhere to the 6 ft (1828 mm) overall length 
standard. See Fig 9.4.9.2 (b) 
2. The minimum width of the safety mat shall be determined by the spacing 
between the conveyor strands. 
3. A local reset control operator station shall be provided in the vicinity of the 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 69 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
safety mat but far enough that it can't be reached by a person standing on the 
mat. 
 
9.4.9.3 
All open pits in the non-work zones are to be guarded with guard railing. 
 
1. 
Hand railing capable of withstanding a 200 lb. (91 kg) horizontal force shall 
be provided around the pits where there is no Powered Material Handling 
Vehicle (PMHV) traffic. 
A. 
The overall height of the railing shall be 42 inches (1066 mm). 
B. 
The lower rail shall be located 21" (533 mm) above the floor. 
C. 
A 4" (100 mm) high kick plate shall be provided. 
 
2. 
Along aisles with PMVH traffic, the guard rail shall be constructed from 
channel or highway guarding with 8" (200 mm) diameter embedded posts at 
10 foot (3048 mm) spacing, maximum. 
A. 
Two (2) strands of 6" (152 mm) removable rails shall be used. 
B. 
The overall height of the railing shall be 42 inches (1066 mm). 
C. 
The lower rail shall be located 21" (533 mm) above the floor. 
D. 
A 4" (100 mm) high kick plate shall be provided. 
 
9.4.10 
Installation of Equipment below Floor Surface 
9.4.10.1 
All machinery and control devices installed below the floor surface, that are not 
flush with the plant floor, shall be provided with a painted yellow all around the 
opening.  The width of the paint stripe around the opening will be 6" (152 mm).

9.4.11 
Gravity Control Devices 
9.4.11.1 
All lifting devices shall be designed with a mechanical means of locking the load 
– e.g., lift, carriage, counterweight, etc. – when in a raised position to ensure safe 
access to areas under the elevated portion of the equipment and parts.  This may 
include, but is not limited to, safety pins, safety pawls, safety posts, etc. 
9.4.11.2 
All new mechanical gravity control devices – i.e., blocks, posts, pins and 
receivers, etc. – shall comply with GVOSS section 1.3.13 unless noted otherwise 
within this GVOSS specification Section 9. 
9.4.11.3 
Maintenance personnel must be capable of installing or engaging the locking or 
support devices without exposing any part of their body to physical harm from a 
falling load. 
9.4.11.4 
The mechanical device or lock must be stored and securely attached to the lift or 
lift area, to ensure its availability when required. 
 
1. 
The mechanical device or lock must be electrically detected and interlocked 
to the auto/manual circuit while in the stored location. The function of the 
interlock is to eliminate the potential hazards created by attempting to 
operate the equipment while locked in position and to ensure its availability 
when required. 
 
2. 
The safety interlock and detection device shall conform to the requirements 
described in GVOSS section 1.3.13 and applicable sections of the Ford 
Motor Company Global Manufacturing Specifications.   
 
3. 
Each gravity pin location in the storage stand shall be labeled with a 1” x 2”  
lamicoid tag, white with black letters, as illustrated in Figure No. 9.4.11.4. 
 
 
 
 
           Figure No. 9.4.11.4 – Example of VDL Gravity Pin Storage Tags 
 
9.4.11.5 
On legacy lift table designs that use an air cylinder actuated transfer frame to 
provide the 4" (100 mm) lift, the locking pin must be connected to the lift frame 
using a plastic-coated galvanized steel cable. Interlocking of the locking pin on 4" 
(100 mm) pneumatic lift table is not required. 
9.4.11.6 
The mechanical device must be labeled in accordance with the lockout/tagout 
procedure and requirements. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 72 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.4.12 
Incline Safety Devices – Anti-Backups 
9.4.12.1 
Conveyor chain incline safety devices shall be provided on the power rail of all 
inclines on monorail conveyors with a 4'-0" (1220 mm) or greater change in 
elevation to prevent the conveyor chain from backing up due to chain breakage. 
9.4.12.2 
Carrier incline safety devices shall be provided on the free rail of power and free 
conveyors with a 2’-0” (610 mm) or greater change in elevation to prevent 
carriers from backing up due to chain breakage or disengagement from the 
chain. 
9.4.12.3 
For elevation changes from 4'-0" (1220 mm) to 5'-0" (1524 mm) for monorail 
conveyors and 2’-0” (610 mm) to 5’-0” (1524 mm) for power and free conveyors, 
one incline safety device is required. 
 
1. 
For each additional 5'-0" (1524 mm) increment of elevation change, one 
additional incline safety device is required. 
 
2. 
Example: 
 
Elevation Change 
 
Minimum Devices Required 
 
Greater than 5'-0" to 10'-0" 
two (2) incline safeties 
 
Greater than 10'-0" to 15'-0" 
three (3) incline safeties 
 
Greater than 15'-0" to 20'-0" 
four (4) incline safeties 
9.4.12.4 
Conveyor chain incline safety devices shall be located on the straight, between 
the upper and lower curves approximately 2’-0 from the lower curve tangent 
point. 
 
1. 
When a second safety device is required, it shall be installed 
approximately 3’-0” (914 mm) measured vertically from the first safety 
stop. 
 
2. 
Additional devices shall be located on 3’-0” (914 mm) centers as measured 
vertically. 
 
3. 
For carrier incline safety devices used on power and free conveyors, the 
spacing and quantity of the incline safety devices shall prevent the run-way 
carrier from contacting the carrier behind it. 
9.4.12.5 
Incline safety devices shall be designed and fitted with bumpers made of sound-
deadening and wear resistant material, to minimize noise. 
9.4.12.6 
Incline safety devices shall never be reused, relocated, or reworked. 
9.4.12.7 
Overhead conveyor chain incline safety devices shall conform to Branch Drawing 
3ZF-20708, sheet No. 19 (for 4-inch) and sheet No. 22 (for 6-inch). 
 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 73 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.4.13 
Decline Safety Devices – Anti-Runaways 
9.4.13.1 
Conveyor chain decline safety devices shall be provided on all monorail declines 
with a 4'-0" (1220 mm) or greater change in elevation to prevent the conveyor 
chain from running away due to chain breakage. 
 
1. 
Each decline safety device (boat anchor) shall be equipped with a limit 
switch, set to trip when the boat anchor flips over by the chain moving faster 
than the normal operating speed. 
 
2. 
A reset shall be located at a safe location within sight of the decline safety 
device. 
3. 
For elevation changes from 4'-0" (1220 mm) to 5'-0" (1524 mm), one decline 
safety device is required. 
 
4. 
For each additional 5'-0" (1524 mm) increment of elevation change, one 
additional decline safety device is required. 
 
5. 
Example: 
Elevation Change 
 
Minimum Devices Required 
Greater than 5'-0" to 10'-0"  
two (2) decline safeties  
Greater than 10'-0" to 15'-0"  
three (3) decline safeties 
Greater than 15'-0" to 20'-0"  
four (4) decline safeties 
 
9.4.13.2 
Conveyor chain decline safety devices shall be located on the straight, between 
the upper and lower curves approximately 2’-0 (610 mm) from the lower curve 
tangent point. 
 
1. 
When a second safety device is required, it shall be installed approximately 
3’-0” (914 mm) measured vertically from the first safety stop. 
 
2. 
Additional devices shall be located on 3’-0” (914 mm) centers as measured 
vertically.  
9.4.13.3 
Overhead conveyor chain downhill safety stop devices must conform to Branch 
Drawing 3ZF-20708, sheets No. 12 (for 4-inch) and sheet No. 15 (for 6-inch).  
9.4.13.4 
Decline safety devices shall be designed and fitted with bumpers made of sound-
deadening and wear resistant material, to minimize noise. 
9.4.13.5 
Decline safety devices shall never be reused, relocated or reworked. 
9.4.14 
Decline Safety Conveyors  
9.4.14.1 
Decline safety conveyors shall be provided on power and free conveyor declines 
with a 4’-0” (1220 mm) or greater change in elevation where production 
personnel are located near the bottom of the vertical curve such that the potential 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 74 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
for injury exists in the event of a run-away carrier. When decline safety conveyors 
are not required, with approval of the Ford VOME engineer, GVOSS specification 
9.4.3.5.1 must be met. The decline safety conveyor shall prevent any overspeed 
run-away motion of the carriers due to chain breakage or disengagement from 
the chain. Decline safety conveyors may be closed loop (aka “merry-go-round”) 
or single-strand (aka, “mouse trap”) chain configuration. 
9.4.14.2 
Decline safety conveyors are to be installed adjacent to the conveyor free track.  
They are to be designed to engage a carrier load trolley or the carrier prior to the 
load reaching the upper tangent of the curve and shall remain engaged to the 
load trolley/carrier until the carrier load trolleys have made the complete 
transition past the tangent of the lower curve. 
9.4.14.3 
Decline safety conveyors are to utilize the identical I-beam as is used on the 
conveyor system except chain trolleys shall be provided on 1'-0" (305 mm) 
centers, maximum. The maximum allowable centers for anti-runaway "flip dogs” 
shall be 2'-0" (610 mm). 
9.4.14.4 
Decline safety conveyors shall be designed such that in the event a carrier is 
lost, it cannot contact the carrier in front of it. 
9.4.14.5 
A keyed reset shall be located at a safe location within sight of the decline safety 
conveyor. 
9.4.14.6 
Decline safety conveyors shall be designed with wear resistant lateral guides for 
the chain and anti-runaway dogs on the load side. 
9.4.14.7 
When sizing the brake or pneumatic stopping device, the Contractor shall 
consider the maximum number of carriers that could be on the decline, the 
weight of the moving load per carrier and the speed of the moving load when 
calculating the imposed stopping force; i.e., the decline safety conveyor shall be 
sized sufficient to hold the load of all carriers that could potentially be on the 
decline vertical curve. 
9.4.14.8 
Continuous loop decline safety conveyors: 
 
1. 
Continuous loop decline safety conveyors shall be designed in such a way 
that, under normal operations, the carriers coming down the decline will 
push the safety conveyor chain. 
 
2. 
Continuous loop decline safety conveyors shall be equipped with an air 
release/spring set "fail-safe" caliper disc brake. 
A. 
Application of braking pressure shall be by means of spring closure. 
B. 
Brakes are to be normally held open by means of a plant air supply 
feed. The normal operating air pressure is 40 to 60 psi. 
C. 
Brakes are normally locked when the power and free conveyor is not 
running, preventing carrier loadings from driving the safety conveyor. 
D.    Continuous loop decline safety conveyor brakes shall be equipped with 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 75 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
quick exhaust valves and silencers on the brake actuator ports. 
 
3. 
Continuous loop decline safety conveyors are to be equipped with an 
overspeed switch, set to trip at a speed slightly higher than the power and 
free operating speed. 
A. 
The speed sensor adjustment controls are to be located inside the 
conveyor control panel located near the brake or stopping pneumatic 
device. 
B. 
When the power and free conveyor is running and overspeed is 
sensed due to either power chain breakage or carrier runaway, the 
continuous loop decline safety conveyor brake is to automatically set, 
causing the power and free carriers on the decline to come to a safe 
stop and shall automatically stop the power and free conveyor drives.  
A visual indication of the fault is to be provided. 
C. 
For continuous loop, or "merry-go-round", style decline safety 
conveyors, the overspeed switch shall have a digital display for ease of 
trip speed setting. The switch setting functions shall have medium 
resolution on the setting dials for ease of adjustment. 
 
D.    Continuous loop, or "merry-go-round", style decline safety conveyors               
                shall be designed with a screw take-up assembly. 
 
9.4.14.9 
Single-strand, “mouse trap” type, decline safety conveyors: 
 
1. 
Single-strand “mouse trap” type decline safety conveyors shall be equipped 
with a pneumatic shock absorbing device located at the top of the vertical 
curve. 
 
2. 
A manual winch system shall be provided to aid system reset following 
activation by providing a means to relieve pressure on the stop blocks that 
are engaged with a carrier. 
 
3. 
An over-speed switch is not used on "mouse trap" style decline safety 
conveyors. 
 
4.     Single strand decline safety conveyors shall be equipped with quick exhaust 
valves and silencers on the cylinder actuator ports. 
 
9.4.15 
Carriers – Design Criteria 
9.4.15.1 
The following design criteria shall be considered minimum safety requirements.  
Adequate safety factoring must be considered for static, dynamic and shock 
loading with the understanding that potential life exposure risk to employees 
working under suspended equipment must be zero. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 76 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.4.15.2 
Primary support systems (carriers) must have a minimum safety factor of 5:1 and 
have secondary safety restraints meeting the criteria described in FAS08-145. 
9.4.15.3 
A Finite Element Analysis (FEA) shall be completed for all new carriers and 
carriers reworked for added product weight, a change in weight distribution or a 
change to the part position on the carrier; e.g., change in center-of-gravity. 
 
1. 
Calculations and FEAs are to be completed by a qualified structural and 
equipment design engineer, appraised of the life exposure risk. 
 
2. 
All design calculations and criteria are to be submitted for review with the 
carrier design drawings and shall be certified, stamped and dated by the 
structural licensed engineering. 
9.4.15.4 
Carriers are to be designed to ensure load containment considering unit 
assembly process, inclines/declines, loading/unloading, fatigue and carrier 
opening potential.  This information shall be documented on the carrier layout. 
9.4.15.5 
The Carriers shall be dynamically tested to demonstrate that the load will stay 
on/within the holding details and shall not create a safety hazard in case a carrier 
de-dogs on an incline/decline vertical curve, goes into a runaway condition and 
comes to a sudden stop when engaging the anti-runaway/-backup device. If 
through physical testing, it is determined that it is impossible, or impractical, to 
contain the load on the carrier in a runaway, GVOSS specification 9.4.3.5.1 must 
be met for a minimum distance of one full station length from the lower vertical 
curve tangent point, except that floor shall be painted red within the danger zone, 
with the approval of the Ford VOME engineer.  The following test criteria shall 
apply: 
 
1. 
The test shall be conducted using the grade and length of track comparable 
to the area that poses the greatest risk potential in the system through 
which carrier shall operate. 
 
2. 
The carrier shall roll freely for the actual runaway distance that can be 
realized in the plant.  The runaway test shall simulate the distance to a 
safety device engagement. 
 
3. 
The results of the test shall be documented on the Carrier Design Drawings. 
 
4. 
The following conditions shall require a run-away test: 
A. 
When a new carrier is built for a new model program/product.  
B. 
When there is a change the incline/decline vertical curve of the 
conveyor. i.e., they are made steeper than that for which the carrier 
was originally designed and tested. 
C. 
When a new product/process is to be included in an existing carrier 
with a different load distribution than the original product for which the 
carrier was designed and tested. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 77 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
D. 
When there is a change to the existing part carrying detail; i.e., the 
supporting points and/or locating pins are moved forward or aft of the 
original design resulting in a potential change to the center of gravity. 
E. 
When the part weight increases above that for which the original 
carrier was designed. 
9.4.15.6 
All welds are to be performed by certified welders only and in compliance with 
AWS specifications, latest revision. 
9.4.15.7 
Fabrication of structural members shall be in accordance with applicable AISC 
requirements. 
 
1. 
All structural members shall be ASTM A36, unless otherwise specified. 
 
2. 
Butt welded connections will not be accepted. 
 
3. 
Use full gusset or stiff back plates at member joints, where required 
9.4.15.8 
The working load limit (safe load strength) is arrived at by dividing the breaking 
strength (ultimate strength) by a specified safety factor. The magnitude of this 
factor depends on how great the cost of failure will be in terms of life, 
seriousness of injury or damage to property. 
9.4.15.9 
The factor of safety is the ratio of the ultimate breaking strength of a member or 
piece of material to the actual working stress or maximum permissible safe load 
stress when in use. 
9.4.15.10 
The following parts are to be individually tested:      
 
1. 
Carrier 
 
2. 
King pin or load pin and attachment 
 
3. 
Load trolley assemblies 
 
4. 
Secondary safety restraint and attachment points 
9.4.15.11 
The above components shall also be tested as an assembly. 
9.4.15.12 
All weld joints on a random 10 percent of the carriers shall be magnafluxed or 
ultrasonically tested prior to delivery to the site. This testing shall be completed 
by a qualified testing laboratory. The testing laboratory shall provide detailed test 
reports to the VO and VO Plant Engineering.  
9.4.15.13 
All (100%) of the king pins (load pins) shall be magnafluxed by a qualified testing 
laboratory. The testing laboratory shall provide detailed test reports to the VO 
and VO Plant Engineering.  

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 78 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.4.15.14 
The Contractor shall perform a Failure Mode Effects Analysis (FMEA) study on 
the carrier design. 
9.4.15.15 
Chording studies shall be completed in the design/conveyor layout phase.  
Vehicle chording for horizontal or vertical curves shall not begin before the entire 
vehicle has entered/left the first/last workstation of a line.  The expectation shall 
be that all workstations identified on the layout present the product flat, straight, 
stable and at production speed. 
9.4.15.16 
When production personnel must access product while the product is held in a 
carrier an Ergonomic study shall be completed per GVOSS Section 1.6. 
Clamshell Carriers shall be designed to allow operator access to the vehicle in an 
ergonomic stress-free manner. The clamshell carrier designer should consider 
the following design principles:   
9.4.15.16.1 The clamshell carrier arm cross braces, stiffeners and gussets, etc. should be 
tucked underneath the vehicle to the greatest extent possible. The intent is to 
allow the operator to stand as close as possible to the vehicle to minimize the 
horizontal reach required to complete the work. i.e., the distance from the outside 
of the clamshell carrier to the outside of the vehicle bodyside should be not more 
than zero inches.

9.5 
CONVEYOR AGV/AGC MECHANICAL DESIGN 
9.5.1 
General 
9.5.1.1 
All calculations and analyses including, but not limited to, loading, stress, strain, 
deflection, cycle time, life cycle cost, etc., shall adhere to the appropriate GVOSS 
and/or GMS specifications. 
9.5.1.2 
Calculations for bearings, couplings, shafts, conveyor/lift belts and chains, load 
bearing wheels, gearmotors and VFD, etc. sizing and selection shall be provided 
by the Conveyor Supplier and shall be reviewed/approved by the Owner at the 
design review meeting prior to placing purchase orders for these commodities. 
9.5.1.3 
Appropriate service and impact factors shall be applied in all engineering 
calculations compliant with commonly accepted industry standards and as 
defined within these specifications. The service and impact factors shall be 
reviewed and approved by the Owner at the design review meeting. 
9.5.1.4 
Information related to non-standard purchased components such as calculations 
for part sizing and selection, material properties and qualities, performance and 
R&M data, and dimensional tolerances shall be provided by the Conveyor 
Supplier and shall be available for review/approval by the Owner at the 
engineering design review. 
9.5.1.5 
All conveyors AGV/AGCs used for production operations shall have removable 
cover plates between the conveying chain(s), belt(s) and/or rollers. Production 
conveyors shall have full height side skirts designed to provide a smooth/flush 
surface to eliminate potential pinch points. Special consideration shall be given to 
areas where transitions to/from various work levels and/or personnel access 
ramps are present. Side skirts are not required on the conveyor where operator 
platforms are located adjacent to the conveyor and the platform height is equal to 
the top-of-chain/belt or top-of-roller. 
9.5.1.6 
Each conveyor AGV/AGC and related equipment or machinery item shall be 
uniquely and clearly identified as follows: 
 
1. 
The identification label shall be visible without removing guards and while 
guards are open or off. It shall be attached to a fixed portion of the 
equipment. Labels shall not be attached to motors, reducers, guards or any 
piece of the equipment that can be removed or replaced during 
maintenance. 
 
2. 
The identification label shall be located such that it is visible from the 
direction of the control panel, lockout valve, mechanical safety post 
installation point, etc. This may require more than one identification label 
per piece of equipment. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 81 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3. 
Labels shall be white background with black letters. Lettering shall be a 
minimum 1 inch (25 mm) in height. 
9.5.1.7 
The Conveyor AGV/AGC Supplier is responsible for providing MTBF and MTTR 
for each unique conveyor design or non-standard component proposed.  This 
data must be submitted with the bid.  Proof of the MTTR values may be 
requested. 
9.5.1.8 
Finite Element Analysis (FEA) 
9.5.1.8.1 
Where FEA is required within this VOSS specification “SECTION 9 – FACILITY 
CONVEYORS” and/or the project bid specification, the FEA and final report shall 
comply with the following specification requirements. 
9.5.1.8.2 
The FEA for facility conveyors and related components shall include static, 
dynamic and impact loading conditions e.g., static stresses of a loaded P&F 
carrier traveling up/down a vertical curve, stresses due to gravity on a VDL 
structure or carriage weldment when accelerating to steady-state speed, or the 
impact forces imposed on a lift-table or transfer/secondary lift-frame when raising 
from “clear position” to “load” position, etc. 
9.5.1.8.3 
The FEA Factor of Safety (FoS) for critical machine elements subjected to static 
loading, only, shall comprehend: 
(A) Maximum stress based on the FEA derived Von Mises stress, 
(B) For ductile materials, such as steel and aluminum alloys, the FOS is equal 
to the material Yield Strength divided by the maximum FEA derived Von 
Mises stress, and 
(C) For brittle materials, such as cast iron, the FOS is equal to the material 
Ultimate Tensile Strength divided by the FEA derived Von Mises stress. 
9.5.1.8.4 
The FEA Factor of Safety (FoS) and endurance limit (Se) for critical machine 
elements subjected to reversing and/or fluctuating cyclical loading shall 
comprehend: 
 (A) Maximum stress based on the FEA derived Von Mises stress, 
(B) Machine element endurance limit based on the Marin formula for 
influence of machine element variations (e.g., Se = ka kb kc kd ke S’e), 
(C) Life expectancy based on “infinite cycles”, or 1 x 10^9 cycles, for the 
machine element in question, and 
(D) Minimum 2.5 FoS calculation based on modified-Goodman criteria. 
9.5.1.8.5 
The FEA report units of measure for dimensions, forces, stresses, pressures, etc. 
shall be defined in both imperial (primary) and metric (secondary) systems. 
9.5.1.8.6 
The FEA final report shall include shop detail drawings and related fabrication 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 82 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
process documentation sufficient to validate the actual part detail is built in 
compliance with the FEA modeling assumptions. The level of detail provided 
shall include fabrication/assembly instructions and design notes relative to the 
assembly of joints where the connections are assumed “100% effective”; e.g., 
weld type, size and length, weldment stress relief, fastener joint design and 
resulting torque requirements, etc. 
9.5.1.8.7 
The FEA report shall be provided for review and approval at the design review 
meeting. The final approved FEA report shall be submitted with the applicable 
as-built drawing(s) as part of the EIS as-built mechanical drawings deliverable 
(re: VOSS section 1.5 Equipment Documentation). 
9.5.1.8.8 
The FEA final report shall include: 
(A) Report title page, 
(B) Model input data page(s), 
(C) Model loading and constraint page(s), 
(D) Resulting stress contour page(s) 
(E) Resulting deflection contour results page(s), 
(F) Resulting calculations page(s), 
(G) FEA summary page(s), and 
(H) Next steps page(s), if applicable. 
9.5.1.8.9 
The TITLE PAGE shall include: 
(A) The name of the applicable Ford new model program and/or the FEA 
project description, 
(B) The assembly/manufacturing plant name, 
(C) The assembly plant manufacturing area (e.g., Body Shop, Paint Shop or 
Trim/Chassis/Final, etc.), 
(D) The conveyor system and/or part being analyzed, 
(E) The FEA report author name, and 
(F) The date of submittal of the FEA report. 
9.5.1.8.10 
The model INPUT DATA page(s) shall include: 
(A) Identification of the shop drawing title(s), drawing number(s) and release 
or revision level that matches the FEA model for the part in question at the 
time the FEA is submitted, 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 83 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
(B) Sketches of the part to be analyzed, 2D and/or 3D, as necessary to 
comprehend the critical part geometry, 
(C) A listing of the material properties for all materials included in the model; 
i.e., yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, Young’s modulus, Poisson’s 
ratio, mass density, etc., 
(D) A listing of all applicable conveyed product/part weights, 
(E) A listing of all applicable conveyor component weights, and 
(F) Identification of any other base FEA model assumptions: e.g., all welds 
assumed 100% effective, weight of a “given part” or component assumed 
applied at center-of-gravity, etc. 
9.5.1.8.11 
The model LOADING AND CONSTRAINT page(s) shall include: 
(A) A sub-title identifying the illustrated load condition: e.g., static, dynamic or 
jam condition, 
(B) A 3D sketch showing the point of load vector location(s) and boundary 
constraint(s) for all applicable degrees of freedom, 
(C) A brief description of the applied loads and boundary conditions as shown 
in the 3D sketch. 
9.5.1.8.12 
 The result STRESS CONTOUR page(s) shall include: 
A) A sub-title identifying the illustrated load condition: e.g., static, dynamic 
or jam condition. 
B) A 3D sketch showing the resulting stress element gradients in multi-
colored band-widths. 
C) A stress element legend identifying the values assigned to the multi-
colored band-widths. 
D) Separate text clearly denoting the maximum stress element value(s) 
and location(s). 
9.5.1.8.13 
The result DEFLECTION CONTOUR page(s) shall include: 
(A) A sub-title identifying the illustrated load condition: e.g., static, dynamic or 
jam condition, 
(B) A 3D sketch showing the resulting deflection gradients in multi-colored 
band-widths, 
(C) A deflection legend identifying the values assigned to the multi-colored 
band-widths, and 
(D) Separate text clearly denoting the maximum deflection value and location. 
   
 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.5.1.8.14 
The resulting CALCULATIONS page(s) shall include: 
(A) Determination of FOS based on resulting maximum (Von Mises) stress for 
all critical materials and load conditions, 
(B)  Determination of endurance strength calculation based on the Marin 
formula for influence of machine element variations (i.e., Se = ka kb kc kd ke 
S’e), and 
(C) Determination of life expectancy based on the calculated endurance 
strength of the critical materials. 
9.5.1.8.15 
The FEA SUMMARY page(s) shall include: 
(A)  A summary table showing the maximum normal stress(es), the maximum 
Von Mises stress(es), the calculated endurance limit(s), the calculated life 
expectancy, and the applicable FOS for all critical materials for all load 
conditions, and 
(B) A brief description of the worst-case load condition(s); i.e., the load 
condition (static, dynamic or jam condition), the maximum stress value(s), the 
location of maximum stress and whether the stress is acceptable or not. 
9.5.1.8.16 
The NEXT STEPS page(s) shall include applicable follow-up tasks to provide for 
implementation of the VOSS specification compliant design.

9.5.2.9 
All drive components, take-ups, ladders, catwalks and platforms shall be 
designed and detailed, and their locations and mark numbers must be shown on 
the area layout(s). 
9.5.2.10 
All overhead installations shall show and call out approximate locations of sway 
bracing in typical sections on the first sheet of each unit assembly drawing.

9.5.2.11 
All guards, guard posts, protective screens and aisle barriers shall be designed 
and detailed, and their locations and mark numbers must be shown on the area 
layout(s). 
9.5.2.12 
The conveyor Supplier fabrication drawings shall be dimensioned with the 
primary unit of measure as is commonly used at the Owner plant location. All 
drawings shall have complete title blocks. 
9.5.2.13 
The conveyor Supplier is required to have Team Center access to retrieve 
product/part(s) drawings and related files for design and build of new or reworked 
part carrier and/or part fixture details.  Supplier is also responsible for uploading 
the final as-built design of all carriers, tooling and perch/pin/nesting details that 
directly interface and contact the vehicle/product to the Team Center site when 
the work is completed. 
9.5.3 
Gearmotors and Power Transmission Components 
9.5.3.1 
This Section is to be used in conjunction with applicable sections of the following 
GVOSS specifications. This Section shall supersede any conflicts between this 
Section and those listed below. 
 
1. 
GVOSS Section 3.2 Typical Machine Components  
 
2. 
GVOSS Section 3.3 Motors, Gear Motors and Speed Reducers 
9.5.3.2 
The required gearmotor(s) for standard conveyor applications shall be as 
identified in VOME Conveyor Motor List. The Contractor is responsible to verify 
the gearmotor selection during engineering and prior to ordering these parts for 
fabrication. 
9.5.3.3 
The conveyor AGV/AGC drive assembly – including the motor, reducer, 
gearmotor and associated power transmission components – shall be sized 
considering a 1.3 (minimum) service factor applied to the driven load and must 
be capable of withstanding the start-up torque caused by the inrush current. As a 
minimum, the drive assembly must be rated for momentary full load amps equal 
to 2.5 times the motor nameplate rating. 
9.5.3.4 
Variable frequency motors shall be sized to provide the required torque, including 
service factor, for all speeds included within the duty cycle. 
9.5.3.5 
All transmission components, including but not limited to the drive chain/belt, the 
conveyor chain/belt, the conveyance framework, bearings, keys, support legs, 
etc. shall be sized considering a 1.3 (minimum) service factor applied to the 
driven load and shall be capable of withstanding, without failure, the forces 
applied by the drive train when the gearmotor is in an E-stop or jam condition. 
9.5.3.6 
The design requirements for all drivetrain components shall comprehend E-stop 
conditions equal to fifteen (15) times per shift wherever light screens, safety mats 
or similar personnel intrusion devices are used. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 89 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.5.3.7 
The motor/gearbox, gearmotor and brake manufacturer must review and approve 
the component calculations and final selection recommendation(s). The 
manufacturer approval documentation shall be submitted to the Owner at the 
engineering design review meeting. 
9.5.3.8 
Gearbox, motor and gearmotor sizing calculations and selection criteria, 
including Supplier validation – e.g., SEW-Eurodrive Workbench study -- shall be 
provided and reviewed/approved at the design review meeting. 
9.5.3.9 
The Contractor shall construct a "Motor and Speed Reducer Use and Ordering 
Information" data sheet for each application as described in GVOSS paragraph 
1.2.8.6.4. 
9.5.3.10 
The power transmission chain, belt and sprocket Supplier must review and 
approve the component calculations and final selection recommendation(s) for all 
non-standard applications. The Supplier approval documentation shall be 
submitted to the Owner at the engineering design review meeting. 
9.5.3.11 
Needle bearings are prohibited for use in facility conveyor applications except as 
identified in GVOSS paragraph 3.2.7.8.1 and section 9.24. All facility conveyor 
needle bearing applications shall be reviewed during the engineering design 
review meetings. Refer to GVOSS section 3.2.7.8 for additional needle bearing 
design specifications. 
9.5.4 
Bolted Connections for Conveyors and AGV/AGCs 
9.5.4.1 
This Section is to be used in conjunction with the following GVOSS 
specifications. This Section shall supersede any conflicts between this Section 
and those listed below. 
1. 
GVOSS Section 3.1 Screws, Dowels and Washers Specification for 
Installation of Threaded Fasteners. 
2. 
GVOSS Section 9.26.7 Bolted Connections (for conveyor structural support 
steel). 
9.5.4.2 
Shop assembly fasteners shall be verified for proper torque at final assembly and 
shall be match marked in compliance with NAAMS standard for fasteners found 
in Chapter F, "Fastener Components". 
9.5.4.3 
All holes shall be drilled, punched or cut with appropriate tools. Burning holes in 
the shop or in the field will not be permitted. 
9.5.4.4 
All slotted connections or field adjustable connections shall be field welded or 
blocked after final alignment. 
9.5.4.5 
All nuts and/or bolt heads used in locations difficult to reach with a standard 
wrench after equipment is assembled shall be welded to the inaccessible side of 
the framing member to which the floor plates, leveling plates, wear bars, adapter 
plates, etc. are to be attached. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
Page 90 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.5.4.6 
Bolt threads shall have a minimum 1.5 times bolt diameter thread engagement in 
threaded holes and tapping plates. 
9.5.4.7 
Tapping plates shall be designed to eliminate the potential for the fastener to 
"bottom out" in the blind hole during assembly.  
9.5.4.8 
Self-tapping sheet metal screws are not allowed for any Final Assembly conveyor 
or conveyor related equipment application e.g., power transmission guards, 
conveyor side skirts, etc. 
9.5.5 
Welded Connections for Conveyors and AGV/AGCs 
9.5.5.1 
All shop and field welded connections shall be performed by a certified welder 
and shall be designed and fabricated in compliance with American Welding 
Society AWS D1.1 and Canadian Standard CSA W47.1, current edition. 
9.5.5.2 
Butt-welded joints for conveyor components, frame members and/or structural 
support connections are prohibited. 
9.5.5.3 
The size and length of all fillet welds shall be proportioned to the material 
thickness with design stresses as indicated per AISC/CISC specifications for 
steel construction. 
 
1. 
The minimum fillet weld size shall be 3/16", unless otherwise noted in these 
specifications and/or applicable Branch Standard drawings. 
 
2. 
All welds shall be based on E7018 Low Hydrogen Electrode, unless 
otherwise noted in these specifications and/or applicable Branch Standard 
drawings. 
9.5.5.4 
Splice plates for load bearing joints shall be as shown in Figure No. 9.5.5.4. 
1. 
Use a minimum 3/16-inch fillet weld for a minimum ¼ inch thick splice plate. 
2. 
The fillet weld shall gap the splice a minimum 2 x the fillet weld size. 
3. 
Use a partial penetration groove weld all around the “miter” with a minimum 
50% effective throat.

Figure No. 9.5.5.4 – Splice Plate for Load Bearing Joint 
 
9.5.6 
Gravity Actuated Over-Travel (GAOT) Devices: 
9.5.6.1 
All turntables, shuttle carts, side mast and 4-post vertical drop lifters and any 
conveyor equipment that becomes discontinuous due to a downstream conveyor 
that moves out of its feed position, shall include GAOT stops assembled to the 
adjacent fixed entering conveyors and the moving conveyor. 
9.5.6.2 
The entering conveyor GAOT shall be actuated by a cam mounted to the moving 
conveyor that pivots the "flop dog" to clear the skid/pallet/carrier during transfer. 
Likewise, the moving conveyor GAOT shall be actuated by a floor/mezzanine 
mounted cam bar that shall pivot the "flop dog" to the clear position for 
skid/pallet/carrier transfer. 
9.5.6.3 
The GAOT design for Turntables, Shuttles and VDLs shall utilize a common “flop 
dog” design as illustrated in the Branch Standard drawing 3ZF-5410-35456, 
sheet No. 26. 
9.5.6.4 
To minimize the stress at impact, the GAOT cam actuators shall be designed 
such that the angle of incline of the cam is not more than 35-degrees from the 
horizontal plane as illustrated in the Branch Standard drawing 3ZF-5410-35456, 
sheet No. 26. Note that the cam final location(s) shall be determined at final 
installation. 
3/16 
¼” Splice Plate 
Gap (min.) 
2 x Fillet Weld

9.5.7 
Main and Standby Equipment Design 
9.5.7.1 
Main and Standby drives are required on the following conveyor types and 
systems: 
 
1. 
On all VLC’s and VDL’s in Body Shop, Paint Shop and Final Assembly Area 
 
2. 
All overhead or inverted P&F and OHM production and delivery conveyors 
in Body Shop, Paint Shop and Final Assembly Area 
 
3. 
On the delivery conveyor from Body Shop to the Paint Shop, if OHM or P&F 
is used, 
 
4. 
Phosphate conveyor, 
 
5. 
E-coat conveyor, 
 
6. 
On the delivery conveyor from Paint Shop to the Trim Shop, if OHM or P&F 
is used, 
 
7. 
All vehicle Transfer Lifts in Final Assembly. e.g., Skid to Skillet, Skillet to 
Clamshell, and Clamshell to Final Line, etc., 
 
8. 
For Skillet conveyor systems where the friction drives shall be designed for 
“drive drop out” capability, 
 
9. 
All Chassis Pedestal conveyors, 
 
10. All Flat-Top conveyors with Final Line operations, and 
 
11. On conveyors with inaccessible drives. (Owner review and approval for this 
situation will be required prior to design and installation.) 
9.5.7.2 
When standby equipment is supplied, it shall be designed and installed such that 
it can be easily and quickly engaged and/or disengaged. The Contractor shall 
include adequate controls to accomplish the engagement and disengagement. 
9.5.7.3 
Prior to acceptance of a system, the functionality of both the main and standby 
equipment shall be validated under production conditions. 
9.5.7.4 
Multiple drive OHM and P&F systems shall be designed such that the conveyor 
chain is capable of running if one drive drops out. Conveyors with high chain 
tension should be given special consideration, especially Phosphate and E-Coat 
conveyors. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

9.6.3 
Distributed Control Standard 
9.6.3.1 
The standard production and delivery conveyor distributed controls architecture 
is a modular design based primarily on the SEW Eurodrive system for 
decentralized motor controls as illustrated in Figure No. 9.6.3.1; i.e., the 
MoviMOT and MoviFIT motor control solution. 
9.6.3.2 
The MoviMOT/MoviFIT motor controls solution applies to motors up to and 
including 4.0kW (5.4HP). Motors greater than 4.0kW require alternative methods 
of control. e.g., MoviPRO, MoviDRIVE, PowerFlex 70, PowerFlex 755, motor 
starter, etc. The Contractor shall refer to the applicable 70ZF- controls design 
package to determine the required motor control method. 
9.6.3.3 
Where a standard design has not been developed for the specific conveyor 
application, the Contractor shall work with the responsible VO Controls Engineer 
to develop the motor control scheme and related controls architecture based on 
the standard designs included within this specification. 
9.6.3.4 
The Contractor is responsible to set the MoviMOT and MoviFIT dip switches as 
required for the specific application.

Control Panels 
9.6.4.1 
Reference to conveyor systems following Next-Gen controls see Section 8; 
systems following GVOSS controls see Section 14 for standard control panel 
designs shall apply to conveyor control system applications.following standard 
control panel designs shall apply to conveyor control system applications: 
9.6.4.2 
All necessary field rework for control panels will be completed by the Contractor 
and shall be managed as part of the FAR process. 
9.6.4.3 
The following abbreviations are used in this specification for the different types of 
control panels that make-up the standard conveyor "Distributed Control System": 
1. 
MP, Next-Gen controls style Central Processing Unit or GuardLogix 
Processor Panel 
2. 
CPD, Next-Gen controls style Control Power Distribution Panel 
3. 
PDP, Next-Gen controls style Power Distribution Panel  
4.

5. 
MC, Next-Gen controls style Distributed Motor Control Panel  
9.6.4.4 
The Contractor shall install the CPD, PDP and VDL, etc. power drops, complete 
with new bus plug and fuses. All power drops shall be sized for maximum bus 
plug capacity, not for the load of the control panel.

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.6.4.5 
The Contractor shall install the GVOSS controls panels or Next-Gen controls MC 
power feeds from the PDP and VDL, etc. panels.  
9.6.4.6 
All new GVOSS controls panels or Next-Gen controls MP, CPD, and PDP control 
panels shall be located on the same level for which the conveyors are to operate 
– i.e., plant floor mounted conveyor panels shall be located on the plant floor and 
mezzanine mounted conveyor panels shall be mezzanine mounted – unless 
otherwise noted in the project bid specification Detailed Equipment Description. 
9.6.4.7 
All new VDL, P&F and FCS panels shall be mounted at the floor level unless 
otherwise noted in the project bid specification Detailed Equipment Description. 
9.6.4.8 
Mezzanine and platform mounted control panel enclosures shall be oriented such 
that the maintenance technician has a clear view of the equipment being 
controlled. 
 
1. 
Control panel enclosures on the mezzanine/platform shall be located so that 
the top of the control cabinet is convenient to a truss bottom chord to 
facilitate conduit runs. 
 
2. 
The Contractor shall provide LED lighting under the platforms in compliance 
with FCS Section 26 51 00 Plant Lighting Systems. 
9.6.4.9 
Each conveyor controls system shall include one (1) CRW Workstation 
programming terminal (Next-Gen controls re: 70ZF-20013209-M or S); (GVOSS 
controls 70ZF-20022865 or GVOSS approved part number). VOME Control 
Engineer to approve which version to be implemented for the Program. 
9.6.4.10 
The Contractor shall provide the mounting stand for all control panels that require 
a stand. e.g., CPD, MP, PV, etc. The stands for the Next-Gen controls style 
operator runbar will be FTF; GVOSS controls style operator runbar will be FTS. 
9.6.4.11 
The Contractor is responsible for supplying all tags within their system. 
9.6.4.12 
The Contractor is responsible to supply generic arc flash labels in compliance 
with GVOSS 1.3.2.6. 
9.6.4.13 
The Contractor shall provide two (2) 4’-0” long LED fixtures at each control panel 
location. The fixtures shall include switch gear, shields, hangers, supports, and 
all other services required to provide a complete and functional system. The light 
fixtures shall be powered from the nearest lighting panels. 
9.6.4.14 
Control panel centers located at the plant floor level adjacent to material handling 
aisles, or other similar areas where the control panels are exposed to material 
handling traffic, shall be guarded by 42-inch (1066mm) high, 6-inch diameter 
posts with removable 6-inch (152mm) channel top and intermediate guard rails. 
The guard posts shall be anchored to the plant floor as illustrated in Figure No. 
9.6.3.13.

9.6.6 
Jam Protection 
9.6.6.1 
All non-reciprocating conveyor constant speed motors other than lift tables, 
power roll beds, power roll stands and other small horsepower motors shall be 
protected by an instantaneous current trip relay (ITR). 
9.6.6.2 
All jam circuit ITRs shall be initially set up at 150 percent of the motor Full Load 
Amperage (FLA) as listed on the motor nameplate. 
9.6.6.3 
When running during the checkout and dry cycling, a current meter shall be used 
to check the actual motor current draw. If the difference between the initial ITR 
current setting and 150 percent of the measured current draw is more than +5 
percent of the initial ITR setting, a correctly sized ITR is to be installed along with 
an engraved phenolic tag that contains the correct ITR setting. 
9.6.6.4 
The instantaneous current trip relay shall be sized to suit the normal running 
current of the motor. An electronic or PLC timer will be used to bypass the 
instantaneous current trip relay during the start cycle. 
9.6.6.5 
The jam protection can be developed inside the processor of a PLC type control 
panel. 
9.6.6.6 
An automatic reset shall be provided with all aisle crossing cross-transfers to 
reset the jam. This shall be adjustable from 1 to 20 seconds and shall have a 
maximum automatic reset of 1 time within a 60 second interval. 
9.6.6.7 
When the motor is controlled by a VFD, this jam protection function is to be set 
by the VFD. 
9.6.7 
Field Wiring 
9.6.7.1 
Conduit and wireway installations shall conform to NFPA 79. 
9.6.7.2 
Reference GVOSS section 8.3.3.6, 8.3.3.8, 8.3.3.13, 8.3.3.14, 8.3.3.22. 
9.6.7.3 
All applications  
9.6.7.4 
Only new material shall be used both in extensions to existing systems and in the 
installation of new or relocated equipment. 
9.6.7.5 
All wireway runs shall rise from the control panel to the truss level and run 
immediately above the bottom chord of trusses to a column closest to the 
equipment being wired. 
9.6.7.6 
The Contractor shall design all cable tray layouts and MoviFIT mounting 
methods. The cable tray and MoviFIT mounting designs shall be submitted for 
review and approval at the design review meeting. 
9.6.7.7 
All wiring for control devices must be terminated at terminal strips; i.e., no splices 
shall be allowed.

9.6.8 
Safety Devices 
9.6.8.1 
Reference to GVOSS Section 1.3.2.7.1 for each safety gate switch included in 
the conveyor system. 
9.6.8.2 
The roller bed that is feeding to, or removing from, a fenced-in piece of 
equipment cannot extend control to other motors. Additionally, this motor control 
shall be on dedicated 480vac and 24vdc trunk lines, separate from other units. 
Safety isolation is achieved by turning off the trunk lines thus inhibiting the 
conveyor operation when the fenced-in equipment has a gate open or a light 
screen violation. 
9.6.9 
Proximity and Limit Switches 
9.6.9.1 
In this section the term limit switch shall mean any type of switch, such as a 
proximity switch, a photoelectric switch, etc. 
9.6.9.2 
All limit switches, proximity switches, and photo switches, etc. located on the side 
of a conveyor shall be mounted on a Uni-Strut type structure backed by angle 
iron welded to the conveyor frame.  
9.6.9.3 
All limit switches, proximity switches, photo-eyes, etc., provided by and/or reused 
by the Contractor shall be positioned and guarded to eliminate damage by a unit 
or skid/pallet/carrier in transit or a unit that may be inadvertently miss-positioned 
during automatic or manual sequencing.  
9.6.9.4 
Unless otherwise noted in the controls design standards, all switches must be 
stationary with the actuator moving in lieu of the limit switch moving. 
9.6.9.5 
Wires shall be connected to limit switches and proximity switches using a M12 
type connector or liquid-tight flexible conduit, as directed by the unit specification.  
9.6.9.6 
On some limit switches, proximity switches, etc. a 90-degree cord will be required 
to prevent the cord from interfering with the skid or carrier. The use of 90-degree 
adapters will not be permitted.  
9.6.9.7 
All limit switches and proximity switches are to be mounted such that they are 
easily adjustable in the up/down, side/side and fore/aft directions. All switch 
bracket bolted connections shall use a minimum of two bolts. Use LOCTITE® 
THREADLOCKER BLUE 242® on all limit switch and proximity switch mounting 
brackets. 
9.6.9.8 
Proximity switches shall be used in place of limit switches to the greatest extent 
possible. Cube type proximity switches are preferred over barrel proxes. 
9.6.9.9 
Refer to the Turck Next Gen 3 Controls Design Guide for approved proximity 
switch design requirements. 
For Next-Gen Controls Architecture

When barrel proxes are approved for use, they shall be provided with serrated 
steel lock nuts on both sides of the mounting brackets to prevent the switch from 
turning.

9.6.9.11 
Barrel proxes used in Body Shop applications shall be weld field immunity type; 
i.e., the sensor shall be suitable for use on resistance welding machines. 
9.6.9.12 
Photocells may be used only as approved by the VO Controls Engineering 
Supervisor. If approved, photocells shall be the polarized light type service and 
shall be protected from damage by a tube enclosure rigidly attached to the 
conveyor. The reflector shall be protected from damage and shall be rigidly 
attached to the conveyor. 
9.6.9.13 
Where switches are used in "zone clear" logic a dual switch configuration shall 
be used to prevent inadvertent carrier release. 
9.6.9.14 
All retractable conveyor drives shall have in/out switches to detect if the drive is 
engaged or disengaged. These switches shall be located in the controls circuits 
for main and standby drives. 
9.6.9.15 
The OHM/P&F downhill vertical curve anti-runaway limit switch inputs shall be 
wired in series per decline. 
9.6.9.16 
When specified in the detailed equipment descriptions, "fault" or "overtravel" limit 
switches shall be provided for bridge cranes, hoists, etc. These limit switches 
shall be provided and installed where required to detect bridge or hoist positions 
which could cause an interference with a conveyor and/or part carrier. 
9.6.10 
Motor Disconnect Switches 
9.6.10.1 
All motors shall be equipped with a 3-pole lockable disconnect switch in the 
460vac circuit. Main and stand-by motors shall have individual local motor 
disconnects. Refer to Global Manufacturing Standard w-ES20 DISCONNECT 
SWITCHES 
AND 
OPERATING 
MECHANISMS 
for 
additional 
design 
specifications. 
9.6.10.2 
All 480v motor disconnect switches shall be positioned to permit complete 
access during normal production mode with clear line-of-sight to the associated 
motor(s). 
9.6.10.3 
The disconnect switch shall have a normally open auxiliary contact that will be 
used as an input to drop out the motor control circuit and for monitoring 
purposes. 
9.6.10.4 
Each disconnect switch shall have a permanent lamacoid identification tag 
clearly indicating the unit, or units, for which it controls.  
9.6.10.5 
All disconnect switch enclosures used on Water Test Flat-Top conveyors shall be 
constructed using SAE 316 stainless steel sheet metal.

maximum of 150 feet (45.7 meter) apart, both sides of the line, staggered 
on 75 feet (22.8 meter) centers.

9.6.11.7

work area. The exact height and location shall be determined by the 
application. e.g., plant conditions, platforms pits, material handling, etc. 
 
3. 
The length of each system shall be determined by the length of the work 
area and the limits of the pull cord system used. 
9.6.11.8 
Quality Assurance System (QAS) Run/Stop Systems: 
 
1. 
Where specified to be provided in the project bid specification Detailed 
Equipment Description section, a QAS (Quality Assurance System) shall be 
used on both sides of the production conveyor outside of the tool rail and 
tooling area. 
 
2. 
The standard QAS pushbutton elevation shall be 6'-6" (1980mm) to the 
lowest part of QAS pushbutton. The exact height and location shall be 
determined by the application. e.g., plant conditions, platforms pits, material 
handling, etc. 
 
3. 
The mounting hanger/stand shall be designed with 1’-0 (305mm) vertical 
adjustment.  
 
4. 
Each QAS pushbutton station shall have 3 pushbuttons. i.e., (1) Run/Stop, 
(2) Foreman Call, and (3) Material Call. 
 
5. 
Each QAS pushbutton station shall have 3 indicators lights. i.e., red to 
indicate “Stop”, (2) amber to indicate “Supervisor or process coach”, and (3) 
blue to indicate “Material”. 
 
6. 
The QAS CRS relays shall be used for QAS only. Do not wire with other 
Run/Stop stations to the QAS system relays. 
 
7. 
A second contact of each QAS Run/Stop pushbutton shall be run to the

9.6.11.9 
Conveyor System Emergency Stop Systems: (See Section 1) 
 
1. 
Emergency stop buttons shall be red, mushroom head, push-pull, and 
maintained contacts. The pushbutton will have to be pulled to the reset 
position, and then the E-stop reset button must be pressed at the nearest 
control panel location. 
 
2. 
Emergency stop systems shall be used on conveyor systems that are 
restricted and protected by safety perimeter fencing and/or light screens. 
e.g., drop lifters, automatic decking systems, and conveyors through tooling, 
etc. 
9.6.12 
Field Lights 
9.6.12.1 
All field mounted indicating lights shall be installed from the truss or building 
columns at an elevation of 11'-0" (3352 mm) above the operating floor and shall 
be located near the activating device or in an area that can be easily seen. 
Strobe lights will not be permitted.  
9.6.12.2 
All field mounted lights shall be hard wired or cabled, depending on location from 
the controlling device.  
9.6.12.3 
The Contractor shall provide one (1) field light per decline vertical curve that has 
one or more anti-runaway switches for all overhead monorail and P&F 
conveyors. On multi-floor declines, the Contractor shall provide one (1) field light 
at each floor level. 
9.6.12.4 
Unless otherwise specified, all covered conveyor related equipment pits with a 
depth of 4'-6" (1372 mm) or greater, shall be illuminated with a minimum of one 
(1) 4'-0" long LED fixture and equipped with a 115-vac outlet. An on-off switch for 
the pit lighting shall be mounted on the pit wall adjacent to the access ladder. All 
lights and conduits shall be wall mounted, not mounted to the pit cover plates. 
9.6.12.5 
Light fixtures and receptacles for pit lighting shall be per FCS Section 26 51 00 
Plant Lighting Systems. 
9.6.13 
Solenoids 
9.6.13.1 
All pneumatic directional valves shall be double solenoid.  Single solenoid valves 
shall be used for free trolley stops on P&F conveyors only.

9.6.14 
Controls for Reworked Conveyor Systems  
9.6.14.1 
The Contractor shall create a layout of all conveyors that are to be reworked 
showing the original locations of all pushbuttons, field lights, limit switches, prox 
switches, valve boards, etc. This layout shall be submitted to the Project 
Engineer before the rework starts. 
9.6.14.2 
For all rework to existing systems, the Contractor shall provide new or modify the 
existing control panel map layouts and remote graphic panel layouts to reflect 
modified conveyor path and device locations.  
9.6.14.3 
All field devices for relocated conveyor AGV/AGC systems shall be new.  
9.6.14.4 
The Contractor is responsible to relocate any control panels, graphic panels, 
operator stations, etc., as required for the conveyor AGV/AGC rework.  
9.6.14.5 
For all rework to existing systems, the Contractor shall provide all control system 
revisions, including control panels, field devices, identification tags, wiring and 
logic as necessary to provide compliance with the existing controls 
standards/architecture or otherwise specified in the project SOW. (Ref. 
2.10.1.11) 
9.6.14.6 
For all rework of existing control panels, the Contractor shall up-grade all 
components in the panel that are being reworked to current VOGO standards. 
The reworked and/or added wiring shall be re-dressed to present a clean and 
orderly panel. Buy off of the reworked panels shall be by the Project Engineer 
and the Plant Engineer.  
9.6.14.7 
After rework is complete, the Contractor shall check and repair all "Ground 
Faults" within the entire system, regardless of whatever the cause of the fault.  
9.6.14.8 
After rework is complete, the Contractor shall ensure the entire system operates 
in automatic mode to the satisfaction of the Project Engineer. If the Contractor 
feels the system is deficient, or automatic operation does not currently operate, 
then he shall identify these areas individually in his proposal. No consideration 
shall be made for relieving the Contractor of this responsibility, or awarding 
additional moneys to correct deficiencies, after submission of quote to Ford 
Purchasing.

Where trusses have been reworked, conduit, cable tray, wireway and other
