9.9 
DROP LIFTERS 
9.9.1 
General 
9.9.1.1 
Drop lifters are conveyors that provide a change in vertical direction.  Drop lifters 
may be fitted with power roll beds for pallet/skid system applications, EMS track 
or free track with indexers for P&F and friction drive trolley conveyor applications. 
Drop lifters may be side mast or 4-post configuration. 
9.9.2 
Drop Lifter Mechanical Safety Requirements 
9.9.2.1 
The requirements of GVOSS Section 9.4 Safety Requirements for Conveyors is 
included as part of this specification. This section is intended to highlight 
mechanical design requirements for safe drop lifter design. Where discrepancies 
may exist between this specification and the requirements identified in GVOSS 
Section 9.4, the requirements identified in GVOSS Section 9.4 shall apply. 
9.9.2.2 
All drop lifters shall be designed with a mechanical means of locking the load (lift 
drum/sprocket, carriage, and counterweight) to control gravity and ensure safe 
access to areas under the elevated portion of the equipment and parts for 
maintenance.  If necessary, access ladders and platforms shall be installed to 
provide safe access to the gravity pin receiver locations. The location of the 
gravity pin receivers shall be such that maintenance personnel can engage the 
pins without requiring additional fall protection or personnel lifting equipment. The 
gravity pin receiver hole shall be designed to account for lift belt stretch/wear-in 
and controls device up/down positioning tolerance. Refer to GVOSS section 
9.4.11 for additional gravity pin and receiver specifications. 
9.9.2.3 
When not in use, the gravity pins shall be stored in stands located outside the 
perimeter fence at the top and bottom access levels. Each location, top and 
bottom, will include gravity pins for both carriage and counterweight. The storage 
stand at the lower level shall be located near the perimeter fence access gate. 
The storage stand at the upper level shall be placed at a location convenient to 
both the carriage and counterweight gravity pin receiver access locations. All 
drop lifter safety pins used for the purpose of locking the equipment in place to 
overcome potential energy shall be electrically interlocked such that all 
lifting/lowering motion is prevented if the pins are not placed in the normally 
stored location. 
9.9.2.4 
A perimeter safety fence, in compliance with applicable GVOSS Section 1.3 and 
GVOSS 9.4 specifications, shall be installed at the lower and upper level around 
the drop lifter conveyor. 
9.9.2.5 
An 18 inch (450mm), minimum, high fixed wire mesh guard shall be installed 
integral to the entrance/exit opening at the lower level. 
9.9.2.6 
The conveyor Supplier shall provide an Owner-approved light screen at the 
entry/exit safety fence opening at the lower level.  The conveyor controls shall be 
designed to immediately stop all lifting/lowering/transferring motion of the drop 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 168 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
lifter and adjacent conveyor(s) when unauthorized access is detected.  These 
controls shall be designed in compliance with applicable GVOSS Section 1.3, 
GVOSS 9.4 and GMS safety specifications for automated equipment. 
9.9.2.7 
At least one suitably positioned hinged access door, a minimum 1.0 meter (3 
feet) wide, shall be provided in the lower level perimeter safety fence. 
9.9.2.8 
The personnel access gate(s) shall be provided with gate plug(s).  The conveyor 
controls shall be designed to immediately stop automatic function of the 
equipment when unauthorized access is detected.  These controls shall be 
designed to be in full compliance with applicable GVOSS Section 9.4, GVOSS 
and GMS safety specifications for automated equipment. 
9.9.2.9 
Refer to GVOSS Section 9.4 for fall hazard identification at the upper level. 
9.9.3 
Mechanical Design Requirements 
9.9.3.1 
All drive assemblies, intermediate mast/guide sections, mast structure, carriage 
framework and/or other standard components provided by the conveyor Supplier 
shall be modular in design and constructed for field bolted assembly and 
installation.  All field welds shall require prior approval from the Owner and shall 
be identified at the first design review meeting. 
9.9.3.2 
All components of the drop lifter, including but not limited to the mast and 
carriage framework, bearings, keys, roller surfaces, support legs, etc., must be 
capable of withstanding, without permanent deflection, the load applied by the 
gearmotor in an E-stop or jam condition. 
9.9.3.3 
Where practical, the carriage design, including carriage lock, bearings and guide 
rollers, etc., shall be common for all drop lifters developed within a specific 
project. 
9.9.3.4 
The drivetrain components, including main lifting belts, shall be engineered and 
sized accordingly for each application. However, lifting belts shall be common for 
all drop lifters provided within a specific project. 
9.9.3.5 
The gearmotors used for all drop lifter lifting, transferring and carriage/track lock 
devices within a given plant project shall be as provided by the identical OEM 
Supplier. 
9.9.3.6 
Safety devices, guarding, fencing, and other peripheral devices shall be common 
and interchangeable for all drop lifter applications, where practical, within a given 
plant project. 
9.9.3.7 
The conveyor Supplier shall provide structural support reactions – i.e., floor 
loadings and connections to building steel, etc. – to the Owner within six weeks 
following award of contract or as specified within the project scope of work 
specifications. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 169 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.9.3.8 
VDL Column Masts Column Splices shall be limited, if feasible. VDL Column 
Masts Column Splices shall have Independent Quality Control and Testing 
Company to perform 100% Visual Inspection and Magnetic Particle / Ultrasonic 
Testing for presence of any web stress cracks development to validate existing 
Baseline condition and submit certified reports. The test to be performed at the 
commissioning of the lifts and after three to six months in service. 
9.9.4 
Lifting Equipment 
9.9.4.1 
Unless otherwise specified, the drop lifter shall be provided with a main lift motor 
only with the lifting shaft prepped for a future standby lifting drive assembly. i.e., 
the head frame shall include mounting structure and the drive shaft shall be 
"coupling ready" for a future standby drive assembly. 
 
1. 
Where standby drives are specified, the main and standby units shall be 
electrically interlocked so that both drives cannot be engaged/disengaged 
simultaneously. 
 
2. 
The “open” end of the lifting shaft shall be guarded to protect against the 
rotating shaft entanglement hazard. 
9.9.4.2 
All new and reused lifting drive gearmotors shall be designed to provide a 
minimum 1.5 service factor as applied to the calculated lift load and duty cycle 
requirements. 
9.9.4.3 
All new and reused lift drive equipment – i.e., drive drum, drive shaft, couplings, 
etc. – shall be designed to provide a minimum service factor of 1.3 as applied to 
the lifting motor nameplate horsepower rating. 
9.9.4.4 
The drop lifter main and standby lifting drive shall utilize a standard inverter duty 
gearmotor with integral brake and encoder for ramped acceleration/deceleration, 
closed-loop torque and brake proving control. 
9.9.4.5 
Unless otherwise specified, the drop lifter shall be provided with a main only 
Variable Frequency Drive (VFD), A/B PowerFlex 755 or Owner approved 
equivalent, with closed loop carriage position feedback. 
 
1. 
The VFD shall provide torque-limiting capability used to protect the structure 
of the drop lifter carriage during an E-stop or jam condition. 
 
2. 
The VFD shall provide brake proving capability to ensure proper brake 
engagement. 
 
3. 
The VFD shall be sized and selected in compliance with applicable GVOSS 
and Ford GMS specifications for reciprocating equipment 
9.9.4.6 
The gearmotor manufacturer and VFD Supplier must approve the conveyor 
Supplier’s drive calculations. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 170 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
1. 
The approval documentation will be reviewed at the initial design review 
meeting. 
 
2. 
It is the responsibility of the conveyor Supplier to provide all necessary 
design data to the gearmotor and VFD manufacturer for application sizing 
verification. 
9.9.4.7 
The horsepower calculations for the lift drive shall comprehend: (1) the force 
required to accelerate the load to steady state speed, and (2) the force required 
to maintain constant velocity at steady state speed.  These design calculations 
shall be reviewed at the initial design review and prior to purchasing the lifting 
gearmotors. 
9.9.4.8 
For “high heat” applications, first floor bottom entry ovens, as a general rule, 
going forward, use Marmacs. They have been in use at KTP since 1994 and 
performed much better than Chain Lifts. Exception/s to the Marmacs must be 
discussed with GPE Program Leader and written approval must be obtained to 
use Chain Lifts. If, chain lifts are to be used, the lifting gearmotor shall be located 
outside the VDL vestibule.      
 
1. 
A jack shaft shall be used to connect the lifting gearmotor to the lifting drum 
shaft. 
 
2. 
The vestibule opening for the lifting/jack shaft penetration shall be sealed. 
9.9.4.9 
The gearbox output shaft to drive drum shaft connection shall utilize a shiftable-
jaw type coupling with a shock absorbing "spider" insert. The coupling shall be 
capable of decoupling without the use of more than one hand tool. i.e., a spanner 
wrench, an Allen wrench or screwdriver, etc. 
 
1. 
Where special tools are required, the tool shall be provided with the 
equipment and shall be installed on a visual management display board at 
or near the device. Final placement of the tool display board shall be 
coordinated with the plant PM coordinator. 
 
2. 
The coupling Supplier must approve the application. The coupling Supplier 
approval documentation will be reviewed at the initial design review 
meeting. 
9.9.4.10 
When backup drive assemblies are specified, as the coupling shall be the 
primary means of switching from the main to the standby drive, the mean time to 
change from one coupling to the other shall not exceed 15 minutes. 
9.9.4.11 
The coupling shall include proximity switches to identify the "closed" and "open" 
position for both main and standby drives. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 171 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.9.4.12 
The drive drums shall be constructed from a steel outer tube with steel end 
plates and a through shaft.  When multiple drive drums are required to be 
mounted to a single drive shaft, the drive drums shall be assembled to the drive 
shaft in "matched pairs"; i.e., they shall have machined surfaces to provide for 
"identical" diameters and axial concentricity. 
9.9.4.13 
The drive drum shall be connected to the drive shaft utilizing shrink disk bushings 
as provided by Ringfeder, B-Loc or Owner-approved equivalent.  The use of 
keyed bushings or welding the drive drum to the drive shaft shall not be allowed. 
9.9.4.14 
All drive and idler shafts shall utilize dual row spherical roller bearings mounted in 
pillow block housings.  The bearing assemblies shall be selected to have a 
minimum static load capacity equal to two times the calculated static load and 
shall have a minimum L10 life expectancy of 40,000 hours.  Sealed and/or "lubed 
for life" bearings are not acceptable.  All selection criteria, calculations and R&M 
data must be submitted and approved by the Owner at the initial design review 
meeting. 
9.9.4.15 
All elevated drives shall include a service rail installed above the drive to facilitate 
removal/replacement of the reducer, motor, and other large or bulky components. 
The rail shall be complete with curved sections, if required, to provide adequate 
access for removal of components. The service rail shall extend beyond the drive 
platform so that components may be lowered to the plant floor. The service rail 
shall be complete with double ends stops, and trolley suitable for the capacity of 
the rail. Attachments shall be included to allow installation of a chain fall on the 
trolley. 
9.9.5 
Lifting Belts and Chains 
9.9.5.1 
The drop lifter shall be provided with a minimum two lifting belts.  The lifting belts 
shall be constructed from multiple steel cords with rubber cover and shall provide 
a minimum service factor equal to 10:1 as applied to the lift load, including force 
required for acceleration.  Test certificates shall be supplied for all lift belts and 
belt fittings. 
9.9.5.2 
Lifting belts shall be connected to the carriage and counterweight utilizing a 
"wrap around" style end connection with clamp as illustrated below.  Bonded 
rubber wedge end connections are prohibited. The belt connections to the lift 
carriage and counterweight frames shall be designed to eliminate the possibility 
of belt twisting. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 172 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
 
 
Figure 9.9.5.2 – Lifting Belt Connection 
9.9.5.3 
The conveyor Supplier shall review the application, end connection and clamp 
design with the conveyor lifting belt manufacturer. The conveyor belt 
specifications, calculations, and belt manufacturer approval documentation must 
be submitted by the conveyor Supplier to the Owner for review/approval at the 
engineering design review meeting. 
9.9.5.4 
For “high heat” applications, lifting chains shall be used in lieu of lift belts. 
 
1. 
Four (4) RC chains shall be provided with ends connected to the top of the 
carriage and counterweight containers. 
 
2. 
The lift chain carriage/counterweight connections shall be compression 
spring shock relieving type. Each RC chain shall be sized with a 7:1 safety 
factor. 
 
3. 
Each RC chain shall wrap over sprockets which are double keyed to shafts 
supported by spherical bearing pillow blocks suitable for operation in the 
high temperature environment. 
 
4. 
Each sprocket shall be provided with chain retainers. 
 
5. 
These lift chain head shaft assemblies are to be installed on the top of the 
vertical mast towers. 
 
6. 
Safety chains shall be provided to retain the load in case of failure of any of 
the lift chains. 
9.9.5.5 
A “slack belt/chain” mechanism, complete with limit switches, shall be provided 
such that all lift/lower movement will be halted if the system detects that a belt 
break or excessive slack condition exists. In the event of a lifting belt failure, the 
second belt shall be sufficient to support the load to allow manual transfer of the 
carriage to the lower terminal end position. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 173 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.9.6 
Mast 
9.9.6.1 
The lift carriage and counterweight assemblies shall be guided directly by the 
frame through guiding rollers bearing against the drop lifter mast structure. 
9.9.6.2 
The drop lifter mast structure shall be constructed from structural steel shapes 
with bracing to the building structure as necessary.  Additionally, the mast 
structure shall be designed such that reaction forces due to loads imposed by the 
carriage wheels are normal to the x- and y-axes of the mast and supporting 
members. Drop lifter mast designs that use skewed lift guides and roller 
assemblies are not acceptable. 
9.9.6.3 
Deflection of the structure shall not exceed L/900 on any axis.  
9.9.6.4 
The length of the load on the carriage shall determine the distance between the 
main column mast assemblies such that the nominal distance between columns 
shall be not less than 1/2 the overall unit length, limited to a minimum of 2.0 
meters (6 feet) center-to-center and a maximum of 3.0 meters (10 feet) center-to-
center, or as regulated by wide load shipping restrictions. All Drop Lifters 
provided for a given project shall have common mast column centers. 
9.9.6.5 
The vertical towers shall consist of structural members or tube framing anchored 
to the plant floor, true and plumb. Footings shall be used to support the towers 
based on floor loading/design. Truss supporting shall not be allowed. 
9.9.6.6 
All loadings imposed shall be supported from the plant floor either by spread 
base framing and /or by new isolated concrete pad.  Building truss connections 
shall be for sway bracing only and shall result in no vertical or lateral deflections 
to the trusses. 
9.9.6.7 
The mast assembly shall include adjustable sensor brackets for Acceleration, 
Deceleration, Creep, In-position (all positions), and Over-travel (all cases), 
9.9.6.8 
The mast assembly shall include mounting supports for the lift motor head frame 
assembly. 
9.9.6.9 
The drop lifter mast and guide rails shall incorporate hard stops at the terminal 
ends to ensure the carriage cannot travel past the ends of the rail.  These stops, 
as well as all other impacting parts, shall be equipped with sound deadening 
bumpers to minimize the impact forces and noise resulting from metal-to-metal 
contact. 
9.9.6.10 
Permanent markers shall be installed to verify "Full Up" and "Full Down" 
positions. These markers shall be clearly visible from outside the perimeter 
guarding. 
9.9.6.11 
The conveyor Supplier shall design the mast assembly base plate based on the 
Owner provided plant floor reinforced concrete construction. The Supplier is 
responsible to confirm proper floor loading distribution. Were required due to 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 174 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
base plate design limitations, the Supplier shall provide a thickened floor slab to 
provide the required VDL mast support. 
9.9.6.12 
The conveyor Supplier shall provide a registered professional structural engineer 
to review/approve the proposed mast column design, including all footing, 
foundation and cross-bracing building connections. All connections required to 
the building steel shall be considered by the Owner only after review and 
certification by a registered professional structural engineer. 
9.9.7 
Guide Rails 
9.9.7.1 
For standard applications, the carriage mounted polyurethane load-/guide-rollers 
shall bear against the inside flange and web faces of the wide flange mast 
columns. 
9.9.7.2 
For high heat applications, cam followers shall be used for the load-/guide-rollers.  
 
1. 
When cam follower type guide rollers are used, the Drop Lifter shall utilize 
guide rails or wear bars constructed of heat-treated hi-carb steel that are 
bolted or welded to the lift mast structure. 
 
2. 
Applications using wear bars welded to the lift mast shall be identified in the 
conveyor Supplier's bid proposal documentation and shall be used only with 
prior written approval of the Owner. 
 
3. 
The guide rail and wear bar life expectancy shall be ten years; i.e., there 
shall not be a loss of material anywhere along the entire surface of the wear 
bar in excess of 1/16 inch during the 10-year life of the conveyor under the 
designed loads. 
 
4. 
Guide rails shall be shimmed and/or machined to provide "plumb, square 
and level" load/guide wheel alignment over the entire length of carriage 
travel.  Individual guide rail sections shall be rigidly mounted to the mast 
structure such that deflections at joints are less than 0.020 inch. 
9.9.8 
Lifting Carriage 
9.9.8.1 
The lifting carriage shall be constructed from structural steel shapes as a single 
weldment to include mounting and assembly details for the pivoting lift belt 
attachment arm, mounting details and/or attachment base for the carriage lock 
assemblies, traveling cable connections, controls device mounting, load bearing 
and guide wheel assemblies, and transfer conveyor mounting details. 
9.9.8.2 
Where site conditions require, either due to weight restrictions or other field 
conditions, the carriage may be constructed from two assemblies, referred to as 
a primary carriage and a secondary carriage. 
 
1. 
The primary carriage shall be defined as the guiding assembly and includes 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 175 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
mounting details for attaching the secondary lift carriage.  The primary 
carriage shall be a generic design that will accept mounting details or 
subassemblies for the various transfer conveyor types used on the specific 
project. e.g., pallet/skid power roll beds and FCS/P&F conveyor. The 
primary carriage design shall be reviewed and approved at the design 
review meeting. 
 
2. 
The secondary lift carriage shall be defined as the assembly used to 
support the transfer conveyor and is assembled to the primary carriage at 
the job site.  Where practical, the transfer conveyor sub-frame shall be 
identical for all transfer conveyor types used within the given project.  The 
secondary carriage design shall be reviewed and approved at the design 
review meeting. 
9.9.8.3 
The secondary carriage may be clamped to the primary carriage only for 
alignment during initial installation.  The final secondary to primary carriage 
mounting details shall utilize bolted connections.  Field welded connections for 
mounting the secondary to primary carriage shall be used only with prior written 
approval from the Owner.  Where welded connections are proposed, an 
approved method of stress relieving and weld quality testing the completed 
weldment shall be included. 
9.9.8.4 
The conveyor Supplier shall complete finite element analysis (FEA) models for 
the proposed carriage design.  The FEA model shall comprehend the static state 
and dynamic operating conditions. i.e., forces due to acceleration from the start 
position to steady-state speed shall be included in the FEA.  Additionally, the 
FEA model shall comprehend stresses induced in a "jam" condition. e.g., the 
carriage locks remain engaged when the drop lifter lift drive begins to lift.  This 
model shall be used to validate the VFD torque-limit parameter setting. 
9.9.8.5 
Fabrication of structural members shall be in accordance with AISC 
requirements.  All structural members shall be ASTM A36 for steel shapes and 
plating and/or ASTM A500-Grade B for tubing, unless otherwise specified. 
9.9.8.6 
All field bolted connections shall utilize ASTM F3125/F3125M bolts, unless noted 
otherwise. 
9.9.8.7 
The use of butt weld connections will not be accepted. 
9.9.8.8 
Load and guide rollers may be cam-follower type or urethane coated steel hub 
wheels, depending on the application. It is the conveyor Supplier’s responsibility 
to make the proper selection for the specific application. The roller bearings used 
for cam-follower and urethane coated rollers must provide a minimum of 40,000 
hours L10 life. 
9.9.8.9 
The load/guide wheel load rating shall be based on a maximum of three wheels 
supporting the combined weight of the part, part carrier and carriage assembly.  

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 176 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
The conveyor Supplier shall review the load/guide wheel calculations and 
selection criteria with the Owner at the design review meeting. 
9.9.8.10 
The guide wheel mounting assembly shall provide for individual wheel alignment, 
square and plumb, to the face of the guide rail.  Assembling multiple load bearing 
and non-load bearing wheels on a common mounting plate that does not provide 
individual wheel alignment will not be accepted. 
9.9.8.11 
Gravity actuated over-travel stops shall be provided on the drop lifter transfer 
conveyor to prevent movement of the skid/pallet or carrier on the drop lifter when 
lifting or lowering.  The over-travel stops shall be a purely mechanical device 
actuated by a fixed cam mounted at the upper and lower terminal positions. 
9.9.8.12 
Gravity actuated over-travel stops shall be provided on the charge conveyors to 
prevent the product/carrier from transferring into the opening when the drop lifter 
is lifting and/or lowering.  The over-travel stops shall be a purely mechanical 
device that is actuated by a cam mounted to the carriage to rotate the stop clear 
of the transferring unit at the upper/lower terminal positions.  
9.9.8.13 
Where precise track alignment between the fixed and lifting conveyors is 
necessary – e.g., pallet, P&F and EMS transfer conveyors – a carriage lock 
mechanism shall be provided to ensure proper alignment between adjacent 
conveyors through the entire transfer cycle. The carriage lock shall be installed in 
a location to operate directly on the transfer conveyor or carriage frame. 
9.9.8.14 
Proximity sensors shall be provided for all carriage lock devices at all 
charge/discharge elevations.  The proximity sensors shall be used to ensure the 
locks are engaged and the conveyor is properly positioned before allowing the 
part carrier to initiate the transfer.  Additional proximity sensors shall be used to 
identify the mast and carriage locks are fully disengaged prior to the lift motor 
receiving a command to lift or lower. 
9.9.8.15 
Highly flexible power and control cables shall provide electrification of all 
electrical equipment located on the movable carriage. Cables shall not be 
bundled but shall be individually secured. Control conductors shall be separated 
from the power conductors. Refer to Article 9.8.10 of this specification for 
additional information relative to VDL traveling cable design requirements. 
9.9.8.16 
4-Post Drop Lifter Carriage 
 
1. 
The 4-Post Drop Lifter carriage assembly shall be constructed from two 
primary lifting carriages and a single secondary carriage. The primary to 
secondary lift carriage connections shall be as specified herein. 
 
2. 
Purchased components – e.g., load bearing and non-load bearing wheels, 
carriage lock components, etc. – shall be common and interchangeable for 
all side mast and 4-post drop lifter applications, where practical, within a 
given plant project. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 177 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
 3. 
The 4-post drop lifter carriage assembly design shall include a 
sliding or pivoting connection between primary and secondary carriages to 
provide guide wheel strain relief during lifting motion due to mast 
misalignment. 
9.9.9 
Counterweight 
9.9.9.1 
The counterweight frame shall be constructed from structural steel shapes as a 
single weldment including the mounting and assembly details for the lift belt, 
guide wheels, and for installation of flat steel counterweight plates. 
9.9.9.2 
The counterweight plates shall be constructed from mild steel flat bar and shall 
weigh not more than 50 pounds each. 
9.9.9.3 
A cover plate shall be provided to close the counterweight plate cavity. The cover 
plate shall include lifting lugs for removal to facilitate the need to add or remove 
counterweight plates for future programs. 
9.9.9.4 
The counterweight shall be guided using cam-followers or urethane coated steel 
hub wheels. It is the conveyor Supplier’s responsibility to make the proper 
selection for the specific application. The cam-followers used for the 
counterweight guide wheels shall be the same design and Supplier as used for 
the carriage guide wheels. 
9.9.9.5 
The weight of the counterweight shall be calculated considering the total lift 
carriage weight plus one-half the maximum live load. The weight of the 
counterweight shall be field verified. The field verified weight shall be stenciled on 
the counterweight cover plate, including date of field verification. 
9.9.9.6 
The counterweight container shall be designed to hold 125 percent of the 
required counterweight. The counterweights shall be guarded with removable 
expanded metal panels which are to be installed the entire height of the vertical 
tower. 
9.9.10 
VDL Traveling Cables 
9.9.10.1 
The Contractor shall provide all power, control and communication cables, 
including the cable support brackets mounted to the mast frame and carriage 
frames as illustrated in Figure No. 9.9.10.1 – Typical VDL Traveling Cable 
System Elevation View. 
9.9.10.2 
The traveling cable support system shall include two bracket assemblies. i.e., 
one (1) mounting bracket attached to the carriage frame and one (1) guide 
bracket attached to the mast at the intermediate location. A third bracket may be 
needed at the top attached to the VDL mast head. The actual mounting/guide 
brackets configuration will be determined based on application and layout 
conditions.

9.10 
OVERHEAD MONORAIL CHAIN TROLLEY AND PENDULUM CONVEYORS 
9.10.1 
General  
9.10.1.1 
This Section is to be used in conjunction with the following specifications.  This 
Section shall supersede any conflicts between this Section and those listed 
below. 
 
1. 
GVOSS Section 9.4 Safety Requirements for Conveyors 
 
2. 
GVOSS Section 9.5 Conveyor Mechanical Engineering 
 
3. 
GVOSS Section 9.6 Conveyor Controls Engineering 
 
4. 
GVOSS Section 9.9 Overhead Monorail Chain Trolley Conveyors 
 
5. 
GVOSS Section 9.25 Lubrication for Conveyors 
 
6. 
GVOSS Section 9.26 Conveyor Support Steel and Platforms 
9.10.1.2 
This Section shall be used in conjunction with the following Branch Drawings: 
 
1. 
3ZF-20712 Pneumatic and hydraulic schematics 
 
2. 
3ZF-20705 Overhead monorail drives and take-ups 
 
3. 
3ZF-20706 OHM Carriers and Hangers 
 
4. 
3ZF-20707 Overhead monorail expansion joints 
 
5. 
3ZF-20708 Overhead monorail safety devices, guards, drip pans and 
miscellaneous equipment 
9.10.1.3 
All welding preparation and process shall be in accordance with the GVOSS 
Section 9.26 Conveyor Support Steel and Platforms.  
9.10.1.4 
All pillow block and flange bearings shall be blocked after alignment to prevent 
movement.  
9.10.1.5 
All slotted connections shall be field welded after final alignment.  
9.10.1.6 
All holes shall be drilled or punched. Burning of holes will not be permitted.  
9.10.1.7 
All load carrying connections at trolley attachments, load bars, etc. shall utilize 
ASTM F3125/F3125M bolts with slotted nuts and cotter pins.  
9.10.1.8 
Pivot points, chording’s, etc. shall be designed to prevent damage at attachment 
points and wear at track web, flange, etc.  

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 181 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.10.1.9 
All trolley conveyors used for production operations shall be provided with Edon, 
or Owner approved equivalent, encoders to monitor conveyor speed, carrier/part 
position and for synchronization with adjacent/adjoining conveyors. The Edon 
encoders shall include service platforms in compliance with the requirements 
specified in GVOSS section 9.26. 
9.10.1.10 
In the design of a New Paint Shop or Program modifications to an Existing Paint 
Shop, in which the Phosphate/ECoat OHM Conveyors utilize hang chains, an 
Error Proofing method shall be provided at the Hang Station to ensure that the 
hang chains have been installed correctly and the Unit is sitting level, prior to any 
vertical change in elevation greater than 2 feet.  The Error Proofing method can 
be accomplished through limit switches, photo eyes and conveyor lifts.  Similarly, 
at the De-chain Station, there shall be an Error Proofing method to ensure that 
the hang chains have been removed, prior to the ECoat OHM Conveyor making 
any change in direction (either vertically or horizontal) after the De-chaining of 
the skids. 
9.10.2 
OHM Superstructure  
9.10.2.1 
All conveyor track, service platform, and screen guard support steel shall comply 
with the requirements identified in GVOSS Section 9.26 Conveyor Support Steel 
and Platforms. 
9.10.2.2 
The support structure hanger spacing shall be such that the maximum deflection 
caused by live loads shall not exceed ¼" between hangers. 
9.10.2.3 
Where process requirements dictate, the conveyor track and hangers are to be 
offset adjacent to bridge crane installations to allow the bridge/hoist to travel 
parallel to the conveyor track. 
9.10.2.4 
The Contractor shall install pipe guards the full length of the bridge/hoist travel to 
avoid entanglement between the conveyor structure and the hoist assembly or 
hoist chain. 
9.10.2.5 
Where the hoist chain shall contact the pipe guards as a normal function of the 
operation, the pipe guards shall be sleeved with a suitable material to prevent 
damage to the hoist chain or assembly. These sleeves shall be free to rotate 
about the pipe guard. 
9.10.2.6 
The Conveyor and bridge crane or other process equipment support steel shall 
be separate and independent systems. 
9.10.2.7 
SPECIAL REQUIREMENTS FOR PHOSPHATE & E-COAT CONVEYORS 
9.10.2.7.1 
All Structural Steel members INSIDE the Phosphate and E-Coat Enclosures shall 
be 304L Low Carbon Stainless Steel made in USA or Canada. Contractor / 
supplier must provide proof that the steel is built in USA or Canada, prior to start 
of installation.

9.10.3 
Conveyor Chain and Attachments  
9.10.3.1 
Standard Application X-458 Conveyor Chain: 
 
1. 
The conveyor chain shall be new rivet less X-458 four inch (102 mm) forged 
from SAE 1552 steel sides and centers with smooth 4140 alloy cold formed 
pins and as specified and illustrated in CEMA Standard No. 601. Deviation 
from this composition will not be accepted. 
 
2. 
The chain shall be manufactured to QS-9000 TE procedures and supplied 
clean and free from dirt, rust, slag, scale and flash extension per ASTM 
A521. 
 
3. 
All chain is to be protected from corrosion prior to shipping to the Owner.  
 
4. 
X-458 chain components to meet the following criteria:  
A. 
Material: SAE 1552 steel sides and centers with smooth 4140 alloy 
cold formed pins  
B. 
Pin Hardness: 362-400 BHN for 4140 (10 mm ball at 3,000 Kg)  
C. 
Center Link Hardness: 362-400 BHN for 1552  
D. 
Side Link Hardness: 362-400 BHN for 1552  
E. 
Minimum Ultimate Strength: Greater than 54,000 pounds  
F. 
Maximum Design Working Pull: 2,500 pounds (with 2 percent friction)  
G. 
All X-458 chain to be pre-stressed/proof loaded to 20,000 pounds prior 
to shipping. 
H.

FOR

         Manufacturing Inc. In case of emergency, if the cold formed Smooth  
         Pins are not available; 8642 High Alloy Pins can be used. All chain  
         components are to be forged in the USA from US or Canadian  
         manufactured steel. Chain with any component/s built outside the  
         USA will not be acceptable. The supplier must provide proof that this  
         requirement is met prior to shipping chain to the plant.    
 
5. 
All chain components to be computer controlled through hardened and 
tempered in a computer-controlled process. Case hardening will not be 
accepted.  
 
6.

9.10.3.2 
Heavy Duty Application X-678 Conveyor Chain: 
 
1. 
The conveyor chain shall be new rivet less X-678 six inch (152 mm) forged 
from SAE 1046 steel side and center links with smooth 4140 alloy cold 
formed pins, or 8642 alloy steel sides and centers with 8642 smooth alloy 
cold formed pin, and as specified and illustrated in CEMA Standard No. 
601. Deviation from this composition will not be accepted. 
 
2. 
The chain shall be manufactured to QS-9000 TE procedures and supplied 
clean and free from dirt, rust, slag, scale and flash extension per ASTM 
A521. 
 
3. 
All chain is to be protected from corrosion prior to shipping to the Owner.  
 
4. 
High alloy 8642 shall be used only where excessive chain tensions are 
encountered and as approved by the Ford Project Engineer. Additionally, 
8642 high alloy chain is acceptable only as a part of a complete chain 
assembly. This assembly must be provided in whole and not mixed with 
other types of pins or center/side links in the same conveyor. 
 
 
5.  X-678 chain components to meet the following criteria:  
A. 
Material: New SAE 1046 steel sides and centers with 4140 smooth 
alloy cold formed pins or SAE 8642 alloy steel sides and centers with 
8642 smooth cold formed pins.  
B. 
Pin Hardness: 344-381 BHN for 1046 & 381-421 for 8642 (10 mm ball 
at 3,000 Kg)  
C. 
Center Link Hardness: 344-381 BHN for 1046 and 381-421 for 8642  
D. 
Side Link Hardness: 344-381 BHN for 1046 and 381-421 for 8642  
E. 
Minimum Ultimate Strength: 86,000 for 1046/4140 and 125,000 for 
8642  
F. 
Maximum Design Working Pull: 4,500 pounds (with 2 percent friction)  
G. 
All 678 chain to be pre-stressed/proof loaded to 30,000 pounds prior to 
shipping

9.10.3.4 
B-Attachments: 
1. 
All B-attachment assemblies shall be as illustrated in Figure No. 9.10.3.4.1. 
The Contractor shall provide all new B-attachments forged from SAE 1045 
steel. 
2. 
The threaded portion of the shank shall be in compliance with Unified 
Coarse Thread Series as specified in ASM B1.1 and shall have Class 2A 
tolerance before plating.  
 
3. 
The shank and threaded portion shall be induction hardened and tempered 
to provide a maximum surface hardness of 38 HRC and a minimum 
hardness value of 35 HRC at 2.5mm below the minor thread diameter.  
 
4. 
All B-attachment assemblies shall be complete with Ford Standard tube 
spacers, spherical washers, ASTM F436 washers, castellated ASTM A194 
Grade 2H or castellated ASTM A563 Grade DH Heavy Hex nuts, and cotter 
pin, as well as the required nuts, bolts and washers for attaching the B-
attachment to the trolley brackets at two locations per attachment.

6. 
The following Quality Control records shall be maintained by the

A.

B.

decarburization is detectable on the surface at 100x magnification and 
that no cracks, seams, laps or other defects are detectable on the B 
Attachment at 5x magnification. Five samples per lot shall be tested as 
described above. The samples shall be taken from throughout the 
hardening process.  
C.

per lot shall be tested. These testing shall be conducted on a sampling 
of threads throughout the threading operations.  
D. 
Magnetic Particle Inspection of each part for longitudinal flaws along 
the shank and root diameter of threads, following ASTM E709.

9.10.4 
Trolleys 
9.10.4.1 
Trolleys shall conform to Ford Manufacturing Standard, Section CX-2 and all 
other applicable sections.  
9.10.4.2 
Chain trolley spacing shall be provided on a maximum 2'-0" (610 mm) centers.  
9.10.4.3 
All trolleys for both 4”- and 6”-OHM systems to be as manufactured by:   

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 188 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
1. 
Frost, Inc.   
2. 
Cignys (formerly Saginaw Products) Corporation 
3. 
Daifuku / Jervis Webb Corporation  
4. 
Blue Water Manufacturing Inc.   Refer to GVOSS Section 9.12 Overhead  
9.10.4.4 
Power and Free Conveyors for design load limits.  
9.10.4.5 
Bearing Construction: 
 
1. 
For standard conditions, with clean environment and temperatures less than 
200 degrees Fahrenheit (93 C), trolleys shall be as follows:  
A. 
Ball bearings shall be high carbon chrome steel 500 grade, single row-
full ball compliment design, with locked race, swaged, closed wheel 
design with triple labyrinth splash resistant seal, welsh plug, and 
grease fitting to accommodate automatic greasing. Trolley wheels for 
4" conveyor systems shall have thirteen ½" diameter balls. Trolley 
wheels for 6" conveyor systems shall have thirteen ¾" diameter balls. 
B. 
Wheels shall be lubricated for life. In addition, the provisions for 
lubrication shall be incorporated into the design of the assembly.  
 
2. 
For corrosive environments such as phosphate, E-coat, DI water, washers, 
etc., as well as in temperatures higher than 200 degrees Fahrenheit (93 C), 
design of all chain trolleys shall be as specified above, with the following 
exceptions:  
A. 
Trolley seal shall include purgeable contact (wiper) seal made of 
NITRILE for applications up to 200 degrees Fahrenheit (93 C), and 
VITON for applications 200 degrees to 450 degrees Fahrenheit (232 
C). 
B. 
For Phosphate/E-Coat applications, the seal shall be “moisture proof” 
and shall be located on the inside of each trolley wheel. The outside of 
the trolley wheels shall have open access to allow for lubrication 
application (threaded zerk fitting with plunger in the Trolley Bracket). 
 
3. 
For temperatures in excess of 200 degrees Fahrenheit (93 C), open trolleys 
may be required. The Contractor shall investigate actual conditions with 
trolley manufacturer and submit recommendations to Owner for review. If 
closed trolleys are used, trolley seals shall be of suitable material for the 
maximum temperature to be encountered.  
9.10.4.6 
Side Arm Construction: 
 
1. 
All trolley side arms shall be drop forged steel construction with integrally 
forged wheel axle. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 189 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2. 
The two (2) trolley side arms shall be assembled along with an "I" 
attachment by means of a minimum of two (2) grade 5 bolts and two (2) 
steel insert heavy full lock nuts. 
 
3. 
One-piece brackets may be considered for certain applications; however, 
approval must be received in writing from the Owner.  
9.10.5 
Conveyor Safety Devices 
9.10.5.1 
For all information relating to equipment safety requirements refer to GVOSS

9.10.6 
Conveyor Guards  
9.10.6.1 
For all information relating to equipment guarding refer to GVOSS Section 9.4 
Safety Requirements for Conveyors. 
9.10.7 
Conveyor Track  
9.10.7.1 
All track shall be new and free from any imperfections, distortion, dirt, rust, slag, 
scale, or other foreign matter. All track shall be of uniform cross section, free of 
warps and twists and with tolerances not exceeding limits set by the ASTM. 
 
1. 
Track shall be formed from AISI 1045 high carbon steel. 
 
2. 
Track for 4" applications shall be S4 X 7.7#. 
 
3. 
Track for 6" applications shall be S6 X 12.5#.  
9.10.7.2 
All track joints shall be assembled with a splice angle. 
 
1. 
The splice angle shall be minimum L2½ x 2 x ¼ by 6" (150 mm) long (LLV) 
for 4" systems. 
 
2. 
The splice angle shall be minimum L2½ x 2½ x 5/16 by 8" (200 mm) long for 
6" systems. 
 
3. 
The splice angle shall be located centered to and shall span each side of 
the track joint. The splice angle shall be welded continuously around on all 
four sides. 
4. 
The track shall be welded at the top and bottom of both flanges and 
continuously on one side of the web. 
 
5. 
All welds shall be ground smooth and follow the surface contour of the 
beam. 
 
6. 
Refer to Ford Manufacturing Standards, classification CX-2, appendix "A" 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 190 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
and "B" for further information.  
9.10.7.3 
All conveyor track ends shall be saw cut square with a gap joint not to exceed 
1/16 inch (1.5 mm). 
9.10.7.4 
Conveyor track splice joints shall be designed and located to accommodate 
installation of hangers adjacent to the splice. Bending moment and radial loads at 
the splice joint shall be zero whenever possible.  
9.10.7.5 
All conveyor track installed in a corrosive environment such as phosphate 
machines, E-coat machines, washers, DI rinse, etc. shall be minimum AISI 304L 
stainless steel. This requirement shall include all supports, hangers, connections, 
splice reinforcements and other steel exposed to the corrosive environment. All 
hardware (nuts, bolts, washers etc., etc.) used to hang the track inside the 
corrosive envelope shall be structural grade galvanized fasteners with self-
locking nuts. In certain countries, were availability and cost are in favor of S316 
this can also be used as an alternate to S304L. 
9.10.7.6 
To assist the preventative/predictive maintenance processes with respect to track 
wear, the Contractor shall be responsible for the following:  
 
1. 
Determine and locate all high stress points on the conveyor rail – typically 
top and bottom vertical curves, near horizontal turns, etc. – that could result 
in accelerated wear of the I-beam flange. These points of high stress shall 
be identified on the system layout.  
 
2. 
Fabricate and attach a laminated template card containing access holes, for 
a measuring tool probe, to the outside surface of the rail flange to be 
measured (reference Figure No. 9.10.7.6.2). This card identifies the point to 
be measured and will be used for all subsequent measurements.  
 
3. 
Provide safe access – i.e., ladders, catwalks, windows, doors, etc. – to the 
high track stress locations. For Phosphate and E-Coat installations, a 
minimum 12” X 12” (minimum) access hole must be provided at the hangers 
that attach the conveyor rail to the structural steel (horizontal member) 
outside the enclosure. Cover this hole with removable hole. Seal the gaps 
with Ford approved sealer. Do NOT weld this plate to the roof of the 
enclosure.     
 
4. 
For new conveyor track installations, measure the flange thickness to 
establish a baseline for future measurements. For existing conveyor track 
locations, measure the opposite non-wear flange for the baseline 
measurement. Include the initial measurements in the baseline data book 
for each conveyor. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025
