9.14 
RESERVED FOR FUTURE CONVEYOR 
9.14.1 
General 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 266 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.15 
SKILLET CONVEYORS 
9.15.1 
General 
9.15.1.1 
This section is intended to identify the minimum Skillet Conveyor system design 
requirements for delivery conveyor applications. Skillet conveyor systems are 
used primarily as production and delivery conveyors in Final Assembly where 
maximum operator access is required. 
9.15.1.2 
All Contractors must adhere to these specification and standards. The VOGO 
Conveyor Project Engineer must approve any deviations from any specifications 
and/or standards in writing. 
9.15.1.3 
The design, manufacture and installation shall conform to all OSHA and NEC 
standards without exception. 
9.15.1.4 
This Section is to be used in conjunction with the following specifications.  This 
document shall supersede any conflicts between this specification and those 
listed below. 
 
1. 
GVOSS Section 9.4 Safety Requirements for Conveyors 
 
2. 
GVOSS Section 9.5 Conveyor Mechanical Engineering 
 
3. 
GVOSS Section 9.6 Conveyor Controls Engineering 
 
4. 
GVOSS Section 9.7 Floor Conveyors 
 
5. 
GVOSS Section 9.8 Drop Lifters 
 
6. 
GVOSS Section 9.25 Lubrication for Conveyors 
 
7. 
GVOSS Section 9.26 Conveyor Support Steel and Platforms 
 
8. 
GVOSS Section 9.27 Conveyor Pits, Trenches and Related Pit Steel 
9.15.1.5 
Definitions: 
 
1. 
Conveyor systems without interfaces to process stations, and which move 
product between major automated process systems, are referred to in this 
document as "delivery conveyors". 
 
2. 
Conveyor systems, which move product between multiple process stations 
within a major automated process system, are referred to in this document 
as "process conveyors". 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 267 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.15.1.6 
The Contractor shall perform all necessary cycle time studies, define conveyor 
speeds, size drives and develop a "finalized" layout prior to submitting the 
original proposal.  
9.15.1.7 
All mechanical designs must adhere to a 1.3 service factor standard as per all 
Ford system designs.  Service factors shall be based on worst case, high output, 
reciprocating conditions. 
9.15.1.8 
All conveyance motors (roller beds, drop lifters, accelerators, decelerators, 
friction drives, turntables) are to be VFD driven and the VOGO Conveyor Project 
Engineer must approve deviations from this specification in writing. This does not 
include holdbacks, cam lifts and/or lock mechanisms used in the lifts. 
9.15.1.9 
Wherever possible a 30" minimum clearance shall be provided between transfer 
points at power roll beds, cross transfers and mechanical intersections. The 
Owner approval must be obtained for any deviation from this specification.  
Specific documentation must be obtained.   
9.15.1.10 
All platforms shall be sized to provide NEC, OSHA, and ANSI required 
clearances for panel access. 
9.15.2 
Skillet Floor Conveyor Track 
9.15.2.1 
The floor track shall be designed as a crane rail-based system. 
9.15.2.2 
A site floor survey is required to determine floor conditions.  The survey shall be 
done in one-foot increments to determine track elevation and benchmark 
settings. 
9.15.2.3 
A maximum gap of 1/8" shall be maintained between the edges of the skillet 
"train" and the cover plate surrounding the skillet train. This gap shall be 
maintained for all skillets and at all times and pertains to all production areas. 
9.15.2.4 
In the case that the conveyor pits are not provided as a part of the Skillet 
Contract, the Skillet system Supplier shall review the pits during construction and 
once completed shall review and submit a formal letter of acceptance to Ford. 
9.15.3 
Power Rail Section 
9.15.3.1 
Electrification and control will be provided to the skillet through hardened copper-
based conductor rails mounted in an insulator. Unless specified in the detailed 
equipment descriptions, the rail shall be as recommended and provided by Vahle 
or Wampfler. Alternate manufacturers can be proposed as an alternate only.  The 
conductor rails shall be equipped with an insulator shroud and provide touch 
protection to minimize hazard to personnel.  
9.15.3.2 
The conductor rails will be fully insulated copper rails, 600 VAC, 100-amp 
capacity, and compatibility matched set from a single manufacturer. 
9.15.3.3 
Conductor rail power feeds shall not exceed a maximum size of 100 amps and 
have disconnects for each run.   

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 268 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.15.3.4 
The conductor rail insulation shroud shall be a solid plastic shroud with individual 
conductor material mounted inside the shroud.  The conductor bar shall be rated 
600 volts and be finger safe. 
9.15.3.5 
The conductor rail shall be mounted in a fixed vertical orientation to the skillet 
collector shoes with steel brackets, approved by and located per the 
manufacturer's recommendations, but in any case, no more than 1 meter 
between brackets.  Incorporated into the brackets, or in addition to the brackets, 
a mechanical guard shall be included to protect the rail from damage from 
pedestrian traffic or foreign objects falling onto the rail section.  This guard shall 
provide access to the rail without the need of guard removal.  
9.15.3.6 
The minimum number of conductor rails will be four for a simple system and up 
to eight for complex systems.  The configuration will be, from the top, L1, L2, L3, 
GRD, SIGNAL 1, SIGNAL 2, SIGNAL 3 and COMMON (bottom rail).  This will 
provide either three simple signals for a very simple system or eight binary 
signals for a more complex simple system. 
9.15.3.7 
The number of conductor rails will be six for a "smart" system.  The configuration 
will be, from the top, L1, L2, L3, GROUND, SIGNAL 1 and SIGNAL 2. 
9.15.3.8 
The GROUND will be a non-current carrying conductor.     
9.15.3.9 
Power feed and other lead-in wires shall be color coded in the following manner: 
 
1. 
Power conductors – Black 
 
2. 
Neutral – White 
 
3. 
Ground – Green 
 
4. 
AC control voltage – Red 
 
5. 
DC control voltage – Blue 
9.15.3.10 
Splices in the conductor rails shall be made to assure reliable, wear-free travel of 
the collector shoes.   
9.15.3.11 
Bus bar expansion joints shall be utilized at all track expansion joints. 
9.15.3.12 
Isolation zones in the conductor rails will be made via the use of plastic 
replacement pieces used to isolate sections of the conductor rail but not create a 
section of rail that is discontinuous.  This will allow the smooth transition of the 
collector shoes through the isolation section of conductor rail. 
9.15.3.13 
Entry and exit guides shall be provided that allows smooth introduction of the 
collector shoes to/from the conductor rail section.  These guides are usually 
flared guides that gather the collector shoes, align and guide the shoes into the 
conductor rail section. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 269 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.15.3.14 
Contractor shall remove all burrs from all bus ends prior to installation. 
9.15.3.15 
The electrification of the conductor rail shall be designed to maximize the length 
of rail section that is powered.  Care should also be taken to ensure that the 
collector shoes are engaging into a skillet that is moving at a rate deemed 
acceptable by the conductor rail manufacturer.  The existing section should be as 
long as possible to ensure power to the conductor rail is maintained while the 
skillet is in the production area and allow for the skillet lift (if applicable) to lower 
after exiting the last production station. In addition, the skillet must be powered 
up prior to entering the first workstation for a sufficient amount of time to allow 
the on-board controller and electronics to energize and complete the required 
self-tests, establish communication with the supervisory controller, and other 
routines prior to receiving an elevation change command. 
9.15.3.16 
Each skillet line electrification section shall look as follows: 
 
 
 
Figure No. 9.15.3.16 – Skillet Line Electrification Section 
 
 
1. 
Lead-in section:  This section is non-powered and is used as the guide into 
the conductor rail. 
 
2. 
"Shoe Check" station:  This section checks the engagement of each 
collector shoe into the conductor rail and ensures that the ground conductor 
is grounded to the skillet frame.  The shoe check shall be no less than 110 
volts. 
 
3. 
Non-powered section:  This section is a non-powered section of the 
conductor rail and is used to isolate the shoe check zone from the power-up 
zone.  Note that this section shall be 1 ½ times the length of the collector 
shoes at minimum. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 270 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
4. 
Power-up section:  This section of conductor rail shall be used to power-up 
each individual skillet. The section of conductor rail is to be non-powered 
when the skillet collector shoes enter it and a contactor shall used to power 
the section when the collector shoes are fully engaged.  It is to be powered 
down when the skillet collector shoes are fully out of the section.  The 
contactor used for this power-up section shall be designed for 5 million 
cycles, minimum. 
 
5. 
Main Line Section:  This section is powered as a normal course of the skillet 
production system. 
 
6. 
Power-down section:  This section is opposite the power-up section.  The 
collector shoes will enter this section of conductor bus and power-down 
through the use of a contactor before exiting the conductor rail.  The 
contactor used in the power-down section shall be design for 5 million 
cycles, minimum. 
 
7. 
Lead-out section:  This section of conductor rail shall be non-powered and 
guide the collector shoes out of the conductor rail.  This section shall be the 
same type of conductor rail as in the lead-in section. 
9.15.3.17 
All conductor joints, cuts and/or splices shall be designed so that the collector 
shoes shall bridge one of these sections when the skillet is at its stations' normal 
start/stop position.  
9.15.3.18 
This is to allow the system to be stopped on a station stop and access the 
conductor rail without having to move the skillet, collector shoes and/or rail joint.  
This will allow for easy maintenance of collector shoes and rail sections.  
Collectors shall be designed for bi-directional travel.   
9.15.3.19 
All conductor rails will be the same color except for the ground rail, which shall be 
identified with a Yellow or Green color and/or stripe on the insulator. 
9.15.3.20 
Supplier of skillet power rail is required to be on site for field installation 
supervision and technical support. 
9.15.3.21 
The power design must protect against polarity reversal during installation and 
rail replacement. 
9.15.3.22 
The skillet collector shoe ground must be designed and manufactured such that 
the possibility of engagement with a power or communication rail does not exist. 
9.15.3.23 
Skillet collector shoes shall have a contact tolerance of +/-30 mm relative to 
power rail conductors. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 271 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.15.3.24 
Rail replacement shall be accomplished without the need to disturb adjacent rail 
sections. Sections should be "snap in" or otherwise easily removed and inserted. 
9.15.3.25 
Inductive systems can be proposed as an alternate design only and must be 
approved by the Ford Conveyor Engineer.  
9.15.3.26 
Cable connections to the power rail system shall be mechanically secured to 
insure they do not become loose or disconnected during use. "Stake-on" or 
similar type connections shall not be employed. 
9.15.4 
Skillet Guidance 
9.15.4.1 
Skillets shall be guided when traversing production main lines, cross transfers, 
and delivery portions of the skillet system. 
9.15.4.2 
The skillet shall be positively guided throughout the production main lines utilizing 
the crane rail identified in GVOSS Section 9.15.2.1.  One side of the skillet shall 
incorporate a load wheel assembly which incorporates side guide rollers which 
will ride against the side of the cane rail. These assemblies shall be identical and 
be installed at the front and rear wheel locations. The other side of the skillet 
shall incorporate an identical set of wheel mounting brackets, but they will not 
incorporate the side guide rollers. 
9.15.4.3 
The skillet shall be positively guided throughout in-line PRBs and cross wise 
conveyors by means of vertical rollers mounted to the PRB assemblies, flanged 
drive wheels, or a combination of both. Sufficient lead-ins are to be provided to 
ensure trouble-free transition from table-to-table. For aisle crossing areas where 
the aisle width is greater than the skillet width, "pop-up" guide rollers shall be 
provided to ensure the skillet is always guided during transfer. Un-guided transfer 
sections shall not be permitted. 
9.15.4.4 
Two skillet alignment bumper wheel assemblies are required at the end of an 
aisle crossing to ensure trouble-free engagement of the skillet guide track. 
9.15.4.5 
At right angle transfers or other areas where a skewed skillet can result in a 
transfer fault, detection switches shall be installed to detect a "skewed skillet" 
condition and stop automatic cycle. In addition, Mechanical guides shall be 
installed to manually guide the skewed skillet down to the receiving rollers or rail. 
9.15.5 
Skillet Load Wheels 
9.15.5.1 
Skillets shall be supported along the production line (push drive systems) by 4 
steel load wheels which will run on the crane rails. The crane rails shall be 
slightly harder than the wheels such that the wheels shall wear before the crane 
rail.  This is desired as the replacement of wheels can be performed while the 
system is running, whereas the replacement of crane rail would require a 
downtime period to complete. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 272 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.15.5.2 
The wheels shall be mounted to brackets which are identical for all wheels and 
installed to the skillet frame in a symmetrical pattern. A means shall be 
incorporated to adjust the vertical and horizontal location of the wheels to allow 
for leveling of the skillet frame. A visible means shall be included to allow for 
quick and easy alignment of the brackets to the frame, in the event of removal or 
replacement. 
9.15.5.3 
The load wheel shall be installed to the bracket with a removable axle. The wheel 
shall be capable of being removed without disturbing the mounting bracket. 
9.15.5.4 
A lubrication fitting shall be accessible from the top to facilitate applying 
lubrication to the unit while the skillet is in the production line. 
9.15.5.5 
Skillet load wheels shall incorporate standard available tapered roller bearings 
designed to accept all lateral and vertical loads imposed.  Wheel design shall 
allow for easy bearing replacement. 
9.15.6 
Friction Drives 
9.15.6.1 
The friction wheel drive is used as the propulsion unit for the continuous transport 
of platforms while supported by the load wheels on the crane rails.  Depending 
on the size (load) of the platforms, friction wheels may be paired at the start of 
the propulsion track or used with an opposite backpressure roller with 6, 4 or 2 
wheels.  In heavy demand systems, several friction wheel systems may be 
required. 
9.15.6.2 
One set of standby drives shall be provided on each skillet main or production 
line so that the line can continue to run in the event that one drive on either side 
of the skillet fails. 
9.15.6.3 
Skillet friction drives to be designed such that the drive wheel is mounted directly 
to the output shaft of the reducer.  Further, the motor/gearbox is mounted under 
the friction drive wheel, to eliminate the possibility of gearbox oil leakage 
contaminating the friction drive wheel. 
9.15.6.4 
Mechanical Design: 
 
1. 
At a minimum, two friction wheel assemblies will be used.  One on either 
side of the skillet train to maintain proper friction on each skillet as it enters 
the production area. 
 
2. 
Each friction drive assembly must be powered. All motors during normal 
operation of the skillet system should run but the system must be capable of 
running with one motor turned off. 
3. 
Tensioner roll section may be used when standby friction drives are not 
required. Drive roller tension shall incorporate mechanical spring pressure 
design. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 273 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
4. 
Brakes will not be used on the friction drive motors.  
 
5. 
Gaps will not be allowed to travel through the drives through normal 
operation. Sensors should be placed within the friction drives to detect this 
condition and alarm. 
 
6. 
Frames for friction assembly should be made with structural steel and bolted 
to floor. Lexan see-through covers shall be used to ensure visibility of 
belts/chains from outside the fenced areas. 
 
7. 
A spring-loaded friction wheel assembly shall be designed by the 
mechanical Contractor and mounted within the friction drive assembly but 
not attached to the drive train mechanics for the purpose of an encoder.  
This encoder is used to track the position of the skillet as it travels through 
the skillet friction assembly. The bracket used to hold this encoder is the 
responsibility of the mechanical Contractor. The encoder itself shall be 
specified and supplied by the controls Contractor. 
 
8. 
Skillet Contractor shall support NextGen Monitoring, plant monitoring and/or 
Ford Error Proofing System for the stopping of the skillets at fixed stop 
positions. This could include mounting of alternate encoders other than 
those required for the skillet system itself. 
 
9. 
In cases where multiple drive units are required to produce the required 
push force, the drive units shall be individual assemblies which can be 
easily placed off-line and/or physically removed without disturbing the 
balance of the drive units. 
 
10. Spring assemblies used to provide the compressions of the rollers against 
the skillet frame shall be adjustable and designed such that the correct 
compression (or tension) or the spring can be verified by visual observation 
of the adjustment rod or other marker. A tensions gage or instrument shall 
not be required to verify correct setting of the spring. 
9.15.6.5 
Electrical Design: 
 
1. 
VFD's are to be used for all friction drive motors. Care shall be used to 
maintain the forward/reverse capability of these VFD's in a manual function 
while eliminating the ability to create gaps when returning back to auto 
mode. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 274 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2. 
Each motor will have its own disconnect accessible from outside the fenced 
area.  Each motor shall have its own enable/disable selector switch used to 
enable or disable a particular motor.  Interlocks will be used from the 
disconnects to determine if conditions are acceptable to run the skillet 
friction drives keeping in mind that the system shall be functional with one 
motor turned off. 
 
3. 
Encoders shall be absolute and shall maintain their position when the 
system is turned off and turned back on. Assurances should be made that 
skillets do not bang or gap during this process. Standby encoders for all 
docking stations shall be required. These encoders should be online 
standby encoders and should be able to automatically detect the failure of 
the main encoder and continue where the main encoder failed. 
 
4. 
System shall have a capability to resync the encoder positions for tracking. 
Encoder positions are to be retained when running the Main drives during 
any Maintenance manual functions. 
 
5. 
Model ID tracking for up to 4 models shall be provided. Model ID’s to PLC 
shall be either through Error Proofing or AVS interface. 
 
6. 
HMI screens shall be provided to control the skillet heights dynamically and 
shall have the capability of handling up to 4 models.  
9.15.7 
Holdback Drives 
9.15.7.1 
Holdback rollers are used to apply constant pressure retarding the flow of the 
skillet "train".  This holdback drive shall be mounted at the opposite end of the 
skillet friction drives and shall be located inside a fenced area. 
9.15.7.2 
Holdback system will be designed as a friction type wheel driven by a torque 
(slip) motor running in the opposite direction of flow.  Counter-pressure will be 
constant and consistent.  No surging or tugging should felt on the skillet train as it 
flows down the system.  
9.15.7.3 
The torque motor will have the ability to run one skillet in the forward and reverse 
direction of travel. 
9.15.7.4 
A reversing starter with properly sized overloads will be used for this application.  
Thermal protection of the motor shall also be provided independent of the motor 
starter overloads.  This motor will run "hot" under normal conditions and should 
be clearly identified as such. 
9.15.7.5 
Brakes will be used on these motors to maintain the spacing of the skillets during 
power-down conditions. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 275 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.15.8 
Accelerator/Decelerator (Undocking/Docking) Drives 
9.15.8.1 
Accelerator drives are used to separate the skillets at the end of the production 
train. They are motor driven friction wheels. Decelerators are the same type 
assemblies used to "dock" skillets at the beginning of the skillet train. 
9.15.8.2 
VFD's will be used to run these motors. 
9.15.8.3 
A spring-loaded friction wheel assembly shall be designed by the mechanical 
Contractor and mounted within the decelerator drive assembly but not attached 
to the drive train mechanics for the purpose of an encoder. This encoder is used 
to track the position of the docking skillet as it travels to the friction drives. 
9.15.8.4 
The accelerator assembly must be fully engaged by the skillet train and 
disengaged from the holdback assembly before separation of the skillet can be 
started. This will allow for smooth transition of skillets from the train. This 
therefore requires that the motor and brake be separately driven. 
9.15.8.5 
Separate clutch engagement/disengagement devices should be used to 
engage/disengage the motor from its friction assembly. 
9.15.8.6 
Disconnects shall be provided for each motor and brake. 
9.15.8.7 
Docking shall be provided that is independent of the speed of either the 
decelerator motor and/or friction drives. Changing the production line speeds 
shall not require any manual intervention in the docking process. 
9.15.9 
Skillet Conveyor Power Roll Beds 
9.15.9.1 
The requirements identified in GVOSS Section 9.8 Floor Conveyors shall be 
adhered to and is hereby made part of this specification. 
9.15.9.2 
The conveyor track shall be designed as a belt driven roller-based system. The 
rollers shall be placed on a track in the floor and skillets shall be guided down the 
center of these rollers. 
9.15.9.3 
The rollers shall be made of a Urethane material enabling the skillets to be 
guided along a smooth and quiet path. All roller bearings are to be lube-for-life 
and require no maintenance. The Vendor shall provide sample roller and bearing 
for approval prior to purchase. 
9.15.9.4 
Inline and cross-wise transfers must be smooth with no noticeable skillet "bump" 
or rough transition between rollers and between adjacent PRB tables and or 
transfers. A minimum of 3 rolls shall contact the skillet at any time, and rolls shall 
be spaced such that no more than 1/3 of the overall skillet platform length (in the 
direction of travel) is unsupported.  
9.15.9.5 
All Power Roll Conveyors shall be VFD controlled for smooth operation. Standby 
VFD's are not required on these conveyors unless specified in the detailed 
equipment descriptions for the specific project. 
9.15.9.6 
Cross line bypass starters/contactors are not required on roller conveyors. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 276 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.15.9.7 
All Right-Angle Transfer (RAT) lifting frames shall be designed with a mechanical 
means of controlling gravity to ensure safe maintenance access to areas under 
the elevated portion of the equipment. The gravity pin and receiver designs shall 
comply with GVOSS section 9.4.11 unless noted otherwise within this 
specification. Each RAT conveyor shall include a gravity pin storage stand 
located outside the perimeter fence near the perimeter fence access gate. The 
gravity pin receiver hole shall be designed to account for the controls device 
up/down positioning tolerance. 
9.15.10 
Skillet Conveyor Turntables 
9.15.10.1 
The requirements identified in GVOSS Section 9.8 Floor Conveyors shall be 
adhered to and is hereby made part of this specification. 
9.15.10.2 
Conveyor track on turntables shall be designed as a belt driven roller-based 
system. 
9.15.10.3 
Rollers shall be made of a Urethane material enabling the skillets to be guided 
along a smooth and quiet path.  All rollers bearings to be tapered roller bearings, 
to counteract the inherent thrust load imposed during rotation.  Contractor is to 
provide a sample roller assembly, for approval, prior to purchase. 
9.15.10.4 
Design and/or concept of turntable (if required) shall be provided with 
Contractor’s bid. 
9.15.11 
Skillet Conveyor Drop Lifters 
9.15.11.1 
The requirements identified in GVOSS Section 9.4 Safety Requirements for 
Conveyors shall be adhered to and is hereby made part of this specification. All 
conflicts between this specification or the project specifications and the GVOSS 
Section 9.4 shall be documented by the Contractor and the list shall be submitted 
with the Contractor proposal. 
9.15.11.2 
The requirements identified in GVOSS Section 9.9 Drop Lifters shall be adhered 
to and is hereby made part of this specification. 
9.15.11.3 
Drive Equipment: 
 
1. 
Each unit shall be driven by an ISO standard geared motor unit with an AC 
inverter control for ramped acceleration or deceleration. A BUHL coupling 
shall be supplied for engaging locking pins, etc. 
 
2. 
Drive equipment shall be readily available from the host country of the plant. 
 
3. 
Easily accessible dog clutches to engage or disengage the drives. Both 
mating parts of the clutches shall be hardened. 
 
4. 
All vertical drop/lifter motors will have a field disconnect switch at the motor 
in addition to the main power disconnect at the Distributive Control Panel.   

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 277 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
5. 
All high lifts shall be provided with safety pins, safety posts, and/or 
removable safety stops and interlocks required to maintain position of the lift 
at both the raised, lowered, and all intermediate transfer positions 
indefinitely for maintenance and safety purposes. 
 
6. 
Lubrication Zerk fittings which are not accessible during production or those 
which are internal to the equipment, safety caging, and enclosures shall be 
terminated at a central lube block. Lube blocks provided shall be installed at 
a location accessible while the equipment is operating. Location shall be 
included in the design review and approval drawings. 
 
7. 
The weight of the counterbalance will be calculated considering the lift 
carriage and other significant factors such as belt weight, rolling friction, 
eccentric loading on the vertical carriage acceleration / deceleration forces 
and other specific factors that affect the system. The design shall be such 
that a downward tension will be applied to the drive drum from the carriage 
side of the lift regardless of whether the carriage is loaded or empty (the 
tension on the drive drum will not reverse with or without a payload present).  
9.15.11.4 
Lift Belts: 
 
1. 
The belts shall have a minimum factor of safety of 10:1. Test certificates 
shall be supplied for all lift belts and fittings. 
 
2. 
The carriage and counterweights shall be connected to the belts. 
 
3. 
Slack belt limit switches shall be installed to indicate when belt tensioning is 
required. 
 
4. 
Belt connections to counterbalance and lift frames shall be designed to 
eliminate the possibility of twisting. 
 
5. 
Belt speeds shall not exceed 150 feet per minute without Owner approval. 
 
6. 
The drive equipment for lift belts shall be positioned at high level.  Non-
detachment pulley guards shall be installed to prevent the belt sliding off the 
pulley under slack conditions.  Floor level drives will be acceptable with 
Owner's prior approval 
9.15.11.5 
Lift and Lower Roller Table: 
 
1. 
The lift and lower roller table shall be rigidly mounted to the mast structure 
so that deflections at table are less than 20 thousands of an inch (0.5 mm) 
at high and low levels. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 278 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
2. 
Roller bed sections shall be suitably stiffened or braced to support the skillet 
at the central position or at 20" (500 mm) offset either side of the center.  
These sections shall incorporate stops at each end to prevent skillet 
overrun. 
 
3. 
At all lift entrance and exit points, table locks shall be installed to guarantee 
table alignment during transit.  Proximity or other switches shall be included 
to detect the locks are fully open and fully closed. 
4. 
The lift station shall be equipped with mechanical devices to assure that the 
stationary and moving rails are properly aligned at all levels regardless of 
variations in the load weight.  These devices shall be installed in a position 
to operate directly on the rails, not remotely on another part of the system, 
such as the counterbalance or the drive unit. 
 
5. 
Sensor switches shall be provided at the ends of the lift table at all 
elevations to assure the rail is properly positioned before allowing the carrier 
to travel. 
 
6. 
The lift table shall be equipped with mechanically actuated end stops at both 
ends to assure that the skillet cannot run off the ends of the table.  These 
stops must be designed with sufficient strength to withstand a collision with 
a skillet moving at high speed with no damage or effect on function of the 
stop. 
 
7. 
These stops, as well as all other impacting parts, shall be equipped with 
sound deadening bumpers to minimize noise and prevent metal to metal 
contact. 
 
8. 
Any electrical equipment on the moving table shall be provided with flexible 
power cables. These cables are to be festooned so that the cables shall not 
be subjected to bending that would produce cable fatigue and/or signal 
interruption. Cables shall not be bundled but be individually secured.  
Control conductors shall be in a separate cable from the power conductors. 
9.15.11.6 
Lift Structure: 
 
1. 
The lift and lower unit structure shall be a rigid 4-post design, with bracing to 
the building structure if necessary. Lift carriage and counterweights are 
guided directly at the frame through guiding rollers. Guide rail parts shall be 
machined to provide true alignment over the length of carriage movement. 
Guide rollers shall be eccentric design to allow for adjustment. 
 
2. 
An access ladder and maintenance platform shall be supplied for access to 
all sprockets and proximity switches at the top of the structure.  Cage 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 279 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
ladders shall comply with Ford Motor Company standards. 
9.15.11.7 
Equipment Guards: 
 
1. 
Safety caging shall be installed around the unit in accordance with GVOSS 
section 9.4. A minimum of one maintenance access door with local lockout 
shall be included to allow for access to all areas within the fenced area. If 
required, additional access doors shall be included to provide this access to 
the entire area. 
2. 
Safety doors shall be electrically interlocked with double cams and plunger 
limits to Ford Motor Company safety standard specifications for automation 
equipment. The Contractor shall be familiar with Ford safety requirements. 
 
3. 
Wherever loading or unloading is required at lift or lower section locations, 
transparent sliding doors shall be installed which shall isolate all equipment 
when opened. Alternatively, Owner approved light beams may be installed 
to isolate all equipment when the beam is broken. 
 
4. 
At screen guard level, a sliding (or hinged) service bridge mounted on 
rollers shall be supplied and installed.  This shall be complete with toe 
plates, handrails and pin locking facilities to enable locking of the bridge in 
either position. The bridge shall be electrically interlocked with the lower 
section. When maintenance actions are required, the bridge shall be moved 
over the carriage aperture to cover all open gaps. 
 
5. 
At screen guard level, red transparent plastic curtains shall be hung one 
meter away from each screen guard end to guard against accidental 
descent of personnel through the aperture. 
 
6. 
At skillet entrance and exit points, spring loaded silhouette guards shall stop 
all movements if operated by misplaced carrier components or by 
personnel. 
 
7. 
Fixed mesh guards of 500 mm minimum height shall be installed integral to 
the entrance and exit apertures. This design shall include an Owner 
approved light guard in the enclosure below carrier level to ensure that no 
intrusions by personnel are unnoticed by the system. 
 
8. 
All motors of 10 hp or greater shall have a 6-inch service rail with hand 
trolley to provide for maintenance removal or installation of motors which 
are not accessible from the floor (i.e. are installed on balconies or overhead 
platforms). All vertical lifts, and similar lifts shall include hoists, bridges, and 
similar equipment for equipment maintenance, product removal, and where 
specified to sustain product operations. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 280 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9. 
All elevated shafts, sprockets, bearings, sheaves, pulleys, drives, controls 
and devices shall be easily accessible via standard catwalk. Stairs for 
maintenance purposes shall be provided to access the elevated 
mechanisms, components, controls, catwalk, all controls, etc. 
 
10. Stairways or rung ladders when specified to overhead platforms shall be 
installed at locations which do not present a pinch point to moving 
production carriers, transfers, and components. They shall be installed to 
eliminate interferences to Material Handling operations. 
11. Fire protection shall be provided in the original proposal under all elevated 
balconies, catwalks, platforms and similar equipment to suit the Owner's 
current IRI guidelines. 
 
12. The Contractor shall provide high lifts that include a safety cage enclosure 
with light screens at the floor entry and exit gates, personnel access gates, 
gate locks and safety pull cords for personnel protection, etc.  Safety gates 
and light screens shall be provided with interlock to immediately stop auto 
function of the equipment when maintenance personnel require access. 
Enclosure shall be high enough to prevent parts or product from exiting the 
enclosure. 
 
13. Positive mechanical stops shall be provided on the power roll tables to 
eliminate product shifting concerns when the lift is in transit. Positive stops 
shall function at each entry and exit position. Bumper rail shall be provided 
for each skillet continuous for the full length of the lift vertical transition to 
prevent skillet from rolling off lift while in transit. 
9.15.11.8 
Belt Break Detection: 
 
1. 
Slack belt detection limit switches shall be installed to stop all lift and lower 
movements if the belt breaks or slack condition occurs. 
 
2. 
In the event of a belt breakage, the second belt shall hold the load and be 
able to stop at its current position. 
9.15.11.9

1. 
A floor level and upper level lift HMI station outside of the safety cage shall 
be provided for all lift stations.  This station must provide a maintenance 
operator with the ability to have complete control of the lift station at low 
speed through the use of pushbuttons.  Manual jog controls should include, 
but not be limited to, "Lift Up", "Lift Down", "Index Skillet", etc. 
 
2. 
Any auxiliary devices such as locks or open and close functions or 
positioners shall also have pushbutton controls.  No safety circuits will be

9.15.12 
Skillets 
9.15.12.1 
Skillet Frame: 
 
1. 
The Contractor responsible for performing a FEA and submitting to the 
Owner for review and approval. 
 
2. 
Steel tube construction, welded. The design of the top surface shall be such 
that the wood sheeting sections shall be continuous – the only steel tubing 
showing shall be the perimeter frame. 
 
3. 
Skillet frame/superstructure to be designed such that all mechanical & 
electrical items can be accessed/serviced/replaced from the top side of the 
skillet. 
 
4. 
Each skillet to have four integral lifting attachment points, complete with 
lifting eyes, and covers to cap the attachment point holes when they are not 
in use. 
 
5. 
Design for wheel / crane load weight distribution. 
 
6. 
Square +/- 1.5mm 
 
7. 
Roller surface flatness +/- 1 mm 
 
8. 
Overall flatness +/- 2mm 
 
9. 
Fixture built. 
 
10. Ford to approve fixture and manufacturing process prior to build 
 
11. Paint top and side surfaces of tube safety orange 
 
12. Two skillet ID tags; one located in the middle of the top leading edge, and 
the other located in the middle of the top trailing edge of the skillet. 
 
13. Integral sweepers to be installed on each skillet such that they sweep debris 
from the aisle crossing pop-up section in an effort to eliminate skillet 
skewing.  Sweepers to be installed on all leading-edge surfaces that are 
directly over an aisle crossing pop-up section.  
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 284 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
14. Attachment of Load wheel brackets, collector shoe assemblies, and all other 
removable brackets or assemblies shall be bolted with through holes to 
allow for a bolt and nut connection.  Easy access to the bolt and nut must be 
provided, ideally from the top surface of the skillet (by removing the wood 
surface) 
 
15. The Skillet frames shall be processed through a stress-relief procedure after 
fabrication. 
9.15.12.2 
Skillet Platform: 
 
1. 
Top surface of skillet to be plywood, with top of plywood flush with perimeter 
tubing. 
 
2. 
The largest allowable gap on the top surface of the skillet is 1/8". 
 
3. 
Lexan windows to view collector shoes and electrical devices.  Window shall 
be a minimum of 24" x 24". The Lexan shall be installed in 2 pieces. The 
first (lower) piece shall be a 5/8" minimum thickness and a second piece 
(3/8" or as required) shall be installed on top of this. The purpose is to 
facilitate easy replacement of the (inexpensive) top piece when it gets 
scratched, while preserving the (expensive) thick lower piece. 
 
4. 
Removable wood access panels over mechanical and electrical devices 
complete with flush mount compression latches to eliminate bulging and trip 
hazards on the skillet platform decking – minimum (4), or (one) at each 
corner – and (2) stainless steel heavy-duty flush lifting handles centered on 
the access panel. 
 
5. 
Wood to be secured with the ability to remove wood panels. Fasteners shall 
be flush with the plywood surface producing a flush and continuous surface.  
The fastener holes in the plywood shall be countersunk as required to 
achieve a flush surface.  
 
6. 
Disconnect mount location shall be permanently marked on the surface of 
the skillet. 
 
7. 
Plywood shall be coated with a minimum of 3 coats of waterproof industrial 
floor coating sealer, gloss finish. The sides of the plywood shall be coated 
with additional applications as required to seal this absorbing surface of the 
plywood. The fastener holes and countersink holes shall be made prior to 
sealing the plywood. The material proposed to be approved by the Ford 
Conveyor Engineer.

9.15.12.3 
On-Board Skillet Adjustable Vehicle Lifts 
 
1. 
Scissor lifts shall be manufactured by Handling Specialty, FlexLift, or 
equivalent.  Scissor lift Supplier must be identified in the Contractor's bid.  
Regardless of scissor lift manufacturer, the following information is to be 
submitted with the Contractor's bid: 
A. 
Expected useful life 
B. 
Duty cycle 
C. 
Raise speed 
D. 
Lower speed 
 
2. 
The top surface around vehicle pick-up details shall be skinned with sheet 
metal. No more than 1/8" gap allowed between any two parts. 
 
3. 
The lift shall be equipped with an anti-freefall device so if there is a 
component failure, the lift will maintain its existing height / position. Anti-free 
fall device must conform to ANSI MH29.1 Sect 8.1.2. and be re-settable 
remotely, without requiring to access the area under the lift platform. 
 
4. 
The lifts shall be skirted with a heavy-duty vinyl accordion style skirt.  The 
skirt shall be attached to the top of the lift table with Velcro. The skirt color 
shall be yellow and black alternating horizontal stripes. 
 
5. 
Each skillet lift shall be equipped with a wire encoder, or similar, so that the 
vertical position, or height, can be communicated to the on-board controller. 
9.15.13 
On-Board Skillet Controls 
9.15.13.1 
Controller: 
 
1. 
The lift controller shall be solid state, with independent processors for the 
controller, I/O modules, and inverters. 
 
2. 
There shall be configurable I/O for the lift devices (proximity switches, rope 
encoder, and the like) that utilize quick disconnects and labeled for easy 
maintenance. The incoming power and lift motor connectors shall be rugged 
connections and labeled for easy maintenance. 
 
3. 
There shall be a diagnostic and information LED readout that is integrated 
into the controller. The wood panel mounted directly above the controller 
diagnostic and information LED readout shall have a cover such that it is 
visible without removing the wood panel. There shall be a diagnostic label 
attached to the controller beside the diagnostic and information LED readout 
that provides assistance for interpreting the LED readout during 
maintenance and troubleshooting. This label too shall be visible without

removing the wood panel. 
4. 
There shall be mounting holes available for attaching brackets to mount to 
the skillet. The heatsink shall be integrated into the controller. 
 
5. 
There shall be a wireless, or plug-in, handheld pendant for transfer of 
operating parameters, use for manual control (jog, reset, and the like), and 
for reading parameters, error data, skillet or product specific data. 
 
6. 
Motor thermistor input. 
 
7. 
The controller shall have software that has the capability for configurable 
heights of 5 mm increments and horizontal positioning. This software shall 
also have functionality to acquire and transfer skillet or product data, error 
logging data, and controller operational data, with the supervisory 
processor. This software shall be flexible as to add custom faults, displays, 
and the like. 
 
8. 
The controller shall have parameter settings to include speeds, acceleration 
and deceleration ramps, fault tolerances, and the like. 
 
9. 
The controller shall have the capability to control lift specific inputs and 
outputs such as motor thermistors, safety catch inputs, proximity switches 
and the like. 
 
10. The controller shall have a separate disconnecting means to isolate 
incoming power. This disconnect shall be separate from the controller 
enclosure. 
 
11. All electrical connections to the controller shall be quick disconnect for ease 
of removal and replacement. 
9.15.13.2 
Positioning: 
 
1. 
The control shall use proximity switches, photo switches, or encoded rail 
(either incremental or absolute encoding) for linear positioning that provides 
highly configurable horizontal positioning and allows for positioning 
granularity appropriate for the application. 
 
2. 
The controller shall use rope or laser encoder or motor encoder for vertical 
positioning. The positioning granularity shall be appropriate for the 
application.

Supervisory Processor Communications:

There shall be a means for transfer of parameters, table heights and

2.

Invalid program or parameters 
4. 
Inverter faults (over voltage, internal fault, etc.) 
5. 
Motor faults (over temperature, over current) 
6. 
Vertical speed fault (set just above max speed) 
7. 
Vertical direction fault 
8. 
Lower and upper limit fault 
9. 
[Insert Ford Standard VFD Parameters]

2. 
The controller manufacturer shall supply, if required or requested, a test 
bench for the controller to be connected to for controller functionality 
verification and maintenance.

9.15.14 
General Skillet Conveyor Electrical Requirements 
9.15.14.1 
When a skillet system is provided by one Contractor, it shall be responsible for all 
controls throughout the system. The CPU and DCP's (Distributed Control Panel) 
shall provide all control, power and monitoring of the skillets. Distributed Control 
Panels (DCP) shall be each provided for each conveyor unit. Each conveyor unit 
is defined by a skillet position, not by motor. Therefore, for example, each roller 
bed would have its own DCP, but if there were two accelerator motors, both 
VFD's would reside in a single control cabinet. Flex I/O Blocks should be utilized 
for these units and their associated I/O. Note that the docking (decelerators), 
main friction motors and rail conductor power should reside in a single control 
DCP (although large). The reason for this is the commonality of switches, 
ganging of VFD's for the motors, minimizing interlocks from one unit to the other 
regarding run/stops on the production lines and providing a single point lockout 
for all conveyor units within the docking area.  Run/stops for the production line 
should be run to this DCP.  Dividing of skillet CPU's shall not be done across the 
skillet production line. It shall reside somewhere within the transfer areas 
between skillet lines.   
9.15.14.2 
A typical skillet control system will consist of a Central Processor Unit (CPU), a 
Power Distribution Panel (PDP), large Docking and lifter DCP's and smaller 
DCP's for each conveyor unit. 
9.15.14.3 
The CPU panel will have the PLC, RSView station / programming terminal. The 
CPU panel will provide the logic for the control of each individual production line 
and the conveyor units entering and exiting the production lines. Great care 
should be used not to divide the friction and holdback/accelerator units from each 
other, while also attempting to duplicate production lines. 
9.15.14.4 
The PDP (if necessary) will provide power to each individual unit from a primary 
power source. It is used to limit the number of plant power drops required for the 
skillet system. An average skillet system could have two or three drops per 
production line, one to the CPU, one to the PDP and one to the Docking DCP.  
Each has its own interface modules, I/O modules, isolation transformers 
(PDP/Docking DCP only) and the magnetic controls. All interlocks will be 
distributed through the CPU panel. Each DCP would interlock to the CPU panel 
and the CPU panel will re-distribute the interlocks throughout the system. 
9.15.14.5 
The CPU will also control any QAS and Error Proofing controls necessary for 
each production line unless otherwise dictated in the Scope of Work. 
9.15.14.6 
All motor connections will be made with quick connections utilizing Harding style 
10-pin connectors. All motors up to and including 5 HP will have these 
connections. Deviations may be made based on motor manufacturer per VO 
Project Engineer written approval. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 289 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.15.14.7 
Manual functions at the docking conveyor are to include but are not limited to 
manual control of each conveyor unit separately (docking motors and /or friction 
motors) as well as a manual docking procedure for recovery. This would include 
manual driving of production line to stop position and docking of a single skillet to 
that position. 
9.15.14.8 
All conveyors except the docking station shall have the ability to position in both 
the forward and backwards directions. This may require the use of a reverse 
deceleration switch and/or second set of switches for skillets in the raised or 
lower positions of a VDL. Deviation of this requirement may be obtained in writing 
from the VO project engineer. 
9.15.14.9 
The Contractor shall install energy lockout controls, devices, components, and 
labeling on all equipment. Reference the Energy Control and Power Lockout 
Program included in General Specifications section. 
9.15.14.10 
All covered pits shall be complete with LED light fixtures per FCS Section 26 51 
00, shielded fixtures, on/off duplex switch, Russell Stolls receptacle, and 
maintenance F.F.R.  Lighting shall provide minimum 60-foot candles. 
9.15.14.11 
If field located Armor-Start or similar drive units are used to power individual 
motors located in a pit or recessed location, they shall be mounted at floor level 
along the perimeter fencing or handrail.  
9.15.14.12 
If field devices, control or field junction boxes, Armor-start type controllers, 
pneumatic or other controls or devices are located under the skillet plating (as in 
the area of an automatic Glass Cell station), a hinged access door shall be 
provided to access the devices or equipment. The device shall be permanently 
marked on the door.  
9.15.14.13 
Central Processor Unit: 
 
1. 
The CPU will be individually powered from the three-phase AC bus. (FNA 
Example 480 VAC) It will contain an Allen Bradley PLC, a PC monitor / 
programmer / RSView station and required modules. Standard software 
block logic will be developed for the standard zones and controls. Additional 
blocks will be developed for special requirements but will not be integrated 
with the standard blocks. 
9.15.14.14 
Power Distribution Panel: 
 
1. 
The PDP provides three-phase AC (FNA example: 480VAC, 3-phase, 60 
hertz power) to the skillet conveyor units. The units powered by this PDP 
panel could include but are not limited to individual DCP's and the lifter 
DCP. The PDP panel shall include a power isolation transformer to ensure 
that ground faults on the plant bus system shall not affect the skillet system.  
The power isolation transformer shall be three-phase AC (FNA example: 
480 VAC delta primary to 480 VAC wye secondary, with center tap to 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 290 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
ground). The power isolation transformer shall be the only device wired to 
the PDP main fused (Next-Gen style) or circuit breaker (GVOSS style) 
disconnect. 
9.15.14.15 
Distributed Control Panel: 
 
1. 
A DCP panel will be provided for each drop / lifter, track switch, automated 
load and unload and repair spurs and will contain the I/O for the local control 
and the magnetic components required for the power control and signals for 
associated areas.   
 
2. 
Flex I/O and/or modular wiring are the only two approved means of 
communications between the DCP and CPU. 
9.15.14.16 
HMIs: 
 
1. 
HMIs (style dictated in scope of work) shall, at a minimum, have the 
following screens: 
A. 
Status of all skillet production lines, including, but not limited to, 
RUNNING, FAULTED, AUTO/MANUAL, QAS STOP, RUN STOPPED 
and HELD. 
B. 
Status of all QAS stations within that production line’s CPU. 
C. 
Status of all switches (ON/OFF/FAULTED) within that production line’s 
CPU, including lifts and transfer areas. 
D. 
Status of all E-stops, pull cords and run/stops for that production line's 
CPU. 
E. 
Fault banner to include all fault messages for that production line.  
Fault banner shall include unique address and/or tag for each fault 
condition to allow for PLC searching capability. 
F. 
All encoder positions and manual reset/set capability. 
 
2. 
HMI quantity and location shall be dictated by scope of work unique to each 
conveyor system. 
 
3. 
If pushbutton stations are used for manual control, reverse functionality 
(with positioning) shall be maintained for each conveyor unit. 
 
9.15.15 
Product touch details/perches for skillets, carriers, etc. general notes: 
9.15.15.1 
The Contractor will be responsible to verify all existing carrying details, obtain the 
3D models of existing and new model vehicles and engineer new carrying details 
to accommodate existing and future products concurrently. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 291 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.15.15.2 
Ford will supply the Contractor with a DS list for Master Packages and the 
contractor will be responsible for data mining Team Center to obtain and create 
the different vehicle models at each transfer point. 
9.15.15.3 
Coordination with VOME, BCE & Product Development, for locating / touch-point 
detail locations approval. 
9.15.15.4 
Coordination with VOME Process Engineers to ensure new carrier details meet 
all process requirements. 
9.15.15.5 
All data mining to ensure carrier design & engineering conforms to Product 
design. 
9.15.15.6 
Portions of the details that contact the product shall be UHMW (or similar), while 
the main structure of the details shall be steel.  There shall be no metal-to-metal 
contact with the product, unless approved by the VOME engineer.   Anytime 
product is touched, UHMW (or similar) material shall be used.  Exception to this 
rule is that locating pins shall be hardened steel.   Class ‘A’ product surfaces 
shall not be contacted under any circumstances. 
Exception: The Carriers that run through the Paint Ovens will have nesting 
details made out of steel. All edges are to be rounded and designed such that 
they don’t damage the floor pan at the contact point, leave no scuff marks and 
cause no damage to the corrosion protection. 
9.15.15.7 
The main structure of the details and the carrier support points shall utilize 4-way 
slotted (adjustable) connections.  These connections are to be welded or dowel-
pinned 3 weeks after MP2 starts. Elevations (Z-axis) of the touch point details 
need to be adjustable via steel shim packs. 
9.15.15.8 
Contractor shall use common product characteristics or clearances to position 
touch-point details.  Touch point details shall be fixed pins (preferred) or fixed 
wipers where possible to simplify system requirements. 
Touch point details, whether pins or wipers, shall constrain the product 
accurately enough to support the dimensional requirements of the specific 
application. Locator Pins are required for Carriers running through Paint Shop 
Sealer Operations and for Carriers running through Prime / Base Coat / Clear 
Coat Booths. If the installation of Pins is not feasible, a written Deviation Request 
must be submitted to the VO Engineer. 
9.15.15.9 
Details shall be engineered, designed and detailed to function with the transfers 
on/off the carrier/skillet/transfer details. 
9.15.15.10 
Regardless of whether locating pins or guides are used, details shall be designed 
to prevent or contain potential body movement (sliding) relative to the carrier to 
less than 5 mm in “X” (f/a) direction for safety & quality purposes. 
9.15.15.11 
Contractor shall provide 3D *. jt models / assembly drawings to Ford as part of 
the design/drawing approval process.  These models shall show all parts that will 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 292 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
be required/used in the final detailed design (e.g., fastening hardware), together 
with each different type of vehicle model with clamshell. Finalized jt as-builts shall 
be uploaded into Team Center for each model type. 
9.15.15.12 
No sharp edges…all edges shall be rounded-over. 
9.15.15.13 
Contractor shall fabricate (2) prototype sets of details for early shipment to 
Dearborn for prototype builds. 
9.15.15.14 
Center of Gravity - Vehicle touch points to be located to support the vehicle 
based on a given COG from Ford.  The Contractor shall confirm the accuracy of 
COG during prototype testing.  Contractor to work with VOME to ensure product 
being used for testing is built up to proper trim level; in the event parts are not 
available the contractor shall add weights to the product to simulate the missing 
parts.  “Tip Test” to be performed during prototype builds and buy-off. For details 
carrying units through production zones, 100 ft lbs. of force is to be applied at the 
extreme leading and trailing edge of the vehicle to ensure the vehicle will remain 
in the details. For details carrying units during transfers or through non-
production zones, 50 ft lbs. of force is to be applied at the extreme leading and 
trailing edge of the vehicle to ensure the vehicle will remain in the details.

9.17 
FLAT-TOP CONVEYORS 
9.17.1 
General 
9.17.1.1 
All new Flat Top conveyor installations shall be dual-strand layout configuration 
with escalator-type flat-top chain as defined within this specification and as 
shown in Branch Standard drawings 3ZF-32103. 
9.17.1.2 
All sharp edges that could potentially come into contact with a tire or wheel, 
including the plated edges of platforms and the edges of angles, shall be 
removed or covered by a split, smooth, round tube or pipe. 
9.17.1.3 
All welds shall be ground smooth and all weld spatter shall be removed on any 
surface that could possibly contact a tire or wheel. 
9.17.1.4 
All bolted connections shall utilize a minimum of two bolts.  All slotted 
connections must be blocked or welded after final field adjustments have been 
made. 
9.17.1.5 
All pillow blocks and flange bearings must be blocked following final alignment. 
9.17.1.6 
The drive motor, reducer, coupling, coupling guard, and driven device shall be 
mounted on a common adjustable drive base. 
9.17.1.7 
All conveyor chain and compensating sprockets shall be steel with flame 
hardened teeth and shall be of a keyless coupling design.    
9.17.1.7.1 
The flat-top conveyor chain head shaft and tail shaft sprockets shall be designed 
for 8 tooth 18-inch pitch conveyor chain (see GVOSS section 9.17.7). The 
conveyor chain sprockets are to be keyed in pairs. 
9.17.1.7.2 
The compensating sprocket shall be designed for 8 tooth (64 tooth effective) 
RC180 multi-strand drive chain. The conveyor supplier is responsible for the 
compensating sprocket design and set-up required to suit the application. 
Drawing(s) shall depict the final locations of the sprockets relative to one another. 
 
                                             
 
  
                       Figure No. 9.17.1.7.2 – Typical Installation of Compensating Sprocket,   
                  Conveyor Sprocket and Roller Chain Drive Sprocket when timing is correct. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 295 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.17.1.7.3 
Design Review at 30% completion shall include chain details regarding intended 
manufacturer, Ultimate Tensile Strength, and Allowable Tensile Strength. 
 
9.17.1.8 
Provide straight track section on return side of chain under tension in the head or 
tail pit for the addition of a lubricator or greaser as specified in the project 
detailed equipment description. 
9.17.1.9 
The flat-top conveyor top track shall be constructed using ASCE 30# rails 
fastened to the support frame using No. 114 rail clips, bolts and nuts. Adjacent 
rail sections shall be fastened together using standard ASCE splice joint bars, 
bolts and nuts sized to the rail. Floor support framework shall be provided at 
each rail end for all rail splices. Return track shall be 4" x 4" x 3/8" angle with all 
joints welded solid and ground smooth. A "Sure Alloy" wear bar shall be mounted 
on the return track. The top strands of the track shall be removable. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
                       Figure No. 9.17.1.9 – General Arrangement & Standard Naming Convention  
                      for Flat Top Track  
9.17.1.10 
Head and Tail pits shall be at such a depth that after support steel for the cover 
plate is installed, 6’ 6” or 1982mm clearance from the pit floor to the bottom of the 
support steel is provided for maintenance and walls located such that to give a 
minimum clearance of 30" (762 mm) from any piece of equipment.  Refer to 
GVOSS Section 9.27 for additional pit design requirements. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 296 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.17.1.11 
The Head and Tail frame of the conveyor shall be located such that all vehicle 
models manufactured by the plant can be driven on to the conveyor in a straight-
on orientation after the vehicle makes a continuous turn towards the conveyor 
(three-point turns are not acceptable). 
9.17.1.12 
When re-using an existing Flat Top conveyor, and either the outside-to-outside, 
or inside-to-inside tire and wheel assembly dimension exceeds the outer, or inner 
Flat Top chain top plate boundary, it is permissible to shim-up the conveyor chain 
top strand, to gain clearance between the tire & wheel assembly and the 
adjacent cover plate / support steel. In addition to shimming, transition bars must 
be installed, the full length of the conveyor, on both sides of each strand.  
Transition bars must be welded to existing cover plate / support steel, with welds 
ground smooth. Refer to Figure 9.17.1.12, below, for general arrangement.  
Prior, written approval must be obtained from the Ford Engineer before 
proceeding with this option. 
 
  
 
Figure No. 9.17.1.12 – General Arrangement for Flat Top Chain Shimming 
9.17.1.13 
Except as noted in GVOSS paragraph 9.17.1.12, the Flat Top conveyor top-of-
chain elevation, including all associated pit cover plates and bar grating, etc. 
shall be equal to the adjacent plant finish floor elevation.

Figure No. 9.17.1.13 – General Arrangement for Flat Top Chain Elevation 
 
 
9.17.1.14 
The head frame, main/stand-by drive and tail frame assemblies shall be mounted 
and welded to grillage steel embedded in the pit floor. Anchoring the head frame, 
drive and tail frame assemblies directly to the concrete pit floor is not allowed. 
Intermediate track sections may be anchored to the concrete pit floor. 
9.17.1.15 
The Flat-Top conveyor controls system shall include field devices and program 
logic to provide a “vehicle at exit” overhead mounted ultrasonic sensor. This 
sensor shall be provided at the head end to stop the conveyor if the vehicle 
approaching the end of the conveyor is not driven away within cycle. This 
condition will be annunciated with an audible alarm mounted on top of the control 
panel. An auto restart will be included to restart conveyor operation once the 
photo eye is cleared. 
 
9.17.1.16 
The drive-on job spacing for DSFT shall be set by controls (no cleats on chain). 
a. Refer to GVOSS paragraph 9.18.4.1 for overhead mounted ultrasonic 
sensor requirements at drive-on. 
b. Refer to GVOSS paragraph 9.18.4.2 for red/green traffic light 
requirements for drivers to properly space units on the conveyor. 
c. Include visual factory signage and markers to show where the driver 
must stop the vehicle. Signs shall be minimum 4’x3’ stiff backed signs. 
Signs must be approved by Ford. 
d. Include all framework necessary to mount the sensors, lights and 
visual factory items. 
9.17.1.17 
Overhang between top plate and outboard rollers shall be minimized to reduce 
chain tipping moment. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014

Revision Date: June 8, 2025

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS

9.17.1.18 
All cover plate adjacent to the flat top conveyor shall have a minimum capacity of 
10,000lb wheel load. Cover plate capacity requirement is dependent on the 
application; cover plate capacity is subject to increase, i.e. a heavier vehicle will 
require a higher minimum cover plate capacity. The final cover plate capacity 
requirement is at the discretion of Ford’s approval. 
9.17.1.19 
Each gearmotor shall have its own over-running clutch to minimize mechanical 
work during gearmotor changeover.   
9.17.2 
End-Plate Assembly 
9.17.2.1 
End plates, or guards, shall be provided at the head and tail ends where the 
chain transfers into the floor and shall be painted yellow. 
9.17.2.2 
The Flat Top end-plate design shall be sufficient to support the weight of the 
vehicle during transfer to/from the Flat Top conveyor. For escalator style flat-top 
chain, the deflection in the end-plate shall be limited such that the finger details 
do not contact the flat top chain during transfer in a dynamic or static condition. 
9.17.2.3 
The individual finger details and end plate assembly in entirety shall be 
constructed from a single continuous homogenous steel plate (re: 3ZF-32103-20) 
welded to the end plate base as required. Use of laminated finger details is not 
acceptable 
9.17.2.4 
The Flat Top end-plate assembly in entirety, which specifically includes but not 
limited to the finger and gusset pieces, needs to be flush with the finished 
concrete floor. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
                    
 
Figure No. 9.17.2.4 – Illustration of Flush-to-Floor Homogenous Finger 
Details 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 299 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.17.2.5 
For Flat Top (non-escalator chain) conveyor installations, the end 
plate 
assembly 
design 
shall 
incorporate 
a 
hinged 
and 
counterweighted design that will allow small parts to pass below the 
plate but will cause the conveyor to stop if a larger object is caught 
at the end of the conveyor. Additionally, the hinged end plate 
design shall include the following: 
1. The hinged plate shall be counterweighted such that a 
maximum 50 pounds or 23 kg of force is required to pivot 
them from the original horizontal position. 
2. Designed such that the conveyor will shut down if the end of 
the end plate pivots 2" or 50 mm from the original horizontal 
position. 
The end plate safety device shall have two limit switches. The first 
limit switch will act as a stop when a smaller object is caught 
between the end plate and the flat top chain. Once the object is 
cleared, the conveyor will be able to be restarted by recycling the 
run/stop pushbutton off then on again. The second limit switch will 
detect a major object caught between the endplate and conveyor 
chain 
9.17.2.6 
A Finite Element Analysis of the End-Plate assembly shall be completed to 
identify the static and dynamic stress conditions and deflection in the end plate 
and finger details. The FEA shall be based on the minimum quantity of fingers 
allowed by the product and conveyor layout configuration and detail design 
dimensions. FEA shall include what assumptions were made regarding contact 
patch width and length. Reinforcement of finger details or additional components 
of the end plate assembly in any capacity is not permitted during FEA 
9.17.3 
Lead-In Assembly 
9.17.3.1 
Ball bearing type lead-ins are not acceptable. 
9.17.3.2 
The lead-in design shall consist of a series of UHMW rollers mounted on a steel 
shaft. The roller axles for wet or corrosive environment applications shall be 
fabricated from 416 stainless steel material. Both ends of the UHMW roller shall 
incorporate a machined radius to eliminate sharp corners. Refer to the following 
3ZF-drawings for flat-top conveyor lead-in design reference: 
• 003ZF-5540-37527-S363 STANDARD PROFILE LEAD-IN ASSEMBLY 
• 003ZF-5540-37528-S374 LOW PROFILE LEAD-IN ASSEMBLY 
These drawings are intended to illustrate specification intent, only. The conveyor 
supplier is responsible to ensure their provided design complies with the specific 
project and product specifications. See GVOSS section 3.19.1 for additional 
lead-in design requirements.

9.17.3.3 
To minimize the approach angle relative to the flat top conveyor chain flow, the 
tangent point of the lead-in shall be located as closely as possible to the point 
where the flat-top chain transitions to the plant floor level (re: GVOSS Figure No. 
9.17.3.3). 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure No. 9.17.3.3 – Sketch of Typical Flat-Top ConveyorSide Guide & 
Lead-In Arrangement 
9.17.3.4 
A checker plate cover plate shall be provided on the lead-in assembly between 
the angled UHMW rollers and the parallel UHMW side guide rollers. The cover 
plate shall be painted with alternating yellow/black “tiger stripe” pattern. 
9.17.4

9.17.4.1 
Ball bearing type tire guides are not acceptable. 
9.17.4.2 
Parallel roller type side guides shall be provided at the entrance to the flat top 
conveyor for a minimum of one complete job length (re: GVOSS Figure No. 
9.17.3.3). The parallel roller guides are required on the inside of the conveyor 
strands only. Refer to the following 3ZF-drawings for flat-top conveyor parallel 
roller side guide design reference:

9.17.4.3 
A checker plate cover plate shall be provided between the parallel roller guides at 
the flat-top conveyor entrance. The cover plate shall be painted with alternating 
yellow/black “tiger stripe” pattern. 
9.17.4.4 
Parallel roller guides shall be provided the full length of conveyor center/knee 
pits, both sides. The parallel guides shall be required on the inside of the 
conveyor strands only. 
9.17.4.5 
Pipe type guides shall be used where tire guides are specified for the full length 
of the conveyor, except as noted in Paragraphs 9.17.4.2 and 9.17.4.4 of this 
Section.  The transition from the roller type tire guide to the pipe type guide shall 
be designed such that it is impossible for the tire to contact the steel frame 
structure of the roller and pipe guide assemblies. If it is not possible to orient the 
pipe type tire guide entrance or exit to avoid all possible tire contact, then the end 
of the guide shall curve toward the floor, ending with the blunt end of the guide 
toward the floor (re: 3ZF-5540-37527-S362 for design intent reference drawing). 
9.17.4.6 
Provide support legs at all pipe type tire guide splices as shown in Figure No. 
9.17.4.6. 
9.17.4.7 
Full penetration groove welds, as shown in Figure No. 9.17.4.7, shall be used for 
all pipe type tire guide splices.  No butt welds are allowed. 
9.17.4.8 
All sharp edges on pipe type tire guides that could contact a tire or wheel shall be 
removed or covered by a split, smooth, round tube or pipe. 
9.17.4.9 
The pipe type guides shall be constructed from 1½" diameter Schedule #80 steel 
pipe and shall be painted Ford Safety Yellow. For wet applications, pipe guides 
and attachment clips shall be constructed out of 304 ASTM A312 stainless steel 
pipe.
