9.24 
AUTOMATED TRANSFERS 
9.24.1 
General 
9.24.1.1 
Automated Transfers are conveyors that move product from one conveyor 
system to another conveyor system. The equipment types included in this 
specification are fork transfer, lift transfer, secondary lifts and overhead 
crab/clamshell transfer systems. 
9.24.1.2 
This Contractor shall provide registered professional engineer review and 
approval of floor and panel point loading, baseplate and/or structural steel 
member sizing, truss/beam clamp and related support system details applicable 
to the design, fabrication and installation of the automated transfer systems at 
the design review meeting. 
9.24.1.3 
The Contractor shall be responsible for all calculations regarding equipment 
sizing, gearmotor selection and system cycle times. 
9.24.1.4 
The Contractor shall review the transfer automation application with the 
gearmotor Supplier for proper gearmotor selection. The sizing and cycle time 
calculations and gearmotor Supplier approval documentation will be submitted at 
the design review meeting. 
9.24.1.5 
Horsepower calculations transfer, lift and fork gearmotors shall comprehend: (1) 
the force required to accelerate/decelerate the load to steady state speed, and 
(2) the force to maintain constant velocity at steady state speed. 
9.24.1.6 
The cycle time calculations for transfer, lift and fork actions shall comprehend: (1) 
the time required to accelerate/decelerate the load to/from steady state speed, 
and (2) the time required to complete the required travel distance at the steady 
state speed. 
9.24.1.7 
All fasteners used to assemble forks, lifting cams, cam followers and guide rails 
shall be tightened to the specified torque and match-marked in compliance with 
the applicable NAAMS standard. Loctite 242 Blue shall be applied to each of 
these fasteners. 
9.24.1.8 
All motors/gearmotors for fork extension and lifting operation shall include an 
integral brake. 
9.24.1.9 
All equipment must be accessible and removable without requiring major 
disassembly of adjacent equipment. 
9.24.1.10 
All wearing surfaces – e.g., lifting cams, cam followers and wear bar, etc. – shall 
be attached such that these components can be replaced as required. 
9.24.1.11 
The adjacent conveyor systems shall be equipped with stop and positioning 
devices to precisely locate the product and/or carriers for the load and unload 
operation. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 338 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.24.1.12 
A detection switch (laser, photo eye or proximity) is to be used to validate that the 
vehicle and been properly transferred to a carrier.  If a miss-deck/miss-transfer 
has occurred, the equipment shall be faulted before the carrier leaves the 
transfer. 
9.24.1.13 
For Final Assembly Engineering applications only, automated transfers shall 
have a monitoring vision system that includes the below Keyence equipment, 
length and quantities to suit the application: 
1. IV2-G500MA Vision Sensor, monochrome standard head  
2. OP-87899 Polarizing Filter, for IV-HG150MA & 500CA or 500MA series 
3.  OP-87910 IV-HG Series Adjustable Mounting Bracket, for IV-HG series 
4. IV2-G30F Learning type controller for vision sensor, with learning mode. 
5. IV2-CP50 control panel module with panel mounting adapter. 
6.  IV2-H1 Vision Sensor Software 
7. OP-87904(IV) Connection cable between head and amplifier, IV-HG series, 
length to suit 
8. OP-87906(IV) IV-HG Series Digital I/O Cable, length to suit. 
9. The vision system shall be packaged in a junction box type enclosure to 
house the control module, amplifier and 1Tb size memory hard drive. 
10. The hard drive shall be used for storing backup images of previous transfer 
cycles. The system shall include a searchable menu to look up and view past 
transfer data. 
11. The SUPPLIER shall provide the adequately sized power drop to the vision 
system panel to suit the application. 
9.24.1.14 
A minimum of 19mm of clearance shall be provided between the product and the 
equipment or carriers, except where intended contact is required. 
9.24.1.15 
Fork Transfer, Lift Transfer, Gantry and Overhead Crab/Clamshell Transfer 
systems, etc. shall include “part present” detection at the charge conveyor 
location, discharge conveyor location and on the transfer mechanism. 
9.24.2 
Transfer Mechanical Safety Requirements 
9.24.2.1 
The requirements of GVOSS Section 9.4 Safety Requirements for Conveyors is 
included as part of this specification. This section is intended to highlight 
mechanical design requirements for safe automated transfer design. Where 
discrepancies may exist between this specification and the requirements 
identified in GVOSS Section 9.4, the requirements identified in GVOSS Section 
9.4 shall apply. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 339 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.24.2.2 
All automated transfers that incorporate lift/lower functionality shall be designed 
with a mechanical means of locking the load (lift sprocket, carriage frame, and 
counter-weight) to control gravity and ensure safe access to areas under the 
elevated portion of the equipment and parts for maintenance. If necessary, 
access ladders and platforms shall be installed to provide safe access to the 
gravity pin receiver locations. The location of the gravity pin receivers shall be 
such that maintenance personnel can engage the pins without requiring 
additional fall protection or personnel lifting equipment. The gravity pin receiver 
hole shall be designed to account for the lift belt stretch/wear-in and controls 
device up/down positioning tolerance. Each automated transfer conveyor shall 
include a gravity pin storage stand located outside the perimeter fence near the 
perimeter fence access gate. 
9.24.2.3 
All automated transfer safety pins used for the purpose of locking the equipment 
in place to overcome potential energy shall be electrically interlocked such that 
all lifting/lowering motion is prevented if the pins are not placed in the normally 
stored location. 
9.24.2.4 
A perimeter safety fence, in compliance with applicable GVOSS Section 1.3 and 
GVOSS 9.4 specifications, shall be installed around the transfer equipment, at 
the lower and upper level as required. 
9.24.2.5 
The conveyor Supplier shall provide an Owner-approved light screen at the 
entry/exit safety fence opening at the lower level. The conveyor controls shall be 
designed to immediately stop all lifting/lowering/transferring motion of the 
automated transfer and adjacent conveyor(s) when unauthorized access is 
detected. These controls shall be designed in compliance with applicable 
GVOSS Section 1.3, GVOSS 9.4 and GMS safety specifications for automated 
equipment. 
9.24.2.6 
At least one suitably positioned hinged access door, a minimum 1.0 meter (3 
feet) wide, shall be provided in the perimeter safety fence. 
9.24.2.7 
The personnel access gate(s) shall be provided with gate plug(s). The conveyor 
controls shall be designed to immediately stop automatic function of the 
equipment when unauthorized access is detected. These controls shall be 
designed to be in full compliance with applicable GVOSS Section 9.4, GVOSS 
and GMS safety specifications for automated equipment. 
9.24.3 
Part Locating Fixture Compliance 
9.24.3.1 
The Contractor shall verify the automated transfer and interfacing conveyor 
carrier/part locating and supporting fixtures on site using laser measuring tools. 
9.24.3.2 
For the checking fixtures, final adjustments of all carrier locating and checking 
devices/surfaces shall be performed and recorded using a laser tracker. The 
preferred supplier for accomplishing this task is Troy Design and Manufacturing 
(TDM). 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 340 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.24.3.3 
After checking fixtures are installed and laser aligned, the Contractor shall master 
the first carrier/skillet and move it to the transfer point to shoot the mastered 
carrier/skillet at the transfer station. The Contractor shall make all necessary 
adjustments to the conveyor and carrier locating devices to ensure the 
carrier/skillet is in the proper position for the transfer operation. 
9.24.3.4 
All Transfers are to be laser checked with each mastered carrier/skillet to ensure 
each carrier/skillet is located correctly for the transfer to take place. 
9.24.3.5 
If secondary lifts are utilized, the laser tracker provider shall verify repeatability of 
the secondary lifting device relative to locating the carrier/skillet in the same 
location every time. 
9.24.3.6 
The laser tracker provider shall verify repeatability of all locating devices for each 
conveyance type in the transfer by testing five (5) different mastered 
carriers/skillets in the transfer. The conveyor Contractor shall make all 
adjustments required to ensure repeatability is achieved. 
9.24.4 
Fork Transfers 
9.24.4.1 
Fork transfers are used to move product from one conveyor to another at right 
angles to the loading and unloading systems. Fork transfers are mounted directly 
to the plant floor (or mezzanine) where “monoplane” transfer is required. 
9.24.4.2 
Fork transfer forks may be mounted to a fixed frame or to a moving frame that 
raises/lowers not more than 24 inches (610 mm). Fork transfer applications 
requiring greater than 24 inches (610 mm) lift shall use Lift Transfers as 
described in GVOSS Section 9.24.5. 
9.24.4.3 
Fork design and construction: 
 
1. 
The fork transfer shall include two forks, minimum. Additional forks may be 
added to suit the load carrying requirements for the given application. 
 
2. 
Each fork shall be capable of supporting a 1100 lb. (500 kg) load, minimum. 
The maximum vertical deflection shall be 1/4 inch (6 mm) at the end of the 
fork with the fork fully extended. 
 
3. 
The fork design shall be a rack and spur type gearing system with 
stationary, intermediate and extended fork sections. 
 
4. 
The rack and spur gear shall be 20-degree pressure angle and shall be 
hardened to a minimum Rockwell C40. 
 
12. The backlash in the fork drive gear train shall be a maximum 1/8 inch (3mm) with 
one fork clamped. 
 
6. 
The fork stopping position(s) shall be repeatable within (+/-) 1/16 inch (1.5 
mm). 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 341 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
7. 
Cylindrical cam followers shall be used to support the fork assemblies. The 
cam follower shall be heavy-duty hex hole stud type mounting with sealed 
needle bearings and grease fitting for lubrication. 
 
8.     The fixture details that contact the product shall be fabricated from UHMW 
or durable nylon type material suitable to support and protect the product. 
The details shall be bolted to the fork assembly and shall provide for 
adjustment in the x-, y- and z-axis. 
 
9. 
The drive system for the forks shall use a right-angle, double output shaft, 
VFD controlled, helical-bevel foot-mounted gearmotor directly coupled to 
the fork drive shafts using Ringfeder, or Owner approved equivalent, 
couplings with shrink disk bushings. 
 
10. Where main and standby drives are required, the gearmotors shall be single 
output shaft gearmotor configuration and shall be connected to the fork 
drive shafts using RC chain and sprockets. The driveshaft mounted 
sprocket shall include a mechanical torque-limiting device. Switching from 
the main to the backup gearmotor shall be accomplished by manually 
removing and installing the main/backup RC chain. 
 
11. The fork drive shaft shall be segmented between the fork assemblies and 
shall be supported on double row spherical roller pillow block bearings. The 
driveshaft shall be directly coupled to the fork spur gear shaft assemblies 
using Ringfeder, or Owner approved equivalent, couplings with shrink disk 
bushings. 
 
12. A main and standby encoder/resolver shall be assembled to the ends of the 
fork driveshaft. The encoders/resolvers shall be easily accessible to allow 
for quick changeover. 
 
13. The forks shall have bolt-on hard stops to prevent overtravel.  
 
14. Each fork assembly shall have the capability to be individually assembled 
and disassembled for maintenance purposes. 
 
15. Each fork assembly shall have individual adjust capability to ensure the fork 
transfer device is truly “plumb, square and level” to the adjacent conveyor 
systems at installation and function. 
9.24.4.4 
Lifting equipment: 
 
1. 
Eccentric arm lift frames shall be provided on fork transfers that require a lift 
height of 24 inches (610 mm), or less. Eccentric arm lifts shall not be used 
for multi-position transfers. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 342 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
2. 
The drive system for the lift frame shall incorporate an in-line, single output 
shaft, VFD controlled, helical-bevel foot-mounted gearmotor connected to 
the lift shaft using double RC chain and sprockets. The gearmotor mounting 
base shall be fastened to the fork transfer base frame. The gearmotor 
mounting base shall include provisions for height adjustment, chain 
tensioning and sprocket alignment. 
 
3. 
Where main and standby drives are required, the main and backup lift frame 
gearmotor mounting bases shall be as described in paragraph 9.26.10.13. 
Switching from the main to the backup gearmotor shall be accomplished by 
manually removing and installing the main/backup double RC chain. 
 
4. 
Gravity control locking pins shall be provided to secure the lift shaft during 
changeover from the main to the standby drive assembly. 
9.24.4.5 
Lifting chains and belts: 
 
1. 
Eccentric arms are the mechanism used for lifting fork transfers. Chains and 
belts are not used for lifting fork transfers. 
9.24.4.6 
Base frame: 
 
1. 
The fork transfer base frame assembly shall include the lift shaft, eccentric 
arm shafts and guide rails. 
 
2. 
The fork transfer base frame shall be fabricated from ASTM A36 structural 
steel shapes and/or ASTM A500 structural steel tubing. 
 
3. 
The base frame weldment shall be set on Contractor provided leveling pads 
that are anchored to the plant floor/mezzanine. 
 
4. 
Leveling the base frame shall be accomplished by using jackscrews. The 
Contractor shall grout or shim below the base frame leveling pads once the 
final adjustments are completed. 
 
5. 
The lift shaft shall be centered on the base frame and shall be supported by 
two (2) double row spherical roller bearing pillow block bearings. The double 
RC drive chain sprocket(s) shall be keyed to the shaft with double 
setscrews over the key and at 90 degrees to the key. Tube spacers shall be 
provided to maintain sprocket position on the shaft. 
6. 
The eccentric arm shaft assemblies shall be assembled on the “outside” of 
the base frame, adjacent to the lift shaft, and shall be supported by two (2) 
double row spherical roller bearing pillow block bearings. The eccentric arm 
shaft shall be directly coupled to the lift shaft using a gear type coupling 
keyed to the shaft with double setscrews over the key and at 90 degrees to 
the key. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 343 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
7. 
The eccentric arm details shall be keyed to the shaft with double setscrews 
over the key and at 90 degrees to the key. A keeper plate shall be 
assembled to the end of the shaft with two flat head cap screws to secure 
the eccentric arm details. 
 
8. 
Loctite shall be applied to all keeper plate screws and setscrews. 
 
9. 
Crowned face cam followers shall be assembled to the eccentric arm details 
used to raise and lower the fork transfer lift frame. The cam follower shall be 
heavy-duty hex hole stud type mounting with sealed needle bearings and 
grease fitting for lubrication. 
9.24.4.7 
Guide rails: 
 
1. 
The lift frame shall be guided by two (2) guide rail assemblies assembled on 
opposite ends of the base frame. The guide rails shall be fabricated from 
machined and heat-treated hi-carb steel and shall be bolted to the base 
frame structure using socket head machine screws. 
 
2. 
The guide rails shall be shimmed to provide "plumb, square and level" 
load/guide wheel alignment over the entire length of lift frame travel. The 
guide rail shall be constructed as a single section rigidly mounted to the 
base frame structure such that deflections at joints are less than 0.020 inch. 
 
3. 
The guide rail and wear bar life expectancy shall be ten years; i.e., there 
shall not be a loss of material anywhere along the entire surface of the wear 
bar in excess of 1/16 inch during the 10-year life of the conveyor under the 
design loads. 
9.24.4.8 
Lift frame: 
 
1. 
The lift frame structure shall be fabricated from ASTM A36 structural steel 
shapes and/or ASTM A500 structural tubing. The lift frame shall include 
mounting plates to suit assembly and alignment of the forks. 
 
2. 
The lift frame shall be guided fore/aft by four (4) cylindrical cam followers – 
one (1) high and one (1) low, each side – assembled to a mounting plate 
and bolted to the lift frame for easy removal. 
 
3. 
The lift frame shall be guided side to side by four (4) sets of plain and 
eccentric cylindrical cam followers – one (1) set high and one (1) set low, 
each side – assembled to a single mounting plate and bolted to the lift 
frame for easy removal. 
 
4. 
All fore/aft and side to side guide cam followers shall be heavy-duty hex 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 344 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
hole stud type mounting with sealed needle bearings and grease fitting for 
lubrication. 
 
 
5. 
The lift frame shall include two (2) support blocks with Sure-Alloy wear bar 
of sufficient length to suit the eccentric arm cam follower rotation. The Sure-
Alloy wear bars shall be secured to the support blocks with flat head caps 
crews and shall include shimming for final adjustment. 
9.24.4.9 
Counterweight: 
 
1. 
Counterweights are not required for fork transfers. 
9.24.4.10 
Controls and electrical 
 
1. 
The fork transfer operation shall be completely automatic, including 
associated interlocks with adjacent conveyors, with the ability to manually 
cycle the equipment for maintenance purposes. 
 
2. 
The fork transfer, secondary lifts (when required) and the adjacent conveyor 
stops and positioners, etc. shall be controlled by the fork transfer PLC as a 
single automated cell. 
9.24.5 
Lift Transfers 
9.24.5.1 
A lift transfer is basically a fork transfer mounted to a 4-Post Belt Lift. Lift 
transfers are used to move product from one conveyor to another at right angles 
to the loading and unloading systems where “multi-level” transfer is required. i.e., 
where vertical travel is greater than 24 inches (610 mm). 
9.24.5.2 
The requirements identified in GVOSS Section 9.9 Drop Lifters shall be included 
as part of this specification. 
9.24.5.3 
Fork design and construction 
 
1. 
Refer to GVOSS Section 9.24.4.3 for specifications referring to this subject. 
9.24.5.4 
Lifting equipment 
 
1. 
Refer to GVOSS Section 9.9.4 for specifications referring to this subject. 
9.24.5.5 
Lifting chains and belts 
 
1. 
Refer to GVOSS Section 9.9.5 for specifications referring to this subject. 
9.24.5.6 
Base frame:

6. Refer to GVOSS Section 9.9.6 for specifications referring to this subject.

The lift frame/carriage shall include mounting plates to suit assembly and

9.24.6 
Secondary Lifts 
9.24.6.1 
Secondary lifts are used to raise and lower the part when there is not sufficient 
space to insert the fork/lift transfer forks for the transfer operation. 
9.24.6.2 
Part pick-up details 
 
1. 
The fixture details that contact the product shall be fabricated from UHMW 
or durable nylon type material suitable to support and protect the product. 
The details shall be bolted to the lift frame assembly and shall provide for 
adjustment in the x-, y- and z-axis. 
9.24.6.3 
Lifting equipment 
 
1. 
Secondary lifts may be actuated electric motors or air cylinders. Eccentric 
arm type lifts using an electric motor for actuation is preferred. 
 
7. 
Eccentric arm secondary lifts shall be limited to a lift height of 24 inches 
(610 mm), or less. Where greater than 24 inches (610 mm) lift is required, 
air cylinders may be used. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 346 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
3. 
The drive system for the electrically actuated secondary lift shall incorporate 
an in-line, single output shaft, VFD controlled, helical-bevel foot-mounted 
gearmotor connected to the lift shafts using two synchronous power 
transmission belts and sprockets. The gearmotor mounting base shall be 
fastened to the secondary lift base frame and shall be located to provide 
safe and efficient maintenance access. The gearmotor mounting base shall 
include provisions for height adjustment, belt tensioning and sprocket 
alignment. 
 
4. 
Where main and stand-by drives are required, the main and backup lift 
frame gearmotor mounting bases shall be as described in paragraph 
9.26.10.13. Switching from the main to the backup gearmotor shall be 
accomplished by manually removing and installing the main/backup power 
transmission belts. 
 
5. 
Gravity control locking pins shall be provided to secure the lift shaft during 
changeover from the main to the stand-by drive assembly. 
9.24.6.4 
Base frame 
 
1. 
The secondary lift base frame assembly shall include the lift shaft, eccentric 
arm shafts and guide rails. 
 
2. 
The secondary lift base frame shall be fabricated from ASTM A36 structural 
steel shapes, ASTM A500 structural steel tubing, and/or laser cut and 
formed plates. 
 
3. 
Leveling of the base frame shall be accomplished by using jackscrews. The 
Contractor shall grout or shim below the base frame leveling pads once the 
final adjustments are completed. 
 
4. 
The lift shaft shall be centered on the base frame and shall be supported by 
two (2) double row spherical roller bearing pillow block bearings. The power 
transmission sprocket(s) shall be keyed to the shaft with double setscrews 
over the key and at 90 degrees to the key. Tube spacers shall be provided 
to maintain sprocket position on the shaft. 
 
5. 
The eccentric arms on opposite sides shall be fixed together on common 
eccentric arm shafts or with adjustable connecting links.  
 
6. 
The eccentric arm shaft assemblies shall be assembled on the “outside” of 
the base frame and shall be supported by two (2) double row spherical roller 
bearing pillow block bearings. The eccentric arm shafts shall be driven by 
the lift shaft using a power transmission sprocket keyed to the shaft with 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 347 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
double setscrews over the key and at 90 degrees to the key. 
 
7. 
The eccentric arm details, one on each end of the eccentric arm shaft, shall 
be keyed to the shaft with double setscrews over the key and at 90 degrees 
to the key. A keeper plate shall be assembled to the end of the shaft with 
two flat head cap screws to secure the eccentric arm details. 
 
8. 
Loctite shall be applied to all keeper plate screws and setscrews. 
 
9. 
Crowned face cam followers shall be assembled to the eccentric arm details 
used to raise and lower the secondary lift frame. The cam follower shall be 
heavy-duty hex hole stud type mounting with sealed needle bearings and 
grease fitting for lubrication. 
9.24.6.5 
Guide rails 
 
1. 
The lift frame shall be guided by two (2) guide rail assemblies assembled on 
opposite ends of the base frame. The guide rails shall be fabricated from 
machined and heat-treated hi-carb steel and shall be bolted to the base 
frame structure using socket head machine screws. 
 
2. 
The guide rails shall be shimmed to provide "plumb, square and level" 
load/guide wheel alignment over the entire length of lift frame travel. The 
guide rail shall be constructed as a single section rigidly mounted to the 
base frame structure such that deflections at joints are less than 0.020 inch. 
 
3. 
The guide rail and wear bar life expectancy shall be ten years. i.e., there 
shall not be a loss of material anywhere along the entire surface of the wear 
bar in excess of 1/16 inch during the 10-year life of the conveyor under the 
design loads. 
9.24.6.6 
Lift frame 
 
1. 
The lift frame structure shall be fabricated from ASTM A36 structural steel 
shapes and/or ASTM A500 structural tubing and/or laser cut and formed 
plates. The lift frame shall include mounting plates to suit assembly and 
alignment of the part pickup details. 
 
2. 
The lift frame shall be guided fore/aft by four (4) cylindrical cam followers – 
one (1) high and one (1) low, each side – assembled to a mounting plate 
and bolted to the lift frame for easy removal. 
 
3. 
The lift frame shall be guided side to side by four (4) sets of plain and 
eccentric cylindrical cam followers – one (1) set high and one (1) set low, 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 348 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
each side –assembled to a single mounting plate and bolted to the lift frame 
for easy removal. 
 
4. 
All fore/aft and side to side guide cam followers shall be heavy-duty hex 
hole stud type mounting with sealed needle bearings and grease fitting for 
lubrication. 
 
5. 
The lift frame shall include two (2) support blocks with Sure-Alloy wear bar 
of sufficient length to suit the eccentric arm cam follower rotation. The Sure-
Alloy wear bars shall be secured to the support blocks with flat head cap 
screws and shall include shimming for final adjustment. 
9.24.6.7 
Counterweight 
 
1. 
Counterweights are not required for secondary lifts. 
9.24.6.8 
Pit work 
 
1. 
Refer to GVOSS Section 9.27 for specifications referring to this subject. 
9.24.6.9 
Controls and electrical 
 
1. 
The secondary lift control shall be included in the fork transfer control 
system. 
 
2. 
Refer to GVOSS Section 9.6 for specifications referring to this subject.

9.25 
CONVEYOR LUBRICATION SYSTEMS 
9.25.1 
General 
9.25.1.1 
The requirements identified in GVOSS section 3.3.9 Lubrication Requirements 
GVOSS section 3.12 Lubrication shall be adhered to and are hereby made part 
of this specification. Where discrepancies exist between the referenced 
documents and this specification, the requirements of this specification shall take 
precedent. 
9.25.1.2 
The Contractor shall furnish all lubricants and shall lubricate all equipment prior 
to and during tryout of the system. The Contractor shall supply the lubricants 
during this period.  
9.25.1.3 
All equipment, whether new, relocated, or reworked, shall receive lubrication per 
these specifications. This includes, but is not limited to, such items as CC5 chain 
pins and rollers, CC5 raised rollers, all sprockets, roller turn rollers, traction wheel 
hub bearings, slides, rack and spur/pinion gears, roller chain related power 
transmission components, motors, speed reducers, rivet less conveyor chain 
pins and trolley wheels, P&F free trolley wheels, side guide rollers, king pin 
bushings, P&F trolley wheels, duckbill and retractable dog components, hold 
back dogs, caterpillar drive components and back-up bars, take-up slides, shaft 
bearings, Flat Top chain pins and bushings, Flat Top chain rollers, etc. In simple 
words: all parts that spin, rotate, slide, pivot or rub against a surface that causes 
friction, must be provided with means of lubrication.     
9.25.1.4 
All lubrication systems shall be properly labelled and adhere to the requirements 
found in GVOSS section 1.4.5 – Lubrication Labels. 
9.25.1.5 
Grease Passages 
9.25.1.5.1 
All bearings including sleeve, anti-friction, thrust etc., shall be equipped with 
accessible lubrication fittings. All sleeve bearings and bushings are to be 
provided with lubrication groove to carry the lubricant to the entire inner surface 
in a uniform and even manner.  
9.25.1.5.2 
When bolts, pins or axles are drilled to provide grease passages, the center 
passage drilling process shall not remove more than 10% of the cross-sectional 
area of the original diameter. 
• 
The cross-drilled portion of the passage shall only extend from the outer 
diameter to the center passage. See example below. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 350 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• Cross-drilling of the passage completely through the diameter of the original 
shape is PROHIBITED. See example below. 
 
• All machined grease passages shall be purged of any chips and debris 
before assembly. 
9.25.1.6 
All sealed Power Trolleys as well as all sealed Free Trolleys and all wheel 
assemblies that utilize a shaft mounted wheel/bearing assembly shall be 
assembled in the Supplier shop with a minimum 33% grease fill. This means that 
33% of the total space available between the two cover plates of the trolley shall 
be full. The wheel/bearing supplier shall review the application for the required 
lubrication type and volume. 
NOTE: For Paint Shop applications, stay with the grease/oil (oil for open face 
trolleys) already used in that particular plant’s Paint Shop. In case the grease is 
of different spec than already present in that particular Paint Shop or different 
than what is approved for Paint Shops in the GVOSS lubrication spec, get it 
tested through the Paint Supplier to make sure that it does not cause quality 
issues such as craters, fish eye or orange peel.       
9.25.1.7 
Pre-lubricated bearings must be checked for grease and shall be field lubricated 
if found deficient. 
9.25.1.8 
All grease fittings and lubrication points for bearings, rollers, wheels, and drive 
components shall be accessible for maintenance by use of hatches, trap doors, 
removable or hinged plates, etc. Remote mounted lube blocks and fittings with 
lube lines/tubing routed to internal parts that are otherwise inaccessible will be 
allowed only with written approval of the Owner. The tubing shall be routed such 
that it does not get damaged in shipping and during normal use and 
maintenance. The lube block shall be located in a location which is easily 
accessible, and from where all the pillow block bearings are visible. The 
Contractor shall purge the air out of all lube blocks and the lube lines before 
installing the equipment. 
9.25.1.9 
All new Gear Reducers shall be supplied with a sight gauge through which the 
lubricant level can be checked without removing the plug. The sight gauge shall 
be located in an easily visible area. The sight gauge shall adhere to the 
requirements identified in GVOSS section 1.4.10.3. 
9.25.1.10 
All Pillow Block Bearings shall be provided with grease fittings. 
9.25.1.11 
Automatic lubricators for OHM, OH P&F and IP&F shall be designed to be track 
mounted in the Contractors shop, tested and debugged for the specific trolley 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 351 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
and chain pin arrangements. Each conveyor shall have its own dedicated Oil 
Reservoir(s), Grease Reservoirs(s), Pump(s) and Controller(s).  
9.25.1.12 
For Overhead and Inverted Power and Free Conveyors, the track section shall 
have yokes identical to those used throughout the installation and shall have all 
electrical and piping securely mounted as an integral part of the modular 
assembly. 
9.25.1.13 
All conveyor RC Chains, sprockets and bearings shall be lubricated by battery 
operated grease canisters. The canisters shall be located in an easily accessible 
area for maintenance. 
9.25.1.14 
All automatic lubricators shall be equipped with an on/off selector switch, 
automatic timer and an indicating light in the lubricator control panel. All 
Automatic Lubricators are to be linked to the conveyor PLC and monitored for 
faults. The faults shall be displayed in the FIS System. As a minimum 
requirement, low oil level, over-cycle, equipment faulted, and cycle not completed 
conditions are to be monitored and displayed.        
9.25.1.15 
The 12-gallon reservoir shall be standard equipment for all systems that are less 
than 3000 feet. The longer systems shall require larger reservoirs. Add 5 gallons 
to the size of the reservoir for every 1,000 feet of length over the 3,000 feet. 
Minimum 30-gallon reservoir shall be required, in applications where more than 8 
strands of the standard CC5-A chain or more than 4 strands of CC5-B (the Roller 
Flight) chain are lubricated from one common reservoir. Use 50-gallon reservoir 
for all Paint Shop Oven applications. Each Oven Conveyor is to have its own 
dedicated reservoir and controller. 
9.25.1.16 
All automatic lubricators shall be as manufactured by Castrol Lubecon, or Owner 
approved equivalent.  
9.25.1.17 
Any centralized system utilizing solvent-based materials is required to be 
explosion proof and shall comply with applicable fire safety regulations. 
9.25.1.18 
In addition to the requirements specified in paragraph 9.25.1.7, the Contractor 
shall locate the remote lube block such that it is easily and safely accessible 
during production operation. e.g., lube blocks for flat-top heads and tails, 
pedestal conveyors, slat conveyors, VDL drive frames, etc. shall be safely 
accessible from outside the perimeter fence and guards. The Contractor shall 
review the proposed lube block locations at the design review meeting. 
9.25.2 
General Conveyor Lubricants 
9.25.2.1 
All Lubricants are to be used with advance approval by the VO engineer and 
after checking with the local Paint Supplier. 
9.25.2.2 
Castrol Series 1M/200, thin film, penetrating lubricant shall be used for: 
 
1. 
Monorail and Power and Free chain pins,

2. 
Open power chain trolleys, 
 
3. 
Open free trolleys, 
 
4. 
Cat Chains, Sprockets, and Back Up Bars, 
 
5. 
RC Chains and Sprockets, 
 
6. 
Retractable free trolley dog, or Dogmagic components, and 
 
7. 
CC5 Chains and Sprockets. 
9.25.2.3 
All applications at temperatures less than 300 degrees Fahrenheit shall use 
Castrol LubeCon HD220-1 grease. For paint Shop applications, the Supplier 
must consult the local plant Manufacturing Manager(s) and the Paint Supplier to 
ensure that the supplied lubricant is compatible with the paint used in that facility. 
9.25.2.4 
Castrol Series 1M/200 lubricant shall be used in applications where the 
temperature does NOT exceed 400 degrees Fahrenheit. For applications 
exceeding 400 degrees Fahrenheit, consult the respective area VO Conveyor 
Engineer, Project Manager or their designated representative.  
9.25.2.5 
The Contractor shall furnish all lubricants and shall lubricate and check all 
equipment prior to and during tryout of the system as follows: 
 
1. 
All new and rebuilt Reducers shall be supplied with manufacturer approved 
synthetic oil, filled to the manufacturer's recommended levels. Castrol 
LubeCon ALPHA-P220 and Mobil 630 are approved by most reducer 
manufactures. However, the Contractor shall verify with reducer 
manufactures to ensure that there are no compatibility issues with any of the 
reducer components.  
 
2. 
Lubricate all traction wheels using Castrol LubeCon Series HD-220-1 
grease. 
 
3. 
Lubricate all roller turns using Castrol LubeCon Series HD-220-1 grease. 
 
4. 
Grease all King Pins with Castrol LubeCon Series HD-220-1 grease.  
 
5. 
Fill trolley lubricator reservoirs using Castrol LubeCon Series HD-220-1 
grease. 
 
6. 
Fill chain pin lubricators using Castrol LubeCon Series 1M/200, Series 663 
or Owner approved oil. 
 
7. 
Lubricate take-up rollers and slides using Castrol LubeCon Series HD-220-1

grease. 
 
8. 
Grease all back-up rollers using Castrol LubeCon Series HD-220-1 grease.  
 
9. 
Fill air line lubricator on air/oil take-up using manufacturer approved oil. 
 
10. Check air/oil tank for proper fill level on air/oil take-up and fill as required 
using Ford Motor Company # M-6C32-B or equivalent oil. 
 
11. Oven two strand conveyors shall use Castrol LubeCon Series HT grease for 
side guide rolls, pillow blocks, etc. 
 
13. Oven two strand conveyors CC5 chain shall be lubricated with Castrol 
LubeCon Series 663 lubricant after the vestibule of the oven. 
9.25.3 
Paint Conveyor Lubricants 
9.25.3.1 
Use of 1M/200 through Paint Spray Booth Conveyors:  1M/200 shall be used on 
Conveyor Chains running through the Paint Spray Booths with down draft airflow. 
This lubricant does not cause paint defects if it does not come in contact with the 
paintable surface. It does cause paint defects if it is rubbed, sprayed or smeared 
onto the paintable surface. Therefore, contact with the paintable surface must be 
avoided.  
9.25.3.2 
Use of 1M/200 through Paint Oven Conveyors:  1M/200 shall be used on 
Conveyor Chains and Open Face Trolleys running through the Paint Ovens with 
operating temperatures not exceeding 400 degrees Fahrenheit. The reservoirs 
and controllers are to be located OUT of the ovens. In new installations, the 
nozzles must also be kept outside the oven. Where installation of the nozzles 
outside the oven is not feasible (in case of retrofitting an existing facility), provide 
a drip pan below the nozzles and drain through the oven floor with a container 
below to catch the excess oil. The ovens must be kept clean of E-Coat and Paint 
build up as well as free of metal shavings. For applications above 400 degrees 
Fahrenheit, use Castrol Series 663 synthetic oil or equivalent (with approval of 
the local Paint Supplier and the Paint Area M.E. Manager).   
9.25.3.3 
Castrol Series 663 or equivalent, water based synthetic lube is to be provided for 
Paint Shop installations exceeding operating temperatures of 400 degrees 
Fahrenheit, unless otherwise dictated by the local facility M.E. Manager, the local 
Paint Supplier or the VO Project Manager (with the approval of the VO Paint 
Group). The conveyor Supplier must consult the local plant Manufacturing 
Manager(s) and the Paint Supplier(s) to ensure that the supplied lubricant is 
compatible with the paint used in that facility. Where compatibility issues arise, 
the make and type of lubricants to be used will be as specified by the Owner 
(local Paint Area M.E. Manager). 
9.25.3.4 
For Paint Shop applications and for temperatures exceeding 300 degrees 
Fahrenheit, use Castrol LubeCon Series HT or equivalent grease. The conveyor 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 354 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
Supplier must consult the local plant Manufacturing Manager(s) and the Paint 
Supplier(s) to ensure that the supplied lubricant is compatible with the paint used 
in that facility. Where compatibility issues arise, the make and type of lubricants 
to be used will be as specified by the Owner. 
9.25.4 
Automatic Lubricators 
9.25.4.1 
Automatic chain pin, chain trolley, free trolley, guide roller, lead trolley retractable 
dog / dog-magic lubricators shall be shop assembled to suit proper location, 
function and placement on their associated straight track, and must be easily 
accessible for maintenance. All new and reworked monorail, overhead power 
and free conveyors, inverted power and free conveyors, CC5 chain conveyors 
and Flat Top Conveyors shall be equipped with Automatic Lubricators and 
Automatic Greasers. In case of Roller Flight Conveyors, an extra nozzle shall be 
provided to lubricate the raised roller. Guidelines for location for the Automatic 
Lubricators and Automatic Greasers are as following: 
9.25.4.2 
Monorail Conveyors: For all Monorail Conveyors, install the Oil Reservoir, 
Grease Reservoirs, the Power Trolley Greaser and the Controller on the Drive 
Platform, near the entrance point of the chain into the drives. The Contractor 
must allow for space on the Drive Platform for the Automatic Lubrication System 
Components during the design stage. All tubes, hoses and cables are to be 
installed such that they do not create a trip hazard, are not damage prone and 
are easily serviceable.  
9.25.4.3 
Power & Free Conveyors: For all Power and Free Conveyors (Overhead and 
Inverted) install the Oil Reservoir, Grease Reservoirs, the Power Trolley Greaser 
and the Controller on the Drive Platform, near the entrance point of the chain into 
the drives. The Contractor must design in and provide the space on the Drive 
Platform for the Automatic Lubrication System Components. All tubes, hoses and 
cables are to be installed such that they do not create a trip hazard, are not 
damage prone and are easily serviceable.  
9.25.4.4 
Free Trolley Lubricators: Install the Lead Trolley and the Free Trolley Lubricator 
and Greaser for all Power and Free Conveyors near the entrance to the Carrier 
Repair Platform. The Automatic Lubrication is to occur at the stop, just before the 
Divert Switch to the Repair Platform. A small platform near the "operator stations" 
is to be designed in and provided for the Automatic Lubrication System 
Components. Access to the Lubrication Platform is to be provided from the 
conveyor screen guard. All tubes, hoses and cables are to be installed such that 
they do not create a trip hazard, are not damage prone and are easily 
serviceable. The lubrication shall occur (when the lubrication cycle is turned on) 
while the carrier is in stop position. The carrier is not to be released until the 
greaser is in home position and its location is verified though a proximity switch. 
Based on the cycle time requirements, if greasing the carriers in a stopped 
position prevents the system from achieving the production rate required by 
design, then the Contractor shall program the greasers to lubricate the carriers 
"on the fly".  

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 355 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.25.4.5 
CC5 Chain Conveyors: For all CC5 Chain Conveyors, Two Strand Conveyors, 
Roller Flight Conveyors and Cross Transfers install the Oil Reservoir and the 
Controller near the head section of the conveyor/s. The Contractor must design 
in and provide the space on the layout for the Automatic Lubrication System 
Components near the Head Section of the conveyors. The location must be 
discussed and approved by the plant representatives and the VO Project 
Manager. For non-production conveyors on mezzanines and in the banks, all 
tubes, hoses and cables are to be installed such that they do not create a trip 
hazard, are not damage prone and are easily serviceable. For CC5 chain 
conveyors in production areas, all tubes, hoses and electrical cords shall be 
encased in trench with removable checker plate cover on top. 
9.25.4.6 
Flat Top Conveyors: For all Flat Top Conveyors (single strand and dual strand), 
install the Oil Reservoir, the Greaser Reservoir and the Controllers near the head 
section of the conveyors. The Contractor must design in and provide the space in 
the Head Section Pit, on the layout for the Automatic Lubrication System 
Components. All components of the system must be easily accessible and 
serviceable. The reservoirs and controllers must always be located outside the 
safety cage of the Head Section. All tubes, hoses and cables are to be installed 
such that they do not create a trip hazard, are not damage prone and are easily 
serviceable.

9.25.6 
Lubrication during Installation and Commissioning 
9.25.6.1 
The initial lubrication shall be the responsibility of the installation Contractor. 
9.25.6.2 
For Overhead Monorail Conveyor, the Contractor shall provide: 
 
1. 
Castrol LubeCon1M/200 Oil to adequately lube the entire system through 
the test/tryout period, through the first 30 days of production (after line 
reaches full production rate) and full reservoir at the end of 30 days. 
 
2. 
Castrol LubeConHD220-1 Grease to lube the system for 2 full revolutions of 
the chain. The initial cycle to occur right after the installation of the chain 
and the second cycle to occur on the 29th day after the line reaches the full 
production rate. During the 1st cycle, insert 1 ounce of grease per trolley 
wheel for the 4" wheels and 2 ounces of grease per trolley wheel for the 6" 
wheels. Repeat the same measurement insertion on the 29th day of 
production. Leave the reservoir full after the 30th day of production. 
9.25.6.3 
For Power and Free conveyor, the Contractor shall provide: 
 
1. 
Castrol LubeCon1M/200 Oil to adequately lube the entire system through 
the test / tryout period, through the first 30 days of production (after line 
reaches full production rate) and full reservoir at the end of 30 days. 
 
2. 
Castrol LubeConHD220-1 Grease to lube the system for 2 full revolutions of 
the chain. The initial cycle to occur right after the installation of the chain 
and the second cycle to occur on the 29th day after the line reaches full 
production rate. During the 1st cycle, insert 1 ounce of grease per trolley 
wheel for the 4" wheels and 2 ounces of grease per trolley wheel for the 6" 
wheels. Repeat the same measurement insertion on the 29th day of 
production. Leave the reservoir full after the 30th day of production. 
9.25.6.4 
For Inverted Power and Free conveyor, the Contractor shall provide: 
 
1. 
Castrol LubeCon1M/200 Oil to adequately lube the entire system through 
the test / tryout period, through the first 30 days of production (after line 
reaches full production rate) and full reservoir at the end of 30 days.  
 
2. 
Castrol LubeConHD220-1 Grease to lube the system for 2 full revolutions of 
the chain. The initial cycle to occur right after the installation of the chain 
and the second cycle to occur on the 29th day after the line reaches full 
production rate. During the 1st cycle, insert 1 ounce of grease per trolley 
wheel for the 4" wheels and 2 ounces of grease per trolley wheel for the 6" 
wheels. Repeat the same measurement insertion on the 29th day of 
production. Leave the reservoir full after the 30th day of production. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.25.6.5 
For Overhead Power and Free conveyor Carrier Trolley the Contractor shall 
provide: 
 
1. 
Castrol LubeCon1M/200 Oil to adequately lube the entire system through 
the test / tryout period, through the first 30 days of production (after line 
reaches full production rate) and full reservoir at the end of 30 days.  
 
2. 
Castrol LubeConHD220-1 Grease to lube the system for 2 full revolutions of 
the chain. The initial cycle to occur right after the installation of the chain 
and the second cycle to occur on the 29th day after the line reaches full 
production rate. During the 1st cycle, insert 1 ounce of grease per trolley 
wheel for the 4" wheels and 2 ounces of grease per trolley wheel for the 6" 
wheels. Repeat the same measurement insertion on the 29th day of 
production. Leave the reservoir full after the 30th day of production. 
9.25.6.6 
For CC5-A Chain Production Conveyor the Contractor shall provide: 
 
1.

the test / tryout period, through the first 30 days of production (after line 
reaches full production rate) and full reservoir at the end of 30 days.  
9.25.6.7 
For CC5-B Chain Roller Flight Conveyor the Contractor shall provide: 
 
1. 
Castrol LubeCon1M/200 Oil to adequately lube the entire system through 
the test / tryout period, through the first 30 days of production (after line 
reaches full production rate) and full reservoir at the end of 30 days.  
9.25.6.8 
For Flat Top Conveyors the Contractor shall provide: 
 
1. 
Castrol LubeCon1M/200 Oil to adequately lube the entire system through 
the test / tryout period, through the first 30 days of production (after line 
reaches full production rate) and full reservoir at the end of 30 days.  
 
2. 
Castrol LubeConHD220-1 Grease to lubricate the system for 2 full 
revolutions of the chain. The initial cycle to occur right after the installation 
of the chain and the second cycle to occur on the 29th day after the line 
reaches full production rate. During the 1st cycle, insert ¼ ounce of grease 
per flanged roller wheel and watch for evidence of grease showing at the 
seals. Repeat until evidence of grease is seen at the seals. Leave the 
reservoir full of grease after the flanged rollers are fully lubricated.  
 
3. 
Flat top chain hinges and the RC chains shall be lubricated using the 
Castrol LubeCon 1M/200 or Owner approved equal. To avoid corrosion 
during shipping, the lubricant shall first be applied by the chain 
manufacturer. To avoid wear, the lubricant shall be applied by the Conveyor 
Contractor during installation.  
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 360 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
4. 
Lubricate the Flat Top Rollers using Castrol LubeConHD220-1 Grease. The 
bearing and seal cavities shall be totally filled after completion of all 
assembly operations and purging assured by a show of lubricant outside of 
the seals and between the side bars and sprocket roller. The lubrication 
must conform to the bearing manufacturer's requirements and shall include 
a hand pre-packing of the cone assemblies.  
 
5. 
All chain assembly field connections are to be fully lubricated after 
installation of the chain and prior to any break-in operations. 
9.25.6.9 
All lubrication points not covered by the Automatic Lubricators must be manually 
lubricated during the installation stage. e.g., if the P&F free trolley retractable dog 
roller is not covered by the Auto Lubricator it MUST be lubricated when the 
carrier/trolley is inserted in the system. As evidence that all manual points have 
been lubed, the Contractor shall provide a verification checklist log to the Owner 
by carrier number that lists all the manually lubed points. In addition to the log, 
the evidence of lubrication must be visually present.  
9.25.6.10 
After carriers, part hooks and/or chain are installed, the conveyor shall be run for 
a minimum of 2 cycles. During the first cycle the lubricators as well as greasers 
will be turned on and tuned in. At the completion of the first cycle, at least 10 
percent of the lubrication points shall be inspected at random to check the 
lubrication. A second application of lubricant shall then be made if the inspection 
indicates the necessity. The inspection procedure shall be repeated for the third 
and fourth cycle and the lubrication adjusted per the results of the inspection. 
The chain, trolleys, guide rollers, slides and all parts requiring lubrication must be 
fully lubricated by the end of the third or fourth cycle.  

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 361 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.26 
CONVEYOR SUPPORT STEEL AND PLATFORMS 
9.26.1 
General 
9.26.1.1 
The Contractor shall be responsible for the design, detail, procurement, 
fabrication and installation of all structural items and miscellaneous steel for the 
units identified in the project bid specification. 
9.26.1.2 
All final structural drawings and calculations shall be sealed by registered 
professional structural engineer with knowledge of automotive operation and 
approved by the Owner. The approval shall be obtained prior to commencing any 
conveyor systems detailed engineering design. Adequate safety factoring must 
be considered for static, dynamic and shock loading with the understanding that 
potential life exposure risk to employees working under suspended equipment 
must be zero. All structures must have a minimum safety factor of 2.5. 
9.26.1.3 
The Contractor shall provide superstructure steel layouts, clamp details, typical 
connection details, concrete layouts and miscellaneous pit steel details for 
approval prior to fabrication or installation. 
9.26.1.4 
The Contractor shall field check and review existing design and construction 
drawings for type and grade of steel and year of construction. Based on this 
information, the steel designer shall review and analyze the structure prior to 
adding new loads and clamping additional new steel structures. Clamp loads 
shall be limited to carrying capacity of bottom flange of truss chord member. In 
the event the new load exceeds the capacity, the Contractor shall design and 
submit to the Owner an alternate method of supporting his new load. This design 
shall be prepared by a registered professional structural engineer and submitted 
for approval to the Owner. 
9.26.1.5 
The existing superstructures with all additional loads applied, shall be analyzed 
for over-stressing, shearing, splice, deflection and clamp capacity. Lateral 
bracing for compression flanges and corresponding carrying capacity must be re-
evaluated for any torsional rotation. 
9.26.1.6 
The minimum elevation from the plant floor to the lowest part of the conveyor 
mezzanine, overhead platform, basket guard and maintenance access catwalk, 
etc. shall be as follows: 
 
1. 
12'-0" minimum in body construction areas. 
 
2. 
10'-0" minimum in all other areas, however, 12'-0" is preferred. 
 
3. 
To match bottom of existing steel elevation where there are other 
suspended structures, such as toilets, unit heaters, platforms, etc. 
9.26.1.7 
Acceptable conveyor superstructure connections are toe clamps, “stop signs” or 
bolted beam connections. Any other connection types, including field welded 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 362 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
beam connections, are not acceptable without obtaining prior written approval 
from the Owner following a complete design review. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 363 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.26.2 
Support Steel 
9.26.2.1 
In addition to the requirements identified in this specification, all structural 
support steel shall conform to the latest specifications for the design, fabrication 
and erection of structural steel for buildings, of the American Institute of Steel 
Construction (AISC), and the Canadian Institute of Steel Construction (CISC) for 
the Canadian Plants. 
9.26.2.2 
Rolled plates and steel shapes shall conform to minimum ASTM A36 designation 
(in Canada, G40-21.44W) and shall be free from twists, bends or open joints. 
Wide flange shapes shall be specified as ASTM A992 (Fy = 50 ksi). 
9.26.2.3 
All structural steel members shall be long enough so that assembling may be 
done without requiring the use of fillers. However, there shall be no projecting 
edges or corners where different structural members are assembled together. 
9.26.2.4 
All supporting steel shall be new and shall be standard shapes as listed by AISC 
and CISC. 
9.26.2.5 
All steel shall be free from rust, scale and scars before erection and before 
turning this work over to the Owner. 
9.26.2.6 
All main support steel shall be connected to the building steel at panel points of 
trusses unless otherwise approved by the Owner and shall be arranged so that 
the allowable panel point loading is not exceeded. Truss and panel point loading 
information shall be obtained from the Owner. No header steel shall be hung 
from roof purlins, joist, struts, sway frames or cross bracing. 
9.26.2.7 
New panel point loads shall be forwarded to the Owner. Until truss reinforcing 
and/or modifications to the existing trusses is complete, no support steel shall be 
erected. 
9.26.2.8 
Reactions for connection design to be at least 75 percent of the uniform loading 
capacity in the AISC/CISC, or for the design loads, whichever is greater. 
9.26.2.9 
Main header support steel shall be attached to the building steel only with beam 
clamps in accordance with guidelines and standard details, included herewith, 
and as approved by the Owner. No welding, cutting or drilling of building steel will 
be allowed unless approved by the Owner. Any rework of main building steel 
shall be designed by registered professional structural engineer and stamped 
design drawings shall be submitted to the Owner for approval. 
9.26.2.10 
All support steel shall be sized according to AISC/CISC specification guidelines 
and all headers/sub-headers shall be limited to a maximum deflection of 1/360th 
of the total span. 
9.26.2.11 
Supporting steel at load and unload areas or equipment shall be sized for the 
maximum loads to be carried plus the impact loading factor as specified by 
AISC/CISC. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 364 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.26.3 
Main Headers 
9.26.3.1 
Main headers shall be clamped to existing building trusses. The header steel 
member shall be of sufficient length to allow the toe clamps on each side of the 
bottom chord of truss. i.e., one sided truss clamps will not be accepted. 
9.26.3.2 
No header shall be set in stool position at truss bottom chord. In the situation 
where header steel is required to be higher than bottom chord of truss, an 
alternate support shall be designed. This design shall be completed by a 
registered professional structural engineer and submitted for approval before 
proceeding with fabrication work. 
9.26.3.3 
Exterior headers shall be designed with consideration for future expansion. 
9.26.4 
Sub-Headers 
9.26.4.1 
Sub-headers shall be used to span between main headers and shall be 
connected with clip angles when attached to the web or with toe-clamps or stop 
signs when connecting to the bottom flange of the main header/sub-header. 
Slotted holes may be used for web connections but must be field welded after 
alignment. 
9.26.5 
Toe Clamps 
9.26.5.1 
Toe clamps shall be used to connect main headers to building truss chord 
members, to connect sub-headers to the bottom flange of main headers/sub-
headers and for hanger/x-bracing to header/sub-header connections. Toe clamps 
shall be as close as possible to the web fillet and shall be sloped so that contact 
occurs on the flange at this point only. 
9.26.5.2 
Spacer bars shall be used on toe clamps so that the bolts and flange are not in 
bending. 
9.26.5.3 
The use of slotted holes in toe clamps will not be permitted. 
9.26.5.4 
The minimum thickness for toe clamps plate shall be ¾" unless otherwise 
approved by the Owner. 
9.26.5.5 
The edge distance, size of bolts and thickness of bar plates shall be in 
compliance with applicable specifications per AISC code, or CISC for Canadian 
Plants. However, these shall be designed and verified by registered professional 
structural engineer before proceeding with installation. This design shall be 
submitted to the Owner for approval. 
9.26.5.6 
Toe clamps shall be connected at bottom flange of truss bottom chord. However, 
these shall be installed at panel points. 
9.26.5.7 
Clamps shall be installed with use of bolts. Minimum 4 bolts shall be used in the 
connection. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 365 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.26.5.8 
The clamps shall be limited to maximum carrying capacity of bottom flange of 
truss chord. The tee section and clamp plate used shall have sufficient thickness 
to carry the designed loads, including consideration of prying force. 
9.26.6 
Stop Signs 
9.26.6.1 
Stop signs shall be used to connect to connect sub-headers to the bottom flange 
of main headers/sub-headers and for hanger/x-bracing to header/sub-header 
connections.  
9.26.6.2 
A registered professional engineer shall review and approve all stop sign designs 
and applications. 
9.26.7 
Hangers 
9.26.7.1 
Hangers shall be used to support the conveyor load from main headers or sub-
headers and shall be connected to headers with bolts through the header web 
and/or to wide flange beam headers with toe clamps or “stop sign”. No "J bolt” or 
companion angle connections will be allowed. 
9.26.7.2 
Hangers shall be connected to a structural tee welded to the top flange of the 
overhead conveyor track continuous along the length and wrap the corners by 1 
inch on each toe. The minimum size tee shall be a 6" structural tee at 17.5 
pounds per foot unless otherwise approved by the Owner. 
9.26.7.3 
The maximum allowable span between hangers shall be 10'-0" (3048mm) for 
power and free conveyors and 10'-0" for monorail conveyors. The actual support 
spacing selected will be based on the track capacity and the conveyor-imposed 
loads and must be approved by the Owner. 
9.26.7.4 
All horizontal turns shall be hung by a minimum of three (3) hangers braced in 
two directions. 
9.26.7.5 
All vertical inclines or declines shall be adequately designed to resist all 
horizontal and vertical forces. Hangers shall not be spaced more than 10'-0" 
measured horizontally. Each hanger shall be diagonally braced from the track to 
the building steel or headers. 
9.26.7.6 
Hangers shall be structural steel angle and shall be connected with a minimum of 
two high-strength bolts at each connection point. Threaded rod hangers are not 
acceptable. 
9.26.7.7 
Required cross bracing shall be provided for lateral stability. Minimum size shall 
be 2½" x 2½" x ¼" angle. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 366 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.26.8 
Bolted Connections 
9.26.8.1 
All bolt holes must be drilled and/or punched and/or reamed to size. The burning 
of holes will not be permitted. 
9.26.8.2 
High strength bolts, nuts and washers shall be used in accordance with the latest 
edition of "Specifications for Structural Joints using ASTM F3125/F3125M or 
A490 bolts" approved by the research council on riveted and bolted structural 
joints of the engineering foundation and distributed by the American Institute of 
Steel Construction. ASTM A490 bolts shall not be used unless specifically called 
for and approved by the Owner. 
9.26.8.3 
Bolted connections shall be a minimum of two ¾" diameter ASTM A 
F3125/F3125M high strength bolts. Machine bolts shall not be used for structural 
connections. 
9.26.8.4 
All conveyor support steel connections, new or reused headers, shall utilize all 
new ASTM F3125/F3125M high strength bolts, Anco locknuts and hardened 
steel flat washers. Field welding for conveyor structural steel connections shall 
be prohibited. 
9.26.8.5 
Flat circular washers and square or rectangular beveled washers shall conform 
to the current requirements of the ASTM for hardened steel washers, ASTM 
F436. 
9.26.8.6 
Paint is permitted on the faying surfaces except in slip critical connections as 
defined in the specification for structural joints. 
9.26.8.7 
All bolts shall be match marked following final assembly in compliance with 
NAAMS Chapter F standard for fasteners. 
9.26.8.8 
The Contractor shall obtain a 3rd Party Local Contractor – paid for by the 
Contractor, and approved by VOME - to test, inspect and certify bolt torque for 
overhead steel and equipment. A minimum of 20% of all bolts shall be inspected. 
Results from the 20% inspections will dictate additional inspections, including 
requiring up to 100% of bolts to be tested. 
9.26.9 
Welded Connections 
9.26.9.1 
No field welding for overhead structural support steel shall be done unless shown 
on the bid drawings or unless advance written approval is obtained from the 
Owner. 
9.26.9.2 
Where field welding for structural support steel is approved, the welding shall be 
performed by certified welders as prescribed in the American Welding Society 
and the Canadian Standard CSA W47.1-09. 
9.26.9.3 
Upon Owner request, the Contractor shall submit copies of certificates and the 
immediate past six-month welding history of each welder employed, both for 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 367 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
shop and field welding. Any welder not meeting the approval of the Owner shall 
be replaced immediately. 
9.26.9.4 
Where welded fabrication is specified on the drawings and where it is approved 
by the Owner in lieu of bolted connections, the field welding shall be done by the 
shielded or submerged arc process, and in accordance with the American 
Welding Society and CSA W47.1 code for structural welds. 
9.26.9.5 
Where welding is used, the Owner may decide to perform non-destructive tests 
of the welds. The non-destructive investigation will be performed by an 
independent testing laboratory qualified in this type of work. The cost of this 
investigation will be assumed by the Owner. In the event that the welding is not 
acceptable, the Contractor shall remove all rejected welding and re-weld all such 
areas. The Contractor shall thereafter pay for all costs in connection with the re-
welding and re-examination of the re-welded connections, conducted by the 
same testing lab. No welds are to be painted until inspection is completed. 
9.26.9.6 
The size and length of all fillet welds shall be proportioned to the material 
thickness and design stresses indicated per AISC/CISC. 
9.26.9.7 
The minimum fillet weld used shall be 3/16" unless otherwise noted. 
9.26.9.8 
All fillet welds shall be completed using electrodes not over 3/16-inch diameter. 
Fillets larger than 5/16 inch are to be executed in not less than two passes. After 
each pass, slag coating is to be entirely removed before starting next pass. 
9.26.9.9 
All fillet welds shall be completed using electrodes in compliance with ASTM 
specifications for iron and steel arc-welding, E70XX AWS class SA-2 (CSA-W48 
for Canadian Plants.) 
9.26.9.10 
All welds shall be ground smooth on sliding or rolling surfaces or conveyors. 
9.26.9.11 
The Contractor shall use intermittent welding wherever possible and shall provide 
shields to prevent welding "Flash" to individual working around welding 
operation. 
9.26.9.12 
The Contractor shall field verify and check existing drawings for type and grade 
of steel. The welding electrodes and methods used shall be adjusted to suit the 
existing steel. Revision to length and size of welds shall be made accordingly. 
9.26.10 
Overhead Mezzanines and Platforms 
9.26.10.1 
Refer to GVOSS 9.26.1.6 for clearance below the bottom of the conveyor 
mezzanines, platforms and falling parts guard. 
9.26.10.2 
Where noted in the project bid specification, the clear height below conveyor 
mezzanines, platforms and/or falling parts guard shall include plant related 
facilities mounted below these structures. e.g., fire protection heads and 
distribution lines, LED lighting, etc. If necessary, the fire protection 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025

branch/distribution piping shall be contained within the "web" of the mezzanine 
steel superstructure members. 
9.26.10.3 
All platforms, handrails, catwalks, stairs and ladders in U.S. plants shall conform 
to the latest release of applicable federal, state and local codes including OSHA 
standards 29CFR Part Number 1910, Subpart D 1910.21-1910.29 or to the 
Ontario Building Code, OBC, Sections 3.3 through 3.4, except for Sentence 
3.3.1.13 (2); and Ministry of Labour Regulations (SOR/86-304) for Canadian 
plants. 
9.26.10.4 
Platforms shall be located on all four sides of the equipment being serviced 
whenever possible. 
9.26.10.5 
Overhead Monorail and P&F cat drive platforms shall be designed to provide 
access for servicing the main drive machinery, the caterpillar chain assembly and 
the back-up roll nest. 
9.26.10.6 
All mezzanines, platforms, and access catwalks shall be complete with handrail, 
intermediate rail, post and toe-plates, etc., in compliance with applicable federal, 
state and local code requirements. 
9.26.10.7 
A maintenance access catwalk with ladder from the nearest available building 
column shall be provided for all overhead control panel platforms, overhead 
conveyor drives, take-ups, or other equipment that may require adjustment, 
repair, or maintenance. If access stairs/ladders are not shown on the bid 
drawings, the final location of the stairs/ladders will be determined by the Owner 
during the design review meetings. 
9.26.10.8 
Ladders and stairs shall be located such that neither end exits to a pinch point 
condition. 
9.26.10.9 
Access catwalks shall be minimum 2'-0" wide with 4.27-pound expanded metal 
(structural grating) walkway flooring, toe board and 42" high handrail with 
intermediate rail and posts. 
9.26.10.10 
Catwalks and platforms shall be hung with hangers from main and/or sub-header 
steel with bolted connections. 
9.26.10.11 
Overhead platforms and conveyor mezzanine flooring: 
 
1. 
Conveyor mezzanines shall be floored with 1/4” steel checker plate, 
minimum. 
 
2. 
Control panel platforms and all access catwalks shall be floored with 4.27-
pound expanded metal (structural grating) walkway grating. Additionally, the 
control panels shall be supported by ¼” checker plate flooring. The checker 
plate shall extend 24-inches beyond the panel at the access doors, full 
length of the panel(s) to be serviced.

Revision Date: June 8, 2025

3. 
Body Shop and Paint Shop repair spur platforms shall be floored with 1/4” 
non-slip Mebac or Algrip steel plating, or Owner approved equivalent. 
 
4. 
Final Assembly repair spur platforms shall be floored with 1/4” steel checker 
plate, minimum. 
9.26.10.12 
The following equipment and service live loads shall be considered when 
designing supports for overhead platforms and conveyor mezzanines: 
 
1. 
Maintenance access catwalks: the actual equipment loads with a 50 psf live 
load. 
 
2. 
Skid, Pallet and Inverted P&F Conveyor mezzanines: use the actual 
equipment loads below the conveyor with the remainder of the area 
designed for 50 psf live load. 
 
3. 
Control panel platforms: use the actual equipment loads with the remainder 
of the area designed for 50 psf live load. 
 
4. 
OHM/P&F cat drive and take-up platforms: use the actual equipment loads 
with a 50 psf live load, minimum, for the service platform. 
 
5. 
OHM/P&F/FCS carrier repair spurs: use the actual equipment loads with a 
60 psf live load, minimum, for the service platform. 
 
6. 
OHM/P&F/FCS falling parts basket guard: use the actual equipment loads 
below the conveyor with the remainder of the area designed for 25 psf live 
load. 
9.26.10.13 
The mounting base for all conveyors and related load bearing equipment shall be 
supported by a structural member. Support on plate or grating is not acceptable. 
9.26.10.14 
Conveyor mezzanines, platforms and access walkways shall be hung with 
hangers from header steel or floor supported using columns as described within 
this specification and the project bid specifications. 
9.26.10.15 
The Contractor shall indicate the capacity and/or the design live load on all 
installed overhead platforms. The Contractor shall coordinate the type and 
location of such signs with the Owner. 
9.26.10.16 
For drives, take-ups, and other devices requiring platforms or access catwalks, a 
minimum 6'-6" (1980 mm) headroom clearance shall be provided.
