9.29 
CONVEYOR, AGV/AGC HEALTH ASSESSMENT 
9.29.1 
General 
9.29.1.1 
This Specification Section intended to provide a general outline of the 
requirements for completing a health assessment survey of conveyors. 
AGV/AGCs and related equipment. 
9.29.1.2 
This Section shall be used in conjunction with the Conveyor Health Assessment 
Inspection Checklist form and the applicable project specification. The Contractor 
shall identify, as part of the base bid, any conflicts that exist between this Section 
and the project specific bid documents. 
9.29.1.3 
Each system inspection shall be documented using the Conveyor Health 
Assessment Inspection Checklist forms. Although these forms may not include all 
conveyor equipment types identified in the project specification, the 
Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier/Contractor is required to assess all conveyors, 
AGV/AGCs and/or special equipment and shall create data sheets for any 
conveyor/AGV/AGC not included in the standard documents. 
9.29.1.4 
GVOSS Section 9.28 identifies the minimal requirements for determining 
reusability of conveyors, AGV/AGCs and related equipment, including 
measurement 
and/or 
determination 
of 
the 
condition 
of 
various 
components/equipment, of which requirements shall be included as part of this 
Section. 
9.29.2 
Intent 
9.29.2.1 
The Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier/Contractor shall provide all materials, equipment, 
labor, supervision (with premium time as required) to detail, engineer, and 
document the scope of work required to complete the health assessment for the 
conveyors identified in the project specification. The Contractor shall complete a 
detailed on-site survey and inspection, gather data and compose reports to 
reflect the current-state condition of the specified conveyors, AGV/AGCs and 
related equipment/components. 
9.29.2.2 
The health assessment deliverables shall include: 
 
1. 
A final report, complete with an Executive Summary, detailed equipment 
descriptions, and drawings, sketches and/or photos necessary to highlight 
critical areas of concern discovered as a result of the health assessment 
survey and records analysis. 
 
2. 
Health assessment report data to be recorded on the forms provided for 
each conveyor included (Conveyor Health Assessment Inspection 
Checklist). 
 
3. 
Trolley conveyor chain pull reports, CP Plus or equal. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 402 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
4. 
Trolley conveyor chain tension reports utilizing a strain gage, only for 
conveyors as directed in the SOW or DED. 
 
5. 
Trolley conveyor chain stretch/wear reports. 
 
6. 
Floor conveyor chain pull reports. 
9.29.2.3 
The Contractor shall perform an inspection and analysis of the overall condition 
of the conveyor system. Major inspection items include the condition of: 
 
1. 
Drive and Take-up assemblies, including motor and reducer and related 
drive components 
 
2. 
Chain tension analysis (with graphical report for P&F and OHM systems) 
utilizing a strain gage, only for conveyors as directed in the SOW or DED. 
 
3. 
Chain pull calculation and drive size confirmation 
 
4. 
Transmission chains/belts, including sprockets/sheaves condition, alignment 
and tension 
 
5. 
Conveyance chains/belts, including sprockets/sheaves, alignment and 
tension 
 
6. 
Head and tail shaft bearings 
 
7. 
Track wear 
 
8. 
Roller wear, including condition of bearings 
 
9. 
OHM/P&F traction wheels, roller turns, switches, and transfers, etc. 
including condition of bearings, framework and associated support structure. 
 
10. Horizontal turns, including track and rollers 
 
11. Vertical curves, including track and anti-runaway/anti-back-up devices 
 
12. All applicable safety related systems. i.e., drive guards, pinch point 
protection, falling parts guards, gate-plugs, etc. 
9.29.2.4 
The conveyor control panels, drives, hydraulic units, lifting cylinders, and take-
ups shall be a primary focus of the inspections. Where applicable, the Contractor 
shall obtain the actual VFD parameter settings as well as the voltage, current, 
and torque traces for a typical hour of cycles. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 403 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.29.2.5 
The chain and rails shall be inspected and measured for wear. The actual 
dimensions shall be compared to the design "like new" dimensions. The 
dimensional data and recommendations for further action shall be included in the 
final report, along with “% remaining useful life estimate”. 
9.29.2.6 
Only for conveyors as directed in the SOW or DED, the actual chain pull, and 
tension shall be determined utilizing an on-line dynamometer load cell. The 
actual chain tension and chain pull shall be compared to the design chain pull 
and previous chain tension tests with the intent to identify any variance from the 
original design and baseline operating parameters. 
9.29.3 
Definitions 
9.29.3.1 
Availability (%): The amount of time the machine was available to run compared 
to the amount of time you expect to run. 
 
 
 
  Operating Time 
 
Availability (%)  = 
 
 
Net Available Time       
9.29.3.2 
100 Hour Reliability (%): The probability that machinery/equipment can perform 
continuously, without failure, for 100 hours. 
 
Reliability100 
= 
e-100 / MTBF 
 
9.29.3.3 
Mean Time Between Failure, MTBF (minutes): The average time between failure 
occurrences.  
 
 
    Total Operating Hrs. 
 
MTBF = 
 
Qty. Failure Occurrences       
 
9.29.3.4 
Mean Time to Repair, MTTR (minutes): The average time to restore machinery 
or equipment to specified conditions. 
 
 
    Total Downtime Hrs. 
 
MTTR = 
 
Qty. Failure Occurrences       

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 404 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.29.3.5 
Average Production (units per hour): The average rate of production during the 
observation period. 
 
 
 
    Qty. Units Produced 
 
Avg Production Rate = 
 
 
        Time Observed 
 
9.29.3.6 
Mean Time Between Events, MTBE (minutes): The average time between 
events.  
 
 
 Total Operating Time 
 
MTBE = 
 
        Qty. of Events 
 
9.29.3.7 
Failure: An event when machinery/equipment is not available to produce parts at 
specified conditions when scheduled or is not capable of producing parts or 
perform scheduled operations to specification.  For every failure, an action is 
required. 
9.29.3.8 
Total Failures: Total incidents where machinery/equipment is not available to 
produce parts at specified conditions when scheduled or is not capable of 
producing parts or perform scheduled operations to specification. 
9.29.3.9 
Units Produced: The total number of units (good and bad) produced during the 
observation period. 
9.29.3.10 
Design Rate (units per hour): The average production rate that the 
machinery/equip was designed to produce in units per hour. 
9.29.3.11 
Event: Any incident that results in the unscheduled stoppage of a machine 
regardless of reason. Events include all stoppages. e.g. stoppages for part 
quality checks, predictive or preventative maintenance, scheduled tool changes, 
breaks, etc. Events include both failure and non-failure incidents. 
9.29.4 
Process 
9.29.4.1 
The Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier/Contractor shall perform a visual inspection, 
thermo and/or vibratory analysis, as identified in the project specification, to 
determine the overall condition of the Plant conveyor, AGV/AGCs systems. 
9.29.4.2 
The Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier/Contractor shall contact the Production Area 
Manufacturing Engineering Manager, or the designated Plant Representative, 
approximately 2 weeks prior to the date for beginning the on-site inspection to 
arrange mutually agreeable dates and times. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025

9.29.4.3 
The successful bidder shall obtain the following information from the Plant for 
review upon arrival at the Plant: 
 
1. 
Summary of all PM routines. 
 
2. 
Summary of all 8Ds and Corrective Work Orders completed for previous 6 
months. 
 
3. 
Copy of reports for breakdowns that occurred for previous 6 months.

7.

Weight data for the conveyed product at the various locations in the build 
process. 
 
10. 
Factory Information System (FIS) downtime data. 
 
11. 
Review of plant constraint package with VIM analysis. 
 
12.      Copy of any and all other predictive trending that may be utilized in the

The 
Tier1/AGV/AGC 
supplier/Contractor 
shall 
obtain 
information 
on 
improvements performed by the Plant since the original commissioning that may 
have resolved reliability and/or maintainability problems with the equipment for 
the purpose of making design improvements on future machinery or equipment. 
9.29.4.5 
The Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier/Contractor shall perform a thorough on-site review 
of the physical condition of the equipment including a visual inspection of 
applicable mechanical, electrical, hydraulic, and structural components, 
inspection of rotating members, visual inspection for fluid leaks, and visual and 
audio inspection for proper lubrication and operation. 
9.29.4.6 
The Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier/Contractor shall perform on-site interviews with 
both hourly and salaried maintenance personnel to gather, document, verify and 
analyze all concerns regarding equipment condition and longevity.

9.29.4.7 
Upon completion of the data review and on-site inspection, the Tier1/AGV/AGC 
supplier/Contractor shall prepare an exit report and review the report with the 
Production Area Manufacturing Engineering Manager, or the designated Plant 
Representative, covering the following: 
 
1. 
Overall summary of the conveyor, AGV/AGC equipment status. 
 
2. 
Any operating problems, either experienced or not experienced by the 
particular plant, that is commonly seen with similar equipment operating at 
other plant locations. 
 
3. 
Any preventive maintenance recommendations including prioritization of the 
immediate corrective actions that can be performed on the equipment 
9.29.4.8 
The exit report may be communicated verbally, however, the key points shall be 
presented in written outline format to act as a temporary file copy until the final 
report is issued. The items discussed as part of the "exit interview", including the 
written outline, shall be included in the final report. 
9.29.4.9 
The Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier/Contractor shall prepare a comprehensive report 
on the condition of the conveyors and related equipment, including preventive 
maintenance recommendations and prioritization of immediate corrective actions. 
A copy of this report shall be submitted to the Manufacturing Engineering 
Manager, the Ford Project Engineer and the Construction Purchasing Buyer. 
9.29.4.10 
The final report shall be submitted in typical professional reporting format and 
shall include all of the following: 
 
1. 
An introduction presenting pertinent data to the study with dates, individuals 
involved, method used, etc. 
 
2. 
An individual sheet highlighting the condition of each individual conveyor, 
AGV/AGC studied with a list of corrective action recommendations including 
recommended priorities and budgetary cost estimate to complete the 
recommended improvements. The format of this sheet shall match the 
example provided Summary & Pricing Work Sheet.xls.  
 
3. 
A data summary for each conveyor, AGV/AGC studied; i.e., a table of 
ranges for Availability, 2,000-hour reliability, Mean Time Between Failures, 
Mean Time to Repair, Average Production Rate, Total Down Time, and 
Total Occurrences.  
 
4. 
Chain Pull reports. See Appendix A and Appendix D for sample output. 
 
5. 
Chain Tension reports. See Appendix B for sample output. 
 
6. 
Chain Wear reports. See Appendix C for sample output.

7.

9.30 
CONVEYOR AND RELATED EQUIPMENT REMOVAL 
9.30.1 
General 
9.30.1.1 
All removals shall be performed in a safe, neat and orderly manner. 
9.30.1.2 
When removing equipment and facilities it is required that: 
 
1. 
Process fluids and fuel lines are properly capped at the source and the 
distribution lines are removed. 
 
2. 
All equipment hangers or cradles are cleanly removed, and no structural 
damage is caused in their removal. 
 
3. 
All pipe, conduit, stacks, or chimneys extending through the roof are 
removed and the roof penetration point is to be capped or re-roofed to 
provide a weather tight seal. 
9.30.1.3 
Service and utility removals shall include all equipment, materials, wiring, 
conduit, piping, hangers, clamps, supports, etc. back to the Plant feeders, mains 
and electrical buses. Branch valves shall remain. Electrical bus plugs shall be 
removed and turned over to the Plant. Connection points at feeders, mains and 
busses shall be capped. 
9.30.1.4 
The scope for removal of electrical equipment shall include all components to 
and including the bus plug. All electrical lines shall be removed at the panel or 
buss plug, pulled from the conduit and the conduit shall also be removed. 
9.30.1.5 
Electrical service removals are to be complete from the device back to the bus 
plug. The bus plugs are to remain (assuming the bus remains). Fuses are to be 
pulled from the bus plugs and returned to the Plant. Pneumatic and weld water 
service removal is to be complete from the removed equipment back to the 
mains of the service. 
9.30.1.6 
Air service connections shall be removed to the main air header gate valve and 
shall be terminated with a capped nipple. 
9.30.1.7 
The scope for removal of support steel shall include all floor/truss mounted 
support steel, sway bracing, beam clamps, etc., up to and including main 
headers. Removal of all headers at truss elevation shall be by unbolting only.  
9.30.1.8 
The scope for removal of equipment in pits and trenches shall include the 
removal of the pit or trench as described in the project bid specification. The pit 
or trench shall be saw cut, demolished, filled and shall be restored to the plant 
finish floor, guarded or plated over as described in the project bid specification. 
9.30.1.9 
All guard postholes, existing pits and trenches from conveyor and equipment 
relocations or removals shall be sand filled, and flooring refinish to as new 
condition. (Refer to Civil Standards section for concrete work standards.) 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 409 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.30.1.10 
Removal of hazardous material shall be completed in compliance with all local, 
state and federal regulations. Removal of hazardous materials shall be by a 
licensed hazardous removal Contractor only. The Contractor shall be responsible 
for hiring a qualified removal Contractor, for transportation and disposal and for 
obtaining all required permits for removal, transportation and disposal of 
hazardous materials. Approval and granting of permits must be obtained prior to 
the start of actual “on site” removal and shall be handled as directed by the 
agency or governing body. 
9.30.1.11 
When removals are required to install new or reused conveyors, the Prime 
Contractor shall remove all equipment including power sources back to the 
mains, all headers, hangers, platforms, pits (including cut, brake out and cap), 
etc. DO NOT ABANDON IN PLACE any equipment associated with these 
removals.  
9.30.1.12 
The Prime Contractor’s scope of work shall include all removals and proper 
disposal of all scrap materials, fills, soil material, and/or existing equipment on a 
daily basis, no exceptions. The Prime Contractor shall remove material from job 
site as it is identified to maintain a neat, safe and clean working area. Removal of 
material from the premises is the responsibility of the Prime Contractor, but this 
removal must be coordinated through the local plant environmental engineer or 
his representative only and must follow local plant procedures and preferences. 
Prime Contractor shall the cover costs for the 3rd party authorized local waste 
management Supplier to remove material from the premises as required to 
complete the scope of work. 
9.30.1.13 
Timing for roof penetrations, siding removals, wall removals, etc. shall be 
carefully scrutinized and scheduled for weather conditions before penetrating the 
facility to prevent leakage and inclement conditions within the Plant. Sealing of all 
openings shall be accomplished as soon as possible to eliminate the possibility 
of openings being exposed to the weather. 
9.30.1.14 
All materials to be reused shall be relocated to an area designated by the 
Assembly Plant until required for re-work and installation. 
9.30.2 
Pallet and Skid Floor Conveyors 
9.30.2.1 
Head frames and take-up frames shall be removed intact. Modular head frames 
shall be removed complete with drive units, junction box and valve board, where 
applicable. 
9.30.2.2 
Intermediate track sections shall be removed in 10 or 20-foot (approximate) 
sections. The use of cutting torches will be permitted to separate sections only if 
splice joints cannot be broken by unbolting. The track sections shall not be 
removed from the support bents. The entire track section assembly – i.e., wear 
bar, support bents and cover plate – shall remain intact for shipment. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 410 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.30.2.3 
Cover plate and drive guards shall be shipped with the head, tail and 
intermediate track sections as a complete module. Where necessary, individual 
or loose cover plates shall be marked, banded together and palletized for 
shipment. 
9.30.2.4 
The CC-5 type conveyor chain assembly shall be removed in 10 feet long 
sections. The individual 10 feet sections shall be individually coiled, banded and 
palletized for shipment. When approved by the Ford Project Engineer, the chain 
sections may be left with the intermediate track sections, secured to prevent 
movement during handling, and shipped with the track section. 
9.30.2.5 
Conveyor slat type chain assembly shall be disassembled in 10-foot lengths 
(maximum) by removal of pins. The individual 10 feet sections shall be 
individually coiled, banded and palletized for shipment. 
9.30.2.6 
Power Roll Conveyors shall be removed intact complete with drives, support 
legs, etc. secured and palletized as required for shipment. 
9.30.2.7 
Center- and End-Pivot Turntables shall be removed intact with base, roll top, 
drives, etc. secured and palletized as required for shipment.  
9.30.2.8 
Lift tables and hold tables shall be removed intact with base, roll top, drives, etc. 
secured and palletized as required for shipment. 
9.30.3 
Vertical Drop Lifters 
9.30.3.1 
Top frames, drive assemblies and carriage frames are to be removed intact. 
Drive assemblies shall be removed complete with gearmotor units and junction 
box as applicable. 
9.30.3.2 
Intermediate track sections shall be removed as standard sections. Do not 
remove track from supports. 
9.30.3.3 
Power Roll Conveyors shall be removed intact complete with drives, junction box, 
etc. secured and palletized as required for shipment. 
9.30.3.4 
Carriage locks, where applicable, shall be removed intact with base, roll top, 
drives, etc. secured and palletized as required for shipment.  
9.30.4 
Trolley Conveyors 
9.30.4.1 
Rivet less chain shall be "loaded" into corrugated steel containers or wire baskets 
in suitable fashion for shipping or disposition as indicated in the project 
specifications. 
9.30.4.2 
Drive and take-up assemblies shall be removed intact. Drive assemblies shall be 
removed complete with gearmotor units and junction box as applicable. 
9.30.4.3 
Intermediate track sections shall be removed as standard sections by unbolting 
at the splices, banded and secured for transportation as indicated in the project 
specifications. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 411 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.30.4.4 
Where reuse of roller turns, traction wheel turns, P&F track switches, etc. is 
identified in the project specification, these items shall be removed as intact 
modules, palletized and secured for shipment as indicated in the project 
specifications. 
9.30.4.5 
All chain anti-runaway and anti-backup, carrier anti-backup and carrier secondary 
safety restraints shall be disposed of as defined in the project specifications. 
Reuse of these devices is strictly prohibited. 
9.30.4.6 
Carrier anti-runaway conveyors may be reused subject to inspection prior to 
being designated for reuse and in compliance with this specification section. 
9.30.5 
Flat Top Conveyors 
9.30.5.1 
All removed chain shall be palletized. Each pallet load shall weigh 4,500 pounds 
maximum with 9'-0" chain lengths stacked flat and separated with hard wood 2x4 
spacers. 
9.30.5.2 
Chain assemblies shall be covered with rust preventive prior to shipping/storage. 
9.30.6 
I-Beam Bridge Cranes and Related Equipment 
9.30.6.1 
Remove hoists, process fixtures, load attachments, balancers, trolleys complete. 
9.30.6.2 
Remove bridge rail complete with end trucks, bridge stiffeners, and attachment 
block. Do not disassemble. 
9.30.6.3 
Torch cut runway beams at weld splices in lengths of no more than 40 linear feet. 
All monorail and runway hangers, splices and related fasteners/material shall be 
removed and scrapped. 
9.30.6.4 
All end stops, safety cables and related fasteners/material shall be removed and 
scrapped. 
9.30.6.5 
Bridge rails, hoists, trolleys and process fixtures shall be carefully removed and 
stored in compliance with the project bid specification. 
9.30.7 
Enclosed Track Bridge Cranes and Related Equipment 
9.30.7.1 
All fasteners shall be scrapped. 
9.30.7.2 
If applicable, remove electrified rail, collector taglines, collector trolleys, internal 
bus and services complete with all wiring, piping and controls. All material shall 
be scrapped. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 412 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
9.30.7.3 
Bridge crane hoist and pendent, process equipment, load attachments, etc. shall 
be carefully removed, and stored in compliance with the project bid specification. 
9.30.7.4 
Hose supports, end caps, bumpers, balancers, valve stations shall be carefully 
removed and stored in compliance with the project bid specification. 
9.30.7.5 
Runway rails, monorail, tool rail and end caps shall be disassembled by unbolting 
only at each splice and shall be carefully removed and stored in compliance with 
the project bid specification. All fasteners shall be scrapped. 
9.30.7.6 
Disassemble and remove all suspension rods, suspension clamps, safety cables, 
rope clamps, alignment adapters and fasteners complete. This equipment shall 
be scrapped. 
9.30.7.7 
All rail, trolleys, track, tool rail, hoists, balancers, process equipment, etc. shall be 
carefully removed, dismantled, and stored in compliance with the project bid 
specification. 
9.30.8 
Patented Rail Bridge Cranes and Related Equipment 
9.30.8.1 
All patented or precision rail shall be removed by unbolting only. Torch cutting at 
the manufactured joints or any bolted connection will not be allowed. 
9.30.8.2 
Remove end stops, bumpers and castings from runways and save. 
9.30.8.3 
Remove hoists, process fixtures, tractors, trolleys, balancers, carriers, etc., 
complete. 
9.30.8.4 
Remove bridge rail, end trucks, truck stiffeners complete. Do not disassemble. 
9.30.8.5 
Remove patented or precision rail by unbolting only. All rail to be saved unless 
directed to scrap due to excessive wear. 
9.30.8.6 
All splice plates, wear plates, web plates, suspension hangers, chair washers, 
beam clamps, gimbals, saddles, track couplings and fasteners are to be 
disassembled from the patented rail. This equipment is to be scrapped. 
9.30.8.7 
Bridge, load trolleys, tractors, process fixtures, balancers and hoists to be 
removed intact and saved. 
9.30.8.8 
If applicable, safe power bar, collector shoes, couplings, insulators, control 
panels and balconies shall be completely removed and scrapped. 
9.30.8.9 
If applicable, body decking hoists and carriers shall be carefully removed, 
dismantled, and stored in compliance with the project bid specification. All 
suspension cables shall be carefully removed and scraped. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JAN. 10, 2014 
SECTION 9 – FACILITY CONVEYORS 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
Page 413 of 414 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS

9.30.9 
Tool Rail, Line Lighting and Related Equipment 
9.30.9.1 
For I-beam tool rail requirements, refer to the specifications identified in GVOSS 
section 9.20. 
9.30.9.2 
For enclosed track tool rail requirements, refer to the specifications identified in 
GVOSS section 9.21. 
9.30.9.3 
Process light fixtures for all units shall be saved and returned to the plant. The 
Contractor shall remove all ballasts on all removed fixtures (to be scrapped). 
Ballasts shall be placed in containers provided by and disposed of by the Plant. 
9.30.10 
Conveyor Controls and Related Equipment 
9.30.10.1 
Any power feed from a bus plug scheduled for demolition or removal shall be 
completely removed from the bus plug back to the control panel. The bus plug 
shall be removed and turned over to the Plant for disposal.  
9.30.10.2 
All field wiring and field cables scheduled for demolition or removal shall be 
removed from all equipment, building columns, trusses, etc. as required to 
present a clean site from the field devices back to the control panel terminal 
strips.  
9.30.10.3 
No field wiring shall be abandoned in place without written permission from the 
Owner. In the event that permission is granted to leave abandoned wiring in 
place, then the wires shall be clearly marked as spare. Each wire shall have a 
unique tag at both ends. Tags shall not be duplicated within the control panel and 
shall indicate control panel ID. 
9.30.10.4 
No conduit, cable tray or wireway or the supports for same shall be abandoned in 
place or left in the trusses place without written permission from the Owner. All 
conduit, cable tray, wireway scheduled for demolition or removal shall be 
completely removed from the equipment, building column, trusses, etc. as 
required to present a neat and clean appearance. The conduit, cable tray and/or 
wireway shall be removed from the field device(s) back to the control panel. 
9.30.10.5 
Conduit run under the floor shall have all the wires pulled out of it, conduit to be 
cut level with floor, ground and filled with epoxy filler at both ends.  
9.30.10.6 
All junction boxes, covers, conduit covers, and other covers damaged and/or 
removed during demolition, removal or rework shall be replaced with new covers.  
9.30.10.7 
All field devices, tools and other electrical equipment scheduled for demolition or 
removal shall be completely removed from all equipment, building columns, 
trusses, etc. as required to present a clean site.  
9.30.10.8 
Control devices, control panels, graphic stations, etc., to be salvaged shall be 
turned over to the Plant at the Plant's direction.

Revision Date: June 8, 2025

9.30.10.9 
All house lighting shall be returned to working order after the demolition or 
removal. A layout of existing house lighting, working and non-working shall be 
submitted to the Field Engineer before the start of demolition or removal for 
verification of condition after demolition or removal.  
9.30.10.10 
If the control panel is to remain, all demolition and removal of wire shall be back 
to the field terminal strips. The components that are to be abandoned in place in 
the control panel shall be marked as "SPARE" and the drawings shall be 
corrected showing the components as "SPARE". Any holes left in the control 
panel shall be plugged with the proper hole closure device.  
9.30.10.11 
If lighting and fans are to be demolished or removed, the power rail, conduit and 
wiring shall be pulled back to the lighting panel and removed.

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS

10.1 
GENERAL 
10.1.1 
Purpose 
10.1.1.1 
This document shall provide the specifications which will be applied to all vision 
applications within Ford Motor Company VOME.  Any attempt to waiver from the 
specifications contained within this document MUST be approved by VOME 
Controls and must be accompanied by a signed deviation outlining the details of, 
and justification for the change. 
10.1.2 
Scope 
10.1.2.1 
The requirements contained within this document apply to all vision applications 
and systems that may be used within VOME. Such applications include, but are 
not limited to:  
• Remote Viewing – Surveillance type systems utilized to give visual feedback 
of process when direct access or line of sight is not possible, i.e. inspecting 
suspect applied sealer outside of fenced safety area.  
• Error Proofing – For in process checking of part presence, absence, 
orientation, or other misc. Poke Yoke applications. 
• Robot Guidance – Part orientation that is calculated from a known location 
and offsets sent to robot interface. 
• Gaging – Dimensional acquisition and reporting applications.  
• Best Fit – The utilization of pertinent body structure edge points to calculate 
the optimum fit of workpiece. This application is utilized in closures hanging 
as well as “Net Form & Pierce”. 
• Bead Inspection – Inspection of various material beads such as sealant and 
adhesive utilizing 3D technologies based on a laser triangulation sensor to 
extract dimensional data as to the bead quality. This is to be utilized when 
normal Error Proofing methods do not provide the required process feedback 
required for critical features. 
 
• Bin Picking – The use of computerized 3D vision to pick parts out of a bin / 
container. Parts can be structured, semi-structured or completely random. 
The system is also capable of aligning the picked part in hand to successfully 
set down in the next station. 
Any application not specifically contained within this document MUST be 
approved by VOME Controls Subject Matter Expert (SME). Refer to the 
Approved Vision Components for camera/systems manufacturers currently 
approved for the above applications. VOME retains final approval of technology 
manufacturer/supplier, or any new/different technologies. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 8, 2012 
SECTION10 - VISION SYSTEMS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
Page 3 of 14 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
10.1.2.2 
Define individual roles and responsibilities of organizations involved: 
• Camera or Vision System Manufacturer  
• System Distributor 
• Tier 1 Integrator 
• Vision System Integrator 
• Ford VOME 
10.1.3 
Qualified Vision Personnel 
10.1.3.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator must contract Vison System Integrators to engineer and 
integrate vision content within a program. The only acceptable circumstances for 
the Tier 1 Integrator to integrate a vision system into the program is when using 
the approved Remote Viewing System (a preconfigured system) or Error 
Proofing System listed on the SOW or obtaining a deviation from the Ford VO 
Vision SME after a review of the Tier 1 Integrator vision integration team and 
support plan. 
10.1.3.2 
The approved Vision Integrator for technologies are listed within the program’s 
Section 3.4 Capitalized Asset System and Special Component Sourcing 
Specification document. 
10.1.3.3 
Planned plant floor launch support hours should be considered part of the 
content of a vision system, and a matrix of these hours provided to the program 
launch lead and Vision SME. 
10.1.4 
Documentation 
10.1.4.1 
All worksheets in this section shall be stored in the Controls TeamCenter folder 
for the program. 
10.1.4.2 
Copies of the worksheets shall be posted at the vision system in a Tarifold. 
10.2 
SYSTEM TYPES AND SPECIFICATIONS 
10.2.1 
General Specifications 
10.2.1.1 
The following criteria apply to all vision systems, regardless of application. 
• All vision systems apart from bin picking shall have a system recovery 
process that assists in camera replacement that maintains a recent 
backup image of a reference target. The bin picking recovery process shall 
use a specially designed calibration aide. 
• For robotic applications apart from bin picking a programmed manual routine 
which drives the camera/sensor to the reference target to take an image of 
the target shall be in place with the ability to view both a live image and the 
reference image on the associated PC controller.  The procedure and the 
target used as a reference to validate the position of the camera must be 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 8, 2012 
SECTION10 - VISION SYSTEMS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
Page 4 of 14 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
validated by the manufacturer of the vision system. Bin picking shall use an 
automated routine. 
• When utilizing vision for error proofing applications that are not robotic a 
reference target should be developed to assist in the re-aiming/focusing of the 
camera, for example a Master workpiece from the process placed in fixture. 
Additionally, the ability to manually trigger the camera and associated lighting 
must be provided.  
• Consideration of ability to observe the system video monitor in relation to any 
potential service activities should be considered; if there is not a clear 
readable line of sight from the service activities back to the system monitor a 
visual source needs to be provided for the application. This source could be a 
piece of software that can be put on a plant laptop, or a monitor placed within 
the station. 
10.2.1.2 
All vision systems shall be capable of supporting a base camera solution 
capacity of the current model year plus the same capacity in addition to support 
another model year, understanding that each specific vision solution will 
potentially require a number of unique cameras to process the vision solution. As 
an example, a current model year may have 2 models that require 4 cameras for 
one model and 5 for the second, 9 in total; therefore, in order to support the 
currently running model, plus the development of the next model year a total 
capacity the system is able to support would be 18 cameras. This requirement 
does not specify that the required hardware for additional model years be part of 
the initial build, only that the system has the base system capability. Only those 
cameras/sensors necessary to accomplish the statement of work need be 
installed. 
• If the system requires that a Tool Changer to be part of the process the vision 
system must recover communications from controller to cameras/sensors 
within 5 seconds from attachment, as well as meeting the requirements under 
the controls/robot standard followed; for Next-Gen Robots see Section 5; for 
GVOSS Robots see Section 13. 
• Any exceptions to this requirement must be reviewed by the VOME Controls 
and Manufacturing Engineer assigned to the project. 
10.2.1.3 
All systems shall support a designed retry strategy per application.  
• Non-robotic applications, such as error proofing, will allow one vision cycle 
attempt and upon unsuccessful completion will identify the part as suspect, 
reject to pullout station, or hold in fixture depending upon plant preference 
and requirements of the process and subject to approval of VOME.  
• De-racking applications will make 2 attempts to obtain offset resolution and if 
both fail the robot will abort back to the home position and complete robot I/O 
housekeeping to put rack count register to proper value.  

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 8, 2012 
SECTION10 - VISION SYSTEMS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
Page 5 of 14 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• BestFit applications will make one fitting cycle and upon failure or out of 
tolerance values will load to average/nominal or have a retry cycle, these 
functions have the requirement of external verification via the robot teach 
pendant. 
• Gaging applications will complete one cycle and upon failure mark the part 
with the failure/reject status in the appropriate plant system (AVS, IBBS, QLS, 
HPCS, etc.). 
• As part of the overall strategy, if a load to nominal or average is not available, 
such as in an error proofing application, there will be a bypass capability built 
into the architecture. 
• Bin picking systems shall be designed to be fault tolerant. Clamp / part 
present faults shall not stop the process or indicate a fault condition until three 
consecutive fault conditions have occurred. In the event of a single clamp / 
part present fault occurrence the system shall be able to recover and retry the 
pick operation without any intervention. 
10.2.1.4 
All PC based vision systems shall be capable of being powered by grounded 
local medium voltage and all peripheral equipment and any "smart camera" or 
self-contained systems shall be capable of being powered by 24 VDC and utilize 
cables and connectors as specified in controls/robot standard followed; for Next-
Gen Controls see Section 8; for GVOSS Controls see Section 14. 
10.2.1.5 
All vision systems shall support an Ethernet IP interface utilizing an M12 
connector interface and cabling as specified in controls/robot standard followed; 
for Next-Gen Controls see Section 8; for GVOSS Controls see Section 14 and 
those systems requiring Power Over Ethernet (POE) shall insure that isolated 
power source is provided for the POE or is sourced from the Control System 24 
VDC safety bus.  
10.2.1.6 
Vision systems PC enclosure and components shall have a minimum of IP65 
rating.  
10.2.1.7 
All lighting necessary for the proper operation of the system shall be supplied by 
or reviewed and signed off by the vision system integrator.  
10.2.1.8 
All vision systems shall be unaffected by ambient light, workpiece variations, or 
any associated coatings and the system should continue to function without error 
in changing ambient lighting conditions. If the ambient lighting will be problematic 
to the application, the Tier 1 Integrator and integrator are responsible to provide 
methods of protecting the area of application from this lighting. The robustness of 
the application will need to be monitored over a one-year period from Protag 
Green to insure seasonal lighting changes do not impact application.  
10.2.1.9 
All vision system suppliers shall provide the equipment necessary to 
interface/program their respective components.  

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 8, 2012 
SECTION10 - VISION SYSTEMS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
Page 6 of 14 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• If a vision system does not require a dedicated PC, such as in the case of 
smart cameras, the supplier will provide a copy of any software required to 
interface with the system, cabling to external PC, and necessary training for 
plant personnel. Such equipment must include an industrial computer used 
for image storage and programming purposes, and must be one of the 
approved units listed at http://www.dynics.net/DynicsFord/index.html. 
• Such equipment may include an industrial computer used for image storage 
and programming purposes and must meet requirements contained within 
Section 11 of the GVOSS Standard.  
• Any system that requires interfacing over the controls Ethernet/IP network 
shall be approved by VOME Controls prior to use if it not listed on the 
approved technology matrix.  
• When installing a plant floor PC for an application, a second Ethernet card 
shall be installed for plant floor communication purposes. IP address will need 
to be assigned by Plant. 
10.2.1.10 
All vision systems shall be capable of executing their function within one second 
timed from trigger to data/complete received. Vision systems that bury the image 
acquisition and processing during nonproductive time after initial cycle are 
exempt from the above statement if station cycle time is not impacted. 
10.2.1.11 
For vision systems that directly interface with welding processes, the integrator 
shall be responsible to choose lenses that are suitable for the welding 
environment.  
• Cameras shall have removable lens covers to facilitate quick changing should 
the lens become contaminated. 
• When adjacent to a weld environment the lens cover will be manufactured of 
Sapphire or have a mechanical means of replaceable/retractable protection. 
Any mechanical protective measures must be approved by VOME Controls 
prior to use. 
• Once system is set up on plant floor the focus and aperture rings shall be 
secured in place with thumbscrews or other means of protecting settings from 
change.  
10.2.1.12 
All vision systems, regardless of application, shall follow the Ford standard robot 
and PLC interface contained within controls/robot standard followed; for Next-
Gen Robots see Section 5; for GVOSS Robots see Section 13. 
10.2.1.13 
All vision system components shall be identified with a tag. In general, this tag 
shall include the component name. For camera’s this tag shall include both the 
name and IP address(s). Dedicated vision PCs shall have a tag that includes the 
name as well as both CPN and local IP addresses. The tag shall be engraved 
with text will no smaller then 0.125” (3mm) and mounted next to the component. 
10.2.2 
Individual System Specs 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 8, 2012 
SECTION10 - VISION SYSTEMS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
Page 7 of 14 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
10.2.2.1 
There are seven main types of vision systems. Each system is discussed 
separately. The main types of systems are: 
• Remote Viewing System (Section 10.2.2.2)  
• Error Proofing System (Section 10.2.2.3) 
• Robot Guidance (Section 10.2.2.4) 
• Best Fit Load System (Section 10.2.2.5)  
• Gauging System (Section 10.2.2.6) 
• Bead Inspection (Section 10.2.2.7) 
• Bin Picking (Section 10.2.2.8) 
10.2.2.2 
Remote Viewing System: This type of system is used for remote viewing of 
parts/processes such as remote seal path inspections, part presence verification, 
process monitoring of enclosed systems. These systems are to provide feedback 
to personnel and have no decision-making ability. 
10.2.2.2.1 
The system may be either fixed or robot mounted depending on application. 
VOME Controls engineer sign off is required per application. 
10.2.2.2.2 
Remote viewing systems shall have no faults associated with their operation, 
though an acceptable backup strategy must be agreed upon between the Tier 1 
Integrator and the VOME ME assigned to the process. 
10.2.2.2.3 
Cameras used must utilize Ethernet as the communications. Power and cabling 
requirements for such systems must be approved by VOME Controls prior to 
use. 
10.2.2.2.4 
Remote viewing camera shall be enclosed in a protective housing appropriate for 
the environment in which it is installed. The enclosure shall have an easily 
replaceable lens cover and installed on a rigid support not susceptible to process 
vibration and in no instance shall be attached to any fencing component. 
10.2.2.2.5 
Power shall be supplied from facilities or tooling lighting panel where available or 
be supplied from the Safety/PLC CPD in all other applications. Any exceptions 
must be reviewed by VOME controls. 
10.2.2.2.6 
System must be according to latest Remote viewing BOM, contact Vision SME 
for details. 
10.2.2.3 
Error Proofing System: This type of system is used for model detection, part 
presence/absence, part location, hole/no hole detection etc. Such systems will 
accept a trigger command and provide pass/fail feedback to the control system. 
10.2.2.3.1 
The system shall be either fixed or robot mounted depending on application. 
VOME Controls engineer signoff is required per application and Error Proofing 
Application Worksheet will be completed by Integrator and supplied to the Tier 1 
Integrator and VOME Controls Vision SME. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 8, 2012 
SECTION10 - VISION SYSTEMS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
Page 8 of 14 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
10.2.2.3.2 
All fixed camera applications shall be processed to utilize a minimize number of 
cameras for the application. 
10.2.2.3.3 
The system shall be immune to vibration and electrical noise inherent to a 
manufacturing/resistance welding environment. The cameras will be mounted 
securely and under no circumstance be attached to fencing or fence post. 
10.2.2.3.4 
Upon recognition of an error proofing system fault, the system shall trigger one 
retry cycle. Should the retry cycle fail, the unit will be held in station until the fault 
is manually accepted via a robot or HMI “soft pushbutton”. 
10.2.2.3.5 
This type of system may be used for 100% inspection of a sealer and must take 
into consideration the requirements of GVOSS Section 7.13 regarding Error 
Handling, and Table 7.18 calling out the dimensional requirements. 
10.2.2.4 
Robot Guidance System: This type of system provides guidance to the robot to 
load or unload parts from a fixed rack. Such systems directly interface with the 
associated robot to provide data which allows the robot to adjust its path in real 
time in order to load/unload a given rack. 
10.2.2.4.1 
All systems that require the associated robot to interface with material handling 
racks shall be capable of handling variations in the rack dimensions, rack 
position or part position of: 
 
Dx / Dy / Dz   +/- 19 mm (.75 inch) 
 
Rx / Ry / Rz   +/- 2 degrees 
10.2.2.4.2 
The system or external hardware shall be able to identify a rack that is 
"unloadable" and/or "loadable" and flag the rack as such before damage is done 
to the vision system or any other equipment associated with the process. 
10.2.2.4.3 
The part counting strategy will reflect that as a part is removed from a rack that 
the count register in the robot decrements and shows actual number of parts left 
in the rack. 
 
10.2.2.5 
Best Fit Load System: This type of system utilizes laser triangulation sensors to 
find workpiece edges and find optimal fit location in: 
• Closure Hanging 
• Roof Loading 
• Glass Decking 
10.2.2.5.1 
Once installed these systems shall be proven capable with a static repeatability 
test performed as follows. 
• Skid/Pallet will remain stationary 
• Robot with sensor will be driven to target 
• System will be triggered 30 times in this position 
• The data will be calculated and must reflect +/- 0.07 mm in the X, Y, Z plane 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 8, 2012 
SECTION10 - VISION SYSTEMS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
Page 9 of 14 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
• Resulting data will be populated in Vision R&R Worksheet 
10.2.2.5.2 
An offset test may also be required to be performed as follows 
• Transfer system (pallet/skid) will remain stationary 
• Robot with closure will be driven to body and fitted 
• Test will be repeated 30 times in this position 
• The data will be calculated and must reflect +/- 0.1 mm in the X, Y, Z 
reference planes. 
• Resulting data will be populated in Vision R&R Worksheet 
10.2.2.5.3 
A full dynamic test of the entire system may also be required: 
• Transfer system (pallet/skid) will be cycled into the station 30 times 
• Complete cycle will be performed from picking up and placing of closure 
• Cycling of fastener systems 
• Releasing closure from end effector  
• Resulting data will be populated in Vision R&R Worksheet 
10.2.2.6 
Gauging System: This type of system measures the dimensional accuracy of 
workpieces or assemblies and should show correlation back to VO accepted 
standard utilizing the Correlation Worksheet with each measurement point 
utilizing a minimal sample size of ten. Currently accepted traceable standards 
are: 
• Gap and Flush systems - DataMyte G&F handheld gage or handheld calipers 
and dial depth gage. 
• Inline Gaging (BIW) - CMM  
 
10.2.2.7 
Bead Inspection: Utilized to inspect sealer and adhesive bead quality. For 2D 
applications this can be accomplished utilizing either Error Proofing camera 
systems as specified in 10.2.2.3 or Remote Viewing camera systems from 
section 10.2.2.2. This section pertains to 3D in-process systems. 
10.2.2.7.1 
VOME ME shall define the strategy associated with an error triggered by the 
vision system and the disposition of the workpiece. 
10.2.2.7.2 
As a minimum the system shall be set up to monitor and trigger an error based 
on bead width and skip length as defined in GVOSS Section 7, Table 7.18. 
10.2.2.7.3 
System shall be capable of communicating with the robot and/or PLC over 
Ethernet. 
10.2.2.8 
Bin Picking. This system is used to pick parts from bins / containers that hold 
parts in structured, semi-structured or randomized arrangements. 
10.2.2.8.1 
The system shall be capable of communicating with the robot over Ethernet. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 8, 2012 
SECTION10 - VISION SYSTEMS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
Page 10 of 14 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
10.2.2.8.2 
The system shall be capable of detecting that the bin / container has been loaded 
in the proper location and orientation. 
10.2.2.8.3 
The system shall be capable of detecting an empty bin and relaying this 
information back to the robot. 
10.2.2.8.4 
Robot EOAT tools shall be designed in a manner that allows setup and 
calibration aides required by the system to be soundly mounted to the EOAT. 
The intention is these aides be mounted only during setup / calibration. During 
regular production the aides shall not be mounted. 
10.2.2.8.5 
Robot EOAT tools shall be painted with a flat finish to reduce glare and 
reflections if the part in hand needs to be brought to a secondary camera for drop 
qualification. 
10.2.2.8.6 
The system shall be capable of robot path planning. 
10.2.2.8.7 
Tier 1 integrator and Ford VOME shall coordinate to ensure a suitable number of 
parts is provided to simulate two hours of production at line rate during system 
setup and tryout. 
10.2.3 
Camera or Vision System Manufacturer Responsibilities 
10.2.3.1 
Support and develop any new or non-standard applications. 
10.2.3.2 
Support of interface software, hardware and camera PLC/Robot interface. 
10.2.4 
Camera or Equipment Supplier Responsibilities (Distributor) 
10.2.4.1 
Provide all application hardware excluding Ford to Furnish Components.  These 
include but are not limited to: 
• Ford specified camera/vision system – Ford program approved component 
list. 
• Lighting 
• Lensing 
• Cabling 
• Specialty hardware (such as 4 to 8 pin Ethernet adapters) 
• Non-custom mounting hardware 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 8, 2012 
SECTION10 - VISION SYSTEMS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
Page 11 of 14 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
10.2.5 
Tier 1 Integrator Responsibilities 
10.2.5.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide the Vision Integrator with a detailed layout of 
the relevant application environment. 
10.2.5.1.1 
Structures/brackets used for mounting vision system components shall be of a 
robust design and isolated so that they are immune to vibration. 
10.2.5.1.2 
Structures/brackets used for mounting camera /vision sensors shall not be 
permitted to have adjustment except for approved vision system components 
defined in GVOSS section 10.2.2.3. 
10.2.5.1.3 
Ball in socket type mounting brackets shall not be used to mount vision system 
components, except for approved vision system components defined in GVOSS 
section 10.2.2.3.   
10.2.5.1.4 
Unistrut shall not be used in the construction of any brackets/structures for the 
mounting of vision system components. 
10.2.5.2 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall perform a preliminary review of the installation area 
with the Vision Integrator for evaluation of external factors such as ambient 
lighting. 
10.2.5.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall procure all equipment as recommended by the Vision 
Integrator. 
10.2.5.4 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall ensure that all procured equipment arrives at the 
installation site. 
10.2.5.5 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall be responsible for the design, fabrication and 
installation of any brackets or structure necessary to mount the given vision 
system and obtain documented acceptance from the Vision Integrator. 
10.2.5.6 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall ensure that the positioning of the camera and any 
related brackets, structure or cabling does not interfere with the robot, robot path 
or any tooling associated with the application. 
10.2.5.7 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall ensure that all required cabling is routed in a best 
practice manner utilizing only hook and loop (Velcro) type wraps. 
10.2.5.8 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall ensure that robot mounted cameras are mounted to 
the vision equipment supplier specifications. 
10.2.5.9 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall oversee and ensure full integration of the vision 
system into the control architecture and facilitate communication between Ford 
VOME, the vision supplier and vision system integrator. 
10.2.5.10 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide a CAD model that ensures robot mounted 
camera field of view is not obstructed by other equipment mounted on the EOAT. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 8, 2012 
SECTION10 - VISION SYSTEMS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
Page 12 of 14 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
10.2.5.11 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall ensure that material handling racks used in vision 
applications are current with that application and meet all necessary criteria to 
ensure the system works as a whole. This includes design elements such as: 
rack locking mechanism, part separation methods, and rough location of rack 
position. If deficiencies are noted the Tier 1 Integrator shall notify the appropriate 
VOME representative in writing detailing the concerns. 
10.2.5.12 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall develop the vision system/robot interface in 
conjunction with Ford and the robot manufacturer and work with Ford to integrate 
the interface into the standard Control Logix bit map, if such an interface does 
not already exist. All other applications shall conform to the standard vision 
interface as defined by Ford VOME Controls. 
10.2.5.13 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall ensure that system parameters are set, and controls 
are in place, to prevent any erroneous offsets that may damage any equipment 
associated with the process. 
10.2.5.14 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall ensure that measures are in place to detect any 
double part pick up, drop off or load. 
10.2.5.15 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall put in place, controls that are able to detect that the 
correct rack has been loaded and the correct part is present in applications that 
require the system to interface with material handling racks. 
10.2.5.16 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall install, upon the receipt of a vision system fault, the 
ability for the PLC to label a part as "Damaged" (either via the shift register or 
RFID tag). The part will then index through the tooling system to the next 
available removal point and wait for manual intervention. 
10.2.5.17 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall ensure that proper bypass methods are programmed 
in to the vision process concurrent with Ford Standards and Ford Controls 
direction. 
10.2.5.18 
Specific to guidance applications the Tier 1 Integrator shall provide the following 
information: 
• Robot make and model 
• Application type 
− Rack/Conveyor Unload 
− Rack/Conveyor Load 
− Geo part place 
− Flow drill screw 
• Part type 
• Part location variability 
• Required accuracy 
• EOAT design 
• Interface design – ensure that EOAT can move within the container 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 8, 2012 
SECTION10 - VISION SYSTEMS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
Page 13 of 14 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
sufficiently to account for expected part shifting 
10.2.6 
Vision System Integrator Responsibilities 
10.2.6.1 
Perform upfront evaluations of the applications in order to provide an engineered 
solution based on the process and provide specifications for: 
• Lighting 
• Lensing Filters 
• Cabling (verify appropriate lengths are available) 
• Vision tool recommendation 
10.2.6.2 
Complete the applicable Worksheet and provide copies to the Tier 1 Integrator 
and VOME SME: 
• Remote Viewing - Remote Viewing Worksheet  
• Error Proofing - Error Proofing Worksheet  
• Robot Guidance - Robot Guidance Worksheet 
10.2.6.3 
Adhere to Ford Standard interfaces when programming vision applications. 
10.2.6.4 
Perform or supervise the installation of camera lighting, lensing and filtering to 
ensure proper location and function. 
10.2.6.5 
Select and implement robust set of vision software tools for the application. 
10.2.6.6 
Review camera/vision system design with the Tier 1 Integrator to ensure proper 
camera orientation and maximum field of view. 
10.2.6.7 
Provide the Tier 1 Integrator and facility where to be installed with a complete 
spare parts list. 
10.2.7 
Ford VOME Responsibilities 
10.2.7.1 
Ford VOME shall ensure that any carryover rack fleet that is retroactively fitted 
with one of the above vision systems are held to the dimensional standards as 
indicated in this document. 
10.2.7.2 
Ford VOME shall ensure that any vision systems that are specified for a given 
process are located as far away from sources of ambient light as possible and 
assist in correcting any light issues with plant personnel. 
10.2.7.3 
Ford VOME shall provide necessary parts for detailed evaluation including parts 
representative of all potential variation. 
10.2.7.4 
Ford VOME shall cascade any process changed to all involved parties to ensure 
the application must not be reevaluated. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: JUNE 8, 2012 
SECTION10 - VISION SYSTEMS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025

 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
10.2.7.5 
Ford VOME shall ensure that if bypass ability is installed into the process that 
there is a failsafe method in place to protect the process should a defect occur 
during bypass operations.

SECTION 12 
 
 
12.1.1 
General  
12.1.2 
Specification Intent 
12.1.2.1 
All AGV/AGC’s shall follow the standards and specifications set forth in the 
GVOSS. Deviations will be allowed only after the submission and approval of a 
GVOSS Equipment Standards Action Request Form. 
12.1.2.2 
All GVOSS and referenced standards, specifications, drawings, etc. – are 
intended to dimension the minimum design requirements for Vehicle Operations 
assembly plant AGV/AGC systems. 
12.1.2.3 
AGV/AGC warranty shall conform to GVOSS section 1.1.27. 
12.1.2.4 
Prior to submission of the quote, the AGV/AGC supplier shall examine existing 
Building Lighting and certify the existing lighting is adequate for proper 
functioning of the AGV/AGC equipment.  
12.1.2.5 
Prior to submission of the quote, the AGV/AGC supplier shall examine existing 
floor conditions and certify the existing floor is adequate for proper functioning of 
the AGV/AGC equipment.  
12.1.2.6 
Any floor improvements to meet the AGV/AGC supplier floor requirements shall 
be the responsibility of the AGV/AGC supplier and included in the quote. 
12.1.2.7 
Guidance components that are external to the vehicle must be installed at or 
below floor level. No component shall protrude from the floor. 
12.1.2.8 
The successful bidder shall be responsible to furnish and set all anchor bolts for 
equipment to be installed in accordance with GVOSS section 1.1.24. 
12.1.2.9 
The successful bidder shall include in their proposal those Subcontractors which 
will be used for engineering and subcontracted portions of the work. It will be a 
condition of contract award that these identified Subcontractors will be used in 
executing the specified work. 
12.1.2.10 
Tooling suppliers shall get AMR control & safety requirements from BCE controls  
before start of control design. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 4 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
12.2 
TOOLING SUPPLIER RESPONSIBILITIES 
12.2.1 
General 
12.2.1.1 
The Tier 1 supplier shall provide the AGV/AGC Supplier with design parameters 
necessary to design the AGV/AGC for approval when the AGV/AGC supplier is 
acting as a Tier 2 supplier to the Tier 1. This shall include but is not limited to: 
 
1. 
Tooling nest layout with significant dimensions and empty/loaded part nest 
center-of-gravity and weight identified. 
 
2. 
Locator scheme for tooling nest. 
 
3. 
Stop locations and elevations at load/unload stations. 
 
4. 
Acceptable positioner tolerance and repeatability required for process 
interface. 
 
5. 
Tooling segment start- and end-roll location, reference elevation and speed 
profile. 
12.2.1.2 
Tooling nest qualification fixtures shall follow GVOSS section 2.8.1.7. 
12.2.1.3 
The Tier 1 Supplier shall design, build and assemble the "part nest" tooling and 
details for part carriers. See Qualification Fixtures for AGV/AGC Tooling. 
12.2.1.4 
The Tier 1 Supplier shall provide the part present-sensing and any mechanical 
units required to stabilize parts at the load and unload points. Part-present 
sensing shall also include error-proofing at the load point to identify an improperly 
loaded part and prevent it from reaching the unload point. 
12.2.1.5 
The Tier 1 Supplier shall verify/validate via Simulation, the number of 
AGV’s/AGC’s needed to meet required Availability per SOW. Reference GVOSS 
section 1.1.10 and 1.2.4. 
12.2.1.6 
RFID shall be used when tooling is mounted directly to the AGV/AGC. When only 
one model is used mounting holes for programs shall be provided. 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 5 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
12.3 
AGV/AGC SUPPLIER RESPONSIBILITIES 
12.3.1 
General 
Applies when supplier is acting as Tier 1 supplier. 
12.3.1.1 
The AGV/AGC Supplier shall be responsible for the proper design, construction 
and installation of the AGV/AGC at the final receiving facility meeting program 
and integrator timelines. It is the responsibility of the AGV/AGC Supplier to 
ensure that the final AGV/AGC design, whether proposed by the AGV/AGC 
Supplier or by the Owner, will meet the original design intent. All bidders must 
submit detailed layout/assembly drawings with their proposal for Vehicle 
Operations review and approval. 
12.3.1.2 
To minimize industrial materials kept in inventory by the plant, the AGV/AGC 
Supplier shall ensure that all mechanical and controls components provided are 
in compliance with the approved components list in GVOSS Section 2 and the 
program SOW. Deviations shall be submitted to the Implementation Supervisor 
and/or their designated representative using the GVOSS deviation request 
process. 
12.3.1.3 
To obtain approval for equipment or material as "Owner approved equal", the 
AGV/AGC Supplier must provide written performance testing results, life 
expectancy test results, Failure Modes Effects Analysis (FMEA) and all other 
baseline data for review and approval consideration by the Implementation 
Supervisor and/or designated representative. 
12.3.1.4 
The reliability and functionality of the equipment, including compliance with all 
applicable specifications, guidelines, and requirements referenced herein, are the 
responsibility of the AGV/AGC Supplier.  
12.3.1.5 
The AGV/AGC Supplier shall be required to provide reliability data for all new 
technology introduced for the first time to Ford Motor Company before the 
equipment is approved for installation in the plant. Reliability and durability data 
shall be collected and/or completed by the AGV/AGC Supplier for all components 
included as part of the new technology application. Reliability data shall be 
collected for all new hardware introduced, both electrical and mechanical, but is 
not needed for new software. Software shall be tried out and debugged in the 
Contractor shop during modular build, try-out and approval as applicable. 
12.3.1.6 
The AGV/AGC Supplier shall provide the Tooling Supplier with a carrier 
dimensional "envelope" and design parameters to use for carrier tooling "part 
nest" design. The AGV/AGC Supplier shall verify the center of mass of the carrier 
tooling "nest" and shall also verify the design will function as intended on the 
AGV/AGC for both the loaded and empty AGV/AGC. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 6 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
12.3.1.7 
The AGV/AGC Supplier shall provide the facility layout engineer and the Tooling 
Supplier with an AGV/AGC dimensional "envelope" and design parameters to 
use for the purpose of determining clearances for material handling and 
production processes and with adjacent equipment. 
12.3.1.8 
The AGV/AGC and Tooling Suppliers shall have regular communications for the 
purpose of simultaneous engineering to exchange information relevant to the 
AGV/AGC design. The AGV/AGC Supplier shall be responsible to ensure the 
Implementation Supervisor and/or their designated representative is made aware 
of the meeting schedule. 
12.3.1.9 
The Contractor shall refer to GVOSS Section 9.3.2 thru 9.3.7 to Project 
documentation, Submittal, Launch Book, Schedule and setting up design 
Review. Refer to GVOSS section 1.2.2 for schedule submission. 
12.3.2 
AGV/AGC System Installation 
12.3.2.1 
The Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier shall complete all phases of the AGV/AGC system 
installation in compliance with the PROTAG Standard FAS08-036 as described 
in GVOSS paragraph 1.1.23.1. The VOME Conveyor Customer Checklist may 
also be completed as part of the PROTAG approval process. 
12.3.2.2 
The on-site construction and installation phases shall be identified on the 
Contractor provided project planning schedule. The Contractor shall submit the 
detailed construction and installation schedule for approval one month, minimum, 
prior to any on-site construction and installation. 
12.3.2.3 
The Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier is responsible to complete a Conveyor System 
Fault Log to identify equipment performance during facility launch, system fill, 
production start-up and standby periods. 
12.3.2.4 
The on-site construction and installation of the AGV/AGC and related equipment 
shall be divided into the following five phases: 
 
1. 
Installation – period for construction of facilities and installation of equipment 
and systems. 
 
2. 
Debug – period for debug and adjustment of AGV/AGC and related 
equipment. Equipment modifications and adjustments shall be 100% 
complete at the end of the Debug Phase. 
 
3. 
Tryout – period to tryout AGV/AGC systems and related equipment to verify 
construction and installations comply with project requirements. This phase 
includes the 20-hour dry cycle test runs. 
 
4. 
Facility Launch – period to prove to the Owner that all equipment and 
systems operate in accordance with specified project parameters. This 
phase includes the 20-hour prove-out runs that simulate normal production 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 7 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
conditions. 
 
5. 
Production Launch – period for the Owner to begin use of AGV/AGC and 
related equipment for production and the Contractors period to provide 
equipment and systems training, system fill, start-up and standby. 
12.3.2.5 
Installation Phase: 
 
1. 
The Contractor shall be responsible for constructing the facility AGV/AGC 
and related equipment and systems in accordance with all applicable project 
specifications. 
12.3.2.6 
Debug Phase: 
 
1. 
During the Debug Phase, the Contractor shall check, verify, correct and 
adjust all equipment, components and systems utilizing production units, 
parts and materials under production conditions to ensure that all Project 
Specification requirements have been met. 
 
2. 
The Contractor shall utilize appropriate skilled trades, engineers and 
technicians to check motor rotations, verify field wiring and piping, check 
and adjust speeds, check and adjust sequences, check and adjust limit/prox 
switch position and operation, check and adjust equipment elevations, 
check and adjust interlocking equipment, etc. to ensure that all design 
requirements have been satisfactorily met. 
 
3. 
The Contractor shall provide all manpower, equipment, materials, skilled 
trades and system support required to operate and maintain all equipment 
and systems during the Tryout Phase. 
12.3.2.7 
Tryout Phase: 
 
1. 
The AGV/AGC Supplier shall be responsible for the satisfactory tryout of all 
equipment and systems including equipment and systems provided by 
Subcontractors and Suppliers. The Tryout Phase will begin only after the 
equipment and systems are capable of operating as designed and in a safe 
manner. 
 
2. 
Programmable safety systems and safety interlocks to adjacent systems 
shall follow the w-EL10 process.  
 
3. 
The AGV/AGC supplier shall provide all manpower and system support 
required to operate and maintain all equipment and systems during the 
Tryout Phase. 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 8 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
4. 
The AGV/AGC supplier shall verify the following as a condition of 
completing the Tryout Phase (prior to the Facility Launch Phase): 
A. 
All equipment and systems shall be tested and determined “launch 
capable” by operating at minimum through maximum loads, limits and 
rates, checking all controls, safety devices and software programs. 
B. 
All equipment and systems shall be operated in the full manual mode 
and full automatic mode to prove operational capacity meets design 
criteria and parameters. 
C. 
All equipment shall be operated, including main and standby 
equipment, to prove operational capacity meets design criteria and 
parameters. 
D. 
Each piece of equipment shall be operated in “dry cycle” mode for two 
(2) consecutive days – i.e., a 20-hour run – without equipment 
malfunction or failure. 
E. 
All equipment and systems that require synchronization with other 
equipment 
and 
systems 
shall 
be 
tested 
to 
assure 
proper 
synchronization and operation. 
F. 
After each piece of equipment has been operated in the 20-hour dry-
cycle run, all equipment and systems shall be able to automatically 
circulate a group of units or parts to prove out the equipment and 
system interlocks, cycle times and synchronization. 
12.3.2.8 
Facility Launch Phase: 
 
1. 
The Facility Launch Phase is when the AGV/AGC supplier shall 
demonstrate that all facilities, equipment and systems including all 
components, perform in compliance with the original design intent. 
 
2. 
The AGV/AGC supplier shall coordinate and provide all manpower, 
equipment, access tools and equipment, materials, skilled trades and 
system support required to run the entire system (and re-run after a 
malfunction or failure, as necessary), document all project requirements, 
and support random verification of Contractor information. 
 
3. 
Prior to acceptance of a system, the functionality and reliability of standby 
equipment under production conditions shall be demonstrated. 
 
4. 
The Contractor shall coordinate and complete a prove-out test that   
                simulates normal production conditions. 
A. 
To demonstrate the system is “ready to run” in the production 
environment, the Contractor shall operate all equipment separately 
and as a system, for 20-hours under normal production conditions, 
with no interruptions due to AGV/AGC or related equipment 
malfunction, fault, or failure. Following a malfunction, fault or failure, 
the prove-out test shall restart the 20-hour run until it is accomplished 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 9 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
without interruption.  
B. 
The prove-out test shall consist of a 20-hour automatic run of 
continuous operation utilizing production pallets//carriers and parts. 
C. 
All Facility Launch activities shall be coordinated and integrated. No 
individual conveyor system shall be launched until all related systems 
are ready to launch. No interdependent AGV/AGC equipment or 
system shall be launched as a stand-alone component. 
D. 
The prove-out test shall be conducted with a minimum grouping 75% 
of the AGV/AGC. The group of 75% of the AGV/AGC shall be run 
through the system together to prove-out AGV/AGC transitions, cycle-
times, unit spacing, sequencing, etc. 
E. 
One time during the 20-hour run and prior to acceptance, the 
equipment and/or systems shall be fully turned off and then restarted 
to insure proper re-start functions. Timing for this test shall be 
scheduled and pre-approved by the Owner. 
 
5. 
Training of plant operational, maintenance and technical personnel may 
begin during this phase. Facility Launch 20-hour runs will not be interrupted 
for training purposes. 
 
6. 
Once all equipment and systems are accepted, the AGV/AGC supplier shall 
be responsible for readjusting the equipment and systems, as needed, to 
comply with the plant production requirements. 
12.3.2.9 
Production Launch Phase: 
 
1. 
After the Facility Launch Phase is complete, there will be a period of time 
described as the Production Launch Phase. During this phase, plant 
production personnel will finish their equipment and system training, 
system-fill will take place, and production will begin. 
 
2. 
Tryout and Debug modifications and Facilities Launch adjustments and re-
testing are not acceptable during the Production Launch Phase. All 
adjustments and retesting must be complete prior to the Production Launch 
Phase. 
 
3. 
The plant personnel shall not have their attention diverted due to facility, 
equipment or system malfunctions, faults or failures. It is expected that plant 
maintenance personnel will not be proficient enough to keep the facility 
running under production conditions. The Contractor shall provide 
manpower to support plant maintenance personnel during the Production 
Launch Phase as indicated in the Start-Up/Standby unit in the project DED 
specification. 
4. 
The AGV/AGV supplier shall update the Facility Launch Book and all 
Baseline Data Sheets to document the status of all facility, equipment and 
system settings and indicators at the end of the Production Launch Phase. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 10 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
12.3.3 
Baseline Data 
12.3.3.1 
Each AGV/AGC and related production system shall be broken down into their 
component parts for compliance verification. All compliance information shall be 
collected to the level of detail as directed by the Owner and shall be documented 
by the Contractor on Baseline Data Sheet Forms. Baseline Data Sheet 
information shall be included in the appropriate section of the Operations and 
Maintenance Manual. 
12.3.3.2 
The Contractor shall complete the Baseline Data Sheet Forms for every 
AGV/AGC Systems installed, relocated or reworked. Baseline Data Sheets shall 
include the following types of component information displayed in check list and 
matrix formats: 
 
1. 
Description and identifier, design cross reference number, location, 
arrangements, model, tag and name plate details, installation and launch 
date, components, component brands, materials, Supplier contract, 
warrantee and guarantee information, etc. 
 
2. 
Design, simulation, performance, system and operational parameters, 
values, ranges, limits, indicators, speeds, timing, transfer rates, integration 
information, safety requirements, etc. 
 
3. 
Actual field operational data, readings, settings, range indicators, cycle 
times, operational, start/stop and interlock tests, launch and test durations 
and conditions, vibration, frequency and infrared signatures, alignment 
measurements, air balance reports, etc. 
12.3.3.3 
For each measurement and test, the Baseline Data Sheet shall indicate the test 
procedure, method of measurement, instrumentation used, the date, and the 
person collecting the field information. The intent is to ensure the integrity and 
repeatability of each measurement and test. 
12.3.3.4 
The Contractor shall ensure that all measurements and tests are performed 
under the supervision of a certified person in accordance with recognized 
industrial standards and procedures. All instrumentation shall be calibrated to the 
accuracy stated in the applicable Ford Motor Co. standards and procedures. 
12.3.3.5 
The final AGV/AGC baseline data form will be developed by the Contractor and 
approved by the Owner for each type of AGV/AGC installed, relocated or 
reworked. 
12.3.3.6 
The AGV/AGC baseline data form(s) shall be submitted as a condition of final 
project close-out.

12.4  
SAFETY REQUIREMENTS FOR AGV/AGC 
12.4.1 
General 
12.4.1.1 
AGV/AGC machine safeguarding standards are intended to protect employees 
from crushing, pinching, shearing and impact injuries which may result from 
exposure to various aspects of machine operation and layout. The intimate 
understanding of equipment and its associated hazards should allow the Supplier 
to meet the intent of this standard. This understanding is a natural outcome of the 
risk assessment each provider shall conduct on both systems and individual 
AGV/AGC elements with respect to machine layout, design and functions. 
12.4.1.2 
This safeguarding specification is classified in five general areas, specific 
requirements for which will be detailed in this standard. 
 
1. 
Guarding

12.4.1.3 
All Suppliers shall be responsible for being thoroughly familiar with the latest 
edition of all Ford Motor Company safety specifications and all applicable 
Federal, State (or Provincial), Local safety standards, laws, codes, ordinances 
and regulations. 
12.4.1.4 
The AGV/AGC may be considered a “Catalog Item” as defined in FAS08-036  
Safety Risk Assessment for Design, Build, Modification and Commissioning of 
Equipment. 
The 
AGV/AGC 
manufacturer 
shall 
submit 
the 
following 
documentation: 
 
1. FAS08-036 Risk Assessment Requirements for The Design, Build, 
Modification and Commissioning of Equipment. 
2. Product Manuals 
3. Product Safety Marks (CE, UL, CSA, CCC) 
4. Relevant safety standards, safety functions and parameters. (PFH, SIL, PL) 
5. List of safety related components (safety controller, I/O devices, presence 
sensing devices, drives) and Emergency Stop controls 
12.4.1.5 
This Section is to be used in conjunction with the following Ford FCS sections.  
This Section shall supersede any conflicts between this Section and those listed 
below.
