12.4.1.8 
The AGV/AGC Supplier shall ensure the equipment provided is in full compliance 
with the identification and written procedures for labeling and control of 
hazardous energy as specified in the FAS08-100 Energy Control and Power 
Lockout and FAS08-102 Energy Control/Power Lockout Placards.  
12.4.1.9 
For major program installations, pedestrian traffic flow must be studied during the 
design stage. The appropriate Ford VOME Engineer, Plant Engineer and MP&L 
Engineer shall provide a layout identifying formally established and clearly 
designated routes to production operations as well as maintenance access to 
equipment. The Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier shall ensure the final AGV/AGC and 
related equipment layouts provide both production and maintenance access 
without exposure to hazards.

12.4.2 
Guarding 
12.4.2.1 
The Tier1/AGV/AGC Supplier shall design product and personnel guarding to 
allow for as much shop pre-assembly as is possible. Reference GVOSS section 
1.3. 
12.4.2.2 
Guarding for moving components and protection from moving parts shall be in 
accordance with ANSI B56.5 and with the following: 
12.4.2.2.1 
All moving parts that represent a hazard to personnel shall be suitably guarded in 
conformance with the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) - Title 29, Part 1910, 
Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) standards. 
 
FORD of EUROPE: Machinery Directive 2006/42/EC including all harmonized 
EN Standards like EN 349 
12.4.2.2.2 
The front bumper and rear bumper (required if the vehicle travels in the reverse 
direction) have an under-clearance of 2 inches (51 mm), minimum, and 3 inches 
(76 mm) maximum. 
 
12.4.2.2.3 
Wheel and toe guards shall be provided to minimize ground clearance while 
remaining sufficiently flexible to allow the vehicle to operate on uneven floors. 
12.4.2.2.4 
Wheel and toe guards shall not interfere with normal vehicle operation or 
maintenance. 
12.4.2.2.5 
Guards shall be installed on the vehicle to prevent foreign material (screws, etc.) 
from becoming engaged in the undercarriage of the vehicle. 
12.4.2.3 
Point of operation guarding includes guards necessary to prevent inadvertent 
access to pinch points created by the drive train. e.g., at the area of engagement 
of traction wheels that are accessible from floor levels, and repair spurs.  
12.4.2.4 
Point of operation guards shall be provided at all moving, sliding and rotating 
parts including, but not limited to, the following: 
 
  
12.4.2.4.1 
All moving parts of the power transmission system – i.e., drives, couplings, etc. – 
shall be guarded.  The power transmission guard shall be secured to the drive 
base and/or machinery framework either by fasteners or by retaining or nesting 
clips. 
12.4.2.5 
Point of operation guarding on AGV/AGC equipment located in close proximity to 
production operations, and as defined by the Risk Assessment, must be secured 
with fasteners requiring hand tools for removal.  
12.4.2.5.1 
Where fasteners are used, they shall be secured to the drive base or machinery 
framework. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 15 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
12.4.2.6 
All point of operation guards shall be constructed of lightweight see-through 
mesh with 1/8" thick framing. The width of the framing is to be determined by the 
application; the intent is to keep the guarding light but able to hold shape during 
installation and removal. 
12.4.2.6.1 
The size of the mesh openings shall conform with ISO-13857.  
12.4.2.6.2 
The guarding is to be constructed with lifting handles.  
12.4.2.6.3 
The guarding shall be designed such that no single piece is longer than 72 
inches (1828 mm), nor weigh more than 30 pounds (14 kg). 
12.4.2.6.4 
Although point of operation guarding is not intended to serve as a step, platform 
or walking surface, these guards shall be designed accordingly where there is 
potential for plant personnel to step on them during routine or non-routine 
maintenance, production and/or other related activities. Refer to Sections 9.4.4 
and 9.4.5 for additional guard design requirements. 
12.4.2.6.4.1 Where an operator will be walking and standing on the AGV/AMR. The 
removable access panels over mechanical and electrical devices complete with 
flush mount compression latches to eliminate bulging and trip hazards on the 
platform decking – minimum (4), or (one) at each corner – and (2) stainless steel 
heavy-duty flush lifting handles centered on the access panel. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 16 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
12.4.2.6.5 
Where physical room permits, machinery guards shall be oversized by 15 
percent to accommodate future size increases of belts, sheaves, couplings, 
sprockets, etc. 
12.4.3 
Automation Cells – Interacting with AGV/AGC 
12.4.3.1 
Automation cells interacting with an AGV/AGC shall conform to the specifications 
in GVOSS section 1.3 and 9.4.3. 
12.4.3.2 
AGV/AGC positioner valve boards shall be placed outside of the automated cell.  
12.4.4 
Pinch Points 
12.4.4.1 
Reference GVOSS Section 1.3 and Section 9.4.4. 
12.4.4.2 
Pinch points are created by the transition from AGV/AGC to another. Examples 
are, AGV/AGC entering Automatic cell, AGV/AGC to Fork Transfer, AGV/AGC to 
Clamshells, AGV/AGC to Gantry systems and other similar configurations. 
12.4.4.2.1 
Transitional pinch points to operator workstations shall be identified by a 
minimum 12 inch / 305mm wide safety red painted stripe on the floor starting at 
the guarding leading towards the AGV/AGC entrance area. The stripe shall cover 
both sides of the main line to the edges of the operator platforms. 
 
                   
 
 
 
 
 
 
12.4.4.3 
Pinch Point hazards are typically found to exist in the following configurations: 
 
1. 
Work platforms and pits with vertical faces perpendicular to the path of the 
AGV/AGC. 
 
2. 
AGV/AGC electrical boxes, drives, devices, etc. that protrude out at 90 
degrees to the AGV/AGC and within the path of the AGV/AGC. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 17 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
 
3. 
Entrances to and exits from booths, body loader/unloader, caged 
equipment, through wall openings or silhouettes. 
 
4. 
Insufficient clearance between carrier paths and fixed objects such as 
building columns, railings, etc. GVOSS ref. 9.4.5.3 
 
5. 
Low headroom clearance over the body or carrier creating the potential for 
decapitation. 
 
6. 
Where a skid/pallet transfers between adjacent AGV/AGC. 
 
7.    Floor mounted Jib Boom for hanging tooling.    
 
8.     Signage indicating shall be posted and clearly visible where employees are 
exposed to the pinch point hazard per GVOSS Section 1.4. 
12.4.4.4 
A risk analysis will identify the necessary measures for hazard remediation.  
Reference GVOSS section 9.4.5.3. 
12.4.5 
Facility Layout  
12.4.5.1 
Facility layout shall conform to FAS08-079 Plant Layout to Optimize Pedestrian 
Safety. 
12.4.5.2 
AMR (CB or QB) driven layout requirements to be considered with MP&L & AMR 
supplier during layout development. Packaging assumptions (size & density) 
along with type of AMR for rack delivery is extremely important for layout 
development to avoid late changes. 
12.4.5.2.1 
The optimal distance between the AMR main path and the station should be a 
distance that allows another AMR to follow their path without disturbing the 
loading or unloading operation. When the AMR, is loading/unloading inside the 
station it is highly recommended that the AMR does not enter in the aisle area to 
avoid having traffic blocking and idle time of the other AMR. 
12.4.5.2.2 
AMR type and it’s turning radius should be considered. Sweep conducted by 
AMR for bigger racks (ex. 140 x 96”) is most unfavorable. 
12.4.5.2.3 
Any rack stand placed closer to the edge of the aisle will disturb the traffic, 
making the flow of the AMRs less productive. If the stand is closer to the edge of 
the aisle, the AMR will need to perform the turn invading both paths of the aisle 
and blocking the traffic in both directions. 
12.4.5.2.4 
The AMR laser scanners field / area must be considered for rack stand 
placement, specifically for side-by-side rack stands to allow one AMR to enter in 
the station when another AMR is already in the station next to it. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 18 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
12.4.5.2.5 
Pick Face of the Rack must be defined by MP&L. Longitudinal delivery or 
Transversal delivery - It affects to traffic management, space etc. 
12.4.5.2.6 
Quadbot (QB) may interfere with manual load station ergo mat and/or raised 
platform. For manual load station counterbalance (CB) preferred. 
12.4.5.2.7 
In ground cross transfer conveyor should be avoided. If not, MP&L must 
accommodate AMR stop time and add counter measures to make up for AMR 
stop time. Tooling supplier with VOME to review and consider specific scenario 
with MP&L. Items to consider are AMR slow down zone, AMR stop zone, AMR 
parking zone near cross transfer for traffic jam etc.     
12.4.5.2.8 
MP&L may need infield rack stands. Space must be identified early during the 
layout development. 
12.4.5.2.9 
AMR supplier may have specific requirement for Rack stand, Lift tables, Lift and 
Tilt tables, Turn tables, Over/Under, Rack indexing system etc. AMR requirement 
may increase the overall footprint. AMR specific rack stand design must be 
consider early in the layout development for rack stands. 
12.4.5.2.10 Lift tables, tilt tables, lift and tilt tables may require additional space due to added 
wipers & controls for AMR interface. 
12.4.5.2.11 Avoidance of any obstructions (ex. routing cable tray) under the rack stand due 
AMR scanning zone. 
12.4.5.3 
Rack Stand General Requirements for AMR 
12.4.5.3.1 
Supplier must obtain AMR specific rack stand design requirements before 
starting stand design from VOME / MP&L / AMR Manufacturer. AMR type (QB or 
CB) may have unique stand design for manual and auto load/unload rack stand. 
Before the start of rack stand design, supplier must confirm AMR type for each 
rack delivery from VOME / MP&L. 
                     All pictures represent a concept and design approval is required. 
12.4.5.3.2 
Avoid external forces applied to the AMR while it is positioning itself to start rack 
drop off process. No rack stops on any side. Rack stops may cause AMR safety 
fault. 
12.4.5.3.3 
AMR QBs have a minimum table height requirement for QB to go under the table 
for rack drop off. For manual load stands this requirement along with Ergo 
requirements may drive CB delivery.   The total height of the stand including front 
wiper is restricted by QB & should be followed during stand design.

SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025

12.4.5.3.4 
For CB, follow the clearance needed around wheel to any fixed element of stand 
& from CB body to stand during stand design.

12.4.5.3.5 
CB Wheel stops / Wheel chucks may or may not be required. If required, follow 
the requirements of AMR supplier for design & it’s placement / distance.   
12.4.5.3.6 
Follow the Manufacturers minimum vertical clearance requirement to any fixed 
structure for AMR loading and unloading of the rack.

12.4.5.3.7 
Rough wiper design requirements may be specific to QB and CB. Wiper vertical 
engagement to rack, gap between the rack and wiper, wiper angle & width etc. 
 
                       
     
 
12.4.5.3.7.1 Wipers must be positioned on the stands to avoid the welded areas of the rack. 
This requirement has been set to ensure that the centering of the rack uses 
areas that do not have weld spatters. The target is to center the racks using 
areas within known geometry of wipers. Check this requirement with MP&L / 
AMR Supplier. 
                     
 
12.4.5.3.7.2 Some racks and bulk packaging (baskets) may need unique wiper location (not in 
corners). Rack & basket require unique design must be reviewed with MP&L / 
AMR supplier.

12.4.5.3.7.3 Turntables, lift tables and lift & tilt table may need wipers with unique design & 
must be design as per AMR supplier. The AMR interface with table may drive 
additional safety requirments. Tooling supplier should work with Ford Safety / VO 
Control / AMR supplier to identify those before start of stand design to avoid late 
changes.    
                     
   
  
 
 
                          
 
12.4.5.3.8 
Auto unlock racks, racks with walkway cutout, racks with rollers etc. may require 
unique design & must be reviewed with MP&L / AMR Supplier. 
12.4.5.3.9 
Ford Bulk Packaging (Baskets) may not work with CB AMRs. Baskets may need 
companion pallets on any type of rack stand. Companion pallet always stays with 
basket. VOME/MP&L/AMR Supplier should confirm if companion pallet is 
required or not before the start of stand design. Rack ergo height requirements 
must be evaluated with companion pallet if applicable.

12.4.5.3.11 Two-Tier Rack Stands 
                      
  
 
12.4.5.3.11.1 Follow the CB rack stand design requirements. Second Tier rack swap may need 
additional cycle time. Tooling supplier shall confirm with VOME / MP&L / AMR 
supplier.       
12.4.5.3.11.2  2-tier rack stand designs shall provide an adequate safety approved solution for 
robot programming the upper tier rack load/unload. 
12.4.6 
Safety/Warning Devices  
12.4.6.1 
Warning devices shall be in accordance with ANSI B56.5 and the following: 
12.4.6.1.1 
All warning lights must be LED type. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 24 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
12.4.6.1.2 
Warning lights must consist of a green, amber, blue and red light and be visible 
on each side of the AGV/AGC. 
12.4.6.1.3 
A solid green light Indicates the AGV/AGC is on path and in automatic mode. 
12.4.6.1.4 
All AGV lights shall comply with Ford w-ZA-65 standard. 
12.4.6.1.5 
A distinctive 5 second audible tone shall be provided when Cycle Start is pressed 
and before the AGV/AGC begins motion.  
12.4.6.1.6 
An audible warning of 80 tones per minute shall be used to indicate an AGV/AGC 
is in motion.  
12.4.6.1.7 
A distinctive audible tone shall be provided indicating start of travel, reversing, 
traversing, and pivoting of 160 tones per minute throughout the duration of the 
motion. 
12.4.6.2 
Gravity Control Devices 
12.4.6.2.1 
All lifting devices shall conform with the specifications in GVOSS 9.4.11. 
12.4.7 
Installation of Equipment below Floor Surface 
12.4.7.1 
All machinery and control devices installed below the floor surface, that are not 
flush with the plant floor, shall be provided with a painted yellow all around the 
opening.  The width of the paint stripe around the opening will be 6" (152 mm). 
12.4.7.2 
All components located at or below floor level shall be IP67 rated. 
12.4.8 
AGV/AGC Lifts – Design Criteria 
12.4.8.1 
The following design criteria shall be considered minimum safety requirements.  
Adequate safety factoring must be considered for static, dynamic and shock 
loading with the understanding that potential life exposure risk to employees 
working under suspended equipment must be zero. 
12.4.8.2 
Primary support systems (Lifts) must have a minimum safety factor of 2.5 and 
conform to FAS08-145 Secondary Safety Restraints for Overhead Equipment. 
12.4.8.3 
Lifts are to be designed to ensure load containment considering unit assembly 
process, loading/unloading.  This information shall be documented on the 
AGV/AGC layout. 
12.4.8.4 
The lifts shall be dynamically tested to demonstrate that the load will stay 
on/within the holding details and shall not create a safety hazard in case a Lift 
fails. If through physical testing, it is determined that it is impossible, or 
impractical, to contain the load on the lift during failure the following test criteria 
shall apply: 
 
1. 
The results of the test shall be documented on the AGV/AGC Design 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 25 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
Drawings. 
 
2. 
The following conditions shall require a test: 
A. 
When a new AGV/AGC is built for a new model program/product.  
B. 
When there is a change the incline/decline  
C. 
When a new product/process is to be included on an existing 
AGV/AGC with a different load distribution than the original product for 
which the carrier was designed and tested. 
D. 
When there is a change to the existing part carrying detail; i.e., the 
supporting points and/or locating pins are moved forward or aft of the 
original design resulting in a potential change to the center of gravity. 
E. 
When the part weight increases above that for which the original 
AGV/AGV lift was designed. 
12.4.8.5 
Fabrication of structural members shall be in accordance with applicable AISC 
requirements. 
 
1. 
All structural members shall be ASTM A36, unless otherwise specified. 
 
2. 
Butt welded connections will not be accepted. 
 
3. 
Use full gusset or stiff back plates at member joints, where required. 
12.4.8.6 
When production personnel must access product while the product is held on an 
AGV/AGC with a Lift, an Ergonomic study shall be completed per GVOSS 
Section 1.6.

12.5.1

12.5.2 
Mechanical Drawings 
12.5.2.1 
Reference GVOSS section 9.5.2. 
In addition, the following drawings shall be submitted to the owner for each unit 
as they apply: 
12.5.2.1.1 
Overall AGV/AGC System Layout that including charging ports/locations/stations 
for AGV/AGC systems and all design information as described herein. Branch 
Standard shop drawing lead sheet(s), or "Sheet 1", indicating all purchased 
equipment used: e.g., gearmotors, couplings, etc. 
 
12.5.2.2 
The following information shall be provided on all AGV/AGC unit/system layout 
sheets. 
• Unit 3ZF number 
• Saw cut outlines for bar magnetic installation. 
• Electrical power/controls equipment designations and locations 
• Mechanical sequence of operation for AGV/AGC and automatic transfers, 
etc. 
• AGV/AGC data: 
• JPH rating – design and maximum (if different) 
• Job spacing (minimum/maximum) 
• AGV/AGC speed production areas 
• Job weight (pallet & product) 
• Number of jobs in buffer (minimum/maximum) 
• Total weight (job + AGV/AGC) 
12.5.2.3 
In addition to the items listed in 12.5.2.5, the following information shall be shown 
on all layout lead sheets: 
 
• Maximum number of AGV/AGC that can be accumulated in each zone. 
• Minimum number of AGV/AGC required in each zone to maintain the

production rate 
• Maximum job spacing 
12.5.2.4 
The AGV/AGC Supplier fabrication drawings shall be dimensioned with the 
primary unit of measure as is commonly used at the Owner plant location. All 
drawings shall have complete title blocks. 
12.5.2.5 
The AGV/AGC Supplier is required to have Team Center access to retrieve 
product/part(s) drawings and related files for design and build of new or reworked 
pallet and/or part fixture details.  Supplier is also responsible for uploading the 
final as-built design of all AGV/AGC, tooling and perch/pin/nesting details that 
directly interface and contact the vehicle/product to the Team Center site when 
the work is completed. 
12.5.1 
On-Board AGV/AMR/AGC Adjustable Vehicle Lifts 
12.5.1.1 
Scissor lifts shall be proprietary make to meet the functionality of the lift & must 
be identified in the contractor’s bid. Regardless of scissor lift manufacturer, the 
following information is to be submitted with the Contractor's bid: 
12.5.1.1.1 
Expected useful life. 
12.5.1.1.2 
Documented cycle testing equivalent to 1M cycles.  
12.5.1.1.3 
Duty cycle. 
12.5.1.1.4 
Raise speed. 
12.5.1.1.5 
Lower speed. 
12.5.1.2 
The top surface around vehicle pick-up details shall be skinned with sheet metal. 
No more than 1/8" gap allowed between any two parts. 
12.5.1.3 
The lift shall be equipped with an anti-freefall device so if there is a component 
failure, the lift will maintain its existing height / position. Anti-free fall device must 
conform to ANSI MH29.1 Sect 8.1.2. and be re-settable remotely, without 
requiring access the area under the lift platform. 
12.5.1.4 
The lifts shall be skirted with a heavy-duty vinyl accordion style skirt.  The skirt 
shall be attached to the top of the lift table with Velcro. 
12.5.1.5 
Each AGV/AGC lift shall be equipped with a wire encoder, or similar, so that the 
vertical position, or height, can be communicated to the on-board controller. 
12.5.2 
Bolted Connections for AGV/AGC 
12.5.2.1 
All bolted connections shall conform to GVOSS Section 3.1 Screws, Dowels and 
Washers Specification for Installation of Threaded Fasteners. 
12.5.3 
Welded Connections for AGV/AGC

12.6 
AGV/AGC ELECTRICAL CONTROLS DESIGN 
12.6.1 
General  
12.6.1.1 
All new facility AGV/AGC system controls hardware and software shall be 
designed based on the Ford VO Final Skillet AGV EMS standards, latest release, 
and as specified herein.  
12.6.1.2 
The Contractor shall ensure all AGV/AGC controls design, fabrication and 
installation is completed in compliance with applicable Federal, State/Provincial 
and local codes and industry standards: See GVOSS Section 1 and Section 2. 
12.6.1.3 
Any unique control panel designs shall be the responsibility of the Contractor to 
create based on similarly applicable hardware and software standard designs.  
 
12.6.1.4 
All controls hardware and software designs will become property of Ford Motor 
Company. 
12.6.1.5 
The Tier1/AGV/AGC supplier shall provide electrical and controls equipment as 
identified on the approved components list for AGV/AGC. 
12.6.2 
Controls Drawings  
12.6.2.1 
Reference GVOSS section 1 and section 2. 
12.6.3

12.6.3.1 
The main system Supervisory PLC shall be an Allen Bradley or Siemens. 
12.6.3.2 
This PLC will Communicates adjacent equipment, system safety, FIS, Error-
Proofing system, NextGen AVS and Andon.

12.6.4.3 
The TC/VM system must be supplied with a user interface to allow the user to put

application functions. 
12.6.4.4 
System Monitoring/Reporting the vehicle management system, and user 
interface software must have system monitoring capabilities. 
12.6.4.5 
The system shall be capable of E-Stopping all vehicles.

graphics display, showing all vehicle paths, vehicle status including battery 
charge and current vehicle locations. 
12.6.4.7

for every vehicle in the system.

system.   
The representations for the AGVs should be color-coded to represent the current 
vehicle status i.e.: 
• Block 
• Starve  
• Faulted

This system shall provide the ability to perform route adjustment.  
12.6.4.10 
This system shall have the capability to display status and diagnostic of each 
AGV.  
12.6.4.11 
This system shall have Pre-programmed functions for each application must be 
available from a menu to allow operators to quickly define the desired vehicle 
operation.

12.6.6 
AGV/AMR/AGC Guidance 
12.6.6.1 
Guidance shall be in accordance with ANSI B56.5 and shall not vary throughout 
the vehicle's battery cycle operating range. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 32 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
12.6.6.2 
Deviation from the intended travel path by more than 150mm when using the 
internal guidance reference shall require an emergency stop. 
12.6.6.3 
Guidance methods  
• Magnetic Bar  
• Magnetic Tape 
• SLAM (simultaneous localization and mapping) Navigation 
12.6.6.4 
Guidance and Navigation 
12.6.6.4.1 
AMRs shall be able to support SLAM navigation for their world of operation; as 
well as localization to a (SLAM) map. 
12.6.6.4.2 
AMRs shall not require external installed physical routing location (e.g., magnetic 
tape). May use external location aids (e.g., fiducial marks such as reflective 
targets). The installation of the external guidance aids should not require 
modification of the facility. 
12.6.6.4.3 
Loss of guidance reference may require manual intervention (remote or direct) to 
restart the AMR. 
12.6.6.4.4 
AMRs shall be able to determine their current location by analyzing their current 
environment (perception). The inability to determine current location shall require 
an AMR/AGV to stop. 
12.6.6.4.5 
The AMR/AGV shall stop if the safety zone cannot be determined for the current 
speed. Safety Encoders are required for speed and distance validation. 
12.6.6.4.6 
AMR/AGV shall be able to attain a minimum operating speed dependent upon 
their environment and load. 
12.6.6.4.7 
AMR/AGV shall securely and stably always retain their load. 
12.6.6.4.8 
AMRs should be able to move in any direction from stationary (i.e., 
omnidirectional) dependent on type and safety devices. AMRs should be able to 
move perpendicular to the line of travel (crabbing) or perform zero radius turns. 
12.6.6.4.9 
Supplier is responsible to evaluate actual floor condition before proposing SLAM 
Navigation. 
12.6.6.5 
Obstacle Detection & Avoidance 
12.6.6.5.1 
AMRs/AGVs shall be able to distinguish different types of objects/obstacles 
(Object Detection) as well as be able to detect obstacles and objects within their 
safety zone. 
12.6.6.5.2 
When possible, the AMR should take evasive action to avoid the obstacle and 
continue its current or revised path. When avoidance is not possible, the AMR 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 33 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
should stop. 
12.6.6.5.3 
AMR/AGV should combine sets of predefined safety/collision avoidance 
maneuvers as well as reinforcement learning based on previous evasive or 
rerouting actions. 
12.6.6.5.4 
Predefined safety/collision avoidance maneuvers and rules will always 
supersede learned maneuvers. Central FMS must be intuitive and easy to use by 
all user roles. GMP02-709MES 
12.6.7 
Field Wiring 
12.6.7.1 
For Next-Gen controls style reference Section 8. For GVOSS controls style 
reference Section 14. 
12.6.8 
Safety Devices 
12.6.8.1 
The AGV/AGC that is feeding to, or removing from, a fenced-in piece of 
equipment shall return to proper scan zone for that location. Printed graph shall 
be provided to show speed vs stopping distant calculation. 
12.6.8.2 
AGV/AGC System Emergency Stop Systems: 
• Emergency stops shall be located on each side of the AGV/AGC. 
• Emergency stop buttons located on or off the vehicle must be readily 
accessible to personnel. 
• Laser scanners (in the normal direction(s) of travel) shall be used as the 
primary emergency stopping device. 
• Coverage area of the laser scanner shall cover the width of the widest 
load plus 20 inches and shall have forward slow down and stopping 
ranges to avoid the vehicle or any load from making contact with any 
obstacle. See FAS08-079 Attachment B. 
• Laser scanners shall utilize a minimum of 3 fields: Slowdown, Controlled 
Stop, and Emergency Stop. 
• The laser scanner system shall stop the vehicle prior to any part of the 
load or vehicle striking the obstacle. 
• Loss of guidance reference shall require an emergency stop. 
• Loss of speed control shall require an emergency stop. 
• Loss of Processor function (watchdog timer) shall require an emergency 
stop. 
12.6.8.3 
AGV/AGC System shall have cycle complete button at each operator station. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Issue Date: June 08, 2020 
SECTION 12 – AGV/AGC 
Revision Date: June 8, 2025

 Printed copies are uncontrolled 
12.6.8.4 
All automatic double solenoid valve on/off stations shall be Snap-Action, 
maintained with make-before break contacts.

12.6.9.1.1 
Reference GVOSS section 9.6.11.8 
12.6.9.1.2 
Each QAS pushbutton station shall have 2 indicators lights: i.e., amber to 
indicate Quality”, and blue to indicate “Material”.  
12.6.9.1.3 
A contact of the “Quality Call” and “Material Call” shall be run to the I/O for input 
to the PLC. These contacts will also light the “Quality” and “Material” lights at the 
QAS station.

“broadcast” sheets, or other production aids. 
12.6.11.3

process. 
12.6.11.4 
When specified, AGV/AGC’s shall incorporate an easily removable containment 
pan to capture vehicle fluid run-off. The pan shall be complete with screen cover 
to lap the pan profile, and a ½” (13 mm) welded coupling with drain plug at the 
bottom of the pan. All pan edges shall be rolled or otherwise smooth.

13.1            GENERAL 
13.1.1 
This section describes the requirements for robots, robot controllers, robotic end 
effectors and their associated tool changing systems, and integration of robotic 
systems into the production equipment for new programs where the Statement of 
Work indicates the GVOSS Controls section is to be followed. 
13.1.2 
Components and systems shall comply with all Ford corporate standards, other 
sections of the GVOSS specification, and local requirements. 
13.1.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall select components used in system integration, end 
effectors and tool changing systems from the project specific 'Approved Source 
List'. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 3 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.1.4 
DRESS PACKAGE 
13.1.5 
No Robot Dress brackets shall be welded to the robot. 
13.1.6 
Robot dress brackets shall be specified to withstand the stress and strain 
induced by the robot in motion for each of the robot’s programs. 
13.1.7 
Robot dress brackets and related hardware shall be comprised of standard "off 
the shelf" components within the robot dress component supplier's product line. 
13.1.8 
Robot dress brackets shall be bolted onto their mounting brackets. 
13.1.9 
Robot dress brackets shall be free of burrs and sharp edges. 
13.1.10 
Robot dress shall not contact product or tooling. 
13.1.11 
"Saddles" are to be used when robot dress hoses and cables are routed from 
elevated cable tray to the robot. 
13.1.12 
The dress bracket liners must be compliant enough to hold the hoses and cables 
in position without pinching off the supply of fluids to the end-of-arm tooling. In 
addition, the dress bracket clamps shall hold the hoses and cables in a way to 
prevent any wear that may be caused from relative rubbing motions between the 
hoses, cables and clamp liners. 
13.1.13 
Straps utilized to gather the dress package hoses and cables shall have the 
strength to endure the forces generated during the given applications throughout 
the life cycle of the dress package. They must be readily removed and reinstalled 
as required. They shall have the frictional and strength characteristics required to 
maintain the positions at which they were originally delivered and signed off on. 
13.1.14 
If conventional (no umbilical or sheathings) dress packages are specified for the 
program, standard "off the shelf" covers from the robot dress component 
supplier's product line will be utilized to protect the hoses and cables from any 
rubbing with the product and/or surrounding tooling. 
13.1.15 
If dress packages with umbilical or sheathing are specified for the program, 
standard "off the shelf" umbilical or sheathing from the robot dress component 
supplier's product line will be utilized. 
13.1.16 
If dress packages with umbilical or sheathing are specified for the program, the 
hoses and cables must have a MTTR (Mean Time to Replace) of no more than 
0.5 hours. The Tier 1 tooling supplier shall demonstrate this to the plant. 
13.1.17 
All robot applications shall be processed to minimize any flex of dressing. 
 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 4 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.1.18 
Robot end effector dressing shall not hit or rub any robot motor, cable or fitting 
throughout range of motion on each program utilized. 
13.1.19 
"Strain Relief" shall be used where required to reduce the frictional, gravitational 
and inertial forces on the hoses and cables. 
13.1.20 
Thick non-flammable synthetic reusable straps shall be utilized instead of tie-
wraps for “bundling” cables and hoses together. 
13.1.21 
Cables and hoses shall not contact the floor. 
13.1.22 
Approved hose quick disconnect couplings shall be used as specified for the 
program. 
13.1.23 
Robot control cable track shall not be used to hold robot dress cables and hoses. 
13.1.24 
Robot dress cables and hoses shall not be attached to robot control cable track. 
13.1.25 
Soldered joints are prohibited on any self-confectioned cable. 
13.1.26 
If conventional (no umbilical or sheathing) dress packages are specified for the 
program, any servo weld gun power, communication or I/O cables required shall 
be ordered with individual corrugated sheathing (tubing) to protect them from the 
other utilities within the dress package. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 5 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.2 
CONTROLS 
13.2.1 
A “Clamp and Hold” selection shall be available on the HMI. This function holds 
the robot at the clamp position and display message “Clamp and Hold Robots 
Selected”. This feature shall be implemented for any station requiring verification 
of critical geometry. 
13.2.2 
Robot controllers shall automatically apply motor brakes and remove drive power 
after a defined period without commanded motion. The robot manufacturer 
should recommend and configure a suitable interval to optimize energy savings 
and equipment wear. 
13.2.3 
All robot load operations shall have an Abort Pick-up Mode of the Operation: 
• For operator/robot shared load stations, the operator may press and hold 
the yellow “Reset” button to unclamp the tooling and return the robot to the 
end of the current program at “Home” position. 
• In addition to the yellow "Reset" button, there shall be a selection on the 
HMI to execute the abort pick-up mode of operation. 
• If a fault occurs while picking up a part, the operator shall be alerted to a 
fault condition and directed to press the “Abort” button to return the part to 
the nest or place in a designated reject location. Where a model-
dependent magazine is provided, the robot will also pick up the next part. 
• If a model-dependent robot load operation detects a part type mismatch 
while picking up a part, the system should be designed to automatically 
abort the pickup cycle. Where this is not practical, the operator shall be 
alerted to a mismatch condition and directed to press the “Abort” button to 
return the part to the nest or place in a designated reject location. Where a 
model-dependent magazine is provided, the robot will also pick up the 
next part. 
13.2.4 
Where the process allows and agreement is defined from Ford Manufacturing 
Engineers, all material handling robots placing a part under a sealing, mastic or 
adhesive dispense system shall require an inspection method that may consist of 
the following functions: 
o  Accept 
o Reject: This may be replaced with ‘Swap, Scrap, Eject, Remove Now, 
remove later etc… based on process. 
o Re-Seal 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 6 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
• Pushbuttons shall be available on the nearest HMI and Robot Teach pendant 
to permit acceptance or rejection of the sealed part with manual intervention. 
• When the sealer, mastic or other dispense system is interrupted any time 
during the automatic “dispense cycle” for any reason (E-stop, low material 
flow, etc.) a bit shall be latched on and the logic will require the part to 
automatically be presented (after the dispense cycle is completed) to the 
fence line in as clear view as possible for an operator to view the dispensed 
material coverage on the part. 
o The part will remain presented to the operator until a push button on 
the nearest HMI is activated to the “Accept” or “Reject” or “Re-Seal” 
position. 
o If “Accept” is selected, the part shall continue to the next station. If 
“Reject” is selected and the process allows then the part shall either be 
identified as a rejected part in the shift register or dropped on a specific 
location inside the cell. If “Re-Seal” is selected, then the robot will 
return to the start of process and apply sealant again. 
13.2.5 
Load capacity of the robot shall be determined according to manufacturer’s 
specifications. Final designs shall be reviewed by the robot manufacturer for 
approval to ensure that specific design conditions have been met. 
13.2.6 
Any overlap of adjacent robot work zones shall be shown and shall have proper 
interlocking added to prevent robot crashes during program cycle. This will be 
accomplished by programming interlocking waits and outputs. 
13.2.7 
Robots shall be supplied with interface to Ford Specifications. The supplier shall 
provide design and equipment required for a fully functional robot station, 
including requests for Ford to furnish items. 
13.3 
ROBOT SIZING 
13.3.1 
The Tier 1 supplier shall verify robot size to the application utilizing design 
programs provided by robot manufacturer prior to ordering robots. Late changes 
regarding the size of the robot shall require an approved deviation defining how it 
was missed in the process, prior to ordering a replacement robot. If the change is 
due to a documented Ford engineering change, then Ford is responsible for the 
cost, otherwise the Tooling supplier is responsible for added cost and timing due 
to design errors resulting in a larger robot. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 7 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.4 
TOOL CHANGER APPLICATION 
13.4.1 
Tool adapter holding fixture shall have a safety RIFD safety rated switch 
indicating tool adapter is in position that is plugged directly into the tool changer 
for hardwired operation to release tool. In addition to the RFID safety switch, a 
separate “tool in stand” switch shall be tied to PLC input(s) that shall be mapped 
to the robot. 
13.4.2 
Tool adapter holding fixture shall have compliant die springs allowing for vertical 
and radial tolerance if required for tool changer. 
13.4.3 
Unless specified by the tool stand supplier then the tool adapter holding fixture 
shall have locating pins undersized by 0.5 mm. 
13.4.4 
Tool adapter holding fixture shall have a dust cover which protects against 
accumulation of contaminants (dust, weld slag and water) on the electrical and 
fluid contacts of the tool adapter. 
• For backup guns or batch build tool change processes or other processes 
where cycle time is not a concern, the dust cover shall be movable from 
tool to tool via the robot master tool changer. 
• Where cycle time requires a fast tool change process the tool adapter 
holding fixture shall have a cylinder actuated cover. Where practical, all 
cylinders shall be controlled by one valve. Valve(s) shall be controlled by 
station PLC with interlocks to the robot assuring it is clear of the tool 
stand zone. 
13.4.5 
Each “cover” cylinder shall have a switch showing “cover” clear to accept or 
remove the tool adapter. Switches shall be wired to station PLC controls. Robot 
entry into the tool stand zone shall be interlocked with the covers clear condition. 
(Reference robot manufacturer’s interface drawing and node adapter bit map.) 
13.4.6 
Tool adapter holding fixture shall not clamp tool adapter in place. 
13.4.7 
A tool changer stand may not have more than one dedicated gun assigned to its 
operation. 
13.4.8 
Each tool adapter holding fixture shall be labelled with the assigned gun number 
with colours as defined within the drawing ‘70ZF-20013205’. Numbers shall be a 
minimum height of 200 mm and legible from outside guarded area. If model 
specific, they shall be colour-coded to match SOW model colour coding 
requirements. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 8 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.4.9 
Tool adapter holding fixtures shall be contained within the perimeter guarding. 
Fixtures shall be located to facilitate safe access by personnel performing normal 
maintenance to the tool held in the fixture. 
13.5 
ROBOT END EFFECTOR 
13.5.1 
The robot shall have a "Qualification Part Verification" or “Tooling Check” 
program that will move the robot into a position that is an appropriate distance 
short of the final position, so that maintenance personnel can move the robot in 
teach mode the remaining distance to the part. 
13.6 
ROBOT END OF ARM TOOLING CHANGER REQUIREMENTS.  
13.6.1 
This section provides guidelines to protect for the addition of tool changers in 
robot applications.  
• This shall apply to all material handling end effectors, including docking and 
geometry setting end effectors. 
NOTE: This does not apply to welding or sealer robots. 
13.6.2 
To protect for future flexibility, material handling end effectors shall be capable of 
being retrofitted with tool changers to support future model requirements.  The 
auto changers shall be protected for but not provided until they are required. 
o Process to Protect for future addition of tool changers: 
• The tool changer weight shall be considered. 
• The tool changer offset to the robot end effector shall be considered. 
• The hole pattern for the tool changer, robot and end effector shall be 
considered. 
• The hole pattern shall be provided on all material handling robots and end 
effectors. 
• The robot sizing shall be calculated with the weight and offset of the 
changer included. 
• A Virtual modelling program as approved by VOME model using the tool 
changer offset shall be used to validate the feasibility of adding the future 
tool changer. 
• A spacer between the robot face plate and the end effector shall be 
installed to protect for the addition of a Tool Changer. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 9 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.7 
ROBOT “CONTROLS” WELD – NO-WELD INTERLOCKING 
13.7.1 
The following defines the performance expected in an automation system with a 
welding robot: 
• Condition: Weld Control placed in a “No Weld Mode” (Internal No Weld) 
through the Timer Data Entry Panel or through its own faceplate. 
o Performance Expected: Robot will initiate a weld, gun will close and 
NOT fire, robot will turn on the #1 Time Delay Exceeded (Weld 
Fault) light, robot will issue a “Re-weld” command, gun will close 
and NOT fire, robot will sit with the gun in the position of the spot 
weld waiting for a “Weld Complete” from the weld control. 
o Requirement To Clear Fault: Operator put the weld control into the 
“Weld Mode” through the timer data entry panel or through its own 
faceplate, operator press the “Manual Weld” pushbutton on the 
Robot Interface Panel which initiates a weld, gun will close and fire, 
robot will receive the “Weld Complete” from the weld control and 
proceed to the next spot location. 
• Condition: “Weld / No Weld” switch on the Robot Interface Panel is placed 
in the “No Weld” position while the robot is in its Start Position. 
o Performance Expected: In the Production Mode with Parts, the 
robot will NOT leave the Home position if the switch is in the “No 
Weld” position. The Smart Display for the station will display a 
message stating that “Robot Timer is Not in the Weld Mode”. 
o Requirement To Clear Fault: Placing the selector switch to the 
“Weld” position will allow the cycle to begin. 
NOTE: In the “Auto Repeat”, the position of the “Weld/No Weld” selector switch 
on the robot interface has no significance. “Auto Repeat” will run in either 
position. 
13.7.2 
The following defines the performance expected in an Automation System with a 
pedestal welding robot: 
• Condition: “Weld/No Weld” switch on the Robot Interface Panel is placed 
in the “No Weld” position during a Welding Program. 
o Performance Expected: If, during the welding program, an Operator 
puts the selector switch in the “No Weld” position, the robot will 
continue its welding program with the weld timer in external no weld 
and no weld faults will be detected by the robot unless the timer 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 10 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
actually faults in the no weld mode. The PLC controls will not set a 
cycle complete if the switch was in the “No Weld” position at the 
last weld of the robot due to a “Timer Fault” signal from the weld 
control. A message will be on the Smart Display indicating “Robot 
Cycle Overtime” or “Robot Timer Fault; Cycle Will Rerun”. 
o Requirement to Clear Fault: An operator will be required to reset 
the timer fault, normally by manually initiating the weld control 
through the robot interface panel with the switch in the “Weld” 
position. Once the fault is cleared, the robot will be told by the PLC 
to run the welding program again. 
NOTE:  
In the “Auto Repeat”, the robot will be instructed to run as if all 
welding was complete without a timer fault. 
• Condition: “Weld/No Weld” switch on the Robot Interface Panel is placed 
in the “No Weld” position during a welding program on the combination 
pedestal welding material handling robot system. 
o Performance Expected: Robot will continue through the welding 
portion of its program and no weld faults will be detected by the 
robot unless the timer actually faults in the no weld mode. The 
Robot will have an external wait signal programmed at the drop off 
location so the robot will not unload the part if the welding program 
ended with a timer fault caused by the switch in the no weld 
position. The smart display will show a message similar to the 
above condition. 
o Requirement To Clear Fault: An operator will be required to perform 
a wait override through the robot control panel or manually initiate a 
weld through the robot interface panel to clear the fault and allow 
the robot to continue with the part unload portion of its program. 
That operator is to assure that the part has been fully welded or 
remove the part and have it recycled through the automation. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 11 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.8 
ROBOT TEMPLATES AND PROGRAMMING GUIDES. 
13.8.1 
General requirements 
13.8.1.1 
Ford robot templates and programming guides have been developed by the Tier 
2 Robot suppliers for commonly used applications. Except as provided in the 
Statement of Work, the Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers shall use existing 
programming guides for all applications. Where an application does not align with 
an existing template, the Tier 1 integrator shall submit a deviation request to the 
responsible Ford controls engineer. Approval of the deviation will direct the 
commissioning of a new template and programming guide, or a change to the 
equipment design to better align with an existing template. 
13.8.1.2 
Current Robot Programming guidelines for the project will be provided on request 
to the responsible Ford controls engineer. 
13.8.1.3 
Low level programming languages, background programming, or use of interrupt 
routines shall not be used in the robot system except where provided in the 
approved base template provided by Tier 2 Robot supplier. 
13.8.1.4 
The robot program start point shall be the same physical location as the robot 
program end point. Robot tool center points shall be validated and demonstrated 
to the responsible Ford controls engineer prior to tool shipment.   
13.8.1.5 
Separate robot programs shall be used for each face of a turntables, trunnions, 
or tool tray even if the tooling plates are identical. The initial program may be 
copied into each unique faceplate program during design and setup. 
13.8.1.6 
First step after “Move to Pounce” shall be away from home position. 
13.8.1.7 
Separate robot programs shall be used for each different model or family of 
components with foreseeably different part, process, or path requirements. 
• 
Dispensing robots in glass cells shall have only one seal path model per 
shape. 
13.8.1.8 
Robot program numbers shall be consistent across all commodities. Tier 1 
integrator shall submit the numbering scheme for approval by Ford controls 
engineer.

13.8.2 
Robot Zones 
13.8.2.1 
Robot Zone Documentation. Robot zone layouts shall be provided in the 
Electrical Controls Application Drawing package for each system. This will be a 
design tool for the PLC and robot programmers and used during validation, 
equipment troubleshooting, and modification. 
13.8.2.2 
Interference zones between the robot and adjacent robots or equipment shall be 
implemented and verified for each robot using the Robot Zone Validation Sheet. 
 
13.8.2.3 
Robot zone numbers should follow the guideline shown below.

13.8.3 
Robot Zone Logic 
13.8.3.1 
Zone logic prevents more than one robot sharing the same physical space at the 
same time, potentially resulting in a hazard or crash condition. Both robot 
controller and PLC shall comply with the Ford Controls and Robot Programming 
Standard.  
13.8.3.2 
The robot programmer shall interlock each robot program according to the 
flowchart of Figure 5.9.3.2, with consideration for the zone interlocks listed in the 
Robot Zone Documentation and Robot Zone Validation Sheet. 
 
Figure 5.9.3.2 Robot Zone Program Flowchart 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 14 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.8.3.3 
The PLC programmer shall adhere to the standard logic structure provided in the 
GVOSS PLC template, modifying only with consideration for the zone interlocks 
listed in the Robot Zone Documentation and Robot Zone Validation Sheet. 
• Permission to enter a zone is controlled by a “Zone Clear Memory” latched 
coil.  
• Rung 3 Zone Clear Memory reset: The robot output “Clear of Zone xx” is 
used to unlatch the “Zone Clear Memory”. Nothing else is in that line of 
logic. Since the robot clear of the zone output is turned on most of the 
time, the Zone Clear Memory is inhibited from turning on. 
• Rung 1 Zone Clear Memory set: “Zone Clear Memory” is set when the 
robot output “Clear of Zone xx” turns off and no other robot has set its 
“Zone Clear Memory” for the same zone (except if the other robot is in 
bypass). For example, if three robots share the same zone, the other two 
“Zone Clear Memory” will be in series with the Not “Clear of Zone xx”.  
• If none of the robots sharing a zone need to enter the zone, all the “Zone 
Clear Memory” are inhibited from turning on. All robots have equal access 
to the zone control. The first robot to request entry into the zone then sets 
its “Zone Clear Memory”, thereby preventing all the other robots from 
entering the shared space.  When the first robot to request entry into the 
zone completes its task, that robot then turns on “Clear of Zone xx” which 
resets its “Zone Clear Memory” allows the next robot to enter the space. 
 
 
Figure 5.9.3.3 Robot Zone PLC Logic Example 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 15 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.8.3.4 
Robot Zone Logic Prove-out 
The critical nature of robot zones programmed correctly requires a thorough 
verification prior to the buy-off process. 
Run line in Auto Repeat  
o Starting with one robot at 10% and watch for it to enter first 
interference zone. 
o Once robot has entered the zone, pause/hold the robot while 
maintaining auto mode. 
o Allow surrounding interfering robots (one at a time) to begin motion 
also at 10% and ensure that the robot stops and waits for a signal 
from the PLC to allow it to enter the interfering zone. 
o If the robot does not wait, put it in hold and check PLC logic and 
add/verify that the interfering robot’s corresponding zone number is 
correctly preventing the robot from receiving its request approval. 
Restart with all robots back at home. 
o Repeat this process by allowing whichever robot entered the 
shared interfering zone 2nd, to now enter the shared interfering 
zone 1st and repeat the ‘pause/hold’ method listed above to back 
check that the zones work in both directions for both scenarios as 
robots always have potential to enter the interfering zone at 
different times in any given machine process. 
o Repeat the entire process for each robot and every zone 
throughout the machines process for each possible robot program 
select and each possible robot program jumps (I.E all program 
loops, return to home scenarios, return from pounce, tip dress, 
maintenance, cut tape, etc.) within each program select while also 
verifying clearances and cable routing. 
o Increase speeds of robots one by one from 10% to 100% 
incrementally (10%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%). Increasing the speed 
of the robots individually will give you situations that under “normal 
circumstances” wouldn’t occur. 
o Once each zone has individually been verified it is to be signed off 
by both the PLC and Robot programmer performing the validation 
on the Robot Zone Validation Sheet. 
o After completion of the Robot Zone Validation Sheet, the signed 
sheet is to then be brought to the Ford VO controls design follow up 
for review and approval. 
 
13.8.4 
The basic comments language shall be English. All user interface texts must be 
in local language unless specified differently within the specific project Statement 
of Work (SOW)

13.8.5 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall guarantee that all persons associated with robot 
programming participate in the specified Robot Supplier's basic training and 
Robot Programming Specification training. This also includes all subcontractors. 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall further guarantee that on any Ford site the same 
persons (including subcontractors) that start with programming of robots 
accompany the project during commissioning and start-up. If legitimate 
requirements define that this is not possible, then satisfactory notice shall be 
given and a replacement person with adequate skills and project knowledge shall 
be declared. 
13.8.6 
All critical inputs such as vacuum, part controls, clamps etc. shall be continuously 
monitored to ensure correct status during a process. This is required to avoid 
damage to grippers, tooling etc. 
13.8.7 
Any change made in the safety section of the robot must be detected and 
identified to the local PLC. When detected then this fault must be acknowledged 
from the local HMI and the screen on the HMI must be password protected. Until 
such times that it is acknowledged then the equipment must not be in a status to 
run in Automatic. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025

EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.9 
LOCATION OF MAINTENANCE POSITIONS 
13.9.1 
The robot must complete the last two feet (60 cm) towards the maintenance 
position at a speed of not more than five inches (130 mm) per second. 
13.9.2 
Pulled Tip Bypass. When the weld water is shut off, the robot shall automatically 
stop closing the weld gun as it proceeds through the rest of the welding program. 
The robot will move to the maintenance position where the skilled trades are to 
replace the weld caps.  Once the water is turned back on, the robot will return the 
part and start the weld process from the beginning 
13.9.3 
For any pedestal application and/or minor end effector maintenance position for 
material handling robots, the robot shall be positioned such that the robot end 
effector and associated equipment can be reached by the maintenance 
personnel and worked on in a safe manner.  The robot shall also be positioned 
such that the part and end effector are no closer than 1500mm or 60 inches from 
any pedestal application (if present). The system integrator shall ensure that the 
maintenance personnel have a clear path from the gate to the pedestal 
application and from the pedestal application to the robot Material Handling end 
effector. 
13.10 
SERVO WELD GUNS 
13.10.1 
Each weld servo gun has a relationship between the servo motor current and 
weld tip force depending on a number of factors.  To that end each weld gun 
must utilize the equivalent of a "look-up table" for proper operation of the servo 
weld gun. A calibration procedure must be performed to establish values of servo 
motor current to weld tip force. Each servo weld gun must have this data stored 
in a robot table. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 18 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.11 
OPERATOR LOADS DIRECTLY TO ROBOT APPLICATION 
13.11.1 
In an operator load to robot application, the robot end of arm tooling becomes the 
part nest/load tooling. The robot and the operator share the same workspace at 
the same time. Power shall be removed from all devices that create a hazard to 
the operator. 
13.11.2 
An operator will be permitted to load directly to a robot provided all the following 
conditions are met: 
• A safety-rated monitored stop function is active, or power is removed from the 
robot servo motors according to PL “d” with structure category 3. 
• The output power to the robot end effector directional valves is interrupted 
according to PL “d” with structure category 3. 
• The robot is in a taught reference position for the load position. 
• The controller verifies the robot is still in the reference position (within 5mm) 
after motor power is removed. 
13.11.3 
If an operator enters the shared space when any of the above conditions are not 
met, a protective stop shall be issued to the robot. 
13.11.4 
Perimeter guarding (fixed or interlocking guards) shall prevent unauthorized 
access to the cell. A violation of any electro-sensitive protective devices (e.g. 
light curtains and safety scanners) at any point in the operation shall stop the 
entire cell. 
13.11.5 
The controls shall be designed to provide at least three reference positions.  
Separate reference positions can be used for load height and/or EOA orientation 
if multiple models are required. 
13.11.6 
The robot reference position limits shall be set in the robot such that the robot will 
not drift more than 5 mm in any direction after motor power is removed.  A loss of 
the reference position signal after motor power is removed is an indication that 
an axis brake is failing. 
13.11.7 
The operator shall be able to change between reference positions.  If a new 
reference position is selected while the robot is in motion, the robot will complete 
its program and return to the new reference position after its cycle is completed. 
13.11.8 
An indicator shall be provided at the operator load station to inform the operator 
that the robot is in a safe state to enter the shared workspace. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 19 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.12 
SAFETY 
13.12.1 
Robot systems shall be designed and constructed to comply with the 
requirements of ISO 10218-1. 
13.12.2 
Integration of robot systems and cells shall comply with the requirements of ISO 
10218-2. 
13.12.3 
Safety-related parts of control systems shall be designed so that they comply 
with PL “d” with structure category 3 as described in ISO 13849-1, unless the 
results of a risk assessment determine that an alternative performance criterion 
is appropriate. 
13.12.4 
During operation of the robot using Teach Mode within the safeguarded space 
(i.e. with interlocked guards open), the robot safety system shall not permit the 
robot tool center point to exceed the safety-rated reduced speed limit as defined 
in ISO 10218-1 (250 mm/s). 
13.12.5 
The pendant or teaching control device shall have a three-position enabling 
device in accordance with IEC 60204-1. When continuously held in a center-
enabled position, the enabling device shall permit robot motion and any other 
hazards controlled by the robot. 
13.12.6 
The robot manufacturer’s Safety Instructions shall be adhered to all times. 
13.12.7 
The Tier 1 Tooling Integrator shall design the robot end effector such that part 
retention is maintained upon loss of power. (Reference the GVOSS Design 
Section - Pneumatic Design) 
13.12.8 
The Tier 1 Tooling Integrator shall implement a protective stop function to 
interrupt output power to the robot end effector directional valves according to PL 
“d” with structure category 3. 
13.12.9 
If a tool changer is employed, the tool changer shall be designed such that the 
control module prevents unclamping in any position other than the tool stand 
according to PL “d” with structure category 3. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025

13.13 
INTERNAL DRESS PACKAGE REQUIREMENTS 
13.13.1 
Minimum Requirements 
13.13.1.1 
Tier 2 Robot Supplier shall provide standard robot cables from the robot 
controller to the robot. The Tier 1 Integrator shall determine the length and select 
from available options. 
13.13.1.2 
Tier 2 Robot Supplier shall provide a connection plate on the base of the robot 
Axis 1 for ancillary functions. These shall be wired through to the connection plate 
at Axis 3 using continuous flex rated cable suitable for the application. 
• The robot manufacturer may provide a proprietary combination cable between 
Axis 1 and Axis 3; however, these should be terminated to standard 
receptacles at the robot base and the Axis 3 connection plate. 
13.13.1.3 
Tier 2 Robot Supplier shall provide Ford-approved double-ended cordsets and 
fluid power lines from Axis 3 to Axis 6 wrist. 
13.13.1.4 
Tier 1 Integrator and Tier 2 Robot supplier shall insulate all electrical equipment 
mounted on the robot from robot arm. The intent is to keep ground currents from 
the robot arm. Except for PELV circuits, plug and socket connections shall 
provide first make last break protective bonding contact. 
13.13.2 
Material Handling (MH) or Tool Changer (TC) 
13.13.2.1 
System to Axis 1 Base – provided by Tier 1 Integrator 
• One (1) Controls Network cable, Industrial Cat 5E unshielded twisted pair with 
M12 D-coded male connectors from robot base connection plate to network 
switch 
• One (1) DC Power Supply cable rated minimum 600V 10A, Mini 7/8” 4-pin 
double-ended cord from robot base connection plate to DC power supply. 
• One (1) Adapter fittings and pneumatic air supply line to robot connection 
plate per GVOSS Section 2 Design and local requirements 
13.13.2.2 
Axis 1 Base to Axis 3 Connection plate – provided by Tier 2 Robot supplier. 
• One (1) Controls Network cable, Industrial Cat 5E twisted pair with M12 D-
coded female receptacles at Axis 1 and 3 
• One (1) DC Power Supply cable rated minimum 250V 10A, Mini 7/8” 4-pin 
male receptacle at Axis 1, 4-pin female receptacle at Axis 3 
• One (1) Pneumatic supply line with ISO 1179-1 BSPP G-1/2” threaded ports 
at Axis 1 and Axis 3 bulkhead connection plate 
13.13.2.3 
Axis 3 to Axis 6 Wrist – provided by Tier 2 Robot supplier. 
• One (1) Controls Network cable, Industrial Cat 5E twisted pair with M12 D-
coded male connectors at Axis 3 and 6 
• One (1) DC Power Supply cable rated minimum 250V 10A, Mini 7/8” 4-pin

including through connection points.

Force Transducer (if required): 
• Pin 1 (+24 VDC), Pin 2 (RESET OUTPUT), Pin 3 (+ INPUT), Pin 4 (- INPUT), 
Pin 5 (0VDC) 
• Cables must be shielded; the shield must be continuous from end-to-end 
including through connection points

Robot Transfer Unit (RTU)

All items listed for non-RTU robot applications, where Robot supplier is 
responsible to provide the cable from Axis 1 to robot control and / or external 
application; the robot supplier shall provide same cable from Axis 1 to entry of 
RTU cat track along with cable from entry of RTU cat track to robot control and / 
or external application.

The Tier 1 supplier shall be responsible for coordinating with robot manufacturer 
required cable routing and lengths. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 26 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.14 
COLLABORATIVE ROBOTS 
13.14.1 
Robot system 
13.14.1.1 
A robot is “collaborative” when the robot and person are moving in the same 
space at the same time – a “collaborative workspace”. Collaborative operations 
include one or more of the following methods: safety-rated monitored stop, hand 
guiding, speed and separation monitoring, or power and force limiting. 
13.14.1.2 
Each collaborative robot application shall have a design and validation plan 
reviewed and approved by VO safety before engineering of the robot system 
begins. 
13.14.1.3 
The robot controller shall be identified on the w-E1-QSSL Electrical Qualified 
Safety Source List and have an approved w-EL10 Appendix. 
13.14.1.4 
All collaborative robots shall comply with ISO/TS 15066, FAS08-131, and 
FAS08-131 attachment 1 “Collaborative Robots and Robotic Systems”. 
13.14.1.5 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall complete a comprehensive risk assessment of each 
collaborative robot system. Where this is integrated into existing equipment or 
integrated into equipment supplied by others, this shall be a separate document. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 27 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.14.2 
Collaborative workspace 
13.14.2.1 
Robot safe zone access placards shall be designed and posted at each area 
where it is possible to enter a safeguarded space not protected by perimeter 
guarding (fixed or interlocking guards). These placards shall include the 
collaborative work area and location of scanners and presence sensing zones. 
 
Figure 5.15.2.1 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 28 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.14.2.2 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide any awareness barriers as needed for the 
application. 
13.14.2.3 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide safety signs or placards as needed at 
approaches to the robot safe zone area. 
13.14.2.4 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall provide and install black-and-yellow striped floor 
markings to identify the collaborative workspace to comply with the requirements 
of FAS08-131 attachment 1. 
 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 29 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.14.3 
Speed and separation monitoring 
13.14.3.1 
Risk reduction is achieved by maintaining a safe distance between the operator 
and robot. The system is usually integrated with electro-sensitive protective 
devices (i.e. safety laser scanners). 
13.14.3.2 
The robot shall be equipped with a safety-rated monitored speed function, a 
safety-rated monitored stop function, and safety-rated soft axis and space 
limiting. 
13.14.3.3 
If the operator is within the safe distance of the robot, the robot shall initiate a 
protective stop and turn off any hazardous tools. The robot may implement a 
speed reduction and transition to a safety-rated monitored stop to avoid violation 
of the safe distance. Robot motion may resume when the operator is clear of the 
hazard if permitted in the risk assessment. 
13.14.4 
 Power and force limiting 
13.14.4.1 
Risk reduction is achieved by inherently safe controls in the robot that prevent 
the robot from exerting a force or pressure that causes injury to the human body. 
13.14.4.2 
The tooling integrator shall assess the parts of the human body the robot is likely 
to contact and limit the speed to ensure that the force and pressure do not 
exceed the biomechanical limits in ISO/TS 15066 Annex A. 
13.14.4.3 
Objects with sharp, pointed, shearing or cutting edges and parts which could 
cause injury shall not be present in the contact area. 
13.14.4.4 
Contact to sensitive body regions, including the head, face, and neck shall be 
prevented. 

 
 
 
 
Issue Date: 08 Jan. 2021 
SECTION 13 - ROBOTS 
Revision Date: June 08 2025 
 
 
 
Page 30 of 30 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
copies are uncontrolled 
13.14.5 
Integration 
13.14.5.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall document all safety functions and settings. 
13.14.5.2 
Collaborative robots shall be interlocked to a safety PLC. 
13.14.5.3 
The robot safety controller shall be protected with a password and should send a 
checksum to the PLC for monitoring of changes to the safety settings. 
13.14.5.4 
Interlock signals between the PLC to Robot interlocks shall include at a 
minimum: 
Robot to PLC 
• Robot at home 
• Robot at work 
• Robot Faulted 
• Robot has Force Fault 
• Robot Ok to Run 
• Robot Process complete 
• Robot clear of zone 
PLC to Robot 
• Initiate robot 
• Robot Stop 
• Robot Ok to proceed 
• Fault Reset 
• Reset process complete 
13.14.6 
Installation 
13.14.6.1 
Robot pedestals shall be designed to comply with the robot manufacturer’s 
recommendations to ensure the safety functions are not compromised. 
13.14.6.2 
The integrator shall ensure that the dress package for end-of-arm tooling does 
not cause protective stop faults during robot operation. 
13.14.6.3 
The dress package shall not create additional hazards to the operator such as 
stabbing, cutting, maiming, or pinching.

14.1 
GENERAL 
14.1.1.1 
This section is for global standard tooling only. For kits, reuse of robots, weld 
controls, sealers, riveters or like equipment: Standard shall be in accordance to 
the “actual” specifications/requirements on site. Except for any safety items or 
additional items as documented in the Statement of Work for that project (SOW). 
14.1.1.2 
Components and systems shall comply with all Ford corporate standards, other 
sections of the GVOSS specification, and local requirements. 
14.1.1.3 
See GVOSS section 2 Design for GVOSS hardware info and requirements. 
14.1.1.4 
See also GVOSS Section 13 for GVOSS Robots. 
14.1.1.5 
Get the latest controls GVOSS Programming Guide from Teamcenter. 
14.1.1.6 
A web server (web page) is preferred for each Ethernet device. The web server 
(web page) shall be reviewed and approved by Ford VOME Controls. 
14.1.1.7 
All supplied materials shall not support combustion. 
14.1.1.8 
Each cable, device and switch shall be tagged per guidelines outlined in GVOSS 
hardware Template in Teamcenter. 
14.1.1.8.1 
All switches shall have a tag that includes the Name, description and I/O 
address. The switch tag shall be engraved with text no smaller than 0.125” (3 
mm) and be mounted adjacent to the switch 
14.1.1.8.2 
Both lamacoid and soft lamacoid (thick sticker) are allowed; provided that they 
are flame resistant and the ink does not fade. In some cases, such as on panels 
for pushbutton boxes; pushbuttons and selector switches for example; the soft 
lamacoid are preferred over lamicoid (as they do not cut your hand). 
14.1.1.9 
Daisy chaining power or ethernet described in this section is only allowed on the 
“same” station, fixture, conveyors, robot or cell. Case needs Ford VOME Controls 
approval.

14.2 
PROCESS 
14.2.1 
Robots (See also GVOSS Section 13 Robots) 
14.2.1.1 
No back up weld robot stations. 
14.2.1.2 
Robot applications: 
14.2.1.2.1 
All external robot applications shall receive the regional 3 phase AC control 
power supply (Example N.A. 480 VAC, 60 Hz).  
14.2.1.2.2 
Applications that need hot AC control power to maintain proper function when the 
robot control power is locked out; such as Sealer controllers, shall receive 
regional single-phase AC control power supply (Example N.A. 120 VAC, 60 Hz) 
separate from the robot. 
14.2.1.2.2.1 For a single sealer / hot AC control power need, review with Ford VOME 
Controls. Review cell power distribution panel for the hot AC power source.  
14.2.1.2.3 
Each application shall be documented as part of the Controls Application 
Package. 
14.2.1.2.4 
All other exceptions shall require a deviation prior to design, testing and 
implementation. 
14.2.1.3 
If it is required to remove power to the robot drives when loading directly to an 
end effector, then a blue lamp is required at the operator load station, to indicate 
that the robot motor power is disabled. See also GVOSS Section 13 Robots. 
GVOSS Section 13 Robots supersedes these guidelines in the event of a 
conflict. 
14.2.1.4 
Welding and tip-dressing by stepping through the robot weld program in teach 
mode shall be permissible per the Global Risk Assessment, BCE-Welding_tip 
dressing with gate open.xlsx, unless superseded by local electrical requirements 
or plant’s direction.  
14.2.1.5 
Robot bypass capability and functionality. 
14.2.1.5.1 
The Tier 1 Integrator shall develop and provide a list of the proposed robots with 
the enabled bypass functionality during the 50% Mechanical Design review. The 
proposed list of robots with the bypass functionality enabled shall be reviewed 
and approved by VIS or Launch Leader.  The Tier 1 Integrators then implement 
and enable the bypass functions for each robot on the approved list. 
14.2.1.5.2 
Prior to bypassing any robot; an approved repair process, which includes welds, 
rivets, clinch, and flow drill shall be followed. Plant management shall be 
responsible to ensure that the bypassed robot process can and will be picked up 
plus set up the control plans for repair joints with in the appropriate system. 

 
 
 
 
Global Vehical Operations Structures Standard 
 
Issue Date: Jan. 08, 2021 
SECTION 14 - CONTROLS 
Revision Date: June 08, 2025 
 
 
 
Page 4 of 34 
EQUIPMENT STANDARDS 
  Printed copies are uncontrolled 
14.2.1.5.3 
The robot bypass capability and functions shall be included and tested for each 
robot’s individual application (e.g. MH with Seal APP + Rivet APP) as well as 
each robot itself (bypassing the entire robot and its application(s)). 
14.2.1.5.4 
Robots that perform geometry or closed out joining (includes dispensed material) 
plus robots not listed in the 14.2.1.5.1 bypass list shall not be bypassed after 
function testing in 14.2.1.5.3 with their robot bypass PB visibility turned off before 
tool buyoff. 
14.2.1.5.5 
Carried applications can be tested with or without parts. Ped applications shall be 
tested with parts.  
14.2.2 
Cells with Roll Bed (or High-Speed Transfer)  
14.2.2.1 
For each operator work station; the next station and proceeding station shall 
utilize cylinder operated pallet stop. Controls shall interface with the mechanical 
team to ensure they are aware of this safety need. 
14.2.3 
Body Framer  
14.2.3.1 
Maximum of 40 valves and 96 proximity switches per tooling gate. 
14.2.3.2 
The Framer station shall have one Mobile HMI inside the cell to provide clear line 
of sight to the gate tooling. The Mobile HMI location must not interfere with future 
gate change storage and transfer. 
The Mobile HMI must have a switch indicating it is in storage position prior to 
allowing the cell to go into auto mode. 
This HMI must only be capable of actuating minor motions. It must not allow any 
major motions (ex. no drive motion). Major motions must only be actuated from 
HMIs outside of the cell with the perimeter safeties reset. 
14.2.4 
Hard Tool Welders 
14.2.4.1 
Pneumatic weld guns shall be utilized. Controls shall interface with the 
mechanical team to ensure they are aware of this need. 
14.2.5 
Projection Welders 
14.2.5.1 
The weld gun actuator shall be robot controlled. Feeder shall be PLC controlled. 
See also GVOSS Section 13 Robots. 
14.2.6 
Tooling and Valves 
14.2.6.1 
Maximum of 8 cylinders per valve. 
14.2.6.2 
Removed. 
14.2.6.3 
Removed.

14.2.6.4 
The valve island and M12 connector version shall utilize the same valve / 
manifold type and be interchangeable. See project approved components. 
14.2.6.4.1 
Example: Applications with 1 pneumatic valve (I.E. date stamp, air prep stand, 
nut feeder escapement / feed, etc.); a single valve manifold block with M12 
connectors connected to an IP6x block I/O shall be used, utilizing the same valve 
/ manifold type listed on the project approved components and interchangeable 
between valve island version. 
14.2.6.5 
No more than two valve island power shall be daisy chained. 
14.2.6.6 
No more than five valve island ethernet shall be daisy chained.

14.3 
CONTROLS DESIGN 
14.3.1 
Hardware Application 
14.3.1.1 
Architecture shall be Ethernet I.P. 
14.3.1.1.1 
Exception for kitting existing tooling: Use existing specifications/requirements to 
which the equipment was initially installed. 
14.3.1.2 
For initial design / installation: 
14.3.1.2.1 
Processor memory used shall be 75% (Rockwell Example: “Green”) or less. 
14.3.1.2.2 
PLC network bandwidth, CIP connections and node counts shall be 80% 
capacity or less. 
14.3.1.2.3 
80ms max scan time. 
14.3.1.2.4 
Ethernet switches shall have a minimum of 2 spare ports. 
14.3.1.2.5 
For dedicated or reserved Ethernet ports see the latest GVOSS hardware 
template. 
14.3.1.3 
For Kit work: 
14.3.1.3.1 
Processor memory used shall be 85% (Rockwell Example: “Yellow”) or less. 
14.3.1.3.2 
PLC network bandwidth, CIP connections and node counts shall be 90% 
capacity or less. 
14.3.1.3.3 
90ms max scan time.  
14.3.1.3.4 
Ethernet switches shall have a minimum of 1 spare ports. 
14.3.1.3.5 
For dedicated or reserved Ethernet ports see the project GVOSS hardware 
template. 
14.3.1.4 
Ford VOME Controls approved panel designs shall be utilized.  
14.3.1.4.1 
All standard controls panels and Ford specified panels are to be stored in 
Teamcenter. 
14.3.1.4.2 
Tier 1 Integrators are required to have Teamcenter access for retrieving controls 
standards and specified designs. 
14.3.1.4.3 
Any unique designs shall be the responsibility of the Tier 1 Integrator to create. 
All designs will then be approved by Ford VOME Controls and documented with 
Ford standard controls naming/number.

14.3.1.7 
Controls drawing design will consist of but not limited to; all interconnects of 
standard panels, product specific tooling controls, power distribution, bill of 
materials, networks, I/O, grounding and bonding, network loading, line layout, 
basic line layout placement of IP5x and IP6x type devices, interlocks to adjacent

and labels, cut outs, all in inches and mm, SCCR rating and shall meet local wire 
codes. 
14.3.1.9 
Ford standard global panel design(s). 
14.3.1.9.1 
Shall have a stand-alone controls design (E-plan / AutoCAD file). 
14.3.1.9.2
